name: DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION

topics.title: Design of a Slab
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design of a Slab

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **slab** is a planar, two-dimensional structural element characterized by a thickness that is small compared to its in-plane dimensions (length and breadth), designed to carry loads primarily through **flexural (bending) action** and to transmit them to the supporting beams, walls, or columns. In the context of farm structures, slabs form critical horizontal load-carrying components in dairy barn floors, poultry house platforms, feed storage godowns, threshing floors, machinery sheds, and greenhouse work areas.

According to **IS 456:2000 (Plain and Reinforced Concrete — Code of Practice)**, slabs are classified based on the **support conditions** and the **ratio of spans**. The two principal categories are:

1. **One-Way Slab**: A slab where the ratio of longer span to shorter span ($L_y/L_x$) is **greater than or equal to 2**. The load is transferred to the supports primarily along the shorter direction.
2. **Two-Way Slab**: A slab where $L_y/L_x < 2$. The load is carried in both principal directions to the four edges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For farm structures, the dominant slab type is the **simply supported one-way slab** used in flooring of single-storey farm buildings. The design follows the **Limit State Method (LSM)** as mandated by IS 456:2000.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wooden Plank Bridge"

Imagine a thin wooden plank laid across a small stream. If you stand on the plank, it bends **only along its shorter direction** (the span between the two banks), and the load is carried by the wood fibres running across that span. The plank does not bend along its length. This is essentially how a **one-way slab** behaves.

Now, suppose the plank is replaced by a **square table top** supported on all four edges. When you place a load at the centre, the table top curves and bends in **both directions** — the load is shared by all four edges. This is the **two-way slab** behaviour. The classification of a slab depends purely on its **plan aspect ratio** ($L_y/L_x$).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Specifications

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Material & Geometric Constants (IS 456:2000 & SP-34):**
> - **Unit weight of Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)**: **25 kN/m³**
> - **Unit weight of Plain Cement Concrete (PCC) finish**: **24 kN/m³**
> - **Clear cover for slabs** (moderate exposure): **20 mm**
> - **Grade of concrete** (farm structures, economical choice): **M20** ($f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm²)
> - **Grade of steel**: **Fe415** ($f_y = 415$ N/mm²)
> - **Partial safety factor for concrete ($\gamma_m$)**: **1.5**
> - **Partial safety factor for steel ($\gamma_m$)**: **1.15**
> - **Minimum percentage of steel in slabs**: **0.12%** of gross cross-sectional area (for Fe415 HYSD bars)
> - **Maximum spacing of main bars in slabs**: lesser of **3d** or **300 mm**
> - **Maximum spacing of distribution bars**: **5d** or **450 mm**

### 1.4 Significance in Farm Structure Design

In agricultural engineering practice, slabs serve as the foundational floor system for:
- **Dairy barn floors** (live load ≈ 4–5 kN/m², abrasion-resistant finish)
- **Poultry house floors** (live load ≈ 2–3 kN/m², damp-proof course essential)
- **Threshing and drying floors** (heavy point loads from machinery ≈ 10–15 kN/m²)
- **Storage godowns** for grain, fertilizer, and equipment
- **Silo and bunker platform slabs**

> [!TIP]
> **Economic Insight for Farm Structures:** A judiciously designed slab must balance **safety** (no cracking, no excessive deflection), **serviceability** (crack widths < 0.3 mm), and **economy** (since farm structures are typically low-budget, cost-effective solutions are preferred over premium designs).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Limit State Design Philosophy

The Limit State Method (LSM) ensures that the structure remains fit for use during its intended lifespan. Two limit states govern slab design:

- **Limit State of Collapse (Strength)**: Ensures the section can resist the factored design moment $M_u$.
- **Limit State of Serviceability (Deflection & Cracking)**: Ensures deflections and crack widths remain within permissible limits.

For slabs, the design is governed by the equation:

$$M_u \leq M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$$

This value of $0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ corresponds to the **balanced section** for Fe415 steel, ensuring a ductile failure mode (under-reinforced design is preferred).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Logic

The design procedure for a simply supported one-way slab proceeds through the following logical stages:

1. **Determine the geometric plan dimensions** — clear span, bearing width, slab thickness assumption.
2. **Compute effective span** $L_{eff}$ as the **lesser** of:
   - Clear span + effective depth ($l_{cl} + d$)
   - Centre-to-centre distance between supports
3. **Estimate trial effective depth** using the **span-to-depth ratio** (deflection control).
4. **Calculate loads** — Dead Load (DL) and Live Load (LL), with appropriate partial safety factors.
5. **Compute design moment** $M_u$ and maximum shear $V_u$.
6. **Check for depth adequacy** ($M_u \leq M_{u,\lim}$).
7. **Calculate main reinforcement** $A_{st}$ in the shorter direction.
8. **Check minimum steel** provision and **maximum spacing** criteria.
9. **Design distribution steel** perpendicular to main steel.
10. **Verify shear stress** and **deflection limits**.
11. **Provide reinforcement detailing** with curtailment and crank bars as needed.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula you must memorize for KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examinations on slab design.

| **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units / Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective Span (Simply Supported) | $L_{eff} = \min(l_{cl} + d,\ l_{c/c})$ | mm; $l_{cl}$ = clear span, $d$ = effective depth |
| 2 | Effective Span (Continuous) | $L_{eff} = \min(l_{cl} + d,\ l_{c/c})$ for end span; $l_{c/c}$ for interior span | mm |
| 3 | Slab Classification | One-way if $L_y/L_x \geq 2$; Two-way if $L_y/L_x < 2$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Basic Span/Depth Ratio (Simply Supported) | 20 (for one-way slab) | From IS 456 Fig. 4 / SP-16 |
| 5 | Modification Factor (Fe415) | 0.8 | Multiplies basic value |
| 6 | Maximum Permissible Span/$d$ | $20 \times 0.8 = 16$ (simply supported, Fe415) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Factored Load (DL + LL) | $w_u = 1.5 \times (D.L. + L.L.)$ | kN/m or kN/m² |
| 8 | Maximum Bending Moment (Simply Supported) | $M_u = \dfrac{w_u L_{eff}^2}{8}$ | kN·m |
| 9 | Maximum Shear Force (Simply Supported) | $V_u = \dfrac{w_u L_{eff}}{2}$ | kN |
| 10 | Limiting Moment of Resistance | $M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ | N·mm (for Fe415) |
| 11 | Main Steel Area (Required) | $A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right] b d$ | mm² |
| 12 | Approx. Main Steel | $A_{st} \approx \dfrac{M_u}{0.87 f_y \, (0.9 d)}$ | mm² (quick estimate) |
| 13 | Minimum Steel in Slabs | $A_{st,\min} = \dfrac{0.12}{100} \times b \times D$ | mm² (Fe415 HYSD) |
| 14 | Spacing of Bars | $s = \dfrac{a_{\phi} \times 1000}{A_{st,req}}$ | mm; $a_{\phi}$ = area of one bar |
| 15 | Maximum Spacing (Main Bars) | $\min(3d,\ 300\text{ mm})$ | mm |
| 16 | Maximum Spacing (Dist. Bars) | $\min(5d,\ 450\text{ mm})$ | mm |
| 17 | Permissible Shear Stress | $\tau_c$ from IS 456 Table 19 (depends on $p_t$ and $f_{ck}$) | N/mm² |
| 18 | Nominal Shear Stress | $\tau_v = \dfrac{V_u}{b \, d}$ | N/mm² |
| 19 | Two-Way Slab Moment Coeff. (Simply Sup.) | $M_x = \alpha_x \, w \, L_x^2$; $M_y = \alpha_y \, w \, L_x^2$ | From IS 456 Table 12 |
| 20 | Torsion Steel at Corners (Two-Way) | $0.75 \times A_{st}$ in each layer extending $\dfrac{L_x}{5}$ | mm² |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pipe Symbol Trap:** In KTU answer scripts, never write absolute value as $|x|$. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $|$x$|$ with spaces, or simply describe in words. The pipe symbol `|` interferes with markdown tables in your presentation; in your answer sheet, use the standard mathematical notation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The slab design equations derived from IS 456 govern the construction of virtually every horizontal floor surface in reinforced concrete farm infrastructure. The **span/depth ratio** ensures human comfort (no perceptible sag), while the **minimum steel clause** prevents sudden brittle failure from shrinkage and thermal stresses. In farm structures specifically, designers often opt for **thicker slabs** (≥ 150 mm) to resist impact loads from cattle hooves, tractor wheels, and dropped equipment — a judgment-based deviation from purely code-derived depths.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Worked Example — Design of a One-Way Slab for a Dairy Barn Floor

**Problem Statement (KTU Exam Style):**
Design a simply supported RCC slab for the flooring of a dairy barn with the following data:
- Clear span ($l_{cl}$) = 3.0 m
- Bearing of slab on supporting masonry walls = 230 mm
- Live Load (LL) = 5 kN/m² (cattle shed as per IS 875 Part 2)
- Floor finish (50 mm thick PCC) = 1.20 kN/m²
- Materials: M20 grade concrete ($f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm²), Fe415 grade HYSD bars ($f_y = 415$ N/mm²)
- Exposure condition: Moderate
- Design the slab per **IS 456:2000 (Limit State Method)**.

---

#### **STEP 1: Determination of Effective Span**

The effective span for a simply supported slab is the **minimum** of:
- (a) Clear span + effective depth = $l_{cl} + d$
- (b) Centre-to-centre distance between supports = $l_{cl} + \text{bearing} = 3000 + 230 = 3230$ mm

We initially assume an effective depth of $d \approx 200$ mm. Thus, the effective span is:

$$L_{eff} = \min(3000 + 200,\ 3230) = \min(3200,\ 3230) = 3200 \text{ mm} = 3.2 \text{ m}$$

We adopt $L_{eff} = 3.2$ m for the design.

---

#### **STEP 2: Estimation of Trial Effective Depth (Deflection Criterion)**

For a simply supported one-way slab, the **basic value** of span/effective depth ratio from IS 456 (Fig. 4) and SP-16 is **20**. The modification factor for Fe415 steel is **0.8**.

Therefore, the **allowable** span/depth ratio is:

$$\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{allow} = 20 \times 0.8 = 16$$

The required effective depth is:

$$d_{req} = \frac{L_{eff}}{16} = \frac{3200}{16} = 200 \text{ mm}$$

We provide $d = 200$ mm.

The overall depth is calculated considering effective cover. For a 10 mm diameter bar and clear cover of 20 mm:

$$D = d + \text{clear cover} + \frac{\phi}{2} = 200 + 20 + \frac{10}{2} = 225 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt overall depth $D = 225$ mm (rounded for practical convenience).

> [!NOTE]
> The **effective cover** = clear cover + bar diameter/2 = 20 + 5 = 25 mm. Thus, $D - d = 25$ mm, confirming our assumed $d = 200$ mm.

---

#### **STEP 3: Load Calculations (Unfactored and Factored)**

**Dead Load (DL) Components:**

(a) Self-weight of slab:
$$w_{sw} = D \times \gamma_c = 0.225 \times 25 = 5.625 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

(b) Weight of floor finish (50 mm PCC):
$$w_{ff} = 0.05 \times 24 = 1.20 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Total Dead Load:**
$$DL = 5.625 + 1.20 = 6.825 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Live Load (LL):** $5.0$ kN/m² (given)

**Total Working Load:**
$$w = DL + LL = 6.825 + 5.0 = 11.825 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Factored Design Load (using partial safety factor 1.5):**
$$w_u = 1.5 \times 11.825 = 17.74 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

For a 1 m wide design strip, the **factored load per meter run** is:
$$w_u = 17.74 \text{ kN/m}$$

---

#### **STEP 4: Design Bending Moment and Shear Force**

For a simply supported slab, the **maximum positive bending moment** at mid-span is:

$$M_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}^2}{8} = \frac{17.74 \times 3.2^2}{8} = \frac{17.74 \times 10.24}{8} = 22.71 \text{ kN·m}$$

The **maximum shear force** at supports is:

$$V_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}}{2} = \frac{17.74 \times 3.2}{2} = 28.38 \text{ kN}$$

---

#### **STEP 5: Check for Depth Adequacy (Under-Reinforced Section)**

The limiting moment of resistance for Fe415 steel with M20 concrete is:

$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$$

Substituting $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm², $b = 1000$ mm, $d = 200$ mm:

$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times (200)^2 = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 40000$$

$$M_{u,\lim} = 110.4 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 110.4 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Comparison:**
$$M_u = 22.71 \text{ kN·m} \ll M_{u,\lim} = 110.4 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_u < M_{u,\lim}$, the section is **under-reinforced** and depth is adequate. ✓

---

#### **STEP 6: Calculation of Main Reinforcement ($A_{st}$)**

Using the standard IS 456 formula for singly reinforced sections:

$$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \, d \left(1 - \frac{A_{st} \, f_y}{b \, d \, f_{ck}}\right)$$

Substituting the known values ($M_u = 22.71 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $f_y = 415$, $b = 1000$, $d = 200$, $f_{ck} = 20$):

$$22.71 \times 10^6 = 0.87 \times 415 \times A_{st} \times 200 \left(1 - \frac{415 \, A_{st}}{1000 \times 200 \times 20}\right)$$

$$22.71 \times 10^6 = 72,210 \, A_{st} \left(1 - 1.0375 \times 10^{-4} \, A_{st}\right)$$

Since $A_{st}$ is small, we first approximate (neglecting the second-order term):

$$A_{st} \approx \frac{22.71 \times 10^6}{72,210} = 314.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

For more accuracy, substituting back:

$$22.71 \times 10^6 = 72,210 \times 314.5 \times (1 - 1.0375 \times 10^{-4} \times 314.5)$$

$$22.71 \times 10^6 = 22,710,045 \times (1 - 0.0326) = 22,710,045 \times 0.9674 = 21.97 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

The approximation is close (within 3.3%). We adopt $A_{st} = 315$ mm².

---

#### **STEP 7: Check for Minimum Reinforcement**

The minimum steel requirement for slabs (Fe415 HYSD bars) is:

$$A_{st,\min} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times b \times D = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 1000 \times 225 = 270 \text{ mm}^2$$

Since $A_{st,req} = 315 \text{ mm}^2 > A_{st,\min} = 270 \text{ mm}^2$, the **required steel governs**.

> [!TIP]
> In many farm structure slabs with light to moderate loading, the minimum steel clause often controls the design. Always check both before finalizing!

---

#### **STEP 8: Selection of Bar Diameter and Spacing**

Using **10 mm diameter** bars, the area of one bar is:

$$a_{\phi} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 10^2 = 78.54 \text{ mm}^2$$

The required spacing is:

$$s = \frac{a_{\phi} \times 1000}{A_{st,req}} = \frac{78.54 \times 1000}{315} = 249.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Check maximum spacing:**
- $3d = 3 \times 200 = 600$ mm
- Maximum allowed = $\min(3d,\ 300) = 300$ mm

Since 249.3 mm < 300 mm, we can provide **10 mm diameter bars at 240 mm centre-to-centre** spacing.

**Steel actually provided:**
$$A_{st,prov} = \frac{78.54 \times 1000}{240} = 327.25 \text{ mm}^2 > 315 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Provide: 10 mm $\phi$ bars @ 240 mm c/c** as main reinforcement (running along the shorter span).

---

#### **STEP 9: Distribution Reinforcement**

The distribution steel is provided perpendicular to the main steel. Its area is:

$$A_{st,dist} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 1000 \times 225 = 270 \text{ mm}^2$$

Using **8 mm diameter** bars ($a_{\phi} = 50.27$ mm²):

$$s_{dist} = \frac{50.27 \times 1000}{270} = 186.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Check maximum spacing:** $\min(5d,\ 450) = \min(1000,\ 450) = 450$ mm. The calculated 186 mm is well within limits.

**Provide: 8 mm $\phi$ bars @ 180 mm c/c** as distribution reinforcement.

---

#### **STEP 10: Check for Shear**

The nominal shear stress is:

$$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \, d} = \frac{28.38 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 200} = 0.142 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

The percentage of tension steel provided:

$$p_t = \frac{A_{st,prov}}{b \, d} \times 100 = \frac{327.25}{1000 \times 200} \times 100 = 0.164\%$$

From **IS 456 Table 19**, for M20 concrete and $p_t = 0.164\%$, the permissible shear stress is:

$$\tau_c \approx 0.40 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

The maximum shear stress for M20 is $\tau_{c,max} = 2.8$ N/mm².

**Comparison:**
$$\tau_v = 0.142 \text{ N/mm}^2 < \tau_c = 0.40 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Shear reinforcement is NOT required.** The concrete alone is sufficient.

---

#### **STEP 11: Check for Deflection**

The actual span-to-effective-depth ratio provided is:

$$\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{actual} = \frac{3200}{200} = 16.0$$

This exactly equals the **allowable** value of 16. The deflection control is **satisfied** marginally. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, engineers often provide a 10–15% safety margin by increasing $d$ slightly (e.g., 220 mm) to ensure long-term deflections from creep and shrinkage are acceptable.

---

#### **STEP 12: Final Design Summary**

| **Parameter** | **Final Value** |
|---|---|
| Overall depth, $D$ | **225 mm** |
| Effective depth, $d$ | **200 mm** |
| Effective span, $L_{eff}$ | **3.2 m** |
| Main Reinforcement | **10 mm $\phi$ @ 240 mm c/c** (bottom, along shorter span) |
| Distribution Steel | **8 mm $\phi$ @ 180 mm c/c** (bottom, along longer span) |
| Clear Cover | **20 mm** (moderate exposure) |
| Maximum Moment | **22.71 kN·m** |
| Maximum Shear | **28.38 kN** |
| Concrete Grade | **M20** |
| Steel Grade | **Fe415** HYSD bars |

> [!TIP]
> **Reinforcement Detailing Rules (as per SP-34):**
> - Main bars should extend at least $L/7$ (or 50 mm) into the supports for simply supported slabs.
> - Alternate bars can be **cranked up** at a distance of $L/4$ from the support face to resist negative moments (if any) and to provide shear resistance.
> - At corners, provide **mesh reinforcement** or cross-bars to prevent shrinkage cracking.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Limiting Moment Formula (For Theoretical Clarity)

The **limiting moment of resistance** for a balanced section with Fe415 steel is derived from the equivalent rectangular stress block (IS 456 Cl. 38.1):

For Fe415, the design yield strain is $\varepsilon_y = f_y / E_s + 0.002$ (allowing for strain hardening). The depth of the neutral axis for a balanced section is:

$$x_{u,\max} = 0.48 \, d \quad \text{(for Fe415)}$$

The depth of the equivalent rectangular stress block is:

$$x = 0.42 \, x_u = 0.42 \times 0.48 \, d = 0.202 \, d$$

For a balanced section, the lever arm is:

$$z = d - 0.42 \, x_u = d - 0.42 \times 0.48 \, d = d (1 - 0.202) = 0.798 \, d$$

The limiting moment is:

$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.36 \, \frac{x_{u,\max}}{d} \left(1 - \frac{0.42 \, x_{u,\max}}{d}\right) f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$$

Substituting $x_{u,\max}/d = 0.48$:

$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.36 \times 0.48 \times (1 - 0.42 \times 0.48) \times f_{ck} \times b \times d^2$$

$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.1728 \times 0.7984 \times f_{ck} \times b \times d^2 = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$$

This is the universally used constant **0.138** for Fe415 steel. ✓

---

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Slab Design Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Geometry and Loading] --> B[Compute Effective Span L effective]
    B --> C[Estimate Trial d from L/d Ratio]
    C --> D[Compute Overall Depth D = d plus Cover]
    D --> E[Calculate Dead Load and Live Load]
    E --> F[Apply Factor 1.5 to get w sub u]
    F --> G[Compute M sub u equals w sub u L squared over 8]
    G --> H{Is M sub u less than 0.138 fck b d squared?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Section is Under Reinforced OK]
    H -- No --> J[Increase Depth and Recalculate]
    I --> K[Calculate A sub st Required from IS 456 Equation]
    K --> L[Check A sub st greater than A sub st min]
    L --> M[Choose Bar Diameter and Compute Spacing]
    M --> N{Is Spacing less than 3d and 300 mm?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Select Bar Spacing Final]
    N -- No --> P[Reduce Bar Diameter and Recompute]
    O --> Q[Design Distribution Steel at 0.12 percent]
    Q --> R[Check Nominal Shear Stress tau sub v]
    R --> S{tau sub v less than tau sub c from Table 19?}
    S -- Yes --> T[No Shear Reinforcement Needed]
    S -- No --> U[Provide Shear Links or Increase d]
    T --> V[Check Span by d ratio for Deflection]
    V --> W{L by d Actual less than Allowable?}
    W -- Yes --> X[DEFLECTION SAFE]
    W -- No --> Y[Increase d to Satisfy Deflection]
    X --> Z[FINAL DETAILING with Crank Bars and Curtailment]
    Y --> Z
    U --> Z
    P --> O
    J --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flowchart:** The design process is **iterative** in nature. If any check fails, the designer returns to the relevant step and modifies the input (usually increasing the depth $d$ or the steel area $A_{st}$) until all checks pass.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Slab in a Farm Building

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Superimposed Live Load Cattle Equipment Stored Material] --> B[Slab Panel 225 mm thick]
    B --> C[Main Reinforcement 10 mm at 240 mm c/c Short Span]
    B --> D[Distribution Steel 8 mm at 180 mm c/c Long Span]
    B --> E[Clear Cover 20 mm]
    B --> F[Supporting Brick Wall 230 mm thick]
    F --> G[Plinth Beam Transferring Load]
    G --> H[Foundation Footing of Farm Structure]
    B --> I[ PCC Floor Finish 50 mm thick Top]
    I --> J[Hygienic Working Surface for Dairy Barn]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For Farm Structures:** Notice the inclusion of a **PCC floor finish on top of the RCC slab**. This is a standard construction practice in dairy barns — it provides a smooth, abrasion-resistant, easy-to-clean surface, while the RCC slab below carries the structural load.

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between a one-way and a two-way slab. Under what condition is a slab designed as a two-way slab?**

**Model Answer:**

A **one-way slab** is a structural element in which the load is transferred to the supports primarily in **one direction** (the shorter span direction), because the ratio of the longer span to the shorter span ($L_y / L_x$) is **greater than or equal to 2**. Main reinforcement is provided only along the shorter direction, with distribution steel perpendicular to it.

A **two-way slab** carries the load in **both principal directions** when the ratio $L_y / L_x < 2$. The load is shared by all four supporting edges, and reinforcement must be designed for both directions. Moment coefficients from **IS 456 Table 12** are used to determine the design moments.

**Condition for two-way slab design:**
$$\frac{L_y}{L_x} < 2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Distribution steel in one-way slabs is not just for shrinkage** — it also resists temperature stresses and helps distribute concentrated loads across the main reinforcement. In two-way slabs, the "distribution steel" is genuinely a structural component.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**State the minimum and maximum percentage of reinforcement to be provided in a RCC slab as per IS 456:2000. What is the maximum spacing of main bars in a slab?**

**Model Answer:**

As per **IS 456:2000 (Clause 26.5.2.1)**:

- **Minimum reinforcement in slabs**: **0.12% of the gross cross-sectional area** of the slab, when using **HYSD bars (Fe415 or higher)**. This is to prevent sudden brittle failure from shrinkage, thermal stresses, and handling loads.
- **Maximum reinforcement in slabs**: **4% of the gross cross-sectional area**, to ensure proper placement of bars and adequate concrete cover.

**Maximum spacing of main bars (IS 456 Cl. 26.3.3):**
- The spacing of main bars shall **not exceed the lesser of**:
  - **$3d$** (three times the effective depth)
  - **300 mm**

For distribution bars, the maximum spacing is the lesser of $5d$ or 450 mm.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a simply supported one-way RCC slab for the flooring of a poultry house with the following data:
- Clear span = 2.5 m
- Bearing on walls = 200 mm
- Live Load = 3 kN/m²
- Floor finish = 1.0 kN/m²
- Materials: M20 concrete and Fe415 steel
- Exposure: Moderate

**(b) [7 Marks]** Draw a neat sketch showing the reinforcement details (plan and cross-section) of the slab designed in part (a). Indicate the spacing, diameter, and cover clearly.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a): Step-by-Step Design**

**Step 1: Effective Span**

Assume $d \approx 180$ mm. Effective span = lesser of:
- (a) $2500 + 180 = 2680$ mm
- (b) $2500 + 200 = 2700$ mm

$$L_{eff} = \min(2680, 2700) = 2680 \text{ mm} = 2.68 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Trial Effective Depth**

Allowable $L/d$ for simply supported one-way slab with Fe415:

$$\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{allow} = 20 \times 0.8 = 16$$

$$d_{req} = \frac{2680}{16} = 167.5 \text{ mm}$$

Provide $d = 170$ mm.

Overall depth with 20 mm cover and 8 mm bar:

$$D = 170 + 20 + 4 = 194 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{adopt } D = 200 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Load Calculation**

- Self-weight = $0.20 \times 25 = 5.0$ kN/m²
- Floor finish = 1.0 kN/m²
- Live Load = 3.0 kN/m²
- Total working load = 9.0 kN/m²
- Factored load $w_u = 1.5 \times 9.0 = 13.5$ kN/m²
- For 1 m strip: $w_u = 13.5$ kN/m

**Step 4: Design Moment**

$$M_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}^2}{8} = \frac{13.5 \times 2.68^2}{8} = 12.12 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5: Check Depth**

$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 170^2 = 79.75 \text{ kN·m} > 12.12 \text{ kN·m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Main Steel**

$$A_{st} = \frac{M_u}{0.87 f_y d} = \frac{12.12 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 415 \times 170} = 201.7 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Check minimum:** $A_{st,\min} = 0.12/100 \times 1000 \times 200 = 240$ mm².

**$A_{st,\min}$ governs.** Provide 240 mm².

Using **8 mm $\phi$ bars** ($a_{\phi} = 50.27$ mm²):

$$s = \frac{50.27 \times 1000}{240} = 209.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Provide 8 mm $\phi$ @ 200 mm c/c** (Area provided = 251 mm² > 240 mm² ✓).

Check max spacing: $\min(3 \times 170, 300) = 300$ mm. 200 mm is OK. ✓

**Step 7: Distribution Steel**

$A_{st,dist} = 0.12/100 \times 1000 \times 200 = 240$ mm².

Using **8 mm $\phi$ bars**:

$$s_{dist} = \frac{50.27 \times 1000}{240} = 209.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Provide 8 mm $\phi$ @ 200 mm c/c** as distribution steel.

**Step 8: Check Shear**

$$V_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}}{2} = \frac{13.5 \times 2.68}{2} = 18.09 \text{ kN}$$

$$\tau_v = \frac{18.09 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 170} = 0.106 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$p_t = (251/1000/170) \times 100 = 0.148\%$

From IS 456 Table 19: $\tau_c \approx 0.38$ N/mm² > 0.106 N/mm² ✓ (No shear reinforcement needed)

**Step 9: Check Deflection**

$L/d$ actual = $2680/170 = 15.76 < 16$ ✓

**Part (b): Detailing Sketch**

```
PLAN VIEW (Reinforcement Layout)
============================================================
              2000 mm (shorter span Lx = 2.5 m clear)
   <------------------------------------------------------------>
   |  *-----*-----*-----*-----*-----*-----*-----*-----*-----*  |
   |  |  Distribution Bars (8 mm @ 200 c/c) running vertically |
   |  |                                                           |
   |  *  Main Bars (8 mm @ 200 c/c) running horizontally          |
   |  |                                                           |
   |  *-----*-----*-----*-----*-----*-----*-----*-----*-----*  |
   |                                                           |
   |                                                           |
   |                                                           |
   ============================================================>
                     2000 mm (Lx direction)

CROSS-SECTION (Thickness Direction)
============================================================
   |  50 mm PCC Floor Finish                            |  |
   |====================================================|  |
   |                                                    |  |
   |  RCC SLAB 200 mm overall depth                     |  |
   |                                                    |  |
   |====================================================|  |
   | 20 mm cover                                        |  |
   | o----o----o----o----o----o----o----o----o----o----o |  |
   |   8 mm @ 200 c/c (Main Steel + Dist Steel in bottom layer) |
   |====================================================|  |
        200 mm thick wall
```

**Key Detailing Points:**
- 20 mm clear cover at bottom
- Bars at bottom: 8 mm $\phi$ @ 200 c/c in both directions
- Alternate bars cranked up at $L/4$ from supports
- L-shaped hooks at corners

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A simply supported two-way slab has clear dimensions of 3.5 m × 4.5 m with corners not held down. Using M20 concrete and Fe415 steel, determine the design moments for the slab panel. Assume a slab thickness of 160 mm and floor finish of 1.0 kN/m², with a live load of 4 kN/m².

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design the mid-strip reinforcement in the shorter span direction for the two-way slab in part (a). Use IS 456 moment coefficients and provide bar spacing.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a): Design Moments for Two-Way Slab**

**Step 1: Geometry and Loading**

- $L_x = 3.5$ m (shorter span)
- $L_y = 4.5$ m (longer span)
- $L_y / L_x = 4.5 / 3.5 = 1.286 < 2$ → **Two-way slab** ✓

**Loads (per m²):**
- Self-weight = $0.16 \times 25 = 4.0$ kN/m²
- Floor finish = 1.0 kN/m²
- Live load = 4.0 kN/m²
- Total = 9.0 kN/m²
- Factored load: $w_u = 1.5 \times 9.0 = 13.5$ kN/m²

**Step 2: Moment Coefficients from IS 456 Table 12**

For $L_y/L_x = 1.286$ (interpolating between 1.25 and 1.5):

| $L_y/L_x$ | $\alpha_x$ | $\alpha_y$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1.25 | 0.075 | 0.056 |
| 1.5 | 0.067 | 0.047 |
| **1.286 (interp.)** | **≈ 0.0734** | **≈ 0.0542** |

**Step 3: Design Moments**

$$M_x = \alpha_x \times w_u \times L_x^2 = 0.0734 \times 13.5 \times 3.5^2 = 12.13 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

$$M_y = \alpha_y \times w_u \times L_x^2 = 0.0542 \times 13.5 \times 3.5^2 = 8.96 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**Part (b): Design of Mid-Strip Reinforcement (Shorter Direction)**

**Step 1: Effective Depth**

Assume 10 mm bars with 20 mm cover. Effective depth $d = 160 - 20 - 5 = 135$ mm.

**Step 2: Required Steel in Shorter Direction**

$$A_{st,x} = \frac{M_x}{0.87 f_y d} = \frac{12.13 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 415 \times 135} = 254.4 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ per meter}$$

**Check minimum:** $A_{st,\min} = 0.12/100 \times 1000 \times 160 = 192$ mm².

So $A_{st,x} = 254.4$ mm² governs.

**Step 3: Bar Selection**

Using **10 mm $\phi$ bars** ($a_{\phi} = 78.54$ mm²):

$$s = \frac{78.54 \times 1000}{254.4} = 308.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Check max spacing:** $\min(3 \times 135, 300) = 300$ mm. 308.7 mm exceeds 300 mm.

**Reduce bar size:** Use **8 mm $\phi$ bars** ($a_{\phi} = 50.27$ mm²):

$$s = \frac{50.27 \times 1000}{254.4} = 197.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Provide 8 mm $\phi$ @ 190 mm c/c** in the shorter direction (mid-strip bottom layer).

**Final Design Summary for Two-Way Slab:**
- **Shorter direction**: 8 mm $\phi$ @ 190 mm c/c (bottom)
- **Longer direction**: 8 mm $\phi$ @ 220 mm c/c (bottom, outer layer)
- **Overall depth**: 160 mm
- **Cover**: 20 mm

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Examinations:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units**: $L$ in mm must give $M_u$ in N·mm, then convert to kN·m. Many students write $M_u$ in N·mm without conversion.
> 2. **Using working load instead of factored load**: Always multiply by 1.5 in LSM. A common 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Ignoring the minimum steel clause**: Even if $A_{st,req}$ is small, you must provide 0.12% of $b \times D$. Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Wrong effective span formula**: For continuous slabs, end span uses $l_{cl} + d$, but interior span uses $l_{c/c}$ only.
> 5. **Confusing "main" and "distribution" steel**: Main steel is along the **shorter span** in a one-way slab.
> 6. **Not checking $3d/300$ mm maximum spacing**: This is a frequent deduction (½ mark).
> 7. **Skipping the deflection check**: Always verify $L/d_{actual} \leq L/d_{allowable}$.
> 8. **Wrong table lookup in IS 456 Table 19** for $\tau_c$: Must use the correct $p_t$ (percentage of steel).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15-Point Slab Design Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> ☐ 1. **Classify the slab** by computing $L_y/L_x$. One-way if ratio $\geq 2$; two-way if $< 2$.
>
> ☐ 2. **Effective span** = lesser of ($l_{cl} + d$) and $l_{c/c}$ for simply supported slabs.
>
> ☐ 3. **Trial $d$** from span/depth ratio: 16 for simply supported, 20 × 0.8 = 16 (Fe415), or 26 × 0.8 = 20.8 (continuous).
>
> ☐ 4. **Loads**: Self-weight (0.025 × $D$ in kN/m² for RCC) + Floor Finish + Live Load.
>
> ☐ 5. **Factored load** = 1.5 × Total working load.
>
> ☐ 6. **$M_u$ for SS slab** = $w_u L^2 / 8$. **$V_u$** = $w_u L / 2$.
>
> ☐ 7. **Check $M_u < 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$** (limiting moment for Fe415).
>
> ☐ 8. **$A_{st}$** from IS 456 Eq. (using $M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st} d$ as quick estimate).
>
> ☐ 9. **Minimum $A_{st}$** = 0.12% of $b \times D$ (always check).
>
> ☐ 10. **Spacing** of bars: $s = (a_\phi \times 1000) / A_{st,req}$.
>
> ☐ 11. **Max spacing (main)**: $\min(3d, 300\text{ mm})$.
>
> ☐ 12. **Distribution steel** = 0.12% × $b \times D$ (in the longer direction for one-way).
>
> ☐ 13. **Shear check**: $\tau_v < \tau_c$ from IS 456 Table 19 (use correct $p_t$).
>
> ☐ 14. **Deflection check**: $L/d_{actual} \leq L/d_{allowable}$ (16 for SS, 26 for continuous with Fe415).
>
> ☐ 15. **Cover**: 20 mm for moderate exposure, 30 mm for severe, 45 mm for very severe.

**Final Practical Tip for Farm Structure Slabs:** Always provide a **50–75 mm PCC overlay** on the structural slab for hygienic, abrasion-resistant working surfaces. This adds ~1.2–1.8 kN/m² to the dead load — account for it in your load calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Slab Spanning in One Direction and Two Directions; Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design of Slab Spanning in One Direction and Two Directions; Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 RCC Slabs — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RCC Slab (Reinforced Cement Concrete Slab):** A planar, two-dimensional reinforced concrete structural element, typically horizontal, having a thickness much smaller than its other two lateral dimensions. It is designed to carry transverse loads (gravity loads like self-weight, live load from stored grain, hay, equipment, occupants) and transfer them to the supporting beams, walls, or columns. Slabs in farm structures commonly serve as **roof slabs for dairy sheds, poultry houses, godowns, and grain storage platforms**.

The **IS 456:2000** (Plain and Reinforced Concrete — Code of Practice) classifies slabs based on the **ratio of longer span to shorter span (Ly/Lx)** and the **support conditions**.

### 1.2 Classification of Slabs

| Slab Type | Governing Condition | Bending Direction |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **One-Way Slab** | Ly/Lx ≥ 2 (or all four edges simply supported with Ly/Lx > 2) | Curvature predominantly in **Lx** direction |
| **Two-Way Slab** | Ly/Lx < 2 (with adequate lateral restraint on all four edges) | Curvature occurs in **both Lx and Ly** directions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Codal Reference:** Per **IS 456:2000, Clause 24.1**, a slab spanning in two directions is termed a **two-way slab** when the ratio of the longer span to the shorter span is less than 2.0 and the slab is supported on all four edges.

### 1.3 RCC Columns — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Column:** A compression member whose effective length exceeds 3 times the least lateral dimension (IS 456, Cl. 25.1.1). It primarily carries **axial compressive loads** with possible moments. In farm structures, columns support roof slabs of large-span storage sheds, elevated platforms, and tower silos.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Slab Analogy — "The Wooden Plank Bridge":**
Imagine walking across a **wooden plank bridge** supported only on two parallel mud banks. The plank bends *only across* the river (short direction). This is a **one-way slab**. Now imagine a **square concrete platform** supported on all four sides by brick pillars. If you stand in the middle, it bends *both ways* (you can feel flexure along the length and width). This is a **two-way slab**.

**Column Analogy — "The Bamboo Post":**
A vertical **bamboo stick** holding up a roof of grass — that's a column. The bamboo can take enormous weight pushing straight down (axial compression) but buckles easily if pushed sideways. We reinforce columns with vertical steel bars (longitudinal) and horizontal rings (lateral ties) just like a **bamboo post wrapped with binding wire at intervals** to prevent it from splitting.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Unit weight of RCC:** **25 kN/m³**
- **Unit weight of plain cement concrete (PCC):** **24 kN/m³**
- **Grade of steel (HYSD bars):** Fe415 or Fe500
- **Concrete grade for farm structures:** M20 (minimum, per IS 456)
- **Clear cover for slab:** **20 mm** (mild exposure)
- **Clear cover for column:** **40 mm** (for main reinforcement)
- **Partial Safety Factor for materials (steel):** **γm = 1.15**
- **Partial Safety Factor for materials (concrete):** **γm = 1.50**
- **Partial Safety Factor for loads (DL):** **γf = 1.5**
- **Partial Safety Factor for loads (LL):** **γf = 1.5**
- **Design bond stress for M20 concrete with HYSD bars:** **1.96 N/mm²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In farm structures (where dust, moisture, and agro-chemicals are common), a **minimum concrete grade of M20** and **mild exposure conditions** are recommended as per IS 456:2000, Table 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Design Philosophy of One-Way Slabs

A one-way slab is designed as a **rectangular beam of unit width (1 m)** spanning in the shorter direction. The bending moment, shear, and deflection are computed assuming the slab acts like a wide shallow beam.

#### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Compute Effective Span (Cl. 22.2, IS 456):** Effective span = clear span + effective depth (d) OR centre-to-centre of supports, whichever is **lesser**.
2. **Compute Loads:** Dead load (DL) = self-weight of slab + floor finish. Live load (LL) = imposed load as per IS 875 (Part 2).
3. **Compute Factored Load:** wu = 1.5 × (DL + LL).
4. **Compute Design Bending Moment:** Mu = wu·L²/Co (where Co depends on support conditions: 8, 10, 12 for simply supported, propped, continuous/fixed).
5. **Compute Effective Depth (d):** From Mu = 0.138·fck·b·d² (Fe415, no compression steel), we get:
   $$d = \sqrt{\frac{Mu}{0.138 \cdot fck \cdot b}}$$
6. **Compute Area of Steel (Ast):** Using the **limit-state design** formula:
   $$Mu = 0.87 \cdot fy \cdot Ast \cdot d \left(1 - \frac{Ast \cdot fy}{b \cdot d \cdot fck}\right)$$
7. **Check for Shear:** τv = Vu/(b·d) ≤ τc·k (per Table 19, IS 456).
8. **Check for Deflection Control (Cl. 23.2.1):** Basic (L/d) ratio modified by percentage of steel provided.
9. **Design Distribution Steel (Cl. 26.5.2.1):** Minimum 0.12% of gross cross-sectional area for Fe415 bars.

### 2.2 Design Philosophy of Two-Way Slabs

Two-way slabs are designed using **Bending Moment Coefficients** from **IS 456, Table 12** (or by **yield line theory** for irregular shapes). The moments are:

$$M_x = \alpha_x \cdot w \cdot L_x^2$$
$$M_y = \alpha_y \cdot w \cdot L_y^2$$

where αx and αy are **coefficients** from Table 12 of IS 456, depending on the **type of panel** (interior, edge, corner), **support conditions** (simply supported, continuous, restrained), and the ratio **Ly/Lx**.

#### Steps:
1. Identify the panel type and support conditions.
2. Compute the load w (factored).
3. Look up αx and αy from Table 12.
4. Compute Mx and My.
5. Compute effective depth and Ast for each direction.
6. Distribute the steel at **bottom** (positive moment) and **top** (negative moment over continuous edges).
7. Check for **punching shear** at corners if column-supported.

### 2.3 Design Philosophy of Axially Loaded Columns (Short Columns)

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 456, Cl. 25.1.2** classifies columns with **slenderness ratio (Leffective / least lateral dimension) ≤ 12** as **short columns**, designed for axial load only. Farm structure columns are typically short and stocky.

#### Governing Design Equation (Cl. 39.3, IS 456):

For members subjected to **axial compression with minimum eccentricity**:

$$P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}$$

where:
- **Pu** = Factored axial load
- **fck** = Characteristic compressive strength of concrete
- **fy** = Characteristic yield strength of steel
- **Ac** = Net area of concrete (Ag – Asc)
- **Asc** = Area of longitudinal steel

#### Minimum Eccentricity (Cl. 25.4, IS 456):

$$e_{x,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \geq 20 \text{ mm}$$

where L is the unsupported length and D is the lateral dimension in the direction considered.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Element | Formula | Codal Reference |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | One-way slab effective span | Leff = clear span + d (or c/c of supports) | IS 456, Cl. 22.2 |
| 2 | Effective depth (singly reinforced) | $d = \sqrt{M_u / (0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b)}$ | Limit state method |
| 3 | Area of tension steel (Fe415) | $A_{st} = (0.5 f_{ck}/f_y)\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - (4.6 M_u)/(f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2)}\right] \cdot b \cdot d$ | LS design |
| 4 | Two-way moment (IS 456) | $M_x = \alpha_x w L_x^2; \quad M_y = \alpha_y w L_y^2$ | IS 456, Table 12 |
| 5 | Shear stress check | $\tau_v = V_u / (b \cdot d) \leq \tau_c \cdot k$ | IS 456, Cl. 40 |
| 6 | Distribution steel | $A_{sd} = 0.12\% \cdot A_g$ (Fe415) | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.1 |
| 7 | Column design (min. eccentricity) | $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ | IS 456, Cl. 39.3 |
| 8 | Minimum eccentricity | $e_{min} = L/500 + D/30 \geq 20$ mm | IS 456, Cl. 25.4 |
| 9 | Min. longitudinal steel in column | 0.8% of Ag | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 10 | Max. longitudinal steel in column | 6% of Ag (or 4% at laps) | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 11 | Diameter of lateral ties | $\phi_{tie} \geq \phi_{main}/4$ (max), not less than 6 mm | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.2 |
| 12 | Pitch of lateral ties | Least of: 300 mm, 16$\phi_{main}$, B (least lateral dim.) | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.2 |
| 13 | Slenderness ratio (short column) | $\lambda = L_{eff} / D \leq 12$ | IS 456, Cl. 25.1.2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these formulas matter in farm structures:** Hay lofts, grain godowns, and dairy parlour roofs impose heavy live loads. Two-way slabs are common in square or near-square milking parlour floors and storage platforms. Stocky columns are typical because farm buildings are single-storey with low heights, keeping slenderness within short-column limits.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **One-way slabs** are economical for **rectangular poultry-house roofs** where one dimension is much longer.
- **Two-way slabs** are used in **square mushroom sheds, milking parlour floors, and silage pit covers** for stiffer behaviour and reduced deflections.
- **Columns** in **elevated grain silos and tower godowns** are designed for both axial load (grain pressure) and bending (wind/seismic forces).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Effective Depth for Singly Reinforced Section (Fe415, Limit State)

Starting from the **standard limit-state moment equation** for a rectangular singly reinforced section:

$$M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st} \cdot d \left(1 - \frac{A_{st} \cdot f_y}{b \cdot d \cdot f_{ck}}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Introduce the parameter $p = \frac{A_{st}}{b \cdot d}$ (steel percentage). The moment becomes:

$$M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot p \cdot b \cdot d^2 \left(1 - \frac{p \cdot f_y}{f_{ck}}\right)$$

**Step 2:** For **Fe415** with partial safety factor already applied (i.e., $0.87 \cdot f_y = 0.87 \times 415 = 360.9$ N/mm²), and limiting the steel ratio so that the section is **under-reinforced** ($x_u/d \leq 0.48$), the maximum moment of resistance for a balanced section simplifies to:

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2$$

This is obtained by substituting $p_{lim} = 0.48 \cdot f_{ck} / (0.87 \cdot f_y)$ for Fe415.

**Step 3:** Equating the design moment Mu with $M_{u,lim}$ and solving for d:

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{M_u}{0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b}}$$

**Step 4:** The required **overall depth D** is:

$$D = d + \text{effective cover}$$

where effective cover = clear cover + half the bar diameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **For farm structures**, clear cover = 20 mm (slab), bar diameter = 10 or 12 mm typical. So effective cover ≈ 25 mm (assuming 10 mm bar).

### 3.2 Derivation of Ast for One-Way Slab (Fe415)

Rearranging the moment equation to make $A_{st}$ the subject:

$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \cdot f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \cdot M_u}{f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2}}\right] \cdot b \cdot d$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- The term $\frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}$ is a **dimensionless moment ratio**.
- If this ratio is **less than 1.0**, the section is singly reinforced and safe.
- If it **exceeds 1.0**, a doubly reinforced section is required (rare for farm slabs).

### 3.3 Design Procedure for a One-Way Slab — Worked Framework

> **Example Data (for understanding):** A poultry shed roof slab has clear span 3.0 m, slab thickness 125 mm, M20 concrete, Fe415 steel, live load 1.5 kN/m², floor finish 1.0 kN/m².

#### Step A: Loads (per metre width)

$$\text{Self-weight of slab} = 0.125 \times 1 \times 25 = 3.125 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$\text{Floor finish} = 1.0 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$\text{Dead Load (DL)} = 4.125 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$\text{Live Load (LL)} = 1.5 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$\text{Total working load, } w = 5.625 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$\text{Factored load, } w_u = 1.5 \times 5.625 = 8.44 \text{ kN/m}$$

#### Step B: Effective Span

Assume d ≈ 100 mm, effective cover 25 mm, so D = 125 mm.

$$L_{eff} = \text{clear span} + d = 3000 + 100 = 3100 \text{ mm} = 3.1 \text{ m}$$

#### Step C: Design Moment (simply supported)

$$M_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}^2}{8} = \frac{8.44 \times 3.1^2}{8} = 10.13 \text{ kN·m}$$

#### Step D: Effective Depth Required

$$d_{req} = \sqrt{\frac{10.13 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 1000}} = \sqrt{3670} = 60.6 \text{ mm}$$

The assumed d = 100 mm is **safe and adequate**.

#### Step E: Ast Calculation

$$\frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2} = \frac{4.6 \times 10.13 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 100^2} = 0.233$$

$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.233}\right] \times 1000 \times 100$$

$$A_{st} = 0.0241 \left[1 - \sqrt{0.767}\right] \times 100000$$

$$A_{st} = 0.0241 \times [1 - 0.876] \times 100000 = 0.0241 \times 0.124 \times 100000$$

$$A_{st} = 299 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provide **8 mm dia. bars @ 160 mm c/c** (Ast provided = 8²/4 × π × 1000/160 = 314 mm²). This is **> 299 mm²**, hence safe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum steel check:** $A_{st,min} = 0.12\% \times 1000 \times 125 = 1500/1000 = 1.5$ mm² × ... 
> $A_{st,min} = 0.12 \times 1000 \times 125 / 100 = 150$ mm². Provided 314 mm² > 150 mm² ✓

#### Step F: Distribution Steel (along Ly)

$$A_{sd} = 0.12\% \times 1000 \times 125 = 150 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provide **8 mm @ 300 mm c/c** (Ast = 167 mm²). OK.

#### Step G: Shear Check

$$V_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}}{2} = \frac{8.44 \times 3.1}{2} = 13.08 \text{ kN}$$

$$\tau_v = \frac{13.08 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 100} = 0.131 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

For M20, p = 0.314% (0.31), from Table 19 of IS 456, $\tau_c = 0.39$ N/mm². Since $\tau_v < \tau_c$, **shear is safe**.

#### Step H: Deflection Check

Basic (L/d) for simply supported = 20 (Fe415).

Permissible (L/d) = 20 × (modification factor for p = 0.31%) = 20 × 1.1 ≈ 22 (approx, from Fig. 4 of IS 456).

Actual (L/d) = 3100/100 = 31. 

> [!WARNING]
> **(L/d) actual = 31 > permissible ≈ 22.** Slab depth must be increased OR the slab must be continuous (reducing permissible). For a simply supported slab, increase d to at least 3100/22 = 141 mm. So provide **150 mm thick slab** in practice.

### 3.4 Design Procedure for a Two-Way Slab — Worked Framework (IS 456 Method)

> **Example Data:** A milking parlour floor slab with $L_x = 4$ m, $L_y = 5$ m, simply supported on all four edges, live load 3 kN/m², slab thickness 150 mm, M20, Fe415.

**Ratio** $L_y / L_x = 5/4 = 1.25$ (< 2), so **two-way slab**.

**Factored load:**
$$w = 1.5 \times [0.15 \times 25 + 3.0] = 1.5 \times 6.75 = 10.125 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Bending moment coefficients** from IS 456, Table 12, for simply supported slab on all four sides, $L_y/L_x = 1.25$:

$$\alpha_x = 0.067, \quad \alpha_y = 0.047$$

**Moments:**
$$M_x = 0.067 \times 10.125 \times 4^2 = 10.85 \text{ kN·m/m}$$
$$M_y = 0.047 \times 10.125 \times 5^2 = 11.89 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**Effective depth required for the larger moment (My):**

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{11.89 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 1000}} = 65.6 \text{ mm}$$

Provide **d = 100 mm, D = 125 mm** (with 25 mm cover, 10 mm bar).

**Ast calculation (for Mx, with d=100):**

$$\frac{4.6 \times 10.85 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 100^2} = 0.249$$

$$A_{st,x} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.249}] \times 1000 \times 100 = 332 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provide **10 mm @ 230 mm c/c** (Ast = 341 mm²). OK.

**For My, since bars lie in two layers, effective d' = 100 - 10 = 90 mm:**

$$A_{st,y} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 11.89 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 90^2}}\right] \times 1000 \times 90$$

$$A_{st,y} = 0.0241 \times [1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.337}] \times 90000$$

$$A_{st,y} = 0.0241 \times [1 - 0.815] \times 90000 = 0.0241 \times 0.185 \times 90000$$

$$A_{st,y} = 401 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provide **10 mm @ 190 mm c/c** (Ast = 413 mm²). OK.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Two-Way Slab Detail:** The reinforcement in the **shorter direction (Lx)** is placed **outermost** (closer to the surface) to maximize its effective depth. Reinforcement in the longer direction is placed **inside**, with reduced effective depth.

### 3.5 Design Procedure for a Short Axially Loaded Column

> **Example Data:** A 3 m tall, 300 × 300 mm column supports an axial load Pu = 1000 kN. Use M20, Fe415.

#### Step 1: Slenderness Check

Assume both ends hinged: $L_{eff} = L = 3000$ mm.

$$\lambda = L_{eff} / D = 3000 / 300 = 10 < 12$$

Hence **short column**, design by Cl. 39.3.

#### Step 2: Minimum Eccentricity

$$e_{min} = \frac{3000}{500} + \frac{300}{30} = 6 + 10 = 16 \text{ mm} < 20 \text{ mm}$$

So adopt $e_{min} = 20$ mm.

#### Step 3: Compute Required Asc

Assume 1% steel: $A_g = 300 \times 300 = 90000$ mm², $A_{sc} = 900$ mm², $A_c = 89100$ mm².

$$P_u = 0.4 \times 20 \times 89100 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 900$$
$$P_u = 712800 + 250245 = 963045 \text{ N} = 963 \text{ kN}$$

This is less than required 1000 kN. **Increase Asc**.

Try 1.2% steel: $A_{sc} = 1080$ mm², $A_c = 88920$ mm².

$$P_u = 0.4 \times 20 \times 88920 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1080$$
$$P_u = 711360 + 300174 = 1011534 \text{ N} = 1011.5 \text{ kN} > 1000 \text{ kN} \checkmark$$

Provide **1.2% steel = 6 bars of 16 mm dia** (each 201 mm² × 6 = 1206 mm², slightly more, conservative).

#### Step 4: Lateral Ties

- Use 8 mm dia. ties (≥ $\phi_{main}/4 = 16/4 = 4$ mm; 6 mm minimum, 8 mm used).
- Pitch = least of: 300 mm, 16 × 16 = 256 mm, 300 mm → **250 mm c/c**.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: One-Way vs Two-Way Slab vs Column

| Parameter | One-Way Slab | Two-Way Slab | Column |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|--------|
| **Primary load path** | Single direction (short) | Both directions | Vertical (axial) |
| **L/d ratio range** | 20–35 (simply supported) | 26–35 | N/A (slenderness based) |
| **Moment equation** | $wL^2/8$ | $\alpha w L^2$ (IS 456) | $0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ |
| **Reinforcement** | Main + distribution | Orthogonal grid | Longitudinal + lateral ties |
| **Typical farm use** | Poultry shed roof | Milking parlour floor | Silo support |
| **Critical check** | Deflection (L/d) | Punching shear | Slenderness, buckling |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Design Flow for One-Way Slab

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: One-Way Slab Design] --> B0[Step 1: Define Geometry Lx, Ly, D]
    B0 --> C0{Ly or Lx more than 2x shorter span?}
    C0 -- YES --> D0[Confirm ONE-WAY behaviour]
    C0 -- NO --> E0[Redirect to Two-Way Slab]
    D0 --> F0[Step 2: Compute DL and LL]
    F0 --> G0[Step 3: Factored Load wu = 1.5 DL+LL]
    G0 --> H0[Step 4: Effective Span Leff]
    H0 --> I0[Step 5: Mu = wu Leff squared by coefficient]
    I0 --> J0[Step 6: d required = sqrt Mu by 0.138 fck b]
    J0 --> K0[Step 7: Ast using Fe415 equation]
    K0 --> L0[Step 8: Check Ast min equals 0.12 percent Ag]
    L0 --> M0[Step 9: Shear check tau v less than tau c k]
    M0 --> N0[Step 10: Deflection check actual L by d less than permissible]
    N0 --> O0[Step 11: Distribution steel = 0.12 percent Ag]
    O0 --> P0[END: Provide Reinforcement Schedule]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Design Flow for Two-Way Slab (IS 456 Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START: Two-Way Slab Design] --> B1[Step 1: Obtain Lx, Ly]
    B1 --> C1[Step 2: Check Ly by Lx less than 2]
    C1 --> D1[Step 3: Identify Panel Type Interior Edge Corner]
    D1 --> E1[Step 4: Compute Factored Load wu]
    E1 --> F1[Step 5: Lookup alpha x alpha y from IS 456 Table 12]
    F1 --> G1[Step 6: Compute Mx and My]
    G1 --> H1[Step 7: Effective depth d from larger moment]
    H1 --> I1[Step 8: Compute Ast x and Ast y separately]
    I1 --> J1[Step 9: Spacing limits check Smax and Smin]
    J1 --> K1[Step 10: Shear and Deflection Checks]
    K1 --> L1[Step 11: Detailing Bars at Top and Bottom]
    L1 --> M1[END: Provide Plan and Section Drawing]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Column Design Flow (IS 456 Cl. 39.3)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[START: Short Column Design] --> B2[Step 1: Determine Pu from Load Combinations]
    B2 --> C2[Step 2: Assume Column Size b and D]
    C2 --> D2[Step 3: Compute Leff and Slenderness lambda]
    D2 --> E2{lambda less than or equal 12}
    E2 -- YES --> F2[Step 4: Short Column, use Cl 39.3]
    E2 -- NO --> G2[Redirect: Long Column Design with SP16]
    F2 --> H2[Step 5: Compute emin using L by 500 plus D by 30]
    H2 --> I2[Step 6: Assume p percent of steel between 0.8 and 4]
    I2 --> J2[Step 7: Compute Pu capacity by 0.4 fck Ac plus 0.67 fy Asc]
    J2 --> K2{Pu capacity greater or equal Pu required}
    K2 -- NO --> L2[Increase Asc and Recompute]
    K2 -- YES --> M2[Step 8: Select Bar Number and Layout]
    L2 --> J2
    M2 --> N2[Step 9: Design Lateral Ties diameter and pitch]
    N2 --> O2[Step 10: Provide Column Schedule and Drawing]
    O2 --> P2[END: Column Design Complete]
```

    SLAB[Roof Slab One Way or Two Way] --> BM1[Primary Beam]
