<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define a one-way slab and a two-way slab as per IS 456:2000. Give one example of each from a farm structure. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **one-way slab** is a slab in which the ratio of the longer span (Ly) to the shorter span (Lx) is greater than or equal to **2.0**, and the load is transferred predominantly in the shorter direction. As per IS 456:2000, Cl. 24.1, it is designed as a rectangular beam of unit width. *Example:* The roof slab of a long rectangular poultry shed (say 12 m × 4 m) is a one-way slab spanning 4 m.

A **two-way slab** is a slab in which Ly/Lx < 2.0 and is supported on all four edges, causing bending in both principal directions. The load is shared in both directions as per IS 456 coefficients (Table 12). *Example:* The floor slab of a square milking parlour (5 m × 5 m) is a typical two-way slab.

---

#### Question 2: What is the minimum eccentricity to be considered while designing a short axially loaded RCC column as per IS 456:2000? Why is it not designed for pure axial load? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

As per **IS 456:2000, Cl. 25.4**, the minimum eccentricity to be considered in the design of an axially loaded column is:

$$e_{min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \geq 20 \text{ mm}$$

where L = unsupported length and D = lateral dimension in the considered direction.

A column is **never designed for pure axial load** because in practice there is always some unintended moment from construction imperfections, lateral loads (wind on the farm shed), uneven loading, or misalignment of supports. The code therefore mandates a **minimum design eccentricity of 20 mm** to ensure the column is safe under these unavoidable secondary effects.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Pattern:** Students must answer **ONE full question** (14 marks) from the module. Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Internal choice is between full questions only.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A.** Design a simply supported one-way RCC slab for the roof of a farm godown with the following data:
- Clear span = 3.5 m
- Live load = 2.0 kN/m²
- Floor finish = 1.5 kN/m²
- Materials: M20 concrete, Fe415 HYSD steel
- Exposure condition: Mild

**(a)** Determine the slab thickness, effective depth, and area of main reinforcement. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Design the distribution steel and perform checks for shear and deflection. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### **Model Solution (a): Slab Thickness, Effective Depth, Main Steel**

**Step 1: Assumed overall depth D = 150 mm, effective cover 25 mm, d = 125 mm**

**Step 2: Loads per metre width (assuming slab thickness 150 mm)**

$$\text{Slab self-weight} = 0.150 \times 1 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$\text{Floor finish} = 1.50 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$\text{DL} = 5.25 \text{ kN/m}; \quad \text{LL} = 2.0 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$w = 7.25 \text{ kN/m}; \quad w_u = 1.5 \times 7.25 = 10.875 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3: Effective Span**

$$L_{eff} = \min(3.5 + 0.125, 3.5) = 3.5 \text{ m (clear span used)}$$

**Step 4: Design Moment (simply supported)**

$$M_u = \frac{10.875 \times 3.5^2}{8} = 16.65 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5: Effective Depth Required**

$$d_{req} = \sqrt{\frac{16.65 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 1000}} = \sqrt{6033} = 77.7 \text{ mm}$$

Provided d = 125 mm > 77.7 mm ✓

**Step 6: Main Steel (Ast)**

$$\frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2} = \frac{4.6 \times 16.65 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 125^2} = 0.245$$

$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.245}\right] \times 1000 \times 125$$

$$A_{st} = 0.0241 \times [1 - 0.869] \times 125000 = 0.0241 \times 0.131 \times 125000$$

$$A_{st} = 394.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Provide 10 mm dia. bars @ 190 mm c/c** (Ast provided = 78.5/190 × 1000 = 413 mm²). Safe.

---

##### **Model Solution (b): Distribution Steel, Shear & Deflection**

**Step 1: Distribution Steel**

$$A_{sd} = 0.12\% \times 1000 \times 150 = 180 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provide **8 mm @ 270 mm c/c** (Ast = 186 mm²). Safe.

**Step 2: Shear Check**

$$V_u = \frac{10.875 \times 3.5}{2} = 19.03 \text{ kN}$$
$$\tau_v = \frac{19.03 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 125} = 0.152 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

For M20, p = 0.275% (interpolating from IS 456 Table 19), $\tau_c ≈ 0.38$ N/mm².

Since $\tau_v = 0.152 < \tau_c = 0.38$, **shear is safe** without shear reinforcement.

**Step 3: Deflection Check**

Basic (L/d) for simply supported = 20 (Fe415).

For p = 0.275%, modification factor from Fig. 4, IS 456 ≈ 1.1.

Permissible (L/d) = 20 × 1.1 = 22.

Actual (L/d) = 3500/125 = 28.

> **Deflection NOT safe** with 150 mm thickness. Increase d to 3500/22 = 159 mm, hence adopt **D = 175 mm (d = 150 mm)**.

Revised check with d = 150 mm: actual (L/d) = 3500/150 = 23.3, slightly above 22. Provide **D = 180 mm** for safety.

**Final Design Summary:**
- Slab thickness D = **180 mm**
- Main steel: **10 mm @ 190 mm c/c** (at bottom, along short span)
- Distribution steel: **8 mm @ 270 mm c/c** (at bottom, along long span)
- Cover: **20 mm**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**B.** Design a short RCC column to support the roof of a farm storehouse with the following data:
- Factored axial load Pu = 1500 kN
- Unsupported length L = 3.5 m
- Both ends hinged
- Materials: M25 concrete, Fe415 steel
- Exposure: Mild

**(a)** Design the column size and longitudinal reinforcement using IS 456 Cl. 39.3. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Design the lateral ties, check slenderness, and minimum eccentricity. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### **Model Solution (a): Column Size and Longitudinal Steel**

**Step 1: Assume column size 350 mm × 350 mm**

$$A_g = 350 \times 350 = 122500 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Slenderness**

$L_{eff} = 3.5$ m (both ends hinged), $L_{eff}/D = 3500/350 = 10 < 12$. **Short column.**

**Step 3: Min Eccentricity**

$$e_{min} = \frac{3500}{500} + \frac{350}{30} = 7 + 11.67 = 18.67 \text{ mm} < 20 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt **e_min = 20 mm**.

**Step 4: Required Asc (Iterative)**

Assume 1.5% steel: $A_{sc} = 0.015 \times 122500 = 1838$ mm², $A_c = 120662$ mm².

$$P_u = 0.4 \times 25 \times 120662 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1838$$
$$P_u = 1206620 + 511259 = 1717879 \text{ N} = 1717.9 \text{ kN}$$

This is **> 1500 kN** ✓. **Adopt 1.5% steel = 1838 mm²**.

**Step 5: Bar Selection**

Use 20 mm dia. bars (area = 314 mm² each).
Number of bars = 1838/314 = 5.85 → adopt **6 bars of 20 mm dia** (provided = 1884 mm²).

Layout: 6 bars symmetrically distributed along the 4 sides of the 350 × 350 column.

---

##### **Model Solution (b): Lateral Ties, Slenderness, Eccentricity**

**Step 1: Diameter of Ties (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.2)**

$$\phi_{tie} \geq \max\left(\frac{\phi_{main}}{4}, 6 \text{ mm}\right) = \max(5, 6) = 6 \text{ mm}$$

Provide **8 mm dia. ties** (conservative).

**Step 2: Pitch of Ties**

Pitch = least of:
- 300 mm
- $16 \phi_{main} = 16 \times 20 = 320$ mm
- Least lateral dimension = 350 mm

**Pitch = 300 mm c/c**.

**Step 3: Ties at Every Alternate Main Bar**

Where bars are spaced more than 150 mm apart, additional ties (supplementary ties) shall be provided. With 6 bars on a 350 mm square, spacing is ≈ 350/2 = 175 mm, so provide one **cross-tie** in addition to the perimeter tie at every level.

**Step 4: Min & Max Steel Checks (Cl. 26.5.3.1)**

- Min steel = 0.8% × 122500 = 980 mm². Provided = 1884 mm² ✓
- Max steel = 6% × 122500 = 7350 mm². Provided = 1884 mm² ✓

**Step 5: Final Design Summary**

- Column size: **350 × 350 mm**
- Longitudinal steel: **6 bars of 20 mm dia. (Fe415)**
- Lateral ties: **8 mm dia. @ 300 mm c/c** with cross-ties
- Clear cover: **40 mm**
- Minimum eccentricity: **20 mm** (governed by code minimum)
- Slenderness: **10** (short column)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Effective span for slabs** — Students frequently use the **clear span** alone, ignoring the **+d** provision. This is a **1–2 mark deduction** in ESE.
> 2. **Deflection check for simply supported slabs** — Many students compute Ast and stop, ignoring the (L/d) check. This is a **2–3 mark deduction** because IS 456 mandates it.
> 3. **Min eccentricity of 20 mm** — If calculated e_min is less than 20 mm, students sometimes use the calculated value. **Always use 20 mm** as the floor.
> 4. **Lateral tie diameter** — Ties must be **at least 1/4 of the largest main bar**, with a **6 mm minimum**. Many students forget the 6 mm floor.
> 5. **Two-way slab reinforcement layering** — Reinforcement in the **shorter direction is placed outermost**. Reversing this reduces the effective depth and may cause cracks.
> 6. **Distribution steel minimum** — Don't forget **0.12% of gross cross-section** for Fe415 in one-way slabs (Cl. 26.5.2.1).
> 7. **Column with load > 0.4 fck Ag** — In such cases, a **detailed design for biaxial bending with SP-16 charts** is required, beyond the scope of Cl. 39.3.

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points to Remember Before the KTU Exam:**

- **One-way slab** ⇔ $L_y / L_x \geq 2$. Designed as a unit-width beam. **Effective span** = clear span or c/c of supports (whichever is less).
- **Two-way slab** ⇔ $L_y / L_x < 2$. Moments from **IS 456 Table 12 coefficients** (αx, αy).
- **Limit-state design constants** for **Fe415, M20, no compression steel**: $0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ for $M_{u,lim}$.
- **Distribution steel in one-way slab** ≥ **0.12% of Ag** for Fe415.
- **L/d basic** for simply supported = 20 (Fe415); for continuous = 26.
- **Shear design strength** $\tau_c$ from **IS 456 Table 19** (depends on p and fck).
- **Column** is **short** if **Leff / D ≤ 12** (both ends hinged).
- **Column design equation** (Cl. 39.3): $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$.
- **Min eccentricity**: $e_{min} = L/500 + D/30 \geq 20$ mm.
- **Longitudinal steel** in column: **0.8% ≤ p ≤ 6%** of Ag.
- **Lateral ties**: dia ≥ $\phi_{main}/4$ (≥ 6 mm); pitch ≤ least of 300, $16\phi_{main}$, B.
- **Cover for column** = **40 mm**; **cover for slab** = **20 mm** (mild exposure).
- **Unit weight of RCC** = **25 kN/m³**; load factor = **1.5** for both DL and LL.
- **Bending moment in slab** is calculated using **factored load** $w_u = 1.5(DL+LL)$.
- For **two-way slabs**, place reinforcement of **shorter direction outermost**.
- For **columns**, **at every alternate main bar** an additional cross-tie is required if bar spacing exceeds 150 mm.
- **Farm structure applications**: poultry shed roofs (one-way), milking parlour floors (two-way), elevated silo supports (short stocky columns).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Short Columns with Axial Loads; Design of Long Column (Brief Description Only); Footings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Design of Short Columns with Axial Loads; Design of Long Column (Brief Description Only); Footings ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Definition of a Column (Farm Structures Context) A **column** is a vertical structural compression member whose effective length exceeds three times the least lateral dimension, used to transmit loads from beams/slabs/trusses (such as roof frames of farm buildings, storage godowns, or poultry sheds) down to the foundation. The axial compressive load is the dominant internal force, although minor bending moments (from wind, eccentric beam reactions, or roof truss reactions) may coexist. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (IS 456:2000 compliant):** > A *compression member* is a structural element primarily subjected to axial compressive forces. When its effective length is small enough that lateral buckling is prevented, it is classified as a **short column**; otherwise, it is a **long column**. ### 1.2 Classification of Columns by Slenderness | Type | Slenderness Ratio (λ = Leff/D) | Mode of Failure | |------|------------------------------|-----------------| | Short (Pedestal) | λ ≤ 12 (Rectangular) | Pure crushing of material | | Short (Spiral) | λ ≤ 4 (Circular with helical binding) | Pure crushing of material | | Long | λ > 12 (Rectangular) | Elastic buckling instability | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Standing Straw vs. The Tall Bamboo":** > Imagine two vertical rods — a *thick stubby straw* and a *slender bamboo shoot*. If you press both along their length, the thick straw crushes at the top (material failure), while the slender bamboo suddenly bends sideways and snaps (buckling instability). The straw is a **short column** (designed for crushing strength), the bamboo is a **long column** (designed for buckling resistance). In a farm godown, the column supporting a heavy RCC roof is short; a tall, unbraced column in an open-sided cattle shed is long. ### 1.4 Definition of a Footing A **footing** (or *foundation pad*) is the widened portion of a column at its base, which spreads the concentrated column load over a larger area of soil so that the safe bearing capacity of the soil is not exceeded. It is the *interface* between the superstructure (column) and the soil. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metrics Used in this Module:** > - Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC) of soil: expressed in **kN/m²** (or kPa). > - Concrete grade **M20, M25**; Steel grade **Fe415, Fe500**. > - Unit weight of plain concrete: **24 kN/m³** ; Reinforced concrete: **25 kN/m³**. > - Partial safety factors (IS 456:2000, Limit State Method): γm (concrete) = **1.5**, γm (steel) = **1.15**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Assumptions in the Design of Axially Loaded Short Columns (IS 456:2000, Cl. 25.1) 1. Plane sections remain plane after loading (Bernoulli's hypothesis). 2. Maximum strain in concrete at the extreme compression fibre = **0.002** (in pure axial compression, it is 0.002 by IS 456; in flexure, 0.0035). 3. The relationship between compressive stress distribution in concrete and the strain is assumed to be **rectangular (parabolic-rectangular simplified)**. 4. Tensile strength of concrete is ignored. 5. Maximum permissible compressive stress in concrete = **0.4 fck**. 6. Maximum permissible compressive stress in steel = **0.67 fy**. ### 2.2 Design Philosophy: Minimum Eccentricity > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pure axial loading never occurs in practice.** Even a perfectly axial design must account for accidental eccentricities. IS 456 (Cl. 25.4) therefore mandates that columns be designed for an *axial load Pu* and a *minimum moment Mu* corresponding to a minimum eccentricity: > > $e_{min} = \dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} \ge 20 \text{ mm}$ > > If the computed $e = M/P$ is less than $e_{min}$, the column must be designed for $e_{min}$. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Short Column with Axial Load) > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are *Limit State of Collapse* (LSM) equations — the only design method accepted by KTU 2024 Scheme. | # | Formula | Description | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | $P_u = 0.4 \, f_{ck} \, A_c + 0.67 \, f_y \, A_{sc}$ | Axial load capacity of a short column (IS 456 Cl. 25.4.1, with full safety factors applied to material strengths) | | 2 | $A_g = A_c + A_{sc}$ | Gross area of column cross-section | | 3 | $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$ | Net area of concrete | | 4 | $A_{sc,min} = 0.008 \, A_g$ | Minimum longitudinal steel (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.1) | | 5 | $A_{sc,max} = 0.04 \, A_g$ | Maximum longitudinal steel (excluding lap-splice zones: 0.06) | | 6 | $\lambda = L_{eff} / D$ | Slenderness ratio | | 7 | $L_{eff} = k \, L$ | Effective length, $k$ depends on end conditions | | 8 | $e_{min} = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} \ge 20 \text{ mm}$ | Minimum design eccentricity | | 9 | $M_u = P_u \cdot e_{min}$ | Minimum factored moment for design | | 10 | $\phi = 0.67 f_y \, A_{sc} \left[ 1 - \dfrac{0.67 f_y A_{sc}}{f_{ck} A_c + 1.67 f_y A_{sc}} \right]$ | IS 456:2000 parameter (Cl. 39.3, for short axially-loaded columns) | | 11 | $e / D = \dfrac{1 + \phi(P_g - 0.5)}{1.33 - (P_g/3)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{D}$ — but for $e_{min}$ use chart | IS 456 SP-16 Design Chart 28 for $P_u$–$M_u$ interaction | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Students often forget the safety factors. In the formula $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$, the constants **0.4** and **0.67** are *not* material factors — they already include them. Compare this with WSM where the formula was $P = 0.85 A_c f_{ck} + A_{sc} f_y$. ### 2.4 Why Short Columns Are Designed for Axial Load This Way - **Material crushing** governs; no buckling. - The composite action of steel and concrete gives the strength: concrete takes ~70% of load, steel takes ~30%. - The constants 0.4 and 0.67 represent the design compressive stress in concrete and steel, respectively, *after* safety factors are applied. - This formula is valid ONLY when $e < 0.05 D$ (approximately); otherwise the column must be designed as a beam-column using **SP-16 interaction charts**. ### 2.5 Long Column — Brief Theoretical Description (KTU-Mandated Concise) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 syllabus, the topic is "*Brief Description Only*". A complete Euler/Rankine treatment is **not** required. However, the following are mandatory bullet points. A long column fails by **elastic instability (buckling)** rather than by crushing. The buckling load is given by **Euler's formula**: $$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_{eff}^2}$$ Where: - $E$ = Modulus of elasticity of the column material (concrete: $E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ MPa). - $I$ = Moment of inertia of the gross section. - $L_{eff}$ = Effective length, dictated by end conditions (both ends hinged: $L_{eff} = L$; one end fixed, other hinged: $L_{eff} = 0.7L$; both ends fixed: $L_{eff} = 0.5L$; one end fixed, other free: $L_{eff} = 2L$). IS 456:2000 (Cl. 25.1.2) prescribes that for **long columns**, design must consider a **reduction factor** to account for slenderness. The standard code-based approach uses **additional moments** due to deflection: $$M_{ax} = \dfrac{P_u \cdot D}{2000} \cdot \left( \dfrac{L_{eff}}{D} \right)^2$$ This additional moment is added to the initial moment (if any), and the column is then designed as a beam-column using the SP-16 interaction chart. ### 2.6 Footings — Theory Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > A footing must satisfy three checks in design: > 1. **Bearing capacity check** (soil pressure $q = P / A \le q_{allow}$). > 2. **Shear check** (one-way and two-way/punching shear). > 3. **Bending moment check** at critical sections (column face, or at $d/2$ from face for shear). **Types of footings** in farm structures: 1. **Isolated (Spread) Footing** — most common; one column. 2. **Combined Footing** — supports two adjacent columns. 3. **Strap (Cantilever) Footing** — two isolated footings connected by a beam. 4. **Strip (Wall) Footing** — under walls. 5. **Raft/Mat Footing** — covers the entire building footprint; used when SBC is poor. ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Isolated Footing) | # | Formula | Description | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | $A_{req} = \dfrac{P}{q_{net}}$ | Required plan area; $q_{net} = q_{allow} - \gamma_{soil} \cdot D_f$ | | 2 | $q_u = \dfrac{P_u}{A_{provided}}$ | Factored upward soil pressure | | 3 | $V_u = q_u \cdot B \cdot \left( \dfrac{L - a}{2} - d \right)$ | One-way shear force at distance $d$ from column face (long direction) | | 4 | $V_{u,punch} = q_u \cdot \left[ A_{footing} - (a + d)(b + d) \right]$ | Punching (two-way) shear | | 5 | $\tau_c$ (table 19, IS 456) | Permissible shear stress of concrete (function of $p_t$) | | 6 | $M_u = q_u \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{(L - a)^2}{8}$ | Bending moment at column face | | 7 | $A_{st} = \dfrac{M_u}{0.87 f_y \, d}$ | Required flexural steel (for singly reinforced) | | 8 | $p_t = \dfrac{100 A_{st}}{B \, d}$ | Percentage of steel (for shear design) | | 9 | $D_{min}$ (governed by shear) | Effective depth $d$ is chosen so $V_u \le \tau_c B d$ (one-way) and $V_{u,punch} \le k_s \tau_c (a+d)(b+d) \cdot 2$ | | 10 | $W_{footing} = A_{provided} \cdot D \cdot 25$ kN | Self-weight of footing (used to compute $q_{net}$) | > [!TIP] > **Real-world engineering utility:** In farm buildings (storage godowns, dairy parlours, poultry sheds), footings are typically *isolated square footings* because the loads are light (10–500 kN per column) and the soil is often reasonably good. Raft footings are used for silos and large barns on weak soils. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Short Column Axial Capacity Formula (IS 456) The design of an axially loaded short column using Limit State Method is derived from the rectangular stress block (IS 456, Cl. 39.3). Consider a column section of gross area $A_g$, with concrete area $A_c$ and steel area $A_{sc}$. **Step 1: Strain compatibility.** At ultimate state, the strain in concrete and steel is the same (since they are bonded): $$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_{cu} \approx 0.002 \text{ (in pure compression)}$$ **Step 2: Stress in concrete.** The design compressive stress in concrete, after safety factor: $$f_{cd} = \dfrac{0.67 \, f_{ck}}{1.5} = 0.4467 \, f_{ck}$$ However, IS 456 Cl. 36.4.1 permits an alternative simpler expression: the maximum compressive stress in a short column (no bending) is taken as $0.4 f_{ck}$ in the equation. This accounts for in-situ conditions and micro-cracking. **Step 3: Stress in steel.** The design stress in steel (mild compression yielding): $$f_{sd} = \dfrac{f_y}{1.15} = 0.8696 \, f_y$$ Again, IS 456 Cl. 25.4.1 simplifies this to the constant **0.67 fy** in the formula. **Step 4: Equilibrium — Sum of internal forces = External axial load.** The total axial capacity is the sum of contributions from concrete and steel: $$P_u = (\text{stress in concrete}) \times (\text{area of concrete}) + (\text{stress in steel}) \times (\text{area of steel})$$ **Step 5: Substitute the design constants.** $$\begin{aligned} P_u &= (0.4 \, f_{ck}) \cdot A_c + (0.67 \, f_y) \cdot A_{sc} \\ &= 0.4 \, f_{ck} (A_g - A_{sc}) + 0.67 \, f_y \, A_{sc} \\ &= 0.4 \, f_{ck} \, A_g + (0.67 \, f_y - 0.4 \, f_{ck}) A_{sc} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **IS 456:2000 design equation for short axially loaded columns (with minimum eccentricity)**. For the *unfactored* working stress version, refer to SP-16 or older WSM textbooks. The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates LSM throughout. ### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Short Column Design **Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** Design a short column to carry an axial factored load $P_u = 1500$ kN. Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. The column is 400 mm × 400 mm. Take $L_{eff} = 3$ m. Verify slenderness. Use LSM. **Step 1: Verify short-column behaviour.** $$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{D} = \dfrac{3000}{400} = 7.5$$ Since $\lambda = 7.5 < 12$, it is a **short column**. ✓ **Step 2: Compute minimum eccentricity.** $$e_{min} = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} = \dfrac{3000}{500} + \dfrac{400}{30}$$ $$\begin{aligned} e_{min} &= 6.0 + 13.33 \\ &= 19.33 \text{ mm} < 20 \text{ mm} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore, use $e_{min} = 20 \text{ mm}$. **Step 3: Compute associated minimum moment.** $$M_u = P_u \cdot e_{min} = 1500 \times 0.020 = 30 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 4: Gross area of column.** $$A_g = 400 \times 400 = 160{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 5: Provide steel (assume 2% of gross area initially).** $$A_{sc,provided} = 0.02 \times 160{,}000 = 3200 \text{ mm}^2$$ Provide 8 bars of 22 mm $\phi$ (each bar = $\pi \times 22^2 / 4 = 380.1$ mm², total $A_{sc} = 8 \times 380.1 = 3040$ mm² ≈ close to required). Or 4 bars of 32 mm $\phi$ ($A_{sc} = 3217$ mm²). **Step 6: Check axial capacity.** Concrete area: $$A_c = A_g - A_{sc} = 160{,}000 - 3040 = 156{,}960 \text{ mm}^2$$ Axial capacity: $$\begin{aligned} P_{u,capacity} &= 0.4 \times 20 \times 156{,}960 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 3040 \\ &= 1{,}255{,}680 + 847{,}165 \\ &= 2{,}102{,}845 \text{ N} \\ &\approx 2103 \text{ kN} > 1500 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7: Provide lateral ties (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2).** - Tie diameter: $\phi_{tie} \ge \phi_{main}/4 = 22/4 = 5.5$ mm → use 8 mm $\phi$ ties. - Tie spacing: least of (i) 16 $\phi_{main}$ = 352 mm, (ii) 48 $\phi_{tie}$ = 384 mm, (iii) least lateral dimension = 400 mm. - Adopt spacing = **300 mm c/c**. ### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Isolated Footing Design **Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** A 400 mm × 400 mm column carries a service axial load $P = 1000$ kN and a moment $M = 50$ kN·m. Design an isolated footing on soil with SBC = 150 kN/m². Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Depth of footing below GL = 1.2 m. Take unit weight of soil = 18 kN/m³, concrete = 25 kN/m³. Adopt LSM. **Step 1: Factored load on column (1.5 for DL, 1.5 for LL — IS 456, Table 18).** Assuming $P$ is already *factored* (typical in KTU questions): $P_u = 1500$ kN, $M_u = 75$ kN·m. **Step 2: Net allowable bearing pressure.** $$q_{net} = q_{allow} - \gamma_{soil} \cdot D_f = 150 - 18 \times 1.2 = 128.4 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 3: Required area of footing (approximate).** $$A_{req} = \dfrac{P_u}{q_{net}} = \dfrac{1500}{128.4} = 11.68 \text{ m}^2$$ For a square footing: $$L = B = \sqrt{11.68} = 3.42 \text{ m} \approx 3.5 \text{ m}$$ Provide $3.5 \text{ m} \times 3.5 \text{ m}$ footing. **Step 4: Factored upward soil pressure.** $$q_u = \dfrac{P_u}{A_{provided}} = \dfrac{1500}{3.5 \times 3.5} = 122.45 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 5: Compute bending moment at column face.** For the long direction (a = 400 mm, projecting length = $(L-a)/2 = (3500-400)/2 = 1550$ mm): $$M_u = q_u \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{(L-a)^2}{8} = 122.45 \times 3.5 \times \dfrac{(1.55)^2}{8}$$ $$\begin{aligned} M_u &= 122.45 \times 3.5 \times 0.3003 \\ &= 128.7 \text{ kN·m} \end{aligned}$$ For the short direction: same $M_u$ by symmetry. **Step 6: Assume effective depth $d$ and compute steel.** Try $d = 550$ mm (overall depth $D = 600$ mm). $$A_{st} = \dfrac{M_u}{0.87 \, f_y \, d} = \dfrac{128.7 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 415 \times 550}$$ $$\begin{aligned} A_{st} &= \dfrac{128.7 \times 10^6}{198{,}502} \\ &= 648 \text{ mm}^2 \end{aligned}$$ Provide **12 mm $\phi$ bars at 150 mm c/c** (each bar = 113 mm², number of bars in 3500 mm = $3500/150 = 23.3$ → use 24 bars; $A_{st} = 24 \times 113 = 2712$ mm²). This is much higher than 648, but is governed by **minimum steel** (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.1 for footings: $A_{st,min} = 0.12\% \times B \times d$). $$A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.12}{100} \times 3500 \times 550 = 2310 \text{ mm}^2$$ Therefore adopt $A_{st,provided} = 2712$ mm² ✓. **Step 7: Check one-way shear.** Critical section at $d$ from column face: Projection beyond critical section = $1550 - 550 = 1000$ mm. $$V_u = q_u \cdot B \cdot (\text{projection}) = 122.45 \times 3.5 \times 1.0 = 428.6 \text{ kN}$$ $$\tau_v = \dfrac{V_u}{B \, d} = \dfrac{428.6 \times 10^3}{3500 \times 550} = 0.223 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $$p_t = \dfrac{100 \times 2712}{3500 \times 550} = 0.141\%$$ From IS 456 Table 19, for M20 and $p_t = 0.141\%$, $\tau_c \approx 0.28$ N/mm². Since $\tau_v < \tau_c$, safe. ✓ **Step 8: Check two-way (punching) shear.** Critical perimeter at $(a + d)/2 = (400+550)/2 = 475$ mm from column edge. $$V_{u,punch} = q_u \cdot \left[ A_{footing} - (a+d)^2 \right]$$ $$= 122.45 \times \left[ 12.25 - (0.95)^2 \right] = 122.45 \times [12.25 - 0.9025]$$ $$= 122.45 \times 11.3475 = 1389.5 \text{ kN}$$ Perimeter of critical section = $4 \times (a+d) = 4 \times 950 = 3800$ mm. $$\tau_v = \dfrac{1389.5 \times 10^3}{3800 \times 550} = 0.665 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ IS 456 Cl. 31.6.3: permissible $k_s \tau_c$ where $k_s = 0.5 + \beta_c$ (with $\beta_c$ = ratio of long to short column side = 1.0 for square column), so $k_s = 1.5$ (max). $$k_s \tau_c = 1.5 \times 0.28 = 0.42 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ Since $\tau_v = 0.665 > 0.42$, punching shear is NOT safe. **Increase depth.** Try $D = 750$ mm, $d = 700$ mm. Recompute: - New projection = $1550 - 700 = 850$ mm. - $V_u = 122.45 \times 3.5 \times 0.85 = 364.2$ kN, $\tau_v = 0.149$ N/mm² ✓. - $V_{u,punch} = 122.45 \times [12.25 - (1.1)^2] = 122.45 \times 11.04 = 1351.8$ kN. - Perimeter = $4 \times 1100 = 4400$ mm; $\tau_v = 1351.8 \times 10^3 / (4400 \times 700) = 0.439$ N/mm². - Still $> 0.42$. Try $D = 800$ mm, $d = 750$ mm. - $\tau_v = 122.45 \times 11.3025 \times 1000 / (4 \times 1150 \times 750) = 0.401$ N/mm² ✓. **Adopt overall depth $D = 800$ mm, $d = 750$ mm.** Re-check flexural steel and minimum steel — adopt same 12 mm $\phi$ @ 150 mm c/c. ## 4.
