For the final design, students should draw the cross-section, plan, and reinforcement schematic by hand on the answer sheet — this is a KTU valuation expectation.* ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Distinguish between a short column and a long column. State the IS 456:2000 slenderness limits.* **Model Answer:** A **short column** is one in which the load capacity is governed by the *crushing strength* of the material, with no significant reduction due to buckling. A **long column** is one in which the load capacity is governed by *buckling (lateral instability)* before the material reaches its crushing strength. IS 456:2000 classifies columns based on the slenderness ratio $\lambda = L_{eff} / D$: - For *rectangular columns*: $\lambda \le 12$ → short column; $\lambda > 12$ → long column. - For *circular columns with helical binding*: $\lambda \le 4$ → short column; $\lambda > 4$ → long column. *(3 marks: 1 for definitions + 1.5 for slenderness limits with formula + 0.5 for classification.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State the IS 456 formula for the axial load capacity of a short column. Explain the significance of the constants 0.4 and 0.67.* **Model Answer:** The IS 456:2000 (Cl. 25.4.1) design equation for a short axially loaded column is: $$P_u = 0.4 \, f_{ck} \, A_c + 0.67 \, f_y \, A_{sc}$$ - The constant **0.4** represents the *maximum permissible compressive stress in concrete* (after applying the partial safety factor $\gamma_m = 1.5$ to the characteristic strength $f_{ck}$). It accounts for in-situ imperfections and micro-cracking. - The constant **0.67** represents the *maximum permissible compressive stress in steel* (after applying the partial safety factor $\gamma_m = 1.15$ to the characteristic yield strength $f_y$). This formula is valid *only* for short columns with minimum design eccentricity. *(3 marks: 1 for formula + 1 each for the two constants.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]** A 450 mm × 450 mm short column in a farm storage godown is to carry a factored axial load of **1800 kN** and a factored moment of **60 kN·m** at its base. The column has an unsupported length of **3.6 m** with both ends effectively held in position but not restrained against rotation. Use M25 concrete and Fe500 steel. Design the column using LSM as per IS 456:2000. Verify slenderness, compute minimum eccentricity, design the longitudinal reinforcement, and specify the lateral ties. **Sub-parts:** **(a)** Verify the column as a short column and compute the design eccentricity. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Design the longitudinal steel and specify lateral ties. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) Verification and Eccentricity Calculation:** Step 1: Effective length. Both ends *held in position* but not restrained against rotation → $L_{eff} = L = 3.6$ m = 3600 mm. (IS 456, Table 25, Case C, $k = 1.0$.) Step 2: Slenderness ratio. $$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{D} = \dfrac{3600}{450} = 8.0$$ Since $\lambda = 8.0 < 12$, it is a **short column**. ✓ Step 3: Actual eccentricity. $$e = \dfrac{M_u}{P_u} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6}{1800 \times 10^3} = 33.33 \text{ mm}$$ Step 4: Minimum eccentricity (IS 456, Cl. 25.4). $$e_{min} = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} = \dfrac{3600}{500} + \dfrac{450}{30} = 7.2 + 15.0 = 22.2 \text{ mm}$$ Since $e = 33.33 > e_{min} = 22.2$, use **actual eccentricity $e = 33.33$ mm**. Step 5: Compute $e/D$ for design-chart reference. $$e/D = 33.33 / 450 = 0.074$$ Step 6: Gross area and dimensions. $$A_g = 450 \times 450 = 202{,}500 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$D = 450 \text{ mm}$$ **Conclusion of (a):** The column is short; design eccentricity is 33.33 mm; $e/D = 0.074$. Use SP-16 interaction Chart 28 with $p = 0.02$–$0.04$ to read off $P_u/f_{ck} b D$. --- **(b) Longitudinal Steel and Tie Design:** Step 1: Try $p = 2.5\%$ (typical short column). $$A_{sc,required} = 0.025 \times 202{,}500 = 5062 \text{ mm}^2$$ Step 2: Use 4 bars of 40 mm $\phi$ at corners + 4 bars of 25 mm $\phi$ on sides (uniform arrangement). - 4 × 40 mm $\phi$: $4 \times 1257 = 5026$ mm². - 4 × 25 mm $\phi$: $4 \times 491 = 1964$ mm². - Total $A_{sc,provided} = 6990$ mm². This gives $p_{provided} = 6990/202500 = 3.45\%$. Step 3: Check $A_{sc,min}$ and $A_{sc,max}$: - $A_{sc,min} = 0.008 \times 202500 = 1620$ mm² ✓ - $A_{sc,max} = 0.04 \times 202500 = 8100$ mm² ✓ Step 4: Capacity check (for pure axial case, conservatively). $$A_c = 202500 - 6990 = 195510 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$P_{u,cap} = 0.4 \times 25 \times 195510 + 0.67 \times 500 \times 6990$$ $$= 1{,}955{,}100 + 2{,}341{,}650 = 4{,}296{,}750 \text{ N} \approx 4297 \text{ kN}$$ Since $4297 \text{ kN} \gg 1800$ kN ✓, the column is safe. (In a strict design, the SP-16 chart is used to verify the combined $P_u$–$M_u$ interaction, but the section is clearly adequate.) Step 5: Lateral ties (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.2). - $\phi_{tie} \ge \phi_{max}/4 = 40/4 = 10$ mm → adopt **10 mm $\phi$ ties**. - Spacing $s$ = least of: - 16 $\phi_{max}$ = 16 × 40 = 640 mm. - 48 $\phi_{tie}$ = 48 × 10 = 480 mm. - Least lateral dimension = 450 mm. - Adopt **$s = 400$ mm c/c** (practical, conservative). **Conclusion of (b):** Provide 4 bars of 40 mm $\phi$ + 4 bars of 25 mm $\phi$ as longitudinal steel, with 10 mm $\phi$ lateral ties at 400 mm c/c. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** Design an isolated square footing for a 400 mm × 400 mm column carrying a *service* axial load of **800 kN** and a *service* moment of **40 kN·m**. The soil has an SBC of **180 kN/m²**. Depth of foundation below ground level = **1.5 m**. Unit weight of soil = **18 kN/m³**, unit weight of RCC = **25 kN/m³**. Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Adopt LSM and IS 456:2000. Sketch the reinforcement details. **Sub-parts:** **(a)** Determine the size of the footing and check for bearing pressure. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Design the depth of the footing and the flexural reinforcement. Check for one-way and punching shear. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) Size of Footing and Bearing Pressure Check:** Step 1: Factored loads. $$P_u = 1.5 \times 800 = 1200 \text{ kN}, \quad M_u = 1.5 \times 40 = 60 \text{ kN·m}$$ Step 2: Net SBC. $$q_{net} = 180 - 18 \times 1.5 = 153 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ Step 3: Required area (approximate, ignoring moment effect on area for first iteration). $$A_{req} = \dfrac{1200}{153} = 7.84 \text{ m}^2$$ For a square footing: $L = B = \sqrt{7.84} = 2.8$ m. Provide **$2.8 \text{ m} \times 2.8 \text{ m}$ footing**. Step 4: Factored upward soil pressure (the moment causes non-uniform pressure; maximum edge pressure governs). $$A_{provided} = 2.8 \times 2.8 = 7.84 \text{ m}^2$$ $$W_{footing} = 7.84 \times 0.6 \times 25 = 117.6 \text{ kN (assumed depth 600 mm)}$$ $$P_{u,total} = 1200 + 1.5 \times 117.6 = 1376.4 \text{ kN}$$ $$q_u = \dfrac{1376.4}{7.84} = 175.6 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ Step 5: Maximum and minimum edge pressures (due to moment). $$S = \dfrac{B \cdot L^2}{6} = \dfrac{2.8 \times 2.8^2}{6} = 3.657 \text{ m}^3$$ $$q_{u,max} = \dfrac{P_{u,total}}{A} + \dfrac{M_u}{S} = \dfrac{1376.4}{7.84} + \dfrac{60}{3.657} = 175.6 + 16.4 = 192.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$q_{u,min} = 175.6 - 16.4 = 159.2 \text{ kN/m}^2 > 0 \quad ✓ \text{(no tension)}$$ Step 6: Bearing pressure check. $$q_{u,max} = 192.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ Bearing capacity factored: $1.5 \times q_{net} = 1.5 \times 153 = 229.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$. Since $192.0 < 229.5$ ✓, safe in bearing. **Conclusion of (a):** Footing size = 2.8 m × 2.8 m; maximum edge pressure 192 kN/m²; safe in bearing. --- **(b) Depth and Reinforcement Design:** Step 1: Critical bending moment at column face. For a footing under axial load with moment, the maximum moment occurs at the face of the column on the more heavily loaded side: $$M_u = \dfrac{q_{u,max} + q_{u,avg}}{2} \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{(L-a)^2}{8}$$ $$q_{u,avg} = (192.0 + 159.2)/2 = 175.6 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$M_u = \dfrac{192.0 + 175.6}{2} \times 2.8 \times \dfrac{(2.8 - 0.4)^2}{8}$$ $$= 183.8 \times 2.8 \times 0.36 = 185.3 \text{ kN·m}$$ Step 2: Try $d = 450$ mm (overall $D = 500$ mm). $$A_{st} = \dfrac{M_u}{0.87 \, f_y \, d} = \dfrac{185.3 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 415 \times 450} = 1139 \text{ mm}^2$$ Step 3: Minimum steel (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.1). $$A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times B \times d = 0.0012 \times 2800 \times 450 = 1512 \text{ mm}^2$$ Adopt $A_{st,provided} = 1512$ mm². Use **12 mm $\phi$ bars @ 130 mm c/c** (12 mm bar area = 113 mm², bars across 2800 mm = 2800/130 = 22 bars; $A_{st} = 22 \times 113 = 2486$ mm² ≥ 1512 ✓). Step 4: One-way shear check. - Projection beyond critical section: $(L - a)/2 - d = (2.8 - 0.4)/2 - 0.45 = 1.2 - 0.45 = 0.75$ m. - $V_u = q_{u,avg} \cdot B \cdot 0.75 = 175.6 \times 2.8 \times 0.75 = 368.8$ kN. - $\tau_v = 368.8 \times 10^3 / (2800 \times 450) = 0.293$ N/mm². - $p_t = (100 \times 2486)/(2800 \times 450) = 0.197\%$. - From IS 456 Table 19, M20, $p_t = 0.197\%$: $\tau_c \approx 0.30$ N/mm². Since $\tau_v < \tau_c$ ✓. Step 5: Two-way (punching) shear check. - Critical section at $d/2$ from column face → perimeter = $4(a + d) = 4(400 + 450) = 3400$ mm. - $V_{u,punch} = q_{u,avg} \times [A - (a+d)^2] = 175.6 \times [7.84 - (0.85)^2] = 175.6 \times 7.1175 = 1249.7$ kN. - $\tau_v = 1249.7 \times 10^3 / (3400 \times 450) = 0.817$ N/mm². - $k_s \tau_c = 0.5 + \beta_c$ times $\tau_c$ (since square column, $\beta_c = 1.0$, $k_s = 1.5$, but maximum $k_s$ for punching is governed by table 61 — value typically 1.0 for $\beta_c = 1.0$). - $k_s \tau_c = 1.0 \times 0.30 = 0.30$ N/mm². - Since $\tau_v = 0.817 > 0.30$, **punching shear is NOT safe**. Increase depth. Step 6: Increase depth. Try $d = 700$ mm ($D = 750$ mm). - New projection beyond critical section: $1.2 - 0.7 = 0.5$ m. - One-way: $V_u = 175.6 \times 2.8 \times 0.5 = 245.8$ kN, $\tau_v = 245.8 \times 10^3 / (2800 \times 700) = 0.125$ N/mm² ✓. - Punching: $V_{u,punch} = 175.6 \times [7.84 - (1.1)^2] = 175.6 \times 6.63 = 1164.3$ kN. - $\tau_v = 1164.3 \times 10^3 / (4 \times 1100 \times 700) = 0.378$ N/mm². - $k_s \tau_c = 1.0 \times \tau_c$; with new $p_t = (100 \times 2486)/(2800 \times 700) = 0.127\%$, $\tau_c \approx 0.28$ N/mm². Still $\tau_v = 0.378 > 0.28$. **Increase further.** Step 7: Try $d = 850$ mm ($D = 900$ mm). - $V_{u,punch} = 175.6 \times [7.84 - (1.25)^2] = 175.6 \times 6.2775 = 1102.3$ kN. - Perimeter = $4 \times 1250 = 5000$ mm; $\tau_v = 1102.3 \times 10^3 / (5000 \times 850) = 0.259$ N/mm². - $p_t = 0.105\%$, $\tau_c \approx 0.26$ N/mm². **Just safe** ($\tau_v = 0.259 < 0.26$). ✓ Step 8: Adopt $D = 900$ mm, $d = 850$ mm. **Conclusion of (b):** Footing depth = 900 mm, with 12 mm $\phi$ bars at 130 mm c/c in both directions at the bottom. Cover = 50 mm at bottom; top mesh of 10 mm $\phi$ @ 250 mm c/c for shrinkage/temperature. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks:** > 1. **Forgetting partial safety factors**: Using $f_{ck}$ and $f_y$ without dividing by $\gamma_m$. The constants **0.4** and **0.67** in the column formula *already incorporate* the safety factors; do not divide again. > 2. **Confusing WSM with LSM**: Working Stress Method is no longer in KTU 2024 Scheme. The formula $P = 0.85 A_c f_{ck} + A_{sc} f_y$ is *not* acceptable. Always use $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$. > 3. **Missing minimum eccentricity**: If $e = M/P < e_{min}$, students often design the column for $e = 0$, which is wrong. Use $e_{min}$ as per IS 456 Cl. 25.4. > 4. **Neglecting self-weight of footing in bearing pressure**: This typically contributes 5–10% additional load. Omitting it makes the design unconservative and **unsafe**. > 5. **Choosing depth based on flexure alone**: The most common error in footing design is computing depth from BM and forgetting that **punching shear** is the actual governing criterion for footings under heavy columns. > 6. **Confusing column short-side with footing long-side**: In one-way shear, use the *width perpendicular to the direction of shear*, not the column dimension. > 7. **Omitting lateral tie detailing**: Even a perfect column design loses 1–2 marks if tie diameter and spacing are not specified. > 8. **Long column**: Students often write Euler's formula $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L^2$ directly for the design load. This is the *buckling load* and is **not** the design capacity. Always apply IS 456's slenderness reduction via additional moment method. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** > > **Short Column (Axial Load):** > - Slenderness limit: $\lambda \le 12$ (rectangular), $\lambda \le 4$ (circular with helix). > - Axial capacity formula (LSM): $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$. > - $e_{min} = L_{eff}/500 + D/30 \ge 20$ mm. > - $M_u = P_u \cdot e_{min}$ if actual $e < e_{min}$. > - $A_{sc,min} = 0.008 A_g$, $A_{sc,max} = 0.04 A_g$. > - Lateral ties: $\phi_{tie} \ge \phi_{max}/4$; spacing = least of 16$\phi_{max}$, 48$\phi_{tie}$, least lateral dimension. > > **Long Column (Brief):** > - Buckling load: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L_{eff}^2$. > - Effective length factor $k$ depends on end conditions (1.0, 0.7, 0.5, 2.0). > - IS 456 design: account for additional moment $M_{ax} = P_u D (L_{eff}/D)^2 / 2000$. > - Treat as beam-column; use SP-16 interaction chart. > > **Footing (Isolated):** > - Required area: $A_{req} = P/q_{net}$ where $q_{net} = q_{allow} - \gamma_{soil} D_f$. > - $q_u = P_u / A_{provided}$ (factored upward pressure). > - $M_u = q_u B (L-a)^2 / 8$ at column face. > - $A_{st} = M_u / (0.87 f_y d)$; check $A_{st,min} = 0.0012 B d$. > - **One-way shear** critical at $d$ from column face: $V_u = q_u B [(L-a)/2 - d]$. > - **Punching shear** critical at $d/2$ from column face: $V_{u,punch} = q_u [A - (a+d)(b+d)]$. > - Permissible $\tau_c$ from IS 456 Table 19 (function of $p_t$ and grade). > - For two-way: $k_s \tau_c$, with $k_s = 0.5 + \beta_c$ (or 1.0 for square column). > - Punching shear usually **governs** the depth, not bending. > > **Engineering Judgement:** > - For farm godowns, isolated square footings are default. > - For tall, slender columns (open cattle sheds), check slenderness — it may be a *long column*. > - For weak soil SBC < 100 kN/m², consider *raft footing*. > - For columns on property lines, use *combined* or *strap* footings.

topics.title: Design of Isolated Footings - Axial and Eccentric Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Design of Isolated Footings - Axial and Eccentric Loading # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Isolated Footing:** An **isolated footing** (also termed a *pad footing* or *spread footing*) is a shallow foundation unit provided beneath an individual column to transfer the superimposed structural load to the supporting soil stratum. It is the most common and economical type of footing for columns carrying predominantly **axial loads**, designed as per the limit state method of **IS 456:2000** and safe bearing capacity provisions of **IS 6403:1981** / **IS 1080:1985**. In agricultural structures such as storage godowns, silo support pedestals, dairy parlour posts, and farm workshop columns, isolated footings form the foundational sub-system of nearly every single-storey framed structure built under the **Kerala State Farm Engineering Programme**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of it like this:** Imagine you are standing on soft beach sand wearing flat-bottomed shoes — your feet sink only slightly. But if you stand on a thin stick, it pierces deep into the sand. The sand cannot support the concentrated pressure. An **isolated footing** works exactly like those flat shoes — it spreads the concentrated column load over a wider soil area so that the **safe bearing capacity (SBC)** of the soil is never exceeded. **Eccentric loading** is like a person standing on one foot leaning slightly to one side — the pressure is no longer uniform under the foot; one edge carries more pressure than the other. The footing must be designed so that this *unequal* pressure distribution does not cause the soil to fail on the heavier side or cause the column to overturn. ## 1.3 Types of Isolated Footings | Type | Description | Common Use | |---|---|---| | **Square Footing** | Plan dimensions equal in both directions (L = B) | Light to moderate axial loads | | **Rectangular Footing** | Plan dimensions unequal (L > B) | When space is restricted in one direction | | **Circular Footing** | Plan shape is circular | Water tanks, silos, circular columns | | **Sloped Footing** | Variable depth (deeper at column) | To economize on concrete | | **Stepped Footing** | Multiple discrete concrete steps | To reduce excavation, common in farm structures | ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Values to Memorise:** > - **Unit weight of RCC** = **25 kN/m³** > - **Unit weight of PCC** = **24 kN/m³** > - **Unit weight of soil (dry)** = **16–18 kN/m³** > - **Grade of concrete** (agricultural structures): **M20** (commonly), **M25** for heavier loads > - **Grade of steel:** **Fe415** (HYSD bars) — minimum as per IS 456 > - **Partial Safety Factor (γₘ) for concrete** = **1.5** > - **Partial Safety Factor (γₘ) for steel** = **1.15** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Design Philosophy — Load Transfer Mechanism The structural load path for an isolated footing under **axial loading** follows this chain: 1. **Column** receives load from the superstructure (slabs, beams, trusses). 2. Column transfers load as **axial compression** to the top of the footing. 3. Footing acts as a **rigid cantilever** projecting out from the column face on all four sides. 4. **Bending moment** develops at the column-footing interface due to upward soil reaction. 5. Footing fails by **one-way shear, two-way (punching) shear, or flexure** — whichever occurs first. > [!NOTE] > The depth of the footing is governed almost always by **shear strength** (especially punching shear for square footings under axial load), while the **area of steel** is governed by the **flexural moment** at the critical section. ## 2.2 Design Under Axial Loading — Step Logic The design proceeds in the following logical sequence: - **Step 1:** Compute the factored (ultimate) load: $P_u = 1.5 \times (P_{DL} + P_{LL})$ - **Step 2:** Estimate the self-weight of footing + soil overburden (≈ **10–15 % of P** for preliminary sizing). - **Step 3:** Calculate the required footing area: $A_{req} = \dfrac{P_{service}}{q_a}$ where $q_a$ = Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC) of soil. - **Step 4:** Adopt plan dimensions (L × B) such that L × B ≥ A_req and bearing pressure $p = \dfrac{P}{A} \leq q_a$. - **Step 5:** Check for **one-way shear** at distance *d* from column face. - **Step 6:** Check for **two-way (punching) shear** at $d/2$ perimeter. - **Step 7:** Design for **flexure** at column face. - **Step 8:** Check **development length** and **bearing stress** at column base. ## 2.3 Design Under Eccentric Loading — Theory When a footing is subjected to an axial load **P** with an eccentricity **e**, the soil pressure distribution is **no longer uniform**. The classical **Meyerhof effective width concept** and the **kern area** govern the analysis. ### 2.3.1 Uniaxial Eccentricity For eccentricity $e_x$ in one direction: $$p_{max,min} = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M_x}{Z_x} = \frac{P}{BL}\left(1 \pm \frac{6e_x}{L}\right)$$ Where: - $M_x = P \times e_x$ - $Z_x = \dfrac{BL^2}{6}$ (section modulus about the axis perpendicular to eccentricity) ### 2.3.2 Biaxial Eccentricity For eccentricities $e_x$ and $e_y$ simultaneously: $$p_{max,min} = \frac{P}{BL} \pm \frac{M_x}{Z_x} \pm \frac{M_y}{Z_y} = \frac{P}{BL} \pm \frac{Pe_x}{BL^2/6} \pm \frac{Pe_y}{B^2L/6}$$ ### 2.3.3 The Kern (Core) Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kern Limit:** When eccentricity $e \leq \dfrac{L}{6}$, the entire base is in **compression** (no uplift). When $e > L/6$, part of the base lifts off the soil and the pressure distribution becomes triangular with a portion of the footing area becoming ineffective. For farm structures and most building footings, IS 456 recommends keeping $e \leq \dfrac{L}{6}$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates every key formula you must memorise for KTU Board Examinations on isolated footings. | S.No | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Required area | $A_{req} = P_{service}/q_a$ | m² | $q_a$ = SBC of soil (kN/m²) | | 2 | Factored load | $P_u = 1.5(DL + LL)$ | kN | Limit state of collapse | | 3 | Soil reaction (uniform) | $p = P/A$ | kN/m² | Axial only | | 4 | Soil reaction (uniaxial ecc.) | $p = (P/A)\left[1 \pm 6e_x/L\right]$ | kN/m² | Linear distribution | | 5 | One-way shear force | $V_{u,1w} = p_u \times B \times (L/2 - d - c/2)$ | kN | At distance *d* from face | | 6 | Two-way (punching) shear | $V_{u,2w} = p_u\left[A - (c+d)(b+d)\right]$ | kN | Critical perimeter at $d/2$ | | 7 | Nominal one-way shear stress | $\tau_{v1} = V_{u,1w}/(Bd)$ | N/mm² | ≤ $k_1\tau_c$ (IS 456 Table 19) | | 8 | Nominal punching shear stress | $\tau_{v2} = V_{u,2w}/[4(c+d)(b+d)]$ | N/mm² | ≤ $k_s\tau_c$ (IS 456 Table 20) | | 9 | Bending moment | $M_u = p_u \times B \times (L/2 - c/2)^2 / 2$ | kN·m | At column face | | 10 | Area of steel | $A_{st} = 0.5 f_{ck}/f_y\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 4.6M_u/(f_{ck}Bd^2)}\right]Bd$ | mm² | Flexure design | | 11 | Effective width (Meyerhof) | $B' = B - 2e_y$ | m | For biaxial ecc. when $e > B/6$ | | 12 | Maximum pressure (biaxial) | $p_{max} = P/(B'L')$ | kN/m² | Using effective width | | 13 | Effective depth (initial) | $d = \sqrt{M_u/(Q \cdot B)}$ | mm | $Q = 0.138 f_{ck}$ for Fe415 | | 14 | Minimum steel (IS 456) | $A_{st,min} = 0.12\% \times B \times D$ | mm² | For slabs/footings | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Farm Structures Isolated footings under eccentric loading are critical in: - **Corridor columns of poultry sheds** where wind pressure creates moment. - **Columns of elevated water tanks** on farms where overturning moment is significant. - **Truss support columns** of semi-open godowns where the truss delivers eccentric thrust. - **Silo support pedestals** where lateral grain pressure creates eccentricity in addition to vertical load. > [!TIP] > KTU examiners frequently test the difference between axial and eccentric footing pressure distribution with a simple diagram + numerical — be prepared to draw the **trapezoidal** and **triangular** pressure blocks from memory. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Eccentric Pressure Distribution (Uniaxial Case) Consider a rectangular footing of plan dimensions $L \times B$ subjected to an axial load $P$ acting at an eccentricity $e$ measured from the centroid (along the L direction). **Step 1 — Statical Equivalence:** Replace the eccentric load with an equivalent axial load $P$ at the centroid plus a moment $M = P \cdot e$. $$P_{eff} = P \quad \text{and} \quad M = P \cdot e$$ **Step 2 — Superposition of Stresses:** The pressure at any point on the base is the sum of the uniform pressure and the bending-induced pressure. $$p(x) = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{M \cdot x}{I}$$ where $x$ is the distance from the centroidal axis (positive toward the eccentricity side). **Step 3 — Substitute Section Properties:** For a rectangular base: $$A = B \cdot L \quad \text{and} \quad I = \frac{B L^3}{12}$$ $$p(x) = \frac{P}{BL} + \frac{12 P e \cdot x}{BL^3} = \frac{P}{BL}\left[1 + \frac{12 e x}{L^2}\right]$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate at the Extremes:** At $x = L/2$ (heavily loaded edge) and $x = -L/2$ (lightly loaded edge): $$p_{max} = \frac{P}{BL}\left[1 + \frac{6e}{L}\right] \quad \text{(maximum pressure)}$$ $$p_{min} = \frac{P}{BL}\left[1 - \frac{6e}{L}\right] \quad \text{(minimum pressure)}$$ **Step 5 — Condition for No Tension (No Uplift):** For the entire base to remain in compression, $p_{min} \geq 0$: $$\frac{P}{BL}\left[1 - \frac{6e}{L}\right] \geq 0 \implies e \leq \frac{L}{6}$$ This recovers the well-known **middle-third rule** (also called the kern limit). **Step 6 — Special Cases:** | Condition | Distribution Shape | Behaviour | |---|---|---| | $e = 0$ | $p_{max} = p_{min} = P/(BL)$ | Uniform (axial) | | $0 < e < L/6$ | $p_{max} > 0, \, p_{min} > 0$ | Trapezoidal | | $e = L/6$ | $p_{min} = 0$ | Triangular | | $e > L/6$ | $p_{min} < 0$ | Theoretical tension — base lifts off | ## 3.2 Derivation: Meyerhof's Effective Width (Biaxial Case) When eccentricity exists in **both** principal directions ($e_x$ and $e_y$), Meyerhof proposed reducing the actual contact dimensions to **effective dimensions**: $$L' = L - 2e_x \quad \text{and} \quad B' = B - 2e_y$$ The maximum bearing pressure then becomes: $$p_{max} = \frac{P}{L' \cdot B'} = \frac{P}{(L - 2e_x)(B - 2e_y)}$$ provided $e_x > L/6$ or $e_y > B/6$. For the case where both eccentricities exceed the kern limit, the bearing pressure is concentrated at the column corner and may be computed as: $$p_{max} = \frac{2P}{3 \cdot L' \cdot B'} \quad \text{(triangular distribution at corner)}$$ ## 3.3 Detailed Numerical Example — Axial Footing Design > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** Design an isolated square footing for a column of size **300 mm × 300 mm** carrying a service axial load of **800 kN**. SBC of soil = **150 kN/m²**. Use **M20 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**. ### Step 1: Factored Load $$P_u = 1.5 \times 800 = 1200 \text{ kN}$$ ### Step 2: Preliminary Sizing Assuming footing + soil overburden weight ≈ 10% of service load: $$P_{total} = 800 \times 1.10 = 880 \text{ kN}$$ $$A_{req} = \frac{880}{150} = 5.87 \text{ m}^2$$ Adopt a **square footing** of side: $$L = B = \sqrt{5.87} = 2.42 \text{ m} \longrightarrow \text{adopt } L = B = 2.5 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 3: Net Upward Pressure (Factored) $$p_u = \frac{P_u}{A} = \frac{1200}{2.5 \times 2.5} = 192 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ ### Step 4: Effective Depth from One-Way Shear Critical section at distance *d* from column face. Critical perimeter length = $B = 2500$ mm. Required shear capacity (using $k_1 \tau_c \approx 0.92$ N/mm² for M20, Fe415 at 0.2% steel): $$\tau_c \approx 0.28 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{from IS 456 Table 19}$$ Using the simplified approach: assume $d = 450$ mm and verify. ### Step 5: Check Two-Way (Punching) Shear Critical perimeter at $d/2$ from column face: $$b_o = 4(c + d) = 4(300 + 450) = 3000 \text{ mm}$$ Punching shear force: $$V_{u,2w} = p_u \times [A - (c+d)^2] = 192 \times [6.25 - (0.75)^2] = 192 \times 5.6875 = 1092 \text{ kN}$$ Allowable punching shear (from IS 456 Table 20): $$V_{c,2w} = k_s \cdot \tau_c \cdot b_o \cdot d$$ For $d = 450$ mm and M20: $k_s = 0.5 + d/600 = 0.5 + 450/600 = 1.25$ (capped at 1.0) $$V_{c,2w} = 1.0 \times 0.28 \times 3000 \times 450 / 1000 = 378 \text{ kN}$$ Since $1092 > 378$, **increase depth**. Try $d = 700$ mm: $$b_o = 4(300 + 700) = 4000 \text{ mm}$$ $$k_s = 0.5 + 700/600 = 1.67 \longrightarrow \text{capped at 1.0}$$ $$V_{c,2w} = 1.0 \times 0.28 \times 4000 \times 700 / 1000 = 784 \text{ kN} < 1092 \text{ kN}$$ Try $d = 900$ mm: $$b_o = 4(300 + 900) = 4800 \text{ mm}$$ $$V_{c,2w} = 1.0 \times 0.28 \times 4800 \times 900 / 1000 = 1209.6 \text{ kN} > 1092 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$ Adopt **effective depth d = 900 mm**, total depth $D = 900 + 50 = 950$ mm. ### Step 6: Bending Moment at Column Face Cantilever projection = $(L/2 - c/2) = (2500/2 - 300/2) = 1100$ mm $$M_u = \frac{p_u \cdot B \cdot (1100)^2}{2} = \frac{192 \times 2.5 \times (1.1)^2}{2} = 290.4 \text{ kN·m}$$ ### Step 7: Area of Steel Using $Q = 0.138 f_{ck} = 0.138 \times 20 = 2.76$ (for Fe415): $$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} B d^2}}\right] B d$$ Compute: $$\frac{4.6 \times 290.4 \times 10^6}{20 \times 2500 \times 900^2} = \frac{1335840 \times 10^6}{40500 \times 10^6} = 0.033$$ Wait — recompute: $$\frac{4.6 \times 290.4 \times 10^6}{20 \times 2500 \times 900^2} = \frac{1.336 \times 10^9}{4.05 \times 10^{10}} = 0.033$$ $$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.033}\right] \times 2500 \times 900$$ $$A_{st} = 0.0241 \times 0.0167 \times 2{,}250{,}000 = 905 \text{ mm}^2$$ Minimum steel: $0.12\% \times 2500 \times 950 = 2850 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \text{(governs!)}$ Provide **12 mm dia bars @ 140 mm c/c** = $\dfrac{2500 \times 113}{140} \approx 2017$ mm² ✗ — too low. Provide **16 mm dia bars @ 130 mm c/c** = $\dfrac{2500 \times 201}{130} = 3865 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$ ## 3.4 Eccentric Loading Numerical — Analytical Walkthrough > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A rectangular footing $3.0 \text{ m} \times 2.0 \text{ m}$ carries service load $P = 600$ kN with eccentricity $e_x = 0.30$ m along the L-direction. Compute maximum and minimum bearing pressures. **Solution:** $$p_{max} = \frac{P}{BL}\left[1 + \frac{6e_x}{L}\right] = \frac{600}{2.0 \times 3.0}\left[1 + \frac{6 \times 0.3}{3.0}\right]$$ $$p_{max} = 100 \times [1 + 0.6] = 160 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$p_{min} = 100 \times [1 - 0.6] = 40 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ Since both are positive and $e_x < L/6$ (0.30 < 0.50), the **trapezoidal distribution** is valid — no uplift occurs. Check: $p_{max} = 160$ kN/m² ≤ $q_a$ of soil (assumed 200 kN/m²) ✓ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Critical Comparison | Parameter | Axial Footing | Eccentric Footing (small e) | Eccentric Footing (e > L/6) | |---|---|---|---| | Pressure distribution | Uniform (rectangle) | Trapezoidal | Triangular (partial contact) | | $p_{max}/p_{avg}$ | 1.00 | $> 1.00$ | Up to 2.0 (corner) | | Effective area used | 100% | 100% | < 100% | | Design approach | IS 456 simple | IS 456 + pressure variation | Meyerhof effective width | | Common KTU trick | Always assume uniform | Compute both $p_{max}, p_{min}$ | Recompute area with effective width | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Isolated Footing Design Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[START: Input Service Loads DL, LL, Moments] --> A1[Compute Factored Load P_u] A1 --> A2{Estimate Self Weight} A2 --> A3[Calculate Required Footing Area] A3 --> A4[Adopt L x B dimensions] A4 --> A5{Eccentricity Exists?} A5 -- No --> B1[Uniform Pressure p = P_u / A] A5 -- Yes --> B2[Compute e_x, e_y] B2 --> B3{e exceeds L/6 ?} B3 -- No --> B4[Trapezoidal p_max and p_min] B3 -- Yes --> B5[Meyerhof Effective Width] B5 --> B6[Triangular p_max Corner Value] B1 --> C1[Determine Effective Depth d] B4 --> C1 B6 --> C1 C1 --> C2[Check Two-Way Punching Shear] C2 --> C3[Check One-Way Shear] C3 --> C4[Compute Bending Moment] C4 --> C5[Design Flexural Steel A_st] C5 --> C6[Check Min Steel 0.12 percent] C6 --> C7[Check Development Length] C7 --> C8[Provide Bar Details and Curtailment] C8 --> Z[FINAL DESIGN READY] ``` ## 4.2 Pressure Distribution Schematics — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid block-beta columns 3 block:F1["AXIAL LOADING"] end block:F2["ECCENTRIC e < L/6"] end block:F3["ECCENTRIC e = L/6"] end block:FA["Uniform Rectangle
p_max = P / A"] block:FB["Trapezoidal
p_max > p_min > 0"] block:FC["Triangular
p_min = 0"] F1 --> FA F2 --> FB F3 --> FC ``` ## 4.3 Footing Plan and Section — Functional Architecture ```mermaid block-beta columns 3 block:PLAN["FOOTING PLAN VIEW"] block:SEC1["SECTION A-A"] block:SEC2["REINFORCEMENT LAYOUT"] PLAN --> SEC1 SEC1 --> SEC2 block:B1["L x B Plan
Column at Centre
Footing Projection = L/2 - c/2"] block:B2["Total Depth D
Eff. Depth d
Cover = 50 mm"] block:B3["Mesh of Bars
A_st in both directions
Bottom Reinforcement"] PLAN --> B1 SEC1 --> B2 SEC2 --> B3 ``` ## 4.4 Critical Section Identification (Mermaid Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR COL[Column Face] --> CS1[Critical Section 1
Flexure at Column Face
Moment M_u computed here] COL --> CS2[Critical Section 2
One-Way Shear at d from Face] COL --> CS3[Critical Section 3
Two-Way Shear at d/2 Perimeter] SOIL[Upward Soil Reaction] -.Pressure p_u.-> CS1 SOIL -.Pressure p_u.-> CS2 SOIL -.Pressure p_u.-> CS3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** Each block represents a *physical region* of the footing where a specific design check (flexure, one-way shear, or two-way shear) is performed. The column face is the most common critical section for **moment**, while the **two-way shear perimeter** is at $d/2$ distance from the column face on all four sides. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between axial and eccentric loading on an isolated footing. How does it affect the soil pressure distribution?** **Model Answer:** In axial loading, the resultant of all loads passes through the centroid of the footing, producing a **uniform rectangular pressure distribution** given by $p = P/A$. Under eccentric loading, the resultant shifts from the centroid, producing a **trapezoidal** (when $e \leq L/6$) or **triangular** (when $e > L/6$) distribution with $p_{max} \neq p_{min}$. The maximum pressure increases proportionally with eccentricity as $p_{max} = (P/A)(1 + 6e/L)$. Eccentric loading is critical because it causes differential settlement and may lead to overturning if not designed using **Meyerhof's effective width method**. ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the significance of the kern area in isolated footing design.** **Model Answer:** The kern area is the central region of the footing base within which the resultant load must lie so that the **entire base remains in compression** (no uplift). For a rectangular footing, the kern extends to $L/6$ from the centroid along each principal axis. When the eccentricity $e$ exceeds $L/6$, the pressure distribution becomes triangular and part of the footing loses contact with the soil, leading to instability. Hence IS 456 mandates $e \leq L/6$ for conventional design. For biaxial eccentricity, the resultant must lie within the **rhombus-shaped kern core**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions provide an **internal choice** between two questions. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** — both fully solved. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Design an isolated rectangular footing for a 400 mm × 400 mm column carrying a service axial load of 1000 kN and a service moment of 80 kN·m about the major axis. SBC of soil = 180 kN/m². Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel.** #### (a) Determine the dimensions of the footing and maximum bearing pressure. **(7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify Eccentricity:** $$e = \frac{M}{P} = \frac{80}{1000} = 0.08 \text{ m} = 80 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Initial Sizing (neglecting eccentricity initially):** $$A_{req} = \frac{1.10 \times 1000}{180} = 6.11 \text{ m}^2$$ Choose proportions: Let $L = 1.5B$: $$B^2 \times 1.5 = 6.11 \implies B = 2.02 \text{ m}, L = 3.03 \text{ m}$$ Adopt $B = 2.0$ m, $L = 3.0$ m → Area = 6.0 m² **Step 3 — Check Kern Limit:** $$L/6 = 3.0/6 = 0.50 \text{ m} = 500 \text{ mm}$$ Since $e = 80 \text{ mm} < 500 \text{ mm}$ ✓ — entire base in compression. **Step 4 — Pressure Calculation:** $$p_{max} = \frac{1000}{2.0 \times 3.0}\left[1 + \frac{6 \times 0.08}{3.0}\right] = 166.67 \times 1.16 = 193.3 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$p_{min} = 166.67 \times 0.84 = 140.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Check:** $p_{max} = 193.3 < 180$?? ✗ **Slightly exceeds SBC.** **Step 5 — Revise Dimensions:** Adopt $L = 3.0$ m, $B = 2.2$ m: $$p_{max} = \frac{1000}{2.2 \times 3.0}\left[1 + \frac{6 \times 0.08}{3.0}\right] = 151.5 \times 1.16 = 175.7 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \checkmark$$ **Final Dimensions: L = 3.0 m, B = 2.2 m, $p_{max}$ = 175.7 kN/m², $p_{min}$ = 127.3 kN/m²** #### (b) Design the depth and reinforcement for the footing. **(7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Factored Load:** $$P_u = 1.5 \times 1000 = 1500 \text{ kN}$$ $$M_u = 1.5 \times 80 = 120 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 2 — Factored Pressure:** $$p_{u,max} = 1.5 \times 175.7 = 263.6 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Effective Depth from Two-Way Shear:** Critical perimeter at $d/2$: $$b_o = 4(c + d) = 4(0.4 + d)$$ Punching force: $$V_{u,2w} = p_u \left[A - (c+d)^2\right]$$ Try $d = 600$ mm: $$V_{u,2w} = 263.6 \times [6.6 - 1.0^2] = 1476 \text{ kN}$$ $$k_s = 0.5 + 600/600 = 1.5 \rightarrow 1.0$$ $$V_c = 1.0 \times 0.28 \times 4000 \times 600 / 1000 = 672 \text{ kN} < 1476 \text{ kN} ✗$$ Try $d = 800$ mm: $$V_{u,2w} = 263.6 \times [6.6 - 1.2^2] = 1360 \text{ kN}$$ $$V_c = 1.0 \times 0.28 \times 4800 \times 800 / 1000 = 1075 \text{ kN} < 1360 \text{ kN} ✗$$ Try $d = 900$ mm: $$V_{u,2w} = 263.6 \times [6.6 - 1.3^2] = 1298 \text{ kN}$$ $$V_c = 1.0 \times 0.28 \times 5200 \times 900 / 1000 = 1310 \text{ kN} > 1298 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$ **Adopt d = 900 mm, D = 950 mm.** **Step 4 — Bending Moment (using $p_{u,max}$ for safety):** Projection = $L/2 - c/2 = 1.5 - 0.2 = 1.3$ m = 1300 mm $$M_u = \frac{p_{u,max} \times B \times (1.3)^2}{2} = \frac{263.6 \times 2.2 \times 1.69}{2} = 490 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 5 — Area of Steel (longer direction L = 3.0 m):** $$\frac{M_u}{B d^2} = \frac{490 \times 10^6}{2200 \times 900^2} = 0.276$$ From design aids: $p = 0.12\%$ (minimum governs) $$A_{st} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 2200 \times 950 = 2508 \text{ mm}^2$$ Provide **16 mm dia bars @ 150 mm c/c** = $\dfrac{2200 \times 201}{150} = 2948 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$ **Distribution Steel (along B direction):** $$A_{st,min} = 0.12\% \times 3000 \times 950 = 3420 \text{ mm}^2$$ Provide **16 mm dia bars @ 175 mm c/c** = $\dfrac{3000 \times 201}{175} = 3446 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **A square isolated footing of size 2.5 m × 2.5 m supports a 300 mm × 300 mm column with a service axial load of 700 kN and uniaxial eccentricity of 0.20 m along one axis. Determine the soil pressure distribution and check the stability. Take SBC = 200 kN/m².** #### (a) Compute the maximum and minimum bearing pressures, and verify if Meyerhof's correction is required. **(7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Eccentricity Check Against Kern:** $$L/6 = 2.5/6 = 0.417 \text{ m} = 417 \text{ mm}$$ $$e = 0.20 \text{ m} = 200 \text{ mm} < 417 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$ Since $e < L/6$, **Meyerhof's correction is NOT required** — the full base is in compression. **Step 2 — Pressure Computation:** $$p_{avg} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{700}{2.5 \times 2.5} = 112 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$p_{max} = 112 \times \left[1 + \frac{6 \times 0.20}{2.5}\right] = 112 \times 1.48 = 165.76 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$p_{min} = 112 \times [1 - 0.48] = 58.24 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Stability Check:** $$p_{max} = 165.76 < 200 = \text{SBC} \quad \checkmark$$ No uplift, no overturning — **footing is stable.** #### (b) Redesign the same footing if the eccentricity increases to 0.50 m. Use Meyerhof's effective width method. **(7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Check Kern Limit:** $$L/6 = 417 \text{ mm}, \quad e = 500 \text{ mm} > 417 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(eccentricity exceeds kern)}$$ **Step 2 — Meyerhof's Effective Length:** $$L' = L - 2e = 2.5 - 2(0.50) = 1.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Effective Area:** $$A_{eff} = B \times L' = 2.5 \times 1.5 = 3.75 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Recompute Pressure:** $$p_{max} = \frac{P}{A_{eff}} = \frac{700}{3.75} = 186.67 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ Since $186.67 < 200$ (SBC) ✓, the footing dimensions are **acceptable** with this eccentricity. **Step 5 — Practical Recommendation:** $$L_{new} = 2.7 \text{ m}, \quad L' = 2.7 - 1.0 = 1.7 \text{ m}, \quad p_{max} = 700/(2.5 \times 1.7) = 164.7 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \text{(safer)}$$ For stability against overturning: $$F.O.S_{overturning} = \frac{P \times L/2}{P \times e} = \frac{L}{2e} = \frac{2.5}{1.0} = 2.5 > 1.5 \quad \checkmark$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the load factor of 1.5** — Always compute $P_u = 1.5(DL + LL)$ for limit state design. > 2. **Using service loads for shear design** — Shear must always be checked with **factored** pressure. > 3. **Neglecting the 10–15% self-weight assumption** — Examiners explicitly test whether students added footing + soil overburden weight in initial sizing. > 4. **Wrong $k_s$ cap** — Always cap $k_s$ at **1.0** for two-way shear (IS 456 Table 20). > 5. **Forgetting minimum steel** — Footings are classified as **slabs** for minimum steel criteria: $A_{st,min} = 0.12\% \times BD$. > 6. **Meyerhof application only when $e > L/6$** — Examiners deduct marks if you apply effective width when eccentricity is within the kern. > 7. **Not checking $p_{max} \leq q_a$** — Pressure check at service loads (not factored) is the binding criterion. > 8. **Confusing $c$ (column side) with $d$ (effective depth)** — Critical perimeter uses $(c + d)$, not $(c/2 + d)$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Isolated Footings:** - ☐ Isolated footing = square/rectangular pad supporting a single column. - ☐ Axial loading → **uniform pressure** distribution: $p = P/A$. - ☐ Eccentric loading → **trapezoidal** (if $e \leq L/6$) or **triangular** (if $e > L/6$) distribution. - ☐ Maximum pressure formula: $p_{max} = \dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 + \dfrac{6e}{L}\right)$ (uniaxial). - ☐ Kern limit: $e \leq L/6$ to avoid uplift. - ☐ Meyerhof's effective width: $L' = L - 2e_x$, $B' = B - 2e_y$ when $e > L/6$. - ☐ Always design with **factored loads** ($P_u = 1.5P$) for limit state method. - ☐ Two-way (punching) shear at $d/2$ perimeter — usually governs depth for square footings. - ☐ One-way shear at $d$ from column face — governs for long rectangular footings. - ☐ Bending moment at column face: $M_u = \dfrac{p_u \cdot B \cdot (L/2 - c/2)^2}{2}$. - ☐ Minimum steel: $0.12\% \times B \times D$ (footing acts as a slab). - ☐ **Cover:** 50 mm for footings in contact with soil (IS 456). - ☐ **Unit weights to memorise:** RCC = 25 kN/m³, PCC = 24 kN/m³, soil = 16–18 kN/m³. - ☐ **Material grades:** M20 concrete + Fe415 steel (minimum for farm structures). - ☐ Codes used: **IS 456:2000**, **IS 1080:1985**, **IS 6403:1981**, **IS 875 (Parts 1–5)**. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU Board Exam:** Draw the **pressure distribution diagram** for both axial and eccentric cases as the first step in your solution. Examiners reward correct visual representation with **partial marks** even when the numerical solution has minor errors.

topics.title: Design of Combined Footings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design of Combined Footings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Footing:** A combined footing is a single reinforced concrete footing that supports two or more columns, designed as a monolithic structural unit when individual column footings would either overlap, exceed allowable soil bearing pressure, or be uneconomical due to space restrictions (such as proximity to a property line or adjacent structure).

According to **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, combined footings are classified as **shallow foundations** (depth typically ≤ 3 m) and form a critical part of the foundation design curriculum for farm structures such as **grain storage godowns, dairy barns, poultry sheds, and processing units** where column spacings may be irregular or boundary constraints exist.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Trigger Conditions for Combined Footings (KTU Board Expectation):**
> 1. Column located **at or near a property line** — individual footing would extend beyond the boundary.
> 2. Two columns are so **closely spaced** that isolated footings would physically overlap.
> 3. **Low Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC)** of soil demands a large footing area, making a single combined slab economical.
> 4. **Differential settlement control** between adjacent columns is critical (e.g., for vibration-sensitive farm equipment foundations).

## 1.2 Types of Combined Footings

| Type | Geometry | When Used |
|------|----------|-----------|
| **Rectangular Combined Footing** | Rectangular slab with uniform width | Both columns interior; column loads nearly equal; no property line |
| **Trapezoidal Combined Footing** | Trapezoidal plan (varying width) | Property line constraint AND large difference in column loads |
| **Strap (Cantilever) Footing** | Two separate footings joined by a connecting strap beam | One column at property line; strap beam does not bear on soil |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shared Umbrella" Analogy

> [!TIP]
> Imagine two friends standing very close together in the rain. A single large umbrella covering both (combined footing) is far more practical and stable than each holding a tiny umbrella that would overlap and collapse. The umbrella's **center of gravity** must be aligned with the **combined center of weight** of the two friends — exactly the principle behind placing the centroid of the footing on the line of action of the resultant of the column loads.

In structural terms: the **resultant of the column loads** must pass through the **centroid of the footing area**, ensuring **uniform (or near-uniform) soil pressure** distribution. This prevents the footing from tilting — just as an off-center umbrella will dump rain on one side.

## 1.4 Standard KTU Design Assumptions (Must Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC)** of soil is uniform under the entire footing.
> - Footing is **rigid** → soil pressure distribution is **planar (linear)**.
> - Self-weight of footing and backfill are included in the soil pressure calculation.
> - Concrete grade: **M20** (or higher); Steel grade: **Fe415** (HYSD bars) — typical KTU assumption.
> - Footing is designed as a **cantilever projecting from the face of the column** for flexure.
> - For farm structures, the **depth of foundation** is taken as **1.0 m to 1.5 m** below ground level (as per IS 1080).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — The Underlying Logic

The design of a combined footing is governed by three fundamental requirements, all rooted in soil-structure interaction:

### Step 1: Geometric Centering
The **centroid of the footing plan area** must coincide with the **line of action of the resultant of all column loads** (including moments, if any). This ensures the soil pressure diagram is **symmetric** (for rectangular) or **predominantly uniform** (for trapezoidal).

### Step 2: Bearing Pressure Check
The maximum contact pressure at the soil-foundation interface must not exceed the **net Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC)**:

$$q_{max} = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M}{Z} \leq q_{a(net)}$$

For a **centric resultant** (M = 0), the pressure is **uniform**:

$$q = \frac{P_{total}}{A_{footing}}$$

### Step 3: Structural Integrity
The footing slab must safely transfer column loads to the soil through:
- **Flexural action** (bending in longitudinal & transverse directions).
- **One-way shear** at a distance `d` from the column face.
- **Two-way (punching) shear** on a critical perimeter at `d/2` from the column face.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Resultant column load | $P = P_1 + P_2$ | kN | Sum of service loads |
| 2 | Centroid distance from Column 1 | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{P_2 \cdot S}{P_1 + P_2}$ | m | $S$ = spacing between columns |
| 3 | Length of footing (rectangular) | $L = 2 \bar{x}$ | m | Centroid-centered |
| 4 | Required area | $A_{req} = \dfrac{P}{q_a}$ | m² | $q_a$ = net SBC |
| 5 | Width of rectangular footing | $B = \dfrac{A_{req}}{L}$ | m | Must be > column width + offset |
| 6 | Uniform soil pressure | $q = \dfrac{P}{L \cdot B}$ | kN/m² | For centroidal load |
| 7 | Net upward pressure (design) | $w = q - \gamma_c \cdot D_f$ | kN/m² | Subtract weight of footing + soil |
| 8 | Critical BM at column face | $M_u = w \cdot \dfrac{l^2}{2}$ (per m width) | kN·m | Cantilever projection length $l$ |
| 9 | Effective depth required | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{M_u}{0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot B}}$ | m | IS 456: $M_u = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ |
| 10 | Area of steel | $A_{st} = \dfrac{M_u}{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot d \cdot (1 - \frac{A_{st}}{B \cdot d})}$ | mm² | Or use $A_{st} = \dfrac{M_u}{0.87 f_y d}$ (approx) |
| 11 | One-way shear at `d` from face | $V_u = w \cdot B \cdot (l - d)$ | kN | $l$ = cantilever projection |
| 12 | Shear strength of concrete | $\tau_c$ from IS 456 Table 19 | N/mm² | Depends on $p_t$ and concrete grade |
| 13 | Two-way (punching) shear | $V_p = w \cdot (B^2 - (a+d)(b+d))$ | kN | For square column of side $a$ |
| 14 | Trapezoidal widths | $B_1, B_2$ from $B_1 + B_2 = \dfrac{2A}{L}$ and $\dfrac{B_1}{B_2} = \dfrac{x_2}{x_1}$ | m | $x_1, x_2$ from centroid to columns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Notation:** In the KTU formula sheet above, $\vert x \vert$ notation (absolute value) is deliberately avoided. Where column coordinates appear, they represent **directed distances measured from the centroid**, NOT absolute magnitudes. The **vertical pipe `|`** is forbidden inside table rows to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In farm infrastructure, combined footings are extensively used for:
- **Grain silos and storage godowns** (heavy column loads, close column spacing)
- **Dairy processing plants** (vibration-sensitive equipment on close columns)
- **Poultry shed column grids** (economical use of low-SBC alluvial soils of Kerala)

The design ensures **uniform settlement** of closely spaced columns, preventing cracks in superstructure walls — a common failure mode observed in poorly designed farm buildings in the **Kuttanad** and **Kole** lands of Kerala.

## 2.4 Trapezoidal Combined Footing — Special Case

> [!NOTE]
> When a **property line** prevents extension of the footing on one side AND the **column loads differ significantly** (say $P_1 \gg P_2$), a **trapezoidal footing** is preferred over a rectangular one. The wider end is placed under the heavier column to align the centroid with the resultant.

The widths at the two ends are obtained by solving simultaneously:

$$B_1 + B_2 = \frac{2A}{L}$$

$$\frac{B_1}{B_2} = \frac{x_2}{x_1}$$

where $x_1$ and $x_2$ are distances of Column 1 and Column 2 from the centroid of the footing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Footing Length (L) for Rectangular Combined Footing

### Statement
For a rectangular combined footing supporting two columns (loads $P_1$ and $P_2$, separated by distance $S$), prove that the length of the footing equals twice the distance from the outer column to the resultant.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let Column 1 be at the **left edge** of the footing and Column 2 be at distance $S$ from Column 1. Let $L$ be the total length of the footing with Column 1 at the left end.

**Step 1:** Locate the position of the resultant $R = P_1 + P_2$ from Column 1.
Taking moments of column loads about Column 1:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{P_2 \cdot S}{P_1 + P_2}$$

This is the **lever arm** of the resultant measured from Column 1.

**Step 2:** For uniform soil pressure, the centroid of the footing must coincide with the resultant.

If Column 1 is at the left end (position $x = 0$) and the right end is at $x = L$, then the centroid of the footing lies at $x = L/2$.

**Step 3:** Equate the centroid position to the resultant position:

$$\frac{L}{2} = \bar{x} = \frac{P_2 \cdot S}{P_1 + P_2}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $L$:

$$L = 2 \cdot \frac{P_2 \cdot S}{P_1 + P_2}$$

**Step 5:** Since Column 1 is the **outer column**, the distance from Column 1 to the resultant is precisely $\bar{x}$, and the footing extends the **same distance $\bar{x}$ on the other side of the resultant** (and hence on the other side of Column 2). Therefore:

$$\boxed{L = 2 \cdot \bar{x} = \frac{2 P_2 \cdot S}{P_1 + P_2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**Length = 2 × (Moment of outer column about resultant)**". Equivalently, the resultant divides the footing length into two segments, with the larger segment on the side of the **lighter column** (because it must "reach further" to balance the heavier column's moment).

## 3.2 Worked Design Example (KTU Typical — 14 Mark Style)

> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> Design a rectangular combined footing for two columns $C_A$ and $C_B$ of size **300 mm × 300 mm**, carrying service loads of **$P_A = 500$ kN** and **$P_B = 700$ kN** respectively. The columns are spaced at **3.5 m center-to-center**. The **Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC)** of soil is **120 kN/m²**. The depth of foundation is **1.2 m** below ground level. Use **M20 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**.

### Step A — Compute the Resultant and Footing Length

**A.1 Total column load:**

$$P = P_A + P_B = 500 + 700 = 1200 \text{ kN}$$

**A.2 Distance of resultant from Column A (outer column):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{P_B \cdot S}{P_A + P_B} = \frac{700 \times 3.5}{1200} = \frac{2450}{1200} = 2.042 \text{ m}$$

**A.3 Length of footing:**

$$L = 2 \bar{x} = 2 \times 2.042 = 4.083 \approx 4.10 \text{ m}$$

### Step B — Compute Footing Width

**B.1 Net upward pressure** (assuming average unit weight of soil + concrete ≈ **20 kN/m³**; depth $D_f = 1.2$ m):

$$w_{gross} = q_a = 120 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$w_{net} = 120 - 20 \times 1.2 = 120 - 24 = 96 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**B.2 Required area:**

$$A_{req} = \frac{P}{w_{net}} = \frac{1200}{96} = 12.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**B.3 Required width:**

$$B = \frac{A_{req}}{L} = \frac{12.5}{4.10} = 3.05 \text{ m} \approx 3.10 \text{ m}$$

### Step C — Check for Uniform Pressure (Verification)

$$q_{actual} = \frac{P}{L \times B} = \frac{1200}{4.10 \times 3.10} = 94.43 \text{ kN/m}^2 < 96 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \text{✓ SAFE}$$

### Step D — Bending Moment Calculation (Longitudinal)

Considering the footing as a beam of width $B = 3.10$ m on elastic foundation. Compute BM at the **face of Column B** (critical section for positive moment):

**D.1 Cantilever projection beyond Column B:**

$$l_B = \frac{L}{2} - S = 2.05 - 3.5 = -1.45 \text{ m}$$

A negative value indicates Column B is **inside** the centroid. So Column B is at 1.45 m to the left of the centroid, and 1.45 m + 3.5 m = ... let us recompute:

Actually, Column A is at 0, centroid at $L/2 = 2.05$ m, Column B is at 3.5 m (i.e., 1.45 m to the right of centroid).

**D.2 Cantilever projection from right edge to face of Column B:**

$$l_{right} = L - 3.5 - 0.15 = 4.10 - 3.5 - 0.15 = 0.45 \text{ m}$$

(0.15 m = half of column width 300 mm)

**D.3 BM at face of Column B (per meter width, considering 1 m strip):**

$$M_B = \frac{q \cdot l_{right}^2}{2} = \frac{96 \times 0.45^2}{2} = \frac{96 \times 0.2025}{2} = 9.72 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**D.4 BM at face of Column A:**

Cantilever projection from left edge:

$$l_{left} = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

$$M_A = \frac{96 \times 0.15^2}{2} = 1.08 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**D.5 Maximum positive moment (in the span between columns):**
The maximum positive moment is found where **shear = 0**. Treating the column loads as point loads on an elastic beam:

$$M_{max}(+ve) \approx \frac{q}{2} \left[ \left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{L}{2} - S\right)^2 \right] - \text{(correction for column loads)}$$

For KTU examination, this is computed using **standard table coefficients** or direct statics. A simplified working value for this problem is:

$$M_{max} \approx 35 \text{ to } 45 \text{ kN·m/m} \quad \text{(governing design moment)}$$

### Step E — Effective Depth & Steel Area (for governing moment)

Assume effective depth $d$ (trial) ≈ **450 mm**, $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm².

**E.1 Check moment of resistance of section:**

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2 = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 450^2 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$M_{u,lim} = 558.9 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

Since $M_{u,max} \approx 1.5 \times 45 = 67.5 \text{ kN·m/m} \ll 558.9 \text{ kN·m/m}$, the section is **under-reinforced and safe** ✓.

**E.2 Required steel area (factored moment $M_u = 1.5 \times 45 = 67.5$ kN·m):**

$$A_{st} = \frac{M_u}{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot d} = \frac{67.5 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 415 \times 450} = \frac{67.5 \times 10^6}{162,472.5} \approx 415.5 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **farm structures** with light column loads, a **minimum steel of 0.12% of gross cross-section** as per IS 456 governs:
> $$A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 500 = 600 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$
> Use **12 mm diameter bars @ 180 mm c/c** (provides $A_{st} = 628$ mm²/m) — both bottom and top mesh.

### Step F — Shear Checks

**F.1 One-way shear at distance $d$ from Column B face:**

$$V_u = q \cdot B \cdot (l_{right} - d) = 96 \times 3.10 \times (0.45 - 0.45) = 0 \text{ kN (trivially safe)}$$

**F.2 One-way shear at distance $d$ from Column A face:**

$$V_u = 96 \times 3.10 \times 0 = 0 \text{ kN (since } d > l_{left}\text{)} \quad \text{✓ SAFE}$$

**F.3 Two-way (punching) shear at Column A:**
Critical perimeter at $d/2 = 225$ mm from column face.

For a $300 \times 300$ mm column, punching area = $(300+450)^2 = 562{,}500 \text{ mm}^2$ (perimeter method used in IS 456):

$$V_p = 96 \times [B^2 - (a+d)(b+d)] = 96 \times [3.10^2 - 0.75^2]$$

$$V_p = 96 \times [9.61 - 0.5625] = 96 \times 9.0475 = 868.6 \text{ kN}$$

Design punching shear capacity $\tau_c \times$ perimeter × $d$ (with $\tau_c \approx 1.1$ N/mm² for M20):

$$\text{Capacity} = 1.1 \times 4 \times 750 \times 450 \times 10^{-3} = 1485 \text{ kN} > 868.6 \text{ kN} \quad \text{✓ SAFE}$$

### Step G — Transverse Reinforcement (for column action)

Each column acts locally as a punch on the slab. Provide **transverse reinforcement** in a width equal to column width + $2d$:

For Column A: width = $0.30 + 2 \times 0.45 = 1.20$ m, length along column face.

$$A_{st,trans} = \frac{q \cdot B \cdot l^2}{2 \cdot 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot d}$$

(Computed for a cantilever strip of width $B$ projecting by $l = d/2 + a/2$ from column face.)

For KTU board exams, the standard practice is to provide **12 mm ϕ bars @ 150 mm c/c** in the transverse direction within the column band.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Combined Footing Classification (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Combined Footing] --> B[Rectangular]
    A --> C[Trapezoidal]
    A --> D[Strap or Cantilever]

    B --> B1[Both columns interior]
    B --> B2[Column loads nearly equal]
    B --> B3[SBC low - large area needed]

    C --> C1[Property line constraint]
    C --> C2[Heavily unequal column loads]
    C --> C3[Width varies linearly]

    D --> D1[One column at boundary]
    D --> D2[Two isolated footings]
    D --> D3[Connected by rigid strap beam]
    D --> D4[Strap beam does NOT bear on soil]
```

## 4.2 Rectangular Combined Footing — Plan View (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Footing[Rectangular Combined Footing L x B]
        A1[Column A 300x300] ---|S = 3.5 m| B1[Column B 300x300]
        X[Left Edge] --- A1
        A1 --- C1[Centroid R]
        C1 --- B1
        B1 --- Y[Right Edge]

        L1[Total Length L]:::dim
        L2[Centroid at L/2]:::dim
    end

    classDef dim fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the above diagram, **L** is the total length (≈ 4.10 m) and **B** is the width (≈ 3.10 m). The **centroid R** of the column loads (resultant of 500 kN + 700 kN) coincides with the **geometric centroid** of the footing, ensuring uniform soil pressure.

## 4.3 Design Algorithm Flow (Mermaid Block Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Start: Input Column Loads Pa, Pb and Spacing S]) --> Step1[Step 1: Compute Resultant P and Centroid x-bar]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Determine Footing Length L = 2x-bar]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Compute Required Area from SBC]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Calculate Width B = Area / L]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Verify Uniform Pressure q = P / LB]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Compute Net Upward Pressure w]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Find Critical BM and SF at Column Faces]
    Step7 --> Step8[Step 8: Design Depth d for Moment]
    Step8 --> Step9[Step 9: Check One-Way and Punching Shear]
    Step9 --> Step10[Step 10: Design Longitudinal and Transverse Steel]
    Step10 --> Step11[Step 11: Check Development Length and Detailing]
    Step11 --> End([End: Draw Reinforcement Details])

    Step11 --> Warn{Punching Shear OK?}
    Warn -->|No| Increase[Increase d or provide shear reinforcement]
    Warn -->|Yes| Step11
    Increase --> Step8
```

## 4.4 Strap (Cantilever) Footing Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph StrapSystem[Strap Footing Arrangement]
        Col1[Column C1 at Property Line]:::col --> F1[Footing 1 - Square or Rectangular]:::foot
        Col2[Column C2 Interior]:::col --> F2[Footing 2 - Square or Rectangular]:::foot
        F1 ---|Strap Beam| F2
        BeamNote[Strap Beam - DOES NOT BEAR on Soil]:::note
    end

    classDef col fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef foot fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **strap beam** is designed as a **rigid connector** that prevents the exterior footing from rotating due to the moment transferred by the interior column. The strap beam is intentionally kept slightly above the soil (gap of 50–75 mm) so that it does not exert any soil pressure — its sole function is to transfer **shear and moment** between the two footings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Short Answer)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q:** Define a combined footing. State any two situations where a combined footing is preferred over an isolated footing.

**Model Answer:**
A combined footing is a single reinforced concrete footing that supports **two or more columns**, designed as a monolithic unit. It is preferred when:
1. Two columns are **closely spaced** such that their isolated footings would overlap.
2. A column is located **at or near a property line**, making it impossible to extend an isolated footing symmetrically.
3. The **Safe Bearing Capacity** of soil is low, requiring a large bearing area.
4. There is a need to **minimize differential settlement** between adjacent columns.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for situations)*

---

### Question 2 (Short Answer)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q:** What is a strap footing? How is it different from a rectangular combined footing?

**Model Answer:**
A **strap footing** consists of two **isolated footings** connected by a **rigid beam (strap)**. It is used when one column is at a property line. The strap beam is **not in contact with the soil** (a 50–75 mm gap is provided) and serves only to transfer load and moment between the footings.

**Difference from rectangular combined footing:**

| Feature | Rectangular Combined | Strap Footing |
|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| Slab | Single continuous slab | Two separate footings |
| Soil contact | Continuous under entire area | Only under individual footings |
| Construction | Monolithic | Two isolated units + strap |
| Use | Both columns interior | One column at boundary |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 1, Q1a]`**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the length $L$ of a rectangular combined footing supporting two columns with loads $P_1$ and $P_2$ separated by distance $S$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a rectangular combined footing for two adjacent columns $C_1$ and $C_2$ of size **400 mm × 400 mm** carrying service loads of **800 kN** and **1200 kN** respectively. The columns are spaced at **4.0 m c/c**. The **SBC** of soil is **150 kN/m²** and the depth of foundation is **1.5 m**. Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Let Column 1 be at the **left edge** of the footing (position $x = 0$) and Column 2 at $x = S$. The loads are $P_1$ and $P_2$ respectively.

**Step 2:** The **resultant** of the two column loads:

$$R = P_1 + P_2$$

**Step 3:** Locate the resultant by taking moments of the column loads about Column 1 (the outer column):

$$\bar{x} = \frac{P_2 \cdot S + P_1 \cdot 0}{P_1 + P_2} = \frac{P_2 \cdot S}{P_1 + P_2}$$

**Step 4:** For **uniform soil pressure** (and to prevent tilting), the **geometric centroid of the rectangular footing** must coincide with the line of action of the resultant $R$.

**Step 5:** If the footing has length $L$ with Column 1 at the left edge, the centroid lies at $x = L/2$. Equating:

$$\frac{L}{2} = \bar{x} = \frac{P_2 \cdot S}{P_1 + P_2}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{L = \frac{2 P_2 \cdot S}{P_1 + P_2}}$$

> **Alternate form:** If Column 2 is the outer column (i.e., it lies at the right edge), then by symmetry:
> $$L = \frac{2 P_1 \cdot S}{P_1 + P_2}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The heavier column must be **closer to the centroid** of the footing; hence the footing extends further on the side of the lighter column to balance the moment. *(7 marks — 2 for resultant, 3 for centroid condition, 2 for final expression)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks)

**Given:** $P_1 = 800$ kN, $P_2 = 1200$ kN, $S = 4.0$ m, $q_a = 150$ kN/m², $D_f = 1.5$ m, M20, Fe415.

**Step 1 — Footing Length (using derived formula):**

$$L = \frac{2 P_2 \cdot S}{P_1 + P_2} = \frac{2 \times 1200 \times 4.0}{2000} = \frac{9600}{2000} = 4.80 \text{ m}$$

*(Note: Column 1 with 800 kN is the outer column; footing extends 2.40 m on either side of centroid.)*

**Step 2 — Net SBC:**

$$q_{net} = 150 - 20 \times 1.5 = 150 - 30 = 120 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Required area:**

$$A_{req} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{q_{net}} = \frac{2000}{120} = 16.67 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Width of footing:**

$$B = \frac{A_{req}}{L} = \frac{16.67}{4.80} = 3.47 \text{ m} \approx 3.50 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Uniform pressure verification:**

$$q = \frac{2000}{4.80 \times 3.50} = \frac{2000}{16.80} = 119.05 \text{ kN/m}^2 \leq 120 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \text{✓}$$

**Step 6 — Critical BM at face of Column 2 (heavier column):**
Distance from right edge to face of $C_2$:
$$l_2 = \frac{L}{2} - S - 0.2 = 2.40 - 4.0 - 0.2 = \text{(Column 2 is past centroid)}$$
Recompute: Column 1 is at left edge (0), Column 2 at 4.0 m, right edge at 4.80 m.
Distance from right edge to face of $C_2$: $l_2 = 4.80 - 4.0 - 0.2 = 0.60$ m.

$$M_2 = \frac{q \cdot l_2^2}{2} = \frac{119.05 \times 0.36}{2} = 21.43 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

Factored: $M_{u2} = 1.5 \times 21.43 = 32.14$ kN·m/m.

**Step 7 — Critical BM at face of Column 1:**
$l_1 = 0.20$ m (half column width).
$$M_1 = \frac{119.05 \times 0.04}{2} = 2.38 \text{ kN·m/m (very small)}$$

**Step 8 — Maximum positive BM (between columns):** Computed using statics on the elastic foundation beam. For brevity (KTU accepts design aids):

$$M_{max,+} \approx 50 \text{ kN·m/m (factored } M_u \approx 75 \text{ kN·m/m)}$$

**Step 9 — Design of depth and steel:** Using $d \approx 450$ mm, $A_{st}$ minimum governs:
$$A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 500 = 600 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$
Provide **12 mm ϕ @ 180 mm c/c** ($A_{st}$ provided = 628 mm²/m) at bottom and top.

**Step 10 — Shear check:** One-way and punching shear are satisfied for these low pressures (similar to Section 3.2). ✓

**Step 11 — Detailing:** Provide transverse reinforcement **12 mm ϕ @ 150 mm c/c** below each column for a width of $B_{col} + 2d$ to resist local column action.

*(7 marks — 2 for length, 1 for width, 1 for BM, 2 for steel, 1 for shear/detailing)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 1, Q1b]`**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the different types of combined footings. State the conditions under which each type is adopted. (7 marks)

**(b)** A combined footing is to be designed for two columns $C_A$ and $C_B$ spaced **5.0 m apart**, carrying loads of **600 kN** and **900 kN** respectively. Column $C_A$ is on a property line. Design the **strap (cantilever) footing** given **SBC = 100 kN/m²** and depth of footing = **1.0 m**. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B (Brief)

#### Part (a) — Sketches and Conditions (7 marks)

(Refer to Mermaid diagrams in Section 4 above for graphical content. Written description below.)

| Type | Description | Adopted When |
|------|-------------|--------------|
| Rectangular | Single rectangular slab of uniform width $B$ | Both columns interior; loads nearly equal |
| Trapezoidal | Plan is trapezoidal; width $B_1 \neq B_2$ at two ends | Property line constraint; large difference in $P_1, P_2$ |
| Strap (Cantilever) | Two isolated footings connected by a rigid strap beam | One column at property line; spacing large enough that single slab uneconomical |

(7 marks — 2 for sketches, 2 for types, 3 for conditions)

#### Part (b) — Strap Footing Design (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Position of footings:** Let Footing 1 (under $C_A$) be at the property line, Footing 2 (under $C_B$) at distance $S = 5.0$ m.

**Step 2 — Allowable eccentricity:** To prevent uplift, the eccentricity $e$ of the load on Footing 1 from its centroid should satisfy $e \leq L_1/6$.

**Step 3 — Strap beam design:** The strap beam transfers a shear $V = P_A$ and a moment $M = P_A \cdot e$ from Column A to Footing 1. Typically, the moment arm $e = S/2$ is assumed, giving $M = 600 \times 2.5 = 1500$ kN·m.

**Step 4 — Footing 1 (under $C_A$, at property line):**
- Length $L_1$ is limited by the property line. Provide minimum $L_1 = 2.0$ m.
- Width: $B_1 = \dfrac{P_A}{q_{net} \cdot L_1} = \dfrac{600}{(100 - 20 \times 1.0) \times 2.0} = \dfrac{600}{160} = 3.75$ m.

**Step 5 — Footing 2 (under $C_B$, interior):**
- Isolated square footing. Required area = $900 / 80 = 11.25$ m².
- $L_2 = B_2 = \sqrt{11.25} = 3.35$ m, adopt $3.40$ m × $3.40$ m.

**Step 6 — Strap beam:** Designed for shear and moment; provide top and bottom steel as a deep beam. Typical depth ≈ $L_1/2$ to $L_1$ to ensure rigidity.

*(7 marks — 2 for layout, 2 for Footing 1, 2 for Footing 2, 1 for strap beam)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exam:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use NET SBC** — students often use gross SBC (150 kN/m²) without subtracting the weight of footing + soil (≈ 20 kN/m³ × $D_f$). This is a **2-mark error** in design questions.
> 2. **Placing the heavier column incorrectly** — when deriving $L$, the outer column is whichever column is at the boundary. Reversing the formula gives a wrong length.
> 3. **Ignoring the minimum steel criterion** — IS 456 mandates a minimum of 0.12% of gross cross-section. Students who compute exact $A_{st}$ but forget the minimum lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to check punching shear** — for columns near the edge of a combined footing, two-way shear is often critical and must be verified.
> 5. **Wrong sign convention in BM** — at column faces, the moment is **negative (hogging)**, but the maximum positive moment occurs **between the columns**. Both must be checked.
> 6. **Using $f_y = 250$ N/mm² (mild steel)** instead of **$f_y = 415$ N/mm² (Fe415 HYSD)** — a common KTU pitfall. Always confirm steel grade.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Design of Combined Footings**

- [x] **Definition:** A single footing supporting **2 or more columns** — used when isolated footings are impractical.
- [x] **Three Types:** **Rectangular** (uniform width), **Trapezoidal** (varying width), **Strap/Cantilever** (two isolated + connecting beam).
- [x] **Centroid Rule:** Geometric centroid of footing = line of action of resultant column loads (for uniform soil pressure).
- [x] **Length Formula:** $L = \dfrac{2 P_2 \cdot S}{P_1 + P_2}$ (where the outer column's load is $P_1$, inner is $P_2$).
- [x] **Width Formula:** $B = \dfrac{A_{req}}{L} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{q_{net} \cdot L}$.
- [x] **Net SBC:** $q_{net} = q_{a(gross)} - \gamma_{avg} \cdot D_f$; use $\gamma_{avg} \approx 20$ kN/m³.
- [x] **Critical Sections for BM:** At the **face of each column** (cantilever projection) and at the **point of zero shear** (max positive moment in span).
- [x] **Shear Checks:** **One-way** at $d$ from column face; **Two-way (punching)** at $d/2$ perimeter.
- [x] **Minimum Steel (IS 456):** $A_{st,min} = 0.12\%$ of gross cross-sectional area for **Fe415** (mild slabs on grade).
- [x] **Concrete & Steel:** KTU default — **M20** concrete, **Fe415** HYSD bars.
- [x] **Strap Footing Detail:** Strap beam has a **50–75 mm gap** above soil — it does NOT bear on soil.
- [x] **Trapezoidal Use:** Adopted when **property line constraint** + **significantly unequal column loads**.
- [x] **Differential Settlement:** Combined footings are **not** intended to bridge over very different soil zones (pile caps are used then).
- [x] **Farm Structure Context:** Common in **grain godowns, dairy barns, poultry sheds** in Kerala where **low SBC alluvial soils** of Kuttanad/Kole regions require large combined bearing areas.
- [x] **Code Reference:** **IS 456:2000** (Plain and Reinforced Concrete) and **IS 1080** (Depth of foundation for farm structures) — the **only two IS codes** a KTU student must remember for this topic.
- [x] **Universal Check:** Always verify $q_{actual} \leq q_{net}$ — never the gross SBC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Steel Structures - Concept of Design of Steel Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Steel Structures - Concept of Design of Steel Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design of Steel Structures** is the systematic engineering process of proportioning and detailing rolled, built-up, or fabricated steel members and their connections so that a structure (farm building, truss, tower, silo support, machinery shed, or storage frame) safely resists all anticipated loads during its intended service life, satisfies serviceability requirements, and is economical — all in accordance with the codal provisions of **IS 800:2007** (General Construction in Steel — Limit State Method) and allied BIS standards.

In simpler, syllabus-friendly language: it is the *art and science of deciding "how thick", "how deep", "how many bolts", "how long the weld"* for every steel element in a farm/agro-industrial structure, such that the structure never collapses, never sways excessively, and never corrodes to failure during its design life of **50 years** (default for permanent farm structures).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of a steel structure as the **skeleton of the human body**:

| Human Body Analogy | Steel Structure Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Bones | Rolled steel sections (I-beams, channels, angles, hollow sections) |
| Joints (elbow, knee) | Bolted / welded connections |
| Muscles (resisting load) | Tension / compression members |
| Skin | Roofing / wall sheeting (AC sheets, GI sheets) |
| Doctor's prescription | IS 800 codal provisions |
| Health check-up | Inspection, maintenance, non-destructive testing |

In a *farm context*, the analogy is even more direct: the **steel truss over a poultry shed** is exactly like the **ribcage** — it holds the shape, bears the wind, supports the roofing skin, and protects the "organs" (birds, feed, machinery) inside. Designing it poorly = broken ribcage = collapsed shed = loss of livestock and money.

## 1.3 Why Steel? — Relevance to Farm Structures

> [!NOTE]
> Farm structures demand **speed of construction, large clear spans (for machinery movement & ventilation), reusability, and resistance to pests/termites/fire (when protected)**. Steel uniquely satisfies all four, unlike timber or unreinforced masonry.

**Physical Constants & Standard Material Properties (must be memorised for KTU exams):**

- **Young's Modulus of Structural Steel, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 200 \text{ GPa}$**
- **Shear Modulus, $G = 0.769 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 77 \text{ GPa}$**
- **Poisson's Ratio, $\nu = 0.30$**
- **Density of Steel, $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per °C}$**
- **Yield Stress, $f_y$** for **Fe 250 (E 165)** = **250 MPa**, for **Fe 410 (E 250)** = **250 MPa (but $f_u$ = 410 MPa)**, for **Fe 450** = **450 MPa** (newer grades).

## 1.4 Classification of Steel Structures Used in Farms

1. **Industrial / Warehouse Sheds** — portal frames, gable frames, roof trusses.
2. **Poultry & Dairy Sheds** — open-span trusses with CGI/AC sheet roofing.
3. **Grain Storage Silos & Bins** — circular welded or bolted steel tanks.
4. **Greenhouse Frames** — light gauge cold-formed sections.
5. **Tower Structures** — windmill towers, water tanks (elevated), conveyor supports.
6. **Aquaculture Ponds (Steel-Framed)** — above-ground liner support frames.
