# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Evolution of Design Philosophy

Steel design has undergone three major philosophical shifts globally. KTU (and IS 800) follows the **Limit State Method**, but conceptual questions frequently ask for a comparison, so all three must be understood.

### 2.1.1 Working Stress Method (WSM) — The "Old Guard"

- Assumes structure behaves **elastically** throughout its life.
- Permissible stress = Yield stress / **Factor of Safety (FoS)**.
- Typical FoS for steel = **1.67** (so permissible = $f_y / 1.67$).
- Used in old IS 800:1984. Still used in some pipe & tank codes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a rope that can hold 100 kg. If you put a Factor of Safety of 2, you only ever load it with 50 kg. WSM is exactly this — it artificially limits how much stress the steel is "allowed" to feel.

### 2.1.2 Ultimate Load Method (ULM) / Load Factor Method

- Computes ultimate (collapse) load by plastic analysis.
- Working load $\times$ Load Factor = Ultimate load.
- Load Factor (steel) = **1.7** (approx).
- Bridged the gap between WSM and LSM but never became a full BIS code for buildings.

### 2.1.3 Limit State Method (LSM) — The "Modern Standard" (IS 800:2007)

Two limit states are checked:

1. **Ultimate Limit State (ULS):** Collapse, buckling, fracture, loss of equilibrium.
2. **Serviceability Limit State (SLS):** Deflection, vibration, drift, crack (for composite), corrosion durability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design philosophy check:** For every member, we must satisfy BOTH $R_d \geq S_d$ (at ULS) AND serviceability criteria. The design is governed by the more critical condition.

## 2.2 The Core Design Equation (LSM)

$$R_d \geq S_d$$

Where:
- $R_d$ = Design strength of the member/connection = $\dfrac{R_n}{\gamma_m}$
- $S_d$ = Design action (load effect) = $\gamma_f \times Q_k$
- $R_n$ = Nominal (characteristic) strength from codal formula
- $\gamma_m$ = Partial safety factor for material
- $\gamma_f$ = Partial safety factor for load
- $Q_k$ = Characteristic load (from IS 875 series)

## 2.3 Partial Safety Factors (KTU High-Yield)

| Load Combination | $\gamma_f$ for DL | $\gamma_f$ for LL | $\gamma_f$ for WL/EL |
|---|---|---|---|
| DL + LL | 1.5 | 1.5 | — |
| DL + WL | 1.5 | — | 1.5 |
| DL + LL + WL | 1.2 | 1.2 | 1.2 |
| DL + LL + EL | 1.2 | 1.0 | 1.2 (EL) |

For farm structures, **Earthquake (EL)** is usually only relevant for very large silos/towers in seismic zones III–V. **Wind Load (WL)** is the dominant lateral load for most open farm sheds.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **minimum formula set** that any KTU board examiner expects you to reproduce or apply for "Concept of Design of Steel Structures" questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | N/mm², mm/mm | Linear elastic range |
| 2 | Strain at yield | $\varepsilon_y = f_y / E$ | — | For Fe 410: ≈ 0.00125 |
| 3 | Poisson effect | $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$ | — | Lateral contraction |
| 4 | Thermal stress | $\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | N/mm² | When fully restrained |
| 5 | WSM permissible stress | $f_{perm} = f_y / FoS$ | N/mm² | FoS ≈ 1.67 |
| 6 | LSM design strength | $R_d = R_n / \gamma_m$ | N/mm² | $\gamma_m$ = 1.10 (bolts), 1.25 (welds) |
| 7 | LSM design load | $S_d = \gamma_f \cdot Q_k$ | kN or N | Combinations in §2.3 |
| 8 | Slenderness ratio (column) | $\lambda = L_{eff} / r_{min}$ | — | $\lambda_{max}$ = 180 (IS 800) |
| 9 | Radius of gyration | $r = \sqrt{I / A}$ | mm | $I$ = MOA, $A$ = area |
| 10 | Effective length factor | $L_{eff} = K \cdot L$ | mm | K from end conditions |
| 11 | Net area (with bolt hole) | $A_n = A_g - n \cdot d_h \cdot t$ | mm² | $d_h$ = hole dia, $t$ = thickness |
| 12 | Buckling load (Euler) | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_{eff}^2$ | N | Long columns only |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Farm Sector

- **Grain silo design:** Uses LSM with **bulk grain pressure (Janssen's equation)** as the characteristic load; partial safety factors convert fluctuating grain moisture load into design load.
- **Greenhouse frames:** Light gauge cold-formed steel design uses **effective width method** — a specialisation of LSM.
- **Poultry truss:** Governed usually by **wind uplift** (negative pressure) rather than gravity load — a classic case where sign convention and load combination matter.
- **Dairy shed portal frame:** Serviceability deflection limit of **L/180** for purlins (IS 800) prevents sheet cracking.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between WSM and LSM Safety Factor

WSM uses a single global **Factor of Safety (FoS)**, while LSM uses **partial safety factors** for loads ($\gamma_f$) and materials ($\gamma_m$). We can relate them as follows.

**Starting Point — WSM definition:**

$$\sigma_{permissible} = \frac{f_y}{FoS}$$

For a simple axially loaded tension member, stress = Load / Area. So:

$$\frac{Q_k}{A} \leq \frac{f_y}{FoS}$$

**Rearranging to get the WSM Area:**

$$A_{WSM} = \frac{FoS \cdot Q_k}{f_y}$$

**Now the LSM Design Equation:**

$$R_d \geq S_d \implies \frac{f_y}{\gamma_m} \cdot A \geq \gamma_f \cdot Q_k$$

**Rearranging to get the LSM Area:**

$$A_{LSM} = \frac{\gamma_f \cdot \gamma_m \cdot Q_k}{f_y}$$

**Equating the Two Areas** (to find the equivalent overall FoS in LSM):

$$FoS_{LSM,equiv} = \gamma_f \cdot \gamma_m$$

**Substituting typical LSM values** (DL+LL): $\gamma_f = 1.5$, $\gamma_m = 1.10$:

$$FoS_{LSM,equiv} = 1.5 \times 1.10 = 1.65$$

This is why LSM gives designs **almost identical to WSM** for ordinary cases — it is a *probabilistic refinement*, not a fundamentally different approach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam insight:** When a KTU question asks "Compare WSM and LSM", quote this derivation. It is the single most-scored line in any design-philosophy question.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Strain Compatibility and Thermal Stress in a Restrained Farm Member

A steel purlin of length $L$ is welded between two rigid supports (typical in a poultry-shed truss). Temperature rises by $\Delta T$. Find the induced thermal stress.

**Step 1 — Free thermal expansion (if unrestrained):**

$$\delta_{thermal} = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Corresponding free strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3 — Since supports are rigid, total displacement = 0.** So the member must compress by an equal amount, inducing a compressive strain $-\varepsilon_{thermal}$:

$$\varepsilon_{mechanical} + \varepsilon_{thermal} = 0$$

$$\varepsilon_{mechanical} = -\alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical strain converts to stress via Hooke's Law:**

$$\sigma_T = E \cdot \varepsilon_{mechanical} = -E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude (compressive):**

$$\vert \sigma_T \vert = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (Kerala farm context):**

Let $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $\Delta T = 40$ °C (typical day-night variation under AC sheet roofing in Kerala summer):

$$\vert \sigma_T \vert = (2 \times 10^5) \times (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 40$$

$$\vert \sigma_T \vert = 96 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Since $f_y$ for Fe 410 = 250 MPa, this 96 MPa is about **38% of yield** — a substantial contribution, justifying why IS 800 mandates **expansion joints** in long farm sheds (>45 m).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Net Sectional Area for a Tension Member with Bolt Holes

A farm truss diagonal is a flat $80 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$ bar connected by 2 bolts of 20 mm diameter through 22 mm holes. Find the net area.

**Step 1 — Gross area:**

$$A_g = 80 \times 10 = 800 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Hole diameter for area deduction** (per IS 800): hole dia + 1.5 mm (for damage) but conventionally, use **clear hole diameter**:

$$d_h = 22 \text{ mm (assumed; adjust if not given)}$$

**Step 3 — Area deducted per hole:**

$$A_{deducted} = d_h \times t = 22 \times 10 = 220 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — For 2 bolts in a staggered pattern perpendicular to load:**

$$A_n = A_g - n \cdot d_h \cdot t$$

$$A_n = 800 - 2 \times 22 \times 10 = 800 - 440 = 360 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Design strength in tension (governed by yielding of gross section or fracture of net section — IS 800 checks BOTH):**

$$T_{dg} = A_g \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0} = 800 \times 250 / 1.10 \approx 181{,}818 \text{ N} = 181.8 \text{ kN}$$

$$T_{dn} = \alpha \cdot A_n \cdot f_u / \gamma_{m1} = 0.8 \times 360 \times 410 / 1.25 \approx 94{,}464 \text{ N} = 94.5 \text{ kN}$$

Governing strength = **94.5 kN** (fracture at net section controls).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $\alpha = 0.8$ for **2 or more bolts**; $\alpha = 0.9$ for **1 bolt**. The factor $\gamma_{m1}$ for ultimate stress = **1.25**, while $\gamma_{m0}$ for yield = **1.10**. This is a favourite KTU numerical trap.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Design Philosophy

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Routine farm shed (truss, purlin) | LSM (IS 800:2007) | Mandatory per BIS |
| Old existing structure re-analysis | WSM (IS 800:1984) | Match original design era |
| Plastic design of portal frame | ULM / Plastic Analysis | Easier moment redistribution |
| Cold-formed light gauge member | LSM with effective width | IS 801 / IS 800 Part 9 |
| Silo under granular pressure | LSM with Janssen + IS 875 Part 5 | Bulk material is a special load |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Working Stress Method (WSM) and Limit State Method (LSM) of design of steel structures.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | WSM | LSM |
|---|---|---|
| Philosophy | Elastic design | Probabilistic semi-empirical design |
| Safety | Single global Factor of Safety (≈1.67) | Partial safety factors: $\gamma_f$ on load, $\gamma_m$ on material |
| Stress | Permissible stress = $f_y$/FoS | Design strength = $f_y$/$\gamma_m$ |
| Limit states | Only strength (elastic) | ULS + SLS (deflection, vibration) |
| Economy | Slightly over-conservative | More realistic, economical |
| Code | IS 800:1984 (withdrawn) | IS 800:2007 (current BIS) |

**Memory line:** *WSM = one number governs safety; LSM = separate factors for load and material realism.*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: List any four advantages of steel as a structural material for farm buildings.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — enables long clear spans (e.g., 30 m poultry shed without intermediate columns).
2. **Speed of construction** — bolted connections allow assembly in days, vital for monsoon-affected Kerala farm projects.
3. **Reusability & recyclability** — dismantled farm sheds can be relocated (unlike RCC).
4. **Termite & rot-proof** — unlike timber, immune to biological degradation; ideal for grain storage where pest control is critical.
5. **Predictable quality** — factory-rolled sections have guaranteed $f_y$ and $f_u$ per IS 2062.
6. **Ductility** — gives warning before collapse (large visible deflections), saving lives and livestock.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For "Concept" questions, **theory marks dominate (≈8/14)**. Do **not** skip philosophy comparisons. Always draw a labelled diagram (even a hand-drawn block diagram) — it earns 2 easy marks. State IS code clause numbers wherever possible. Avoid the trap of writing only formulas without *physical meaning*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **Limit State Method** of design of steel structures as per IS 800:2007. Discuss the various limit states to be considered. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A flat steel tie member in a farm roof truss is **80 mm wide × 12 mm thick**, connected by **4 nos. of 16 mm diameter bolts** in a single row. Hole diameter = **18 mm**. Steel grade is **Fe 410**. Determine the **design tensile strength** of the member as per IS 800:2007. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definition:**
The Limit State Method is a design philosophy in which a structure is proportioned so that it does not reach any of its **limit states** during its intended design life under the most adverse combination of loads.

**Step 2 — Classification of Limit States (IS 800:2007, Clause 4.3):**

1. **Ultimate Limit States (ULS)** — correspond to collapse or similar failure:
   - Loss of equilibrium (overturning, sliding)
   - Failure by rupture, excessive deformation
   - Buckling (flexural, torsional, lateral-torsional)
   - Fatigue failure (for crane girders, machinery supports)

2. **Serviceability Limit States (SLS)** — correspond to conditions beyond which specified service requirements are no longer met:
   - Excessive deflection (e.g., L/180 for purlins)
   - Excessive vibration (footbridge / working platform floors)
   - Repairable damage (cracking, local buckling)
   - Corrosion & durability limits

**Step 3 — Design Criterion:**
$$R_d = \dfrac{R_n}{\gamma_m} \geq S_d = \gamma_f \cdot Q_k$$

For **every member** and **every connection**, both ULS and SLS checks must be satisfied.

**Step 4 — Partial Safety Factors for Material ($\gamma_m$) — IS 800 Table 5:**

| Condition | $\gamma_{m0}$ (yielding) | $\gamma_{m1}$ (ultimate) |
|---|---|---|
| Shop-fabricated, reasonable QC | 1.10 | 1.25 |
| Site-fabricated, poor QC | 1.25 | 1.50 |

**Step 5 — Why LSM is superior for farm structures:**
- Farm loads are **highly variable** (grain weight changes with moisture; live loads vary with stocking density). LSM's load-factor approach handles this probabilistically.
- Wind uplift on a Kerala poultry shed during monsoon is the dominant ULS case — WSM would not capture the partial safety on negative wind correctly.

*(End of Part (a))* [7 marks]

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Plate: 80 mm × 12 mm
- 4 bolts of 16 mm dia, holes = 18 mm
- Steel: Fe 410 → $f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa

**Step 1 — Gross Area:**

$$A_g = 80 \times 12 = 960 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Net Area (all 4 holes across critical section):**

$$A_n = A_g - n \cdot d_h \cdot t = 960 - 4 \times 18 \times 12 = 960 - 864 = 96 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Design Strength Against Yielding of Gross Section (IS 800 Cl 6.1):**

$$T_{dg} = \dfrac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{960 \times 250}{1.10}$$

$$T_{dg} = 218{,}182 \text{ N} \approx 218.18 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Design Strength Against Rupture at Net Section (IS 800 Cl 6.3.1):**

For 4 bolts, the **shear lag factor** $\alpha = 0.8$ (since it's connected through one leg/element only; if connection is by both legs, $\alpha = 1.0$).

$$T_{dn} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}} = \dfrac{0.8 \times 96 \times 410}{1.25}$$

$$T_{dn} = \dfrac{31{,}488}{1.25} = 25{,}190 \text{ N} \approx 25.19 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Block Shear Check (optional but good practice for KTU):**

$$T_{db} = \dfrac{A_{vg} \cdot f_y / \sqrt{3}}{\gamma_{m0}} + \dfrac{A_{tn} \cdot 0.9 \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$$

(Computed separately; will exceed 25 kN, so rupture governs.)

**Step 6 — Governing Design Strength:**

$$T_d = \min(T_{dg}, T_{dn}) = \min(218.18, 25.19) = \boxed{25.19 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students forget to deduct **all 4 holes** and obtain an inflated $A_n$. Also, students frequently use $\gamma_m = 1.10$ for the rupture check instead of $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ — this **loses 1 full mark**.

*(End of Part (b))* [7 marks]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the various **loads and load combinations** to be considered in the design of a farm steel structure as per IS 875. Why is wind load critical for open farm sheds in Kerala? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **stress-strain behaviour of structural steel**. Indicate the significance of yield plateau and strain hardening. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Classification of Loads (IS 875 series):**

| IS Code | Load Type | Typical Magnitude for Farm Shed |
|---|---|---|
| IS 875 Part 1 | Dead Load (DL) | Self-weight + sheeting ≈ 0.15–0.20 kN/m² |
| IS 875 Part 2 | Imposed / Live Load (LL) | 0.75 kN/m² (light maintenance access) |
| IS 875 Part 3 | Wind Load (WL) | Kerala basic speed 39 m/s (Zone III revised), up to 50 m/s coastal |
| IS 875 Part 4 | Snow Load | Negligible in Kerala |
| IS 875 Part 5 | Special Loads | Grain pressure, livestock impact, silo discharge |
| IS 1893 | Earthquake Load (EL) | For silos and tall towers in seismic zones |

**Step 2 — Load Combinations (IS 800:2007, Table 4):**

$$1.5 (DL + LL)$$
$$1.5 (DL + WL)$$
$$1.2 (DL + LL \pm WL)$$
$$1.5 (DL \pm EL)$$
$$0.9 DL \pm 1.5 WL \text{ (overturning check)}$$

**Step 3 — Why Wind Dominates for Kerala Farm Sheds:**
- Kerala's coastal geography exposes open farm sheds to **high wind zones (39–50 m/s)**.
- Open-sided poultry/dairy sheds have **high permeability**, causing **internal pressure suction** combined with external uplift.
- Roof sheeting can experience **uplift = 1.5 × external suction coefficient × design wind pressure**, easily exceeding dead load — meaning **bolts would lift off** unless specifically designed.
- Monsoon winds come with rain, adding **ponding load** (water collected in roof sag) — this can double the uplift scenario.

**Step 4 — Recommended Reference Wind Pressure at Kerala Coast (Kasaragod to Alappuzha):**

$$p_d = 0.6 \cdot V_b^2 = 0.6 \cdot 50^2 = 1500 \text{ N/m}^2 = 1.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

*(End of Part (a))* [7 marks]

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Sketch (already in Section 4.3). Describe verbally:**

The stress-strain curve of mild steel (Fe 410) consists of **four distinct regions**:

1. **Region I — Linear Elastic (0 → A):** Stress proportional to strain, slope = Young's Modulus $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². Hooke's law strictly valid. Strain at A (proportional limit) ≈ 0.0001.

2. **Region II — Elastic-Perfectly Plastic / Yield Plateau (A → B):** Stress remains nearly constant at $f_y$ while strain increases from ≈ 0.001 to ≈ 0.012 (12 times yield strain). This plateau is unique to mild steel and is what makes **plastic design possible**.

3. **Region III — Strain Hardening (B → C):** After the plateau, the material hardens; stress rises again up to $f_u$ (ultimate tensile strength). Strain at C ≈ 0.15–0.20.

4. **Region IV — Necking and Fracture (C → D):** Cross-section reduces locally ("neck"), true stress continues to rise but engineering stress drops. Failure occurs at point D.

**Step 2 — Significance of Yield Plateau:**

- Indicates **ductility** — material gives visible warning before collapse.
- Enables **plastic hinge formation** in indeterminate structures (e.g., portal frames of farm sheds), allowing **moment redistribution** and hence more economical design.
- Provides **seismic energy dissipation** through yield — vital for farm silos in seismic zones.

**Step 3 — Significance of Strain Hardening:**

- Provides **reserve strength** beyond yield — design codes use $f_u$ (ultimate) for fracture checks.
- Strain hardening ratio $f_u / f_y$ should be ≥ **1.2** for ductile steel (IS 2062 mandates this).

**Step 4 — Why This Matters for Farm Design:**

- For a **ductile steel** member, local buckling is allowed (capacity design principle) — the structure "sheds" load to alternative paths.
- For a **brittle steel** (low $f_u/f_y$ ratio), no warning, no redistribution — failure is sudden and catastrophic. IS 800 **prohibits** use of brittle steel.

*(End of Part (b))* [7 marks]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Forgetting $\gamma_{m1} \neq \gamma_{m0}$:**
> Most students use $\gamma_m = 1.10$ for *both* yield and ultimate checks. IS 800 mandates **1.25 for ultimate** (rupture) — losing this distinction loses **1 mark** in every numerical.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 — Wind Uplift = positive force (not negative):**
> On farm sheds, wind suction on roof produces **uplift**, which acts *opposite* to gravity dead load. Many students subtract wrongly: use $0.9 DL - 1.5 WL$ combination explicitly. Watch the sign.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 — Shear Lag Factor $\alpha$ confusion:**
> For a flat plate connected by bolts through one leg only, $\alpha = 0.8$. For welded end or both-leg connection, $\alpha = 1.0$. Skipping $\alpha$ entirely gives inflated capacity — examiner will deduct 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must be at your fingertip during the KTU exam):**

- **Steel — Young's Modulus:** $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² (universal for all structural steel).
- **Density of Steel:** $7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (use $78.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$ for unit weight).
- **Fe 410 properties:** $f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa.
- **LSM master equation:** $R_d \geq S_d$.
- **Partial safety factors for material:** $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ (yield), $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ (ultimate).
- **Partial safety factors for loads (DL+LL):** $\gamma_f = 1.5$ for both.
- **Deflection limits (SLS):** Purlins L/180; Beams L/250; Portal frame drift H/150.
- **Maximum slenderness:** Tension member 400; Compression member 180 (IS 800).
- **Bolt hole size:** nominal bolt dia + 2 mm (clearance) for deduction.
- **Shear lag factor:** $\alpha = 0.8$ for end-connected single-leg plates.
- **Code reference:** All design uses **IS 800:2007** + **IS 875 (Parts 1–5)** + **IS 2062** (material).
- **Wind Zone for Kerala:** Basic wind speed **39–50 m/s** depending on district proximity to coast.
- **Thermal expansion coefficient:** $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$ — remember to multiply with $\Delta T$ and $E$.
- **Strain at yield:** $\varepsilon_y = f_y / E$ — for Fe 410, ≈ **0.00125** (often tested).
- **Equivalent WSM safety factor:** $\gamma_f \cdot \gamma_m \approx 1.65$ — close to old WSM's 1.67, hence similar designs.
- **Why LSM is better than WSM:** Captures load variability and material uncertainty **separately** and **realistically**.
- **Farm structures favoured by steel:** Long-span trusses, silos, portal frames, tower supports, light-gauge greenhouse frames.
- **Service life of farm steel structure:** Default **50 years** per IS 800 (with normal maintenance).
- **Two limit states must always be checked:** **ULS (strength)** AND **SLS (deflection + drift)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of IS Codes and Handbooks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Use of IS Codes and Handbooks # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are IS Codes? **IS Codes (Indian Standards)** are the national technical standards formulated and published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, which operates under the Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food and Public Distribution, Government of India. Each IS Code is identified by a unique alphanumeric designation, e.g., **IS 456:2000**, where the digits denote the code number and the year represents the latest revision. > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *IS Codes are a set of standardized design specifications, material specifications, testing procedures, and code of practices that ensure safety, reliability, durability, and uniformity in the planning, analysis, design, and execution of civil and agricultural engineering structures.* ## 1.2 What are Handbooks? **Handbooks** in the structural engineering domain are auxiliary reference publications that simplify and elaborate upon the application of IS Codes. They include: - **SP (Special Publication) Series** by BIS (e.g., SP 16, SP 24, SP 6) - **Handbook for Structural Engineers** (issued by BIS and INSDAG) - **Standard Data Book for Analysis of Rates** (issued by CPWD / State PWD) - **Schedule of Rates (SoR)** documents - **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)** — a comprehensive umbrella document ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Engineering Cookbook"** > Think of IS Codes as the **official recipe book** in a professional kitchen, and Handbooks as the **chef's personal notebook** that simplifies the recipe with shortcuts, pre-calculated tables, ready-to-use charts, and worked examples. A structural engineer, like a chef, cannot "cook up" a building without following the standardized recipes — every ingredient (material), quantity (load), and technique (design method) is prescribed. Deviating from the recipe is risky and not accepted by regulatory bodies. For farm structures, where the engineer designs **dairy sheds, poultry houses, greenhouses, grain silos, irrigation structures, and farm buildings**, the IS Codes act as the universal language of safety and quality. ## 1.4 Why are IS Codes Indispensable for Farm Structure Design? - **Safety Assurance:** Prevents collapse and structural failure. - **Legal Compliance:** Building permits and approvals require IS Code compliance. - **Standardization:** Allows engineers across India to communicate uniformly. - **Insurance Validity:** Insurers require compliance for claim settlement. - **Durability:** Ensures the structure lasts its intended design life (typically **50 to 100 years**). - **Cost Economy:** Avoids over-design and ensures economy in material use. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of IS Codes Relevant to Farm Structures IS Codes are broadly classified into the following categories: | **Category** | **Purpose** | **Example Codes** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Material Codes** | Specify material properties, grades, and testing | IS 432 (Mild Steel), IS 1786 (TMT Bars), IS 269/8112/12269 (Cement) | | **Design Codes** | Provide the philosophy and equations for structural design | IS 456 (RCC), IS 800 (Steel), IS 875 (Loads) | | **Code of Practice** | Prescriptive guidance for construction activities | IS 2751 (Scaffolding), IS 456 (Concrete Practice) | | **Testing Codes** | Standardized procedures for material testing | IS 516 (Concrete Strength), IS 1608 (Steel Tensile) | | **Specialized Farm Codes** | Specific to agricultural applications | IS 14462, IS 14855, IS 15498 (Greenhouses) | ## 2.2 Major IS Codes for Farm Structure Design > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Know Codes (High-Yield for KTU Board Exam):** ### 2.2.1 IS 456:2000 — Plain and Reinforced Concrete - Governs design of **all RCC elements** (beams, columns, slabs, footings, foundations). - Specifies **Limit State Method (LSM)** as the primary design philosophy. - Provides partial safety factors for materials and loads. - Essential for **farm building foundations, columns, beams, and roof slabs**. ### 2.2.2 IS 800:2007 — General Construction in Steel - Governs design of **steel roof trusses, girders, columns, and purlins** extensively used in farm sheds. - Mandates **Limit State Design (LSD)** with partial safety factors. - Critical for **pre-engineered farm buildings, godowns, and storage structures**. ### 2.2.3 IS 875 (Parts 1 to 5) — Code of Practice for Design Loads - **Part 1:** Dead loads (self-weight of materials, IS 1911 tables). - **Part 2:** Imposed (live) loads — for farm buildings, typical **1.5 to 5.0 kN/m²** depending on usage. - **Part 3:** Wind loads — critical for **open-sided farm sheds and tall silos**. - **Part 4:** Snow loads — relevant for **cold-storage farm buildings in hilly regions**. - **Part 5:** Special loads and load combinations. ### 2.2.4 IS 1893 (Part 1):2016 — Earthquake Resistant Design - Seismic zone classification (**Zone II to V** across India; Kerala falls in **Zone III**). - Design horizontal seismic coefficient calculation. - Ductile detailing as per the code. ### 2.2.5 IS 15498:2004 — Greenhouse Structures - Specific design parameters for **polyhouses and greenhouses**. - Wind load coefficients for curved roofs. ### 2.2.6 IS 14462:1997 / IS 14855 — Protected Cultivation - Design of **polyhouses, net houses, and shade net structures**. ### 2.2.7 IS 15827 & IS 16059 — Storage Structures - Design guidance for **grain silos, godowns, and bulk storage**. ## 2.3 Important Handbooks (SP Series) | **Handbook** | **Full Title** | **Application** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **SP 16:1980** | Design Aids for IS 456 | Pre-calculated tables for beams, slabs, columns (RCC design) | | **SP 24:1983** | Explanatory Handbook on IS 456 | Clause-by-clause explanation of IS 456 | | **SP 6 (1 to 6)** | Handbook for Structural Engineers (Steel) | Steel section properties, design tables | | **SP 34:1987** | Handbook on Concrete Reinforcement Detailing | Bar bending schedule, detailing rules | | **SP 38:1987** | Handbook for Typified Designs | Standard designs for small structures | ## 2.4 Cost Estimation Handbooks (Equally Important) For **Cost Estimation of Farm Structures** (Module 2): - **CPWD Schedule of Rates (SoR)** — Central Public Works Department rates. - **Kerala PWD Schedule of Rates (State SoR)** — Kerala-specific item rates. - **Standard Data Book for Analysis of Rates (SDAR)** — material consumption norms. - **Delhi Schedule of Rates (DSR)** — national reference. ## 2.5 Design Philosophies Prescribed by IS Codes ### 2.5.1 Working Stress Method (WSM) - Based on **elastic theory**. - Uses a single factor of safety. - Permissible stress = Ultimate stress / Factor of Safety (typically **1.5 to 2.0**). ### 2.5.2 Limit State Method (LSM) — Modern Approach - **Two limit states** are considered: 1. **Limit State of Collapse (Strength)** — ultimate load capacity. 2. **Limit State of Serviceability (Deflection, Cracking, Vibration)** — usability. - Uses **partial safety factors** on both loads and materials. - Mandatory in **IS 456:2000** and **IS 800:2007**. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formula Sheet for KTU Board Exam** | **Sl. No.** | **Formula / Concept** | **Description** | **Unit / Value** | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $f_{ck}$ | Characteristic compressive strength of concrete | $\text{N/mm}^2$ (e.g., 20, 25, 30) | | 2 | $f_y$ | Characteristic yield strength of steel | $\text{N/mm}^2$ (e.g., 415, 500) | | 3 | $\gamma_m$ | Partial safety factor for material (RCC) | **1.5 (concrete), 1.15 (steel)** | | 4 | $\gamma_f$ | Partial safety factor for load (LSM) | **1.5 (DL), 1.5 (LL), 1.2 (WL combo)** | | 5 | $\sigma_{bc} = \dfrac{0.67\, f_{ck}}{\gamma_m}$ | Permissible stress in concrete (LSM design) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | | 6 | $\sigma_{st} = \dfrac{0.87\, f_y}{\gamma_m}$ | Permissible stress in steel (LSM design) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | | 7 | $V_z = V_k \cdot V_d \cdot V_i$ | Wind speed (as per IS 875 Part 3) | $\text{m/s}$ | | 8 | $q_z = 0.6\, V_z^2$ | Wind pressure | $\text{N/m}^2$ | | 9 | $Z = \dfrac{I\, S_a}{2\, R\, g}$ | Seismic coefficient (IS 1893) | dimensionless | | 10 | $\text{Design Load} = \gamma_f \times \text{Characteristic Load}$ | Factored load equation | $\text{kN or kN/m}^2$ | > **Note:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ is used in IS codes for **absolute value** (e.g., $\vert V_z \vert$). In this sheet, $\cdot$ is used for multiplication to maintain markdown table integrity. ## 2.7 Real-World Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > In actual farm structure projects, the consulting engineer must reference **multiple codes simultaneously**: > - **Foundation design** → IS 456 + IS 1080 > - **Steel truss roof** → IS 800 + IS 875 (Parts 1, 2, 3) + IS 1893 > - **Cost estimation** → CPWD SoR + Kerala PWD SoR + Standard Data Book > - **Greenhouse design** → IS 15498 + IS 14462 A structure designed **without** IS Code compliance is **unsafe, non-insurable, and legally non-habitable**. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Limit State Load Combination as per IS 875 Part 5 In Limit State Method, every load is **factored** (multiplied by a partial safety factor $\gamma_f$) before being used in design. The most commonly used **load combination** for farm structures (RCC frame) as per **IS 875 (Part 5) Table 1** is: $$ \text{Design Load} = 1.5 \times (\text{DL} + \text{LL}) $$ For more critical combinations involving wind or earthquake: $$ \text{Design Load} = 1.2 \times (\text{DL} + \text{LL} \pm \text{WL}) $$ $$ \text{Design Load} = 1.2 \times (\text{DL} + \text{LL} \pm \text{EL}) $$ $$ \text{Design Load} = 1.5 \times (\text{DL} \pm \text{WL}) $$ $$ \text{Design Load} = 0.9 \times \text{DL} \pm 1.5 \times \text{WL} $$ ### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation: **Step 1 — Identify loads:** Dead Load (DL), Live Load (LL), Wind Load (WL), Earthquake Load (EL). **Step 2 — Select governing combination:** The design must satisfy **all** combinations; the **maximum** design effect governs. **Step 3 — Apply partial safety factors:** As per IS 456, Table 18. **Step 4 — Compute ultimate design moment, shear, or axial force:** Using the factored load. **Step 5 — Provide section** to resist the **factored action** with material strength divided by $\gamma_m$. The fundamental **inequality of safety** in LSM is: $$ \text{Design Action (Factored Load Effect)} \leq \text{Design Strength (Material Capacity / } \gamma_m\text{)} $$ $$ \gamma_f \cdot Q_k \leq \dfrac{R_k}{\gamma_m} $$ Where: - $Q_k$ = Characteristic load effect - $R_k$ = Characteristic material resistance - $\gamma_f$ = Partial safety factor for load - $\gamma_m$ = Partial safety factor for material ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Wind Pressure on a Farm Building > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem (as per IS 875 Part 3):** Calculate the design wind pressure for a farm building located in Thrissur, Kerala. Basic wind speed $V_b = 39 \text{ m/s}$. The building is a **2-storey cattle shed**, terrain category **2** (open terrain with scattered obstructions), building class **B**, structure height $h = 8 \text{ m}$, and roof angle $\theta = 10°$. ### Given Data $$ V_b = 39 \text{ m/s}, \quad k_1 = 1.00 \text{ (50-year return)}, \quad k_2 = 0.91 \text{ (class B, h=8m)}, \quad k_3 = 1.00 \text{ (category B)}, \quad k_4 = 1.00 $$ ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Compute the **design wind speed** $V_z$: $$ V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4 $$ $$ V_z = 39 \times 1.00 \times 0.91 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 = 35.49 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the **design wind pressure** $p_z$: $$ p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2 $$ $$ p_z = 0.6 \times (35.49)^2 = 0.6 \times 1259.5 = 755.72 \text{ N/m}^2 $$ $$ p_z \approx 0.756 \text{ kN/m}^2 $$ **Step 3:** Apply the **external pressure coefficient** $C_{pe}$ (from IS 875 Part 3, Table 4) for the windward wall: $$ C_{pe} = +0.8 \text{ (windward)}, \quad C_{pe} = -0.5 \text{ (leeward)} $$ **Step 4:** Net pressure on the building: $$ p_{\text{net}} = (C_{pe,\text{wind}} - C_{pe,\text{lee}}) \times p_z $$ $$ p_{\text{net}} = (0.8 - (-0.5)) \times 0.756 = 1.3 \times 0.756 = 0.983 \text{ kN/m}^2 $$ **Step 5:** Internal pressure coefficient $C_{pi}$ (for open sheds, IS 875 allows $C_{pi} = \pm 0.5$): $$ p_{\text{design}} = (C_{pe} - C_{pi}) \times p_z $$ $$ p_{\text{design}} = (0.8 - 0.5) \times 0.756 = 0.227 \text{ kN/m}^2 \text{ (minimum, internal pressure positive)} $$ **Final Design Pressure** is the **worst-case** of the above combinations, and is used to design the **roof truss, purlins, and columns** of the farm building. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Code Reference Matrix for Farm Building Elements > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use this matrix as a "decision table" to identify which code to use for which element of a farm structure.** | **Farm Structure Element** | **Applicable IS Code(s)** | **Handbook(s)** | **Key Design Parameter** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **RCC Foundation** | IS 456, IS 1080 | SP 16, SP 34 | Bearing capacity, footing dimensions | | **Steel Roof Truss** | IS 800, IS 875 (Part 1, 2, 3) | SP 6 (Steel Handbook) | Section sizing, slenderness ratio | | **Purlins & Sheeting** | IS 800, IS 277 (GI sheets) | SP 6 | Spacing, gauge, deflection | | **Masonry Walls** | IS 1905, IS 2185 | NBC 2016 (Part 6) | Compressive strength, slenderness | | **Greenhouse Frame** | IS 15498, IS 14462 | NBC 2016 | Wind load on curved surface | | **Grain Silo** | IS 15827, IS 16059 | ACI 313 (reference) | Lateral pressure from grain | | **Dairy Shed Floor** | IS 456 (RCC), IS 1834 (Mosaic) | SP 16 | Slab thickness, abrasion resistance | | **Cost Estimation** | CPWD SoR, State PWD SoR | Standard Data Book | Item rate, material consumption | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Code Hierarchy and Application Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Project Initiation
Farm Structure Requirement] --> B[Identify Structure Type
Building Silo Greenhouse Shed] B --> C{Code Selection} C --> D[IS 456:2000
Plain and Reinforced Concrete] C --> E[IS 800:2007
General Construction in Steel] C --> F[IS 875 Parts 1 to 5
Design Loads Dead Live Wind Snow] C --> G[IS 1893 Part 1:2016
Earthquake Resistant Design] C --> H[IS 15498:2004
Greenhouse Structures] C --> I[IS 15827
Grain Storage Silos] D --> J[Apply Design Philosophy
Limit State Method] E --> J F --> J G --> J H --> J I --> J J --> K[Compute Loads
Use Partial Safety Factors] K --> L[Refer to Handbooks
SP 16 SP 6 SP 24 SP 34] L --> M[Design Sections
Beams Columns Slabs Footings Trusses] M --> N{Cost Estimation Phase} N --> O[CPWD Schedule of Rates] N --> P[Kerala PWD SoR] N --> Q[Standard Data Book for Analysis of Rates] O --> R[Final Bill of Quantities
and Project Cost] P --> R Q --> R R --> S[Execution Stage
With IS Code Compliant Drawings] ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Map: Relationship Between IS Codes and Handbooks ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Bureau of Indian Standards BIS] --> B[IS Codes Core Standards] A --> C[SP Series Handbooks] A --> D[National Building Code NBC] B --> E[IS 456 RCC] B --> F[IS 800 Steel] B --> G[IS 875 Loads] C --> H[SP 16 Design Aids RCC] C --> I[SP 6 Steel Sections] C --> J[SP 24 Explanations] D --> K[Part 6 Structural Design] E --> L[Farm Structure Design] F --> L G --> L H --> L I --> L J --> L K --> L L --> M[Safe Economical and Compliant Farm Building] ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow: How a Structural Engineer Uses IS Codes in Farm Building Design ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Client Brief and Site Data] --> B[Preliminary Sizing using NBC 2016] B --> C[Compute Dead Load as per IS 875 Part 1] C --> D[Compute Live Load as per IS 875 Part 2] D --> E{Wind Zone Applicable?} E -->|Yes| F[Compute Wind Load IS 875 Part 3] E -->|No| G[Compute Seismic Load IS 1893] F --> H[Form Load Combinations IS 875 Part 5] G --> H H --> I[Apply Limit State Method IS 456 or IS 800] I --> J[Select Section from SP 16 or SP 6] J --> K[Check Serviceability Deflection Cracking] K --> L{Compliant?} L -->|No| I L -->|Yes| M[Prepare Drawings and BoQ] M --> N[Cost Estimation using SoR and Data Book] N --> O[Final Design Report] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) > **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]** > *Define IS Code. List any four major IS Codes used in the design of farm structures along with their year of publication.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** IS Codes (Indian Standards) are national standards formulated by the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) that prescribe design rules, material specifications, and construction practices for engineering structures. Four major IS Codes for farm structures: 1. **IS 456:2000** — Plain and Reinforced Concrete. 2. **IS 800:2007** — General Construction in Steel. 3. **IS 875 (Parts 1 to 5)** — Code of Practice for Design Loads. 4. **IS 15498:2004** — Greenhouse Design. *(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for listing 4 codes = 3 marks)* --- > **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** > *What is the role of Handbooks (SP Series) in the design of farm structures? Name any two handbooks with their use.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Handbooks (SP Series) are supplementary publications by BIS that simplify the application of IS Codes. They provide **pre-calculated design tables, design aids, ready-to-use charts, and clause-by-clause explanations**, saving the engineer's computation time. Two handbooks: 1. **SP 16:1980** — Design Aids for IS 456. Provides pre-calculated tables for beams, slabs, and columns, eliminating repetitive calculations. 2. **SP 6 (1 to 6)** — Handbook for Structural Engineers (Steel). Provides properties of steel sections and design tables for IS 800. *(1 mark for role + 1 mark each for two handbooks = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (Module Internal Choice) ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]* > **Q A.** (a) Explain in detail the **classification of IS Codes** relevant to farm structure design. List at least **six major codes** with their area of application. **(7 Marks)** > (b) Describe the **design philosophy of the Limit State Method (LSM)** as recommended by IS 456:2000. Compare it briefly with the Working Stress Method. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Q A(a) — Classification of IS Codes (7 Marks) The Bureau of Indian Standards publishes a wide range of codes, classified into the following categories relevant to farm structure design: **1. Material Codes** — Specify properties and grades of construction materials. - *Example:* IS 432 (Mild Steel), IS 1786 (High Strength Deformed Steel Bars), IS 269 (Ordinary Portland Cement). **2. Design Codes** — Provide design philosophy and equations. - *Example:* **IS 456:2000** (RCC Design), **IS 800:2007** (Steel Design). **3. Code of Practice / Loading Codes** — Specify loads and construction practices. - *Example:* **IS 875 (Parts 1–5)** (Dead, Live, Wind, Snow, Special Loads). **4. Specialized Farm Structure Codes:** - **IS 15498:2004** — Greenhouse structures. - **IS 14462:1997** — Polyhouse design parameters. - **IS 15827** — Grain storage silos. - **IS 1893 (Part 1):2016** — Earthquake-resistant design of structures. **5. Testing Codes:** - IS 516 — Compressive strength of concrete. - IS 1608 — Tensile testing of steel. **6. Cost Estimation Codes / Handbooks:** - CPWD Schedule of Rates, Standard Data Book for Analysis of Rates (SDAR). *Self-explanatory tabular presentation recommended for full marks.* #### Model Solution for Q A(b) — Limit State Method (7 Marks) **Limit State:** A state beyond which the structure is considered to have failed to perform its intended function. Two limit states are defined in IS 456:2000: **1. Limit State of Collapse (Strength):** - The structure must safely resist the **factored design loads** without collapse. - The general safety inequality is: $$ \gamma_f \times \text{Load Effect} \leq \dfrac{\text{Material Strength}}{\gamma_m} $$ - Partial safety factors used: - $\gamma_f = 1.5$ (for DL and LL) - $\gamma_m = 1.5$ (concrete), $1.15$ (steel) **2. Limit State of Serviceability:** - The structure must remain functional under service loads. - Checks include **deflection** (max. $\dfrac{L}{250}$ for beams), **crack width** (max. 0.3 mm for RCC), and **vibration limits**. **Comparison with Working Stress Method:** | **Aspect** | **WSM** | **LSM** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Theory basis | Elastic | Inelastic / Statistical | | Safety factor | Single (e.g., 1.5) | Partial (on load & material) | | Design for | Working loads | Factored loads | | Serviceability check | Not mandatory | Mandatory | | Economy | Conservative | Economical (~15–20% saving) | | Adoption | Older (IS 456:1978) | Current (IS 456:2000) | *Diagrammatic comparison and equation highlighting fetches full marks.* --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **Q B.** (a) Explain the **step-by-step procedure to determine the design wind pressure** for a farm building as per IS 875 (Part 3). Use the following data: $V_b = 47$ m/s, terrain category 3, class A, height 10 m, and roof angle $20°$. **(7 Marks)** > (b) Write detailed notes on the **Schedule of Rates (SoR)** and **Standard Data Book**, highlighting their role in cost estimation of farm structures. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Q B(a) — Wind Pressure (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Basic Wind Speed ($V_b$):** Given as $47 \text{ m/s}$ (from IS 875 Part 3, Fig. 1 map). **Step 2 — Risk Coefficient ($k_1$):** For 50-year return period, $k_1 = 1.00$. **Step 3 — Terrain, Height & Structure Size Factor ($k_2$):** For category 3, class A, height 10 m (from Table 2): $k_2 = 0.91$. **Step 4 — Topography Factor ($k_3$):** Flat terrain, $k_3 = 1.00$. **Step 5 — Importance Factor ($k_4$):** For general farm building, $k_4 = 1.00$. **Step 6 — Design Wind Speed ($V_z$):** $$ V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4 = 47 \times 1.00 \times 0.91 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 = 42.77 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 7 — Design Wind Pressure ($p_z$):** $$ p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2 = 0.6 \times (42.77)^2 = 0.6 \times 1829.27 = 1097.56 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 1.098 \text{ kN/m}^2 $$ **Step 8 — Apply External Pressure Coefficients:** For windward wall $C_{pe} = +0.7$, leeward $C_{pe} = -0.3$, and roof angle $20°$: $$ C_{pe,\text{windward roof}} = -0.5, \quad C_{pe,\text{leeward roof}} = -0.4 $$ **Step 9 — Net Design Pressure:** $$ p_{\text{net,wall}} = (0.7 + 0.3) \times 1.098 = 1.098 \text{ kN/m}^2 $$ $$ p_{\text{net,roof}} = (-0.5 + 0.4) \times 1.098 = -0.11 \text{ kN/m}^2 \text{ (suction)} $$ **Conclusion:** The wall must be designed for $1.098 \text{ kN/m}^2$ horizontal pressure; the roof experiences $0.11 \text{ kN/m}^2$ suction. #### Model Solution for Q B(b) — Schedule of Rates and Data Book (7 Marks) **Schedule of Rates (SoR):** - A document published periodically (annually or biannually) by **CPWD, Kerala PWD, and other state agencies**. - It lists the **unit rate of every item of work** (e.g., per cubic meter of concrete, per kg of steel, per square meter of plastering). - It accounts for material cost, labour cost, contractor's profit, and overheads. - **Role:** Acts as the **base price reference** for preparing cost estimates, tender documents, and contract agreements for farm structure projects. **Standard Data Book for Analysis of Rates:** - Published by **CPWD** as "Standard Data Book for Analysis of Rates (SDAR)". - Contains **material consumption norms**, **labour hours required**, and **machinery deployment** for each item of work. - **Role:** Allows the engineer to **analyse** the cost components of an item when SoR rates are outdated or unavailable, ensuring transparent and justified rate analysis. **Importance in Farm Structure Cost Estimation:** 1. **Uniformity:** Ensures fair and uniform pricing across government projects. 2. **Accuracy:** Reduces ambiguity in tender evaluation. 3. **Time-Saving:** Prevents the engineer from re-calculating basic rates for every project. 4. **Audit Compliance:** Government auditors verify estimates against SoR. 5. **Updates:** Both SoR and Data Book are updated for **inflation, GST, and material price variations**. *Tabular comparison and example item-rates fetch full marks.* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deduction:** > > 1. **Forgetting the year of publication** of IS Codes (e.g., writing only "IS 456" instead of "IS 456:2000"). The KTU examiner expects the **latest revision year**. > 2. **Confusing WSM and LSM** partial safety factors. Memorize: $\gamma_m = 1.5$ for concrete and $\gamma_f = 1.5$ for DL/LL. > 3. **Wrong $C_{pe}$ values** in wind load problems. Always cite the **table number from IS 875 Part 3** and choose the correct roof slope category. > 4. **Not using units consistently** in numerical problems (mixing N and kN). This alone can cost 1 mark. > 5. **Listing only the code name without application** in "List IS Codes" questions. Always mention the **specific use** in farm structures. > 6. **Skipping the design philosophy explanation** when asked about IS 456 — students often write the design procedure but forget to mention **Limit State vs. Working Stress**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **IS Codes** are mandatory national standards published by **BIS**; non-compliance is illegal for public structures. - **Handbooks (SP series)** are **supporting reference** publications, not substitutes for IS Codes. - **IS 456:2000** governs RCC; **IS 800:2007** governs steel; both use **Limit State Method (LSM)**. - **IS 875** has **5 parts**: Dead, Live, Wind, Snow, and Special Loads. - **Partial safety factors (LSM):** $\gamma_f = 1.5$ (DL/LL); $\gamma_m = 1.5$ (concrete), $1.15$ (steel). - **Wind pressure formula:** $p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$, where $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$. - **Seismic design** uses IS 1893:2016; Kerala falls in **Zone III** ($Z = 0.16$). - **Cost estimation references:** **CPWD SoR, Kerala PWD SoR, Standard Data Book (SDAR)**. - **For farm-specific structures:** IS 15498 (Greenhouses), IS 15827 (Silos), IS 14462 (Polyhouses). - **Design philosophy shift:** From **WSM (IS 456:1978)** to **LSM (IS 456:2000)** — the current standard. - **Always cite** both the code number and the **year of latest revision** in exam answers. - **Use Mermaid flowcharts and tables** in your answer sheets to score extra marks for presentation. - **NBC 2016** is the **umbrella document** for all building-related codes in India. - **SP 16** is the most-used handbook for RCC design (pre-calculated beam/slab/column tables). - **SP 6 (1 to 6)** is the most-used handbook for steel section properties. - **Always mention "as per IS 875 (Part 3)"** when computing wind loads — citing the specific part fetches full credit. - **Limit States:** **Collapse (Strength)** and **Serviceability (Deflection, Cracking, Vibration)** — both must be satisfied.

topics.title: Properties of Structural Steel; Design of Connections - Types of Connection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties of Structural Steel; Design of Connections - Types of Connection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Properties of Structural Steel

### Formal Academic Definition

**Structural steel** is a category of carbon steel used in the construction of farm buildings, storage godowns, trusses for poultry houses, dairy sheds, greenhouses, and ancillary farm infrastructure. It conforms to the Indian Standard **IS 2062:2011** "Hot Rolled Medium and High Tensile Structural Steel — Specification." The grade most commonly adopted for farm structures is **E250 (Fe 410)** because of its excellent weldability, predictable ductility, and economical availability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PCAGT501 (Farm Structures), the relevant steel grade is **E250 (Fe 410)** as per IS 2062:2011, with characteristic yield strength **f_y = 250 N/mm²** and ultimate tensile strength **f_u = 410 N/mm²**.

### Key Mechanical Properties (as per IS 2062:2011)

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value (E250/Fe410) | Importance in Farm Structures |
|----------|--------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Yield Stress | **f_y** | **250 N/mm²** | Governs design under service loads |
| Ultimate Tensile Stress | **f_u** | **410 N/mm²** | Governs ultimate / collapse state |
| Modulus of Elasticity | **E** | **2.00 × 10⁵ N/mm²** | Used for deflection checks in trusses |
| Shear Modulus | **G** | **0.769 × 10⁵ N/mm²** | Used in shear and torsion calculations |
| Poisson's Ratio | **ν** | **0.30** | Used in bi-axial stress states |
| Density | **ρ** | **7850 kg/m³** | Used for self-weight of trusses |
| Elongation at fracture | **ε_u** | **23 % (min)** | Ductility — warning before failure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ductility** of structural steel is its most prized property for farm buildings exposed to cyclic wind loads (monsoons in Kerala). A minimum elongation of **23 %** ensures the steel deforms visibly before fracture, giving occupants and livestock time to evacuate.

### Chemical Composition (per IS 2062:2011, Grade E250)

| Constituent | Maximum % (Ladle Analysis) | Effect on Steel |
|-------------|----------------------------|-----------------|
| **Carbon (C)** | **0.23 %** | Increases strength, reduces weldability |
| **Manganese (Mn)** | **1.50 %** | Improves strength and toughness |
| **Silicon (Si)** | **0.40 %** | De-oxidizer |
| **Sulphur (S)** | **0.045 %** | Impurity — causes hot shortness |
| **Phosphorus (P)** | **0.045 %** | Impurity — causes cold shortness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** must be limited to **≤ 0.45 %** for welded farm structures. CE = C + Mn/6 + (Cr+Mo+V)/5 + (Ni+Cu)/15. Higher CE values demand preheating before welding.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

Imagine you have to build a **cattle-shed roof truss** in Wayanad. You need a material that is:
- **Strong** enough to carry purlins, sheeting, wind load, and even an occasional coconut-fall impact,
- **Predictable** so the engineer can certify the design,
- **Cheap** because farm profits are tight,
- **Easy to join** at site using bolts or welding.

Structural steel (Fe 410) behaves exactly like a **strong but flexible fishing rod**: it stretches under load (ductile), warns you before snapping (large elongation), and can be joined simply by welding two rods together. Concrete, by contrast, is strong but brittle — like a chalk stick.

---

## 1.2 Design of Connections — Types of Connection

### Formal Academic Definition

A **connection** is the engineered joint that transmits forces between two or more structural members of a farm truss (e.g., rafter–tie, rafter–strut, column–base plate). Connections must be designed so that the strength of the joint is **at least equal to the strength of the connected member**, ensuring failure occurs in the member rather than at the joint — the classic **"strong joint–weak member"** design principle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Learning Outcome:** After studying this topic, the student should be able to identify, classify, and preliminarily design riveted, bolted, and welded connections used in farm trusses, godown frames, and equipment-support structures.

### Three Principal Types of Connections

1. **Riveted Connections** — *predominantly historical; rarely used today in new farm construction.*
2. **Bolted Connections** — *most common in field-erected farm trusses and towers.*
3. **Welded Connections** — *most efficient; used in prefabricated shop-welded farm buildings.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Three Ways to Join Two Bamboo Poles"

Picture two bamboo poles that you must join end-to-end to make a longer pole for a farm trellis:

| Real-life method | Construction equivalent | Force transfer mechanism |
|------------------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| Tying with coconut rope | **Riveting** | Friction + bearing after plastic deformation of the fastener |
| Nailing with iron nails | **Bolting** (bearing type) | Direct bearing of bolt shank against hole wall |
| Gluing with epoxy | **Bolting** (friction-grip / HSFG) | Friction between plates clamped by high tension in bolt |
| Burning the ends and fusing | **Welding** | Atomic-level fusion of parent metals |

> [!NOTE]
> In modern farm-structure practice (per IS 800:2007), **bolted** and **welded** connections are the only two recommended. Riveting is retained only for studying because old godown drawings still show it, and the KTU syllabus lists it for completeness.

### Classification Chart (Hierarchical Overview)

```
CONNECTIONS
├── 1. Riveted Connections
│       ├── Hot-driven (workmanship-sensitive)
│       └── Cold-driven (light gauge only)
├── 2. Bolted Connections
│       ├── (a) Bearing-type Bolts
│       │        ├── Ordinary (Grade 4.6)
│       │        └── High-strength (Grade 8.8 / 10.9 used as bearing)
│       └── (b) Friction-grip / HSFG Bolts
│                ├── Pre-tensioned (slip-critical)
│                └── Categories B & C per IS 3757
└── 3. Welded Connections
        ├── Fillet Weld
        │       ├── Transverse (loaded ⟂ to weld axis)
        │       └── Parallel / Longitudinal (loaded ∥ to weld axis)
        └── Butt Weld
                ├── Single-V, Double-V, Single-U, Double-U, J-groove
                ├── Complete penetration (full strength)
                └── Incomplete penetration (not allowed in main members)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Study Steel Properties Before Connections?

Every connection design equation in **IS 800:2007** uses either:
- **f_y** → design of plates and welds under direct / bending stress (Limit State of Yielding).
- **f_u** → design of bolts and welds in shear, plates in tension (Limit State of Rupture / Fracture).

If you misremember f_y or f_u, *every* connection answer in the exam loses 1–2 marks. The values **250 N/mm²** and **410 N/mm²** must be muscle memory.

---

## 2.2 Mechanical Properties — Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Stress-Strain Behaviour (Hot-rolled E250):**
- Linear elastic up to **f_y = 250 N/mm²** at strain ≈ 0.00125.
- Perfectly plastic (yield plateau) up to strain ≈ 0.020.
- Strain hardening beyond ultimate stress **f_u = 410 N/mm²** at strain ≈ 0.18.
- Fracture at strain ≈ 0.23.

**Step 2 — Why Two Strength Values?**
- **f_y** is used to check that the steel does not yield under **working loads × partial safety factor (γ_M0 = 1.10)**. This is *serviceability*.
- **f_u** is used to check that the steel does not rupture under **ultimate loads × γ_M1 = 1.25**. This is *safety against collapse*.
- For farm trusses in Kerala, ultimate wind (Vu) and ultimate live load (LLu) are computed from IS 875 (Part 3) and (Part 5).

**Step 3 — Why Modulus of Elasticity Matters for Connections?**
Deflection of a roof truss governs purlin spacing. Connections must be **rigid enough** to develop assumed joint behaviour (pinned vs. rigid). IS 800:2007 recommends most farm trusses be designed as **pin-jointed**, so bolts in bearing allow rotation without bending.

**Step 4 — Why Ductility Governs Connection Choice?**
Ductile connections (welds, HSFG bolts) absorb impact loads — e.g., a fallen coconut branch hitting the cattle-shed roof. Brittle connections (rivets in old trusses) fail suddenly.

---

## 2.3 Design Philosophy — Limit State Method (LSM)

Per **IS 800:2007**, farm-structure steel design uses the **Limit State Method**:

$$\text{Design Action} \le \text{Design Resistance}$$

where

$$R_d = \frac{R_n}{\gamma_m}$$

and **γ_m** is the *partial safety factor for material*:

| Failure Mode | γ_m | Used for |
|--------------|------|----------|
| Yielding (γ_M0) | **1.10** | Plates in bending / tension at yield |
| Ultimate stress (γ_M1) | **1.25** | Plates in fracture, bolts, welds, rupture |
| Shop / Field welds (γ_Mw) | **1.25 / 1.50** | Weld strength at shop vs. site |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The older Working Stress Method (Permissible Stress = 0.6 f_y for steel) is still asked in Part A for definition. But all *Part B 14-mark* numerical problems are solved using **Limit State Method**.

---

## 2.4 Detailed Theory — Riveted Connections

### Mechanism of Force Transfer
- The rivet is heated red, inserted, and hammered to form the closing head. On cooling, it shrinks and **clamps** the plates together (initial tension).
- External load is resisted by **shear** in the rivet shank + **bearing** on the hole wall.
- Failure modes: **shearing of rivet**, **bearing on hole**, **tearing of plate (block shear)**, **splitting of plate (tension)**, **net-section rupture**.

### Why Rivets Are Obsolete for New Farm Construction
- Skilled riveting labour is scarce.
- Quality depends on workmanship (cannot be visually inspected after cooling).
- Noisy and slow.
- IS 800:2007 essentially **discontinued** riveted construction.

---

## 2.5 Detailed Theory — Bolted Connections

### 2.5.1 Bearing-Type Bolts (Grade 4.6 / 8.8)

In bearing-type joints, **no controlled pretension** is applied. Load is transferred by the bolt shank **bearing** against the hole wall, plus a small shear component. Slip is permitted under service load. Common grades:

| Bolt Grade | f_yb (N/mm²) | f_ub (N/mm²) | Typical Use |
|------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| **4.6** | 240 | 400 | Light roof trusses, purlin cleats |
| **8.8** | 640 | 800 | Heavy trusses, column bases |
| **10.9** | 900 | 1040 | Wind-tower splices |

### 2.5.2 Friction-Grip / HSFG Bolts (Grade 8.8 / 10.9)

These are **highly pre-tensioned** so that the clamping force generates friction between plates. Load is transferred by **friction only** — no bearing, no slip at serviceability limit state. Used where:
- Vibration or fatigue matters (e.g., machinery support in dairy plant).
- Slip is unacceptable (e.g., long-span trusses where deflection is critical).

### 2.5.3 Failure Modes of Bolted Joints
1. Shearing of bolt shank.
2. Bearing failure of plate / bolt.
3. Tension failure of plate at net section.
4. Block shear failure.
5. Shear rupture of bolt at thread root.

### 2.5.4 Bolt Diameter Convention
- **Nominal hole diameter (d_0) = d + clearance**
  - For d ≤ 14 mm → clearance = 1.0 mm
  - For 16 mm ≤ d ≤ 24 mm → clearance = 2.0 mm
  - For d ≥ 27 mm → clearance = 3.0 mm
- **Net area** of plate = (gross width − Σd_0) × thickness
- **Effective net area** (for staggered holes) uses the **ductile shear lag factor** with reduction factor k = 0.85 (for connections with ≥ 2 bolts in the line of force).

---

## 2.6 Detailed Theory — Welded Connections

### 2.6.1 Fillet Weld
- Triangular cross-section, joining two surfaces at right angles (or any angle between 60°–120°).
- Most common in farm trusses (because it requires no edge preparation of plates).
- Size **s** is the leg length; throat thickness **t_t = 0.707 × s** (for 90° weld).

### 2.6.2 Butt Weld
- Plates are aligned edge-to-edge and fully penetrated.
- Strength = strength of parent metal (when complete penetration is achieved).
- Used for splicing heavy rafters and column shafts.

### 2.6.3 Failure Modes of Welds
- Throat-cracking (fracture along effective throat).
- Fusion-boundary failure (poor fusion).
- Heat-affected zone (HAZ) softening.
- Lamellar tearing in thick plates (T-joints).

### 2.6.4 Weld Strength (LSM)

$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

For shop weld: **f_wd = 0.6 × f_u / 1.25 ≈ 410 N/mm² × 0.48 = 197 N/mm²** for Fe410.
For field weld: 197 / 1.20 ≈ **164 N/mm²** (since γ_mw = 1.50 for field).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following table for the exam.** All quantities in **N and mm** unless stated.

### A. Material Constants (E250 / Fe410)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Yield stress | f_y | **250** | N/mm² |
| Ultimate stress | f_u | **410** | N/mm² |
| Modulus of elasticity | E | **2 × 10⁵** | N/mm² |
| Shear modulus | G | **0.769 × 10⁵** | N/mm² |
| Poisson's ratio | ν | **0.30** | — |
| Density | ρ | **7850** | kg/m³ |

### B. Partial Safety Factors

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| γ_M0 (yielding) | γ_M0 | **1.10** |
| γ_M1 (rupture) | γ_M1 | **1.25** |
| γ_Mb (bolt) | γ_Mb | **1.25** |
| γ_Mw (weld) | γ_Mw | **1.25 (shop) / 1.50 (field)** |
| γ_Mf (HSFG slip) | γ_Mf | **1.10 (service) / 1.25 (ultimate)** |

### C. Bolt Strength Equations (Bearing-type, threads in shear plane)

| Strength | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| Nominal shear strength of bolt | V_nsb = (f_ub / √3) × A_nb × n_s |
| Nominal bearing strength of bolt | V_npb = 2.5 × k_b × d × t × f_u |
| A_nb (threaded shank area) | 0.78 × A_sb |
| A_sb (shank area) | (π/4) × d² |

> Where k_b = min(e/(3d_0), p/(3d_0) − 0.25, f_ub/f_u, 1.0)

### D. Weld Strength Equations

| Weld type | Strength (per mm length) |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| Fillet weld throat stress | f_wd = f_u / (√3 × γ_mw) |
| Strength per mm of fillet weld | R_w = 0.707 × s × f_wd |
| Strength of butt weld (complete penetration) | R_bw = t × f_y / γ_M0 (yielding) or t × f_u / γ_M1 (rupture) |

### E. Plate Net Section Efficiency

| Quantity | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| Net area (straight holes) | A_n = (b − n × d_0) × t |
| Net area (staggered holes) | A_n = (b − Σd_0 + Σp²/(4g)) × t |
| Strength of plate at net section | T_dn = 0.9 × f_u × A_n / γ_M1 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section demonstrates the **complete working** for three exam-grade problems. Read the derivation line-by-line to learn the logic.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Effective Throat Thickness of a 90° Fillet Weld

A fillet weld has an isosceles-right-triangle cross-section with leg length **s** (assumed equal on both sides). The throat **t_t** is the shortest distance from the weld root to the hypotenuse (face).

**Geometric setup (Step 1):**
Place the weld in a coordinate system where the two legs lie along the x-axis and y-axis, both of length **s**. The hypotenuse lies along the line connecting points (s, 0) and (0, s).

**Length of hypotenuse (Step 2):**

$$h = \sqrt{s^2 + s^2} = \sqrt{2}\,s$$

**Equation of the hypotenuse (Step 3):**

$$\frac{x}{s} + \frac{y}{s} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x + y = s$$

**Perpendicular distance from origin (root) to this line (Step 4):**

For the line $x + y - s = 0$, the distance from (0, 0) is

$$d = \frac{\vert 0 + 0 - s \vert}{\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.7071 \, s$$

**Result (Step 5):**

$$t_t = 0.707 \, s$$

This derivation shows why the **effective throat = 0.707 s** is universal for fillet welds — it is the geometric altitude of a right-isosceles triangle.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Strength per mm Length of a Fillet Weld (Fe 410, Shop Weld)

**Given:**
- Plate ultimate strength f_u = 410 N/mm²
- Shop weld partial safety factor γ_mw = 1.25
- Weld size s (mm)

**Step 1 — Design shear stress in the weld throat:**

The fillet weld is assumed to fail in shear through the throat. Per IS 800:2007 Clause 10.5.7:

$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values for Fe 410 shop weld:**

$$f_{wd} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.25} = \frac{410}{2.165}$$

$$f_{wd} = 189.4 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Strength per mm length of fillet weld:**

The resisting area per mm = throat thickness = 0.707 × s.

$$R_{w,\text{per mm}} = f_{wd} \times 0.707 \times s = 0.707 \times 189.4 \times s$$

$$R_{w,\text{per mm}} = 133.9 \, s \quad \text{N/mm}$$

**Step 4 — Total weld strength for weld length L:**

$$R_w = 133.9 \times s \times L \quad \text{N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb (Fe 410 shop weld):** A 6 mm fillet weld can carry ≈ **133.9 × 6 = 803 N/mm** of length. For a 100 mm weld, that's ≈ **80.3 kN**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Design of a Bolted Lap Joint (Bearing-Type, Fe 410)

### Problem Statement (KTU-style)
Two plates, each **80 mm wide × 10 mm thick**, of Fe 410 steel are joined in a single-bolt **lap joint** using a **16 mm diameter bolt of grade 4.6**. Check whether the joint can safely transfer a factored tensile load of **60 kN** acting along the plates.

**Given:**
- Plate f_u = 410 N/mm², f_y = 250 N/mm²
- Bolt grade 4.6: f_ub = 400 N/mm², f_yb = 240 N/mm²
- Bolt diameter d = 16 mm
- Hole diameter d_0 = d + 2.0 = **18 mm**
- Plate thickness t = 10 mm
- Plate width b = 80 mm
- Factored load T_u = 60 kN = **60 000 N**

### Solution

**Step 1 — Shear strength of the bolt (single shear, threaded portion in shear plane)**

Nominal shear strength:

$$V_{nsb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \times A_{nb} \times n_s$$

Where A_nb = 0.78 × A_sb = 0.78 × (π/4) × d² = 0.78 × (π/4) × 16²

$$A_{nb} = 0.78 \times 201.06 = 156.83 \, \text{mm}^2$$

Substituting:

$$V_{nsb} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \times 156.83 \times 1 = \frac{400 \times 156.83}{1.732}$$

$$V_{nsb} = \frac{62\,732}{1.732} = 36\,220 \, \text{N} = 36.22 \, \text{kN}$$

Design shear strength:

$$V_{dsb} = \frac{V_{nsb}}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{36.22}{1.25} = 28.98 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Bearing strength of the bolt**

$$V_{npb} = 2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u$$

Assume edge distance e ≥ 1.5 d_0 and pitch p ≥ 2.5 d, so k_b = 1.0 (default assumption for standard spacing).

$$V_{npb} = 2.5 \times 1.0 \times 16 \times 10 \times 410 = 164\,000 \, \text{N} = 164.0 \, \text{kN}$$

Design bearing strength:

$$V_{dpb} = \frac{164.0}{1.25} = 131.2 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Bolt strength = minimum of shear and bearing**

$$V_{db} = \min(V_{dsb}, V_{dpb}) = \min(28.98, 131.2) = 28.98 \, \text{kN}$$

The joint is governed by **bolt shear** (the usual case for thin plates).

**Step 4 — Tensile strength of the plate at the net section**

Net area (single hole, straight chain):

$$A_n = (b - d_0) \times t = (80 - 18) \times 10 = 620 \, \text{mm}^2$$

Design tensile strength:

$$T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times f_u \times A_n}{\gamma_{M1}} = \frac{0.9 \times 410 \times 620}{1.25}$$

$$T_{dn} = \frac{228\,780}{1.25} = 183\,024 \, \text{N} = 183.0 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Joint capacity = minimum of bolt strength and plate strength**

$$T_{dj} = \min(V_{db}, T_{dn}) = \min(28.98, 183.0) = 28.98 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with factored load**

$$T_u = 60 \, \text{kN} \; > \; T_{dj} = 28.98 \, \text{kN}$$

**Conclusion:** The joint is **NOT SAFE** with a single 16 mm bolt. The student must either:
- Increase bolt diameter (try 20 mm → d_0 = 22 mm),
- Use **multiple bolts** in the line of force,
- Upgrade to HSFG 8.8 bolt,
- Increase plate thickness.

### Verification by Try-2 (Multiple Bolts)
Required total bolt shear capacity ≥ 60 kN. Number of 16 mm bolts (grade 4.6) needed:

$$n = \left\lceil \frac{60}{28.98} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.07 \rceil = 3 \text{ bolts}$$

**Provide 3 bolts** in line. Re-check net section area:

$$A_n = (80 - 3 \times 18) \times 10 = (80 - 54) \times 10 = 260 \, \text{mm}^2$$

$$T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times 410 \times 260}{1.25} = 76.75 \, \text{kN} \; > \; 60 \, \text{kN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final design: 3 nos. 16 mm dia., grade 4.6 bolts in a single row, 80 × 10 mm Fe 410 plates.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Connection Type vs. Property

| Property | Riveted | Bearing Bolt | HSFG Bolt | Fillet Weld |
|----------|---------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| Slip allowed at SLS | Yes | Yes | **No** | Negligible |
| Ductility | Moderate | Moderate | High | **High** |
| Pre-tension required | No | No | **Yes (controlled)** | No |
| Inspection ease | Poor | Easy | Easy (torque) | Moderate (NDT) |
| Strength efficiency (% of plate) | 60–70 | 70–80 | **85–95** | **95–100** |
| Site labour skill | High (lost) | Medium | High | High |
| Common in farm structures | **None now** | **Yes — purlin cleats, bracing** | **Yes — main trusses** | **Yes — prefabricated sheds** |

---

## 3.5 Quick Step Algorithm — Solving any "Connection" Question

Use this flow whenever you see a 14-marks "design/check a connection" problem:

**Step 1 — Identify materials and grade** (steel, bolt grade, weld type, shop/field).

**Step 2 — List all relevant f_y, f_u, γ_m values.** Write them in a small box at the top of the answer.

**Step 3 — Identify governing failure mode** (bolt shear, bearing, plate rupture, weld throat).

**Step 4 — Compute nominal strength** of each failure mode.

**Step 5 — Divide by γ_m to get design strength.**

**Step 6 — Compare design strength with factored load.**
- Strength ≥ Load → SAFE
- Strength < Load → UNSAFE → redesign (more bolts, larger diameter, thicker plate, higher grade).

**Step 7 — State clearly: SAFE / UNSAFE and the controlling failure mode.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Steel Connections

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Steel Connections in Farm Structures]
    A0 --> B1[Type 1 - Riveted]
    A0 --> B2[Type 2 - Bolted]
    A0 --> B3[Type 3 - Welded]

    B1 --> C1A[Hot driven]
    B1 --> C1B[Cold driven]

    B2 --> C2A[Bearing type]
    B2 --> C2B[Friction grip HSFG]

    C2A --> D2A1[Grade 4.6]
    C2A --> D2A2[Grade 8.8 bearing]
    C2B --> D2B1[Grade 8.8 HSFG]
    C2B --> D2B2[Grade 10.9 HSFG]

    B3 --> C3A[Fillet weld]
    B3 --> C3B[Butt weld]

    C3A --> D3A1[Transverse load]
    C3A --> D3A2[Parallel load]
    C3A --> D3A3[Combined load]
    C3B --> D3B1[Single V groove]
    C3B --> D3B2[Double V groove]
    C3B --> D3B3[U and J groove]

    D2A1 --> E1[Used in purlin cleats and bracing]
    D2B2 --> E2[Used in long span trusses and towers]
    D3A3 --> E3[Used in prefabricated godown frames]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Design Algorithm for Bearing-Type Bolted Connection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start - Given factored load Tu and plate details]
    S1[Step 1 - Assume bolt diameter d]
    S2[Step 2 - Compute hole d0 = d + clearance]
    S3[Step 3 - Compute bolt shear strength Vdsb]
    S4[Step 4 - Compute bolt bearing strength Vdpb]
    S5[Step 5 - Compute plate net section strength Tdn]
    S6[Step 6 - Bolt strength Vdb = min Vdsb and Vdpb]
    S7[Step 7 - Joint capacity Tdj = min Vdb and Tdn]
    S8{Tdj greater or equal Tu}
    S9[Design is SAFE - Finalise spacing and edge distance]
    S10[Design is UNSAFE - Increase d or n or plate thickness]

    S0 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
    S8 -- Yes --> S9
    S8 -- No --> S10
    S10 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fillet Weld Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G0[Right isosceles triangle]
    G1[Leg length s mm]
    G2[Effective throat tt = 0.707 s mm]
    G3[Force F applied parallel to plate surface]
    G4[Failure plane inclined at 45 degrees along throat]
    G5[Weld strength per mm = 0.707 s x fwd]

    G0 --> G1
    G0 --> G2
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> G5
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Typical Farm Truss Joint (Bolted Gusset)

```
                RAFTER
                  |
                  |  (incline)
                  |________
                  |        \
                  |   GUSSET PLATE
                  |     ____|____
                  |    | o   o   |  ← Bolts (HSFG 10.9)
   ———————————————+————| o   o   |———————————————
        TIE MEMBER     |____|____|
                         |
                         |  (vertical strut)
                         |
```

**Reading the diagram:** The gusset plate receives the rafter's thrust from above and the tie member's pull from the side. Bolts are arranged in **two vertical columns** (chain of bolts in the line of force), providing redundancy — if one bolt slips, the rest can hold temporarily.

## 4.5 Force-Flow Block Diagram — Failure Mode Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Factored Load Tu]
    F0 --> F1{Where does failure occur?}
    F1 -- Bolt shank --> F2[Shear rupture Vdsb]
    F1 -- Hole wall --> F3[Bearing failure Vdpb]
    F1 -- Plate section --> F4[Tension rupture Tdn]
    F1 -- Corner of plate --> F5[Block shear]
    F2 --> F6[Governing capacity = minimum of all four]
    F3 --> F6
    F4 --> F6
    F5 --> F6
    F6 --> F7[Compare with Tu and conclude]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]** 
*List any **six mechanical properties** of structural steel that are relevant to the design of farm buildings. State the corresponding **IS code reference**.*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Yield strength** f_y = 250 N/mm² for E250 (Fe 410).
2. **Ultimate tensile strength** f_u = 410 N/mm².
3. **Modulus of elasticity** E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm².
4. **Shear modulus** G = 0.769 × 10⁵ N/mm².
5. **Poisson's ratio** ν = 0.30.
6. **Density** ρ = 7850 kg/m³.
7. **Elongation at fracture** ≥ 23 %.

**IS code:** Mechanical properties are governed by **IS 2062:2011**; design rules by **IS 800:2007**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]** 
*Distinguish between **bearing-type** and **friction-grip (HSFG) bolted connections** in two points each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bearing-Type Bolt | HSFG Bolt |
|--------|-------------------|-----------|
| Pre-tension | **Not controlled** | **Highly pre-tensioned (~ 70 % of bolt capacity)** |
| Load transfer | Bolt shank **bears** against hole wall | **Friction** between clamped plates |
| Slip at SLS | **Permitted** | **Not permitted** (slip-critical) |
| Typical grade | 4.6 / 8.8 | 8.8 / 10.9 |
| Application | Light roof trusses, bracing | Heavy trusses, machinery bases |

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A(a) [7 Marks]** — *Derive the expression for the **effective throat thickness** of a 90° fillet weld and state its value in terms of weld size s.* **[KTU University Exam — Module 2, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Solution:**

Place the weld cross-section in an x-y plane with legs along the axes.

**Step 1:** Coordinates of hypotenuse endpoints = (s, 0) and (0, s).

**Step 2:** Equation of hypotenuse = x/s + y/s = 1 → x + y − s = 0.

**Step 3:** Perpendicular distance from origin (weld root) = |0 + 0 − s| / √(1² + 1²) = s / √2.

**Step 4:** Numerical evaluation: s / 1.4142 = **0.707 s**.

$$\boxed{t_t = 0.707 \, s}$$

**Step 5:** Hence strength per mm of weld = 0.707 × s × f_wd, where f_wd = f_u / (√3 × γ_mw).

---

**Q.A(b) [7 Marks]** — *A tie member in a farm godown truss consists of a single angle **75 × 75 × 8 mm** of Fe 410 steel, connected to a 10 mm gusset plate by **four 16 mm dia. bolts of grade 4.6**. Check whether the joint can transfer a **factored tensile load of 200 kN**.* **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**

**Given:**
- Plate & angle: Fe 410, f_u = 410 N/mm², f_y = 250 N/mm².
- Bolt 4.6: f_ub = 400 N/mm²; d = 16 mm; d_0 = 18 mm; n_s = 1 (single shear, lap joint).
- T_u = 200 kN.

**Step 1 — Bolt shear strength (per bolt):**

$$A_{nb} = 0.78 \times \tfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 156.83 \, \text{mm}^2$$

$$V_{nsb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \times A_{nb} = \frac{400}{1.732} \times 156.83 = 36.22 \, \text{kN}$$

$$V_{dsb} = \frac{36.22}{1.25} = 28.98 \, \text{kN per bolt}$$

For 4 bolts: 4 × 28.98 = **115.92 kN**

**Step 2 — Bolt bearing strength (per bolt):**

Assume k_b = 1.0 and bearing on 8 mm angle leg.

$$V_{npb} = 2.5 \times k_b \times d \times t \times f_u = 2.5 \times 1.0 \times 16 \times 8 \times 410$$

$$V_{npb} = 131\,200 \, \text{N} = 131.2 \, \text{kN per bolt}$$

$$V_{dpb} = \frac{131.2}{1.25} = 104.96 \, \text{kN per bolt}$$

For 4 bolts: 4 × 104.96 = **419.84 kN**

**Step 3 — Net section rupture of angle:**

Area of 75 × 75 × 8 angle = **1154 mm²** (from steel tables).

Net area (1 hole in line):

$$A_n = (1154 - 18 \times 8) = 1154 - 144 = 1010 \, \text{mm}^2$$

(Note: actually use connected leg deduction method from IS 800:2007 Table — but using simplified approach here.)

$$T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times 410 \times 1010}{1.25} = \frac{372\,690}{1.25} = 298.2 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Joint capacity:**

$$T_{dj} = \min(115.92, 419.84, 298.2) = \mathbf{115.92 \, kN}$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$T_u = 200 \, \text{kN} > T_{dj} = 115.92 \, \text{kN} \Rightarrow \boxed{\text{UNSAFE}}$$
