**Conclusion:** Governing failure mode is **bolt shear**. Need either more bolts, larger diameter, or higher grade.

**Quick re-design:** Use **8 nos. 16 mm bolts** → 8 × 28.98 = 231.84 kN > 200 kN ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B(a) [7 Marks]** — *Explain with neat sketches the **three principal types of steel connections** used in farm structures. Mention the **IS code** governing each.* **[KTU University Exam — Module 2, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Solution:**

The three principal types are: **(1) Riveted, (2) Bolted, (3) Welded.**

**(1) Riveted Connection:** A heated rivet is inserted into pre-punched holes of the plates and hammered to form a closing head. On cooling, it grips the plates by shrinkage and transfers load via shear in the rivet and bearing on the hole. **IS code: IS 800:2007** (rules retained for historical reference; not for new design).

**(2) Bolted Connection:** A bolt with a head on one side and nut on the other clamps the plates together. Two sub-types: (a) **Bearing-type** — load is transferred through bearing of the bolt shank on the hole wall (slip is permitted), (b) **HSFG / friction-grip** — high pre-tension produces clamping friction; no slip at service. **IS code: IS 800:2007, IS 3757** (HSFG).

**(3) Welded Connection:** Two steel surfaces are fused by melting filler metal at high temperature. Two sub-types: (a) **Fillet weld** (triangular cross-section, no plate edge preparation), (b) **Butt weld** (full penetration through plate thickness). **IS code: IS 816:1969, IS 800:2007, AWS D1.1**.

**Sketches:** The ASCII schematic in §4.4 above illustrates a bolted gusset connection in a typical farm truss.

---

**Q.B(b) [7 Marks]** — *A roof truss in a dairy plant uses **fillet welds** to connect a 100 × 8 mm rafter to a gusset plate. Design the **shop fillet weld length** required to transfer a **factored tensile load of 120 kN** along the rafter. Assume **Fe 410** steel and 6 mm weld size.* **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**

**Given:** T_u = 120 kN; s = 6 mm; shop weld; Fe 410.

**Step 1 — Design weld stress:**

$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} = 189.4 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Effective throat:**

$$t_t = 0.707 \times 6 = 4.243 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Strength per mm length:**

$$R_w = 4.243 \times 189.4 = 803.6 \, \text{N/mm} \approx 0.804 \, \text{kN/mm}$$

**Step 4 — Required weld length (assuming weld on both sides of rafter, i.e., 2 welds in parallel):**

$$L_{total} = \frac{120}{0.804} = 149.3 \, \text{mm}$$

Provide **two welds, each 75 mm long** on opposite sides of the rafter (one on each edge of the gusset). Use effective length per side = 75 mm, after deducting 2 × 6 = 12 mm for run-on/run-off (as per IS 800:2007 clause for end returns).

$$L_{eff} = 75 - 12 = 63 \, \text{mm per side}$$

Total effective length = 2 × 63 = 126 mm.

$$R_w = 126 \times 0.804 = 101.3 \, \text{kN} \quad < 120 \, \text{kN} \quad \text{(UNSAFE)}$$

**Step 5 — Re-design: increase side length:**

Need L_total_eff ≥ 120 / 0.804 = **149.3 mm** → use 2 × 85 mm per side (with end returns).

$$L_{eff,total} = 2 \times (85 - 12) = 146 \, \text{mm}$$

Try 2 × 90 mm per side:

$$L_{eff,total} = 2 \times (90 - 12) = 156 \, \text{mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_w = 156 \times 0.804 = 125.4 \, \text{kN} \quad > \; 120 \, \text{kN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final design:** **Two 90 mm long, 6 mm size shop fillet welds** (one on each side of the 100 × 8 mm rafter).

---

## 5.6 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.78 factor** for threaded shank area (A_nb) — costs **1 full mark** in shear-strength calculation.
> 2. **Using f_y instead of f_u** in bolt/weld strength equations — **2 marks deducted**. Remember: **f_y for plate yielding; f_u for bolt/weld rupture.**
> 3. **Mixing γ_M1 = 1.25 with γ_M0 = 1.10** — for plate in tension at **net section (rupture)**, use **γ_M1 = 1.25**, *not* γ_M0.
> 4. **Wrong clearance for hole**: d_0 = d + 2 mm for d = 16–24 mm; d_0 = d + 3 mm for d ≥ 27 mm. Wrong d_0 → wrong net area → wrong answer.
> 5. **Forgetting end-return deduction** for fillet welds — weld length should deduct 2 × s for start/stop, especially important in 7-mark parts.
> 6. **Not specifying shop vs. field weld** — γ_mw is **1.25 (shop) vs. 1.50 (field)**. This distinction is worth 1 mark.
> 7. **Forgetting units** (N/mm² vs. MPa) — usually not penalized, but inconsistent units across one calculation are.
> 8. **Final conclusion** — students write "UNSAFE" but forget to **propose a re-design** (e.g., "increase to 8 bolts"). This costs 1 mark in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### A. Material Memory Points (Write Once, Recall Forever)
- **E250 / Fe 410**: f_y = **250 N/mm²**, f_u = **410 N/mm²**, E = **2 × 10⁵ N/mm²**, ν = 0.30, ρ = **7850 kg/m³**.
- IS code: **IS 2062:2011** for material; **IS 800:2007** for design.

### B. Connection Selection Decision Tree (Quick Recall)
- **Field joint + light load** → Bearing bolt grade 4.6.
- **Field joint + heavy / cyclic load** → HSFG bolt grade 8.8/10.9.
- **Shop joint + maximum strength** → Fillet or butt weld.
- **Old drawings** → Rivets (mention as obsolete).

### C. Formula Memory Anchors
- Throat of fillet weld: **t_t = 0.707 s**.
- Weld design stress: **f_wd = f_u / (√3 × γ_mw)** = **189.4 N/mm²** (shop, Fe 410) or **157.8 N/mm²** (field).
- Net area with straight holes: **A_n = (b − n·d_0)·t**.
- Stagger deduction (per pitch-gage): **s²/(4g)** added back to net width.
- Bolt shear capacity: **V_dsb = f_ub·A_nb·n_s / (√3·γ_mb)**.
- Bolt bearing capacity: **V_dpb = 2.5·k_b·d·t·f_u / γ_mb**.

### D. Key Partial Safety Factors (LSM)
- γ_M0 = **1.10** (yielding), γ_M1 = **1.25** (rupture), γ_Mb = **1.25** (bolt), γ_Mw = **1.25 / 1.50** (weld, shop / field), γ_Mf = **1.10 / 1.25** (HSFG service / ultimate).

### E. Exam Strategy — 14 Mark Question
1. **1 mark** — Restating given data.
2. **2 marks** — Computing bolt/weld nominal strength.
3. **2 marks** — Dividing by γ_m → design strength.
4. **2 marks** — Checking plate net section.
5. **1 mark** — Final comparison.
6. **1 mark** — Conclusion + re-design recommendation.

### F. Three Things to Say in Every "Types of Connection" Theory Answer
1. **Riveted** (obsolete, historical).
2. **Bolted** — *bearing type + HSFG (friction-grip)*.
3. **Welded** — *fillet + butt (with full / partial penetration)*.
Always cite **IS 800:2007** as the governing design code.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Welded Connection and Riveted Connection; Design of Tension Members and Compression Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Welded Connection and Riveted Connection; Design of Tension Members and Compression Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Welded Connection — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WELDED CONNECTION (As per IS 816:1969 & IS 800:2007)**
> A welded connection is a permanent, monolithic joint formed by heating the contacting surfaces of two structural steel members to a plastic or fluid state, with or without the application of filler metal, and allowing the molten metal to fuse and solidify into a continuous metallurgical bond possessing strength equal to or greater than the parent metal.

In farm structures (trusses for storage sheds, poultry buildings, dairy barns, greenhouses, agricultural warehouses), welded connections are widely preferred over riveted connections due to their **higher strength-to-weight ratio, faster fabrication, lower material wastage, and superior aesthetic finish**.

### 1.2 Riveted Connection — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RIVETED CONNECTION (As per IS 800:2007)**
> A riveted connection is a permanent, non-loosening joint formed by inserting a ductile metal pin (called a rivet) through pre-punched or drilled holes in the members to be joined, and upsetting one end (the manufactured head) to form a second head (the shop head) on the protruding shank, thus clamping the connected plates between the two heads.

Although largely replaced by welding in modern farm construction, riveted connections remain relevant for:
- Repair and rehabilitation of **old heritage agricultural buildings**.
- Light-weight trusses where **field conditions limit welding** (e.g., remote hill farms).
- Historical understanding of structural behavior in IS 800 design examples.

### 1.3 Tension Member — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TENSION MEMBER**
> A structural member of a farm building (typically a tie/rod/purlin tie in roof trusses, bottom chord of a roof truss, or bracing member) that is subjected to axial tensile force tending to elongate the member along its longitudinal axis.

### 1.4 Compression Member — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **COMPRESSION MEMBER**
> A structural member of a farm building (top chord of a roof truss, vertical post, column, strut, or diagonal web member) that is subjected to axial compressive force tending to shorten the member, making it susceptible to buckling failure.

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — Real-World Intuition

| Connection Type | Real-World Analogy | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Welded Connection** | Imagine joining two pieces of butter by melting their edges and pressing them — they become ONE piece. No separate fastener. | Atomic-level bonding; no weak interfaces. |
| **Riveted Connection** | Imagine fastening two pieces of leather with a metal pin and hammering the protruding end flat — the pin grips the plates by friction and shear. | Mechanical interlock through clamping force. |
| **Tension Member** | A rope pulling a bucket of grain from a well — the rope stretches (elongates) under load. | Resists elongation via axial pull. |
| **Compression Member** | A bamboo pole holding up a tarpaulin roof of a farm shed — the pole shortens, but may buckle sideways. | Resists shortening, but vulnerable to instability. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reference (IS 800:2007)**
> - Yield stress of structural steel $f_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$ (Fe 410 grade)
> - Ultimate tensile strength $f_u = 410 \text{ MPa}$
> - Partial safety factor for material, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.10$ (yielding)
> - Partial safety factor for material, $\gamma_{m2} = 1.25$ (ultimate)
> - Modulus of Elasticity, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa} = 200 \text{ GPa}$
> - Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.30$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Types of Welded Joints

**A. Fillet Weld** — The most common in farm trusses. Triangular cross-section filling the corner of two plates.

- **Size of fillet weld (s):** Minimum leg dimension measured on the face of the triangle.
- **Throat thickness (t_t):** The perpendicular distance from the root to the hypotenuse. For an equal-legged fillet weld:
$$t_t = s \cos 45^\circ = 0.707\,s$$

- **Effective throat thickness:** $t_e = 0.7 \times s$ (IS 800:2007, Clause 10.5.3.1)

- **Strength of a fillet weld per unit length:**
$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \times \gamma_{mw}}$$

where $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ for shop welds and **1.50** for field welds (with matching electrodes).

- **Design strength of fillet weld:**
$$R_{dw} = 0.7 \times s \times f_{wd} \text{ (per mm length)}$$

**B. Butt Weld** — Plates lie in the same plane; weld metal fills the gap.

- If weld strength = parent metal strength, butt weld is called a **complete penetration butt weld** — designed for full capacity of the parent member.
- If full strength is not needed, **partial penetration butt weld** is used (effective throat = depth of penetration).

**C. Plug & Slot Welds** — Used to fill holes/slots, mainly for shear transfer in light farm structures.

### 2.2 Failure Modes of a Riveted Joint

A riveted joint in a farm truss can fail in **four** distinct ways. The joint must be designed such that the lowest failure load governs the design.

1. **Shearing of the rivet** (single or double shear).
2. **Bearing / Crushing** of the rivet on the plate hole.
3. **Tearing of the plate** (tension failure along the net section).
4. **Edge shearing / Tearing of the plate** (along the edge — addressed by edge distance).

### 2.3 IS 800:2007 Formula Sheet — Welded & Riveted Connections

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective throat of fillet weld | $t_e = 0.7\,s$ | mm | Per IS 800 Cl. 10.5.3.1 |
| 2 | Strength of fillet weld (per mm) | $f_{wd} = f_u / (\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw})$ | N/mm | $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ |
| 3 | Design strength of fillet weld | $R_{dw} = t_e \cdot f_{wd}$ | N/mm |  |
| 4 | Rivet single shear strength | $R_{dvs} = (\pi/4)\,d^2 \cdot \tau_{vf} / \gamma_{mb}$ | N | $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ |
| 5 | Rivet double shear strength | $R_{dvd} = 2 \times R_{dvs}$ | N | For double shear |
| 6 | Rivet bearing strength | $R_{dpb} = d \cdot t \cdot f_{pb} / \gamma_{mb}$ | N | $f_{pb} = 0.5\,f_u$ |
| 7 | Tensile strength of net section | $T_{dn} = 0.9\,A_n \cdot f_u / \gamma_{mb}$ | N | For plates with holes |
| 8 | Strength of gross section (yielding) | $T_{dg} = A_g \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | N | $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ |

### 2.4 Design of Tension Members — Theory

**Key design checks (IS 800:2007, Cl. 6.1):**

1. **Gross section yielding:** $T_{dg} = A_g \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$
2. **Rupture of net section:** $T_{dn} = 0.9 \cdot A_n \cdot f_u / \gamma_{m1}$ (where $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$)
3. **Block shear failure:** $T_{db} = (A_{vg} \cdot f_y / \sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}) + (0.9 \cdot A_{tn} \cdot f_u / \gamma_{m1})$

**Net sectional area for tension members with staggered holes:**

$$A_n = (B - n \cdot d_h) \cdot t + \sum \frac{s^2}{4g} \cdot t$$

> Where:
> - $B$ = width of plate
> - $n$ = number of holes in critical section
> - $d_h$ = diameter of hole (= rivet diameter + clearance)
> - $s$ = staggered pitch (transverse spacing)
> - $g$ = gauge (longitudinal spacing between holes)
> - $t$ = plate thickness

### 2.5 Design of Compression Members — Theory

**Axial compressive strength of a column (IS 800:2007, Cl. 7.1.2.1):**

$$P_d = A_e \cdot f_{cd}$$

where the design compressive stress $f_{cd}$ depends on the **slenderness ratio** $\lambda$.

**Non-dimensional slenderness:**
$$\lambda_{nd} = \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{f_{cc}}} = \sqrt{\frac{f_y \cdot (KL/r)^2}{\pi^2 \cdot E}}$$

where $f_{cc} = \pi^2 E / (KL/r)^2$ is the **Euler buckling stress**.

**Design compressive stress (for $\lambda_{nd} \le \sqrt{2}$ — short column):**
$$f_{cd} = \frac{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_{nd}^2}} \le f_y / \gamma_{m0}$$

where $\phi = 0.5 \cdot [1 + \alpha \cdot (\lambda_{nd} - 0.2) + \lambda_{nd}^2]$ and $\alpha = 0.49$ (imperfection factor, buckling curve 'a').

**Slenderness ratio limits (Cl. 7.2):**
- For **braced compression members**: $\lambda = L/r \le 180$
- For **unbraced compression members**: $\lambda = L/r \le 250$

> [!NOTE]
> **Slenderness ratio (λ)** is the single most important parameter for compression members. A farm building column in a humid environment (e.g., a poultry farm) is exposed to moisture, so designers prefer **stocky columns** (low λ) to avoid flexural buckling.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Farm Roof Trusses** (gable, lean-to, king-post, queen-post, fink-type): Top chord = compression, bottom chord = tension, diagonals = alternate tension/compression depending on wind/snow load direction.
- **Welded gusset plate connections** at the joints of trusses carry 100% of the member forces.
- **Riveted truss connections** in heritage buildings (e.g., old colonial-era warehouses) need re-evaluation per IS 800 to assess remaining life.
- **Compression columns** in open-sided cattle sheds are vulnerable to slenderness buckling due to long, slender posts supporting roof loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Strength of an Equal-Legged Fillet Weld

**Given:** A fillet weld with leg size $s$, connecting two plates of Fe 410 grade steel. Weld is shop-welded.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Weld:**
For a $45^\circ$ fillet weld, the throat is the altitude of the isosceles right triangle:
$$t = s \cdot \sin 45^\circ = s \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707\,s$$

**Step 2 — Effective Throat (as per IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.5.3.1):**
The actual throat is conservatively reduced:
$$t_e = 0.7\,s$$

**Step 3 — Shear Yield Stress in the Throat Plane:**
By Von-Mises yield criterion, the shear yield stress of the weld metal is:
$$\tau_y = \frac{f_y}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{0.577 \cdot f_y}{1}$$

For the weld electrode (matching parent metal $f_u$):
$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

**Step 4 — Design Strength per Unit Length:**
$$R_{dw} = t_e \cdot f_{wd} = 0.7\,s \cdot \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**
For Fe 410 ($f_u = 410$ MPa), $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$, weld size $s = 6$ mm:
$$R_{dw} = 0.7 \times 6 \times \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.50} \text{ N/mm}$$
$$R_{dw} = 4.2 \times \frac{410}{2.598} = 4.2 \times 157.78 = 662.7 \text{ N/mm}$$

> For a 100 mm long fillet weld, total strength = $662.7 \times 100 = 66{,}270$ N = **66.27 kN**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Strength of a Riveted Joint (Single Shear)

**Step 1 — Shear Failure of Rivet:**
Shear area of a single rivet:
$$A_{sv} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2$$

**Step 2 — Design Shear Strength:**
$$R_{dvs} = \frac{A_{sv} \cdot \tau_{vf}}{\gamma_{mb}}$$

For **4.6 grade** rivets: $\tau_{vf} = 320$ MPa; for **property class 4.6** $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$.

**Step 3 — Bearing Failure of Rivet on Plate:**
Projected bearing area = $d \times t$ (rivet diameter × thinner plate thickness).
$$R_{dpb} = \frac{d \cdot t \cdot f_{pb}}{\gamma_{mb}}$$

where $f_{pb} = 0.5 \cdot f_u$ of the plate (as per Table 1 of IS 800).

**Step 4 — Design Strength of the Rivet:**
$$R_{dv} = \min(R_{dvs},\; R_{dpb})$$

**Step 5 — Number of Rivets Required:**
$$n = \frac{P}{R_{dv}}$$

where $P$ = applied tensile force on the joint.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Net Sectional Area of a Tension Member with Staggered Holes

**Given:** A plate of width $B$ and thickness $t$ has 3 rivet holes in a zig-zag (staggered) pattern. The critical section passes through hole 1, then **diagonally** to hole 2, then to hole 3.

**Step 1 — First Path (Straight Across Through Holes 1 & 3):**
$$A_{n1} = (B - 2 d_h) \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Second Path (Staggered):**
$$A_{n2} = \left[(B - 2 d_h) + \frac{s^2}{4g}\right] \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Choose Critical Section:**
$$A_n = \min(A_{n1}, A_{n2}, A_{n3}, \ldots)$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The diagonal path is longer, so it has **more material**, making it less critical. However, we must check ALL possible failure paths.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Design Compressive Stress Using IS 800 Perry-Robertson Formula

**Step 1 — Euler Buckling Stress:**
$$f_{cc} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E}{\lambda^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^5}{(L_e/r)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Non-dimensional Slenderness:**
$$\lambda_{nd} = \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{f_{cc}}} = \frac{L_e/r}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{E}}$$

**Step 3 — Perry-Robertson Parameter (Buckling Curve 'a'):**
$$\phi = 0.5 \cdot \left[1 + 0.49 \cdot (\lambda_{nd} - 0.2) + \lambda_{nd}^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Design Compressive Stress:**
$$f_{cd} = \frac{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_{nd}^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Design Axial Strength:**
$$P_d = A_e \cdot f_{cd}$$

> For **slenderness λ ≤ 26.5** ($\lambda_{nd} \le 0.2$): Column fails by **crushing (squash load)**, $P_d = A \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 800:2007 Buckling Curve Selection (Table 7, Cl. 7.1.2.2):**
> - Curve 'a' → Hollow tubes, rolled I-sections (major axis)
> - Curve 'b' → Welded I-sections, rolled I (minor axis), channels
> - Curve 'c' → Built-up box sections, riveted plate columns
> - Curve 'd' → Highly imperfect, hot-rolled angles

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Design of a Tension Member in a Farm Roof Truss

**Problem:** Design a single-angle tension member of a fink-type roof truss for a farm storage shed to carry a factored tensile force $T_u = 280$ kN. Use Fe 410 steel.

**Step 1 — Gross Area Required (to prevent yielding):**
$$A_g \ge \frac{T_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{f_y} = \frac{280 \times 10^3 \times 1.10}{250} = 1232 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Try 80 mm × 80 mm × 10 mm angle (IS 808):**
$A_g = 1510 \text{ mm}^2$, $r_{min} = 15.6$ mm, $C_{xx} = 23.0$ mm, $C_{yy} = 23.0$ mm.

**Step 3 — Hole Diameter:**
For 16 mm rivets: $d_h = 16 + 1.5 = 17.5$ mm.

**Step 4 — Net Area (one hole through one leg):**
$$A_n = (A_g - d_h \cdot t) = 1510 - 17.5 \times 10 = 1335 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Check Net Section Rupture:**
$$T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \cdot A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}} = \frac{0.9 \times 1335 \times 410}{1.25} = 394{,}038 \text{ N} = 394 \text{ kN} \ge 280 \text{ kN} \; \surd$$

**Step 6 — Check Gross Section Yielding:**
$$T_{dg} = \frac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{1510 \times 250}{1.10} = 343{,}182 \text{ N} = 343.2 \text{ kN} \ge 280 \text{ kN} \; \surd$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** 80 × 80 × 10 mm single angle is **SAFE** for 280 kN tension.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example: Design of a Compression Member (Column) in a Cattle Shed

**Problem:** Design a single-angle strut of length $L = 3.0$ m, pinned at both ends ($K = 1.0$), to carry a factored compressive load $P_u = 180$ kN. Fe 410 steel.

**Step 1 — Try 90 × 90 × 10 mm angle:** $A_g = 1700 \text{ mm}^2$, $r_{min} = 17.1$ mm.

**Step 2 — Effective Length:** $L_e = K \cdot L = 1.0 \times 3000 = 3000$ mm.

**Step 3 — Slenderness Ratio:**
$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r_{min}} = \frac{3000}{17.1} = 175.4$$

This **exceeds the IS 800 limit of 180 for braced members** — borderline, but acceptable.

**Step 4 — Euler Buckling Stress:**
$$f_{cc} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^5}{175.4^2} = \frac{1.974 \times 10^6}{30{,}765} = 64.16 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Non-dimensional Slenderness:**
$$\lambda_{nd} = \sqrt{\frac{250}{64.16}} = \sqrt{3.896} = 1.974$$

**Step 6 — Perry Parameter (Curve 'c' for angle strut):**
$$\phi = 0.5 \cdot [1 + 0.49 \cdot (1.974 - 0.2) + 1.974^2] = 0.5 \cdot [1 + 0.869 + 3.896] = 2.883$$

**Step 7 — Design Compressive Stress:**
$$f_{cd} = \frac{250 / 1.10}{2.883 + \sqrt{2.883^2 - 1.974^2}} = \frac{227.27}{2.883 + \sqrt{8.312 - 3.896}} = \frac{227.27}{2.883 + 2.105} = 45.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 8 — Design Strength:**
$$P_d = A_g \cdot f_{cd} = 1700 \times 45.6 = 77{,}520 \text{ N} = 77.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Result: 77.5 kN < 180 kN — UNSAFE!** ✗

**Step 9 — Revise: Try 100 × 100 × 12 mm angle:** $A_g = 2270 \text{ mm}^2$, $r_{min} = 19.4$ mm.
$$\lambda = \frac{3000}{19.4} = 154.6, \quad f_{cc} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}{154.6^2} = 82.5 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\lambda_{nd} = \sqrt{250/82.5} = 1.741, \quad \phi = 0.5 \cdot [1 + 0.49 \cdot 1.541 + 3.031] = 0.5 \cdot [1 + 0.755 + 3.031] = 2.393$$
$$f_{cd} = \frac{227.27}{2.393 + \sqrt{5.726 - 3.031}} = \frac{227.27}{2.393 + 1.640} = 56.3 \text{ MPa}$$
$$P_d = 2270 \times 56.3 = 127{,}801 \text{ N} = 127.8 \text{ kN} < 180 \text{ kN — Still UNSAFE!}$$

**Step 10 — Try Double Angle (Back-to-Back, 100 × 100 × 10 mm, gusset 12 mm):**
Combined $A_g = 2 \times 1920 = 3840$ mm², $r_{min}$ of section = 30.6 mm.
$$\lambda = \frac{3000}{30.6} = 98.0, \quad f_{cc} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}{98^2} = 205.5 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\lambda_{nd} = \sqrt{250/205.5} = 1.103, \quad \phi = 0.5 \cdot [1 + 0.49 \cdot 0.903 + 1.217] = 0.5 \cdot [1 + 0.443 + 1.217] = 1.330$$
$$f_{cd} = \frac{227.27}{1.330 + \sqrt{1.769 - 1.217}} = \frac{227.27}{1.330 + 0.742} = 109.7 \text{ MPa}$$
$$P_d = 3840 \times 109.7 = 421{,}248 \text{ N} = 421.2 \text{ kN} \ge 180 \text{ kN} \; \surd \text{ SAFE!}$$

> **Conclusion:** A double-angle back-to-back 100 × 100 × 10 mm strut is required to safely carry 180 kN over 3.0 m.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define (a) effective throat thickness of a fillet weld, and (b) size of a fillet weld. State the IS code relationship.**

> **Model Answer:**
> - **(a) Effective throat thickness (t_e):** The minimum distance from the root of the fillet weld to the hypotenuse of the inscribed triangle. It is the design dimension used for strength calculation. As per IS 800:2007 (Cl. 10.5.3.1): $t_e = 0.7\,s$.
> - **(b) Size of a fillet weld (s):** The leg length of the fillet weld, measured along the plate face from the joint root to the toe of the weld. For a $45^\circ$ fillet weld, the throat is $0.707\,s$ geometrically, but conservatively taken as $0.7\,s$ for design.
> - **Standard relationship:** $t_e = 0.7 \times s$ (where $s$ is the leg size in mm).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the four possible failure modes of a riveted joint and write the formula for each.**

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Shearing of rivet** — $R_{dvs} = (\pi/4)\,d^2 \cdot \tau_{vf} / \gamma_{mb}$ (single shear)
> 2. **Bearing/Crushing of plate** — $R_{dpb} = d \cdot t \cdot f_{pb} / \gamma_{mb}$ where $f_{pb} = 0.5\,f_u$
> 3. **Tearing of plate at net section** — $T_{dn} = 0.9 \cdot A_n \cdot f_u / \gamma_{m1}$
> 4. **Shearing of edge of plate** — Prevented by providing **edge distance** = 1.5 × hole diameter (for sheared edges) or 1.7 × hole diameter (for rolled/sawn edges).
>
> The joint strength is governed by the **minimum** of these four values.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Welded + Tension Member Design**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Question]**

A simply-supported fink-type roof truss of a farm storage shed spans **8 m** and is spaced at **3.5 m c/c**. The truss has been designed, and a diagonal web member carries a maximum factored tensile force of **220 kN**. Design:
**(a)** The welded connection of the diagonal member to a **12 mm thick gusset plate** using **6 mm equal-legged fillet welds** along the two sides of the member. Assume the diagonal is a single angle 80 × 50 × 8 mm. Verify whether the welds are adequate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The tension member itself (single angle 80 × 50 × 8 mm) connected to the gusset plate with **one 16 mm diameter bolt** at each connection through the 80 mm leg. Check if the net section and the gross section are safe. Take $f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa. Use IS 800:2007. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Weld Strength Check

**Given:**
- Size of fillet weld, $s = 6$ mm
- Length of weld on each side (assuming full depth of angle 80 mm minus edge cut) $\approx 60$ mm on each side
- Factored tensile force $T_u = 220$ kN
- $f_u = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$

**Step 1 — Effective throat thickness:**
$$t_e = 0.7 \times 6 = 4.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Design strength of weld per mm length:**
$$f_{wd} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.50} = \frac{410}{2.598} = 157.78 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Strength of weld per mm:**
$$R_{dw} = t_e \cdot f_{wd} = 4.2 \times 157.78 = 662.7 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 4 — Total effective weld length (2 sides × 60 mm each = 120 mm):**
$$R_{total} = 662.7 \times 120 = 79{,}524 \text{ N} = 79.52 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Load per weld group:**
Required weld strength = 220 kN > 79.52 kN ⟹ **WELD INADEQUATE** ✗

**Step 6 — Increase weld size to 8 mm (maximum allowed for 8 mm angle leg = 8 − 1.5 = 6.5 mm, so use 6 mm only). Try 6 mm with full length 70 mm on each side:**

$$L_{req} = \frac{220 \times 10^3}{662.7 \times 2 \text{ sides}} = \frac{220{,}000}{1325.4} = 166 \text{ mm per side}$$

**Result:** Use 6 mm fillet welds of length **170 mm on each side** (return welds of 12 mm at top and bottom).

#### Part (b) — Tension Member Check

**Given:** 80 × 50 × 8 mm single unequal angle.
- $A_g = 952$ mm² (Crompton Greaves SP-6, IS 808)
- One 16 mm hole in 80 mm leg
- $d_h = 16 + 1.5 = 17.5$ mm, $t = 8$ mm

**Step 1 — Net Sectional Area:**
$$A_n = 952 - (17.5 \times 8) = 952 - 140 = 812 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Net Section Rupture Strength:**
$$T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times 812 \times 410}{1.25} = \frac{299{,}628}{1.25} = 239{,}702 \text{ N} = 239.7 \text{ kN}$$
$$239.7 \text{ kN} \ge 220 \text{ kN} \;\; \surd \text{ SAFE}$$

**Step 3 — Gross Section Yielding:**
$$T_{dg} = \frac{952 \times 250}{1.10} = 216{,}364 \text{ N} = 216.4 \text{ kN}$$
$$216.4 \text{ kN} < 220 \text{ kN} \;\; \text{✗ GROSS SECTION YIELDING FAILS!}$$

**Step 4 — Revise Section to 90 × 50 × 8 mm:**
$A_g = 1070$ mm², $A_n = 1070 - 140 = 930$ mm².
$$T_{dg} = \frac{1070 \times 250}{1.10} = 243{,}182 \text{ N} = 243.2 \text{ kN} \ge 220 \text{ kN} \; \surd$$
$$T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times 930 \times 410}{1.25} = 274{,}512 \text{ N} = 274.5 \text{ kN} \ge 220 \text{ kN} \; \surd$$

**Conclusion:** Use **90 × 50 × 8 mm** unequal angle, with **6 mm fillet welds of 170 mm length on each side**.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Riveted + Compression Member Design (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A **double-angle back-to-back strut** of length **3.6 m** in a poultry-shed roof truss carries a **factored compressive load of 320 kN**. The strut is pin-connected at both ends. Each angle is 100 × 100 × 10 mm, and the two angles are separated by a **10 mm gusset plate**. The angles are connected to a gusset plate at each end through **riveted connections** (one 18 mm rivet hole at each joint section).

**(a)** Design the **riveted connection** at the gusset plate for the strut. Check shear, bearing, and net section. Use 4.6 grade rivets and Fe 410 plate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Check the **compressive strength** of the built-up double-angle section. Take $K = 1.0$, $f_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, slenderness ratio limit = 180. Use IS 800 buckling curve 'b'. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Rivet Design

**Given:** $P = 320$ kN, 18 mm rivet, $d_h = 19.5$ mm, $t = 10$ mm, Fe 410 ($f_u = 410$ MPa), 4.6 grade rivet ($\tau_{vf} = 320$ MPa).

**Step 1 — Single Shear Strength of Rivet (single shear configuration as the gusset is between the two angles):**
$$R_{dvs} = \frac{(\pi/4) \times 18^2 \times 320}{1.25} = \frac{254.47 \times 320}{1.25} = 65{,}144 \text{ N} = 65.14 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Bearing Strength:**
$$f_{pb} = 0.5 \times 410 = 205 \text{ MPa}$$
$$R_{dpb} = \frac{18 \times 10 \times 205}{1.25} = \frac{36{,}900}{1.25} = 29{,}520 \text{ N} = 29.52 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Governing Strength:** $R_{dv} = \min(65.14,\; 29.52) = 29.52$ kN (bearing governs)

**Step 4 — Number of Rivets Required:**
$$n = \frac{320}{29.52} = 10.84 \approx 11 \text{ rivets}$$

Provide **12 rivets** in 2 rows × 6 columns.

#### Part (b) — Compressive Strength Check

**Given:** 100 × 100 × 10 mm double angle, $A_g = 2 \times 1920 = 3840$ mm², $r_{min} = 19.4$ mm (about the weaker axis considering the section as built-up; for back-to-back with gusset, use $r_{min} = 19.4$ mm approximately, but including lacing effects).

**Step 1 — Effective Length:** $L_e = 1.0 \times 3600 = 3600$ mm.

**Step 2 — Slenderness Ratio:**
$$\lambda = \frac{3600}{19.4} = 185.6 \;\; \text{> 180 (LIMIT VIOLATED!)}$$

**Step 3 — The single built-up section exceeds slenderness.** Reduce effective length OR increase $r_{min}$.

**Step 4 — Recalculation with Laced Built-up Section:**
If we take $r_{min}$ of the built-up section as approximately 30.6 mm (considering parallel axis theorem with 10 mm separation):
$$\lambda = \frac{3600}{30.6} = 117.6 \;\; \le 180 \;\; \surd$$

**Step 5 — Euler Buckling Stress:**
$$f_{cc} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{117.6^2} = \frac{1.974 \times 10^6}{13{,}830} = 142.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Non-dimensional Slenderness:**
$$\lambda_{nd} = \sqrt{\frac{250}{142.7}} = \sqrt{1.752} = 1.324$$

**Step 7 — Perry Parameter (Curve 'b', $\alpha = 0.49$):**
$$\phi = 0.5 \cdot [1 + 0.49 \cdot (1.324 - 0.2) + 1.324^2] = 0.5 \cdot [1 + 0.551 + 1.753] = 1.652$$

**Step 8 — Design Compressive Stress:**
$$f_{cd} = \frac{250/1.10}{1.652 + \sqrt{1.652^2 - 1.324^2}} = \frac{227.27}{1.652 + \sqrt{2.729 - 1.753}} = \frac{227.27}{1.652 + 0.987} = 86.16 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 9 — Design Compressive Strength:**
$$P_d = 3840 \times 86.16 = 330{,}854 \text{ N} = 330.85 \text{ kN} \ge 320 \text{ kN} \;\; \surd \text{ SAFE}$$

**Conclusion:** Double-angle 100 × 100 × 10 mm back-to-back strut with **12 rivets of 18 mm diameter** is safe.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS**
> 1. **Forgetting to use 0.7s (not 0.707s) for effective throat** — IS 800:2007 uses 0.7 conservatively; using 0.707 will be technically correct but KTU examiners may deduct 0.5 mark for not citing Cl. 10.5.3.1.
> 2. **Confusing γ_mw with γ_mb** — Weld uses $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ and rivet uses $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$. Wrong factor = −1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting block shear check** for tension members with coped ends or staggered holes.
> 4. **Not checking slenderness ratio limit (180/250)** — A column exceeding the limit is unsafe by code, irrespective of Euler load.
> 5. **Forgetting to use $A_e$ (effective area)** instead of $A_g$ for perforated compression members.
> 6. **Sign error in compression buckling formula** — $\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_{nd}^2}$, NOT $\phi - \sqrt{\ldots}$.
> 7. **No mention of buckling curve selection** — Always state the curve (a, b, c, or d) and the corresponding $\alpha$ value.
> 8. **For weld return length** — At end of fillet weld, return weld = 2s (minimum) is mandatory per IS 800 Cl. 10.5.4.1.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — Module 2**
>
> **WELDED CONNECTIONS:**
> - Fillet weld: $t_e = 0.7s$, design stress $f_{wd} = f_u / (\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.50)$.
> - Butt weld (complete penetration): Full parent metal strength.
> - Minimum weld size: depends on thicker plate (3 mm for $t \le 10$ mm, 5 mm for 10–20 mm, 6 mm for 20–32 mm).
> - Maximum weld size on edge: $s_{max} = t_{edge} - 1.5$ mm.
> - Return welds of length $2s$ are mandatory at terminations.
>
> **RIVETED CONNECTIONS:**
> - 4 failure modes: shear, bearing, net tension, edge shear.
> - Clearance for hole: $d_h = d_{rivet} + 1.5$ mm (for $d \le 24$ mm) and 2.0 mm (for $d > 24$ mm).
> - Power-driven shop rivets preferred over hand-driven field rivets.
> - Pitch (minimum) = $2.5d$; (maximum) = $32t$ or 300 mm (whichever is less) in tension members.
> - Edge distance = $1.5 d_h$ (sheared edge) or $1.7 d_h$ (rolled/sawn edge).
>
> **TENSION MEMBERS:**
> - Two checks: gross section yielding AND net section rupture.
> - Net section with staggered holes: $A_n = (B - n d_h) t + \sum(s^2/4g) t$.
> - Shear lag factor for single/double angle: 0.8 (4 bolts), 0.9 (8 bolts), 1.0 (16+ bolts) — applied to net area.
> - Block shear must be checked at end connections.
>
> **COMPRESSION MEMBERS:**
> - Slenderness limits: 180 (braced), 250 (unbraced).
> - Buckling curves (a, b, c, d) with $\alpha$ values 0.21, 0.49, 0.49, 0.76.
> - Perry-Robertson formula governs for all columns except very stocky ones ($\lambda_{nd} < 0.2$).
> - Effective length factor $K$ depends on end conditions: 1.0 (pin-pin), 0.8 (fixed-pin), 0.65 (fixed-fixed), 2.0 (fixed-free).
> - Built-up sections: lacing/battening must be designed per IS 800 Cl. 7.3.
>
> **FARM STRUCTURE APPLICATIONS:**
> - Roof trusses (fink, fink-with-pratt, howe, pratt) — all four member types present.
> - Gusset plates at joints: minimum 8 mm thick for light farm sheds.
> - Purlins and sheeting rails: cold-formed steel sections (separate IS 801 design).
> - Column base plates: minimum 16 mm for sheds, with 4 anchor bolts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beam - Design of Laterally Supported and Unsupported Beams; Columns - Design of Axially Loaded Compression Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Beam - Design of Laterally Supported and Unsupported Beams; Columns - Design of Axially Loaded Compression Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Beams — Fundamental Classification

A **beam** is a structural member subjected predominantly to **transverse loads**, producing **bending moment (M)** and **shear force (V)** along its length. In the context of farm structures (trusses, purlins, rafters, gantry girders, gable columns, and platform beams), beams form the skeleton that transfers loads from the roof/cladding to the columns and ultimately to the foundation.

Based on **lateral restraint to the compression flange**, beams are classified into two fundamental categories under **IS 800:2007 (Limit State Method of Steel Design)**:

| Classification | Compression Flange Status | Governing Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Laterally Supported Beam** | Restrained against lateral movement and twisting | **Yielding of the section** (full plastic/compact moment capacity is mobilised) |
| **Laterally Unsupported Beam** | Free to displace laterally and rotate about the longitudinal axis | **Lateral-Torsional Buckling (LTB)** (strength reduced below Mp) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2**
> The design of beams and columns in farm structures is primarily governed by **IS 800:2007** (for steel sections like ISMB, ISMC, ISA, tubes used in trusses/godowns) and **IS 456:2000** (for RCC members in silos, platforms, and pucca flooring). The treatment below uses **IS 800:2007** as the principal code since farm superstructures (trusses, purlins, rafters) are predominantly steel.

---

### Conceptual Analogy — The Bookshelf vs. The Diving Board

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump — How Lateral Support Works**
> - Imagine a **bookshelf loaded with heavy books**. The vertical "side walls" of the shelf act as **lateral supports**, preventing the loaded horizontal "shelf-board" from tipping sideways. Even if the board bends downward (sags), it cannot twist or buckle sideways. → This is a **laterally supported beam**.
> - Now imagine a **diving board** sticking out from a swimming pool with no support at the free end. When a person stands on the tip, the board bends *and* wants to roll sideways (twist). This sideways rolling failure is **Lateral-Torsional Buckling**. → This is a **laterally unsupported beam**.
> - In farm structures: a **roof purlin continuously fastened to a corrugated GI sheet** is *laterally supported*. The same purlin between two adjacent points of attachment, before the sheet is screwed, is *unsupported*.

---

## 1.2 Core Definitions (KTU Board-Examiner Vocabulary)

**Definition 1 — Effective Lateral Restraint**
> A beam is considered *laterally supported* when its compression flange is prevented from lateral movement relative to the tension flange (or to an adjacent restrained member) at intervals such that the **lateral buckling strength is not less than the yield strength** of the section.

**Definition 2 — Lateral-Torsional Buckling (LTB)**
> LTB is a mode of instability in which the compression flange of an unrestrained beam buckles sideways while the cross-section twists about the longitudinal axis, occurring before the full plastic moment is reached.

**Definition 3 — Axially Loaded Compression Member (Column)**
> A structural member whose primary internal force is a **compressive axial load** (P), acting along its centroidal axis with negligible eccentricity. Examples in farm buildings: **gable end posts, stanchions, truss top-chord members, side columns of godowns**.

**Definition 4 — Slenderness Ratio (λ)**
> The ratio of **effective length (KL)** of the column to the **appropriate radius of gyration (r)**: $\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r}$.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Code Limits (Bolded)

- **Partial safety factor for steel material, γ_m0 = 1.10** (IS 800:2007, Cl. 5.4.1)
- **Partial safety factor for factory/field bolts, γ_mb = 1.25**
- **Modulus of Elasticity, E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² (MPa)**
- **Poisson's ratio, ν = 0.30**
- **Maximum permissible slenderness ratio for compression members = 180** (IS 800:2007, Cl. 7.1.2)
- **Maximum slenderness ratio for a tension member = 400**
- **Limiting width-to-thickness ratios** for classification as Class 1 (plastic), Class 2 (compact), Class 3 (semi-compact) per Table 2 of IS 800:2007.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limit State of Collapse** → Strength check (M, V, P)
> **Limit State of Serviceability** → Deflection check (δ ≤ L/325 for purlins; L/360 for general beams)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design of Laterally Supported Beams (IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.2.1)

When the compression flange is **effectively restrained laterally** throughout the span, the beam can develop its **full flexural strength** based only on the cross-sectional classification.

### Governing Conditions
1. The beam is **compact** (Class 1 or Class 2) OR semi-compact (Class 3) — but for plastic design only Class 1 is allowed.
2. The member is **not subject to torsion** or **fatigue** in the relevant limit state.
3. Lateral restraint is provided at appropriate spacing.

### Step-by-Step Design Logic

**Step 1 — Factored Bending Moment (M_u)**
$$M_u = 1.5 \, M_{DL} + 1.5 \, M_{LL}$$

(Note: For Limit State of Strength, partial safety factor for dead load γ_D = 1.5 and for imposed load γ_L = 1.5, as per IS 800:2007 Table 4.)

**Step 2 — Select Trial Section**
Assume trial Z_p (plastic section modulus) from elastic design:
$$Z_{e,\text{req}} = \dfrac{M_u}{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}$$

Choose section with Z_p ≥ Z_e,req from steel tables (SP 6-1).

**Step 3 — Check Classification of Section**
Check **b/T_f** (flange outstand), **d/t_w** (web), and **h_1/t_w** (web under flexure) ratios from IS 800:2007 Table 2. Confirm the section is at least **semi-compact**.

**Step 4 — Check Bending Strength**

For **Class 1 / Class 2 sections**:
$$M_{d} = \dfrac{\beta_b \, Z_p \, f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

For **Class 3 (semi-compact) sections**:
$$M_{d} = \dfrac{Z_e \, f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

For **Class 4 (slender) sections**:
$$M_{d} = \dfrac{Z_e \, f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \quad (\text{with } Z_e \text{ as effective modulus})$$

Here, **β_b = 1.0 for simply supported beams** and **β_b = Z_e/Z_p for cantilever/overhang** (per Cl. 8.2.1.2).

**Step 5 — Serviceability Check**
$$\delta_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I} \leq \dfrac{L}{325} \text{ (for purlins)}$$

**Step 6 — Shear Strength Check**
$$V_d = \dfrac{f_{yw} \, h_w \, t_w}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

If high shear (V_u > 0.6 V_d), use the **high-shear provision** (Cl. 9.2.5) — moment capacity is reduced.

---

## 2.2 Design of Laterally Unsupported Beams (IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.2.2)

When the compression flange is **NOT laterally restrained**, the design bending strength is governed by **lateral-torsional buckling**. The reduction in strength is captured by the **bending stress reduction factor χ_LT**.

### Step-by-Step Design Logic

**Step 1 — Factored Load & Bending Moment**
Same as supported case.

**Step 2 — Trial Section**
Assume trial Z_p or Z_e and compute required χ_LT:
$$\chi_{LT,\text{req}} = \dfrac{M_u \, \gamma_{m0}}{Z_p \, f_y}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Non-dimensional Slenderness (λ_LT)**
$$\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\beta_b \, Z_p \, f_y}{M_{cr}}}$$

where **M_cr** is the **elastic lateral-torsional buckling moment**:
$$M_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 \, E \, I_y \, h^2}{2 L_{LT}^2}} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{\pi^2 \, E \, I_w}{L_{LT}^2 \, G \, I_t}}$$

Or more conveniently, using Perry-Robertson-type IS formula:
For **rolled sections** with **φ_LT = 0.5[1 + α_LT(λ_LT − 0.2) + λ_LT²]**:
$$\chi_{LT} = \dfrac{1}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}} \leq 1.0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imperfection factor α_LT** (Table 14 of IS 800:2007):
> - Rolled I-section: α_LT = **0.21**
> - Welded I-section: α_LT = **0.49**
> - Other sections: α_LT = **0.76**

**Step 4 — Design Bending Strength**
$$M_d = \chi_{LT} \, \dfrac{\beta_b \, Z_p \, f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \quad \text{(Class 1/2)}$$
$$M_d = \chi_{LT} \, \dfrac{Z_e \, f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \quad \text{(Class 3)}$$

The condition to be checked:
$$M_u \leq M_d$$

**Step 5 — Serviceability**
Deflection check is mandatory (often governs for farm purlins).

---

## 2.3 Design of Axially Loaded Compression Members (Columns)

### Step-by-Step Design Logic

**Step 1 — Factored Axial Load**
$$P_u = 1.5 \, D.L. + 1.5 \, L.L.$$

**Step 2 — Trial Section**
Assume trial Area (A_g) using:
$$A_{g,\text{req}} = \dfrac{P_u \, \gamma_{m0}}{f_y \, (f_{cd}/f_y)}$$

For initial guess, f_cd ≈ 0.4 to 0.6 f_y.

**Step 3 — Classify Buckling Curve (IS 800:2007, Table 11)**
Buckling curves **a, b, c, d** are chosen based on section type and buckling axis:
- Buckling about **z-z axis (major)**: a (rolled I, H), b (welded I), c (RHS), d (CHS)
- Buckling about **y-y axis (minor)**: b (rolled I, H), c (welded I, RHS), d (CHS)

**Step 4 — Compute Slenderness Ratio**
$$\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Compute f_cd (Design Compressive Stress)**

Imperfection factor α (Table 12):
- Curve a: α = 0.21
- Curve b: α = 0.34
- Curve c: α = 0.49
- Curve d: α = 0.76

$$\phi = 0.5\left[1 + \alpha(\lambda - 0.2) + \lambda^2\right]$$
$$f_{cd} = \dfrac{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Design Strength**
$$P_d = A_e \, f_{cd}$$

For **sections not susceptible to local buckling** (Class 1, 2, 3), A_e = A_g.

**Step 7 — Capacity Check**
$$P_u \leq P_d$$

**Step 8 — Slenderness Limit Check**
$$\lambda_{\max} = \dfrac{KL}{r_{\min}} \leq 180$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Topic | Formula | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plastic moment capacity | $M_d = \beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.2.1.2 |
| 2 | Elastic moment capacity (semi-compact) | $M_d = Z_e f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.2.1.2 |
| 3 | Buckling parameter φ_LT | $\phi_{LT} = 0.5[1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT}-0.2)+\lambda_{LT}^2]$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.2.2.1 |
| 4 | χ_LT | $\chi_{LT} = 1/[\phi_{LT}+\sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2-\lambda_{LT}^2}]$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.2.2.1 |
| 5 | Unsupported beam Md | $M_d = \chi_{LT} \beta_b Z_p f_y/\gamma_{m0}$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.2.2.1 |
| 6 | Buckling parameter φ | $\phi = 0.5[1+\alpha(\lambda-0.2)+\lambda^2]$ | IS 800 Cl. 7.1.4.1 |
| 7 | Design compressive stress | $f_{cd} = (f_y/\gamma_{m0})/[\phi+\sqrt{\phi^2-\lambda^2}]$ | IS 800 Cl. 7.1.4.1 |
| 8 | Column design strength | $P_d = A_e f_{cd}$ | IS 800 Cl. 7.1.4.1 |
| 9 | Slenderness ratio | $\lambda = KL/r$ | IS 800 Cl. 7.1.2 |
| 10 | Shear strength | $V_d = f_{yw} h_w t_w/\gamma_{m0}$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.4 |
| 11 | Partial safety factor | $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ | IS 800 Cl. 5.4.1 |
| 12 | Maximum λ (compression) | $\lambda_{\max} = 180$ | IS 800 Cl. 7.1.2 |
| 13 | E for steel | $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm² | IS 800 Cl. 2.2.4.1 |
| 14 | β_b for SS beam | $\beta_b = 1.0$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.2.1.2 |
| 15 | β_b for cantilever | $\beta_b = Z_e/Z_p$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.2.1.2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility in Farm Engineering**
> - **Laterally supported beam** design is used for **roof purlins of a poultry shed** (continuously connected to GI sheet), **floor beams of a godown**, and **rails of a cattle fence**.
> - **Laterally unsupported beam** design governs for **open-web joists**, **gantry girders of a tractor shed**, and **unsupported bottom chords during erection**.
> - **Axially loaded column** design dominates in **vertical silo supports**, **pole-type barn columns**, **truss stanchions**, and **storage rack posts**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Perry-Robertson Column Curve (IS 800 Form)

The Euler load for a perfect column is:
$$P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_e^2}$$

For an **imperfect column** with initial bow of δ₀, the maximum fibre stress at mid-height equals yield stress f_y at the critical load P_cr:
$$\dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} + \dfrac{P_{cr} \, \delta_0 \, c}{I \, (1 - P_{cr}/P_E)} = f_y$$

Letting η = δ_0 · c / r² (imperfection parameter) and σ_cr = P_cr/A:
$$\sigma_{cr} \left[1 + \dfrac{\eta \, \sigma_{cr}}{(f_y - \sigma_{cr})}\right] = \text{... } (\text{Perry formulation})$$

Substituting slenderness ratio λ = √(f_y · A / P_E) and after algebraic manipulation:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{f_y}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda^2}}, \quad \phi = 0.5[1 + \alpha(\lambda - 0.2) + \lambda^2]$$

This is the **exact closed-form used in IS 800:2007** and is **NOT** a numerical iteration — it directly gives f_cd once λ and α are known.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — ISMB 400 Beam Check (Laterally Unsupported)

**Problem:**
A simply supported roof purlin of an agricultural storage shed spans **5.0 m** between roof trusses. It carries a **factored UDL of 12 kN/m** (including self-weight, GI sheet, and live load). The section is **ISMB 400 @ 0.604 kN/m**, f_y = **250 MPa**, with **no intermediate lateral support** along the span. Verify its adequacy.

**Step 1 — Section Properties of ISMB 400**
From SP 6-1 (Table for Indian Standard Medium Weight Beams):
- Depth, h = 400 mm
- Flange width, b_f = 140 mm
- Web thickness, t_w = 8.9 mm
- Flange thickness, T_f = 16.0 mm
- Area, A_g = 7690 mm²
- I_xx = 20458 × 10⁴ mm⁴
- I_y = 622 × 10⁴ mm⁴
- Z_p,x = 1138.6 × 10³ mm³
- Z_e,x = 1022.9 × 10³ mm³
- r_y = 28.9 mm

**Step 2 — Factored Moment**
$$M_u = \dfrac{w_u L^2}{8} = \dfrac{12 \times 5^2}{8} = 37.5 \, \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Classification Check (IS 800:2007 Table 2)**

Flange outstand b = (b_f − t_w)/2 = (140 − 8.9)/2 = 65.55 mm

For Class 1 (flange): b/T_f ≤ 9.4ε where ε = √(250/f_y) = 1.0
→ 65.55/16.0 = **4.10 ≤ 9.4** ✓ (Class 1)

Web check: d/t_w for Class 1 = 84ε = 84
→ d/t_w of ISMB 400 ≈ 329/8.9 = 36.97 ≤ 84 ✓ (Class 1)

→ Section is **Class 1 — Plastic**.

**Step 4 — Plastic Moment Capacity**
$$\beta_b = 1.0 \text{ (simply supported)}$$
$$M_{d,\text{plastic}} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 1138.6 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10 \times 10^6} = 258.77 \, \text{kN·m}$$

This would be the capacity **if laterally supported** → hugely sufficient.

**Step 5 — Elastic LTB Moment, M_cr**

For I-section, simplified IS 800 formula (Cl. 8.2.2.1.1):
$$M_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi}{L_{LT}} \sqrt{E \, I_y \, G \, I_t} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{\pi^2 \, E \, I_w}{L_{LT}^2 \, G \, I_t}}$$

For rolled ISMB 400 (without warping constants detailed): use the **simplified equation from SP 6**:
$$M_{cr} = \beta_b \cdot C_1 \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_y}{(L_{LT}/r_y)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{k}{k_w}\right)^2 + \dfrac{I_w}{I_y}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{h}$$

For uniform moment (C_1 = 1.0), k/k_w ≈ 1.0, using approximate:
$$M_{cr} \approx \dfrac{\pi \sqrt{E I_y G I_t}}{L_{LT}}$$

I_t for I-section ≈ Σ(b·t³)/3 = (2 × 140 × 16³ + (400 − 32) × 8.9³)/3
= (2 × 140 × 4096 + 368 × 705)/3
= (1,146,880 + 259,440)/3
= 1,406,320/3 ≈ 4.69 × 10⁵ mm⁴

G = E/[2(1+ν)] = 2 × 10⁵/[2 × 1.3] = 7.69 × 10⁴ N/mm²

$$M_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi \sqrt{2 \times 10^5 \times 622 \times 10^4 \times 7.69 \times 10^4 \times 4.69 \times 10^5}}{5000}$$

Numerator inside sqrt:
$2 \times 10^5 \times 6.22 \times 10^6 = 1.244 \times 10^{12}$
$7.69 \times 10^4 \times 4.69 \times 10^5 = 3.607 \times 10^{10}$
Product = $4.488 \times 10^{22}$
√(4.488 × 10²²) = 2.118 × 10¹¹

$$M_{cr} = \pi \times 2.118 \times 10^{11} / 5000 = 1.331 \times 10^8 \, \text{N·mm} = 133.1 \, \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 6 — Non-dimensional Slenderness**
$$\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\beta_b Z_p f_y}{M_{cr}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.0 \times 1138.6 \times 10^3 \times 250}{133.1 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{2.138} = 1.462$$

**Step 7 — Buckling Parameter φ_LT**
For rolled I-section, α_LT = 0.21:
$$\phi_{LT} = 0.5[1 + 0.21(1.462 - 0.2) + (1.462)^2]$$
$$= 0.5[1 + 0.265 + 2.137] = 0.5[3.402] = 1.701$$

**Step 8 — χ_LT**
$$\chi_{LT} = \dfrac{1}{1.701 + \sqrt{(1.701)^2 - (1.462)^2}}$$
$$= \dfrac{1}{1.701 + \sqrt{2.894 - 2.137}}$$
$$= \dfrac{1}{1.701 + \sqrt{0.757}}$$
$$= \dfrac{1}{1.701 + 0.870} = \dfrac{1}{2.571} = 0.389$$

**Step 9 — Design Moment Capacity (Unsupported)**
$$M_d = \chi_{LT} \cdot \dfrac{\beta_b Z_p f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = 0.389 \times 258.77 = 100.66 \, \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 10 — Check**
$$M_u = 37.5 \, \text{kN·m} \leq M_d = 100.66 \, \text{kN·m} \quad ✓ \text{ SAFE}$$

**Step 11 — Deflection Check**
Working load (unfactored) ≈ 12/1.5 = 8 kN/m
$$\delta = \dfrac{5 \times 8 \times 5000^4}{384 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 20458 \times 10^4} \, \text{mm}$$
$$= \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{16}}{1.571 \times 10^{13}} = 1.591 \, \text{mm}$$
Allowable = L/325 = 5000/325 = 15.38 mm ✓

**Conclusion:** ISMB 400 is **safe** as a laterally unsupported purlin over 5 m span. The LTB strength (100.66 kN·m) >> applied moment (37.5 kN·m).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When is Beam "Laterally Supported"?

| Parameter | Condition | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Compression flange restraint interval L_LT | $\lambda_{LT} \leq \sqrt{\beta_b Z_p f_y / M_{cr,\min}}$ where $M_{cr,\min} = 0.5 M_d$ | Laterally Supported (no LTB check) |
| Section classification | Class 1 or Class 2 | Full plastic moment allowed |
| Section classification | Class 3 | Use $Z_e$ in place of $Z_p$ |
| Section classification | Class 4 (slender) | Use effective $Z_e$ (Cl. 9.3.2.2) |

---

## 3.4 Column Worked Example — ISMB 300 Column

**Problem:** Design an axially loaded column of length **3.5 m**, pinned at both ends (K = 1.0), carrying a factored load of **600 kN**. Use f_y = 250 MPa. Section is buckled about the **minor (y-y) axis**.

**Step 1 — Trial**
Try **ISMB 300 @ 0.469 kN/m**:
- A_g = 5980 mm², r_y = 31.6 mm, r_z = 128.6 mm

**Step 2 — Slenderness Ratios**
$$\lambda_z = \dfrac{1.0 \times 3500}{128.6} = 27.2 \text{ (governs? let's check)}$$
$$\lambda_y = \dfrac{1.0 \times 3500}{31.6} = 110.8 \quad \text{(governs — minor axis)}$$

**Step 3 — Buckling Curve for Minor Axis (Table 11)**
Rolled I-section, minor axis = **Curve b**, α = 0.34.

**Step 4 — Compute φ**
$$\phi = 0.5[1 + 0.34(110.8 - 0.2) + (110.8)^2]$$

⚠ **CRITICAL POINT** — λ in IS 800 is **NON-DIMENSIONAL**, computed as:
$$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y \, A_g}{P_E}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cc,Euler}}}$$

In the **non-dimensional formulation** (Cl. 7.1.4.1):
$$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y \, (KL/r)^2}{\pi^2 \, E}}$$
$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{250 \times (110.8)^2}{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}}$$
$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{250 \times 12277}{1.974 \times 10^6}}$$
$$= \sqrt{1.555} = 1.247$$

**Step 5 — Recompute φ**
$$\phi = 0.5[1 + 0.34(1.247 - 0.2) + (1.247)^2]$$
$$= 0.5[1 + 0.356 + 1.555] = 0.5[2.911] = 1.455$$

**Step 6 — f_cd**
$$f_{cd} = \dfrac{250/1.10}{1.455 + \sqrt{(1.455)^2 - (1.247)^2}}$$
$$= \dfrac{227.27}{1.455 + \sqrt{2.117 - 1.555}}$$
$$= \dfrac{227.27}{1.455 + 0.750} = \dfrac{227.27}{2.205} = 103.1 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 7 — Design Capacity**
$$P_d = A_g \cdot f_{cd} = 5980 \times 103.1 / 1000 = 616.5 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 8 — Check**
$$P_u = 600 \, \text{kN} \leq P_d = 616.5 \, \text{kN} \quad ✓ \text{ SAFE (marginal)}$$

**Step 9 — Slenderness Limit**
$$\lambda_y = 110.8 \leq 180 \quad ✓$$

**Step 10 — Section Classification Check**
For ISMB 300, the section is Class 1 → A_e = A_g ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result: ISMB 300 is just adequate.** For safer design, retry with **ISMB 350 @ 0.514 kN/m** or add intermediate bracing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Beam & Column Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 0: Input - Loads, Span, fy, E] --> B[STEP 1: Compute Factored Loads]
    B --> C{Member Type?}
    C -->|Beam| D[STEP 2: Assume Trial Section Zp Ze I]
    C -->|Column| E[STEP 2: Assume Trial Section Ag rmin]
    D --> F[STEP 3: Classify Section - IS 800 Table 2]
    E --> G[STEP 3: Classify Buckling Curve - Table 11]
    F --> H{Laterally Supported?}
    H -->|Yes| I[STEP 4a: Compute Md = b Zp fy gm0]
    H -->|No| J[STEP 4b: Compute Mcr then chiLT]
    J --> K[STEP 4c: Md = chiLT b Zp fy gm0]
    I --> L[STEP 5: Deflection Check L/325]
    K --> L
    G --> M[STEP 4d: Compute lambda non-dim]
    M --> N[STEP 5: Compute phi then fcd]
    N --> O[STEP 6: Pd = Ag fcd]
    O --> P[STEP 7: Slenderness Lambda less than 180]
    L --> Q{All Checks PASS?}
    P --> Q
    Q -->|Yes| R[FINAL DESIGN SAFE]
    Q -->|No| S[REVISE SECTION - Go to STEP 2]
    R --> T[Provide Drawing: Section, Spacing, Connection Details]
    S --> T
```

## 4.2 Lateral-Torsional Buckling — Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Transverse Load] --> B[Compression Flange Tries to Buckle Sideways]
    B --> C[Cross-Section Rotates about Beam Axis]
    C --> D[TWIST of Cross-Section]
    D --> E[Reduced Moment Capacity below Plastic Mp]
    E --> F{Is Support Provided?}
    F -->|Yes - Purlins tied by Sheeting| G[Compression Flange Restrained]
    G --> H[Full Mp Achieved]
    F -->|No - Open Web Joist during erection| I[LTB Governs]
    I --> J[Use chiLT reduced Md]
```

## 4.3 Buckling Curve Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Section Type] --> B{Rolled or Welded?}
    B -->|Rolled I-Section| C{Axis of Buckling?}
    B -->|Welded I-Section| D{Axis of Buckling?}
    B -->|Hollow Section RHS| E{All axes}
    B -->|CHS Pipe| F{All axes}
    C -->|Major z-z axis| G[Curve a - alpha 0.21]
    C -->|Minor y-y axis| H[Curve b - alpha 0.34]
    D -->|Major z-z axis| I[Curve b - alpha 0.34]
    D -->|Minor y-y axis| J[Curve c - alpha 0.49]
    E --> K[Curve c - alpha 0.49]
    F --> L[Curve d - alpha 0.76]
```

## 4.4 Beam Type Identification Map (Farm Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Farm Structural Element] --> B{Role in Structure?}
    B -->|Horizontal load transfer| C[Beam]
    B -->|Vertical load transfer| D[Column]
    C --> E{Lateral Restraint?}
    E -->|Continuous from sheeting or brace| F[Laterally Supported Beam]
    E -->|No intermediate support| G[Laterally Unsupported Beam]
    F --> F1[Examples: Roof Purlins, Floor Joists of Godown]
    G --> G1[Examples: Crane Girder, Open Web Joist]
    D --> D1[Examples: Truss Stanchion, Pole Barn Post, Silo Leg]
```

## 4.5 Cross-Section Stress Distribution under Bending

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam Cross Section - I Shape] --> B[Bending Moment Mu Applied]
    B --> C[Top Flange in COMPRESSION]
    B --> D[Bottom Flange in TENSION]
    C --> E{Tries to buckle sideways?}
    E -->|Yes if unsupported| F[LTB Failure]
    E -->|No if restrained| G[Full Mp Reached]
    F --> H[Reduced capacity chiLT Mp]
    G --> I[Mp achieved - Plastic Hinge forms]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define laterally supported and laterally unsupported beams. Under what conditions does lateral-torsional buckling govern the design?**

**Model Answer:**
A beam whose compression flange is **prevented from moving laterally** at sufficiently close intervals (such that the section can develop its full plastic moment M_p without buckling sideways) is termed a **laterally supported beam**.

A beam whose compression flange is **free to displace sideways and twist** about its longitudinal axis (because lateral restraint is absent or inadequate) is a **laterally unsupported beam**.

Lateral-Torsional Buckling (LTB) governs when the **non-dimensional slenderness parameter λ_LT exceeds the limit corresponding to full plastic moment**, i.e., when the unrestrained length L_LT of the compression flange between lateral restraints is large enough that elastic buckling occurs before yielding. The strength reduction is captured by the **bending stress reduction factor χ_LT ≤ 1.0** per IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.2.2.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is slenderness ratio of a compression member? State the maximum permissible slenderness ratio for a compression member as per IS 800:2007.**

**Model Answer:**
The **slenderness ratio (λ)** of a compression member is defined as the ratio of its **effective length (KL)** to the **appropriate radius of gyration (r)**:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r}$$

where K = effective length factor (depends on end conditions), L = actual length, and r = radius of gyration (r_min is usually used).

As per **IS 800:2007, Cl. 7.1.2**, the maximum permissible slenderness ratio for a compression member is **180** (for members carrying dead and imposed loads). For members carrying only dead load, the limit is **250**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

### QUESTION A — Laterally Unsupported Beam + Column Design

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** A simply supported roof purlin of an open-front cattle shed spans **4.5 m** between roof trusses and is **NOT laterally supported** along its length. It carries a **factored UDL of 15 kN/m** (including self-weight of GI sheeting and live load). Design a suitable section using **ISMB series with f_y = 250 MPa**. Verify by LTB check. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** An axially loaded column of length **4.0 m**, **effectively held in position at both ends but not restrained against rotation** (K = 1.0), carries a factored axial load of **750 kN**. Design a suitable column section using rolled I-sections with **f_y = 250 MPa**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — ISMB Selection for LTB

**Step 1 — Trial**
Assume Z_p,req assuming χ_LT ≈ 0.5:
$$Z_{p,\text{req}} = \dfrac{M_u \, \gamma_{m0}}{\chi_{LT} \, f_y} = \dfrac{37.97 \times 10^6 \times 1.10}{0.5 \times 250} = 334 \, \text{cm}^3 \text{ (approx.)}$$

**Step 2 — Try ISMB 300 @ 46.1 kg/m**
- Z_p = 651.7 × 10³ mm³, Z_e = 573.6 × 10³ mm³
- I_y = 4538 × 10⁴ mm⁴, r_y = 27.6 mm
- I_t ≈ (2 × 140 × 12.4³ + (300 − 24.8) × 7.5³)/3 ≈ 3.05 × 10⁵ mm⁴

**Step 3 — M_cr**
G = 7.69 × 10⁴ N/mm²
$$M_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi \sqrt{2 \times 10^5 \times 4538 \times 10^4 \times 7.69 \times 10^4 \times 3.05 \times 10^5}}{4500}$$
Inside √: 2×10⁵ × 4.538×10⁷ × 7.69×10⁴ × 3.05×10⁵
= 2 × 4.538 × 7.69 × 3.05 × 10²¹
= 213 × 10²¹
√ = 4.61 × 10¹¹
M_cr = π × 4.61 × 10¹¹ / 4500 = 3.22 × 10⁸ N·mm = **322 kN·m**

**Step 4 — λ_LT**
$$\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.0 \times 651.7 \times 10^3 \times 250}{322 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{0.506} = 0.711$$

**Step 5 — φ_LT (α_LT = 0.21)**
$$\phi_{LT} = 0.5[1 + 0.21(0.711-0.2) + 0.711^2] = 0.5[1+0.107+0.505] = 0.806$$

**Step 6 — χ_LT**
$$\chi_{LT} = \dfrac{1}{0.806 + \sqrt{0.650 - 0.505}} = \dfrac{1}{0.806 + 0.381} = \dfrac{1}{1.187} = 0.842$$

**Step 7 — M_d**
$$M_d = 0.842 \times \dfrac{651.7 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10 \times 10^6} = 124.7 \, \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 8 — Check**
$M_u = 37.97$ kN·m $\leq M_d = 124.7$ kN·m ✓ **SAFE**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (a):** Students often use Z_e instead of Z_p for plastic design (losing ~2 marks). Always check **section classification first** before deciding whether to use Z_p or Z_e.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Column Design

**Step 1 — Trial**
Assume f_cd ≈ 0.5 f_y/γ_m0 = 113.6 MPa
A_g,req = 750,000/113.6 = **6602 mm²**

**Step 2 — Try ISMB 400 @ 60.4 kg/m**
- A_g = 7690 mm², r_min = r_y = 28.9 mm
- Classification: buckled about y-y → Curve b → α = 0.34

**Step 3 — λ (non-dimensional)**
$$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y (KL/r_y)^2}{\pi^2 E}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{250 \times (4000/28.9)^2}{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}}$$
$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{250 \times 19153}{1.974 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{2.426} = 1.558$$

**Step 4 — φ**
$$\phi = 0.5[1 + 0.34(1.558-0.2) + 1.558^2] = 0.5[1+0.462+2.427] = 1.945$$

**Step 5 — f_cd**
$$f_{cd} = \dfrac{250/1.10}{1.945 + \sqrt{3.785 - 2.427}} = \dfrac{227.27}{1.945 + 1.166} = \dfrac{227.27}{3.111} = 73.06 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6 — P_d**
$$P_d = 7690 \times 73.06 / 1000 = 561.8 \, \text{kN} \quad < 750 \, \text{kN} \quad ✗ \text{ NOT ADEQUATE}$$

**Step 7 — Retry with ISMB 500 @ 86.9 kg/m**
- A_g = 11074 mm², r_y = 35.2 mm

$\lambda = \sqrt{250 \times (4000/35.2)^2 / (π² × 2×10⁵)} = \sqrt{250 × 12919/1.974×10⁶} = \sqrt{1.636} = 1.279$

$\phi = 0.5[1 + 0.34(1.079) + 1.636] = 0.5[3.003] = 1.501$

$f_{cd} = 227.27 / [1.501 + \sqrt{2.253 - 1.636}] = 227.27 / [1.501 + 0.786] = 227.27 / 2.287 = 99.37$ MPa

$P_d = 11074 × 99.37/1000 = 1100.4$ kN ✓ **SAFE**

**Step 8 — Final Check**
$\lambda_{\max} = 4000/35.2 = 113.6 \leq 180$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (b):**
> 1. Students often forget to convert λ to **non-dimensional form** using $\sqrt{f_y(KL/r)^2/(\pi^2 E)}$ and instead use the geometric ratio directly in φ formula — leading to wildly wrong answers. **Lose 3-4 marks here.**
> 2. Forgetting to take **r_min** (typically r_y for I-sections) loses 2 marks.
> 3. Using the wrong **buckling curve** for the **y-y axis** (must be Curve b for rolled I-section, not Curve a) loses 2 marks.

---

### QUESTION B — Alternative Internal Choice (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 (Alternate Set)]** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** Design a **laterally supported beam** for a floor of a 5 m wide farm godown, carrying a **factored UDL of 25 kN/m** (including dead load, grain bag weight, and live load). Effective span = 5 m. Use **ISMB series, f_y = 250 MPa, γ_m0 = 1.10**. Check for **shear** also. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A **circular hollow section (CHS) of 114.3 mm OD × 5.4 mm thick** is used as a vertical column for a poultry shed equipment post. Length = 3.0 m, **both ends fixed** (K = 0.65), f_y = **250 MPa**, factored load = **125 kN**. Check its adequacy. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Laterally Supported ISMB Design

**Step 1 — M_u**
$$M_u = \dfrac{25 \times 5^2}{8} = 78.125 \, \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 2 — Z_e,req**
$$Z_{e,\text{req}} = \dfrac{78.125 \times 10^6 \times 1.10}{250} = 343.75 \times 10^3 \, \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Select ISMB 250 @ 37.3 kg/m**
- Z_e = 410.5 × 10³ mm³, Z_p = 469.6 × 10³ mm³
- I_xx = 5131 × 10⁴ mm⁴, h_w = 234.4 mm, t_w = 6.9 mm, f_yw = 250 MPa

**Step 4 — Classification**
Class 1 (rolled section, dimensions well within limits)

**Step 5 — M_d**
$$M_d = \dfrac{1.0 \times 469.6 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10 \times 10^6} = 106.7 \, \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 6 — M_u Check**
$78.125 \leq 106.7$ ✓ **SAFE**

**Step 7 — Shear Check**
$V_u = 25 \times 5/2 = 62.5$ kN
$V_d = 250 \times 234.4 \times 6.9 / (1.10 \times 1000) = 367.5$ kN
$V_u = 62.5 \leq 0.6 V_d = 220.5$ kN ✓ → **No high-shear reduction needed**

**Step 8 — Deflection**
Working UDL = 25/1.5 = 16.67 kN/m
$\delta = 5 \times 16.67 \times 5000^4/(384 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 5131 \times 10^4) = 13.21$ mm
Allow = 5000/325 = 15.38 mm ✓

**Conclusion:** **ISMB 250** is adequate as a laterally supported beam.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — CHS Column Check

**Step 1 — Section Properties (CHS 114.3 × 5.4)**
- OD = 114.3 mm, t = 5.4 mm, ID = 103.5 mm
- A_g = π/4 × (114.3² − 103.5²) = π/4 × (13064 − 10712) = π/4 × 2352 = **1847 mm²**
- I = π/64 × (114.3⁴ − 103.5⁴) = π/64 × (1.706 × 10⁸ − 1.147 × 10⁸) = π/64 × 5.59 × 10⁷ = **2.74 × 10⁶ mm⁴**
- r = √(I/A) = √(2.74 × 10⁶/1847) = √1484 = **38.5 mm**

**Step 2 — Effective Length**
K = 0.65, L = 3000 mm → KL = 1950 mm

**Step 3 — Slenderness**
$\lambda_{\text{geom}} = 1950/38.5 = 50.65$
$\lambda = \sqrt{250 \times 50.65² / (\pi² \times 2 \times 10⁵)} = \sqrt{250 \times 2565 / 1.974 \times 10⁶} = \sqrt{0.325} = 0.570$

**Step 4 — Buckling Curve for CHS → Curve d (α = 0.76)**
$$\phi = 0.5[1 + 0.76(0.570 - 0.2) + 0.570^2] = 0.5[1 + 0.281 + 0.325] = 0.803$$

**Step 5 — f_cd**
$$f_{cd} = \dfrac{250/1.10}{0.803 + \sqrt{0.645 - 0.325}} = \dfrac{227.27}{0.803 + 0.566} = \dfrac{227.27}{1.369} = 166.0 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6 — P_d**
$$P_d = 1847 \times 166.0 / 1000 = 306.6 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 7 — Check**
$P_u = 125 \leq P_d = 306.6$ kN ✓ **SAFE**

$\lambda_{\max} = 50.65 \leq 180$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (CHP Column):**
> 1. Confusing **CHS** with **RHS** — CHS uses Curve **d (α = 0.76)**, while RHS uses Curve **c (α = 0.49)**. Using the wrong curve gives 30%+ error.
> 2. Forgetting the **K-factor** for end conditions (Fixed-Fixed = 0.65, Fixed-Pinned = 0.80, Pinned-Pinned = 1.0) loses 2 marks.
> 3. Computing **area of hollow section** as A = π·d·t (wrong) instead of A = π/4·(D² − d²) loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — MODULE 2**

### A. Beam Design — Key Takeaways

1. ✅ **Laterally Supported** → Use **M_d = β_b Z_p f_y / γ_m0** (Class 1/2) or **Z_e f_y / γ_m0** (Class 3).
2. ✅ **Laterally Unsupported** → Multiply the above by **χ_LT ≤ 1.0** computed via Perry-Robertson curve.
3. ✅ **β_b = 1.0** for simply supported; **β_b = Z_e / Z_p** for cantilever.
4. ✅ **α_LT = 0.21 (rolled I)**, 0.49 (welded I), 0.76 (others).
5. ✅ **High-shear rule**: If V_u > 0.6 V_d, the bending capacity reduces (Cl. 9.2.5).
6. ✅ **Deflection limit** for purlins = **L/325**; for general beams = **L/360** (use Table 6 of IS 800:2007).

### B. Column Design — Key Takeaways

7. ✅ **Slenderness λ (non-dimensional) = √[f_y (KL/r)² / (π² E)]** — **NOT** the geometric ratio.
8. ✅ **Buckling Curve selection**: Rolled I (major = a, minor = b), Welded I (major = b, minor = c), RHS = c, CHS = d.
9. ✅ **α values**: a=0.21, b=0.34, c=0.49, d=0.76.
10. ✅ **Maximum λ = 180** (compression), 400 (tension).
11. ✅ **P_d = A_e · f_cd** where A_e = A_g for non-slender sections.
12. ✅ Always check both **major and minor axes** — take the **governing (lower) capacity**.

### C. Common Code Values (Memorize)

13. ✅ **γ_m0 = 1.10**; γ_mb = 1.25; γ_mw = 1.25.
14. ✅ **E = 2.0 × 10⁵ MPa**, **G = 0.769 × 10⁵ MPa**, **ν = 0.30**.
15. ✅ **ε = √(250/f_y)** — used in slenderness limits for classification.

### D. KTU 2024 Exam Strategy

16. 🎯 **Always state code clause** with each formula → earns 1 extra mark.
17. 🎯 **Show classification check** explicitly before selecting Z_p or Z_e.
18. 🎯 **Trial-and-error is acceptable** — show at least two trials in columns.
19. 🎯 **Serviceability check** (deflection/slenderness) is mandatory even if strength OK.
20. 🎯 For **14-mark problems**, expect ~7 marks for calculations + 5 marks for checks + 2 marks for diagram/justification.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — BEAMS & COLUMNS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lacing and Battening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lacing and Battening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 800:2007 Aligned)

**Lacing** and **Battening** are two classical methods of connecting the components of a **built-up compression member** (column) in a farm/industrial steel structure, when a single rolled section is inadequate to carry the required axial compressive load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Built-up Column:** A compression member fabricated by tying together two or more standard rolled sections (channels, angles, I-sections) longitudinally using lateral connecting systems, so that the assembly acts as a single integral load-carrying unit.

- **Lacing Bars:** Slender diagonal members (flat bars, angles, or channels) riveted/bolted/welded to the components of the built-up column, arranged in an inclined zig-zag pattern to form a triangulation.
- **Batten Plates (Battens):** Solid plates or rolled sections placed transversely (perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the column) at regular intervals along the length, connecting the two main components.

> [!NOTE]
> The principal objective of lacing and battening is to ensure **composite action** of the disconnected main components, hold them in their relative positions, and resist the **transverse shear forces** that arise due to buckling deformations, accidental eccentricities, and lateral loads.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pencil Bundle" Intuition

Imagine holding **two wooden sticks side-by-side** with a gap between them and pressing them at the ends (axial compression). They will immediately bow out sideways in opposite directions, like the letter **"S"**. They are individually weak.

Now imagine tying them together with:
- **X-shaped strings diagonally** (this is **LACING** — it forms triangles, the strongest shape in nature, like a trussed bridge).
- **Flat plates nailed at intervals** (this is **BATTENING** — like the slats in a ladder rung connecting the two vertical rails).

Both methods make the bundle behave like one giant cross-section. In farm structures (trusses of poultry houses, supporting columns of storage sheds, stanchions in dairy barns), single rolled sections often cannot supply the required stiffness, so the engineer **builds up** the column and stitches it together using lacing or battening.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Design Metrics

The following standard values are mandated by **IS 800:2007 (Cl. 7.6)** and are critical for KTU numerical problems:

- **Transverse shear force (V):** $V = 2.5\%$ of the axial compressive force in the column, OR the maximum shear force computed from analysis, **whichever is greater**.
- **Angle of lacing with the longitudinal axis ($\theta$):** between **30° and 60°** (commonly **45°**).
- **Spacing of lacing/battens along the length:** governed by slenderness ratio of the component (single rolled section) between consecutive connections.
- **Minimum slenderness ratio of lacing bars:** $\lambda_{lacing} \leq 145$.
- **Effective slenderness ratio of battened column:** taken as **1.1 times** the maximum actual slenderness ratio of the column, OR the slenderness ratio based on minimum radius of gyration of the built-up section, **whichever is greater**.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Board Examination 2024 Scheme, every numerical problem on lacing/battening must explicitly refer to the **transverse shear of 2.5% of axial load** — this is the single most-tested value in the entire module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why are Built-up Columns Needed?

A single standard rolled steel section (say an I-section ISMB 600) has a **limiting slenderness ratio of 180** for a compression member (IS 800:2007, Cl. 7.1.2). If the unsupported length of the column is large (typical in tall farm godowns or open-sided poultry sheds), the available radius of gyration of a single section may be insufficient, causing the column to buckle prematurely. By separating the section into two and joining them through a batten/lacing system, we increase the **radius of gyration about the weaker axis**, thereby reducing the slenderness ratio and increasing buckling load.

## 2.2 Behavioural Difference: Lacing vs. Battening

| Feature | Lacing | Battening |
|---|---|---|
| **Connecting member** | Inclined bars (angles/flats) | Plates (rectangular) |
| **Structural action** | Triangulated — resists shear by axial action of bars | Frame action — resists shear by flexure of plates |
| **Spacing along column** | Closer, defined by angle $\theta$ | Wider, defined by slenderness of component |
| **Loads resisted** | Transverse shear mainly by **axial force** in diagonal | Transverse shear mainly by **bending** in plate |
| **Fabrication cost** | Higher (more pieces, more connections) | Lower (simple plates with bolts) |
| **Preferred use** | Heavy loads, tall columns | Light to moderate loads, short columns |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **one-stop revision chart** for every KTU Board question on this topic. Memorise it before entering the exam hall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No `|` (pipe) characters appear inside any formula cell** — vertical bars for absolute values or determinants are written using the LaTeX macro `\vert` to prevent Markdown table-breaking.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transverse shear on built-up column | $V = \dfrac{2.5}{100} \times P$ where $P$ = axial compressive load | kN | IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.6.1 |
| 2 | Design force in each lacing bar (single lacing) | $F_{lacing} = \dfrac{V}{2 \sin\theta}$ | kN | Statics of triangulation |
| 3 | Design force in each lacing bar (double lacing) | $F_{lacing} = \dfrac{V}{4 \sin\theta}$ | kN | Two systems share shear |
| 4 | Axial force in main component due to shear (battens) | $F_{b,comp} = \dfrac{V \cdot s}{2 \cdot d}$ | kN | $s$ = batten spacing, $d$ = c/c distance between components |
| 5 | Moment in batten plate | $M = \dfrac{V \cdot s}{2}$ | kN·m | Plate treated as cantilever/double-cantilever |
| 6 | Shear in batten plate | $F_{q} = \dfrac{V \cdot s}{d}$ | kN | Horizontal shear across plate |
| 7 | Slenderness ratio of lacing bar | $\lambda_{lacing} = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$ | dimensionless | $L_{eff}$ between connections |
| 8 | Effective length of lacing bar | $L_{eff} = $ length between inner end connections of bars (for welded) | mm | IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.6.3 |
| 9 | Effective slenderness ratio of battened column | $\lambda_{eff} = 1.1 \times \lambda_{max}$ (OR) $\sqrt{\lambda_{m}^{2} + \lambda_{c}^{2}}$, whichever is greater | dimensionless | IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.7.1.2 |
| 10 | Min. width of batten plate | $b_{min} = $ distance between c/c of outstands of two components, OR $\dfrac{2}{3} \times$ c/c distance, whichever is more | mm | IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.7.1.1 |
| 11 | Min. thickness of batten plate | $t_{min} = \dfrac{1}{50} \times$ spacing of battens, OR $6$ mm, whichever is more | mm | Empirical, IS 800:2007 |
| 12 | Spacing of battens (max) | Governed by $\lambda_{comp} \leq 50$ OR $\lambda_{comp} \leq 0.7 \lambda_{column}$ | mm | IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.7.1.1 |
| 13 | Slenderness ratio limit (lacing) | $\lambda_{lacing} \leq 145$ | dimensionless | IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.6.3 |
| 14 | Limiting slenderness for column | $\lambda_{lim} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{f_{y}}} \approx 98$ for $F_{y} = 250$ MPa | dimensionless | IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.1.2 |

## 2.4 Real-world Engineering Utility

In **farm structures**, built-up laced/battened columns are commonly used for:
- **Stanchions supporting roof trusses of dairy barns** (spans 18–24 m).
- **Corner posts of poultry broiler houses** (tall, slender, light loading).
- **Mast columns of grain storage silos** (high axial loads from stored grain).
- **Purlins and rafters** of large-span **greenhouse frames**.

The laced/battened design allows the farmer-engineer to **economically** obtain a high radius of gyration without resorting to heavy, expensive built-up welded box sections.
