# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force in a Single Lacing Bar (Triangulation)

Consider a built-up column of length $L$ subjected to axial compression $P$. Due to accidental eccentricity and initial imperfections, a **transverse shear force $V$** acts perpendicular to the column axis. The lacing system resists this shear.

Let the lacing bars be inclined at angle $\theta$ to the longitudinal axis of the column. For a single lacing system, shear $V$ is resisted by **two lacing bars in the same panel** (one in tension, one in compression by symmetry, but for design both are taken as compression with effective length = full length of bar).

### Step-by-step resolution:

**Step 1 — Sum of vertical components of lacing forces must equal $V$:**

$$V = F_1 \sin\theta + F_2 \sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — For a single lacing system, by symmetry of design, both bars develop the same design force $F$:**

$$V = 2 F \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the design compressive force in each lacing bar:**

$$F = \dfrac{V}{2 \sin\theta}$$

**Step 4 — For a double lacing system, four bars share the shear (two in each direction):**

$$F = \dfrac{V}{4 \sin\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the derivation examiners expect to see in a 7-mark sub-part. The "two bars in tension/compression" physical reasoning is the **'Why'** behind the formula.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Forces in a Batten Plate and Main Component

For a battened column, the system behaves as a **Vierendeel truss** — the batten plates are the vertical members and the two main components are the chords.

Let:
- $V$ = transverse shear on column (kN)
- $d$ = centre-to-centre distance between the two main components (mm)
- $s$ = centre-to-centre spacing of batten plates (mm)
- $L$ = total length of column (mm)

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1 — At any horizontal cut, moment equilibrium of the cut portion of the column gives axial force in the main component due to bending:**

$$F_{b,comp} = \dfrac{V}{2} \times \dfrac{L_{un}}{d}$$

where $L_{un}$ is the lever arm (≈ half the spacing $s$ for symmetric batten arrangement).

For a **uniform shear** along the column, taking the **maximum** at a batten plate (using the cantilever half-spacing approach):

$$F_{b,comp} = \dfrac{V \cdot s}{2 \cdot d}$$

**Step 2 — The batten plate acts as a vertical cantilever fixed at one component and loaded at the other. The bending moment at the fixed edge of the plate:**

$$M = \dfrac{V}{2} \times \dfrac{s}{2} = \dfrac{V \cdot s}{4}$$

Wait — for a **double-cantilever** plate (fixed at both edges to the two components, shear $V$ applied at the centre along the column axis transfer), the moment is:

$$M = \dfrac{V \cdot s}{2}$$

This is the **maximum bending moment in the batten plate** (occurring at the line of connection with the main component).

**Step 3 — Horizontal shear force on the batten plate (for bolt/weld design):**

The horizontal shear in the connection between batten and main component equals:

$$F_q = \dfrac{V \cdot s}{d}$$

**Step 4 — Effective slenderness of battened column (per IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.7.1.2):**

$$\lambda_{eff,column} = 1.1 \times \lambda_{max,actual}$$

This $1.1$ factor accounts for the lower efficiency of the battened column (lacing is more efficient than battening).

## 3.3 Complete Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** A built-up column consists of two channels ISMC 250 placed back-to-back with clear gap of 100 mm. Effective length of column = 4.5 m. The column carries an axial compressive load of 1100 kN. The two channels are connected by **battens** at suitable intervals. **Design the batten system** and check the column for safety. Take $f_y = 250$ MPa.

### Given:
- Section: $2 \times$ ISMC 250 (back-to-back)
- $A_{gross}$ of one ISMC 250 = **$3870 \text{ mm}^2$**, $I_{xx} = 4380 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^4$, $I_{yy} = 218 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{4}$
- $r_{xx} = 106$ mm, $r_{yy} = 23.7$ mm
- $C_{yy} = 23.7$ mm (c.g. of channel from back of web)
- Effective length $L_{eff} = 4500$ mm
- $P = 1100$ kN
- $f_y = 250$ MPa

### Step 1 — Transverse shear to be resisted by batten system:

$$V = \dfrac{2.5}{100} \times P = 0.025 \times 1100 = 27.5 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 2 — Centre-to-centre distance between channels:

$$d = 100 \text{ (clear gap)} + 2 \times C_{yy} = 100 + 2(23.7) = 147.4 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Slenderness ratio of column about y-axis (weak axis) for built-up section:

For back-to-back channels, $I_{yy,b uilt} = 2 \times [I_{yy} + A \times (C_{yy} + 50)^{2}]$

$$I_{yy,built} = 2 \times [218 \times 10^{4} + 3870 \times (23.7 + 50)^{2}]$$

$$= 2 \times [218 \times 10^{4} + 3870 \times 5431.7]$$

$$= 2 \times [2180000 + 21020679] = 2 \times 23200679 = 4.64 \times 10^{7} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$A_{built} = 2 \times 3870 = 7740 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$r_{yy,built} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.64 \times 10^{7}}{7740}} = 77.4 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda_{yy} = \dfrac{4500}{77.4} = 58.1$$

### Step 4 — Slenderness ratio about x-axis:

$$I_{xx,built} = 2 \times 4380 \times 10^{4} = 8760 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$r_{xx,built} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8760 \times 10^{4}}{7740}} = 106.4 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda_{xx} = \dfrac{4500}{106.4} = 42.3$$

Maximum actual $\lambda = 58.1$. Effective slenderness for battened column:

$$\lambda_{eff} = 1.1 \times 58.1 = 63.9$$

### Step 5 — Spacing of battens:

For the individual component channel to act efficiently, the slenderness of the channel **between battens** should not exceed **0.7 × $\lambda$ of column** (i.e., $0.7 \times 58.1 = 40.7$). Also, $\lambda_{component} \leq 50$.

Take $\lambda_{comp} = 40$ → spacing $s = \lambda_{comp} \times r_{yy, channel} = 40 \times 23.7 = 948$ mm ≈ **950 mm**.

Provide battens at $s = 950$ mm c/c. Number of panels = $4500 / 950 \approx 4.7$ → use **5 panels** with $s = 900$ mm.

### Step 6 — Design of batten plate:

**Width:** $b_{min} = \dfrac{2}{3} \times d = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 147.4 = 98.3$ mm. Also, $b_{min} \geq d - 2 \times$ flange projection of channel ≈ $147.4 - 2(80) = -12.6$ mm (so flange-width criterion not governing). Provide $b = 250$ mm (flange width of channel + 50 mm allowance) → use **250 mm × 8 mm plate**.

**Check thickness:** $t_{min} = \max\left(\dfrac{900}{50}, 6\right) = \max(18, 6) = 18$ mm. This is uneconomical; IS 800:2007 permits using $t = 8$ to $10$ mm in practice for short columns. Provide **$t = 10$ mm**.

**Forces:**
- Axial force in main component: $F_{b,comp} = \dfrac{V \cdot s}{2 \cdot d} = \dfrac{27.5 \times 0.9}{2 \times 0.1474} = 83.9$ kN
- Moment in batten: $M = \dfrac{V \cdot s}{2} = \dfrac{27.5 \times 0.9}{2} = 12.375$ kN·m
- Shear in connection: $F_q = \dfrac{V \cdot s}{d} = \dfrac{27.5 \times 0.9}{0.1474} = 167.9$ kN

### Step 7 — Connection (Bolt) Design:

Bearing-type bolts of grade 4.6, 16 mm dia., in clearance hole 18 mm, double shear. Shear capacity $\approx 30$ kN/bolt. Number of bolts on each side of batten to transfer $F_q$:

$$n = \dfrac{167.9}{30} \approx 5.6 \rightarrow 6 \text{ bolts per side}$$

**Provide 6 nos. 16 mm dia. bolts on each side of every batten plate.**

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Flow for Lacing & Battening

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision-based design process** for selecting and proportioning the lateral connecting system of a built-up farm-structure column.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Built-up Column Required] --> B[Identify Main Components and Axial Load P]
    B --> C[Compute Transverse Shear V = 2.5 percent of P]
    C --> D{Span and Load Category}
    D -- Light to Moderate --> E[Battening System Selected]
    D -- Heavy and Tall --> F[Lacing System Selected]
    E --> G[Compute Centre to Centre Distance d]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Set Lacing Angle theta between 30 and 60 deg]
    H --> I{Single or Double Lacing}
    I -- Single --> J[Force in Bar F = V divided by 2 sin theta]
    I -- Double --> K[Force in Bar F = V divided by 4 sin theta]
    J --> L[Check Slenderness of Bar lambda less than 145]
    K --> L
    E --> M[Compute Batten Spacing s using lambda comp criterion]
    M --> N[Compute Width and Thickness of Batten Plate]
    N --> O[Compute Force Axial and Moment in Plate]
    O --> P[Design Bolts or Welds for Connection]
    L --> P
    P --> Q[Check Effective Slenderness of Built-up Column]
    Q --> R[Compute Buckling Load and Check Against Applied P]
    R --> S[End: Final Drawing and Detailing]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
**Define a built-up column. Why is lacing or battening necessary in such columns?**

**Model Answer:**
A built-up column is a compression member fabricated by connecting two or more standard rolled steel sections longitudinally using lateral connecting elements (lacing bars or batten plates), so that the assembly behaves as a single load-carrying unit.

Lacing or battening becomes necessary when:
1. A single rolled section is **insufficient to carry the required axial load** due to its limited cross-sectional area.
2. The **slenderness ratio** of a single section about the weaker axis exceeds the code-permissible limit (180 for a compression member), leading to premature buckling.
3. The connecting system ensures **composite action**, holds the components in their relative positions, and resists the **transverse shear** arising from accidental eccentricities, initial imperfections, and lateral loads.
4. It **increases the radius of gyration** of the section about the weak axis, thereby reducing slenderness and increasing the buckling load.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**Differentiate between lacing and battening of a built-up column.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lacing | Battening |
|---|---|---|
| Connecting element | Inclined diagonal bars | Transverse plates |
| Structural action | Triangulated truss — shear resisted by axial force in bars | Frame action — shear resisted by flexure of plates |
| Spacing | Closer, governed by angle θ | Wider, governed by component slenderness |
| Cost | Higher (more pieces) | Lower (simple plates) |
| Efficiency | More efficient in load transfer | Less efficient; column effective slenderness = 1.1 × actual |
| Slenderness of connector | $\lambda_{lacing} \leq 145$ | Thickness of plate $\geq s/50$ or 6 mm |
| Typical use | Heavy, tall columns | Light to moderate, short columns |

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **BATTERING**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** A built-up column consists of two channels ISMC 300 placed back-to-back with a clear distance of 120 mm between them. The effective length of the column is **5.0 m**, and it carries an axial compressive load of **1500 kN**. Design a suitable **batten system** for the column. Take $f_y = 250$ MPa. Use grade 4.6 bolts of 20 mm diameter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the cross-section of the built-up column with battens and explain the force transfer mechanism. State the IS 800:2007 clause references used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Given:**
- Section: $2 \times$ ISMC 300
- $A = 4625 \text{ mm}^2$ per channel
- $I_{xx} = 6362 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^4$, $I_{yy} = 308 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^4$ per channel
- $r_{xx} = 117$ mm, $r_{yy} = 25.8$ mm
- $C_{yy} = 25.8$ mm (c.g. of channel from back of web)
- $P = 1500$ kN, $L_{eff} = 5000$ mm, $f_y = 250$ MPa

**Step 1 — Transverse shear:**

$$V = 0.025 \times 1500 = 37.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — c/c distance between channels:**

$$d = 120 + 2(25.8) = 171.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Slenderness ratios of built-up section:**

$$r_{yy,built} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{yy,built}}{A_{built}}}$$

$$I_{yy,built} = 2 \times \left[308 \times 10^4 + 4625 \times (25.8 + 60)^2\right]$$

$$= 2 \times \left[3080000 + 4625 \times 7361.6\right]$$

$$= 2 \times [3080000 + 34047400] = 2 \times 37127400 = 7.43 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$r_{yy,built} = \sqrt{\dfrac{7.43 \times 10^7}{9250}} = 89.6 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda_{yy} = \dfrac{5000}{89.6} = 55.8$$

$$\lambda_{xx} = \dfrac{5000}{117} = 42.7$$

**Step 4 — Effective slenderness for battened column:**

$$\lambda_{eff} = 1.1 \times 55.8 = 61.4$$

**Step 5 — Spacing of battens:**

Allow $\lambda_{comp}$ of single channel between battens $\leq 0.7 \times \lambda_{yy} = 0.7 \times 55.8 = 39.1$ and $\leq 50$.

Take $\lambda_{comp} = 40$. Spacing:

$$s = 40 \times 25.8 = 1032 \text{ mm} \approx 1000 \text{ mm}$$

Number of panels = $5000 / 1000 = 5$ panels → provide 6 battens at $s = 1000$ mm c/c.

**Step 6 — Dimensions of batten plate:**

- Width: $b_{min} = \max\left(\dfrac{2}{3} \times 171.6, \text{ flange width} + 50\right) = \max(114.4, 300 + 50)$ → governing is **300 mm**. Provide $b = 300$ mm.
- Thickness: $t_{min} = \max\left(\dfrac{1000}{50}, 6\right) = \max(20, 6) = 20$ mm.

(To economise, IS 800 allows thinner plates for short columns; in practice 10–12 mm is used with closer spacing.)

**Provide $300 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$ batten plates at 1000 mm c/c.**

**Step 7 — Forces in batten and connection:**

- Force in channel: $F_{b,comp} = \dfrac{37.5 \times 1.0}{2 \times 0.1716} = 109.3$ kN
- Moment in batten: $M = \dfrac{37.5 \times 1.0}{2} = 18.75$ kN·m
- Shear on batten connection: $F_q = \dfrac{37.5 \times 1.0}{0.1716} = 218.5$ kN

**Step 8 — Bolt design:**

20 mm dia. 4.6 grade bolt, single shear capacity $V_{dsb} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \times \dfrac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} \times 10^{-3} = 72.5$ kN.

Number of bolts per side of batten = $218.5 / 72.5 = 3.0$ → provide **4 nos. 20 mm dia. bolts** on each side of every batten.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

The **cross-section** consists of two ISMC 300 channels placed back-to-back with their webs 120 mm apart. The channels are connected by 300 mm × 10 mm rectangular batten plates riveted/bolted to the web faces of the channels. Battens are placed on **both faces** (front and back of the webs) to provide symmetry.

**Force Transfer Mechanism:**
1. The axial compressive load $P$ enters the column through the gusset at the top, distributing into the two channels based on their axial stiffness.
2. Any lateral load or accidental eccentricity generates a transverse shear $V$ (taken as 2.5% of $P$).
3. This shear $V$ is transferred across the column by **axial forces in the channels** and **bending + shear in the batten plates**.
4. The batten acts as a vertical cantilever transferring the shear between the two channel components, while the channels act as the chords of a **Vierendeel truss** resisting the bending moment.
5. The bolts (or welds) on each side of every batten plate transfer the design shear $F_q$ from the plate to the channel web.

**IS 800:2007 Clauses Referenced:**
- **Cl. 7.6.1:** Transverse shear = 2.5% of axial load.
- **Cl. 7.7.1.1:** Batten dimensions and spacing.
- **Cl. 7.7.1.2:** Effective slenderness of battened column = $1.1 \times \lambda_{max}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **LACING**

**(a)** Two channels ISMC 250 are placed back-to-back with a clear gap of 100 mm and connected by a **single lacing system** at 45° to the longitudinal axis. The column has an effective length of 4.0 m and carries an axial load of 900 kN. Design the **lacing bars** and check for compliance with IS 800:2007. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A built-up laced column is subjected to accidental transverse load during a cyclonic wind event in a coastal farm shed. Justify why a **double lacing** is preferred over a **single lacing** in cyclonic zones. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Given:** $P = 900$ kN, $L_{eff} = 4000$ mm, $\theta = 45°$, $2 \times$ ISMC 250 (back-to-back).

**Step 1 — Transverse shear:**

$$V = 0.025 \times 900 = 22.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Design force in each lacing bar (single lacing):**

$$F = \dfrac{V}{2 \sin 45°} = \dfrac{22.5}{2 \times 0.7071} = 15.91 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Length of lacing bar:**

Spacing of lacing nodes along column: governed by angle $\theta$ and c/c distance between channels. For $\theta = 45°$, the horizontal pitch $p$ = vertical pitch of lacing nodes = $d = 147.4$ mm.

Length of lacing bar:

$$l = \sqrt{d^2 + p^2} = \sqrt{147.4^2 + 147.4^2} = 147.4\sqrt{2} = 208.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Effective length of lacing bar (per IS 800:2007):**

For welded connection: $L_{eff} = 0.7 \times l = 0.7 \times 208.5 = 146$ mm.
For bolted connection: $L_{eff} = l = 208.5$ mm.

**Step 5 — Slenderness check:**

For single lacing, the bar is treated as a compression member with effective length = length of bar (between connections). The radius of gyration of a flat bar of size $b \times t$ about its weaker axis is $r = t/\sqrt{12}$.

Try flat bar **50 mm × 8 mm**:

$$r_{min} = \dfrac{8}{\sqrt{12}} = 2.31 \text{ mm}$$

For bolted: $\lambda = \dfrac{208.5}{2.31} = 90.3 \leq 145$ ✓

**Provide 50 mm × 8 mm flat bar lacing at 45° on both faces of the column.**

**Step 6 — Connection:**

Single 16 mm dia. 4.6 bolt per end of each lacing bar; bearing capacity governs at low values of $F = 15.91$ kN. With 1 bolt of 16 mm dia., bearing capacity on 8 mm plate is:

$$V_{dpb} = 2.5 \times k_b \times d \times t \times f_u = 2.5 \times 0.5 \times 16 \times 8 \times 400 = 64{,}000 \text{ N} = 64 \text{ kN} \gg 15.91 \text{ kN} \checkmark$$

**Use 1 bolt of 16 mm dia. at each end of the lacing bar.**

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

In **cyclonic coastal zones** (Kerala's Idukki, Alappuzha coastal belt, Karnataka coast), farm structures are subjected to **high lateral wind loads** (basic wind speed up to 50 m/s per IS 875 Part 3). Under such cyclic reversal of wind direction:

1. **Lateral load reverses** — the lacing bar that is in tension in one wind direction becomes compression in the reverse direction. A **single lacing system** would have only ONE bar in tension in any panel, so under reversal, the previously compression bar now goes into tension. The system is **asymmetric in force reversal** and the entire shear must be carried by only two bars in each direction.

2. **Double lacing** has two independent lacing systems — one in each diagonal direction. Under wind from one side, one system resists; under wind from the opposite side, the second system resists. This provides **symmetric resistance to cyclic lateral loading**, eliminating the danger of bar buckling under fluctuating load.

3. **Double lacing provides redundancy** — if one lacing bar is damaged by corrosion (common in coastal farm sheds) or by impact, the parallel bar continues to resist the shear, providing **structural robustness**.

4. **Smaller individual bar forces** ($F = V/4\sin\theta$ instead of $V/2\sin\theta$) reduce the slenderness and the buckling risk of each individual bar.

5. **Code recommendation** — IS 800:2007 (Cl. 7.6.1) recommends **double lacing when the column length exceeds 10 m** or when the lacing bars are subjected to **load reversals** (typical in cyclonic regions). Single lacing is acceptable for short, lightly loaded, single-direction wind farm columns.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the 2.5% transverse shear** ($V = 0.025 P$): This is the most-skipped step. A numerical problem with no $V$ is automatically awarded **0 marks for the design part**, even if the rest is correct.
> 2. **Using $\lambda = 180$** (single column limit) **instead of $\lambda \leq 145$** (lacing bar limit). Examiners are strict on this distinction.
> 3. **Not multiplying by 1.1** for effective slenderness of a battened column. This is a free 2-mark mark!
> 4. **Forgetting to compute the c/c distance $d$** properly — using clear gap instead of centre-to-centre. Forces in bars and moments in plates will all be off.
> 5. **Confusing single-lacing force $V/(2\sin\theta)$ with double-lacing force $V/(4\sin\theta)$** — a single line of algebra error that destroys the entire solution.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print This Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- [ ] A **built-up column** is needed when a single rolled section is inadequate in area or radius of gyration.
- [ ] The **two lateral connecting systems** are: **Lacing** (inclined bars) and **Battening** (transverse plates).
- [ ] The **universal transverse shear** to be resisted by the connecting system: $V = 2.5\%$ of axial compressive load $P$ (IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.6.1).
- [ ] **Lacing angle** $\theta$ with the longitudinal axis: between **30° and 60°** (recommended **45°**).
- [ ] **Single lacing** force per bar: $F = V / (2 \sin\theta)$; **Double lacing** force per bar: $F = V / (4 \sin\theta)$.
- [ ] **Slenderness of lacing bar** $\lambda_{lacing} \leq 145$.
- [ ] **Batten width** $b_{min} = (2/3) d$ (c/c distance between components), and width should overlap the flange of the component fully.
- [ ] **Batten thickness** $t_{min} = \max(s/50, 6 \text{ mm})$.
- [ ] **Effective slenderness ratio of battened column** = $1.1 \times$ maximum actual slenderness ratio (this is the **most-asked** numerical).
- [ ] **Slenderness of component between battens** should be $\leq 50$ and $\leq 0.7 \times$ slenderness of the built-up column.
- [ ] Lacing is more **structurally efficient**; battening is more **economical** for light loads.
- [ ] In **cyclonic/reversal load zones**, use **double lacing** for redundancy and reversibility.
- [ ] Always check the **buckling class** (a, b, c, d) from IS 800 Table 10 of the built-up section before computing $f_{cd}$ — a common KTU question stem requires the student to **read the design stress from tables** after computing $\lambda_{eff}$.
- [ ] **Farm structure application hot-locations:** stanchions of dairy barns, columns of poultry sheds, masts of grain silos, supports for greenhouse frames.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Column Bases - Slab Bases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Column Bases - Slab Bases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Column Base** is a structural element that serves as the critical load-transfer interface between a **superstructure steel column** and its **substructure concrete foundation (pedestal)**. Its primary engineering purpose is to **spread the highly concentrated axial load** coming from the relatively small cross-sectional area of the steel column over a sufficiently large bearing area on the concrete pedestal, such that the induced bearing stress never exceeds the permissible bearing strength of the concrete.

A **Slab Base** is the **simplest, most economical, and most commonly adopted category of column base**, specifically employed for **light, axially-loaded columns** where bending moment transfer is negligible or absent. It consists essentially of a **single thick rectangular (or square) steel plate**—called the **base plate**—rigidly welded (or, in older riveted construction, cleated) to the end of the column shaft, and firmly anchored to the concrete pedestal using **anchor bolts (foundation bolts)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 2):**
> The slab base is explicitly listed as a fundamental sub-topic under Column Bases. Students are expected to master the **complete design workflow**: determination of plate plan dimensions, calculation of bending moment at the critical section, sizing of plate thickness, and provision of anchor bolts. The design must follow the **limit state method** as per **IS 800:2007**, using partial safety factors $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ for steel yield and a **bearing strength factor of 0.45 × f_ck** for concrete pedestal design.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building the Intuition

Imagine you are standing on **soft, fresh concrete** wearing a pair of sharp, narrow **stiletto heels**. The pressure under each tiny heel point is enormous—the concrete will crack and your foot will sink. To prevent this, you naturally step onto a **broad, flat wooden plank** (say, a thick base plate). The same body weight is now distributed over the entire plank area, the pressure underneath drops dramatically, and the soft concrete comfortably carries you.

**This wooden plank is exactly what a slab base does for a steel column.** The steel column I-section is the "stiletto heel" — its load is concentrated over a tiny area (say 150 mm × 150 mm). The concrete pedestal cannot tolerate such concentrated pressure because concrete is strong in compression but weak in tension/shear under concentrated punch-through loads. The thick **base plate** acts as the load-spreading "wooden plank," enlarging the contact footprint so the bearing pressure on concrete falls within its safe limit.

**Why is it called a *Slab* Base?**
Because the base plate behaves structurally like a short, thick, **two-way or one-way cantilever slab** projecting outward from the edges of the column flange/web. The pressure from below (concrete reaction) pushes upward, and the plate bends in the **overhanging portions** beyond the column profile. Its thickness must be sized to resist this bending — just like a reinforced concrete slab resists bending due to loads on it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:**
> - **Slab Base** → Used for **light axial loads** (e.g., roof truss columns, small building stanchions, farm structure columns).
> - **Gusseted Base** → Used for **heavy columns** carrying **axial load + bending moment** (e.g., bridge piers, heavy industrial columns).
> - **Bearing Plates** (for beams) are a related but distinct concept — they also use slab-type analysis.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (IS 800:2007 / IS 456:2000) |
|---|---|---|
| Yield strength of structural steel (Fe 410 grade) | $f_y$ | **250 MPa** |
| Ultimate tensile strength of structural steel | $f_u$ | **410 MPa** |
| Partial safety factor for yield (Limit State) | $\gamma_{m0}$ | **1.10** |
| Partial safety factor for ultimate stress | $\gamma_{m1}$ | **1.25** |
| Characteristic compressive strength of concrete pedestal (typical) | $f_{ck}$ | **20 MPa** (M20) or **25 MPa** (M25) |
| Permissible bearing stress on concrete pedestal (working stress) | $\sigma_{brg}$ | **0.45 × f_ck** |
| Design bearing strength of concrete (limit state) | $f_{brg}$ | **0.45 × f_ck** (same numerical value) |
| Minimum diameter of anchor bolt (mild steel) | — | **20 mm** (typical); **16 mm** (minimum practical) |
| Hole diameter in base plate for anchor bolt | — | **Bolt diameter + 3 to 6 mm clearance** |
| Edge distance from anchor bolt to plate edge | — | **≥ 1.5 × hole diameter** |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb for Farm Structures:**
> For a typical farm structure column carrying an axial load of **200 kN to 600 kN**, the base plate thickness usually works out to be in the range of **20 mm to 40 mm**. The plate plan size is often dictated by **2 to 3 times the column depth × 2 to 3 times the flange width**. This rule of thumb helps you quickly verify the order of magnitude of your final answer during the KTU exam.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Slab Base — Itemized Anatomy

A properly designed slab base consists of the following components, each with a defined engineering role:

1. **Base Plate** — A thick rectangular/square MS plate that spreads the column load.
2. **Column Shaft** — The I-section (or other section) that delivers the load to the plate.
3. **Weld Connection** — Full-penetration or fillet weld joining the column to the plate. In KTU design problems, this weld is generally treated as **strong enough to transfer the full axial load** without being checked (a common assumption for an examination context).
4. **Anchor Bolts (Foundation Bolts)** — Two or four MS bolts embedded in the concrete pedestal. They serve **two distinct functions**:
   - **During erection:** Hold the column in proper position and plumb.
   - **During service:** Resist **any small uplift / tension** due to accidental moments, wind, or seismic action. For pure axial slab bases, they are **not designed for full column tension**—they are nominal.
5. **Grout Layer** — A 25 mm to 50 mm thick layer of rich cement mortar (typically 1:2 cement-sand) poured between the underside of the base plate and the top of the concrete pedestal. It ensures uniform contact and full bearing pressure distribution.
6. **Concrete Pedestal** — The reinforced concrete block (usually part of a larger RCC footing) on which the slab base rests.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Engineering Logic

The structural design of a slab base proceeds through a clearly defined sequence of checks. Each step answers a specific engineering question:

**Step 1 — Determine the Load to be Transferred.**
The factored axial compressive load (in limit state design) is obtained from structural analysis of the parent frame:
$$P_u = \gamma_f \times P_{service} = 1.5 \times P_{DL+LL}$$
where $\gamma_f$ is the partial safety factor for loads (= 1.5 for DL+LL combination).

> **Why?** Because limit state design checks structural adequacy against the worst-case (factored) loads, not the service loads.

**Step 2 — Compute the Required Bearing Area on Concrete.**
The base plate must be large enough that the bearing pressure beneath it does not exceed the bearing strength of the concrete:
$$A_{req} = \dfrac{P_u}{0.45 \cdot f_{ck}}$$

> **Why 0.45 × f_ck?** This is the empirical bearing strength factor specified in **IS 456:2000 (Clause 34.4)** for bearing on concrete when the supporting surface is wider on all sides than the loaded area by at least the thickness of the bearing plate. It accounts for the confinement effect that significantly enhances concrete's local crushing capacity.

**Step 3 — Decide the Plan Dimensions of the Plate.**
For a column of depth $D$ and flange width $b_f$, the plate length $L$ and width $B$ are chosen as:
$$L \geq D + 2 \cdot a \quad ; \quad B \geq b_f + 2 \cdot b$$
where $a$ = projection beyond column depth, $b$ = projection beyond flange width. Both $a$ and $b$ should be **roughly equal** (a square plate is preferred) and the chosen $L \times B$ must satisfy $L \times B \geq A_{req}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the Actual Bearing Pressure.**
$$w = \dfrac{P_u}{L \cdot B} \;\; \leq \;\; 0.45 \cdot f_{ck}$$

> **Why?** This is the actual upward pressure exerted by the pedestal on the underside of the plate. It is uniformly distributed (a standard assumption, justified because the grout layer and large plate thickness make the contact effectively uniform).

**Step 5 — Identify the Critical Section for Bending.**
The plate bends as a **two-way cantilever** beyond the column edges. The **critical section** for bending moment calculation is taken at the **face of the column** for each projection arm.
- Projection along depth direction: $a = (L - D) / 2$
- Projection along width direction: $b = (B - b_f) / 2$

**Step 6 — Compute the Bending Moment per Unit Width.**
For an **I-section column**, the bending moments are evaluated for the two distinct bending directions, and the **larger** governs the thickness:

$$M_x = \dfrac{w \cdot a^2}{2} \quad \text{(bending about axis perpendicular to flange, using depth projection } a\text{)}$$

$$M_y = \dfrac{w \cdot b^2}{2} \quad \text{(bending about axis along flange, using width projection } b\text{)}$$

The governing moment $M = \max(M_x, M_y)$.

> **Note on I-Section Geometry:** The critical section for the bending moment is **not** at the column face for both directions. A more rigorous treatment uses the actual footprint geometry:
> - For the projection beyond the **flange tip** (length $b$): the cantilever bends due to pressure on area $b \times 1$.
> - For the projection beyond the **column web face** (length $a$): the cantilever bends due to pressure on area $a \times 1$.
> - For the region **between the flanges** (length $a$ and depth $h-2t_f$), the plate bends due to pressure from below but is supported on two edges by the flanges. The critical section here is at the **flange face** (i.e., at distance $a$ from column centerline on either side).
> The conventional design equation $M = w \cdot a^2 / 2$ (or $w \cdot b^2 / 2$) captures the dominant cantilever behaviour and is the **standard KTU exam formulation**.

**Step 7 — Calculate Required Plate Thickness.**
Using the elastic section modulus of a unit strip (1 mm wide) of plate:
$$\dfrac{M}{1} = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \cdot \dfrac{(1 \cdot t^2)}{6}$$
$$\Rightarrow \quad t_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \cdot M \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{f_y}}$$

> **Why 1.10 ($\gamma_{m0}$)?** This is the standard partial safety factor for yielding of steel in IS 800:2007 limit state design. It accounts for variability in material strength.

**Step 8 — Add Corrosion Allowance.**
The calculated thickness is increased by **at least 2 mm** to account for corrosion over the design life:
$$t_{provided} = t_{req} + 2 \text{ mm (minimum)}$$
**Rounded up** to the next available **standard plate thickness** (e.g., 16, 20, 22, 25, 32, 36, 40, 45, 50 mm).

**Step 9 — Provide Anchor Bolts.**
For pure axial loads, **nominal anchor bolts** of 16 mm to 20 mm diameter are provided. The minimum recommendations per **IS 800:2007 Clause 7.4**:
- A minimum of **4 bolts** for major columns, **2 bolts** for minor/tubular sections.
- Bolt diameter ≥ **20 mm** for cleat/gusseted bases; **≥ 16 mm** is acceptable for light slab bases.
- Embedment length into pedestal ≥ **12 × bolt diameter** (with hook at the bottom, if straight bolt).
- Plate hole diameter = bolt diameter + **3 mm (clearance for 16-27 mm bolts)** or **6 mm (for 30 mm+ bolts)**.

**Step 10 — Check Weld Strength (Optional in KTU).**
For a fillet weld of size $s$ on both sides of the column flange and web, the weld strength must be ≥ $P_u$. If not, the problem statement usually pre-specifies a sufficient weld size.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Slab bases are ubiquitous in:

- **Steel-framed farm buildings** (godowns, dairy parlors, poultry sheds, machinery sheds) — the **exact KTU 2024 context** for PCAGT501.
- **Roof truss columns** for industrial sheds, warehouses, and agricultural storage structures.
- **Sign posts, light poles, transmission towers** (slab bases for stub columns).
- **Mezzanine floor columns** in light commercial structures.
- **Pre-engineered buildings (PEB)** with light end-wall columns.

Their **low cost**, **fabrication simplicity**, and **rapid erection** make them ideal for light-duty agricultural and industrial applications where the column carries predominantly axial load.

> [!TIP]
> **For Farm Structures (PCAGT501 Context):**
> Agricultural structures often have low roof loads and minimal wind/seismic demand. Most columns in poultry sheds, storage godowns, and dairy buildings qualify perfectly for slab base design. Always check whether the column carries any **eccentricity** from truss reactions before selecting a slab base; if significant bending exists, escalate to a gusseted base.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Factored axial load | $P_u = 1.5 \times P_{service}$ | kN or N | DL + LL combo |
| 2 | Required plate area | $A_{req} = \dfrac{P_u}{0.45 \cdot f_{ck}}$ | mm² | Based on bearing |
| 3 | Actual bearing pressure | $w = \dfrac{P_u}{L \cdot B}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | Must be ≤ 0.45 f_ck |
| 4 | Projection beyond column depth | $a = (L - D)/2$ | mm | Cantilever arm |
| 5 | Projection beyond flange width | $b = (B - b_f)/2$ | mm | Cantilever arm |
| 6 | Bending moment (depth direction) | $M_x = \dfrac{w \cdot a^2}{2}$ | N·mm/mm | Per unit width |
| 7 | Bending moment (width direction) | $M_y = \dfrac{w \cdot b^2}{2}$ | N·mm/mm | Per unit width |
| 8 | Governing moment | $M = \max(M_x, M_y)$ | N·mm/mm | Selects thickness |
| 9 | Required plate thickness | $t_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \cdot M \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{f_y}}$ | mm | Limit state |
| 10 | Provided thickness (after corrosion) | $t_{prov} = t_{req} + 2$ mm, round up | mm | Standard size |
| 11 | Anchor bolt nominal dia | $\geq 16$ mm (slab base) | mm | IS 800 Cl. 7.4 |
| 12 | Anchor bolt embedment | $L_e \geq 12 \cdot d_b$ | mm | Hooked at bottom |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Critical Mistake to Avoid:**
> Do **NOT** confuse the **two projections $a$ and $b$** when computing the bending moments. The projection $a$ is associated with the **column depth $D$** (bending about the minor axis—plate projecting beyond column top and bottom flanges), while $b$ is associated with the **flange width $b_f$** (bending about the major axis—plate projecting beyond column side flanges). Mixing them up leads to a structurally incorrect (and unsafe) thickness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Worked Example — Slab Base Design

**Problem Statement:**
Design a slab base for a steel column **ISMB 300** carrying an axial factored load of **$P_u = 1500$ kN**. The column is supported on an RCC pedestal of grade **M25**. The steel grade is **Fe 410 ($f_y = 250$ MPa)**. Use the limit state method as per IS 800:2007.

**Given Data:**
- Column section: **ISMB 300** → Depth $D = 300$ mm, Flange width $b_f = 140$ mm, Web thickness $t_w = 7.5$ mm, Flange thickness $t_f = 12.4$ mm
- Factored load: $P_u = 1500$ kN = $1500 \times 10^3$ N
- Concrete grade: M25 → $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa
- Steel grade: Fe 410 → $f_y = 250$ MPa
- Partial safety factor: $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$

---

**Step 1 — Calculate Required Plate Area.**

Using the bearing strength of concrete = $0.45 \times f_{ck}$:

$$A_{req} = \dfrac{P_u}{0.45 \cdot f_{ck}}$$

$$A_{req} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^3}{0.45 \times 25} = \dfrac{1500000}{11.25}$$

$$\boxed{A_{req} = 133333.33 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 1333.33 \text{ cm}^2}$$

---

**Step 2 — Choose Plate Plan Dimensions.**

For ISMB 300 ($D = 300$ mm, $b_f = 140$ mm), provide a projection of approximately 100 mm on each side:

$$L = D + 2 \cdot a = 300 + 2(100) = 500 \text{ mm}$$

$$B = b_f + 2 \cdot b = 140 + 2(100) = 340 \text{ mm}$$

But to keep the plate more square (better for two-way bending) and provide slightly larger bearing area, let's adopt:

$$L = 500 \text{ mm}, \quad B = 400 \text{ mm}$$

$$A_{prov} = 500 \times 400 = 200000 \text{ mm}^2 > A_{req} \;\; \checkmark$$

---

**Step 3 — Verify Actual Bearing Pressure.**

$$w = \dfrac{P_u}{L \cdot B} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^3}{500 \times 400} = \dfrac{1500000}{200000}$$

$$\boxed{w = 7.5 \text{ MPa}}$$

Check: $w = 7.5$ MPa $< 0.45 \times 25 = 11.25$ MPa $\;\; \checkmark$ (Safe)

---

**Step 4 — Calculate Cantilever Projections.**

Projection beyond column depth:
$$a = \dfrac{L - D}{2} = \dfrac{500 - 300}{2} = 100 \text{ mm}$$

Projection beyond flange width:
$$b = \dfrac{B - b_f}{2} = \dfrac{400 - 140}{2} = 130 \text{ mm}$$

---

**Step 5 — Compute Bending Moments.**

Bending moment due to projection $a$ (along depth direction):
$$M_x = \dfrac{w \cdot a^2}{2} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 100^2}{2} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 10000}{2}$$

$$M_x = 37500 \text{ N·mm/mm} = 37.5 \text{ N·m/m}$$

Bending moment due to projection $b$ (along width direction):
$$M_y = \dfrac{w \cdot b^2}{2} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 130^2}{2} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 16900}{2}$$

$$M_y = 63375 \text{ N·mm/mm} = 63.375 \text{ N·m/m}$$

Governing moment:
$$M = \max(M_x, M_y) = M_y = 63375 \text{ N·mm/mm}$$

---

**Step 6 — Calculate Required Plate Thickness.**

Using the limit state bending stress equation:
$$t_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \cdot M \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{f_y}}$$

$$t_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 63375 \times 1.10}{250}}$$

$$t_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{418275}{250}} = \sqrt{1673.1}$$

$$\boxed{t_{req} \approx 40.91 \text{ mm}}$$

---

**Step 7 — Add Corrosion Allowance and Standardize.**

$$t_{final} = t_{req} + 2 \text{ mm} = 40.91 + 2 = 42.91 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt the next higher standard plate thickness:
$$\boxed{t_{provided} = 45 \text{ mm}}$$

---

**Step 8 — Anchor Bolt Provision.**

Provide **4 numbers of 20 mm diameter mild steel anchor bolts**, one at each corner of the plate.

- Bolt hole diameter in plate = 20 + 3 = **23 mm**
- Edge distance = 1.5 × 23 = **35 mm** (minimum)
- Embedment length into pedestal = 12 × 20 = **240 mm** (with hook at bottom, or use a bent bolt)

For axial load only, these anchor bolts are **not stressed** by the column load — they are nominal/erection bolts.

---

**Step 9 — Weld Check (Brief).**

The column ISMB 300 has two flanges + one web to be welded to the plate. Assuming a fillet weld of size 10 mm around all three elements:

Approximate weld length:
$$l_w = 2(b_f) + 2(D - 2t_f) = 2(140) + 2(300 - 24.8) = 280 + 550.4 = 830.4 \text{ mm}$$

Weld throat thickness: $t_t = 0.707 \times s = 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07$ mm

Weld strength per mm (Fe 410):
$$f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m1}} = \dfrac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} \approx 189.4 \text{ MPa}$$

Total weld capacity:
$$R_{wd} = l_w \times t_t \times f_{wd} = 830.4 \times 7.07 \times 189.4 \approx 1112 \text{ kN}$$

Since $R_{wd} = 1112$ kN $< P_u = 1500$ kN, the 10 mm weld is **not adequate**. We need to either:
- Increase weld size to 12 mm: $t_t = 8.485$ mm, $R_{wd} = 830.4 \times 8.485 \times 189.4 \approx 1334$ kN (still less)
- Or use 14 mm weld: $t_t = 9.9$ mm, $R_{wd} = 830.4 \times 9.9 \times 189.4 \approx 1557$ kN $\geq 1500$ kN $\checkmark$

Therefore, provide a **14 mm fillet weld** all around.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Flowchart Summary

| Condition Encountered | Engineering Decision / Action |
|---|---|
| Column carries only axial load (no moment) | **Slab base is suitable** |
| Axial load + small moment | Slab base possible; size for combined stress |
| Heavy axial load + large moment | **Escalate to gusseted base** |
| Multiple columns close together on one pedestal | Use a **common base plate** spanning several columns |
| Column section is hollow tubular | Use slab base with **ring/cross stiffeners** to prevent plate buckling |
| Corrosive environment (farm effluent, ammonia in dairy) | **Add 3 mm corrosion allowance** (instead of 2 mm) and consider galvanized plate |
| Insufficient concrete bearing area | **Increase pedestal size** or use higher concrete grade (M30 instead of M25) |
| Calculated plate thickness > 50 mm | Consider **gusseted base** or use **stiffeners** to reduce effective span |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Slab Base — Plan and Elevation Block Diagram

```mermaid

        direction TB
        P1["Top-Left Corner<br/>Anchor Bolt 1"]
        P2["Top-Right Corner<br/>Anchor Bolt 2"]

        P2 --- P3
        P3 --- P4
        P4 --- P1
    end

        direction TB

        C2["Column Depth D = 300 mm"]
    end

    end

        direction TB

    end

    Plate ---|"w = P_u / LB"| Column

```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Slab Base Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["STEP 1<br/>Receive Factored<br/>Axial Load P_u"] --> B["STEP 2<br/>Compute Required<br/>Plate Area A_req"]
    B --> C{"STEP 3<br/>A_req Feasible<br/>on Available Pedestal?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["STEP 4<br/>Select L x B Dimensions<br/>L = D + 2a, B = b_f + 2b"]
    C -- No --> E["ACTION<br/>Enlarge Pedestal<br/>or Raise f_ck Grade"]
    E --> D
    D --> F["STEP 5<br/>Compute Bearing<br/>Pressure w = P_u / LB"]
    F --> G["STEP 6<br/>Verify w <= 0.45 f_ck"]
    G -- OK --> H["STEP 7<br/>Compute Projections<br/>a and b"]
    G -- NOT OK --> D
    H --> I["STEP 8<br/>Compute M_x = w.a.a/2<br/>and M_y = w.b.b/2"]
    I --> J["STEP 9<br/>Select M = Max of M_x, M_y"]
    J --> K["STEP 10<br/>Calculate t_req using<br/>Limit State Equation"]
    K --> L["STEP 11<br/>Add 2 mm Corrosion<br/>and Round to Standard"]
    L --> M["STEP 12<br/>Provide Anchor Bolts<br/>Nominal 20 mm x 4 nos"]
    M --> N["STEP 13<br/>Design Weld<br/>Between Column and Plate"]
    N --> O["FINAL DESIGN<br/>Slab Base Completed"]

    classDef processStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4500,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef finalStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    class A,B,D,F,H,I,J,K,L,M,N processStyle
    class C,G decisionStyle
    class E,O finalStyle
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic of the Cantilever Bending Action

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cantilever["CANTILEVER ACTION ON BASE PLATE"]
        direction LR
        subgraph Above["ABOVE COLUMN (Top Projection)"]
            direction TB
            AB1["Bearing Pressure<br/>w pushing UP"]
            AB2["Moment M_x<br/>= w . a.a / 2"]
            AB3["Critical Section<br/>at Column Face"]
        end

        subgraph Column1["COLUMN SHAFT"]
            direction TB
            CB1["ISMB 300<br/>Cross-Section"]
            CB2["Acts as Rigid Support"]
        end

        subgraph Below["BELOW COLUMN (Bottom Projection)"]
            direction TB
            BB1["Bearing Pressure<br/>w pushing UP"]
            BB2["Moment M_x<br/>= w . a.a / 2"]
            BB3["Critical Section<br/>at Column Face"]
        end

        Above ---|"Pressure on a x B area"| Column1
        Column1 ---|"Pressure on a x B area"| Below
    end

    subgraph Stress["STRESS DISTRIBUTION ACROSS PLATE"]
        direction LR
        SS1["Region 1:<br/>Directly under column<br/>No bending"]
        SS2["Region 2:<br/>Cantilever portion<br/>Maximum bending"]
        SS3["Edge:<br/>Zero bending<br/>at free end"]
    end

    Cantilever ---|"Plate thickness sized to resist M = w.a.a/2"| Stress

    classDef cantileverStyle fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef columnStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stressStyle fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class Above,AB1,AB2,AB3,Below,BB1,BB2,BB3 cantileverStyle
    class Column1,CB1,CB2 columnStyle
    class Stress,SS1,SS2,SS3 stressStyle
```

---

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Force Visualization Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Vertical["VERTICAL CROSS-SECTION THROUGH SLAB BASE"]
        direction TB
        V1["Column Shaft<br/>ISMB 300<br/>Depth D = 300 mm"]
        V2["Weld Connection<br/>14 mm Fillet Weld"]
        V3["Base Plate<br/>45 mm Thick x 500 mm Long"]
        V4["Grout Layer<br/>30 mm Thick<br/>1:2 Cement Mortar"]
        V5["Concrete Pedestal<br/>M25 Grade<br/>Reinforced"]
        V6["Anchor Bolt<br/>20 mm dia<br/>Embedded 240 mm"]

        V1 ---|"Axial Load P_u = 1500 kN"| V2
        V2 ---|"Load Transfer via Weld"| V3
        V3 ---|"Bearing Pressure w = 7.5 MPa"| V4
        V4 ---|"Uniform Distribution"| V5
        V5 ---|"Holds Anchor Bolt"| V6
    end

    subgraph Forces["FORCE FLOW DIAGRAM"]
        direction TB
        F1["P_u = 1500 kN<br/>Applied at Column Top"]
        F2["Spread via Plate<br/>to area 500x400 mm"]
        F3["w = 7.5 MPa<br/>on Pedestal Top"]
        F4["Transferred to<br/>PCC/RCC Foundation"]

        F1 --> F2
        F2 --> F3
        F3 --> F4
    end

    Vertical ---|"Load Transfer Path"| Forces

    classDef verticalStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef forceStyle fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class Vertical,V1,V2,V3,V4,V5,V6 verticalStyle
    class Forces,F1,F2,F3,F4 forceStyle
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define a slab base. State the situations where slab bases are preferred over gusseted bases.*

**Model Answer:**
A slab base is a type of column base consisting of a single thick steel base plate welded to the bottom of the column and anchored to a concrete pedestal using nominal anchor bolts. It is used to spread the concentrated axial column load over a larger bearing area on the concrete pedestal.

Slab bases are preferred over gusseted bases in the following situations:
1. When the column carries **predominantly axial compressive load** with negligible bending moment.
2. When the **column section is light** (such as ISMB up to 450 or small tubular sections).
3. When **fabrication economy and speed** are critical (e.g., pre-engineered farm buildings, warehouse sheds).
4. When the **pedestal area is sufficient** to accommodate the required bearing plate dimensions.
5. For **farm structures, light roof trusses, and storage godowns** where loads are relatively low.

(3 marks awarded for clear definition + any 3 valid situations)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Question Bank]**
*Write a short note on the role of anchor bolts in a slab base. Specify IS 800 recommendations for bolt diameter.*

**Model Answer:**
**Role of Anchor Bolts in a Slab Base:**
- **Erection function:** Hold the column in correct position and plumb during installation before the grout and concrete gain strength.
- **Service function:** Resist any small **tensile uplift force** arising from wind, seismic action, or accidental eccentric loading on the column.
- For **pure axial compression** (typical slab base case), the bolts are **not designed to resist the column load itself**; they are nominal.

**IS 800:2007 Recommendations:**
- Minimum **2 anchor bolts** for light/tubular columns.
- Minimum **4 anchor bolts** for built-up or major columns.
- Minimum bolt diameter: **20 mm** for cleat or gusseted bases; **16 mm** is acceptable for simple slab bases.
- Embedment length: **12 × bolt diameter** with a hook at the bottom (or use a bolt with anchor plate at the bottom).

(3 marks awarded for explaining both roles + IS recommendations)

---

### 5.2 Part B Question with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice - Option A]

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Slot B]**

A steel column **ISMB 400** carries an axial factored load of **$P_u = 1800$ kN**. Design a suitable slab base for the column given:
- Column section ISMB 400: $D = 400$ mm, $b_f = 140$ mm, $t_w = 8.9$ mm, $t_f = 16.0$ mm
- Concrete pedestal grade: **M20** ($f_{ck} = 20$ MPa)
- Steel grade: **Fe 410** ($f_y = 250$ MPa)
- Partial safety factor $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$

**(a)** Determine the plan dimensions and thickness of the base plate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Provide the complete design of the base plate including anchor bolts and weld sketch. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Plate Dimensions and Thickness:**

**Step 1:** Required bearing area on concrete.
$$A_{req} = \dfrac{P_u}{0.45 \cdot f_{ck}} = \dfrac{1800 \times 10^3}{0.45 \times 20} = \dfrac{1800000}{9} = 200000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Choose projections of 100 mm on all sides.
$$L = D + 2a = 400 + 2(100) = 600 \text{ mm}$$
$$B = b_f + 2b = 140 + 2(100) = 340 \text{ mm}$$
$$A_{prov} = 600 \times 340 = 204000 \text{ mm}^2 > A_{req} \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** Actual bearing pressure.
$$w = \dfrac{P_u}{L \cdot B} = \dfrac{1800 \times 10^3}{600 \times 340} = \dfrac{1800000}{204000} \approx 8.82 \text{ MPa}$$

Check: $w = 8.82 < 0.45 \times 20 = 9.0$ MPa $\checkmark$ (Safe)

**Step 4:** Projections.
$$a = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad b = 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Bending moments.
$$M_x = \dfrac{w \cdot a^2}{2} = \dfrac{8.82 \times 100^2}{2} = 44100 \text{ N·mm/mm}$$
$$M_y = \dfrac{w \cdot b^2}{2} = \dfrac{8.82 \times 100^2}{2} = 44100 \text{ N·mm/mm}$$
$$M = 44100 \text{ N·mm/mm}$$

**Step 6:** Required thickness.
$$t_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 44100 \times 1.10}{250}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{291060}{250}} = \sqrt{1164.24} \approx 34.12 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7:** Add corrosion allowance.
$$t_{final} = 34.12 + 2 = 36.12 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \boxed{t_{provided} = 40 \text{ mm (standard)}}$$

**Plate Size: 600 mm × 340 mm × 40 mm thick.** **(7 marks completed)**

---

**Part (b) — Anchor Bolts, Weld and Final Sketch:**

**Anchor Bolts:**
Provide **4 numbers of 20 mm diameter mild steel anchor bolts**, one at each corner.
- Hole diameter in plate = 20 + 3 = **23 mm**
- Edge distance = 1.5 × 23 ≈ **35 mm** (minimum)
- Embedment length in pedestal = 12 × 20 = **240 mm**, hooked at bottom
- Minimum spacing between bolts = 2.5 × 20 = **50 mm**

**Weld Design:**
Weld length required (around column perimeter):
$$l_w = 2 b_f + 2(D - 2 t_f) = 2(140) + 2(400 - 32) = 280 + 736 = 1016 \text{ mm}$$

Assuming a fillet weld of size $s$, weld strength:
$$R_{wd} = l_w \times 0.707 s \times \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m1}}$$
$$1800 \times 10^3 = 1016 \times 0.707 \times s \times \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25}$$
$$1800000 = 1016 \times 0.707 \times s \times 189.4$$
$$s = \dfrac{1800000}{1016 \times 0.707 \times 189.4} = \dfrac{1800000}{136071.7} \approx 13.23 \text{ mm}$$

Provide **14 mm fillet weld** all around. **(7 marks completed)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice - Option B]

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Slot A]**

A **hollow circular steel column** of external diameter **300 mm** and 10 mm thick carries a factored axial load of **$P_u = 1200$ kN**. Design a slab base for this tubular column. The concrete pedestal is of **M25 grade**. Use Fe 410 steel.

**(a)** Determine the base plate plan dimensions and thickness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the designed slab base and specify anchor bolt details. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Plate Dimensions and Thickness:**

**Step 1:** Required bearing area.
$$A_{req} = \dfrac{P_u}{0.45 \cdot f_{ck}} = \dfrac{1200 \times 10^3}{0.45 \times 25} = \dfrac{1200000}{11.25} \approx 106667 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Provide a square plate. Minimum projection = 100 mm.
$$L = 300 + 2(100) = 500 \text{ mm}$$
$$B = 300 + 2(100) = 500 \text{ mm}$$
$$A_{prov} = 500 \times 500 = 250000 \text{ mm}^2 > A_{req} \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** Bearing pressure.
$$w = \dfrac{1200 \times 10^3}{500 \times 500} = \dfrac{1200000}{250000} = 4.8 \text{ MPa}$$

Check: $w = 4.8 < 0.45 \times 25 = 11.25$ MPa $\checkmark$ (Very safe — could reduce plate, but 500x500 is practical)

**Step 4:** Cantilever projection.
$$a = (500 - 300)/2 = 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Bending moment (symmetric square plate, so $M_x = M_y$).
$$M = \dfrac{w \cdot a^2}{2} = \dfrac{4.8 \times 100^2}{2} = 24000 \text{ N·mm/mm}$$

**Step 6:** Required thickness.
$$t_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 24000 \times 1.10}{250}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{158400}{250}} = \sqrt{633.6} \approx 25.17 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7:** With corrosion allowance.
$$t_{final} = 25.17 + 2 = 27.17 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \boxed{t_{provided} = 28 \text{ mm (use standard 32 mm for safety)}}$$

**Plate Size: 500 mm × 500 mm × 32 mm thick.** **(7 marks completed)**

---

**Part (b) — Sketch and Anchor Bolt Details:**

**Sketch Description:**
A square plate of 500 mm × 500 mm × 32 mm thick is placed on a 50 mm thick grout layer above an M25 concrete pedestal. The hollow circular column of 300 mm outer diameter and 10 mm thickness is welded to the plate using a continuous fillet weld of size 10 mm around the entire 360° perimeter.

To prevent local buckling of the base plate beneath the thin column wall (10 mm is quite thin), provide **4 numbers of triangular stiffeners (gussets)** at 90° intervals between the column wall and the plate. Each stiffener is 100 mm wide × 12 mm thick × 150 mm tall (vertical). These stiffeners act as additional supports for the plate, but for a conservative slab base design they may be omitted and the plate thickened — which is what we did by providing 32 mm instead of 28 mm.

**Anchor Bolts:**
Provide **4 numbers of 20 mm diameter mild steel anchor bolts** at the four corners.
- Hole diameter in plate = 23 mm
- Edge distance from bolt center to plate edge = 75 mm (more than adequate)
- Embedment in pedestal = 240 mm (hooked at bottom)
- Bolt pitch (center-to-center) = 500 - 2(75) = **350 mm**

For hollow tubular columns, the anchor bolts are particularly important during **erection** since the tubular section cannot itself be easily bolted to the plate. **(7 marks completed)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Slab Base Problems:**
>
> **1. Forgetting the 0.45 factor:**
> Students often write $A_{req} = P_u / f_{ck}$ instead of $P_u / (0.45 \cdot f_{ck})$. This gives a **45% smaller plate**, which is **unsafe**. **Loss: 1 mark**.
>
> **2. Mixing up projections a and b:**
> Using $a = (B - b_f)/2$ and $b = (L - D)/2$. The projection $a$ is **always** along the depth direction (associated with $D$), and $b$ along the flange width (associated with $b_f$). **Loss: 1-2 marks**.
>
> **3. Forgetting corrosion allowance:**
> Always add **at least 2 mm** to $t_{req}$. Some textbooks add 1.5 mm, but **2 mm is the IS code recommendation**. **Loss: 1 mark**.
>
> **4. Using service load instead of factored load:**
> In limit state design, **always multiply by 1.5**. If you forget, your answer will be ~33% undersized. **Loss: 2-3 marks**.
>
> **5. Not rounding up to standard plate thickness:**
> IS 800 standard plate thicknesses are: 16, 20, 22, 25, 32, 36, 40, 45, 50, 56, 63 mm. If you calculate $t = 27.2$ mm, you must say **provide 32 mm**, not 27.2 mm. **Loss: 1 mark**.
>
> **6. Omitting anchor bolt provision:**
> Even for nominal bolts, **mentioning them with diameter, length, embedment, and number** is essential for full marks. **Loss: 1-2 marks**.
>
> **7. Wrong unit for bending moment:**
> Use **N·mm/mm** or **kN·m/m**, not just "moment". Examiners check units. **Loss: 0.5 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] A **slab base** is used for **light axially-loaded steel columns** on concrete pedestals.
- [x] It consists of a **single thick base plate**, **anchor bolts**, and a **grout layer**.
- [x] **Bearing strength of concrete** = $0.45 \cdot f_{ck}$ (as per IS 456).
- [x] **Required plate area** $A_{req} = P_u / (0.45 \cdot f_{ck})$.
- [x] Plate length $L = D + 2a$; Plate width $B = b_f + 2b$; keep $a \approx b$ for square plate preference.
- [x] **Bearing pressure** $w = P_u / (L \cdot B) \leq 0.45 \cdot f_{ck}$.
- [x] **Critical section for bending** is at the **column face**, for cantilever projection arms.
- [x] **Bending moment** $M = w \cdot (\text{projection})^2 / 2$ — take **maximum** of the two directions.
- [x] **Plate thickness** $t_{req} = \sqrt{6 M \gamma_{m0} / f_y}$ with $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$.
- [x] Always add **2 mm corrosion allowance** and round up to **standard plate thickness**.
- [x] **Anchor bolts** for slab bases are nominal — typically **4 nos × 20 mm dia**, embedded **12d into pedestal**, hooked at bottom.
- [x] **Bolt hole diameter** = bolt diameter + 3 mm (small bolts) or + 6 mm (large bolts).
- [x] **Edge distance** ≥ 1.5 × hole diameter; **bolt pitch** ≥ 2.5 × bolt diameter.
- [x] **Weld between column and plate** must transfer full factored load $P_u$. Calculate size as $s = P_u / (l_w \times 0.707 \times f_{wd})$.
- [x] Use **fillet weld** typically 8 to 14 mm size for slab bases.
- [x] **Grout** of 25-50 mm thickness (1:2 cement-sand mortar) ensures uniform bearing.
- [x] For **farm structures** (KTU PCAGT501 context), axial loads are typically 200-800 kN → plate thickness 20-35 mm, plate size 300×300 to 500×500 mm.
- [x] If **bending moment is significant**, **escalate to gusseted base** — slab base is inadequate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Farm Infrastructures - Functional and Structural Design of Farm Infrastructures and Analysis of Stability of the Structures; Design of Residential Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Farm Infrastructures - Functional and Structural Design of Farm Infrastructures and Analysis of Stability of the Structures; Design of Residential Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Farm Infrastructure — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Farm Infrastructure** refers to the **constructions, facilities, and built systems** that physically support agricultural production, post-harvest operations, livestock management, and rural habitation. These include **storage godowns, processing sheds, dairy barns, poultry houses, farm offices, workshops, greenhouses, silos, and residential buildings** for farm owners and laborers.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context for **PCAGT501**, farm infrastructure is studied under two parallel design streams:

1. **Functional Design** — "What does the building *do*, and how efficiently does it serve its purpose?"
2. **Structural Design** — "Will the building *stand up* safely under all expected loads?"

## 1.2 Functional Design — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Design** of farm structures is the process of planning the **internal layout, spatial workflow, ergonomics, ventilation, lighting, drainage, and accessibility** such that the building optimally supports the intended agricultural operation (e.g., milking, drying, storage, processing) while ensuring the **safety, comfort, and productivity** of humans and animals.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Layout" Analogy

Think of designing a farm dairy like designing a **restaurant kitchen**:
- A chef (the farmer/cow) needs ingredients, tools, and movement space.
- The **workflow path** (cow enters → milking stall → feed trough → exit) should never cross a "dirty" path (manure drain) — just like raw chicken should not cross the salad prep area in a kitchen.
- The **size of each station** (stall width, feed manger length) is determined by the **"customer"** (the animal or produce), not the architect's aesthetic preference.
- If the kitchen is *pretty* but the chef cannot reach the stove, it has **failed functionally** — no matter how strong the concrete is.

This is exactly why **functional design comes BEFORE structural design**: a building that collapses is bad, but a building that does not let a farmer milk a cow efficiently is equally useless.

## 1.3 Structural Design — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structural Design** is the engineering process of proportioning the **load-carrying members** (foundations, columns, beams, slabs, walls, trusses, and connections) of a farm structure so that it can safely resist all **anticipated loads** (dead, live, wind, seismic, snow, equipment vibration) throughout its **design life (typically 50–75 years)** with an adequate **factor of safety** and acceptable **serviceability** (deflection, crack width, vibration limits).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Skeleton and Skin" Analogy

A farm building is like a **human body**:
- The **structural frame** (columns, beams, trusses, foundation) is the **skeleton** — it holds the body up and lets you move.
- The **walls, roof sheeting, doors, windows** are the **skin and clothes** — they protect from weather, give privacy, and provide comfort.
- The **foundation** is the **feet** — if they are weak or on soft soil, the whole body tilts and falls.
- Structural design = making sure the **bones are strong enough** to carry the weight of the body + any backpack (loads) it might carry, even during an earthquake or strong wind.

## 1.4 Stability Analysis — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Analysis** of a farm structure is the verification that the structure, when subjected to **overturning, sliding, and uplift forces** (caused by wind, seismic events, water pressure, or eccentric loads), does **not topple, slide, or float** because the **resisting forces and moments** (from self-weight, friction, anchorage, and passive earth pressure) are sufficient to maintain equilibrium.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Tall Bookshelf on a Tiled Floor" Analogy

Imagine a **tall, heavy bookshelf** on a smooth marble floor:
- **Overturning**: Push it at the top → it tries to tip forward like a falling domino.
- **Sliding**: Push it at the middle → it tries to skid across the floor.
- **Uplift**: A strong gust from below (e.g., wind under a roof) → it tries to lift off the ground.
- The bookshelf's **self-weight** keeps it pressed down (resists uplift), **friction** between base and floor (resists sliding), and the **width of the base × weight** creates a restoring moment (resists overturning).

A **farm silo, water tank, or tall poultry house** behaves exactly the same way under wind or earthquake — and that is what stability analysis quantifies.

## 1.5 Residential Building (Farmhouse) Design — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **farm residential building** (farmhouse, labor quarters, estate manager's bungalow) is a dwelling unit located on or adjacent to a farm, designed to provide **habitable, hygienic, and structurally safe** accommodation. Its design must follow the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)**, **IS 8888 (Farm Buildings)**, and local **Panchayat / Municipality building rules** for Kerala.

Key functional requirements specific to Kerala farmhouses:
- **Monsoon-resilient roofing** (sloped, ≥30° pitch, with wide overhangs ≥600 mm)
- **Cross-ventilation** (prevailing SW/NE winds; window-to-floor ratio ≥1/6)
- **Stilt / plinth raising** ≥450 mm above finished ground level (flood protection)
- **Pest and termite control** (anti-termite treatment as per **IS 6313**)
- **Septic tank + soak pit** (since farms are typically off-municipal sewer lines)

## 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard values (as per **IS 875 (Parts 1–5): 1987** and **NBC 2016**) are essential for KTU exam answers:
>
> - **Unit weight of reinforced concrete:** $\gamma_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$
> - **Unit weight of plain cement concrete:** $\gamma_{pcc} = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$
> - **Unit weight of brick masonry:** $\gamma_{bm} = 20 \text{ kN/m}^3$
> - **Unit weight of structural steel:** $\gamma_s = 78.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$
> - **Unit weight of water:** $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3 \approx 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$
> - **Design life (farm structure):** **50 years** (NBC 2016, Table 3)
> - **Design life (residential):** **50 years**
> - **Basic wind speed for Kerala (Zone V/Z):** **Vb = 39 m/s** to **44 m/s** (IS 875 Part 3, Map of India)
> - **Seismic zone of Kerala:** **Zone III** (Kasaragod, Kannur, Wayanad) and **Zone II** (rest of Kerala) — **IS 1893:2016**
> - **Minimum factor of safety against overturning:** **1.5** (IS 456, Clause 20.1 for water tanks; NBC for buildings = 1.5)
> - **Minimum factor of safety against sliding:** **1.5**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two-Track Design Philosophy

A farm infrastructure project is delivered in **two parallel design tracks** that converge at the **final working drawing**:

### Track A — Functional Design (Architecture + Planning)
- **Step 1:** Define the **activity** (e.g., "store 50 tonnes of paddy", "house 20 dairy cows", "process 200 L milk/day").
- **Step 2:** Quantify **inputs and outputs** (raw material in, finished product out, waste out).
- **Step 3:** Derive **space standards** using standard references (e.g., floor area per animal, ventilation openings as % of wall area).
- **Step 4:** Sketch the **layout** — zoning (clean/dirty, hot/cold, public/private), circulation paths, sunlight direction, wind direction.
- **Step 5:** Verify against **ergonomic and animal-welfare norms** (e.g., dairy cow stall: 1.2 m × 2.0 m; pig pen: 0.9 m × 2.1 m per pig).
- **Step 6:** Finalize the **plan, elevation, and section** drawings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why?** A structurally perfect barn that forces a farmer to walk 200 m to fetch feed is a failed design. Functional design ensures **operational efficiency**, which determines whether the farm makes money.

### Track B — Structural Design (Engineering)
- **Step 1:** Read functional drawings → extract **loads** (dead load from materials, live load from occupancy/storage/animals).
- **Step 2:** Apply **load combinations** as per **IS 875 Part 5** (e.g., 1.5(DL + LL), 1.2(DL + LL ± WL), etc.).
- **Step 3:** Analyze the structural frame → compute **bending moments, shear forces, axial forces**.
- **Step 4:** Design **individual members** using **limit state method (IS 456 for RCC, IS 800 for steel)**.
- **Step 5:** Check **serviceability** (deflection ≤ L/250 for beams; crack width ≤ 0.3 mm for RCC).
- **Step 6:** Detail the **reinforcement / steel sections, connections, and foundations**.
- **Step 7:** Verify **stability** against overturning, sliding, and uplift.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why?** Agriculture in Kerala has high humidity, monsoon cyclones, and seismic risk. A structure that fails during the **2018 Kerala floods** or a future cyclone is a liability, not an asset.

## 2.2 Functional Design Standards for Common Farm Structures

| Structure Type | Key Functional Standard | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Paddy storage godown | Floor area: 0.5 m² per tonne; stack height ≤ 3 m | IS 6071, FCI norms |
| Dairy cow shed | Covered area: 3.5–4.0 m² per cow; stall 1.2 m × 2.0 m | IS 11799 |
| Poultry house (deep litter) | 0.3–0.4 m² per bird; ridge ventilation ≥ 1/3 roof area | IS 14684 |
| Piggery | 0.9–1.2 m² per pig (fattening); 1.5 m² per sow | IS 3916 |
| Mushroom house | Hygrometer-controlled; 80–95% RH, 22–28 °C | BIS guidelines |
| Greenhouse (polyhouse) | 1000 m² typical polyhouse with gutter-connected arches | NHM / MIDH norms |
| Farmhouse (residential) | Min. plinth area 25 m² (1BHK); rooms 9–12 m² | NBC 2016, IS 8888 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU exam tip:** When a question asks for "functional design of a dairy barn", always mention: **stall size, manger dimensions, drainage slope (1 in 60), orientation (long axis East-West to minimize direct sun), roof slope (≥ 30°), and ridge ventilator (continuous, 300–450 mm throat width).**

## 2.3 Structural Loads on Farm Buildings (IS 875)

### 2.3.1 Dead Load (DL) — IS 875 Part 1
The self-weight of all permanent components:
- Roof sheeting (AC sheets: **0.10 kN/m²**; GI sheets: **0.05–0.10 kN/m²**)
- Purlins, trusses, bracing
- Slab self-weight (150 mm RCC slab = **25 × 0.15 = 3.75 kN/m²**)
- Wall self-weight (230 mm brick wall = **20 × 0.23 = 4.60 kN/m²** plus plaster 0.5 kN/m²)

### 2.3.2 Live Load (LL) — IS 875 Part 2
For farm structures, LL is **highly variable**:

| Farm Occupancy | Live Load (kN/m²) |
|---|---|
| Residential floors | 2.0 |
| Office floors in farm | 2.5 |
| Storage godown (paddy, 3 m stack) | 15.0 (approx. 5 kN/m³ bulk density × 3 m) |
| Cattle shed floor | 2.0 (animal weight spread) |
| Poultry floor (deep litter) | 2.0 |
| Workshop / garage | 5.0 |
| Staircase | 3.0 |
| Balcony | 3.0 |

### 2.3.3 Wind Load (WL) — IS 875 Part 3
Wind pressure on a structure:

$$p_z = 0.6 \, V_z^2$$

where $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$ (in m/s) and $p_z$ is in N/m².
- $V_b$ = basic wind speed (m/s)
- $k_1$ = risk coefficient (depends on building importance)
- $k_2$ = terrain, height, and structure size factor
- $k_3$ = topography factor
- $k_4$ = importance factor for cyclonic regions

**Design wind force** on the whole structure:

$$F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$$

where $C_f$ = force coefficient, $A_e$ = effective frontal area.

### 2.3.4 Seismic Load (EL) — IS 1893:2016
For Kerala (Zone II/III), the design horizontal seismic coefficient is:

$$A_h = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g}$$

where:
- $Z$ = zone factor (**0.10 for Zone II, 0.16 for Zone III**)
- $I$ = importance factor (1.0 for normal, 1.5 for critical)
- $R$ = response reduction factor (3.0 for ordinary RC frame, 5.0 for SMRF)
- $S_a/g$ = spectral acceleration ratio (depends on natural period T)

**Design base shear**:

$$V_B = A_h \cdot W$$

where $W$ = seismic weight of the building (DL + fraction of LL).

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (CHEAT SHEET)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula you must memorize** for the KTU board exam on this topic. **Print this and revise before the exam.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wind pressure | $p_z = 0.6 \, V_z^2$ | $p_z$ in N/m², $V_z$ in m/s | IS 875 Part 3 |
| 2 | Design wind speed | $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$ | All in m/s | IS 875 Part 3 |
| 3 | Wind force | $F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$ | $F$ in N, $A_e$ in m² | IS 875 Part 3 |
| 4 | Seismic coefficient | $A_h = \dfrac{Z}{2} \cdot \dfrac{I}{R} \cdot \dfrac{S_a}{g}$ | Dimensionless | IS 1893:2016 |
| 5 | Design base shear | $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ | $V_B$ in kN, $W$ in kN | IS 1893:2016 |
| 6 | FOS against overturning | $\text{FOS}_{OT} = \dfrac{M_R}{M_O} \geq 1.5$ | Both in kN·m | IS 456, NBC 2016 |
| 7 | FOS against sliding | $\text{FOS}_{SL} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot W + F_p}{H} \geq 1.5$ | Forces in kN | IS 456, NBC |
| 8 | FOS against uplift | $\text{FOS}_{UP} = \dfrac{W + D_a}{U} \geq 1.5$ | Forces in kN | IS 456, NBC |
| 9 | Max bearing pressure | $q_{max} = \dfrac{W}{B \cdot L} \left(1 + \dfrac{6e_x}{B} + \dfrac{6e_y}{L}\right)$ | kN/m² | Soil Mechanics |
| 10 | Slab self-weight | $w_{slab} = \gamma_c \cdot t$ | $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³, $t$ in m | IS 875 Part 1 |
| 11 | Brick wall load | $w_{wall} = \gamma_{bm} \cdot t_w + \text{plaster}$ | $\gamma_{bm} = 20$ kN/m³, $t_w$ in m | IS 875 Part 1 |
| 12 | Load combo (limit state) | $1.5(DL + LL)$; $1.2(DL + LL \pm WL)$; $0.9 DL \pm 1.5 WL$ | — | IS 875 Part 5 |
| 13 | Min. deflection (beam) | $\delta \leq L/250$ (general); $L/350$ (roof) | mm | IS 456 Cl. 23.2 |
| 14 | Min. crack width (RCC) | $w \leq 0.3$ mm | mm | IS 456 Cl. 35.3.2 |
| 15 | Slump for RCC (medium) | 75–100 mm | mm | IS 456 Cl. 7.1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** When writing load combinations, **always use the most unfavorable combination**. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you miss the wind/seismic combinations on a structure that is clearly exposed (e.g., a farm godown in Kasaragod).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cyclone-resilient farm design** (e.g., post-2018 Kerala floods and Cyclone Ockhi 2017): Proper FOS calculations prevented loss of stored grain and livestock shelters.
- **Flood-resistant farmhouse** in Kuttanad: Plinth raising + stilts verified by stability analysis reduced flood damage.
- **Dairy cooperative buildings** (e.g., Milma, Hatsun): Designed for 20 kN/m² live load (heavy milk cans, refrigerated storage) — without functional design, the operations would not fit.
- **Coconut and spice warehouses** (Kerala GAP-certified): Ventilation design (≥20% wall opening) prevented fungal spoilage — direct economic gain.
