# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Stability Analysis — Detailed Derivation Framework

Stability analysis of a farm structure (e.g., an overhead water tank on a staging, a tall godown, or a tower silo) requires verification of three failure modes: **overturning, sliding, and uplift**. The governing equations are derived from **static equilibrium**.

### 3.1.1 Overturning Stability — Full Derivation

Consider a rectangular structure of base width $B$ and height $H$, subjected to a horizontal wind force $F_W$ acting at height $H/2$ (for uniform pressure) or at $H$ (for triangular pressure).

**Step 1:** Identify the **overturning moment** (M_O). The horizontal force $F_W$ acts at a lever arm $h$ above the base (where $h$ is the height of the resultant):

$$M_O = F_W \cdot h$$

**Step 2:** Identify the **restoring moment** (M_R) due to the self-weight $W$ acting at the centroid of the base. For a symmetric base, the centroid is at $B/2$ from the toe:

$$M_R = W \cdot \frac{B}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **factor of safety** criterion:

$$\text{FOS}_{OT} = \frac{M_R}{M_O} \geq 1.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation logic:** The factor of safety is defined as the ratio of the **capacity** (resisting moment) to the **demand** (overturning moment). A safety factor of 1.5 means the structure can withstand 50% more load than designed before toppling.

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — Overturning of a Farm Water Tank Stand

**Given:** A square water tank stand has a base of $B = 3$ m and total height $H = 9$ m. Self-weight of empty tank + staging = **$W = 150$ kN**. Wind force at the top of staging = **$F_W = 25$ kN** acting horizontally at $h = 7$ m above the base.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the overturning moment.

$$M_O = F_W \cdot h = 25 \times 7 = 175 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the restoring moment.

$$M_R = W \cdot \frac{B}{2} = 150 \times \frac{3}{2} = 150 \times 1.5 = 225 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the FOS.

$$\text{FOS}_{OT} = \frac{M_R}{M_O} = \frac{225}{175} = 1.286$$

**Step 4:** Compare with the code minimum.

Since $\text{FOS}_{OT} = 1.286 < 1.5$ **(required)**, the structure **FAILS** the overturning check.

**Step 5:** Design correction.
Increase base width so that $B_{new}$ satisfies:

$$W \cdot \frac{B_{new}}{2} \geq 1.5 \cdot M_O$$

$$B_{new} \geq \frac{2 \times 1.5 \times 175}{150} = \frac{525}{150} = 3.5 \text{ m}$$

**Adopt $B = 3.5$ m** (revised square base).

### 3.1.3 Sliding Stability — Full Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the horizontal driving force (e.g., wind or earthquake):

$$H = F_W \quad \text{or} \quad H = V_B \text{ (base shear)}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the **resisting force** = friction at base + passive earth pressure (if foundation is embedded).

For a base on soil with coefficient of friction $\mu$:

$$F_R = \mu \cdot W$$

**Step 3:** Apply the FOS:

$$\text{FOS}_{SL} = \frac{\mu \cdot W}{H} \geq 1.5$$

### 3.1.4 Worked Example — Sliding of a Grain Godown

**Given:** A paddy godown of weight $W = 800$ kN is subjected to a horizontal seismic force $H = 120$ kN. Coefficient of friction between base and PCC bed = $\mu = 0.45$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute resisting friction.

$$F_R = \mu \cdot W = 0.45 \times 800 = 360 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2:** Compute FOS against sliding.

$$\text{FOS}_{SL} = \frac{360}{120} = 3.0$$

**Step 3:** Check the requirement.

Since $\text{FOS}_{SL} = 3.0 \geq 1.5$ **(required)**, the structure is **SAFE** against sliding.

### 3.1.5 Bearing Pressure under Eccentric Load — Full Derivation

When a wind or seismic force creates an overturning moment $M_O$, the resultant vertical load $W$ shifts from the centroid by an **eccentricity** $e$:

$$e = \frac{M_O}{W}$$

**Step 1:** For a rectangular base $B \times L$, the **kern limit** is $B/6$. If $e \leq B/6$, the entire base is in compression (no uplift).

**Step 2:** The bearing pressure distribution becomes **trapezoidal or triangular**. Using the **elastic foundation theory**:

$$q_{max,min} = \frac{W}{A} \pm \frac{M_O \cdot x}{I}$$

For a rectangular base, the moment of inertia about the axis perpendicular to the eccentricity is $I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$, and the section modulus is $Z = \frac{L \cdot B^2}{6}$.

**Step 3:** The simplified formula for **maximum and minimum bearing pressure** under eccentricity $e_x$ along $B$ and $e_y$ along $L$ is:

$$q_{max} = \frac{W}{B \cdot L} + \frac{6 \cdot W \cdot e_x}{L \cdot B^2} + \frac{6 \cdot W \cdot e_y}{B \cdot L^2}$$

$$q_{min} = \frac{W}{B \cdot L} - \frac{6 \cdot W \cdot e_x}{L \cdot B^2} - \frac{6 \cdot W \cdot e_y}{B \cdot L^2}$$

**Step 4:** Verify:

$$q_{max} \leq q_{allowable} \quad \text{(Safe Bearing Capacity, SBC of soil)}$$

**Step 5:** If $q_{min}$ becomes **negative**, the base is partially **uplifted**, and the effective base reduces. The above formula must be re-applied with the reduced contact area.

### 3.1.6 Worked Example — Bearing Pressure under a Farm Sump

**Given:** A water-sump tower of weight $W = 200$ kN, base $B = 2.5$ m, $L = 2.5$ m. Overturning moment from wind $M_O = 30$ kN·m. Allowable bearing pressure of soil $q_{allow} = 150$ kN/m².

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute eccentricity.

$$e = \frac{M_O}{W} = \frac{30}{200} = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Check kern.

$$\frac{B}{6} = \frac{2.5}{6} = 0.417 \text{ m}$$

Since $e = 0.15 \text{ m} < 0.417 \text{ m}$ **(within kern)**, the base is **fully in compression**.

**Step 3:** Compute $q_{max}$ and $q_{min}$ (with $e_y = 0$):

$$q_{max} = \frac{W}{A} + \frac{6W e_x}{L B^2} = \frac{200}{2.5 \times 2.5} + \frac{6 \times 200 \times 0.15}{2.5 \times 2.5^2}$$

$$q_{max} = 32 + \frac{180}{15.625} = 32 + 11.52 = 43.52 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$q_{min} = 32 - 11.52 = 20.48 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Compare with allowable.

$$q_{max} = 43.52 \text{ kN/m}^2 \ll 150 \text{ kN/m}^2 = q_{allow}$$

**Structure is SAFE.** A high margin exists → the design can be **economized** by reducing the base to $B = 2.0$ m (try as a follow-up problem).

## 3.2 Load Combination Table for Farm Structures (IS 875 Part 5)

| Combination No. | Load Combination | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1.5 (DL + LL)$ | No wind, no seismic; gravity-only check |
| 2 | $0.9 DL \pm 1.5 WL$ | Wind controls, uplift check |
| 3 | $1.2 (DL + LL \pm WL)$ | Wind + gravity combined |
| 4 | $1.2 (DL + LL \pm EL)$ | Seismic + gravity |
| 5 | $0.9 DL \pm 1.5 EL$ | Seismic uplift check |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners' trap:** Students often forget combination 2 (uplift check). For a lightweight farm roof with strong wind, **uplift** (suction) is more critical than gravity, and the roof may **lift off** if the dead load is insufficient.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Tree

| Condition | Parameter | Value | Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roof slope $< 10°$ | Drainage | Ponding risk | Increase slope to ≥ 30° (Kerala monsoon) |
| Stall width $< 1.0$ m$ | Cow comfort | Animal injury | Increase to 1.2 m |
| $q_{max} > q_{allow}$ | Bearing | Soil failure | Enlarge base OR improve soil (RCC piles) |
| $\text{FOS}_{OT} < 1.5$ | Overturning | Collapse risk | Widen base OR add hold-down bolts |
| $\text{FOS}_{SL} < 1.5$ | Sliding | Shear failure | Add shear key in foundation |
| Window area $< 1/6$ of floor | Light/ventilation | Poor hygiene | Increase openings, add ridge vent |
| Plinth height $< 450$ mm | Flood risk | Wetting of floor | Raise plinth to 600–900 mm |

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Load Path in a Farm Godown

This **Mermaid block diagram** shows how vertical and lateral loads travel from the roof down to the foundation, which is essential for understanding stability.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Roof Sheeting AC/GI] -->|DL+WL| node2[Purlin]
    node2 -->|Reaction| node3[Truss Top Chord]
    node3 -->|Axial Compression| node4[Truss Bottom Chord]
    node4 -->|Reaction| node5[Truss Joint]
    node5 -->|Vertical Load| node6[Column Top]
    node6 -->|Axial+BM| node7[Column Base]
    node7 -->|Compressive Load| node8[Column Footing]
    node8 -->|Bearing Pressure q| node9[Soil SBC]
    node5 -->|Wind Horizontal| node10[Bracing System]
    node10 -->|Lateral Force| node7
    style node1 fill:#ffd6a5
    style node9 fill:#caffbf
    style node10 fill:#ffadad
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Orange block** (node1) = starting point of load.
- **Green block** (node9) = the soil — ultimate receiver of all loads.
- **Red block** (node10) = horizontal load path (bracing). Note that **bracing** carries wind/seismic loads horizontally to the column base, where they are resisted by **friction** and **passive earth pressure**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between functional design and structural design of a farm building. Give one example of a situation where a functionally well-designed building may still fail structurally.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Functional design** is the planning of a building's **internal layout, space, workflow, ventilation, lighting, and ergonomics** to suit its intended agricultural purpose (e.g., dairy, storage, processing). It answers the question: *"Will the building let the farmer do his work efficiently?"*

**Structural design** is the engineering of the building's **load-carrying members** (foundation, columns, beams, roof trusses, connections) to safely resist all **dead, live, wind, and seismic loads** throughout its design life. It answers: *"Will the building stand up safely under all expected loads?"*

**Example of functional success + structural failure (1 mark):**
A beautifully laid-out **dairy barn** with proper stall spacing, drainage slope, and ventilation — but with a **roof truss of undersized angle sections** that buckles and collapses under the first heavy monsoon wind of 50 km/h. The cows cannot be milked because the building has failed structurally, even though the layout was perfect.

---

**Model Answer (3 marks = 6 codes × 0.5 mark each):**

| # | IS Code | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **IS 875 (Part 1): 1987** | Unit weights of building materials (Dead Load) |
| 2 | **IS 875 (Part 2): 1987** | Live loads on floors and roofs |
| 3 | **IS 875 (Part 3): 2015** | Wind loads on structures |
| 4 | **IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016** | Earthquake-resistant design of buildings |
| 5 | **IS 456: 2000** | Plain and reinforced concrete design |
| 6 | **IS 800: 2007** | General construction in steel (Limit State Method) |
| 7 (bonus) | **IS 6313** | Anti-termite treatment in buildings |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) A farm overhead water tank of total weight (empty tank + staging) W = 180 kN is supported on a square base. The tank is located in a coastal Kerala district where the basic wind speed is Vb = 44 m/s. The structure has a height H = 10 m and a square base of side B = 3.5 m. Compute the design wind pressure and check the stability of the structure against overturning. Take k2 = 1.05 (Category 2 terrain, 10 m height), k1 = 1.0, k3 = 1.0, k4 = 1.0, and force coefficient Cf = 1.2. Assume the wind acts at 2H/3 from the base (triangular pressure distribution).** **[7 marks]**

**(b) Explain the functional design requirements of a dairy cattle shed suitable for Kerala conditions. List at least six functional requirements with justification.** **[7 marks]**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (a) — Overturning Check**

**Step 1: Compute the design wind speed.**

$$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$$

$$V_z = 44 \times 1.0 \times 1.05 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 46.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the design wind pressure.**

$$p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2 = 0.6 \times (46.2)^2$$

$$p_z = 0.6 \times 2134.44 = 1280.66 \text{ N/m}^2 = 1.2807 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the effective frontal area.**

For a square tower, the projected area is:

$$A_e = B \times H = 3.5 \times 10 = 35 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute the design wind force.**

$$F_W = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z = 1.2 \times 35 \times 1.2807$$

$$F_W = 53.79 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Compute the overturning moment (M_O).**

For triangular pressure, the resultant acts at $h = \frac{2H}{3}$ from the base:

$$h = \frac{2 \times 10}{3} = 6.667 \text{ m}$$

$$M_O = F_W \cdot h = 53.79 \times 6.667 = 358.55 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 6: Compute the restoring moment (M_R).**

For a square base with weight acting at the centroid ($B/2$ from the toe):

$$M_R = W \cdot \frac{B}{2} = 180 \times \frac{3.5}{2} = 180 \times 1.75 = 315 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 7: Compute the Factor of Safety.**

$$\text{FOS}_{OT} = \frac{M_R}{M_O} = \frac{315}{358.55} = 0.878$$

**Step 8: Conclusion.**

Since $\text{FOS}_{OT} = 0.878 \ll 1.5$ **(required)**, the structure is **UNSAFE against overturning**.

**Step 9: Design correction.**

Required base width $B_{new}$:

$$B_{new} \geq \frac{2 \times 1.5 \times M_O}{W} = \frac{2 \times 1.5 \times 358.55}{180} = 5.98 \text{ m}$$

**Adopt B = 6.0 m** (revised square base) OR keep B = 3.5 m and **add a base raft/ballast of additional weight**:

$$W_{req} \geq \frac{1.5 \times M_O}{B/2} = \frac{1.5 \times 358.55}{1.75} = 307.3 \text{ kN}$$

Additional dead load needed = $307.3 - 180 = 127.3$ kN.

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (b) — Functional Design of a Dairy Cattle Shed**

A dairy cattle shed for Kerala should satisfy the following **six functional requirements** (each 1 mark, with 1 mark for the introduction/overall layout):

| # | Functional Requirement | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Orientation: Long axis East–West** | Minimizes direct solar gain on the long sidewalls; keeps the shed cooler. |
| 2 | **Floor space: 3.5–4.0 m² per cow** | Provides adequate lying and movement area; reduces aggression among cows. |
| 3 | **Stall dimensions: 1.2 m wide × 2.0 m long** | Accommodates a typical 400–500 kg crossbred cow (Holstein-Friesian × Sahiwal). |
| 4 | **Floor slope: 1 in 60 (≈ 2%) toward drainage channel** | Ensures urine and wash water drain away; keeps cows dry; prevents mastitis. |
| 5 | **Roof slope: ≥ 30° (with 0.5 m overhang)** | Sheds heavy Kerala monsoon rain (3000+ mm/year) and protects walls. |
| 6 | **Ridge ventilator: continuous, 300–450 mm throat width** | Removes hot, humid air (essential in Kerala's tropical climate); prevents respiratory diseases. |
| 7 | **Manger height: 400–500 mm; width 600 mm** | Ergonomic feeding; reduces feed wastage. |
| 8 | **Door dimensions: 2.5 m wide × 2.5 m high (minimum)** | Allows free cow movement and easy cleaning with tractor-mounted equipment. |

**Concluding statement (1 mark):**
A well-designed Kerala dairy shed integrates these functional features with the **monsoon-resilient structural design** (truss roof, RCC columns, isolated footings) to provide a **productive, hygienic, and durable** environment for the herd.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) A reinforced concrete grain godown has the following data: Plan size = 8 m × 16 m. Height of walls = 4.5 m. 230 mm brick walls with 12 mm plaster on both sides. RCC roof slab 120 mm thick. The godown stores paddy up to 3.0 m height (bulk density 6 kN/m³). Compute: (i) Total Dead Load on the roof slab per m², (ii) Total Live Load on the roof slab per m², (iii) Factored Load for limit state design as per IS 875 Part 5.** **[7 marks]**

**(b) Explain the design considerations for a residential farmhouse in Kerala. List at least six design criteria, covering site selection, foundation, plinth, walls, roof, ventilation, and flood protection.** **[7 marks]**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (a) — Load Calculation**

**Given:**
- Plan: 8 m × 16 m; Wall height = 4.5 m
- Wall: 230 mm brick + 12 mm plaster (both sides)
- Roof slab: 120 mm thick RCC
- Stored paddy height: 3.0 m; bulk density = 6 kN/m³

**Step 1: Dead Load on the roof slab per m² (IS 875 Part 1).**

(i) Self-weight of RCC slab:

$$DL_{slab} = \gamma_c \times t = 25 \times 0.120 = 3.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

(ii) Floor finish (1 mark; assume 40 mm average cement mortar):

$$DL_{finish} = 0.04 \times 20 = 0.8 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

(iii) Total Dead Load on slab:

$$DL_{total} = 3.0 + 0.8 = 3.8 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Live Load on the roof slab.**

For an RCC roof slab (not accessible except for maintenance), **LL = 1.5 kN/m²** as per IS 875 Part 2.

(If the question meant the **floor load** due to paddy storage, see below.)

**Live load on the floor due to paddy storage (for completeness, 1 mark):**

$$LL_{paddy} = \rho_{paddy} \times h_{stack} = 6 \times 3.0 = 18 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Factored Load (Limit State, IS 875 Part 5).**

For a storage structure, the most critical gravity combination is:

$$\text{Factored Load} = 1.5 (DL + LL)$$

For the roof slab:

$$W_u = 1.5 \times (3.8 + 1.5) = 1.5 \times 5.3 = 7.95 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

For the floor (storage combination):

$$W_{u,floor} = 1.5 \times (DL_{floor} + LL_{paddy}) = 1.5 \times (0 + 18) = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

(Treating the floor DL as negligible compared to the paddy LL; in practice, include 2.5 kN/m² finish.)

**Step 4: Total Factored Load on the whole godown roof.**

$$W_{u,total} = 7.95 \times (8 \times 16) = 7.95 \times 128 = 1017.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Note for the student:** If the godown has a **truss roof** (more common in Kerala for spans > 6 m), the load is applied as **point loads at truss nodes** rather than UDL on a slab. The structural designer must convert the UDL to node loads using the **tributary area method**.

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (b) — Design of a Residential Farmhouse in Kerala**

A **farmhouse in Kerala** must address the unique challenges of **heavy monsoon, high humidity, cyclonic winds, occasional seismic activity, and isolated location** (no municipal services). The following **six criteria** cover the design holistically (each 1 mark):

| # | Design Criterion | Specification & Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Site Selection** | Choose **elevated, well-drained ground** with a slope of 1–2% away from the building. Avoid low-lying paddy fields (Kuttanad-type areas) due to recurring floods. The site should have **road access for a tractor or small truck** (farm inputs/outputs). |
| 2 | **Foundation** | Use **isolated RCC footings** for column loads and **strip footing** for load-bearing walls. Depth: ≥ 1.0 m below GL (below zone of seasonal moisture variation). In coastal/clayey soils, provide **anti-termite treatment** as per **IS 6313** before backfilling. |
| 3 | **Plinth** | Raise the plinth **minimum 450 mm** above the **highest recorded flood level** (HFL) of the locality. In flood-prone areas (e.g., Kuttanad), go for **stilts** with ground floor used as parking/storage. Provide a **DPC (Damp-Proof Course)** of 50 mm thick PCC (1:2:4) with waterproofing admixture. |
| 4 | **Walls** | **230 mm brick wall** (or 200 mm solid concrete block) with **12 mm cement plaster** on both sides. For Kerala's hot-humid climate, **external walls may be 200 mm hollow block** with a 25 mm air gap and inner 100 mm brick for thermal insulation. Apply **waterproof paint or lime wash** on external plaster. |
| 5 | **Roof** | **Sloped roof (≥ 30°)** using **Mangalore tiles, AC sheets, or GI sheets** on **trussed framework** (steel or timber). Provide **600 mm eave overhang** to protect walls from rain. A **false ceiling** with **50 mm glass-wool insulation** is recommended to reduce heat gain (typical Kerala summer attic temperature can reach 55 °C). |
| 6 | **Ventilation & Daylighting** | Provide **cross-ventilation** by placing **windows on opposite walls** (area ≥ 1/6 of floor area as per NBC). Use **casement windows with mosquito nets** and **louvered ventilators near the ceiling**. A **central courtyard (nadumuttam)** is a traditional Kerala feature that provides light, ventilation, and a pleasant microclimate. |

**Additional considerations (1 mark):**
- **Sanitation:** Septic tank + soak pit (sizing as per IS 2470).
- **Electrical:** Use **weatherproof fittings** for outdoor areas; provide a **lightning arrestor** for the farmhouse.
- **Fire safety:** Provide a **dry-powder fire extinguisher (4 kg ABC type)** in the kitchen.
- **Cost economy:** Locally available materials (laterite blocks, coconut timber, Mangalore tiles) reduce cost by 20–30% compared to all-RCC construction.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING & PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Exam Pitfalls — How students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing functional vs structural design (–2 marks):** Many students answer the "design of a dairy shed" by writing only about roof truss sizes, completely ignoring stall dimensions, ventilation, and drainage. The examiner allocates **3–4 marks specifically for functional design**, and these are forfeited.
>
> 2. **Wrong load combination (–2 marks):** Students apply only **1.5(DL + LL)** and forget **wind/seismic combinations** (1.2(DL+LL±WL) or 0.9 DL ± 1.5 WL). For Kerala structures, this is a **guaranteed deduction**.
>
> 3. **Unit errors in wind pressure (–1 mark):** $p_z$ in IS 875 comes out in **N/m²**, not kN/m². Students forget to divide by 1000 and report 1280 kN/m² instead of 1.28 kN/m².
>
> 4. **Wrong lever arm for overturning moment (–1 mark):** For **triangular wind pressure**, the resultant acts at $2H/3$ from the base, not at $H/2$ (uniform) or $H$ (top). Examiners deduct a full mark.
>
> 5. **Missing kern check (–1 mark):** When computing bearing pressure, students must first verify $e \leq B/6$ (no uplift). Skipping this step loses a mark.
>
> 6. **Omitting IS code references (–0.5 mark per item):** Always write *"as per IS 875 Part 3"* or *"as per IS 456:2000"*. Examiners reward code-aware answers.
>
> 7. **Sketch-less answers (–1 mark):** Always include a **neat labeled sketch** for any "design of ___" question. A figure with a few clear labels is worth 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Revise this list on the morning of the KTU exam.**

### Core Concepts
- **Farm infrastructure** includes storage, processing, animal housing, and residential buildings on a farm.
- **Two-track design:** Functional (architecture, ergonomics, workflow) **AND** Structural (load, analysis, member design, stability). Both must be integrated.
- **Stability of a farm structure** is checked against **three failure modes**: overturning, sliding, and uplift. The minimum **factor of safety is 1.5** in each case.

### Design Codes to Memorize
- **IS 875 (Parts 1–5)** — Loads (dead, live, wind, snow, load combinations).
- **IS 456:2000** — RCC design.
- **IS 800:2007** — Steel design.
- **IS 1893 (Part 1):2016** — Earthquake design.
- **IS 6313** — Anti-termite treatment.
- **NBC 2016** — National Building Code (general standards).
- **IS 8888** — Farm buildings (specific to farm structures).

### Key Formulas (must be on your fingertip)
- Wind pressure: $p_z = 0.6 V_z^2$ in N/m².
- Base shear: $V_B = A_h \cdot W$.
- FOS overturning: $M_R / M_O \geq 1.5$.
- FOS sliding: $\mu W / H \geq 1.5$.
- Bearing pressure: $q = W/A \pm Mc/I$ (check $e \leq B/6$).
- Slab self-weight: $w = 25 \times t$ (RCC), $20 \times t$ (brick).
- Bulk storage load: $LL = \rho_{bulk} \times h_{stack}$.

### Functional Design Quick Numbers
- **Dairy stall:** 1.2 m × 2.0 m; floor slope 1 in 60; roof slope ≥ 30°.
- **Poultry:** 0.3–0.4 m² per bird; ridge ventilator ≥ 1/3 roof.
- **Piggery:** 0.9–1.2 m² per fattening pig.
- **Storage godown:** 0.5 m² per tonne of paddy; stack ≤ 3 m high.
- **Window-to-floor ratio:** ≥ 1/6 (NBC 2016).
- **Plinth height:** ≥ 450 mm above FGL; 600–900 mm in flood areas.

### Load Values to Memorize
- **RCC:** 25 kN/m³; **Brick masonry:** 20 kN/m³; **Steel:** 78.5 kN/m³.
- **Live loads:** Residential 2 kN/m²; Office 2.5 kN/m²; Storage up to 15–20 kN/m²; Staircase 3 kN/m²; Roof 1.5 kN/m².
- **Wind:** Kerala Vb = 39–44 m/s; **Seismic:** Zone II (Z=0.10) / Zone III (Z=0.16).

### Stability Design Logic
- **If FOS < 1.5 → increase base width OR increase dead load (ballast) OR add anchorage.**
- **Kern check:** $e \leq B/6$ → no uplift; otherwise, redesign for full uplift-resistant base.
- **Wind lever arm:** Triangular pressure → $2H/3$ from base; Uniform → $H/2$ from base.

### Residential Farmhouse (Kerala) Special Requirements
- **Monsoon-resilient sloped roof** (≥ 30°).
- **Cross-ventilation** with central courtyard (nadumuttam).
- **Stilt or raised plinth** for flood protection.
- **Anti-termite treatment** (mandatory in Kerala).
- **Septic tank + soak pit** (off-municipal sewer).
- **Local materials** (laterite, coconut timber, Mangalore tiles) for cost economy.

### Common Exam Mistakes to AVOID
- ❌ Using SI units inconsistently (kN vs N).
- ❌ Forgetting to convert $p_z$ from N/m² to kN/m².
- ❌ Mixing service load and factored load.
- ❌ Omitting IS code references.
- ❌ Forgetting the **uplift** load combination (0.9 DL ± 1.5 WL).
- ❌ Writing only structural design when the question asks for "functional design".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Farm Offices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Farm Offices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Farm Office** is a dedicated, semi-permanent or permanent structure within a farmstead layout that serves as the administrative, managerial, record-keeping, and communication nerve centre of an agricultural enterprise. It is engineered to consolidate all paper-based and digital documentation related to crop planning, livestock records, financial ledgers, labour payrolls, input-output inventories, and statutory compliance under one protected roof.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAGT501)**, the term *Farm Office* is positioned within the broader framework of **farmstead planning**, where it functions as one of the primary functional zones — alongside the farmhouse, livestock buildings, storage godowns, machinery sheds, and processing units. The office is engineered in accordance with:

- Functional zoning principles of farmstead layout.
- Ergonomic standards for administrative work.
- Climatic design guidelines (passive ventilation, daylighting, thermal comfort).
- Cost-optimal construction using locally available materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):**
> A farm office is **NOT** the same as the farmhouse. The farmhouse is a residential dwelling; the farm office is a **workspace** for the farm manager, owner, accountant, or extension officer. Confusing these two is a common KTU valuation trap.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **cockpit of an aircraft**. Pilots do not fly the plane from the passenger cabin — they fly from a small, highly organized, instrument-rich cabin where every gauge, lever, and chart is within arm's reach. A **farm office plays exactly this role** for the farm.

- The farm = the aircraft (vast, complex, multi-system).
- The farmer / farm manager = the pilot.
- The farm office = the cockpit (compact, organized, full of records, plans, accounts, and communication tools).

Without a cockpit, the pilot cannot manage the aircraft safely. Without a farm office, the modern commercial farmer cannot manage the multi-lakh-rupee enterprise efficiently.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of a Farm Office

A farm office is engineered to perform the following functions:

1. **Administrative Work** — record keeping, correspondence, telephone communication.
2. **Financial Management** — maintaining accounts, bills, vouchers, payroll, loan documents.
3. **Planning & Decision Making** — preparing cropping plans, livestock schedules, budgets.
4. **Inventory Control** — tracking seed, fertilizer, pesticide, feed, and produce stocks.
5. **Supervision Hub** — directing labour, recording attendance, issuing work orders.
6. **Documentation** — legal records, land deeds, insurance papers, government subsidy files.
7. **Communication Centre** — liaison with extension officers, buyers, suppliers, and banks.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Design Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values for Farm Office Design (General Agricultural Engineering Practice):**

| Parameter | Recommended Standard |
|---|---|
| Floor area (small farm) | **9 m² to 12 m²** |
| Floor area (medium farm) | **15 m² to 25 m²** |
| Floor area (large commercial farm / estate) | **30 m² to 50 m²** |
| Ceiling height | **3.0 m to 3.5 m** |
| Window-to-floor ratio | **1 : 5 to 1 : 6** |
| Door width (single) | **0.9 m to 1.0 m** |
| Door width (double) | **1.5 m to 1.8 m** |
| Floor space per desk occupant | **3.5 m² to 5.0 m²** |
| Plinth height | **0.45 m to 0.6 m** above ground level |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Farm Offices

Farm offices can be classified along multiple axes. KTU examiners frequently test classification questions.

### A. Based on Permanence
- **Permanent Office** — constructed with brick, stone, RCC, or concrete; lifetime ≥ 30 years.
- **Semi-Permanent Office** — constructed with asbestos sheet, GI sheet, or timber framing; lifetime 10–20 years.
- **Temporary Office** — thatched, bamboo, or mud-plastered; lifetime ≤ 5 years.

### B. Based on Function
- **Type 1 — Pure Administrative Office** — only record keeping and accounts.
- **Type 2 — Combined Office + Store** — includes a small input/output store attached.
- **Type 3 — Estate Manager's Office** — for large plantations; may include a conference room.
- **Type 4 — Integrated Farm Office + Laboratory** — for research farms or polytechnic demonstration farms.

### C. Based on Construction Material
- **Masonry Office** — load-bearing brick or stone walls.
- **RCC Framed Office** — column-beam-slab construction.
- **Steel Prefab Office** — for rapid deployment on newly acquired land.
- **Timber/Bamboo Office** — for hilly or tribal regions.

## 2.2 Site Selection Criteria for a Farm Office

The location of the farm office within the farmstead is a **high-yield KTU topic**. The following criteria govern placement:

1. **Centrality** — should be centrally located for easy access from all farm zones.
2. **Elevation** — placed on slightly elevated, well-drained ground to avoid waterlogging.
3. **Distance from Livestock** — minimum **30 m to 50 m** downwind from animal sheds to avoid odour and fly nuisance.
4. **Distance from Farmhouse** — typically **20 m to 30 m** from the residential dwelling for privacy yet accessibility.
5. **Approach Road** — must have a motorable approach for visitors, officers, and vehicles.
6. **View** — should command a visual overview of the main farm entrance and the principal activity zone.
7. **Sun Orientation** — long axis oriented **East-West** in tropical Kerala climate to minimize solar heat gain on long walls.
8. **Noise Buffer** — kept away from threshing floors, machinery sheds, and processing units.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** The ideal farm office location is *central, elevated, downwind of livestock, near the farmhouse, and overlooking the main gate.*

## 2.3 Functional Components / Rooms of a Farm Office

A well-designed farm office typically contains the following zones:

| Room / Zone | Function | Approximate Area |
|---|---|---|
| **Main Office Room** | Daily desk work, record keeping | 10–15 m² |
| **Accounts / Cash Room** | Secure storage of money, vouchers | 4–6 m² |
| **Record / File Room** | Long-term document storage | 5–8 m² |
| **Waiting / Reception Area** | Visitors, meetings | 4–6 m² |
| **Store-cum-Office** | Combined input store + desk | 8–12 m² |
| **Toilet** (optional) | Sanitation | 1.5–2 m² |

## 2.4 Design Considerations

### 2.4.1 Structural Design
- Foundation depth in Kerala lateritic / alluvial soil: typically **0.9 m to 1.2 m**.
- Wall thickness (load-bearing brick): **200 mm (single brick)** or **300 mm (1½ brick)**.
- Roof: **RCC slab**, **AC sheet on steel truss**, or **tile roof on timber truss**.

### 2.4.2 Ventilation
- **Cross ventilation** through opposite windows is mandatory in tropical Kerala climate.
- Window area should be **15 % to 20 %** of floor area.
- Ventilation coefficient (cross-sectional opening / floor area) target: **≥ 1/4**.

### 2.4.3 Lighting
- Daylight factor for office interiors: **≥ 2 %**.
- Artificial lighting: **200 lux to 300 lux** at desk level (fluorescent tube preferred).
- Window sill height: **0.75 m to 0.9 m** above floor.
- Window head height: **2.1 m to 2.4 m** above floor.

### 2.4.4 Ergonomics
- Desk height: **0.72 m to 0.75 m**.
- Chair seat height: **0.42 m to 0.45 m**.
- Aisle width between desks: **0.75 m minimum**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Sheet — Farm Office Design & Cost Estimation**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{floor} = N \times A_{per\,person}$ | Total floor area = number of occupants × area per person | m² |
| 2 | $A_{per\,person} = 3.5$ to $5.0$ | Standard space per desk occupant | m²/person |
| 3 | $A_{window} = 0.15 \times A_{floor}$ to $0.20 \times A_{floor}$ | Window area for daylight + ventilation | m² |
| 4 | $WFR = \dfrac{A_{window}}{A_{floor}}$ | Window-to-Floor Ratio (recommended 1:5 to 1:6) | — |
| 5 | $V_{room} = A_{floor} \times H$ | Room volume (for ventilation rate) | m³ |
| 6 | $ACR = \dfrac{A_{opening}}{A_{floor}}$ | Air Change Ratio (target ≥ 1/4) | — |
| 7 | $DF = \dfrac{E_{interior}}{E_{exterior}} \times 100$ | Daylight Factor (target ≥ 2 %) | % |
| 8 | $C_{total} = C_{materials} + C_{labour} + C_{overhead}$ | Total construction cost | ₹ |
| 9 | $C_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{A_{floor}}$ | Unit cost per m² | ₹ / m² |
| 10 | $V_{plinth} = L \times B \times H_{plinth}$ | Volume of plinth (for excavation / concrete) | m³ |
| 11 | $V_{concrete} = L \times B \times t$ | Concrete volume for foundation/DPC | m³ |
| 12 | $V_{brickwork} = L_{wall} \times t_{wall} \times H_{wall}$ | Brickwork volume (subtract openings) | m³ |
| 13 | $N_{bricks} = V_{brickwork} \times 500$ | Bricks per m³ of brickwork (nominal) | nos |
| 14 | $W_{steel} = V_{RCC} \times \rho_{steel} \times \dfrac{\%\,steel}{100}$ | Steel quantity in RCC | kg |
| 15 | $A_{plaster} = 2 \times H_{wall} \times (L + B)$ | Internal + external plaster area | m² |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mastering farm office design is not academic alone. It directly impacts:

- **Loan Eligibility** — NABARD and commercial banks require a documented farm office before sanctioning large agricultural loans.
- **FPO Compliance** — Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) require a registered office structure.
- **Insurance Claims** — proper documentation supported by an office space simplifies claim processing.
- **Subsidy Release** — central and state agricultural subsidies are released only to applicants with verifiable records.
- **Audit Trail** — Income Tax and GST audits require systematic bookkeeping, feasible only with a dedicated office.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sizing a Farm Office

### Problem Statement
A medium-sized farm employs **3 office staff** (farm manager, accountant, and clerk). The owner wants to design a farm office. The desk-occupant space standard is **4.5 m² per person**. A separate accounts room of **6 m²**, a waiting area of **5 m²**, and a toilet of **1.8 m²** are also required. Compute the total carpet area, the total built-up area (assuming 20 % allowance for walls and circulation), and the recommended window area (using WFR = 1 : 5).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute Main Office Area**

$$A_{main} = N \times A_{per\,person}$$

$$A_{main} = 3 \times 4.5 = 13.5 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Add Ancillary Rooms**

$$A_{total\,carpet} = A_{main} + A_{accounts} + A_{waiting} + A_{toilet}$$

$$A_{total\,carpet} = 13.5 + 6 + 5 + 1.8$$

$$A_{total\,carpet} = 26.3 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply 20 % Allowance for Walls and Circulation**

$$A_{built\,up} = A_{carpet} \times 1.20$$

$$A_{built\,up} = 26.3 \times 1.20$$

$$A_{built\,up} = 31.56 \;\text{m}^2 \approx 31.6 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute Recommended Window Area using WFR = 1 : 5**

$$A_{window} = \dfrac{A_{floor}}{5}$$

Taking floor area ≈ total carpet area = 26.3 m²:

$$A_{window} = \dfrac{26.3}{5} = 5.26 \;\text{m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The farm office should have a total window opening of at least 5.26 m², distributed across multiple windows on opposite walls for cross-ventilation. For instance, four windows of size 1.2 m × 1.1 m each would yield 5.28 m² of opening.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cost Estimation of a Farm Office

### Problem Statement
A farm office is to be constructed on a rectangular plot of **6 m × 5 m** (external dimensions). Plinth height above ground = **0.5 m**. Wall thickness = **0.23 m (single brick)**. Wall height above plinth = **3.0 m**. Roof is RCC slab of **0.12 m** thickness. Doors = 1 no. of 1.0 m × 2.1 m. Windows = 4 nos. of 1.2 m × 1.1 m. Estimate quantities of:
(a) Earthwork excavation for foundation (depth 0.9 m, width 0.6 m).
(b) PCC for foundation (1 : 4 : 8, 0.1 m thick below brickwork).
(c) Brickwork in foundation and superstructure.
(d) RCC roof slab.
(e) Plastering (internal + external).

Assume standard rates and compute the **total estimated cost**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### (a) Earthwork in Excavation

The foundation trench runs along all four external walls (centerline length):

$$L_{centerline} = 2 \times (6 + 5) = 22 \;\text{m}$$

Volume of excavation:

$$V_{excavation} = L_{centerline} \times W_{trench} \times D_{trench}$$

$$V_{excavation} = 22 \times 0.6 \times 0.9 = 11.88 \;\text{m}^3$$

#### (b) PCC (1 : 4 : 8) in Foundation

PCC bed thickness = 0.1 m, width = 0.6 m (same as trench):

$$V_{PCC} = 22 \times 0.6 \times 0.1 = 1.32 \;\text{m}^3$$

#### (c) Brickwork in Foundation and Superstructure

**Foundation brickwork** (from −0.9 m to 0.0 m = 0.9 m height, plus 0.1 m PCC already accounted, plus DPC 0.05 m):

Let foundation height above PCC = 0.75 m (upto GL).

$$V_{foundation\,brick} = 22 \times 0.6 \times 0.75 = 9.9 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Superstructure brickwork** (from plinth top = 0.5 m to roof bottom = 3.0 m above plinth):

Wall thickness = 0.23 m. Centerline length = 22 m.

Gross wall area:

$$A_{wall\,gross} = 22 \times 3.0 = 66 \;\text{m}^2$$

Deductions for openings:
- Doors: $1 \times (1.0 \times 2.1) = 2.1 \;\text{m}^2$
- Windows: $4 \times (1.2 \times 1.1) = 5.28 \;\text{m}^2$
- Total deduction: $2.1 + 5.28 = 7.38 \;\text{m}^2$

Net wall area:

$$A_{wall\,net} = 66 - 7.38 = 58.62 \;\text{m}^2$$

Volume of superstructure brickwork:

$$V_{super\,brick} = 58.62 \times 0.23 = 13.48 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Total brickwork:**

$$V_{brick\,total} = 9.9 + 13.48 = 23.38 \;\text{m}^3$$

Number of bricks (500 bricks per m³):

$$N_{bricks} = 23.38 \times 500 = 11{,}690 \;\text{bricks}$$

#### (d) RCC Roof Slab

Internal plan dimensions:

$$L_{int} = 6 - 2(0.23/2 + 0.115) = 6 - 0.345 = 5.66 \;\text{m}$$

Wait — corrected: Internal dimension = external − wall thickness.

$$L_{int} = 6 - 2(0.23) = 6 - 0.46 = 5.54 \;\text{m}$$

$$B_{int} = 5 - 2(0.23) = 5 - 0.46 = 4.54 \;\text{m}$$

Plus roof projection / bearing on wall = 0.115 m on each side:

$$L_{slab} = 6 - 2(0.23) + 2(0.115) = 5.54 + 0.23 = 5.77 \;\text{m}$$

$$B_{slab} = 5 - 2(0.23) + 2(0.115) = 4.54 + 0.23 = 4.77 \;\text{m}$$

Slab thickness = 0.12 m.

$$V_{RCC\,slab} = 5.77 \times 4.77 \times 0.12 = 3.30 \;\text{m}^3$$

Steel quantity (assume 1 % steel by volume):

$$\rho_{steel} = 7850 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

$$W_{steel} = 3.30 \times 7850 \times 0.01 = 259 \;\text{kg}$$

#### (e) Plastering Area

Internal plaster:

$$A_{internal} = 2 \times H_{wall} \times (L_{int} + B_{int})$$

$$A_{internal} = 2 \times 3.0 \times (5.54 + 4.54) = 2 \times 3.0 \times 10.08 = 60.48 \;\text{m}^2$$

External plaster (on outer face of wall):

$$A_{external} = 2 \times H_{wall} \times (L_{ext} + B_{ext})$$

$$A_{external} = 2 \times 3.0 \times (6 + 5) = 66 \;\text{m}^2$$

Total plaster area:

$$A_{plaster\,total} = 60.48 + 66 = 126.48 \;\text{m}^2$$

#### (f) Total Cost Estimation (using standard rates)

| Sl. No. | Item | Quantity | Unit | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earthwork excavation | 11.88 | m³ | 250 | 2,970 |
| 2 | PCC (1:4:8) | 1.32 | m³ | 4,500 | 5,940 |
| 3 | Brickwork | 23.38 | m³ | 6,000 | 1,40,280 |
| 4 | Bricks (procurement) | 11,690 | nos | 12 | 1,40,280 (incl.) |
| 5 | RCC slab concrete (M20) | 3.30 | m³ | 6,500 | 21,450 |
| 6 | Steel reinforcement | 259 | kg | 70 | 18,130 |
| 7 | Plastering (internal + external) | 126.48 | m² | 250 | 31,620 |
| 8 | Doors (1 no.) | 2.1 | m² | 4,500 | 9,450 |
| 9 | Windows (4 nos.) | 5.28 | m² | 3,800 | 20,064 |
| 10 | Flooring (IPS 50 mm) | 22.78 | m² | 600 | 13,668 |
| 11 | Electrical + misc | LS | — | — | 15,000 |
| | **Subtotal** | | | | **₹ 4,18,852** |
| | Contractor’s overhead @ 10 % | | | | **₹ 41,885** |
| | **Grand Total** | | | | **₹ 4,60,737** |

**Unit Cost per m²:**

$$C_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{A_{carpet}}$$

where $A_{carpet} = 5.54 \times 4.54 = 25.15 \;\text{m}^2$.

$$C_{unit} = \dfrac{4{,}60{,}737}{25.15} \approx ₹\,18{,}318 \;\text{per m}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This unit cost (₹ 18,000 – ₹ 19,000 per m²) is a representative benchmark for a permanent masonry farm office in Kerala's 2024 market. KTU examiners often ask students to **back-calculate unit cost** after a detailed estimate is provided.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Office Type

| Criterion | Temporary | Semi-Permanent | Permanent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial cost | Very low | Moderate | High |
| Lifetime | ≤ 5 yr | 10–20 yr | ≥ 30 yr |
| Maintenance | High | Moderate | Low |
| Suitability for records | Poor | Fair | Excellent |
| Insurance / loan acceptance | Poor | Fair | Excellent |
| Climate comfort | Poor | Fair | Good |
| Recommended for | Pilot farms | Small farms | Commercial farms |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Farm Office

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    B --> C[FARM OFFICE COMPLEX]

    C3 --> E3[VOUCHER FILE]

    H --> H3[WATER SUPPLY]

```

## 4.2 Conceptual Farmstead Layout Showing Office Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[FARMHOUSE] --- P2[FARM OFFICE]
    P2 --- P3[LIVESTOCK SHED]

    P5 --- P6[WORKSHOP]
    P2 --- P7[STORE GODOWN]

    P1 --- P10[MAIN GATE]
    P10 --- P2
    P2 --- P11[APPROACH ROAD]
    P11 --- P8
    P3 --- P12[VETERINARY ROOM]
    P12 --- P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Farm Office** (P2) sits at the geometric and functional centre of the farmstead, connected to the farmhouse, livestock shed, machinery shed, and store godown by short paved paths. The main gate connects directly to the office so that visiting officers reach the office **without crossing livestock or machinery zones**.

## 4.3 Internal Floor Plan Schematic


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Define a farm office. List any four functions it performs on a commercial farm.**

**Model Answer:**

A farm office is a structure within a farmstead designed and used for carrying out administrative, managerial, accounting, and record-keeping activities of the farm. It serves as the central information hub of the farm enterprise.

**Four functions:**
1. **Record keeping** of cropping plans, livestock data, and input-output inventory.
2. **Financial management** including accounts maintenance, payroll, and bills.
3. **Communication centre** for correspondence with banks, extension officers, suppliers, and buyers.
4. **Documentation and compliance** storage of legal papers, insurance policies, and subsidy files.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for four functions at 0.5 each)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**State any six design considerations for the location of a farm office in a farmstead.**

**Model Answer:**

Six design considerations for siting a farm office:
1. **Central location** for easy accessibility from all farm zones.
2. **Slight elevation** of site to prevent waterlogging.
3. **Distance from livestock sheds** (30–50 m) and located **downwind** to avoid odour and flies.
4. **Near the farmhouse** (20–30 m) yet providing privacy.
5. **Motorable approach road** connecting to the main gate.
6. **East-West orientation** of long axis in tropical climate to reduce solar heat gain.

*(3 marks — 0.5 mark per consideration)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **functional components / rooms** of a well-designed farm office for a medium-sized commercial farm. Provide typical dimensions and area requirements for each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **construction materials and structural design** considerations for a permanent farm office in Kerala's lateritic soil. Include foundation depth, wall specifications, and roof options. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A well-designed farm office for a medium commercial farm contains the following functional components:

| Sl. No. | Component | Function | Typical Dimensions | Area |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Main Office Room | Daily desk work, planning | 5 m × 3 m | 15 m² |
| 2 | Accounts / Cash Room | Secure financial storage | 4 m × 1.5 m | 6 m² |
| 3 | Record / File Room | Long-term documents | 2.5 m × 2 m | 5 m² |
| 4 | Reception / Waiting | Visitors, meetings | 3 m × 1.5 m | 4.5 m² |
| 5 | Store-cum-Office | Small input stock | 4 m × 2.5 m | 10 m² |
| 6 | Toilet | Sanitation | 1.5 m × 1.2 m | 1.8 m² |

**Total Carpet Area ≈ 42.3 m²**
**Built-up Area (with 20 % allowance) ≈ 50.8 m²**

**Design rationale:**
- The **Main Office Room** is the largest, sized for 3 occupants at 5 m² per person.
- The **Accounts Room** is isolated and lockable for security of cash and vouchers.
- The **Record Room** uses compact steel almirahs to maximize vertical storage.
- The **Reception** provides the first point of contact for outside visitors.
- The **Store** doubles as a small input-stock room for seeds, agrochemicals.
- The **Toilet** is mandatory for staff welfare and hygiene compliance.

*(7 marks — 1 for listing components, 2 for dimensions, 2 for rationale, 2 for layout logic)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Construction Materials and Structural Design for Kerala Lateritic Soil:**

**1. Site Preparation:**
- Clear vegetation and topsoil up to **0.15 m** depth.
- Mark the building footprint with reference to the central axis of the farm.

**2. Foundation:**
- **Type:** Isolated / strip footing (depending on wall load).
- **Depth:** **0.9 m to 1.2 m** below ground level (laterite is stable but soft rock begins deeper).
- **Width:** **0.6 m** for single-storey office.
- **PCC bed:** 1 : 4 : 8 mix, **0.1 m** thick, **0.6 m wide**, as a levelling course.
- **First-class brick footing** in steps up to 0.45 m below GL.

**3. Plinth:**
- Plinth height: **0.45 m to 0.6 m** above surrounding ground level.
- DPC (Damp Proof Course): **50 mm thick PCC (1:2:4)** with waterproofing compound, laid at plinth level.
- Filling the plinth with compacted laterite murrum.

**4. Superstructure Walls:**
- **Load-bearing brick walls**, **230 mm (single brick)** thick, in 1:6 cement mortar.
- Height above plinth: **3.0 m**.
- **Lintel:** RCC lintel of **150 mm depth** with 2 nos. 10 mm tor bars over all openings.

**5. Roof Options (Choose one):**
- **Option A — RCC slab:** 110–120 mm thick, M20 concrete, with 1 % steel (Fe415).
- **Option B — AC sheet on steel trusses:** Lightweight, economical, but poor insulation.
- **Option C — Mangalore tile on timber rafters:** Traditional Kerala aesthetic, moderate cost, good thermal comfort.

**6. Flooring:**
- **IPS (Indian Patent Stone)** 40–50 mm thick over 100 mm PCC base.
- Or **vitrified tiles** for a more formal office look.

**7. Doors and Windows:**
- **Main door:** Teak wood, 1.0 m × 2.1 m, with panic bar for fire safety.
- **Windows:** Teak / hard wood frames with glazed shutters, 1.2 m × 1.1 m, four numbers for cross-ventilation.

**8. Finishes:**
- **Internal plaster:** 12 mm thick, 1:6 cement mortar, finished smooth with lime putty.
- **External plaster:** 15 mm thick, 1:5 cement mortar, with waterproofing paint.

*(7 marks — 1 for site prep, 2 for foundation, 1.5 for plinth, 1 for walls, 1.5 for roof)*

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **ventilation, lighting, and ergonomic** requirements for a farm office in tropical Kerala climate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A farm office of internal dimensions **5 m × 4 m** and ceiling height **3.2 m** has two windows of size **1.5 m × 1.2 m** each and one door of **1.0 m × 2.1 m**. Compute the **window-to-floor ratio**, **air change ratio**, and **room volume**. Comment on whether the office meets the recommended standards. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Ventilation, Lighting, and Ergonomic Requirements:**

**1. Ventilation:**
- **Type:** Cross-ventilation through opposite wall windows is mandatory.
- **Window area:** Should be **15 % to 20 %** of floor area.
- **Air Change Ratio (ACR):** Cross-sectional opening area / floor area should be **≥ 0.25 (1/4)**.
- **Ventilators:** High-level louvered ventilators (0.6 m × 0.3 m) above windows aid stack ventilation.
- **Ceiling fan:** 1.2 m sweep, mounted at 2.7 m height, for forced circulation.

**2. Lighting:**
- **Daylighting:** Window-to-floor ratio (WFR) = **1 : 5 to 1 : 6** recommended.
- **Daylight Factor (DF):** ≥ **2 %** measured at desk level.
- **Artificial lighting:** Fluorescent tube lights (40 W, 1200 mm) providing **200–300 lux** at desk level.
- **Wall and ceiling colours:** Light shades (white, ivory) to maximize reflectance.
- **Glare control:** Use light-coloured curtains or venetian blinds on south and west windows.

**3. Ergonomics:**
- **Desk height:** **0.72–0.75 m**.
- **Chair seat height:** **0.42–0.45 m**.
- **Aisle width:** **0.75 m minimum** between desks.
- **Drawer clearance:** **0.6 m** under desk for knee room.
- **File cabinet height:** **1.4 m maximum** for top-drawer access without strain.
- **Computer monitor top:** At eye level, **0.7 m** above floor.

*(7 marks — 2 for ventilation, 2 for lighting, 3 for ergonomics)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Internal dimensions: $L = 5\;\text{m}$, $B = 4\;\text{m}$, $H = 3.2\;\text{m}$
- Two windows: $1.5\;\text{m} \times 1.2\;\text{m}$
- One door: $1.0\;\text{m} \times 2.1\;\text{m}$

**Step 1: Floor Area**

$$A_{floor} = L \times B = 5 \times 4 = 20 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Total Window Area**

$$A_{window} = 2 \times (1.5 \times 1.2) = 2 \times 1.8 = 3.6 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 3: Total Opening Area (windows + door)**

$$A_{opening} = 3.6 + (1.0 \times 2.1) = 3.6 + 2.1 = 5.7 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4: Window-to-Floor Ratio (WFR)**

$$WFR = \dfrac{A_{window}}{A_{floor}} = \dfrac{3.6}{20} = 0.18 = 1:5.56$$

Recommended range: **1 : 5 to 1 : 6**.

$$\text{Status: } \boxed{\text{WFR = 1 : 5.56 — SATISFACTORY (within range)}}$$

**Step 5: Air Change Ratio (ACR)**

$$ACR = \dfrac{A_{opening}}{A_{floor}} = \dfrac{5.7}{20} = 0.285$$

Recommended: ≥ 0.25.

$$\text{Status: } \boxed{\text{ACR = 0.285 — SATISFACTORY (above minimum)}}$$

**Step 6: Room Volume**

$$V_{room} = L \times B \times H = 5 \times 4 \times 3.2 = 64 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Comment:**
The office satisfies both the WFR (1 : 5.56 lies between 1 : 5 and 1 : 6) and the ACR (0.285 > 0.25). The room volume of 64 m³ provides adequate air per occupant (assuming 3 occupants, this is ~21.3 m³ per person, above the recommended 15 m³ per person for office work). Hence, the design is **acceptable** for tropical Kerala climate.

*(7 marks — 1 for floor area, 1 for window area, 1 for WFR + comment, 1 for ACR + comment, 1 for volume, 2 for overall comment)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Farm Office Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing farmhouse with farm office** — these are **different structures**. Marks are deducted if a 14-mark answer drifts into residential design.
> 2. **Forgetting WFR deduction for doors** — the standard WFR uses *window* area only, but ACR uses *total opening* area (windows + doors). Mixing these is a classic error.
> 3. **Ignoring soil context** — Kerala's lateritic / alluvial soils demand foundation depth of 0.9 m minimum. Writing "0.5 m foundation" loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping cost overhead** — final cost estimate without 10 % contractor's overhead and contingencies is considered incomplete.
> 5. **Improper unit usage** — confusing m² and m³ in brickwork / plastering calculations. Always state units explicitly.
> 6. **Not specifying orientation** — answers about office location that omit East-West orientation in tropical Kerala lose marks.
> 7. **Missing daylight factor** — answering only about artificial lighting and not mentioning **DF ≥ 2 %** is a common KTU penalty trigger.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Farm Offices:**
>
> - A **farm office** is the administrative hub of a farm, distinct from the residential **farmhouse**.
> - Standard floor area ranges: **9 m² (small)** → **25 m² (medium)** → **50 m² (large estate)**.
> - **Per-desk-occupant** space: **3.5–5.0 m²**.
> - Optimal location: **central, elevated, downwind of livestock, near the farmhouse, near the main gate**.
> - **East-West** long-axis orientation in tropical Kerala to minimize solar heat gain.
> - Components: main room, accounts room, record room, reception, store, toilet.
> - **WFR** recommended **1 : 5 to 1 : 6**; **ACR** ≥ **0.25**; **DF** ≥ **2 %**.
> - **Ceiling height:** 3.0–3.5 m; **Plinth height:** 0.45–0.6 m.
> - Ventilation via **cross-ventilation** (opposite windows) is mandatory.
> - **Lighting:** 200–300 lux at desk; daylight preferred, fluorescent tube as supplement.
> - **Ergonomics:** desk 0.72 m; chair 0.42 m; aisle ≥ 0.75 m.
> - **Construction materials** in Kerala: laterite blocks / first-class bricks in 1:6 mortar; RCC roof or AC sheet on truss.
> - **Foundation depth** in lateritic soil: **0.9 m to 1.2 m**.
> - **Cost formula:** $C_{total} = C_{materials} + C_{labour} + C_{overhead}$, plus 10 % contractor's profit.
> - **Unit cost** benchmark for permanent farm office in Kerala: **₹ 18,000 – ₹ 22,000 per m²** (2024 estimates).
> - **Brick quantity:** ≈ **500 bricks per m³** of brickwork.
> - **Steel quantity in RCC:** ≈ **1 %** of RCC volume × density of steel (7850 kg/m³).
> - Functions performed: record keeping, financial management, planning, inventory, supervision, documentation, communication.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyhouses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polyhouses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Polyhouse** is a protective agricultural structure, framed in galvanised iron (GI) pipes or equivalent structural members, and covered primarily with a transparent **polyethylene (PE) film** — usually of thickness **200 micron (0.20 mm)** — designed to cultivate horticultural crops under partially or fully controlled microclimatic conditions. It is functionally a low-cost variant of a greenhouse, optimised for the tropics and sub-tropics of India.

According to the **National Horticulture Board (NHB)** and the **Ministry of Agriculture, Government of India**, polyhouses are officially classified as **protected cultivation structures** under the **MIDH (Mission for Integrated Development of Horticulture)** subsidy scheme, with a standard unit sizing of **1000 m² (0.1 ha)** and a benchmark subsidy reference cost of approximately **₹ 935 per m²** for low-cost naturally ventilated tubular structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 3 – Polyhouses):** The examiner expects students to be able to **(a)** classify polyhouses by structure, shape, and covering, **(b)** explain the role of each structural and covering component, **(c)** design a polyhouse for given climatic and crop conditions, and **(d)** prepare a preliminary cost estimate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Transparent Raincoat" Idea

Think of a polyhouse as a **giant transparent raincoat** draped over a metal skeleton and stretched over your field.

- On a cold, windy, or rainy day, you wear a raincoat to keep yourself warm and dry. The raincoat lets light in (so you can see) but blocks cold wind and rain.
- A polyhouse does the same for plants: it lets **sunlight in** (so photosynthesis can occur) but blocks **rain, wind, pests, and excess heat loss** at night.
- Unlike a glass house, the "raincoat" is made of **polyethylene film** — a plastic sheet — making it far cheaper to build but with a shorter life (typically **3–5 years** for the film before UV degradation requires re-covering).

So, in plain English: **A polyhouse is a transparent plastic-covered shelter that grows plants in a controlled environment.**

## 1.3 Key Performance & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (India) |
|---|---|
| Standard size (NHB unit) | **1000 m²** |
| Ridge height | **6.0 – 6.5 m** |
| Gutter/eave height | **3.0 – 4.0 m** |
| Structural span | **8.0 m × 4 bays of 4.0 m** |
| Covering film thickness | **200 micron (µm)** |
| UV-stabilised film life | **3 – 5 years** |
| Light transmission (PE film) | **85 – 90 %** |
| Design wind speed (IS 875) | **44 m/s (Zone V coastal) to 33 m/s (Zone III plains)** |
| Design temperature range | **0 °C to 45 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Whenever a numerical design problem is posed, the examiner will give you the crop (e.g., capsicum, tomato, gerbera), location (e.g., Wayanad, Palakkad), and size. You must apply the **ridge height, gutter height, and film thickness** standards above. Memorising the **200 micron** and **6.0 m ridge** values will fetch easy marks in Part A.

## 1.4 What a Polyhouse is NOT

- It is **not** an open field — it is enclosed.
- It is **not** a glass house — the covering is plastic film, not rigid glass panels.
- It is **not** a shade net house — shade nets only **reduce** light (typically 50–75 % shade), whereas polyhouse films **transmit and diffuse** light (85–90 % PAR transmission).
- It is **not** fully climate-controlled like a **phytotron** — most polyhouses in Kerala are **naturally ventilated**, relying on side roll-up vents and roof vents for cooling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Polyhouses (High-Yield for KTU)

Polyhouses can be classified on **three different bases**, and the KTU examiner loves to test all three. You must be ready to draw/describe each.

### A. Based on Environmental Control
1. **Naturally Ventilated Polyhouse** – No fans; cooling is by **side and roof vent roll-ups**. Most common in India. Cost: low.
2. **Fan-and-Pad Cooled Polyhouse** – Uses **evaporative cooling pads** on one end and **exhaust fans** on the opposite end. Maintains temperature 5–8 °C below ambient. Cost: high.
3. **Environmentally Controlled Polyhouse** – Fully automated control of temperature, humidity, CO₂, and light. Used for research and high-value cut flowers. Cost: very high.

### B. Based on Structural Shape
1. **Even-span / A-shaped (Gable)** – Two equal slopes meeting at a central ridge. Most common.
2. **Uneven-span** – Two unequal slopes; used on hilly terrain (relevant to Wayanad/Idukki).
3. **Quonset / Gothic-arch** – Curved or arched roof made of bent GI pipes; better wind resistance.
4. **Sawtooth / Multi-span** – Multiple connected spans with vertical vent openings on alternate roof slopes.

### C. Based on Covering Material
1. **Polyethylene (PE) film** – 200 µm, UV-stabilised; life 3–5 years.
2. **Double-layer inflated PE** – Two layers separated by air cushion; gives better insulation.
3. **Polycarbonate / Poly-soft film** – Premium; longer life (10+ years).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Trick:** "Classify polyhouses" can be asked in **Part A (3 marks)**. Do not give only one basis — always mention **shape, control, and covering** to grab the full 3 marks.

## 2.2 Structural Components — The Skeleton of a Polyhouse

A polyhouse has six functional components. Visualise them as **bones, skin, lungs, and thermostat** of a human body.

| Component | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Foundation** | Concrete (1:2:4) | Transfers structural load to ground; typical size 0.6 m × 0.6 m × 0.9 m |
| **Structural frame** | GI pipes (Class B, 25 mm / 42 mm / 60 mm dia) | Carries wind, crop, and dead loads |
| **Covering (cladding)** | 200 µm UV-stabilised PE film | Transmits PAR, blocks rain, traps IR |
| **Ventilation system** | Roll-up side vents (motorised) + roof vents | Removes heat and humidity |
| **Shading / thermal net** | 50 % green shade net (interior retractable) | Reduces peak summer temperature by 4–6 °C |
| **Irrigation & fertigation** | Drip system with venturi/fertiliser tank | Precision water and nutrient delivery |

## 2.3 Design Parameters — The "KTU 5-Point Rule"

When the examiner gives you a polyhouse design problem, always check and specify these **5 parameters**:

1. **Site orientation** – Ridge should run **East-West** so the long slope faces **South** (in Northern hemisphere this gives maximum winter sun; in Kerala, it minimises peak summer heat gain).
2. **Span and length** – Standard 8 m × 40 m = 320 m², repeated to 1000 m².
3. **Gutter and ridge height** – 4.0 m gutter, 6.0 m ridge (gives **30° roof slope**).
4. **Vent area** – Minimum **15–20 % of floor area** for natural ventilation.
5. **Cladding** – Single 200 µm PE for low cost; double-layer inflated for temperature-sensitive crops.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **must-know** for any numerical problem on polyhouse design or cost estimation.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Floor area of polyhouse | $A_f = L \times W$ | m² | L = length, W = width |
| 2 | Volume of polyhouse (A-type) | $V = L \times W \times \left(\dfrac{h_g + h_r}{2}\right)$ | m³ | Average of gutter and ridge height |
| 3 | Roof slope angle | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h_r - h_g}{W/2}\right)$ | degrees | Typical 25°–30° |
| 4 | Vent area ratio | $R_v = \dfrac{A_{vent}}{A_f}$ | dimensionless | Required 0.15 – 0.20 |
| 5 | Air changes per hour (ACH) | $ACH = \dfrac{Q_v}{V}$ | h⁻¹ | Q_v = ventilation flow m³/h |
| 6 | Sensible heat gain (cooling load) | $Q_s = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | U = overall heat transfer coeff. (W/m²·K) |
| 7 | Overall U-value (single PE film) | $U \approx 6.5$ | W/m²·K | For 200 µm clear PE |
| 8 | Cooling pad area (fan-pad) | $A_{pad} = \dfrac{Q_s}{h_{pad} \cdot \Delta h}$ | m² | h_pad = pad effectiveness × enthalpy difference |
| 9 | Cost per unit area | $C = \dfrac{\text{Total cost}}{A_f}$ | ₹/m² | For comparative cost estimation |
| 10 | Drip line spacing | $s = \dfrac{A_f}{n_{lines} \times L}$ | m | Typical 1.0 – 1.2 m for vegetables |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table formatting safeguard:** In the formulas above, any absolute-value-style notation has been written using \vert for clarity. The examiner will not deduct marks for minor LaTeX variations.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Horticulture & Floriculture** – Off-season cultivation of capsicum, tomato, cucumber, gerbera, rose (Wayanad, Idukki districts of Kerala).
- **Nursery Raising** – Clonal multiplication of black pepper, cardamom, tea.
- **Hi-tech Farming Hub** – Used by **ICAR-IIHR Bengaluru**, **KAU Thrissur**, and **KFRI Peechi** for research.
- **Export-oriented cultivation** – Polyhouse-grown coloured capsicum fetches ₹ 80–150/kg in retail.
- **Disaster-resilient farming** – Useful in Kerala's monsoon season when open-field vegetable production is hit by *Phytophthora* and heavy rain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Roof Slope Angle of an Even-Span Polyhouse

**Problem Statement:** A polyhouse has ridge height $h_r = 6.0$ m, gutter height $h_g = 4.0$ m, and width $W = 8.0$ m. Determine the roof slope angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
The roof forms a right triangle with:
- **Vertical leg (rise):** $\Delta h = h_r - h_g$
- **Horizontal leg (run):** half the width of the polyhouse, i.e., $W/2$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$
\Delta h = h_r - h_g = 6.0 - 4.0 = 2.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\text{Run} = \frac{W}{2} = \frac{8.0}{2} = 4.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the slope formula.**

$$
\tan \theta = \frac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}} = \frac{\Delta h}{W/2}
$$

$$
\tan \theta = \frac{2.0}{4.0} = 0.500
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the angle.**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.500)
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 26.57^{\circ}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A roof slope of ~27° is the **optimal range (25°–30°)** for Indian polyhouses. It ensures:
- Adequate **rain runoff** during the Kerala monsoon.
- Sufficient **solar gain** in winter.
- A **self-cleaning effect** — dust slides off when washed by rain.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volume of an Even-Span Polyhouse

**Problem Statement:** For a polyhouse of length $L = 40$ m, width $W = 8$ m, gutter height $h_g = 4$ m, and ridge height $h_r = 6$ m, compute the enclosed volume $V$.

**Step 1 — Understand the shape.**
The cross-section is a **pentagon**: a rectangle of height $h_g$ topped by a triangle of height $(h_r - h_g)$ and base $W$.

**Step 2 — Compute the cross-sectional area.**

$$
A_{cs} = (W \times h_g) + \left( \frac{1}{2} \times W \times (h_r - h_g) \right)
$$

$$
A_{cs} = (8 \times 4) + \left( \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 2 \right)
$$

$$
A_{cs} = 32 + 8 = 40 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Alternative: average-height method.**

$$
A_{cs} = W \times \left( \frac{h_g + h_r}{2} \right) = 8 \times \frac{4 + 6}{2} = 8 \times 5 = 40 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by length to get volume.**

$$
V = A_{cs} \times L = 40 \times 40 = 1600 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V = 1600 \text{ m}^3}
$$

**Use of this value:** This volume is the basis for sizing **fans, foggers, and CO₂ generators** in a fan-pad cooled polyhouse.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Side Vent Area for Natural Ventilation

**Problem Statement:** For the polyhouse above, design the side vent area such that the **vent area ratio is at least 18 %** of floor area.

**Step 1 — Compute the floor area.**

$$
A_f = L \times W = 40 \times 8 = 320 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the vent area ratio formula.**

$$
A_{vent} = R_v \times A_f
$$

$$
A_{vent} = 0.18 \times 320 = 57.6 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Distribute between two long sides.**

$$
A_{vent, each side} = \frac{A_{vent}}{2} = \frac{57.6}{2} = 28.8 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the roll-up vent height.**

If the side vent runs the **entire length of 40 m**, the **height of the roll-up** is:

$$
h_{vent} = \frac{28.8}{40} = 0.72 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{vent} = 57.6 \text{ m}^2, \; h_{vent} \approx 0.72 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, designers use a **2.0 m roll-up side vent** (much higher than the 0.72 m theoretical minimum) to account for **wind direction variability and insect-screen blockage** (which reduces effective area by ~40 %).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sensible Heat Gain and Cooling Load

**Problem Statement:** Compute the peak **sensible heat gain (cooling load)** through the roof and side walls of the polyhouse when the **outside temperature is 38 °C** and the **desired inside temperature is 28 °C**. Use overall $U = 6.5$ W/m²·K.

**Step 1 — Determine total cladding area.**
Assume the cladding area $A \approx 1.4 \times A_f$ (typical ratio for a gable polyhouse).

$$
A = 1.4 \times 320 = 448 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute temperature difference.**

$$
\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in} = 38 - 28 = 10 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the heat transfer formula.**

$$
Q_s = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
Q_s = 6.5 \times 448 \times 10
$$

$$
Q_s = 29120 \text{ W} = 29.12 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_s \approx 29.1 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Engineering Implication:** A 30 kW cooling load requires **roughly 4–5 exhaust fans of 1 HP each** (each fan ≈ 6 kW equivalent cooling capacity at 0.5 Pa static pressure).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Preliminary Cost Estimation per m²

**Problem Statement:** For a 1000 m² naturally ventilated polyhouse, the following cost items are estimated. Compute the **total cost and unit cost (₹/m²)**.

| Item | Cost (₹) |
|---|---|
| GI pipe structure (40 % of total) | 4,00,000 |
| 200 µm PE film covering | 1,20,000 |
| Drip + fertigation system | 1,50,000 |
| Shade net + insect net | 50,000 |
| Civil foundation + flooring | 80,000 |
| Labour + erection (15 %) | 1,00,000 |
| Miscellaneous (10 %) | 1,00,000 |

**Step 1 — Sum the costs.**

$$
C_{total} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{total} = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \; (= ₹ 10 \text{ lakh})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute unit cost.**

$$
C = \frac{C_{total}}{A_f} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = 1000 \text{ ₹/m}^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **₹ 1000/m²** benchmark is in line with the **NHB 2023–24 subsidy reference cost of ₹ 935/m²** for a low-cost polyhouse. KTU students should be ready to compare their computed cost with this standard.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Polyhouse

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the functional interaction between all components of a polyhouse system. This is the kind of schematic examiners expect students to draw in Part B 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A5 --> A6[Ventilation Subsystem]
    A5 --> A7[Shading and Thermal Screen]
    A5 --> A8[Drip Fertigation Subsystem]
    A5 --> A9[Crop Production Module]
    A6 --> A10[Temperature and Humidity Sensor]
    A7 --> A11[Light Intensity Sensor]
    A8 --> A12[Soil Moisture Sensor]
    A10 --> A13[Climate Controller]

    A12 --> A13
    A13 --> A6
    A13 --> A7
    A13 --> A8
    A9 --> A14[Yield Output in kg per m square]
```


## 4.3 Design Decision Flowchart (Functional Architecture)

