## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
>
> **Define silage. List any four advantages of silage making over hay making.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Silage** is the fermented, high-moisture fodder (typically **60–70% moisture**) that is preserved by **anaerobic lactic acid fermentation** of green forage crops in an airtight storage structure called a **silo**.

**Four Advantages of Silage over Hay:**

1. **Independence from weather** — Silage making is not dependent on bright sunshine, unlike hay drying. It can be done even during the monsoon season.
2. **Lower nutrient losses** — Fermentation losses are typically **5–10%** vs. **20–30%** for hay due to leaf shattering and bleaching.
3. **Higher palatability and digestibility** — Lactic acid fermentation improves the taste and retains more protein and energy.
4. **Year-round feeding** — Silage provides green fodder throughout the year, including lean/dry seasons, ensuring **balanced nutrition** for high-yielding dairy animals.

---

> **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
>
> **What is the function of a tower silo? State the materials used for its construction.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Tower Silo** is a vertical, cylindrical structure used for the storage and preservation of **silage** through anaerobic fermentation. Its primary functions are:
> 1. To provide an **airtight, vertical storage environment** for fermented fodder.
> 2. To enable **mechanized filling from the top** and **gravity-assisted unloading from the bottom**.

**Construction Materials:**

- **Walls:** Reinforced cement concrete (RCC), brick masonry with plaster, or prefabricated **stave silo** (wooden staves with steel bands).
- **Floor:** RCC slab with slope (1:60) towards central drainage sump.
- **Roof:** RCC dome, conical steel sheet, or FRP (Fiberglass Reinforced Plastic) for waterproofing and weather protection.
- **Foundation:** RCC footing or raft foundation (depending on soil bearing capacity).
- **Door:** Airtight wooden or metal **Dutch door** at multiple levels for top unloading.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]`
>
> **A dairy farm has 150 crossbred cows. Design a cylindrical tower silo for the storage of silage for 180 days. The daily silage requirement is 25 kg/cow. Bulk density of silage is 650 kg/m³. The silo height should not exceed 12 m. Assume storage efficiency as 85%.**
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **total silage quantity** required and the **required volume** of the silo. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Determine the **diameter of the silo** and compute the **lateral pressure on the wall** at the base using **Janssen's equation**. Given: $\mu = 0.4$, $K = 0.5$, and the silo is filled to a depth of 12 m. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Total Silage Quantity and Required Volume (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Given data.
- $N = 150$ cows
- $q_d = 25 \text{ kg/cow/day}$
- $T = 180 \text{ days}$
- $\rho_b = 650 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $h_{max} = 12 \text{ m}$
- $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 2:** Total silage weight.

$$Q_{total} = N \times q_d \times T = 150 \times 25 \times 180$$

$$Q_{total} = 675{,}000 \text{ kg} = 675 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3:** Required silage volume.

$$V_{req} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{\rho_b} = \dfrac{675{,}000}{650} = 1038.46 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Accounting for storage efficiency.

$$V_{design} = \dfrac{V_{req}}{\eta} = \dfrac{1038.46}{0.85} = 1221.72 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Result:** **V_design ≈ 1222 m³** (1 mark each step; full marks for clarity and units)

---

**(b) Silo Diameter and Lateral Pressure (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Geometric relation for a cylinder.

$$V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times h$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 V_{design}}{\pi h}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 1221.72}{\pi \times 12}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4886.88}{37.70}} = \sqrt{129.62}$$

$$D = 11.39 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Cross-check — consider practical alternative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A diameter of 11.4 m is large. **Engineering Recommendation:** Use **two silos of 8 m diameter × 12 m height**, each with $V = \pi \times 4^2 \times 12 = 603 \text{ m}^3$, total = **1206 m³ ≈ 1222 m³** ✓

**Step 3:** Apply Janssen's equation for lateral pressure.

Hydraulic radius: $R = D/4 = 8/4 = 2.0 \text{ m}$ (using alternative design).

Exponent: $\mu K z / R = (0.4)(0.5)(12)/2.0 = 1.2$

$e^{-1.2} = 0.3012$

$$L = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\mu} \left( 1 - e^{-\mu K z / R} \right)$$

With $\gamma = 650 \times 9.81 / 1000 = 6.38 \text{ kN/m}^3$:

$$L = \dfrac{6.38 \times 2.0}{0.4} \times (1 - 0.3012) = \dfrac{12.76}{0.4} \times 0.6988$$

$$L = 31.9 \times 0.6988 = 22.29 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Vertical pressure for reference.

$$\sigma_v = L / K = 22.29 / 0.5 = 44.58 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> **Final Results:**
> - **Diameter (single silo): D = 11.39 m** (or **2 silos of 8 m diameter**)
> - **Lateral pressure at base: L ≈ 22.3 kN/m²**
> - **Vertical pressure at base: σ_v ≈ 44.6 kN/m²**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
>
> **A 200-cow dairy farm needs a bunker silo. Design the bunker dimensions and the reinforced concrete wall thickness. Given:** Daily silage requirement = **30 kg/cow**, Storage period = **120 days**, Silage density = **600 kg/m³**, Bunker width = **5 m**, Maximum silage height = **3 m**, Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.45$, Lateral pressure ratio $K = 0.5$, Allowable concrete stress = **5000 kN/m²**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **length of the bunker** and the **total silage weight**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Calculate the **lateral pressure on the bunker wall** and the **required wall thickness**. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Bunker Length and Silage Weight (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Total silage weight.

$$Q_{total} = 200 \times 30 \times 120 = 720{,}000 \text{ kg} = 720 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2:** Required volume.

$$V_{req} = \dfrac{720{,}000}{600} = 1200 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Bunker volume = $L \times W \times H = L \times 5 \times 3 = 15L$.

$$L = \dfrac{V_{req}}{15} = \dfrac{1200}{15} = 80 \text{ m}$$

> **Engineering Recommendation:** 80 m is long. Better: **2 bunkers of 5 m × 3 m × 40 m** (parallel bays).

---

**(b) Lateral Pressure and Wall Thickness (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** For a bunker, Janssen's equation applies with hydraulic radius $R = W/2$ (for wide bunkers) or $R = WH / (2W + 2H)$.

$R = (5 \times 3) / (2(5+3)) = 15/16 = 0.9375 \text{ m}$ (for the bunker).

**Step 2:** Lateral pressure at base ($z = 3$ m):

$\mu K z / R = (0.45)(0.5)(3) / 0.9375 = 0.72$

$e^{-0.72} = 0.4868$

$$L = \dfrac{6.0 \times 0.9375}{0.45} \times (1 - 0.4868)$$

(Note: $\gamma = 600 \times 9.81 / 1000 = 5.89 \text{ kN/m}^3$)

$$L = \dfrac{5.89 \times 0.9375}{0.45} \times 0.5132 = 12.27 \times 0.5132 = 6.30 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Maximum bending moment (assuming the wall is a vertical cantilever fixed at base with triangular pressure distribution):

$$P_{max} = 6.30 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{P_{max} \cdot h^2}{6} = \dfrac{6.30 \times 3^2}{6} = 9.45 \text{ kN·m per meter run}$$

**Step 4:** Wall thickness calculation. For a 1 m strip:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot 6}{b \cdot d^2} \leq \sigma_{allow}$$

$$5000 = \dfrac{9.45 \times 6}{1.0 \times d^2}$$

$$d^2 = \dfrac{9.45 \times 6}{5000} = 0.01134$$

$$d = 0.1065 \text{ m} = 106.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Add cover (25 mm) and use **effective depth** logic; redesign with $d_{eff} = 125$ mm:

$$M_{capacity} = \dfrac{5000 \times 1.0 \times 0.125^2}{6} = 13.02 \text{ kN·m} > 9.45 \text{ kN·m} \checkmark$$

> **Final Results:**
> - **Lateral pressure: L ≈ 6.3 kN/m²**
> - **Required wall thickness: ~125 mm RCC** (with 12 mm main bars @ 150 mm c/c, 8 mm distribution bars @ 200 mm c/c)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing $\text{kg/m}^3$ with $\text{kN/m}^3$ in the Janssen equation. **Always convert density to $\text{kN/m}^3$** by multiplying by $9.81/1000$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Storage Efficiency (–1 mark):** Real silos have **10–15% wastage** (top spoilage, settling losses). Multiply required volume by $1/\eta$.
>
> 3. **Using Rankine's formula instead of Janssen's (–3 marks):** For **tall silos** ($h/D > 2$), Janssen's equation is mandatory because the material does not behave as a simple granular mass.
>
> 4. **No sketch/diagram (–1 mark):** Always include a **labeled cross-section** showing dimensions, materials, and pressure distribution.
>
> 5. **Ignoring surcharge load (–2 marks):** If the top is filled by tractor/loader, add a **surcharge load of 10–15 kN/m²** to the top.
>
> 6. **Wrong hydraulic radius for non-circular sections (–2 marks):** For rectangular bunkers, $R = \dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{WH}{2(W+H)}$, NOT $W/2$.
>
> 7. **Not specifying construction material (–1 mark):** Always state **"RCC M25 grade"**, **"Fe415 steel"**, or equivalent, as per IS 456:2000.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Exam Ready**

- ☐ **Silage** is fermented green fodder preserved by **anaerobic lactic acid fermentation** at **60–70% moisture**.
- ☐ **Bulk density** of silage is **500–700 kg/m³**; of hay is **60–80 kg/m³**; of grain is **720–780 kg/m³**.
- ☐ **Daily fodder requirement** = **2.5–3.0% of body weight** (dry matter basis).
- ☐ **Storage period** = **90 to 365 days** (typically 180 days for Indian conditions).
- ☐ **Janssen's equation** is used for **lateral pressure in deep silos** — not Rankine's formula.
- ☐ **Hydraulic radius** $R = A/P$; for cylinder, $R = D/4$; for rectangle, $R = WH / [2(W+H)]$.
- ☐ **K value** for silage ≈ **0.4–0.6**; **μ** (wall friction) ≈ **0.3–0.5**.
- ☐ **Tower silo** is for **large farms** (>200 cows); **bunker** for **medium farms** (50–200); **trench** for **small farms** (<50).
- ☐ **Floor slope** of bunker = **1:60** towards drainage sump.
- ☐ **Wall thickness** of bunker = typically **100–150 mm RCC** with proper reinforcement.
- ☐ **Hay barn** must have **cross-ventilation** (ridge + eave vents) to maintain moisture <15%.
- ☐ **Feed godown** floor should be **elevated 60 cm above ground** to prevent dampness and rodent entry.
- ☐ Always include **storage efficiency** factor ($\eta = 0.85$ to $0.95$) in volume calculations.
- ☐ Always **convert density to kN/m³** before using in Janssen's equation.
- ☐ **IS Codes to cite in exam:** IS 456 (RCC design), IS 875 (Loads), IS 1893 (Earthquake), IS 15475 (Silos — design and construction).
- ☐ **Design flow:** Livestock data → Total quantity → Volume → Dimensions → Loads → Structural design → Cost estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Design of Feed/Fodder Storage Structures**

topics.title: Design of Grain Storage Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Grain Storage Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grain Storage Structures** are engineered enclosures specifically designed to preserve harvested agricultural commodities (cereals, pulses, oilseeds) against biotic (insects, rodents, fungi) and abiotic (moisture, temperature, sunlight) deterioration over extended storage periods, while maintaining the physiological, biochemical, and physical integrity of the grain bulk.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a **storage structure** is classified by the **depth-to-diameter (or characteristic width) ratio** as follows:

- **Shallow Bins / Bunkers:** Depth/Diameter ratio < **1.5** (governed by Airy's wedge theory)
- **Deep Bins / Silos:** Depth/Diameter ratio ≥ **1.5** (governed by Janssen's equation)
- **Very Deep Bins:** Depth/Diameter ratio > **3.0** (special provisions for switch pressure, eccentric discharge)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Indian Standards Governing Grain Storage Design:**
> - **IS 9172:1979** – Criteria for design of steel bins for granular materials
> - **IS 6065:1971** – Code of practice for construction of floor silos
> - **IS 14444:1997** – Code of practice for storage of food grains
> - **FSSAI Standards** – For food-grade storage (moisture < 14%)
> - **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** – Bulk grain handling

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

**The "Water Tower vs. Sand Hourglass" Analogy:**

Imagine pouring fine dry sand into a tall glass tube versus a wide bowl:

1. **Wide bowl (shallow bin):** The sand piles up with a natural angle of repose (~30° for wheat). The horizontal pressure on the bowl walls is minimal because the sand behaves like a "solid heap" — governed by **Airy's wedge failure theory**.

2. **Tall narrow tube (deep silo):** Initially, the sand weight pushes downward, BUT after a certain depth, the grains "lock up" against the walls. Vertical pressure on the bottom *decreases* relative to weight of overburden because **friction at the walls** transfers much of the load sideways. This is the **Janssen arching effect (1895)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world intuition:** When you stand in a deep grain silo, the pressure on your feet is LESS than the weight of grain above you — because the walls carry most of the load through friction. This is why Janssen's equation is critical for tall silos.

**Why does this matter for Kerala's agricultural economy?**
- Kerala's primary crops: **paddy, coconut, pepper, cardamom, rice, pulses**
- Average post-harvest losses in India: **6–10%** (₹92,651 crore annually as per ICAR-CIPHET 2022 estimates)
- A correctly designed silo can reduce this to **<2%** through moisture control and aeration

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Wheat) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk density (loose) | γ | **7.5 – 8.5** | kN/m³ |
| Bulk density (compacted) | γ | **8.5 – 9.5** | kN/m³ |
| Angle of internal friction | φ | **25° – 30°** | degrees |
| Angle of wall friction | δ | **0.5φ to 0.75φ** | degrees |
| Lateral pressure ratio (active) | K | **0.4 – 0.6** | dimensionless |
| Angle of repose | α | **25° – 35°** | degrees |
| Coefficient of friction (steel wall) | μ | **0.30 – 0.40** | dimensionless |
| Coefficient of friction (concrete wall) | μ | **0.40 – 0.50** | dimensionless |
| Safe moisture content for storage | M | **≤ 13%** | % (wet basis) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **hydraulic radius (R)** is defined as the **cross-sectional area divided by the perimeter** — it is the characteristic length governing Janssen's arching.
> - Circular bin: $R = D/4$
> - Square bin: $R = a/4$
> - Rectangular bin: $R = (ab)/(2(a+b))$

---

### 1.4 Classification of Grain Storage Structures

```
CLASSIFICATION OF GRAIN STORAGE STRUCTURES
│
├── 1. TRADITIONAL (Low Capacity < 5 tonnes)
│   ├── Underground pits (Pittas, Kumbham, Kacheri)
│   ├── Mud bins / Bamboo structures
│   ├── Gunny bag stacks in godowns
│   └── Kattu (Kerala-specific palm leaf structures)
│
├── 2. SEMI-MODERN (5 – 100 tonnes)
│   ├── Pusa bins (metal)
│   ├── NCCB bins (Nagaoka Chemical Co.)
│   ├── Ferro-cement bins
│   └── Bagged storage godowns (CAP / FCI)
│
└── 3. MODERN (100 – 10,000+ tonnes)
    ├── Steel silos (bolted / welded)
    ├── RCC / Prestressed concrete silos
    ├── Bag silos (PVC membrane)
    └── Bunkers (horizontal storage)
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Physical Behaviour of Granular Materials

Grain is a **cohesive-frictional particulate medium**. Unlike liquids, grains:

1. **Distribute load through arching** (inter-particle friction)
2. **Exhibit switch pressure** during discharge (pressure spikes)
3. **Have time-dependent settlement** (consolidation under own weight)
4. **Are temperature and moisture sensitive** (respiration generates heat)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two distinct failure states** govern the lateral pressure on bin walls:
> - **Static (filling) state:** Pressures are lower, grain at rest
> - **Dynamic (discharge) state:** Pressures can be **1.4 – 2.0 times** static due to flow patterns and arch collapse

---

### 2.2 Janssen's Equation — Deep Bin Theory (1895)

#### 2.2.1 Assumptions of Janssen's Theory

1. Grain is treated as a **cohesionless** Coulomb material
2. **Vertical pressure (p_v)** is uniform across any horizontal section
3. **Lateral pressure ratio (K)** is constant with depth
4. **Wall friction coefficient (μ)** is constant
5. The bin is **vertical, prismatic, and centrally loaded**
6. Steady-state flow condition (after initial filling)

#### 2.2.2 Derivation Logic (Step-wise)

Consider an **infinitesimal horizontal slice** of grain of thickness `dy` at depth `y` from the top surface:

```
    ┌─────────────────────┐
    │     γ (bulk density) │
    │   ↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓    │  ← Weight of slice
    │ ┌─────────────────┐ │
    │ │                 │ │
    │ │  p_v (top)      │ │  ← Vertical pressure on top face
    │ │                 │ │
    │ │  p_v + dp_v     │ │  ← Vertical pressure on bottom face
    │ │  (bottom)       │ │
    │ │                 │ │
    │ │← μ·K·p_v · U·dy→│ │  ← Wall friction
    │ └─────────────────┘ │
    │         ↑           │
    │   p_h = K·p_v       │  ← Lateral pressure on walls
    └─────────────────────┘
```

**Equilibrium of vertical forces on the slice:**

$$\text{Weight of slice} = \text{Net vertical pressure increase} + \text{Wall friction force}$$

$$(\gamma \cdot A \cdot dy) = A \cdot dp_v + (\mu \cdot K \cdot p_v \cdot U \cdot dy)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dp_v}{dy} = \gamma - \left(\frac{\mu K U}{A}\right) p_v$$

Define the **Janssen characteristic parameter:**

$$\lambda = \frac{\mu K U}{A} = \frac{\mu K}{R}$$

where $R = A/U$ is the **hydraulic radius**.

The differential equation becomes:

$$\frac{dp_v}{dy} = \gamma - \lambda \cdot p_v$$

**Solution** (with boundary condition $p_v = 0$ at $y = 0$):

$$p_v(y) = \frac{\gamma}{\lambda}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda y}\right)$$

**Lateral pressure on wall:**

$$p_h(y) = K \cdot p_v(y) = \frac{K \gamma}{\lambda}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda y}\right) = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda y}\right)$$

**Vertical pressure at the bottom of the bin (y = H):**

$$p_v(H) = \frac{\gamma}{\lambda}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda H}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical observation:** As $y \to \infty$, $p_v \to \gamma/\lambda = \gamma R / (\mu K)$ — a **finite asymptote**! This is the **arching effect**. Beyond a depth of about $3R/(\mu K)$, the vertical pressure becomes essentially constant.

---

### 2.3 Airy's Wedge Theory — Shallow Bins (Bunkers)

#### 2.3.1 Applicable When:

- Depth/width ratio < **1.5**
- The grain **fails in active state** following a plane of rupture
- The wedge slides down the wall surface

#### 2.3.2 Governing Equations

**Lateral pressure on vertical wall (Rankine active state):**

$$p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot h$$

where $K_a$ is the **active earth pressure coefficient:**

$$K_a = \tan^2\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$

**For inclined walls with wall friction (Coulomb's wedge):**

$$K_a = \frac{\cos^2(\phi - \alpha)}{\cos^2\alpha \cdot \cos(\alpha + \delta)\left[1 + \sqrt{\frac{\sin(\phi+\delta)\sin(\phi-\beta)}{\cos(\alpha+\delta)\cos(\alpha-\beta)}}\right]^2}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = wall inclination from vertical
- $\delta$ = wall friction angle
- $\beta$ = slope of top surface of grain
- $\phi$ = angle of internal friction

**Total lateral force on a wall of height H:**

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2$$

**Point of application:** At **H/3** from the base (triangular distribution).

---

### 2.4 Comparison: Janssen vs. Airy

| Feature | Janssen (Deep Bin) | Airy (Shallow Bin) |
|---|---|---|
| Governing mechanism | Arching + wall friction | Active wedge failure |
| Pressure distribution | Non-linear, asymptotic | Linear (hydrostatic) |
| Lateral pressure equation | $p_h = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu}(1-e^{-\lambda y})$ | $p_h = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot h$ |
| Vertical pressure at base | $\frac{\gamma R}{\mu K}(1-e^{-\lambda H})$ | $\gamma H$ (full overburden) |
| Switch pressure effect | Significant | Negligible |
| IS Code | IS 9172, ACI 313 | IS 14444, IS 6065 |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these 6 equations for the KTU ESE — they cover 90% of grain silo design problems.**

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \frac{A}{U}$ | Hydraulic radius | m |
| 2 | $\lambda = \frac{\mu K U}{A} = \frac{\mu K}{R}$ | Janssen's parameter | m⁻¹ |
| 3 | $p_v = \frac{\gamma}{\lambda}(1-e^{-\lambda y})$ | Vertical pressure at depth y | kN/m² |
| 4 | $p_h = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu}(1-e^{-\lambda y})$ | Lateral pressure on wall | kN/m² |
| 5 | $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$ | Active earth pressure coeff. | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_a = \frac{1}{2}K_a \gamma H^2$ | Total lateral force on wall | kN/m |
| 7 | $W = \gamma V$ | Total weight of stored grain | kN |
| 8 | $T = \mu p_h \cdot H$ | Total wall friction | kN/m |
| 9 | $\sigma_b = \frac{W}{A_f} + \frac{M}{Z}$ | Base bearing stress | kN/m² |
| 10 | $t_{min} = \frac{p_h D}{2 \sigma_{allow}}$ | Cylindrical shell thickness | m |

---

### 2.6 Design Loads on Grain Storage Structures

A complete design must account for:

1. **Dead Load (DL):** Self-weight of structure + grain weight
2. **Live Load (LL):** Personnel, equipment, bagged materials
3. **Lateral Pressure (LP):** From Janssen or Airy theory
4. **Wind Load (WL):** IS 875 Part 3
5. **Seismic Load (EL):** IS 1893 (Zone III for Kerala)
6. **Thermal Load (TL):** Due to sun exposure (ΔT = 30–40°C)
7. **Switch Pressure (SP):** Dynamic discharge — **multiplier of 1.5–2.0** on lateral
8. **Eccentric Discharge Load (ED):** Asymmetric flow patterns
9. **Aeration Equipment Load:** Fans, ducts, blowers

**Load Combination (per IS 800:2007 / IS 456:2000):**

$$1.5(DL + LP) + 1.5(SP) + 1.2(LL) + 0.6(WL/EL)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Switch pressure** is the most critical design consideration in deep silos. IS 9172 recommends a **dynamic pressure factor of 1.75 × static Janssen pressure** for symmetric discharge and **2.0 × static** for eccentric discharge.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Derivation: Janssen's Equation for a Circular Silo

**Given:** A circular steel silo of diameter $D = 6$ m, height $H = 12$ m, storing wheat.

**Material Properties:**
- Bulk density $\gamma = 8.0$ kN/m³
- Internal friction angle $\phi = 28°$
- Wall friction angle $\delta = 22°$
- Lateral pressure ratio $K = 0.5$

**Step 1: Compute geometric properties**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (6)^2}{4} = 28.27 \text{ m}^2$$

$$U = \pi D = \pi (6) = 18.85 \text{ m}$$

$$R = \frac{A}{U} = \frac{28.27}{18.85} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute Janssen's parameter $\lambda$**

The wall friction coefficient from wall friction angle:

$$\mu = \tan \delta = \tan(22°) = 0.404$$

$$\lambda = \frac{\mu K U}{A} = \frac{\mu K}{R} = \frac{(0.404)(0.5)}{1.5} = 0.1347 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Vertical pressure distribution $p_v(y)$**

$$p_v(y) = \frac{\gamma}{\lambda}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda y}\right) = \frac{8.0}{0.1347}\left(1 - e^{-0.1347 y}\right)$$

$$p_v(y) = 59.39\left(1 - e^{-0.1347 y}\right) \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Lateral pressure distribution $p_h(y)$**

$$p_h(y) = K \cdot p_v(y) = (0.5)(59.39)\left(1 - e^{-0.1347 y}\right)$$

$$p_h(y) = 29.69\left(1 - e^{-0.1347 y}\right) \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Values at key depths**

| Depth y (m) | $e^{-0.1347y}$ | $p_v$ (kN/m²) | $p_h$ (kN/m²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.0000 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 0.7644 | 14.00 | 7.00 |
| 4 | 0.5843 | 24.69 | 12.35 |
| 6 | 0.4467 | 32.86 | 16.43 |
| 8 | 0.3415 | 39.10 | 19.55 |
| 10 | 0.2611 | 43.88 | 21.94 |
| 12 | 0.1996 | 47.54 | 23.77 |
| 15 | 0.1329 | 51.50 | 25.75 |
| $\infty$ | 0 | 59.39 | 29.70 |

**Step 6: Total lateral force on wall**

The lateral force per unit perimeter at depth y is $p_h(y)$. For total force per unit perimeter:

$$P = \int_0^H p_h(y) \, dy = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu}\left[H + \frac{e^{-\lambda H} - 1}{\lambda}\right]$$

Substituting:

$$P = \frac{8.0 \times 1.5}{0.404}\left[12 + \frac{e^{-1.616} - 1}{0.1347}\right]$$

$$P = 29.70\left[12 + \frac{0.1996 - 1}{0.1347}\right]$$

$$P = 29.70\left[12 + (-5.94)\right]$$

$$P = 29.70 \times 6.06 = 180.0 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 7: Wall friction check (vertical equilibrium)**

Total grain weight:

$$W = \gamma \cdot A \cdot H = 8.0 \times 28.27 \times 12 = 2714 \text{ kN}$$

Vertical pressure at base (from Janssen): $p_v(12) = 47.54$ kN/m²

Total vertical force on floor: $V_{floor} = p_v \cdot A = 47.54 \times 28.27 = 1344$ kN

Force carried by walls through friction:

$$F_{wall} = W - V_{floor} = 2714 - 1344 = 1370 \text{ kN}$$

Percentage carried by walls:

$$\frac{1370}{2714} \times 100\% = 50.5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** In a deep silo, approximately **50% of the grain weight is carried by the walls through friction** — this is the arching effect quantified.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Airy's Theory for a Shallow Bunker

**Given:** A shallow bunker of width $B = 8$ m, depth $H = 4$ m, with vertical walls, storing paddy.

**Material:** $\gamma = 6.5$ kN/m³, $\phi = 30°$, $\delta = 20°$, $K_a$ for vertical wall with $\beta = 0$:

$$K_a = \frac{\cos^2(30° - 0°)}{\cos^2(0°) \cdot \cos(0° + 20°)\left[1 + \sqrt{\frac{\sin(30°+20°)\sin(30°-0°)}{\cos(0°+20°)\cos(0°-0°)}}\right]^2}$$

Numerator: $\cos^2(30°) = 0.7500$

Denominator step 1: $\cos(20°) = 0.9397$

Denominator step 2: 
$$\frac{\sin(50°)\sin(30°)}{\cos(20°)\cos(0°)} = \frac{(0.7660)(0.5000)}{(0.9397)(1.0)} = 0.4076$$

$$\sqrt{0.4076} = 0.6385$$

Denominator step 3: $(1 + 0.6385)^2 = (1.6385)^2 = 2.685$

$$K_a = \frac{0.7500}{(0.9397)(2.685)} = \frac{0.7500}{2.523} = 0.297$$

**Lateral pressure at base:**

$$p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H = 0.297 \times 6.5 \times 4 = 7.72 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Total lateral force on wall (per meter length):**

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.297)(6.5)(4)^2 = 15.45 \text{ kN/m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Application point:** The resultant acts at **H/3 = 1.33 m** from the base.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Cylindrical Shell Thickness

For a steel silo, the **hoop tension** in the cylindrical wall at depth y:

$$T = \frac{p_h \cdot D}{2}$$

The required shell thickness:

$$t_{req} = \frac{T}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{p_h \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{allow}}$$

For our example, with maximum $p_h = 23.77$ kN/m² and $\sigma_{allow} = 165$ MPa (steel):

$$T = \frac{23.77 \times 6}{2} = 71.31 \text{ kN/m}$$

$$t_{req} = \frac{71.31 \times 10^3}{165 \times 10^6} = 4.32 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.43 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> With corrosion allowance of **2 mm** and buckling consideration, practical minimum thickness is **6–8 mm** for welded steel silos.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Design Workflow

| Step | Design Action | Reference | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify storage capacity | Client brief | Volume V, tonnes |
| 2 | Select type (bin/silo) | H/D ratio | Geometry |
| 3 | Determine bulk density | IS 9172 Annex A | γ value |
| 4 | Compute hydraulic radius R | Geometry | R (m) |
| 5 | Choose K and μ | Material testing | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Apply Janssen (or Airy) | Theory | p_h(y) profile |
| 7 | Apply switch pressure factor | IS 9172 Cl. 5.3 | p_h dynamic |
| 8 | Compute hoop tension | T = p_h·D/2 | T (kN/m) |
| 9 | Design wall thickness | σ_allow | t (mm) |
| 10 | Design hopper | Jenike's flow | Angle, opening |
| 11 | Design foundation | IS 456 | Footing size |
| 12 | Design aeration system | FSSAI | CFM, duct size |
| 13 | Check seismic | IS 1893 | Lateral force |
