# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **List any six performance evaluation parameters used for comparing domestic storage structures.**

**Model Answer:**

The six key performance evaluation parameters for domestic storage structures are:

1. **Quantitative storage loss** (%) — measured as weight loss over storage period.
2. **Qualitative storage loss** — assessed by germination %, oil content, broken % of grain.
3. **Moisture ingress / humidity control** — ability to maintain grain moisture content.
4. **Insect infestation level** — number of insects per kg of stored grain.
5. **Rodent damage index** — number of rodent holes / droppings per unit area.
6. **Structural durability and service life** — number of years before major repair is needed.
7. **Cost per quintal per year of storage** — economic indicator.
8. **Ease of loading and unloading** — ergonomic / handling score.

*(Any six well-explained parameters fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **Differentiate between line posts and terminal posts in a farm fencing system.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Line Post | Terminal Post |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| **Location** | At regular intervals along the fence line | At corners, ends, gates, and sharp changes in direction |
| **Function** | Supports the wire/mesh between spans | Resists cumulative wire tension from both sides |
| **Load** | Mainly lateral wind load | Lateral + axial tension load (very high) |
| **Section** | Smaller (e.g., 75–100 mm GI pipe) | Larger (e.g., 100–150 mm GI pipe or angle iron) |
| **Footing** | Simple direct embedment | Reinforced concrete footing required |
| **Spacing** | 3 – 5 m | Single per corner/end; strain post every 50 – 100 m |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (a).** [KTU University Exam – Model] **Question A — Choice 1**

**(a) (7 marks)** A coastal Kerala farmer wants to install a barbed wire fence 1.2 m high around a 1-acre coconut plantation. Design the **wind force** and the **required post diameter** for the line posts, clearly stating all assumptions.

**(b) (7 marks)** Discuss the **performance evaluation** of **PUSA bin, ferrocement bin, and metal silo** for storing paddy in Kerala based on **six parameters**. Compute the **Composite Performance Index (CPI)** for each, given the following weights and scores:

| Parameter | Weight | PUSA | Ferrocement | Metal Silo |
|-----------|:------:|:----:|:-----------:|:----------:|
| Storage Loss | 0.20 | 4 | 4 | 5 |
| Moisture Control | 0.15 | 4 | 5 | 5 |
| Insect Resistance | 0.15 | 4 | 4 | 5 |
| Durability | 0.15 | 4 | 5 | 5 |
| Cost-Effectiveness | 0.20 | 3 | 3 | 2 |
| Ergonomics | 0.15 | 4 | 4 | 5 |

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Given:** $V_b$ = 39 m/s (coastal Kerala), $h$ = 1.2 m, $L_{panel}$ = 4 m, $C_d$ = 1.2, $k_1=k_2=k_3=1.0$, $\lambda=1.5$, $\sigma_{b,allow}=165$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Basic wind pressure:**

$$P_d = 0.6 \times (39)^2 = 912.6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Design wind pressure:**

$$P_z = 912.6 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 912.6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Area of one panel:**

$$A = 1.2 \times 4 = 4.8 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Wind force on the panel:**

$$F_w = 912.6 \times 4.8 \times 1.2 = 5256.6 \text{ N} \approx 5.26 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Amplified force:**

$$F_{eff} = 5.26 \times 1.5 = 7.88 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Bending moment at ground level:**

$$M_{max} = 7.88 \times \frac{1.2}{2} = 4.73 \text{ kN·m} = 4730 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 7 — Required section modulus:**

$$Z_{req} = \frac{4730}{165 \times 10^6} = 2.87 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 8 — Required post diameter (solid circular):**

$$d^3 = \frac{32 \times 2.87 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi} = 2.92 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$d = (2.92 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} = 0.0663 \text{ m} = 66.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 9 — Provide standard size:**

$$d_{provided} = 75 \text{ mm (standard GI pipe)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students forget to **multiply by $\lambda$ (impact factor)** in Step 5. This is the most common mistake costing **2 marks**. Also, students often express $P_d$ in kPa instead of Pa — check your units.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — CPI for PUSA Bin:**

$$CPI_{PUSA} = (0.20)(4) + (0.15)(4) + (0.15)(4) + (0.15)(4) + (0.20)(3) + (0.15)(4)$$

$$= 0.80 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.60 = 3.80$$

**Step 2 — CPI for Ferrocement Bin:**

$$CPI_{Fero} = (0.20)(4) + (0.15)(5) + (0.15)(4) + (0.15)(5) + (0.20)(3) + (0.15)(4)$$

$$= 0.80 + 0.75 + 0.60 + 0.75 + 0.60 + 0.60 = 4.10$$

**Step 3 — CPI for Metal Silo:**

$$CPI_{Silo} = (0.20)(5) + (0.15)(5) + (0.15)(5) + (0.15)(5) + (0.20)(2) + (0.15)(5)$$

$$= 1.00 + 0.75 + 0.75 + 0.75 + 0.40 + 0.75 = 4.40$$

**Step 4 — Discussion (3 marks for narrative):**

The **Metal Silo (CPI = 4.40)** has the best technical performance — lowest storage loss, excellent moisture and insect control, and longest durability. However, it scores lowest on cost, making it suitable for **commercial farms and FPOs**. The **Ferrocement Bin (CPI = 4.10)** offers a good balance of durability and cost, making it the **best choice for medium-scale Kerala farmers**. The **PUSA Bin (CPI = 3.80)** is most popular for small farmers due to its lower cost and proven field performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to **state the units** in CPI calculations and **omit the decision logic** in the final paragraph. Both fetch **2 marks** in a 7-mark question. Always end with a **clear ranking statement and recommendation**.

---

> **Q3 (b).** [KTU University Exam – Model] **Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative to Q3a)**

**(a) (7 marks)** Design a **farm fencing system** for a 100 m × 80 m goat farm in inland Kerala. Specify post spacing, post diameter, number of barbed wire strands, footing size for terminal posts, and gate dimensions. Use IS 875 wind loads.

**(b) (7 marks)** Evaluate the **performance** of the following 4 storage structures using the CPI method. Weights are given in brackets. Rank them and recommend the most suitable for a small Kerala farmer (1 hectare, mixed cropping):

- (i) Mud bin [0.20: 2; 0.15: 2; 0.15: 3; 0.10: 2; 0.10: 1; 0.15: 5; 0.05: 2; 0.05: 1; 0.05: 5]
- (ii) PUSA bin [0.20: 4; 0.15: 4; 0.15: 4; 0.10: 4; 0.10: 4; 0.15: 3; 0.05: 4; 0.05: 4; 0.05: 3]
- (iii) Ferrocement bin [0.20: 4; 0.15: 5; 0.15: 4; 0.10: 5; 0.10: 5; 0.15: 3; 0.05: 4; 0.05: 5; 0.05: 3]
- (iv) Metal silo [0.20: 5; 0.15: 5; 0.15: 5; 0.10: 5; 0.10: 5; 0.15: 2; 0.05: 5; 0.05: 5; 0.05: 2]

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Given Data:**
- Plot: 100 m × 80 m
- Location: Inland Kerala, $V_b$ = 33 m/s
- Fence height, $h$ = 1.5 m
- For goats, **6 strands of barbed wire** recommended (every 25 cm)

**Step 1 — Design wind pressure:**

$$P_d = 0.6 \times (33)^2 = 0.6 \times 1089 = 653.4 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_z = 653.4 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 653.4 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Line post spacing:** Adopt **3 m** (closer spacing for goat fence to prevent pushing).

**Step 3 — Panel area:**

$$A = 1.5 \times 3 = 4.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Wind force on panel:**

$$F_w = 653.4 \times 4.5 \times 1.2 = 3528.4 \text{ N} = 3.53 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Amplified force and moment:**

$$F_{eff} = 3.53 \times 1.5 = 5.30 \text{ kN}$$

$$M_{max} = 5.30 \times \frac{1.5}{2} = 3.97 \text{ kN·m} = 3970 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Required line post diameter:**

$$Z_{req} = \frac{3970}{165 \times 10^6} = 2.41 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$

$$d^3 = \frac{32 \times 2.41 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi} = 2.45 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$d = (2.45 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} = 0.0626 \text{ m} = 62.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Provide: 75 mm GI pipe** (standard) for line posts, 1.5 m above ground + 0.6 m embedded = 2.1 m total length.

**Step 7 — Terminal post design:**

Corner posts: **150 mm GI pipe**, 2.4 m total (1.5 m above + 0.9 m below), set in **concrete footing 300 mm dia × 900 mm deep**.

Total post count:
- Perimeter = 2(100+80) = 360 m
- Line posts: 360/3 ≈ 120 posts
- Corner posts: 4
- Gate posts: 2 (one extra for main gate)

**Step 8 — Gate:** Main gate **3.5 m wide × 1.5 m high**, with 6 horizontal barbed wire strands; personnel gate **1 m × 1.5 m**.

**Step 9 — Material summary table:**

| Item | Specification | Quantity |
|------|---------------|:--------:|
| Barbed wire (12 gauge, IS 278) | 6 strands | 2160 m + 10% wastage = 2376 m |
| Line posts | 75 mm GI pipe, 2.1 m long | 120 nos |
| Corner posts | 150 mm GI pipe, 2.4 m long | 4 nos |
| Gate posts | 150 mm GI pipe, 2.4 m long | 2 nos |
| Main gate | 3.5 m × 1.5 m | 1 no |
| Personnel gate | 1.0 m × 1.5 m | 1 no |
| Concrete (1:2:4) for corner footings | 0.064 m³ each | 4 × 0.064 = 0.256 m³ |

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute CPI for each structure:**

**CPI (Mud Bin):**
$$= (0.20)(2) + (0.15)(2) + (0.15)(3) + (0.10)(2) + (0.10)(1) + (0.15)(5) + (0.05)(2) + (0.05)(1) + (0.05)(5)$$
$$= 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.45 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.75 + 0.10 + 0.05 + 0.25 = \mathbf{2.60}$$

**CPI (PUSA Bin):**
$$= (0.20)(4) + (0.15)(4) + (0.15)(4) + (0.10)(4) + (0.10)(4) + (0.15)(3) + (0.05)(4) + (0.05)(4) + (0.05)(3)$$
$$= 0.80 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.40 + 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 = \mathbf{3.80}$$

**CPI (Ferrocement Bin):**
$$= (0.20)(4) + (0.15)(5) + (0.15)(4) + (0.10)(5) + (0.10)(5) + (0.15)(3) + (0.05)(4) + (0.05)(5) + (0.05)(3)$$
$$= 0.80 + 0.75 + 0.60 + 0.50 + 0.50 + 0.45 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.15 = \mathbf{4.20}$$

**CPI (Metal Silo):**
$$= (0.20)(5) + (0.15)(5) + (0.15)(5) + (0.10)(5) + (0.10)(5) + (0.15)(2) + (0.05)(5) + (0.05)(5) + (0.05)(2)$$
$$= 1.00 + 0.75 + 0.75 + 0.50 + 0.50 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.10 = \mathbf{4.40}$$

**Step 2 — Ranking:** **Metal Silo (4.40) > Ferrocement Bin (4.20) > PUSA Bin (3.80) > Mud Bin (2.60)**

**Step 3 — Recommendation:**

For a **small Kerala farmer with 1 hectare mixed cropping**, the **Ferrocement Bin (CPI = 4.20)** is the most suitable choice. It offers:
- Excellent moisture and insect control (matches Kerala's humid climate)
- Long service life (15–20 years) — amortizes capital cost
- Cost per quintal per year is reasonable
- Locally constructible by trained masons

The Metal Silo is technically best but capital-intensive for a 1-hectare farmer. The Mud Bin, though cheap, has poor durability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Q3b):** Many students skip the **final recommendation paragraph** and only list CPI values. This costs 2 marks. Also, students often **mix up weights and scores** while reading the question — re-read carefully before substituting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3:**

### A. Domestic Storage Structures
- ✅ Domestic storage structures are **on-farm units** for small-to-medium grain storage.
- ✅ Three categories: **Traditional, Improved Traditional, Modern**.
- ✅ Performance is evaluated on **9 parameters** (loss %, moisture, insects, rodents, durability, cost, ergonomics, reusability, environment).
- ✅ The **Composite Performance Index (CPI)** uses weighted scores to rank structures.
- ✅ **PUSA bin, ferrocement bin, and metal silo** are the most recommended improved structures for Kerala.
- ✅ Hermetic structures (silos, PUSA bins) give the **lowest storage loss (1–2%)**.
- ✅ Mud bins and bamboo baskets, though cheap, suffer **6–15% loss** and are not reusable.

### B. Farm Fencing System
- ✅ A fence has **5 components**: posts, wire/mesh, gates, anchorage, stays.
- ✅ **Line posts** (75–100 mm) are spaced at 3–5 m; **terminal posts** (150 mm) at corners and gates.
- ✅ Wind load formula: $P_d = 0.6 V_b^2$; design pressure: $P_z = P_d \cdot k_1 k_2 k_3$.
- ✅ Wind force on a panel: $F_w = P_z \cdot A \cdot C_d$.
- ✅ Post design uses **cantilever bending** with moment at ground level: $M = F_w \cdot h/2 \cdot \lambda$.
- ✅ Section modulus: $Z = \pi d^3 / 32$ (solid post) — invert to get $d_{required}$.
- ✅ Wire sag: $S = W L^2 / (8T)$ — keep sag small (L/150 to L/200) for tautness.
- ✅ **Kerala wind zones:** Coast = 39 m/s (Zone V), Interior = 33 m/s (Zone II).
- ✅ Terminal posts need **concrete footings** with $FOS \geq 1.5$ against overturning.
- ✅ Standard barbed wire: **12 gauge, IS 278**; 2–6 strands depending on animal type.

### C. Numerical Problem-Solving Template
- ✅ Always **state the given data** clearly with units.
- ✅ Compute $P_d$ → $P_z$ → $F_w$ → $M_{max}$ → $Z_{req}$ → $d$ → **standard size up**.
- ✅ For CPI problems: **verify weights sum to 1.0** before calculation.
- ✅ Always end with a **ranking statement and engineering recommendation**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Questions on this module typically test **either** (a) a numerical wind-force + post-design problem (7 marks) **or** (b) a CPI-based comparison (7 marks). Practice both formats thoroughly. The CPI method is **also called MCDM (Multi-Criteria Decision Making)** in research literature — knowing this term can earn you an extra mark if you mention it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Estimation" in Farm Structures?

### Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Estimation** is the scientific process of calculating the **quantities** (volumes, areas, weights, lengths) of various items of work involved in a proposed farm construction project, and subsequently determining the **probable cost** of the project **before** the actual execution of work begins. It is essentially a *forecasting exercise* that bridges engineering design and field execution.

In the context of **Agricultural Engineering**, farm structures include:
- **Dairy barns** (cattle sheds, milking parlours)
- **Poultry houses** (brooder, grower, layer houses)
- **Godowns / Warehouses** for grain and produce storage
- **Greenhouse / Polyhouse structures**
- **Irrigation structures** (tank sluices, canal falls, drop structures)
- **Rural godowns, threshing floors, drying yards**
- **Biogas plants, compost pits, silage pits**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Shopping List Before Grocery" Approach**
>
> Imagine your mother asks you to host a wedding feast for 200 people. Before you go shopping, you sit down and **list every ingredient** (50 kg rice, 30 L oil, 100 eggs...), calculate the **total cost**, and check whether the budget is realistic. You don't just walk into the market and buy whatever you see — that would be chaos.
>
> **Estimation in farm construction is exactly that "shopping list and budget":**
> - **Quantity Survey (QS)** → the *shopping list* (how much cement, how much steel, how many bricks)
> - **Cost Estimate** → the *total bill* (₹ labor + ₹ materials + ₹ overheads)
> - **Rate Analysis** → the *unit price* (₹ per kg, ₹ per m³)
>
> Without an estimate, you'd start building a ₹ 15 lakh dairy barn and discover halfway through that the budget was only ₹ 8 lakh — catastrophic for any farmer.

### The Two Pillars of Estimation

| Pillar | What it Does | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantity Survey (QS)** | Measures how much of each material/labour is needed | Quantities (m³, kg, m², nos.) |
| **Cost Estimation** | Multiplies quantities × rates + adds overheads | Total Project Cost (₹) |

### Why Estimation is Non-Negotiable in Farm Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three reasons KTU examiners love testing this concept:
> 1. **Sanction from funding agencies** — NABARD, RKVY, PM-Kisan schemes require an *Abstract Estimate* for loan/grant approval.
> 2. **Tendering** — Contractors quote based on the *Detailed Estimate* (Bill of Quantities / BOQ).
> 3. **Cost control & valuation** — *Valuation* determines the *present worth* of an existing farm structure for insurance, sale, or taxation.

### Key Standard Metrics You Must Memorise

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational units used throughout KTU estimation problems:**
> - **Volume of earthwork** → measured in **cubic metres (m³)** or **cumec**
> - **Concrete (PCC/RCC)** → **m³** of finished concrete
> - **Brick masonry** → **m³** of brickwork (1 m³ = ~500 bricks nominal)
> - **Steel reinforcement** → **kilogram (kg)** or **quintal (1 Q = 100 kg)**
> - **Plastering / flooring / roofing** → **square metres (m²)**
> - **Centring / shuttering** → **m²** of contact area
> - **Labour** → often charged per day (1 *mazdoor-day* = **8 hours** under **CPWD** norms)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Estimates

Estimates are classified by **purpose, accuracy, and stage** of the project. This classification is a **favourite KTU Part-A question** every year.

### A. Approximate (Preliminary / Abstract) Estimate

Used at the **feasibility stage** when detailed drawings are NOT yet available.

| Method | Formula | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Plinth Area Method** | $C = P \times R_p \times A$ | Buildings where plinth area is known |
| **Carpet Area Method** | $C = P_c \times R_c \times A_c$ | Residential & farm houses |
| **Cubic Rate Method** | $C = P_v \times R_v \times V$ | Multi-storey or tall structures (silos, towers) |
| **Unit Service Method** | $C = U \times C_u$ | Water supply, drainage, electrification |
| **Bay / Structural Unit Method** | $C = N \times C_b$ | Repetitive units (bays of a barn) |
| **Cost Per Hectare / Per Animal** | $C = N_{heads} \times C_{head}$ | Dairy/poultry farms — **very common in KTU** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $C$ = Total estimated cost
> - $P$ = Plinth area percentage (typically **65–70%** of total built-up for farm sheds)
> - $A$ = Total plinth area (m²)
> - $R_p$ = Plinth area rate (₹/m²) — taken from **CPWD Plinth Area Rates** or **State PWD SOR**
> - $V$ = Volume of building (m³) = plinth area × height
> - $C_b$ = Cost per bay / per structural unit

### B. Detailed Estimate (Item-Wise / Quantity Estimate)

Used at the **execution stage** with full design drawings, specifications, and rates.

**Procedure:**
1. Take off quantities from drawings → *Quantity Survey (QS)*
2. Apply Schedule of Rates (SOR) → get item-wise cost
3. Add **contingencies (3–5%)** and **work-charged establishment (1.5–2%)**
4. Add **supervision charges (5–10%)** if executed through a department
5. Add **gst / taxes** as applicable

### C. Revised & Supplementary Estimates

| Type | When Prepared |
|---|---|
| **Revised Estimate** | When original sanctioned cost exceeds by **>10%** due to genuine design changes |
| **Supplementary Estimate** | When *additional* work (not in original scope) is required |
| **Annual Repair Estimate** | For routine maintenance of existing farm structures |

---

## 2.2 Units of Measurement — The KTU Standard Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **IS 1200** (Method of Measurement) and **CPWD DSR (Delhi Schedule of Rates)** conventions, every item has a *standard unit*. KTU problems ALWAYS quote quantities in these units.

| Item of Work | Standard Unit | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Earthwork in excavation | Cubic metre | m³ |
| Earthwork in filling | Cubic metre | m³ |
| PCC / RCC concrete | Cubic metre | m³ |
| Brick masonry | Cubic metre | m³ |
| Stone masonry | Cubic metre | m³ |
| Damp-proof course (DPC) | Square metre | m² |
| Centring & shuttering | Square metre | m² |
| Steel reinforcement | Quintal / kg | Q / kg |
| Plastering, pointing, painting | Square metre | m² |
| Flooring (IPS, tiles, stone) | Square metre | m² |
| Roofing (AC sheet, GI sheet, tile) | Square metre | m² |
| Doors & windows | Square metre / Number | m² / No. |
| Waterproofing | Square metre | m² |
| Plumbing / sanitary | Number / running metre | No. / Rm |
| Electrical wiring | Running metre / Point | Rm / Pt |
| Timber / woodwork | Cubic metre | m³ |
| Glazing (glass) | Square metre | m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rules of thumb examiners use to test you:**
> - Length, breadth, thickness → m, m, m → **m³** (volume work)
> - Length × breadth (no thickness) → **m²** (area work)
> - Long items like pipes, cables → **Running Metre (Rm)**
> - Distinct items like fans, switches, traps → **Number (No.)**
> - **PCC = Plain Cement Concrete (no steel)** ; **RCC = Reinforced Cement Concrete (with steel)**

---

## 2.3 Rate Analysis — The Heart of Estimation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rate of any item = Material cost + Labour cost + Plant/machinery cost + Contractor’s profit & overheads**

### The Master Formula

$$
R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times M_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} (H_j \times L_j) + P_{plant} + O_{head} + P_{profit}
$$

Where:
- $Q_i$ = Quantity of material *i* required per unit of work
- $M_i$ = Market rate of material *i* (₹/unit)
- $H_j$ = Hours/days of labour *j* required
- $L_j$ = Wage of labour *j* (₹/day or ₹/hr)
- $P_{plant}$ = Hire charges of machinery (e.g., mixer, vibrator)
- $O_{head}$ = Overheads (site office, transport, wastage ~1.5–2%)
- $P_{profit}$ = Contractor's profit (typically **10–15%**)

### Standard Wastage Allowances (CPWD Norms)

| Material | Wastage Allowed |
|---|---|
| Cement | **2%** |
| Sand | **7–10%** (bulk) |
| Aggregate (coarse) | **5–7%** |
| Bricks | **5%** |
| Steel | **2.5%** (binding & cutting) |
| Timber | **5%** |
| Bitumen | **2.5%** |

### Concrete Mix Proportions (Nominal Mix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard nominal mixes (by volume) per IS 456:**
> - **M5** → 1 : 5 : 10
> - **M7.5** → 1 : 4 : 8
> - **M10** → 1 : 3 : 6
> - **M15** → 1 : 2 : 4 *(most common in farm foundations)*
> - **M20** → 1 : 1.5 : 3 *(RCC slabs, beams)*
> - **M25** → 1 : 1 : 2 *(water-retaining structures)*

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 15 formulas — they appear in 90% of KTU Module-4 problems:**

| # | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{excav} = L \times B \times D$ | Foundation trench earthwork |
| 2 | $V_{conc} = L \times B \times D$ | PCC / RCC volume |
| 3 | $V_{brick} = V_{wall} - V_{openings}$ | Masonry volume |
| 4 | $N_{bricks} = \dfrac{V_{brickwork} \times 500}{1}$ | Number of bricks per m³ ≈ **500** |
| 5 | $W_{steel} = V_{steel} \times 7850$ | Weight of steel (kg); density = **7850 kg/m³** |
| 6 | $D_{steel} = \dfrac{7850 \times \pi \times \phi^2}{4} \times L$ | Weight of single bar; $\phi$ = dia (m) |
| 7 | $C_{plinth} = A_{plinth} \times R_p$ | Approximate estimate |
| 8 | $C_{cubic} = V_{bldg} \times R_v$ | Cubic rate method |
| 9 | $C_{total} = C_{items} + C_{conting} + C_{supv}$ | Final project cost |
| 10 | $W_{cement} = \dfrac{V_{conc} \times \text{density}}{1 + a + b} \times 1.02$ | Bag count (50 kg/bag) |
| 11 | $V_{sand} = \dfrac{V_{conc} \times a}{1 + a + b} \times 1.10$ | With 10% wastage |
| 12 | $V_{agg} = \dfrac{V_{conc} \times b}{1 + a + b} \times 1.07$ | With 7% wastage |
| 13 | $A_{plaster} = A_{wall} - A_{openings}$ | Net plaster area |
| 14 | $A_{roof} = L \times B + \text{sloped factor}$ | Sloped roof area |
| 15 | $V_{annual\_depn} = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | Straight-line valuation; $S$=scrap, $n$=life |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where KTU-tested estimation skills are deployed in industry:**
> - **NABARD-funded dairy/poultry sheds** → BOQ preparation
> - **MNREGA / PMAY farm assets** → Abstract estimate for administrative sanction
> - **PM-KUSUM solar farm structures** → Structural + civil estimation
> - **APMC market yard construction** → Detailed estimate
> - **Cold storage / ripening chambers** → Cost per tonne capacity
> - **Valuation of existing farm structures** for **insurance** (Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana-linked farm assets)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Detailed Estimate of a Cattle Shed Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style, 14 marks):**
> *Estimate the quantity of Earthwork in excavation, PCC (1:4:8), and Brickwork in CM (1:6) for the foundation of a **cattle shed** measuring **20 m × 8 m** (external dimensions). Wall thickness = 0.30 m, Depth of foundation = 1.20 m below GL, PCC bed thickness = 0.15 m, Foundation offsets in PCC = 0.10 m on each side of brickwork. Brickwork of foundation = up to GL only.*

### Step A — Draw the Centre-line Plan

For an external dimension building of 20 m × 8 m with 0.30 m walls:
- **Centre-line length (long wall)** = $20 - 0.30 = 19.70$ m
- **Centre-line length (short wall)** = $8 - 0.30 = 7.70$ m

> Total centre-line length = $2 \times (19.70 + 7.70) = 54.80$ m

### Step B — Earthwork in Excavation

Width of excavation (PCC width + offsets) = $(0.30 + 2 \times 0.10) = 0.50$ m

$$
V_{excav} = \text{Centre-line length} \times \text{Width} \times \text{Depth}
$$

$$
V_{excav} = 54.80 \times 0.50 \times 1.20
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{excav} = 32.88 \; \text{m}^3}
$$

### Step C — PCC (1:4:8) in Foundation Bed

$$
V_{PCC} = 54.80 \times 0.50 \times 0.15
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{PCC} = 4.11 \; \text{m}^3}
$$

### Step D — Brickwork in CM (1:6) up to GL

Brickwork starts from top of PCC (depth = $1.20 - 0.15 = 1.05$ m)

Width of brickwork = 0.30 m

$$
V_{brick} = 54.80 \times 0.30 \times 1.05
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{brick} = 17.26 \; \text{m}^3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Number of bricks required** (using nominal count of 500 bricks/m³ of brickwork):
> $$
> N_{brick} = 17.26 \times 500 = 8{,}630 \; \text{bricks} \;\; \text{(nominal, before wastage)}
> $$
> With 5% wastage → **9,062 bricks** (≈ 9,100 ordered).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Rate Analysis of M20 RCC (1:1.5:3)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** *Analyse the rate (per m³) of M20 RCC (1:1.5:3 nominal mix) using the following data:*
> - Cement = ₹ 380 per bag (50 kg), Sand = ₹ 1,400/m³, Aggregate (20 mm) = ₹ 1,600/m³
> - Steel reinforcement already excluded (will be paid separately)
> - Wastage: Cement 2%, Sand 10%, Aggregate 7%
> - Labour for 1 m³ RCC = 0.27 mason-day + 0.90 mazdoor-day
> - Mason wage = ₹ 600/day, Mazdoor wage = ₹ 500/day
> - Mixer & vibrator hire = ₹ 80/m³
> - Contractor's profit = 10%

### Step 1 — Wet Volume Calculation

Nominal mix ratio 1 : 1.5 : 3 → Total parts = $1 + 1.5 + 3 = 5.5$

Dry volume factor (standard = 1.54 for nominal mix):

$$
V_{dry} = V_{wet} \times 1.54 = 1 \times 1.54 = 1.54 \; \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 2 — Cement Quantity

$$
V_{cement} = \dfrac{1}{5.5} \times 1.54 \times 1.02 = 0.2857 \; \text{m}^3
$$

Convert to weight using bulk density of cement = **1440 kg/m³**:

$$
W_{cement} = 0.2857 \times 1440 = 411.41 \; \text{kg}
$$

Number of bags:

$$
N_{bags} = \dfrac{411.41}{50} = 8.23 \; \text{bags} \;\; (\approx 8.25 \text{ bags})
$$

Cost of cement:

$$
C_{cem} = 8.23 \times 380 = ₹ \; 3{,}127.40
$$

### Step 3 — Sand Quantity (with 10% wastage)

$$
V_{sand} = \dfrac{1.5}{5.5} \times 1.54 \times 1.10 = 0.4620 \; \text{m}^3
$$

$$
C_{sand} = 0.4620 \times 1400 = ₹ \; 646.80
$$

### Step 4 — Aggregate Quantity (with 7% wastage)

$$
V_{agg} = \dfrac{3.0}{5.5} \times 1.54 \times 1.07 = 0.8990 \; \text{m}^3
$$

$$
C_{agg} = 0.8990 \times 1600 = ₹ \; 1{,}438.40
$$

### Step 5 — Labour Cost

$$
C_{lab} = (0.27 \times 600) + (0.90 \times 500) = 162 + 450 = ₹ \; 612.00
$$

### Step 6 — Plant & Machinery

$$
C_{plant} = ₹ \; 80.00
$$

### Step 7 — Subtotal (Material + Labour + Plant)

$$
C_{sub} = 3127.40 + 646.80 + 1438.40 + 612.00 + 80.00
$$

$$
C_{sub} = ₹ \; 5{,}904.60
$$

### Step 8 — Add Contractor's Profit @ 10%

$$
C_{final} = 5904.60 \times 1.10 = ₹ \; 6{,}495.06
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{M20\;RCC} \approx ₹ \; 6{,}495 \; \text{per m}^3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Always include the **dry-to-wet volume factor (1.54)** in step 1 — this is where **60% of students lose marks**. They directly use 1 m³ = 1 m³ without accounting for the *bulking of sand* and *voids in aggregate*.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Approximate Estimate (Cubic Rate Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** *Estimate the cost of a **poultry house** of size **30 m × 10 m × 4.5 m (height)** using the **cubic rate method**. Take cubic rate as ₹ 1,200 per m³.*

$$
V_{building} = 30 \times 10 \times 4.5 = 1{,}350 \; \text{m}^3
$$

$$
C_{approx} = V \times R_v = 1350 \times 1200
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{approx} = ₹ \; 16{,}20{,}000 \; (16.2 \; \text{Lakhs})}
$$

> **Plinth area method check** (if plinth area rate = ₹ 5,400/m²):
> $A = 300 \; m^2 \Rightarrow C = 300 \times 5400 = ₹ \; 16{,}20{,}000$ ✓ *(matches because $R_p = R_v \times H$)*

---

## 3.4 Valuation — Straight Line Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** *A dairy building was constructed 15 years ago at ₹ 12,00,000. Its expected useful life is 60 years with a scrap value of ₹ 1,00,000. Find its present book value using the Straight-Line Method.*

Annual straight-line depreciation:

$$
D = \dfrac{C - S}{n} = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{60} = ₹ \; 18{,}333.33 \; \text{per year}
$$

Depreciation for 15 years:

$$
D_{15} = 15 \times 18{,}333.33 = ₹ \; 2{,}75{,}000
$$

Present book value:

$$
V_{present} = C - D_{15} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}75{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{present} = ₹ \; 9{,}25{,}000}
$$

---

## 3.5 Master Step-by-Step Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to decide which estimation method to apply:**

| Project Type & Available Data | Recommended Method |
|---|---|
| Only site dimensions known | **Plinth Area Method** |
| Height + dimensions known | **Cubic Rate Method** |
| Repetitive bays (sheds) | **Bay Method** |
| Specific items (electrical, plumbing) | **Unit Service Method** |
| Per-head / per-tonne capacity farm | **Unit Capacity Method** |
| Detailed drawings + SOR available | **Detailed Estimate (Item-wise)** |
| Existing structure, age known | **Valuation (Straight-Line / Sinking Fund)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START Project Conceptualisation]
    A1[Receive Design Drawings and Specifications]
    A2{Estimate Type Required?}
    A3[Preliminary Abstract Estimate using Plinth or Cubic Rate]
    A4[Detailed Itemwise Estimate using Quantity Survey]
    A5[Quantity Survey QS off the Drawings]
    A6[Refer CPWD DSR or State PWD SOR for Unit Rates]
    A7[Calculate Material Cost plus Labour Cost plus Plant Cost]
    A8[Add Wastage and Overheads]
    A9[Add Contractor Profit 10 to 15 percent]
    A10[Add Contingencies 3 to 5 percent]
    A11[Add Supervision Charges 5 to 10 percent]
    A12[Apply GST and applicable Taxes]
    A13[Final Abstract of Cost Prepared]
    A14[Submit to Funding Agency NABARD or State Dept]
    A15[Administrative and Technical Sanction]
    A16[Tendering Process]
    A17[Execution at Site]
    A18[END]

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 -- Preliminary --> A3
    A2 -- Detailed --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13
    A13 --> A14
    A14 --> A15
    A15 --> A16
    A16 --> A17
    A17 --> A18
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Rate Analysis Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Item Specification e.g. M20 RCC 1:1.5:3] --> B2[Identify Mix Ratio]
    B2 --> B3[Compute Dry Volume using 1.54 factor]
    B3 --> B4[Material Quantities per Cubic Metre]
    B4 --> B5[Apply Market Rates]
    B5 --> B6[Material Cost Subtotal]
    B7[Labour Productivity Data] --> B8[Labour Cost]
    B8 --> B6
    B9[Plant Hire Charges] --> B6
    B6 --> B10[Add Wastage and Overheads]
    B10 --> B11[Add Contractor Profit]
    B11 --> B12[Final Rate per Cubic Metre]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram of Detailed Estimate Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Detailed Estimate of Farm Structure] --> C2[Earthwork]
    C1 --> C3[Concrete Works PCC and RCC]
    C1 --> C4[Masonry Brick or Stone]
    C1 --> C5[Reinforcement Steel]
    C1 --> C6[Formwork and Centring]
    C1 --> C7[Doors Windows and Ventilators]
    C1 --> C8[Flooring and Plastering]
    C1 --> C9[Roofing and Waterproofing]
    C1 --> C10[Plumbing and Sanitary]
    C1 --> C11[Electrical Installation]
    C1 --> C12[External Works Yard Drain etc]

    C2 --> D1[Cost per Cubic Metre]
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D2[Cost per Kilogram]
    C6 --> D3[Cost per Square Metre]
    C7 --> D3
    C8 --> D3
    C9 --> D3
    C10 --> D4[Cost per Number or Point]
    C11 --> D4
    C12 --> D1

    D1 --> E1[Itemised Abstract Sheet]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> E2[Add Contingency 3 to 5 percent]
    E2 --> E3[Add Establishment 1.5 to 2 percent]
    E3 --> E4[Add GST 18 percent]
    E4 --> E5[GRAND TOTAL in Rupees]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Cattle-Shed Foundation Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Ground Level GL] --> F2[Earthwork Excavation Depth 1.20 m]
    F2 --> F3[PCC 1:4:8 Bed Thickness 0.15 m]
    F3 --> F4[Brickwork CM 1:6 Up to GL]
    F4 --> F5[Superstructure Wall above GL]
    F5 --> F6[Roofing Truss and AC Sheet]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the diagram as the layered construction from bottom (substructure) to top (superstructure).** This is the standard sequence followed while preparing a *Detailed Estimate* — you work from **foundation upward** because each layer's quantity depends on the layer above it.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Distinguish between an *Abstract Estimate* and a *Detailed Estimate*.**

#### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Abstract Estimate | Detailed Estimate |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Rough cost forecasting | Accurate costing for tendering |
| **Stage** | Pre-design / feasibility | Post-design (drawings ready) |
| **Accuracy** | ±15–25% | ±5% |
| **Methods used** | Plinth area, Cubic rate, Bay method | Item-wise quantity survey |
| **Output** | Lump-sum cost in ₹ | Item-wise BOQ + abstract |

*(Any 3 valid distinguishing points = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **What is meant by "Schedule of Rates" (SOR)? What is its role in farm structure estimation?**

#### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Schedule of Rates (SOR)** is a publication issued by government departments (CPWD, State PWD, PWD-Agriculture) that lists the **standard unit rates** of every construction item (e.g., ₹/m³ for RCC, ₹/m² for plastering, ₹/kg for steel) for a given period and locality.
>
> **Role in farm structure estimation:**
> 1. Provides a **standardised, transparent** rate for tendering.
> 2. Ensures **uniform pricing** across all government farm projects.
> 3. Acts as a **ceiling rate** — private contractors cannot exceed it without justification.
> 4. Forms the **base data for rate analysis** when site-specific rates differ.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — 14 Marks (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module-4 Choice Pattern]**
> *Attempt ANY ONE of the following:*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Prepare a detailed estimate for the **Earthwork in excavation, PCC (1:4:8), and first-class brickwork in CM (1:6)** for the foundation of a farm godown measuring **15 m × 6 m externally**. Walls are 0.30 m thick. Foundation depth below GL = 1.00 m. PCC bed thickness = 0.10 m. PCC offset on each side = 0.075 m. Brickwork rises up to GL.

**(b) [7 marks]** Analyse the **rate per m³** for **M15 (1:2:4)** RCC using:
- Cement = ₹ 360/bag (50 kg), Sand = ₹ 1,300/m³, Aggregate = ₹ 1,500/m³
- Wastage: Cement 2%, Sand 10%, Coarse aggregate 7%
- Labour: 0.30 mason-day @ ₹ 580/day + 0.85 mazdoor-day @ ₹ 480/day
- Mixer & vibrator hire = ₹ 75/m³
- Contractor's profit = 12%

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Centre-line dimensions**
- Long wall centre-line = $15 - 0.30 = 14.70$ m
- Short wall centre-line = $6 - 0.30 = 5.70$ m
- Total C/L = $2 \times (14.70 + 5.70) = 40.80$ m

**Step 2: Excavation**
- Width = $0.30 + 2 \times 0.075 = 0.45$ m
- Depth = $1.00$ m
- $V_{excav} = 40.80 \times 0.45 \times 1.00 = \mathbf{18.36 \; m^3}$

**Step 3: PCC (1:4:8)**
- $V_{PCC} = 40.80 \times 0.45 \times 0.10 = \mathbf{1.836 \; m^3 \approx 1.84 \; m^3}$

**Step 4: Brickwork in CM (1:6)**
- Height of brickwork = $1.00 - 0.10 = 0.90$ m
- $V_{brick} = 40.80 \times 0.30 \times 0.90 = \mathbf{11.016 \; m^3 \approx 11.02 \; m^3}$
- Bricks ≈ $11.02 \times 500 = 5{,}510$ → with 5% wastage = **5,786 bricks**

**Item-wise Abstract:**

| Sl | Item | Quantity | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earthwork excavation | 18.36 | m³ |
| 2 | PCC (1:4:8) | 1.84 | m³ |
| 3 | Brickwork CM (1:6) | 11.02 | m³ |
| 4 | Bricks (with wastage) | 5,786 | No. |

*(Step-wise quantities + final tabular abstract = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Dry volume**
$V_{dry} = 1 \times 1.54 = 1.54 \; m^3$

Total parts = $1 + 2 + 4 = 7$

**Step 2: Cement**
$V_{cem} = \dfrac{1}{7} \times 1.54 \times 1.02 = 0.2244 \; m^3$
$W_{cem} = 0.2244 \times 1440 = 323.14 \; kg \Rightarrow \dfrac{323.14}{50} = 6.46$ bags
$C_{cem} = 6.46 \times 360 = ₹ \; 2{,}325.60$

**Step 3: Sand**
$V_{sand} = \dfrac{2}{7} \times 1.54 \times 1.10 = 0.4840 \; m^3$
$C_{sand} = 0.4840 \times 1300 = ₹ \; 629.20$

**Step 4: Aggregate**
$V_{agg} = \dfrac{4}{7} \times 1.54 \times 1.07 = 0.9414 \; m^3$
$C_{agg} = 0.9414 \times 1500 = ₹ \; 1{,}412.10$

**Step 5: Labour**
$C_{lab} = (0.30 \times 580) + (0.85 \times 480) = 174 + 408 = ₹ \; 582.00$

**Step 6: Plant**
$C_{plant} = ₹ \; 75.00$

**Step 7: Subtotal**
$C_{sub} = 2325.60 + 629.20 + 1412.10 + 582.00 + 75.00 = ₹ \; 5{,}023.90$

**Step 8: Add 12% profit**
$C_{final} = 5023.90 \times 1.12 = ₹ \; 5{,}626.77$

$$
\boxed{R_{M15} \approx ₹ \; 5{,}627 \; \text{per m}^3}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** A **dairy cattle shed** has plinth area of 240 m² and height of 4.0 m. Estimate its cost using:
(i) Plinth area method @ ₹ 6,500/m²
(ii) Cubic rate method @ ₹ 1,700/m³
State which method is more appropriate and why.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain with formulas the **Straight-Line Method** and **Sinking Fund Method** of depreciation. A farm implement shed was constructed at ₹ 5,00,000 with a useful life of 40 years and scrap value of ₹ 50,000. Calculate the annual depreciation and present value after 10 years using both methods. Take sinking fund interest rate = 6%.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks**

**Plinth Area Method:**
$C_{plinth} = 240 \times 6500 = ₹ \; 15{,}60{,}000$

**Cubic Rate Method:**
$V = 240 \times 4.0 = 960 \; m^3$
$C_{cubic} = 960 \times 1700 = ₹ \; 16{,}32{,}000$

> **Conclusion:** Both methods are close (within 5%). The **Plinth Area Method** is preferred for **single-storey farm sheds** because height is roughly constant. The **Cubic Rate Method** is more accurate for **tall structures like silos or water tanks**, where volume (not just area) drives material cost. Here, **Plinth Area Method is more appropriate**.

*(3 marks for calculations + 4 marks for comparison/recommendation)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks**

**Straight-Line Method (SLM):**

$$
D_{SLM} = \dfrac{C - S}{n}
$$

$$
D_{SLM} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{40} = ₹ \; 11{,}250 \; \text{per year}
$$

After 10 years:
$D_{10} = 10 \times 11{,}250 = ₹ \; 1{,}12{,}500$
$V_{SLM} = 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}12{,}500 = ₹ \; 3{,}87{,}500$

**Sinking Fund Method (SFM):**

Annual sinking fund deposit:
$$
A = \dfrac{S \times i}{(1+i)^n - 1} + \dfrac{C - S}{n} \times \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1} \times \big[(1+i)^n - 1\big]
$$

Simpler form (depreciation each year):
$$
D_{SFM} = (C - S) \times \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

Where $i = 0.06$, $n = 40$:

$(1.06)^{40} = 10.286$

$$
D_{SFM} = (5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000) \times \dfrac{0.06}{10.286 - 1} = 4{,}50{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.06}{9.286}
$$

$$
D_{SFM} = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.006461 = ₹ \; 2{,}907.45 \; \text{per year}
$$

After 10 years:
$D_{10,SFM} = 10 \times 2907.45 = ₹ \; 29{,}074.50$

> **Sinking Fund accumulated** = $A \times \dfrac{(1.06)^{10} - 1}{0.06}$, where $A = (C-S) \times \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1} = ₹ \; 2{,}907.45$
>
> $(1.06)^{10} = 1.7908 \Rightarrow \dfrac{1.7908 - 1}{0.06} = 13.18$
>
> $Sinking\; fund = 2907.45 \times 13.18 = ₹ \; 38{,}320$

Present value after 10 years (SLM):
$V_{SLM,10} = ₹ \; 3{,}87{,}500$

Present value after 10 years (SFM):
$V_{SFM,10} = C - D_{depreciated\;charge} - Sinking\; Fund = ...$ *(full deduction)*

*(Final figures vary based on convention; KTU accepts either constant-charge or progressive-charge interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks in estimation problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.54 dry-volume factor** in concrete rate analysis → loses 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Wrong unit for steel** — quoting tonnes instead of kg, or missing the *7850 kg/m³* density.
> 3. **Centre-line vs external dimensions confusion** — using 20 m instead of 19.70 m for long-wall C/L.
> 4. **No wastage added** — examiners always give 2 marks for *“adding wastage and overheads”*.
> 5. **Mixing up SLM and SFM** — SFM gives a *progressively decreasing* annual depreciation, while SLM gives a *constant* annual charge. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — always write *“Assuming standard CPWD norms, no extra depth, dry soil, etc.”* at the start.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert** cement from m³ to **kg** (using 1440 kg/m³ bulk density) **and then to bags** (50 kg/bag) — chain error costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Estimation**
>
> ✅ **Two main classes:** Abstract (Plinth Area, Cubic Rate, Bay, Unit Service, Per-head/Per-tonne) vs Detailed (item-wise BOQ).
>
> ✅ **Plinth Area Formula:** $C = A \times R_p$. Standard plinth area rates for **farm sheds = ₹ 4,000–7,000/m²** (2024 SOR reference).
>
> ✅ **Cubic Rate Formula:** $C = V \times R_v = (L \times B \times H) \times R_v$.
>
> ✅ **Centre-line method** for foundation: total C/L = $2 \times (L + B - w)$ for rectangular buildings.
>
> ✅ **Standard units:** Earthwork, concrete, brickwork → m³ ; Plaster, floor, roof → m² ; Steel → kg/Q ; Long items → Rm.
>
> ✅ **Nominal concrete mixes:** M5, M7.5, M10, M15 (1:2:4), M20 (1:1.5:3), M25 (1:1:2). M15 is the *workhorse* for farm PCC.
>
> ✅ **Dry-volume factor = 1.54** for nominal mixes.
>
> ✅ **Cement density = 1440 kg/m³** ; Steel density = **7850 kg/m³** ; 1 bag = **50 kg** ; 1 m³ brickwork ≈ **500 bricks** (nominal).
>
> ✅ **Standard wastages:** Cement 2%, Sand 10%, Aggregate 7%, Bricks 5%, Steel 2.5%.
>
> ✅ **Add to estimate:** Wastage → Profit (10–15%) → Contingency (3–5%) → Establishment (1.5–2%) → Supervision (5–10%) → GST (18%).
>
> ✅ **Straight-Line Depreciation:** $D = (C-S)/n$ — constant yearly.
>
> ✅ **Sinking Fund Depreciation:** Variable, with compounding — used for **high-value, long-life** structures like silos, godowns.
>
> ✅ **Bay Method** is preferred for **large repetitive farm sheds** (each bay ≈ 3–4 m).
>
> ✅ **Per-animal / per-bird cost** is widely used for dairy and poultry farm sanctioning.
>
> ✅ **Always cross-check** with **CPWD DSR** (Delhi Schedule of Rates) or the **State Agricultural Engineering Department's SOR** for the latest unit rates.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Terms and Types of Estimates - Revised Estimate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Terms and Types of Estimates - Revised Estimate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamental Terminology in Cost Estimation

Before diving into the various types of estimates, it is mandatory for every KTU student to first master the **basic vocabulary** of the estimation discipline. These terms form the foundation upon which every quantity surveying and valuation question is built in the ESE (End Semester Examination).

### 1.1.1 Key Definitions (KTU Board Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Official KTU Definition — Estimate**
> An **estimate** is a calculated approximation of the probable cost of a proposed engineering work (farm building, irrigation channel, storage godown, cattle shed, etc.) prepared before the actual construction is undertaken. It is a forecast of expenditure based on current market rates of materials, labour, equipment, and overheads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition — Estimation**
> **Estimation** is the *process* or *art* of preparing an estimate. It is the systematic scientific procedure followed to determine quantities (volumes, areas, lengths, numbers) of various items of work and then converting those quantities into monetary value using standardized rates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Official KTU Definition — Cost**
> **Cost** is the actual expenditure incurred *after* the completion of the project. The relationship is: **Estimated Cost ≈ Actual Cost** (with acceptable tolerance of **±5% to ±10%** for well-prepared estimates).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition — Rate Analysis**
> **Rate analysis** is the procedure adopted to determine the *unit rate* of an item of work (e.g., cost per cubic metre of RCC, cost per square metre of plastering) by summing the costs of materials, labour, tools, plant, and a reasonable contractor's profit (usually **10% to 15%**).

### 1.1.2 Other Critical Terms

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Quantity Survey (QS)** | The detailed measurement and listing of quantities for every item of work. |
| **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** | A tabular document listing each item, its quantity, rate, and amount (Quantity × Rate). |
| **Abstract of Estimate** | A summary sheet showing the total cost of each major trade/work head, then summed to give the grand total. |
| **Schedule of Rates (SOR)** | A government-published document (e.g., PWD SOR, KSRB rates) giving standardized rates for items of work in a given district/year. |
| **Tender / Bid** | A written offer by a contractor to execute the work at a quoted price, submitted in response to a notice inviting tenders. |
| **Contingencies** | A provision (typically **3% to 5%** of total estimate) to cover unforeseen expenses. |
| **Work-charged Establishment** | The cost of supervisory staff deployed exclusively for the project. |
| **Lump-Sum (LS) Item** | An item whose cost is paid as a whole, without detailed quantity measurement (e.g., site clearance, water supply). |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> **Analogy: Building a House on a Budget**
>
> Imagine you are planning to build a small **farmhouse** for your family. Before laying a single brick, you sit down with a notebook and ask:
> *“How much cement, sand, steel, bricks, and labour will I need, and how much money will the whole thing cost?”*
>
> That calculation you do is an **estimate**. The act of sitting down and doing the calculation is **estimation**. Once the house is built and you tally the actual bills, that figure is the **cost**.
>
> Now, suppose midway through construction, you suddenly decide to add a **veranda** and a **rainwater harvesting tank** that were *not* in the original plan. You must go back, re-calculate, and revise your budget. That fresh, updated budget is the **Revised Estimate**.
>
> In short:
> - **Estimate** = the budget document.
> - **Estimation** = the act of preparing it.
> - **Cost** = what you actually spent.
> - **Revised Estimate** = the budget that has been *updated* to reflect new realities.

---

## 1.3 Why These Terms Matter in Farm Structures

In **farm structure projects** — dairy sheds, poultry houses, farm godowns, greenhouses, irrigation channels, fencing — the cost is almost always borne by a government department, a bank (for agricultural loans), or a development authority. Because the money is public or institutional, every rupee must be **justified, documented, and pre-approved**. This is why estimates are a legal and financial necessity, not just an academic exercise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis (2024 Scheme)**
> Examiners specifically test whether students can *distinguish* between the various types of estimates and can correctly identify the *situation* in which each is required. Memorizing definitions alone is not enough — you must demonstrate *application*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Estimates — Master Classification

The KTU syllabus recognizes **two broad categories** of estimates, each containing multiple sub-types. Understanding this hierarchy is essential.

### 2.1.1 Broad Classification

| Category | Purpose | Accuracy Level | Used At |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A. Approximate Estimates** | To get a quick, rough idea of cost. | Low (±20% to ±30%) | Initial feasibility / project sanction stage. |
| **B. Detailed Estimates** | To get an accurate, item-wise cost. | High (±5% to ±10%) | After administrative approval, before tendering. |

### 2.1.2 Sub-Classification of Approximate Estimates

1. **Plinth Area Estimate** — Cost is computed by multiplying the *plinth area* (built-up covered area at plinth level) of the proposed building by the *plinth area rate* of a similar existing building in the locality.

2. **Cubical Content (Cube Rate) Estimate** — Cost is computed by multiplying the *cubical volume* of the building (Length × Breadth × Height) by the *cube rate* (cost per m³) derived from a similar building.

3. **Approximate Quantity Method Estimate** — Cost is computed by working out the approximate quantities of major items (foundation, walls, roofing, flooring) using thumb rules, and pricing them at standard rates.

4. **Cost per Unit Basis Estimate** — Cost is derived from cost per unit of *function* (e.g., per student for a school, per seat for an auditorium, per animal for a cattle shed, per cubic metre of storage for a godown).

### 2.1.3 Sub-Classification of Detailed Estimates

1. **Detailed Estimate (Original/Detailed)** — Item-by-item measurement, multiplication by rates, and summation.
2. **Revised Estimate** — A *fresh, complete* detailed estimate prepared when the original estimate is likely to be exceeded by **more than 5%** due to design changes, rate changes, or scope changes.
3. **Supplementary Estimate** — A *supplementary* detailed estimate prepared when *additional* works (not in the original) are required, while the original work proceeds. It is *added* to the original.
4. **Annual Repair & Maintenance (ARM) Estimate** — Prepared yearly for upkeep of existing structures.
5. **Special Repair Estimate** — For major special repairs (e.g., re-roofing a damaged godown).
6. **Quantity Estimate** — Only quantities are worked out; rates are not applied (used for checking contractor bills).

---

## 2.2 The Revised Estimate — Core Focus

### 2.2.1 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Official KTU Definition — Revised Estimate**
> A **Revised Estimate** is a detailed estimate that is *re-prepared* (ab initio, i.e., from the very beginning, as if for the first time) when the original sanctioned detailed estimate is likely to be **exceeded by more than 5%**, OR when there is a material change in the design, specifications, or scope of the work, such that the original estimate no longer represents a fair approximation of the probable cost.

### 2.2.2 Why is it called "Revised" and not "Re-estimated"?

The terminology is crucial. The estimate is *revised* in the sense that:
- It re-uses the same fundamental procedure (take-off → rate application → abstract).
- It incorporates the *latest* design, drawings, and prevailing *Schedule of Rates* (SOR).
- It includes the *actual* quantities that are now known with greater certainty (since construction has either begun or design has matured).

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Flow — When to Prepare a Revised Estimate

The following structured conditions trigger the need for a revised estimate:

- **Condition 1 — Cost Escalation Beyond 5%:** When the cost of the project is anticipated to rise beyond 105% of the sanctioned amount due to genuine rate inflation, even without scope change.
- **Condition 2 — Design Modification:** The structural design, foundation depth, wall thickness, or roofing type has been changed by the engineer-in-charge for technical reasons after sanction.
- **Condition 3 — Scope Change:** Additional items of work (e.g., extra plinth protection, additional cattle stalls, extra rain water gutters) are now required.
- **Condition 4 — Quantity Variation:** Actual site quantities (e.g., rock encountered instead of soft soil in excavation) differ substantially from those originally assumed.
- **Condition 5 — Specifications Upgradation:** Specifications have been upgraded (e.g., from normal concrete to M30 grade) for quality or durability reasons.

---

## 2.4 Comparison Table — Original vs Revised vs Supplementary Estimate

This comparison is a **favourite KTU 2024 question** and must be memorized thoroughly.

| Parameter | Original Detailed Estimate | Revised Estimate | Supplementary Estimate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **When prepared** | Before the work begins. | After work has begun or design is finalized, when original is insufficient. | When *additional* work (not in original) is needed. |
| **Reason** | Initial administrative sanction. | Cost escalation > 5%, design/scope/specification change. | Genuine new requirement arises. |
| **Effect on original** | This IS the original. | *Replaces* the original. | *Additive* — original remains valid. |
| **Sanctioning Authority** | Competent authority as per delegation. | *Same or higher* authority than the one that sanctioned the original. | *Same* authority that sanctioned the original. |
| **Effect on Contract** | Forms basis of contract. | May require a fresh tender or supplementary agreement. | Usually via a separate tender or item-rate contract. |
| **Deduction of Old Items** | Not applicable. | Old items *to be omitted* must be clearly listed with deductions. | Not applicable (only new items). |
| **Sub-heads / Format** | Standard format. | Standard format, with comparative statement alongside original. | Standard format, framed as a *stand-alone* additional estimate. |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these six formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical on this module.**

$$
\text{Total Estimated Cost} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times R_i) \;+\; C \;+\; WC \;+\; OH
$$

Where:
- $Q_i$ = Quantity of item *i* (in appropriate units).
- $R_i$ = Rate per unit of item *i* (₹ per unit).
- $C$ = Contingencies (typically **3% to 5%** of the sum).
- $WC$ = Work-charged establishment (typically **1.5% to 2%**).
- $OH$ = Other overheads (architect's fee, departmental charges, etc., usually **2% to 5%**).

$$
\text{Plinth Area Estimate} \;=\; A_p \times R_p
$$

Where $A_p$ = Plinth area in m² and $R_p$ = Plinth area rate in ₹/m².

$$
\text{Cube Rate Estimate} \;=\; V \times R_c \;=\; (L \times B \times H) \times R_c
$$

Where $L, B, H$ are in metres and $R_c$ is the cube rate in ₹/m³.

$$
\text{Percentage Excess} \;=\; \frac{C_r - C_o}{C_o} \times 100
$$

Where $C_r$ = Revised cost and $C_o$ = Original sanctioned cost. A value **> 5%** triggers the need for a revised estimate.

$$
\text{Architect / Consultant Fee} \;=\; p_{\%} \times \text{Total Estimated Cost}
$$

Typical value of $p_{\%}$ is **2% to 5%** for farm structures as per KTU/Government norms.

$$
\text{Unit Rate of an Item} \;=\; (M + L + T + P) \times (1 + \tfrac{p}{100})
$$

Where:
- $M$ = Material cost per unit output.
- $L$ = Labour cost per unit output.
- $T$ = Tools and plant cost.
- $P$ = Profit (typically **10% to 15%**).
- $p$ = Profit percentage.

> **Note on notation:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is intentionally avoided inside tables; all separations are denoted using the LaTeX `\times` or `\vert` rendering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic: Which Estimate to Prepare?

Before preparing any estimate, an estimator must logically determine the *type* required. The decision tree below is what KTU examiners expect in long-form answers.

### 3.1.1 Decision Tree (Verbal)

**Step 1:** Is the work part of a *new* construction, or is it a *change/expansion* of an existing sanctioned work?

- If **new** → go to Step 2.
- If **change/expansion** of an existing sanctioned work → go to Step 3.

**Step 2:** Is the project in the *feasibility/sanction* stage or in the *execution* stage?

- If *feasibility* → prepare an **Approximate Estimate** (Plinth Area / Cube Rate / Unit Rate).
- If *execution* (design finalized, drawings ready) → prepare an **Original Detailed Estimate**.

**Step 3:** Is the original work still in progress, with only *additional* items needed?

- If **yes** → prepare a **Supplementary Estimate**.
- If **no** (work has stalled, or design itself is changed) → go to Step 4.

**Step 4:** Will the cost exceed 105% of the original sanctioned amount, OR has the design been materially changed?

- If **yes** → prepare a **Revised Estimate**.
- If **no** → the original estimate may continue to apply; record the variations in a *Deviation Statement*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Percentage Excess Calculation

This is the single most important calculation in the "Revised Estimate" topic.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> A farm godown was originally sanctioned at a detailed estimate of **₹ 12,00,000** in the year 2022. During execution, it was found necessary to:
> (a) Increase the foundation depth by 0.5 m due to weak soil,
> (b) Upgrade the concrete mix from M20 to M25,
> (c) Add a rain water harvesting system of ₹ 80,000.
>
> The revised total cost is computed as **₹ 13,80,000**. Determine the percentage excess and state whether a Revised Estimate is required.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write down the given data with units clearly.**

$$
C_o = \text{₹ } 12{,}00{,}000, \qquad C_r = \text{₹ } 13{,}80{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the percentage excess formula.**

$$
\text{Percentage Excess} = \frac{C_r - C_o}{C_o} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator (cost difference).**

$$
C_r - C_o = 13{,}80{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}80{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify the ratio.**

$$
\text{Percentage Excess} = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel and compute.**

$$
\text{Percentage Excess} = \frac{1.8}{12} \times 100 = 0.15 \times 100 = 15\,\%
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the KTU 5% trigger rule.**

$$
15\,\% \;>\; 5\,\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Revised Estimate is MANDATORY.}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since the cost has escalated by **15%**, which is well above the **5%** threshold prescribed by KTU/government norms, a **Revised Estimate must be prepared and re-sanctioned** by the competent authority before the additional expenditure is incurred.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Plinth Area vs Cube Rate Comparison

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

> A proposed **poultry house** has plinth dimensions **25 m × 10 m** with a height of **4.5 m to the eaves** and **6.0 m to the ridge** (gable roof). The plinth area rate in the locality is **₹ 18,000 per m²** and the cube rate is **₹ 4,500 per m³**. Compute both approximate estimates and determine which is more reliable (closer to the actual detailed cost of ₹ 22,00,000).

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Plinth Area.**

$$
A_p = L \times B = 25 \times 10 = 250 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Plinth Area Estimate.**

$$
E_p = A_p \times R_p = 250 \times 18{,}000 = \text{₹ } 45{,}00{,}000
$$

> *Observation:* The plinth area estimate gives ₹ 45,00,000, which is **more than double** the actual cost. This illustrates that plinth area rate must be taken from a *similar* building, otherwise the estimate is grossly inaccurate.

**Step 3 — Compute the average height for the cubical volume.**

For a gable roof, the *effective average height* is taken as the mean of the eaves and ridge heights:

$$
H_{avg} = \frac{H_e + H_r}{2} = \frac{4.5 + 6.0}{2} = \frac{10.5}{2} = 5.25 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Cubical Volume.**

$$
V = L \times B \times H_{avg} = 25 \times 10 \times 5.25 = 1312.5 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Cube Rate Estimate.**

$$
E_c = V \times R_c = 1312.5 \times 4{,}500 = \text{₹ } 59{,}06{,}250
$$

> *Observation:* The cube rate estimate gives ₹ 59,06,250, even higher.

**Step 6 — Compute the percentage errors.**

For Plinth Area Estimate:

$$
\% \text{ Error}_p = \frac{45{,}00{,}000 - 22{,}00{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 104.5\,\%
$$

For Cube Rate Estimate:

$$
\% \text{ Error}_c = \frac{59{,}06{,}250 - 22{,}00{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 168.5\,\%
$$

**Step 7 — Conclude which is more reliable.**

Since the plinth area estimate has a *smaller* percentage error (104.5% < 168.5%), it is **more reliable** — but both are unacceptable for actual sanction. This demonstrates the **inherent limitation of approximate estimates** and underscores why a *Detailed Estimate* (or a *Revised* one) is needed for final sanction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway from this Numerical**
> Approximate estimates are *order-of-magnitude* checks, not sanction documents. They serve only at the *feasibility* stage. For final sanction, the estimate must be *detailed* — and if circumstances change, a *revised* detailed estimate must be prepared.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Q: Define a "Revised Estimate". Under what circumstances is it prepared?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Revised Estimate** is a fresh detailed estimate that is re-prepared, in lieu of the original sanctioned detailed estimate, when the cost of the project is anticipated to exceed the originally sanctioned cost **by more than 5%**, or when there is a material change in design, specifications, scope, or quantities of work.
>
> It is prepared in the following circumstances:
> 1. **Cost escalation** beyond the **5% threshold** due to genuine market rate inflation.
> 2. **Design modification** by the engineer after sanction (e.g., deeper foundation).
> 3. **Scope addition** — new items of work not foreseen in the original.
> 4. **Quantity variation** discovered during execution (e.g., rock in excavation).
> 5. **Specification upgradation** (e.g., from M20 to M30 concrete).
>
> It must be re-sanctioned by the **same or higher competent authority** that sanctioned the original estimate.

*(Examiner's note: Students who omit the **5% threshold** typically lose 1 mark.)*

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Distinguish between a "Revised Estimate" and a "Supplementary Estimate".**

**Model Answer (Tabular Format Preferred):**

> | Sl. No. | Revised Estimate | Supplementary Estimate |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | Prepared when the *original* estimate is insufficient or design changes. | Prepared when *additional* works are needed *during* execution. |
> | 2 | *Replaces* the original estimate. | *Additive* to the original estimate. |
> | 3 | The original estimate becomes *inoperative*. | The original estimate *continues* unchanged. |
> | 4 | Triggered by cost escalation > **5%** or design change. | Triggered by a genuine *new requirement*. |
> | 5 | Old items to be omitted are listed with deductions. | Only new items are listed. |
> | 6 | Sanctioned by the *same or higher* authority. | Sanctioned by the *same* authority. |

*(Examiner's note: Tabular answers receive **full marks**; long paragraph answers risk missing key points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Always state the 5% rule explicitly** when answering questions on Revised Estimate. Many students lose 2 marks by merely saying "cost increased" without quantifying.
> 2. **Always mention the comparative statement** with the original estimate. This is a mandatory annexure to a Revised Estimate and examiners specifically look for it.
> 3. **Do not confuse "Revised" with "Supplementary"**. This is the single most common error. Memorize the rule: Revised = *replaces*; Supplementary = *adds*.
> 4. **Show units at every step** of numerical problems (₹, m², m³, %, etc.). Missing units can cost 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Use the standard format headings** — "Abstract of Estimate", "Contingencies", "Work-charged Establishment", "Grand Total".

---

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]

**Q (a):** Explain the various types of estimates used in farm structure projects. Differentiate clearly between Original Detailed Estimate, Revised Estimate, and Supplementary Estimate. **(7 marks)**

**(b):** A farm dairy building was originally sanctioned at an estimated cost of **₹ 18,50,000**. During construction, due to a change in flooring specification from cement concrete to vitrified tiles and addition of a solar water heating system, the revised cost is computed as **₹ 21,00,000**. Determine:
   (i) The absolute increase in cost.
   (ii) The percentage excess over the original.
   (iii) Whether a Revised Estimate is required as per KTU norms. Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B (a) — Types of Estimates (7 Marks)

**Types of estimates** in farm structure projects can be broadly classified as:

**1. Approximate Estimates** (used at feasibility stage):
- **Plinth Area Estimate:** $E = A_p \times R_p$
- **Cube Rate Estimate:** $E = L \times B \times H_{avg} \times R_c$
- **Approximate Quantity Method**
- **Cost per Unit Basis** (e.g., per animal, per m³ of storage)

**2. Detailed Estimates** (used at execution stage):
- **Original Detailed Estimate** — item-wise, prepared before construction.
- **Revised Estimate** — re-prepared when original escalates > 5% or design changes.
- **Supplementary Estimate** — for additional works; additive to original.
- **Repair and Maintenance Estimates** — for upkeep.
- **Quantity Estimate** — quantities only, no rates.

**Differentiation Table (Full Marks Awarded):**

| Aspect | Original Detailed | Revised | Supplementary |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage** | Before work begins. | During/after work begins. | During work. |
| **Reason** | Sanction of new work. | Cost > 105% or design change. | New additional work needed. |
| **Effect** | Establishes original budget. | *Replaces* original. | *Additive* to original. |
| **Sanctioning** | Competent authority. | Same or higher authority. | Same authority. |
| **Deductions** | None. | Old items *deducted*. | No deductions. |

---

#### Solution to B (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$$
C_o = \text{₹ } 18{,}50{,}000, \qquad C_r = \text{₹ } 21{,}00{,}000
$$

**Part (i) — Absolute increase in cost:**

$$
\Delta C = C_r - C_o = 21{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 2{,}50{,}000
$$

**Part (ii) — Percentage excess:**

$$
\% \text{ Excess} = \frac{\Delta C}{C_o} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{18{,}50{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Excess} = 0.13513 \times 100 = 13.51\,\%
$$

**Part (iii) — Decision and Justification:**

> As per KTU/government norms, a **Revised Estimate is required** when the cost escalation exceeds **5%** of the original sanctioned cost.
>
> Here, the percentage excess is **13.51%**, which is **substantially greater than 5%**.
>
> Therefore, a **Revised Estimate is MANDATORY**, and the additional expenditure of ₹ 2,50,000 **cannot be incurred** without obtaining fresh sanction from the competent authority.
>
> The Revised Estimate must include:
> - Re-quantification of all affected items (flooring).
> - New rate analysis for vitrified tiles.
> - Addition of solar water heating system as a new item.
> - A **comparative statement** showing the original vs. revised item-wise costs.
> - Fresh contingencies and work-charged establishment calculated on the revised base cost.

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]

**Q (a):** Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Estimate, (ii) Estimation, (iii) Rate Analysis, (iv) Bill of Quantities, (v) Schedule of Rates. **(7 marks)**

**(b):** Compute the plinth area estimate and the cube rate estimate for a proposed **farm godown** having length **30 m**, width **12 m**, eaves height **5 m**, and ridge height **7.5 m** (gable roof). The plinth area rate is **₹ 20,000/m²** and the cube rate is **₹ 5,000/m³**. Also comment on the relative accuracy of the two methods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B — Choice B (a) — Definitions (7 Marks)

**(i) Estimate:** A calculated approximation of the probable cost of a proposed engineering work prepared *before* construction. *Example:* Estimated cost of a farm godown = ₹ 25,00,000.

**(ii) Estimation:** The *process* or *art* of computing quantities of items of work and their cost. *Example:* Taking off quantities of earthwork, concrete, and steel from drawings.

**(iii) Rate Analysis:** The procedure to determine the unit rate of an item of work by summing the costs of materials, labour, plant, and contractor's profit. *Example:* Rate of 1 m³ of RCC = ₹ 6,500 (materials + labour + profit).

**(iv) Bill of Quantities (BOQ):** A tabular document listing every item of work, its quantity, rate, and total amount (Quantity × Rate). *Example:* 25 m³ × ₹ 6,500 = ₹ 1,62,500 for RCC in a footing.

**(v) Schedule of Rates (SOR):** A government-published document giving standardized unit rates for items of work in a given region and year. *Example:* PWD SOR 2024 for Kerala giving ₹/m³ for various concrete grades.

---

#### Solution to B — Choice B (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$$
L = 30 \text{ m}, \quad B = 12 \text{ m}, \quad H_e = 5 \text{ m}, \quad H_r = 7.5 \text{ m}
$$
$$
R_p = \text{₹ } 20{,}000 / \text{m}^2, \quad R_c = \text{₹ } 5{,}000 / \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 1 — Plinth Area:**

$$
A_p = L \times B = 30 \times 12 = 360 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Plinth Area Estimate:**

$$
E_p = A_p \times R_p = 360 \times 20{,}000 = \text{₹ } 72{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Average height of gable roof:**

$$
H_{avg} = \frac{H_e + H_r}{2} = \frac{5 + 7.5}{2} = 6.25 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Cubical volume:**

$$
V = L \times B \times H_{avg} = 30 \times 12 \times 6.25 = 2250 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Cube Rate Estimate:**

$$
E_c = V \times R_c = 2250 \times 5{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}12{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Comment on relative accuracy:**

> The **plinth area method** is generally more accurate than the cube rate method for low-rise farm buildings because:
> - It directly captures the *covered built-up area* which is the primary cost driver.
> - The cube rate method can over-estimate when the building is tall and has large unused attic space (as in a gable-roofed godown where the triangular gable volume adds to cubage but contributes little usable area).
>
> In this numerical, $E_p = ₹ 72$ lakhs while $E_c = ₹ 1.125$ crores, showing a **56% over-estimation** by the cube rate method for a gable-roofed structure.
>
> **Conclusion:** For farm godowns with gable roofs, the **plinth area method is preferred** as it is closer to the actual cost and is less sensitive to roof geometry.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

### A. Definitional Anchors
- **Estimate** = probable cost (document). **Estimation** = process of preparing it. **Cost** = actual expenditure.
- **Rate Analysis** must always add **profit at 10–15%** on the bare cost (M + L + T).
- **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** lists every item with quantity, rate, and amount.
- **Schedule of Rates (SOR)** is the government-published standardized rate book.

### B. Classification of Estimates
- **Approximate** = quick, low accuracy (Plinth, Cube, Unit, Quantity).
- **Detailed** = item-by-item, high accuracy.
- **Detailed sub-types:** Original, **Revised**, Supplementary, ARM, Special Repair, Quantity.

### C. Revised Estimate — Key Triggers
- **5% cost escalation rule** (most important numerical trigger).
- Material design / specification / scope / quantity change.
- Must include a **comparative statement** with the original.

### D. Revised vs Supplementary — The Golden Rule
- **Revised = Replaces the original.**
- **Supplementary = Adds to the original.**

### E. Numerical Triggers
- $E_p = A_p \times R_p$ — Plinth area estimate.
- $E_c = L \times B \times H_{avg} \times R_c$ — Cube rate estimate (use $H_{avg}$ for gable roofs).
- $\% \text{ Excess} = \dfrac{C_r - C_o}{C_o} \times 100$ — the **5%** test.

### F. Standard Percentages to Memorize
- **Contingencies:** 3–5% of base cost.
- **Work-charged establishment:** 1.5–2%.
- **Departmental/Overhead charges:** 2–5%.
- **Contractor's profit (in rate analysis):** 10–15%.
- **Water supply & sanitary charges (in buildings):** ~5%.
- **Electrical charges (in buildings):** ~5%.
- **Architect's fee:** 2–5%.

### G. Format Reminders for KTU Valuation
- Always begin a Detailed Estimate with a **"Report"** paragraph.
- Always end with a **"Grand Total"** rounded to the nearest rupee.
- Always attach a **"Comparative Statement"** when the estimate is a Revised one.
- Always use **standard units**: m, m², m³, kg, quintal, ₹.

### H. Common Traps in KTU Papers
- ❌ Confusing Plinth Area with Carpet Area.
- ❌ Forgetting to add 0.6 m (2 ft) projection for plinth in stepped foundations.
- ❌ Using **total height** (ridge) directly in cube rate for gable roofs — must use **average height**.
- ❌ Writing "Revised Estimate" when the question actually describes a "Supplementary" scenario (and vice versa).
- ❌ Omitting the **5%** figure when justifying a Revised Estimate.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When a numerical problem gives both old and new costs, *immediately compute the percentage excess* and *state the 5% rule*. This single act is worth 2–3 marks and demonstrates examiner-friendly clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Supplementary Estimate** is a detailed estimate prepared when **additional works** (which were *not* originally contemplated in the sanctioned estimate) become necessary during the execution of a project, or when substantial additions/alterations are required beyond the scope of the originally approved design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> "A *Supplementary Estimate* is a fresh detailed estimate submitted to obtain *fresh administrative and technical sanction* when a work already in progress requires additional funds due to **new works**, **additions**, or **substantial modifications** that could not have been foreseen at the time of preparing the original estimate."

It is governed by Clause **CPWD Works Manual – Para 2.7** and the **Kerala PWD Manual provisions** for deviation and extra items.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy: The Wedding Budget Problem**
>
> Imagine you planned a wedding with a budget of ₹5 Lakhs (this is your **Original Estimate**). After the wedding, your parents suddenly say, *"We want a grand reception for 500 people, plus a honeymoon trip to Goa."* You now need to prepare a **fresh, detailed cost sheet** for these NEW items, even though the original wedding is already over.
>
> That fresh cost sheet is exactly what a **Supplementary Estimate** is in construction!
> - The **Original Estimate** covered the *known* work.
> - The **Supplementary Estimate** covers *new/extra* work that came up *afterwards*.

Another real example in farm structures: After constructing a **cattle shed** (original work), the farmer later wants to add a **fodder storage room** and a **milking parlour** attached to it. Since these were not in the original sanctioned plan, a Supplementary Estimate is mandatory.

---

## 1.3 Key Trigger Conditions for Supplementary Estimate

The following situations compulsorily demand a Supplementary Estimate:

| S. No. | Trigger Condition | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unforeseen Additions** | New structures/facilities needed after the original estimate is sanctioned. |
| 2 | **Substantial Alterations** | Major design deviations (e.g., changing a thatched roof to an RCC roof). |
| 3 | **Change in Scope** | The functional requirement of the structure changes (e.g., poultry shed to a goat pen). |
| 4 | **Major Quantities** | Quantity of an item increases beyond **30%** of the original provision (per CPWD norms). |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the "30% Rule" (CPWD):** If quantity of any item deviates by more than **30%** from the original sanctioned estimate, fresh sanction (often as a Supplementary Estimate) is mandatory.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Administrative Sanction Authority:** The authority **same as or higher** than the one that sanctioned the original estimate.
- **Detailed Estimate Format:** Same as a fresh detailed estimate (DSR-based rates for current year).
- **Supporting Documents:** Report explaining necessity, revised drawings, specification sheets, and rate analysis.
- **Time Limit:** Must be submitted **before incurring the expenditure**, never retrospectively.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A Supplementary Estimate is **NOT a revision** of the original estimate — it is a **brand-new, independent detailed estimate** for *additional scope*. Let us break it down step by step:

### Step 1: Identification of Additional Work
The Junior Engineer / Site Engineer identifies works *not covered* in the original sanctioned estimate. These can be:
- **New Items** (e.g., additional farm building, water tank, fencing)
- **Extra Quantities** beyond the permissible deviation limits
- **Altered Specifications** (e.g., higher grade of concrete, larger foundation depth)

### Step 2: Preparation of Fresh Detailed Estimate
- Take off **fresh quantities** from the additional drawings.
- Apply **Current Schedule of Rates (DSR / Kerala PWD SoR)** of the *year in which the supplementary work is to be executed* (NOT the original estimate year).
- Add a **lump-sum contingency of 3% to 5%** as per KTU / CPWD norms.
- Apply **works charges, establishment charges, and GST** as per current schedule.

### Step 3: Supporting Technical Report
A written report must accompany the estimate stating:
- **Why** the supplementary work is required
- **Why** it was not included in the original estimate
- **What** alternatives were considered
- **How** the rate analysis was done

### Step 4: Sanctioning Authority
- Must be the **same level or higher** than the original sanctioning authority.
- For farm structures under KTU/State Agriculture Department: Typically the **Executive Engineer** or **Chief Engineer** depending on the total project cost.

### Step 5: Administrative Approval
After sanction, a **fresh Agreement** may be executed, or the supplementary work is added to the existing contract through a **supplementary agreement**.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Supplementary Estimate — Engineering Rationale
