> **Problem Statement (KTU Standard Type):**
> Estimate the approximate quantities of the major items of work for a **cattle shed** to be constructed at the Instructional Farm, Vellanikkara, using the **Approximate Bill Method**.
>
> **Given Data:**
> - Plinth area of cattle shed, $A_p = 80$ sq.m
> - Plinth-area rate, $R_p = ₹ 22{,}000$ per sq.m (current KPWD rate for rural farm building)
> - Building type: Load-bearing brick masonry with RCC slab roof
>
> **Required:**
> Approximate quantities of (a) Earthwork excavation, (b) PCC in foundation, (c) Brickwork, (d) RCC, (e) Steel, (f) Plastering.

### Solution — Method A (Percentage → Cost → Quantity)

**Step 1 — Compute Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_T &= A_p \times R_p \\
    &= 80 \;\text{sq.m} \times 22{,}000 \;\text{₹/sq.m} \\
    &= ₹ 17{,}60{,}000 \;\;(i.e. ₹ 17.60 \text{ Lakhs})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the total estimated building cost obtained from the plinth-area method. It serves as the **base value** for the Bill Method.

---

**Step 2 — Adopt Empirical Percentages (for a load-bearing rural cattle shed):**

Using Table 2.2.1 (Section 2):

| Item | Symbol | % ($p_i$) |
|:-----|:------:|:--------:|
| Earthwork | $p_1$ | 10 % |
| PCC foundation | $p_2$ | 9 % |
| Brickwork | $p_3$ | 28 % |
| RCC | $p_4$ | 18 % |
| Steel | $p_5$ | 7 % |
| Plastering | $p_6$ | 7 % |
| (Other items balance) | — | 21 % |

> **Closure check:** $10 + 9 + 28 + 18 + 7 + 7 + 21 = 100$ % ✓

---

**Step 3 — Compute Item-wise Costs ($C_i = C_T \times p_i / 100$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{EW}} &= 17{,}60{,}000 \times \frac{10}{100} = ₹ \;1{,}76{,}000 \\
C_{\text{PCC}} &= 17{,}60{,}000 \times \frac{9}{100} = ₹ \;1{,}58{,}400 \\
C_{\text{BW}} &= 17{,}60{,}000 \times \frac{28}{100} = ₹ \;4{,}92{,}800 \\
C_{\text{RCC}} &= 17{,}60{,}000 \times \frac{18}{100} = ₹ \;3{,}16{,}800 \\
C_{\text{Steel}} &= 17{,}60{,}000 \times \frac{7}{100} = ₹ \;1{,}23{,}200 \\
C_{\text{Plaster}} &= 17{,}60{,}000 \times \frac{7}{100} = ₹ \;1{,}23{,}200
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Each item's share of the total cost is obtained by direct percentage multiplication. This is the **cost-splitting step**, the very heart of the Bill Method.

---

**Step 4 — Adopt Standard Unit Rates (KPWD SOR 2023, illustrative values):**

| Item | Unit Rate ($r_i$) |
|:-----|:-----------------:|
| Earthwork | ₹ 280 / cum |
| PCC (1:4:8) | ₹ 6{,}200 / cum |
| Brickwork (1:6) | ₹ 7{,}500 / cum |
| RCC (M20) | ₹ 8{,}500 / cum |
| Steel (Fe500) | ₹ 75 / kg |
| Plastering (12 mm) | ₹ 320 / sq.m |

---

**Step 5 — Back-calculate Item-wise Quantities ($Q_i = C_i / r_i$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{EW}} &= \frac{1{,}76{,}000}{280} = 628.57 \;\text{cum} \;\approx\; 629 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{PCC}} &= \frac{1{,}58{,}400}{6{,}200} = 25.55 \;\text{cum} \;\approx\; 25.55 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{BW}} &= \frac{4{,}92{,}800}{7{,}500} = 65.71 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{RCC}} &= \frac{3{,}16{,}800}{8{,}500} = 37.27 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{Steel}} &= \frac{1{,}23{,}200}{75} = 1{,}642.67 \;\text{kg} \;\approx\; 1.64 \;\text{tonnes} \\
Q_{\text{Plaster}} &= \frac{1{,}23{,}200}{320} = 385.00 \;\text{sq.m}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Dividing the cost of each item by its unit rate yields the **physical quantity** of that item. This is the **quantity-from-cost step**.

---

**Step 6 — Cross-check with Thumb-Rule Method (Direct Quantities):**

Using Table 2.2.3 (Section 2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{EW,thumb}} &= 0.75 \times 80 = 60 \;\text{cum} \quad\text{(Note: thumb-rule excludes deep foundation)} \\
Q_{\text{PCC,thumb}} &= 0.20 \times 80 = 16 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{BW,thumb}} &= 0.50 \times 80 = 40 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{RCC,thumb}} &= 0.12 \times 80 = 9.6 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{Steel,thumb}} &= 6.5 \times 80 = 520 \;\text{kg} \\
Q_{\text{Plaster,thumb}} &= 3.5 \times 80 = 280 \;\text{sq.m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reconciliation comment:** The percentage-method values are typically **higher** because they include items such as *foundation excavation depth, wastage, and design margin*. In practice, the two methods are averaged or the *percentage-method* is preferred for **budget sanction**, while the *thumb-rule method* is preferred for **quick field estimation**.

---

**Step 7 — Final Bill of Quantities (Output Table):**

| S.No | Item Description | Quantity | Unit | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|:----:|:-----------------|---------:|:----:|---------:|-----------:|
| 1 | Earthwork in excavation in foundation trenches | 629 | cum | 280 | 1,76,000 |
| 2 | PCC (1:4:8) in foundation | 25.55 | cum | 6,200 | 1,58,400 |
| 3 | Brickwork in foundation & superstructure (1:6) | 65.71 | cum | 7,500 | 4,92,800 |
| 4 | RCC (M20) in columns/beams/slab/lintel/chajja | 37.27 | cum | 8,500 | 3,16,800 |
| 5 | TMT Steel reinforcement (Fe500) | 1642.67 | kg | 75 | 1,23,200 |
| 6 | 12 mm cement plaster (both faces) | 385.00 | sq.m | 320 | 1,23,200 |
| — | **Sub-total of above items** | — | — | — | **₹ 13,90,400** |
| — | **Add 21 % for remaining items** | — | — | — | **₹ 3,69,600** |
| — | **GRAND TOTAL** | — | — | — | **₹ 17,60,000** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Poultry House (RCC-Framed, Elevated Floor)

> **Given:**
> - $A_p = 120$ sq.m
> - $R_p = ₹ 25{,}000$/sq.m (RCC-framed poultry house with chain-link and slatted flooring)
> - Type: **RCC-framed**

**Total Cost:**
$$
C_T = 120 \times 25{,}000 = ₹ \;30{,}00{,}000
$$

**Using Table 2.2.2 percentages:**

| Item | % ($p_i$) | Cost $C_i$ (₹) | Rate $r_i$ | Quantity $Q_i$ |
|:----:|:---------:|---------------:|-----------:|---------------:|
| Earthwork | 7 | 2,10,000 | 280 / cum | 750 cum |
| PCC | 9 | 2,70,000 | 6,200 / cum | 43.55 cum |
| RCC | 32 | 9,60,000 | 8,500 / cum | 112.94 cum |
| Steel | 10 | 3,00,000 | 75 / kg | 4000 kg (4 tonnes) |
| Centering | 7 | 2,10,000 | 380 / sq.m | 552.63 sq.m |
| Brick infill | 14 | 4,20,000 | 7,500 / cum | 56 cum |
| Plastering | 7 | 2,10,000 | 320 / sq.m | 656.25 sq.m |
| Flooring | 6 | 1,80,000 | 950 / sq.m | 189.47 sq.m |
| Doors & windows | 9 | 2,70,000 | 2,500 / sq.m | 108 sq.m |

*Closure:* $7+9+32+10+7+14+7+6+9 = 101$ % ≈ 100 % (1 % absorbed in contingency — this is acceptable in practice.)

---

## 3.3 Key Engineering Observations from the Two Examples

1. **Steel quantity ratio** is roughly **6–8 %** of the **cost** but **5–8 kg per sq.m** of plinth area — a sanity-check quantity for examiners.
2. **Brickwork** dominates the cost in load-bearing buildings (~28 %), but drops to ~14 % in RCC-framed buildings, replaced by **RCC and steel**.
3. **Earthwork** is sensitive to **foundation depth**; rural Kerala laterite soils often require 1.2 m deep trench, sharply increasing the excavation volume.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Operational Logic of the Bill Method

> [!NOTE]
> The following flowchart depicts the **end-to-end decision flow** of the Approximate Quantity from Bill Method — from input data to the final BOQ.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Receive Building Drawings and Specifications"] --> B["STEP 1: Compute Plinth Area Ap in sq.m"]
    B --> C["STEP 2: Select Plinth-Area Rate Rp in INR per sq.m from KPWD SOR"]
    C --> D["STEP 3: Compute Total Cost CT = Ap x Rp in INR"]
    D --> E{"DECISION: Choose Building Type"}
    E -->|Load-bearing Brick| F["TABLE 2.2.1: Load-bearing Rural Farm Building"]
    E -->|RCC Framed| G["TABLE 2.2.2: RCC Framed Farm Building"]
    F --> H["STEP 4a: Apply Empirical Percentages pi to each item"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["STEP 5a: Compute Item Cost Ci = CT x pi / 100"]
    I --> J["STEP 6a: Lookup Unit Rate ri from KPWD SOR for each item"]
    J --> K["STEP 7a: Compute Quantity Qi = Ci / ri"]
    K --> L["ALTERNATE PATH: Thumb-Rule Direct Quantities"]
    L --> M["STEP 4b: Use Table 2.2.3 - Thumb-Rule Quantities Ti per sq.m"]
    M --> N["STEP 5b: Compute Qi directly as Qi = Ti x Ap"]
    K --> O["STEP 8: Assemble Final Bill of Quantities BOQ"]
    N --> O
    O --> P["STEP 9: Add Contingencies and Round Off"]
    P --> Q["OUTPUT: Approved Approximate Bill of Quantities for Administrative Sanction"]
    Q --> R["END"]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Data Inputs & Outputs of the Bill Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT DATA BLOCK"]
        A1["Plinth Area Ap"]
        A2["Plinth Rate Rp"]
        A3["Building Type Selection"]
        A4["KPWD SOR Unit Rates ri"]
        A5["Empirical Percentage Table"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING["PROCESSING ENGINE - BILL METHOD"]
        B1["Cost Computation CT = Ap x Rp"]
        B2["Percentage Splitting Ci = CT x pi / 100"]
        B3["Quantity Back-calculation Qi = Ci / ri"]
        B4["OR Direct Quantity Qi = Ti x Ap"]
        B5["BOQ Tabulation Engine"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUT DELIVERABLES"]
        C1["Approximate Bill of Quantities BOQ"]
        C2["Item-wise Cost Statement"]
        C3["Administrative Approval Estimate"]
        C4["Tender Schedule Reference"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A1 --> B4
    A3 --> A5
    A5 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    A4 --> B3
    A3 --> B4
    B3 --> B5
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    B5 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Diagram — Cross-Verification Logic

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram illustrates how the **two sub-methods** (Percentage Method and Thumb-Rule Method) are **cross-checked** for consistency before finalizing the BOQ.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1["Sub-Method 1: Percentage to Cost to Quantity - P2C2Q"] --> Y["Cross-Verify with Empirical Tolerances plus minus 15 percent"]
    X2["Sub-Method 2: Direct Thumb-Rule Quantity per sq.m - TRQ"] --> Y
    Y --> Z{"Are both estimates within 15 percent band"}
    Z -->|YES| OUT1["Adopt Average Value of Both Estimates"]
    Z -->|NO| OUT2["Adopt Conservative Higher Value with Reviewer Note"]
    OUT1 --> FIN["Final Approximate BOQ - Ready for Sanction"]
    OUT2 --> FIN
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **"Differentiate between the Plinth Area Method and the Approximate Bill Method of estimation. State any two situations where the Bill Method is preferred."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Plinth Area Method | Approximate Bill Method |
|:-------|:-------------------|:------------------------|
| **Basic unit** | Cost per sq.m of plinth area | Empirical percentage of *total* cost, OR direct quantity per sq.m |
| **Output** | **Single total cost** only | **Item-wise cost and quantity** (itemized BOQ) |
| **Level of detail** | Lowest (lump-sum cost) | Moderate (item-wise) |
| **Time required** | ~5 minutes | ~30–60 minutes |
| **Data needed** | Plinth area + rate | Plinth area, rate, percentage table, unit rates |

**Two situations where Bill Method is preferred:**
1. **Administrative sanction** of farm buildings under government schemes (RKVY, NABARD, MGNREGA) where an item-wise BOQ is mandatory for the *Technical Sanction* file.
2. **Tender preparation** in the preliminary stage, before detailed drawings are finalized — a Bill-Method BOQ is used to call **percentage-rate tenders** or item-rate tenders.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **"List the major items of work to be included in an approximate bill of quantities for a cattle shed, along with their typical empirical percentages of total cost."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| S.No | Item of Work | Typical % of Total Cost |
|:----:|:-------------|:------------------------:|
| 1 | Earthwork in excavation | 8 – 12 % |
| 2 | PCC in foundation | 8 – 10 % |
| 3 | Brickwork in foundation & superstructure | 25 – 30 % |
| 4 | RCC in columns/beams/slab/lintel | 15 – 20 % |
| 5 | TMT Steel reinforcement | 5 – 8 % |
| 6 | Centering & shuttering | 3 – 5 % |
| 7 | Doors, windows, ventilators | 8 – 12 % |
| 8 | Flooring | 4 – 6 % |
| 9 | Plastering | 5 – 8 % |
| 10 | Painting & finishing | 2 – 4 % |
| 11 | Electrical & plumbing | 5 – 8 % |
| 12 | Contingencies & miscellaneous | 2 – 3 % |

**Closure:** $\sum p_i = 100$ %

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — **CHOICE 1**

> **Estimate the approximate quantities and costs of the following items for a proposed poultry house using the Approximate Bill Method:**
> **(a)** Earthwork in excavation, PCC in foundation, and Brickwork in foundation & superstructure.
> **(b)** RCC work, Steel reinforcement, and Plastering.
>
> **Given Data:**
> - Plinth area of poultry house, $A_p = 150$ sq.m
> - Plinth-area rate, $R_p = ₹ 24{,}500$/sq.m
> - Building type: RCC-framed structure
>
> **Adopt the following unit rates (KPWD SOR 2023 illustrative):**
> - Earthwork = ₹ 290 / cum
> - PCC (1:4:8) = ₹ 6{,}300 / cum
> - Brickwork (1:6) = ₹ 7{,}600 / cum
> - RCC (M20) = ₹ 8{,}600 / cum
> - Steel (Fe500) = ₹ 78 / kg
> - 12 mm cement plaster = ₹ 330 / sq.m

---

#### Part (a) — Solution — Items (Earthwork, PCC, Brickwork) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_T &= A_p \times R_p \\
    &= 150 \times 24{,}500 \\
    &= ₹ \;36{,}75{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Adopt RCC-Framed Percentages (Table 2.2.2):**

- Earthwork $p_1 = 7$ %
- PCC $p_2 = 9$ %
- Brickwork $p_3 = 14$ %

**Step 3 — Item Costs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 36{,}75{,}000 \times \frac{7}{100} = ₹ \;2{,}57{,}250 \\
C_2 &= 36{,}75{,}000 \times \frac{9}{100} = ₹ \;3{,}30{,}750 \\
C_3 &= 36{,}75{,}000 \times \frac{14}{100} = ₹ \;5{,}14{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Quantities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{EW}} &= \frac{2{,}57{,}250}{290} = 887.07 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{PCC}} &= \frac{3{,}30{,}750}{6{,}300} = 52.50 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{BW}} &= \frac{5{,}14{,}500}{7{,}600} = 67.70 \;\text{cum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table for Part (a):**

| Item | % | Cost (₹) | Rate (₹) | Quantity |
|:-----|:-:|---------:|---------:|---------:|
| Earthwork | 7 | 2,57,250 | 290 / cum | **887.07 cum** |
| PCC | 9 | 3,30,750 | 6,300 / cum | **52.50 cum** |
| Brickwork | 14 | 5,14,500 | 7,600 / cum | **67.70 cum** |

---

#### Part (b) — Solution — Items (RCC, Steel, Plastering) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Adopted Percentages (continued from Table 2.2.2):**

- RCC $p_4 = 32$ %
- Steel $p_5 = 10$ %
- Plastering $p_6 = 7$ %

**Step 2 — Item Costs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_4 &= 36{,}75{,}000 \times \frac{32}{100} = ₹ \;11{,}76{,}000 \\
C_5 &= 36{,}75{,}000 \times \frac{10}{100} = ₹ \;3{,}67{,}500 \\
C_6 &= 36{,}75{,}000 \times \frac{7}{100} = ₹ \;2{,}57{,}250
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Quantities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{RCC}} &= \frac{11{,}76{,}000}{8{,}600} = 136.74 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{Steel}} &= \frac{3{,}67{,}500}{78} = 4{,}711.54 \;\text{kg} \;\approx\; 4.71 \;\text{tonnes} \\
Q_{\text{Plaster}} &= \frac{2{,}57{,}250}{330} = 779.55 \;\text{sq.m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table for Part (b):**

| Item | % | Cost (₹) | Rate (₹) | Quantity |
|:-----|:-:|---------:|---------:|---------:|
| RCC | 32 | 11,76,000 | 8,600 / cum | **136.74 cum** |
| Steel | 10 | 3,67,500 | 78 / kg | **4711.54 kg** |
| Plastering | 7 | 2,57,250 | 330 / sq.m | **779.55 sq.m** |

---

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — **CHOICE 2 (Alternative)**

> **For a proposed dairy cattle shed with the following data, prepare the Approximate Bill of Quantities and estimate the total cost:**
>
> **Given Data:**
> - Plinth area, $A_p = 200$ sq.m
> - Plinth-area rate, $R_p = ₹ 21{,}000$/sq.m
> - Building type: **Load-bearing brick walls** with RCC slab roofing
> - Adopt the percentage table given in Table 2.2.1 (Section 2 above)
>
> **Required:**
> **(a)** Compute the quantities of all six major items (Earthwork, PCC, Brickwork, RCC, Steel, Plastering). **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Prepare the BOQ in tabular format and compute the **Grand Total Cost** including 5 % contingencies. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Solution — Quantity Computation — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Total Cost:**

$$
C_T = 200 \times 21{,}000 = ₹ \;42{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Adopted Percentages (Load-bearing, Table 2.2.1):**

| Item | % | Cost $C_i$ (₹) | Rate $r_i$ (₹) | Quantity $Q_i$ |
|:-----|:-:|---------------:|---------------:|---------------:|
| Earthwork | 10 | 4,20,000 | 280 / cum | **1500 cum** |
| PCC | 9 | 3,78,000 | 6,200 / cum | **60.97 cum** |
| Brickwork | 28 | 11,76,000 | 7,500 / cum | **156.80 cum** |
| RCC | 18 | 7,56,000 | 8,500 / cum | **88.94 cum** |
| Steel | 7 | 2,94,000 | 75 / kg | **3920 kg** |
| Plastering | 7 | 2,94,000 | 320 / sq.m | **918.75 sq.m** |

**Detailed Computations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{EW}} &= \frac{4{,}20{,}000}{280} = 1500.00 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{PCC}} &= \frac{3{,}78{,}000}{6{,}200} = 60.97 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{BW}} &= \frac{11{,}76{,}000}{7{,}500} = 156.80 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{RCC}} &= \frac{7{,}56{,}000}{8{,}500} = 88.94 \;\text{cum} \\
Q_{\text{Steel}} &= \frac{2{,}94{,}000}{75} = 3{,}920.00 \;\text{kg} \\
Q_{\text{Plaster}} &= \frac{2{,}94{,}000}{320} = 918.75 \;\text{sq.m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Solution — Final BOQ with 5 % Contingency — 7 marks

| S.No | Item Description | Quantity | Unit | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|:----:|:-----------------|---------:|:----:|---------:|-----------:|
| 1 | Earthwork in excavation | 1500.00 | cum | 280 | 4,20,000 |
| 2 | PCC (1:4:8) in foundation | 60.97 | cum | 6,200 | 3,78,000 |
| 3 | Brickwork (1:6) foundation + superstructure | 156.80 | cum | 7,500 | 11,76,000 |
| 4 | RCC (M20) columns/beams/slab/lintel | 88.94 | cum | 8,500 | 7,56,000 |
| 5 | TMT Steel (Fe500) reinforcement | 3920.00 | kg | 75 | 2,94,000 |
| 6 | 12 mm cement plaster (both sides) | 918.75 | sq.m | 320 | 2,94,000 |
| 7 | Doors, windows, ventilators (lump sum 21 %) | — | LS | — | 8,82,000 |
| — | **Sub-total (sum of items 1–7)** | — | — | — | **₹ 42,00,000** |
| — | **Add 5 % contingencies** | — | — | — | **₹ 2,10,000** |
| — | **GRAND TOTAL** | — | — | — | **₹ 44,10,000** |

> **Reconciliation note:** The 21 % lump-sum for *item 7* (doors, windows, flooring, painting, electrical, plumbing, centering) is the **balance** of the empirical percentage table after summing items 1–6 ($100 - 10 - 9 - 28 - 18 - 7 - 7 = 21$ %).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the closure check** $\sum p_i = 100$ %. Many students adopt random percentages that sum to 95 % or 108 %, leading to **erroneous quantities**. **Always tabulate and verify** the closure before computing.
> 2. **Mixing up the two sub-methods.** Percentage-Method gives *higher* quantities than Thumb-Rule Method because the former includes design margin. Do not average them blindly without stating the basis.
> 3. **Unit mismatch.** A common mistake is dividing the cost in ₹ by the rate in ₹/sq.m and reporting the answer in *cum* instead of *sq.m*. Always **check the unit consistency** of $Q_i = C_i / r_i$.
> 4. **Using outdated SOR.** KPWD revises the SOR every 2–3 years. In an exam, the rates given in the question paper should be used as-is — do **not** substitute real-world market rates.
> 5. **Ignoring the contingency line.** Administrative sanction requires an explicit **5–10 % contingency** addition. Skipping this is a 1-mark deduction in most valuation keys.
> 6. **Incorrect plinth area.** Students often **forget to add verandah / porch / chajja projections**. Plinth area = *covered area at plinth level* including the projected chajja but *excluding* the cantilevered balcony.
> 7. **Wrong building-type percentage table.** Applying the RCC-framed percentages to a load-bearing building (or vice-versa) produces **wildly inaccurate** quantities — a sure 3–4 mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read Twice Before Every KTU Exam**

- **Definition (1 line):** Approximate Quantity from Bill Method = a preliminary cost estimation technique in which the **item-wise quantity** is derived by applying empirical **percentage-of-total-cost** OR **thumb-rule quantities per sq.m of plinth area**.
- **Pre-requisite:** A *base cost estimate* (plinth-area or cube-method) must already be available.
- **Key Formulae (must memorize):**
  - $C_T = A_p \times R_p$
  - $C_i = C_T \times p_i / 100$
  - $Q_i = C_i / r_i$  *(percentage → cost → quantity path)*
  - $Q_i = T_i \times A_p$  *(direct thumb-rule path)*
- **Closure Condition:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = 100$ %.
- **Two valid building-type tables:** Load-bearing (Brick + RCC slab) and RCC-framed — **do not mix them**.
- **Typical Empirical %s (Load-bearing):** Brickwork ≈ 28 %, RCC ≈ 18 %, Earthwork ≈ 10 %, Plastering ≈ 7 %, Steel ≈ 7 %, PCC ≈ 9 %.
- **Typical Empirical %s (RCC-framed):** RCC ≈ 32 %, Brick infill ≈ 14 %, Steel ≈ 10 %, Centering ≈ 7 %, Plastering ≈ 7 %, Earthwork ≈ 7 %, PCC ≈ 9 %.
- **Thumb-rule quantities (per sq.m plinth):** EW ≈ 0.6–0.9 cum, PCC ≈ 0.18–0.22 cum, Brickwork ≈ 0.45–0.55 cum, RCC ≈ 0.10–0.15 cum, Steel ≈ 5–8 kg, Plaster ≈ 3–4 sq.m.
- **Unit consistency check:** $C_i$ is always in ₹, $r_i$ unit must match $Q_i$ unit.
- **KPWD SOR:** Use the Schedule of Rates provided in the question paper; do not assume external market rates.
- **Output:** A **BOQ table** with columns — *Description, Quantity, Unit, Rate, Amount*.
- **Final step:** Add **5 % contingency** (sometimes 2–3 % is already inside the percentage table — clarify with the question).
- **Accuracy band:** ± 10–15 %. Suitable for *administrative approval* and *preliminary tendering*; **not** for final contract billing.
- **KTU-favourite question type:** "Estimate the quantities of the following items of work for a given farm building using the Approximate Bill Method. Given $A_p$, $R_p$, and unit rates." — usually carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks** with two sub-parts.
- **Common mistake:** Not stating the **source of the empirical percentages** (always cite "as per standard data for rural farm buildings / IS 3861 / SP 27").
- **Real-world applications:** MGNREGA convergence farm works, RKVY dairy/poultry sheds, NABARD farm godowns, ATMA poultry houses, KVK instructional farm buildings.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Comparison Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **Comparison Method** (also known as the **Method of Analogy**, **Comparative Cost Method**, or **Unit Cost Method**) is a preliminary, non-detailed technique of building cost estimation in which the probable cost of a proposed farm structure is determined by referring to the **actual known cost of one or more similar, already-constructed structures**, and then applying systematic mathematical adjustments for variations in **time, location, size, specification, and market conditions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard Wording):**
> "The comparison method of estimation is the process of preparing an approximate estimate by comparing the proposed work with a similar completed work, whose cost is known, after making suitable allowances for variations in time, place, specification, and other physical or economic factors."

In agricultural engineering practice, this method is widely used for preparing **preliminary cost reports** for farm buildings such as:
- Dairy cattle sheds and milking parlours
- Poultry houses (brooder, grower, and layer houses)
- Goat/sheep shelters
- Piggery units
- Farm stores, godowns, and implement sheds
- Greenhouses and polyhouses
- Farm processing units (mini rice mill, dairy chilling centre)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The House Valuation Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Explanation for First-Time Learners:**

Imagine you want to know the **fair market value of a 100 m² farm store** you plan to build. Instead of measuring every brick and counting every labour hour (which takes months), you simply look at your **neighbour's identical 90 m² farm store** that was built last year for **₹ 4,50,000**.

You then ask: "Is my store exactly like his? When did he build it? Is the location similar? Is the specification the same?"

You **mentally adjust** the price:
- His was 90 m², mine is 100 m² → mine is **bigger**, so it will cost **proportionally more**.
- He built it when cement was cheap, but today it is costlier → I must **inflate** the price.
- My site has rocky soil and needs deeper foundations → add a **premium**.
- His locality is far from market, mine is closer to the highway → slight **location premium**.

After all these adjustments, you arrive at a **reliable ballpark figure** for your farm structure. **That entire mental exercise IS the Comparison Method.**

> [!TIP]
> The method is essentially **"Cost Mirroring with Corrections"** — a mirror that reflects a known cost, corrected by adjustment factors.

## 1.3 Why This Method is Critical in Farm Structure Design

In KTU 2024 Scheme Agricultural Engineering, students are expected to:
1. Produce **quick preliminary estimates** during the project feasibility stage.
2. Assist farmers and agri-preneurs in **budget preparation** for NABARD/ bank loan applications.
3. Provide **alternative cost comparisons** between RCC, steel, and timber farm buildings.
4. Cross-validate detailed estimates prepared by the **Detailed Quantity Survey Method**.

The method is officially recognised in **IS 3861: 2002 (Method of Measurement of Works in Civil Engineering)** and in the **CPWD Plinth Area Rate (PAR) guidelines**, both of which form the basis of KTU's valuation questions.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in the Method (Constants & Reference Values)

| Parameter | Standard Reference |
|---|---|
| **Floor Space Index (FSI)** | 1.0 to 1.5 for rural farm structures |
| **Plinth Area Rate (PAR)** | ₹ 1,800 – ₹ 3,500 per m² (Kerala 2024) |
| **Cost Index Base Year** | 100 (commonly 1999–2000 = 100) |
| **Cubic Content Rate** | ₹ 5,500 – ₹ 9,500 per m³ (Kerala farm buildings) |
| **Annual Cost Escalation** | 5% to 8% for agricultural buildings |
| **Standard Plinth Height** | 0.45 m to 0.60 m above GL |

> [!WARNING]
> Always cite the **base year** of the cost index being used. The most common KTU Board trap is forgetting the index year, which leads to wrong time adjustments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Comparison Method (Stepwise)

The method follows a **5-stage logical pipeline**:

1. **Identification of Reference Structure** — Locate one (or more) similar farm structures whose **actual completed cost is documented**.
2. **Data Collection of Reference** — Record:
   - Total actual cost (C₁)
   - Plinth area / built-up area (A₁)
   - Cubic content (V₁) = A₁ × Height
   - Year of construction
   - Cost index in that year (I₁)
   - Location, specification, and special features.
3. **Quantification of New Structure** — Determine the corresponding parameters of the proposed structure:
   - Plinth area (A₂)
   - Cubic content (V₂)
   - Desired specification level.
4. **Application of Adjustment Factors** — Multiply C₁ by ratios (correction factors) for time, size, location, specification, and other deltas.
5. **Reconciliation & Reporting** — Sum the adjusted cost with **5% to 10% contingency** to obtain the final preliminary estimate.

## 2.2 The Five Canonical Adjustment Factors (KTU High-Yield)

### (i) Time Adjustment (Cost Index Method)
Accounts for **inflation / cost escalation** between the year of the reference building and the present.

$$
C_{\text{time-adjusted}} = C_{1} \times \frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{1}$ = Original cost of reference building
- $I_{1}$ = Cost index in the year of reference building
- $I_{2}$ = Cost index in the current year
- $C_{\text{time-adjusted}}$ = Cost escalated to current price level

### (ii) Size Adjustment (Plinth Area or Cubic Content Ratio)
Accounts for **difference in physical dimensions** between the two structures.

**Plinth Area Ratio Approach:**
$$
C_{\text{size-adjusted}} = C_{1} \times \frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}
$$

**Cubic Content Ratio Approach (more accurate for multi-storeyed or tall farm buildings):**
$$
C_{\text{size-adjusted}} = C_{1} \times \frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> For tall farm structures like **grain godowns** with high walls, use **cubic content ratio**. For low-height poultry houses, use **plinth area ratio**.

### (iii) Location Adjustment
Accounts for **regional cost variation** (e.g., rural Kerala vs. urban Kochi). The factor is empirical, usually between **0.85 and 1.20**.

$$
C_{\text{loc-adjusted}} = C_{1} \times L
$$

Where $L$ = Location factor (1.0 = same locality, 1.15 = metro region, 0.90 = remote village).

### (iv) Specification / Quality Adjustment
Reflects the difference in **materials, finishes, and workmanship** (e.g., RCC roof vs. asbestos roof; mosaic floor vs. cement floor).

$$
C_{\text{spec-adjusted}} = C_{1} \times S
$$

Where $S$ = Specification factor (typically 0.85 to 1.30).

### (v) Combined Master Formula
$$
\boxed{\;C_{\text{new}} \;=\; C_{1} \times \frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}} \times \frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}} \times L \times S \;}
$$

When a **cubic content ratio** is preferred for size adjustment, $A_{2}/A_{1}$ is replaced by $V_{2}/V_{1}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **only formula table** you need to memorise for this topic in the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination.

| # | Formula Name | Expression | When to Use | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time Adjustment (Index) | $C_{2} = C_{1} \times (I_{2}/I_{1})$ | Any age-difference > 6 months | Ratio (dimensionless) |
| 2 | Plinth Area Ratio | $C_{2} = C_{1} \times (A_{2}/A_{1})$ | Same shape, different footprint | Ratio (dimensionless) |
| 3 | Cubic Content Ratio | $C_{2} = C_{1} \times (V_{2}/V_{1})$ | Different height too | Ratio (dimensionless) |
| 4 | Combined Master Formula | $C_{2} = C_{1} \times (I_{2}/I_{1}) \times (A_{2}/A_{1}) \times L \times S$ | Full preliminary estimate | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 5 | Cost per m² (PAR) | $C = A \times R$ | Direct rate × area | ₹/m² |
| 6 | Cost per m³ | $C = V \times R_{c}$ | For godowns, tall barns | ₹/m³ |
| 7 | Cost Index (Annual Escalation) | $I_{2} = I_{1} \times (1 + r)^{n}$ | When only one year's index known | Percentage |
| 8 | Contingency Addition | $C_{\text{final}} = C_{\text{est}} \times 1.10$ | All preliminary estimates | Multiplier (10% for farm structures) |

**Critical Notations Table:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{1}, C_{2}$ | Cost of old (reference) and new structure | ₹ (Rupees) |
| $I_{1}, I_{2}$ | Cost index in old and new year | Dimensionless ratio |
| $A_{1}, A_{2}$ | Plinth area of old and new | m² |
| $V_{1}, V_{2}$ | Cubic content of old and new | m³ |
| $L$ | Location adjustment factor | Dimensionless (0.85 to 1.20) |
| $S$ | Specification adjustment factor | Dimensionless (0.85 to 1.30) |
| $R$ | Plinth area rate | ₹/m² |
| $R_{c}$ | Cubic rate | ₹/m³ |
| $n$ | Number of years elapsed | Years |
| $r$ | Annual escalation rate | Decimal (e.g., 0.06) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NABARD Project Reports** — Farmers must submit cost estimates for bank loans; the comparison method produces these in 2–3 days.
- **Insurance Valuation** — Used to assess the **rebuilding cost** of damaged farm structures.
- **Feasibility Studies** — Quick cost comparison between RCC, steel portal, and timber-truss farm buildings.
- **Government Tender Sanction** — Used to set the **project cost ceiling** before detailed estimates are prepared.
- **Contractor's Bidding** — Helps contractors prepare **quick tender bids** when detailed drawings are unavailable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Time Adjustment Formula

### Conceptual Basis
The cost index $I$ reflects the **average market price of a basket of construction materials and labour** in a given year, normalised to a base year (where $I = 100$). If a building cost ₹ 100 when the index was 100, the same building would cost ₹ 150 when the index rises to 150, because the materials and labour have become 1.5 times costlier.

### Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1: Define cost proportionality.**
Assume the cost of a building is directly proportional to the prevailing cost index in that year.

$$
C \;\propto\; I
$$

**Step 2: Write the proportionality as an equation.**
Introducing a constant of proportionality $k$ (the "intrinsic structural cost per unit index"):

$$
C \;=\; k \times I
$$

**Step 3: Express the constant for the reference building.**
For the reference building built in year 1:

$$
C_{1} \;=\; k \times I_{1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k \;=\; \frac{C_{1}}{I_{1}}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the same constant to the new year.**
The intrinsic structural cost $k$ is assumed invariant (the building's design has not changed), but the index has changed to $I_{2}$:

$$
C_{2} \;=\; k \times I_{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{C_{1}}{I_{1}}\right) \times I_{2}
$$

**Step 5: Final rearrangement.**

$$
\boxed{\;C_{2} \;=\; C_{1} \times \frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}\;}
$$

This is the **fundamental time-adjustment equation** of the comparison method.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Size Adjustment Formula

### Plinth Area Ratio
For two geometrically similar structures (same plan shape, same height, only footprint scaling), the cost is assumed proportional to the plinth area:

$$
C \;\propto\; A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{C_{2}}{C_{1}} \;=\; \frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;C_{2} \;=\; C_{1} \times \frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}\;}
$$

### Cubic Content Ratio
For two geometrically similar structures where both plan AND height differ, cost is proportional to volume (cubic content $V = A \times h$):

$$
\boxed{\;C_{2} \;=\; C_{1} \times \frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the "Six-Tenths Rule"** — In some international references, a 0.6 power factor is used for industrial buildings to reflect **economy of scale**:
> $C_{2} = C_{1} \times (A_{2}/A_{1})^{0.6}$. However, for KTU examinations, the **simple linear ratio** is expected unless explicitly stated.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

### Problem Statement
> *"A 75 m² dairy cattle shed in Wayanad was constructed in **2021** at a total cost of **₹ 6,75,000**. The cost index in 2021 was **280**. The current cost index (2024) is **340**. A farmer now wants to build a **90 m²** shed of similar specification in the same locality."*
>
> *Estimate the probable cost of the new shed using the Comparison Method. Assume standard 10% contingency.*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Reference building cost $C_{1} = ₹ 6,75,000$
- Old cost index $I_{1} = 280$ (Year 2021)
- New cost index $I_{2} = 340$ (Year 2024)
- Old plinth area $A_{1} = 75$ m²
- New plinth area $A_{2} = 90$ m²
- Same location → $L = 1.00$
- Same specification → $S = 1.00$
- Contingency = 10%

**Step 1: Apply the Time Adjustment Factor.**

$$
\text{Time factor} \;=\; \frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}} \;=\; \frac{340}{280} \;=\; 1.2143
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Size Adjustment Factor (Plinth Area Ratio).**

$$
\text{Size factor} \;=\; \frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}} \;=\; \frac{90}{75} \;=\; 1.2000
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Combined Master Formula.**

$$
C_{\text{new}} \;=\; C_{1} \times \frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}} \times \frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}} \times L \times S
$$

$$
C_{\text{new}} \;=\; 6{,}75{,}000 \times 1.2143 \times 1.2000 \times 1.00 \times 1.00
$$

$$
C_{\text{new}} \;=\; 6{,}75{,}000 \times 1.45714
$$

$$
C_{\text{new}} \;=\; ₹ 9{,}83{,}571.43
$$

**Step 4: Add 10% Contingency.**

$$
\text{Contingency} \;=\; 9{,}83{,}571.43 \times 0.10 \;=\; ₹ 98{,}357.14
$$

$$
C_{\text{final}} \;=\; 9{,}83{,}571.43 + 98{,}357.14 \;=\; ₹ 10{,}81{,}928.57
$$

**Step 5: Round to nearest rupee and present in lakhs (KTU standard).**

$$
\boxed{\;C_{\text{final}} \;\approx\; ₹ 10.82 \text{ Lakhs (or ₹ 10,81,929)}\;}
$$

### Verification of Each Numerical Transition

| Operation | Input | Operation | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time index ratio | 340 ÷ 280 | Division | 1.2143 |
| Area ratio | 90 ÷ 75 | Division | 1.2000 |
| Combined factor | 1.2143 × 1.2000 | Multiplication | 1.45716 |
| Adjusted cost | 6,75,000 × 1.45716 | Multiplication | ₹ 9,83,580 |
| Contingency (10%) | 9,83,580 × 0.10 | Multiplication | ₹ 98,358 |
| **Final cost** | 9,83,580 + 98,358 | Addition | **₹ 10,81,938** |

> [!NOTE]
> Minor rounding differences (₹ 10,81,928 vs. ₹ 10,81,938) are **acceptable** in KTU valuation. The examiner awards full marks if the **method is correct** and the final answer is within ± 0.5% of the model answer.

## 3.4 Second Worked Example — Specification & Location Adjustment

### Problem
> *"A 100 m² poultry house in Thrissur (2022) cost ₹ 8,00,000. Cost indices: 2022 = 300, 2024 = 350. A new 120 m² poultry house is planned in **Palakkad** (location factor 0.95) with **better-quality** roofing sheets (specification factor 1.10). Estimate the current cost."*

### Solution

**Step 1: Time factor** = 350 / 300 = 1.1667

**Step 2: Size factor** = 120 / 100 = 1.2000

**Step 3: Location factor** $L = 0.95$

**Step 4: Specification factor** $S = 1.10$

**Step 5: Apply the master formula.**

$$
C_{\text{new}} \;=\; 8{,}00{,}000 \times 1.1667 \times 1.2000 \times 0.95 \times 1.10
$$

Intermediate multiplications:

$$
8{,}00{,}000 \times 1.1667 \;=\; 9{,}33{,}360
$$

$$
9{,}33{,}360 \times 1.2000 \;=\; 11{,}20{,}032
$$

$$
11{,}20{,}032 \times 0.95 \;=\; 10{,}64{,}030.4
$$

$$
10{,}64{,}030.4 \times 1.10 \;=\; 11{,}70{,}433.44
$$

**Step 6: Add 10% contingency.**

$$
C_{\text{final}} \;=\; 11{,}70{,}433.44 \times 1.10 \;=\; ₹ 12{,}87{,}476.78
$$

$$
\boxed{\;C_{\text{final}} \;\approx\; ₹ 12.87 \text{ Lakhs}\;}
$$

## 3.5 Comparison with Alternative Estimation Methods (Analytical Matrix)

| Parameter | Comparison Method | Plinth Area Method | Cubic Rate Method | Detailed Quantity Survey |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | ± 10–15% | ± 10–12% | ± 8–10% | ± 2–5% |
| **Time Required** | 1–2 days | 1 day | 1 day | 2–4 weeks |
| **Data Required** | Reference cost + index | PAR + area | Rate + volume | Full drawings + SOR |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | 14 marks (full Q) | 7 marks (sub-Q) | 7 marks (sub-Q) | 14 marks (full Q) |
| **Suitability for Farm Structures** | Excellent (preliminary) | Good | Good for godowns | Best for final estimates |
| **Dependency on Historical Data** | High | Low | Low | None |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Comparison Method

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete process pipeline** of the Comparison Method, from the identification of the reference structure to the final estimated cost. This serves as the conceptual map for problem-solving in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA([START Comparison Method]) --> nodeA1[Identify Reference Farm Structure]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2[Collect Reference Data: Cost Area Volume Year]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Quantify New Structure Parameters]
    nodeA3 --> nodeB1{Time Difference More Than 6 Months?}
    nodeB1 -- YES --> nodeB2[Apply Time Index Factor I2 over I1]
    nodeB1 -- NO --> nodeC1[Skip Time Adjustment]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2{Size Difference Significant?}
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2
    nodeC2 -- YES --> nodeC3[Apply Plinth Area Ratio OR Cubic Content Ratio]
    nodeC2 -- NO --> nodeD1[Skip Size Adjustment]
    nodeC3 --> nodeD2{Location Different?}
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2
    nodeD2 -- YES --> nodeD3[Multiply by Location Factor L]
    nodeD2 -- NO --> nodeE1{Specification Different?}
    nodeD3 --> nodeE1
    nodeE1 -- YES --> nodeE2[Multiply by Specification Factor S]
    nodeE1 -- NO --> nodeF1[Skip Specification Adjustment]
    nodeE2 --> nodeF2[Compute Adjusted Cost C Adjusted]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2
    nodeF2 --> nodeG1[Add 10 Percent Contingency]
    nodeG1 --> nodeH1[Add 4 Percent GST if Applicable]
    nodeH1 --> nodeI1[Round to Nearest Rupee]
    nodeI1 --> nodeJ1[Present as Lakhs in Final Report]
    nodeJ1 --> EndA([FINAL Estimated Cost Output])
```

## 4.2 Data Flow Diagram — Inputs, Processes, and Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        i1[Reference Cost C1 in Rupees]
        i2[Cost Index Old I1]
        i3[Cost Index New I2]
        i4[Plinth Area Old A1 in m2]
        i5[Plinth Area New A2 in m2]
        i6[Location Factor L]
        i7[Specification Factor S]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSES
        p1[Compute Time Ratio I2 over I1]
        p2[Compute Area Ratio A2 over A1]
        p3[Apply Master Formula]
        p4[Add Contingency 10 Percent]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        out1[Adjusted Cost in Rupees]
        out2[Final Estimated Cost in Lakhs]
        out3[Cost per m2 Benchmark]
    end
    i1 --> p3
    i2 --> p1 --> p3
    i3 --> p1
    i4 --> p2 --> p3
    i5 --> p2
    i6 --> p3
    i7 --> p3
    p3 --> out1
    out1 --> p4 --> out2
    out2 --> out3
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Adjustment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Is Reference Building Older Than 1 Year?}
    q1 -- YES --> a1[Apply Time Index Adjustment]
    q1 -- NO --> a2[Time Factor Equals 1.0]
    a1 --> q2{Is Plinth Area Different by More Than 10 Percent?}
    a2 --> q2
    q2 -- YES --> a3[Apply Area Ratio A2 over A1]
    q2 -- NO --> a4[Area Factor Equals 1.0]
    a3 --> q3{Is Building Tall Above 4.5 m?}
    a4 --> q3
    q3 -- YES --> a5[Prefer Cubic Content Ratio V2 over V1]
    q3 -- NO --> a6[Plinth Area Ratio is Sufficient]
    a5 --> q4{Is Location Different?}
    a6 --> q4
    q4 -- YES --> a7[Apply Location Factor 0.85 to 1.20]
    q4 -- NO --> a8[Location Factor Equals 1.0]
    a7 --> q5{Is Specification Different?}
    a8 --> q5
    q5 -- YES --> a9[Apply Specification Factor 0.85 to 1.30]
    q5 -- NO --> a10[Specification Factor Equals 1.0]
    a9 --> fin[Final Adjusted Cost]
    a10 --> fin
```

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU valuation script, **always show the decision explicitly** for each factor (e.g., "Since the time gap is 3 years, time adjustment is required"). This earns the **methodology marks** (1–2 marks) even if the arithmetic has a small slip.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Q: Define the Comparison Method of cost estimation. List any four situations where it is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Comparison Method of cost estimation is a preliminary cost-estimation technique in which the probable cost of a proposed farm structure is obtained by comparing it with a **similar already-constructed structure** whose actual cost is known, after applying adjustments for time, size, location, and specification.

**Preferred situations (any four):**
1. Preliminary cost estimates during project feasibility studies.
2. Quick budget preparation for NABARD / bank loan applications.
3. Insurance valuation of farm structures after damage.
4. Sanction of project cost ceilings before detailed estimates are made.
5. Comparison of alternative designs (RCC vs. steel vs. timber farm buildings).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1.5 marks, Four situations = 0.375 × 4 = 1.5 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q: What is a Cost Index? How is it used in the Comparison Method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Cost Index** is a numerical ratio that expresses the **average market price of a standard basket of construction materials and labour** in a given year, with reference to a fixed **base year** (where index = 100). Examples: CPWD Cost Index, NBC Cost Index, State PWD Cost Index.

**Use in Comparison Method:** It is used to perform the **time adjustment** of the reference building cost. If the reference building cost $C_{1}$ was incurred when the cost index was $I_{1}$, then the equivalent cost at the current index $I_{2}$ is:

$$
C_{2} \;=\; C_{1} \times \frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Always state the **base year** of the index (e.g., "Base 1999–2000 = 100") in your answer. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks if the base year is omitted.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: A 60 m² piggery unit constructed in Palakkad in 2020 cost ₹ 4,80,000. The cost index in 2020 was 250 and the current cost index (2024) is 340. A farmer wishes to construct a similar piggery unit of 84 m² in Thrissur district. Assuming the location factor for Thrissur is 1.05 and the specification factor is 1.00, estimate the cost of the new piggery unit using the Comparison Method. Add 10% contingency.**

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Apply the time and size adjustment factors and compute the basic adjusted cost.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $C_{1} = ₹ 4,80,000$ (2020)
- $I_{1} = 250$, $I_{2} = 340$
- $A_{1} = 60$ m², $A_{2} = 84$ m²
- $L = 1.05$, $S = 1.00$

**Step 1: Time Adjustment Factor.**

$$
\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}} \;=\; \frac{340}{250} \;=\; 1.360
$$

**Step 2: Size Adjustment Factor (Plinth Area Ratio).**

$$
\frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}} \;=\; \frac{84}{60} \;=\; 1.400
$$

**Step 3: Adjusted Cost (before location and specification).**

$$
C_{\text{adj}} \;=\; 4{,}80{,}000 \times 1.360 \times 1.400
$$

$$
4{,}80{,}000 \times 1.360 \;=\; 6{,}52{,}800
$$

$$
6{,}52{,}800 \times 1.400 \;=\; 9{,}13{,}920
$$

$$
\boxed{\;C_{\text{adj}} \;=\; ₹ 9{,}13{,}920\;}
$$

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Apply the location factor, specification factor, and contingency; present the final cost.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 4: Apply Location and Specification Factors.**

$$
C_{\text{final-est}} \;=\; 9{,}13{,}920 \times 1.05 \times 1.00
$$

$$
9{,}13{,}920 \times 1.05 \;=\; 9{,}59{,}616
$$

**Step 5: Add 10% Contingency.**

$$
\text{Contingency} \;=\; 9{,}59{,}616 \times 0.10 \;=\; ₹ 95{,}961.6
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} \;=\; 9{,}59{,}616 + 95{,}961.6 \;=\; ₹ 10{,}55{,}577.6
$$

**Step 6: Round and Express in Lakhs.**

$$
\boxed{\;C_{\text{total}} \;\approx\; ₹ 10.56 \text{ Lakhs (or ₹ 10,55,578)}\;}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute only the time-and-size adjusted cost and **forget the contingency** (Step 5). This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. Always add 10% contingency for farm structures per KTU valuation key.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: A 120 m³ farm godown built in Kottayam in 2019 cost ₹ 7,20,000. The cost indices were 220 in 2019 and 330 in 2024. A new godown of 180 m³ is planned in Idukki with a specification factor of 1.15 (premium construction) and location factor of 0.92 (remote hilly area). Estimate the cost using the Comparison Method (Cubic Content Ratio approach). Add 10% contingency.**

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Compute the basic adjusted cost using the cubic content approach.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $C_{1} = ₹ 7,20{,}000$ (2019)
- $I_{1} = 220$, $I_{2} = 330$
- $V_{1} = 120$ m³, $V_{2} = 180$ m³

**Step 1: Time Factor.**

$$
\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}} \;=\; \frac{330}{220} \;=\; 1.500
$$

**Step 2: Cubic Content Ratio.**

$$
\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}} \;=\; \frac{180}{120} \;=\; 1.500
$$

**Step 3: Adjusted Cost (time + volume).**

$$
C_{\text{adj}} \;=\; 7{,}20{,}000 \times 1.500 \times 1.500
$$

$$
7{,}20{,}000 \times 1.500 \;=\; 10{,}80{,}000
$$

$$
10{,}80{,}000 \times 1.500 \;=\; ₹ 16{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\;C_{\text{adj}} \;=\; ₹ 16{,}20{,}000\;}
$$

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Apply location, specification, and contingency.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 4: Apply Location and Specification Factors.**

$$
C_{\text{est}} \;=\; 16{,}20{,}000 \times 0.92 \times 1.15
$$

$$
16{,}20{,}000 \times 0.92 \;=\; 14{,}90{,}400
$$

$$
14{,}90{,}400 \times 1.15 \;=\; ₹ 17{,}13{,}960
$$

**Step 5: Add 10% Contingency.**

$$
17{,}13{,}960 \times 0.10 \;=\; ₹ 1{,}71{,}396
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} \;=\; 17{,}13{,}960 + 1{,}71{,}396 \;=\; ₹ 18{,}85{,}356
$$

**Step 6: Final Answer in Lakhs.**

$$
\boxed{\;C_{\text{total}} \;\approx\; ₹ 18.85 \text{ Lakhs}\;}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use **plinth area ratio instead of cubic content ratio** for godowns (which are tall structures). For buildings with height > 4.5 m, **always use cubic content ratio** — the area-only ratio will under-estimate the cost. Deduct 2 marks if the wrong ratio is used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it for last-day exam revision.**

### Core Concept
- [x] The **Comparison Method** = estimating new cost from a known reference cost using **adjustment factors**.
- [x] It is a **preliminary** method, accuracy ± 10–15%.
- [x] It is the **fastest** method, ideal for farm project feasibility.

### Master Formula (Must Memorise)
- [x] $C_{\text{new}} = C_{1} \times (I_{2}/I_{1}) \times (A_{2}/A_{1}) \times L \times S$
- [x] For tall farm buildings (godowns, hay barns), replace area ratio with **cubic content ratio** $V_{2}/V_{1}$.
- [x] Always add **10% contingency** for farm structures (5% for industrial, 10% for agricultural).

### Adjustment Factors — Typical Range
- [x] **Time (Index) factor** = $I_{2}/I_{1}$, always between 1.0 and 2.0.
- [x] **Size factor (area)** = $A_{2}/A_{1}$, dimensionless.
- [x] **Size factor (volume)** = $V_{2}/V_{1}$, use for tall farm structures.
- [x] **Location factor** $L$ = **0.85 to 1.20** (1.00 if same locality).
- [x] **Specification factor** $S$ = **0.85 to 1.30** (1.00 if same quality).

### Mandatory Steps in KTU Solution Writing
- [x] **Step 1:** List all given data in a tabular box at the start.
- [x] **Step 2:** State which factors are required (time, size, location, specification).
- [x] **Step 3:** Compute each factor separately, show the division.
- [x] **Step 4:** Substitute into the master formula, multiply step-by-step.
- [x] **Step 5:** Add contingency and round off.
- [x] **Step 6:** Present the final answer **in Lakhs (₹ X.XX Lakhs)** — this is KTU's preferred format.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [x] ❌ Forgetting the **base year** of the cost index.
- [x] ❌ Using plinth area ratio for **tall godowns** (use cubic content).
- [x] ❌ Omitting the **10% contingency** (loses 2 marks).
- [x] ❌ Writing the answer in **rupees instead of lakhs**.
- [x] ❌ Not showing the **intermediate multiplication steps** (loses process marks).
- [x] ❌ Confusing the **location factor** direction (a cheaper location has $L < 1$, not > 1).

### Numerical Benchmarks (Memorise for Sanity Check)
- [x] Average farm dairy shed cost: **₹ 10–15 Lakhs** for 75–100 m² in Kerala 2024.
- [x] Average poultry house (1000-bird): **₹ 18–25 Lakhs**.
- [x] Average farm godown: **₹ 6,000 – ₹ 9,000 per m³** (Kerala 2024).
- [x] Typical cost index escalation: **5% to 8% per year** for farm structures.

> [!TIP]
> If your calculated cost is wildly outside these ranges in the KTU exam, **re-check your multiplication and the index year** before submitting.

### One-Line Definition for Quick Recall
> *"The Comparison Method estimates the cost of a new farm structure by adjusting the known cost of a similar old structure for time, size, location, and specification variations."*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost from Materials and Labour; Preparation of Detailed Estimate for Buildings - Centre Line and 'Long Wall - Short Wall' Method; Preparation of Detailed Estimate for Sanitary and Water Supply Works

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Cost from Materials and Labour"?

### Formal KTU Definition
**Cost from Materials and Labour** refers to the systematic calculation of the direct expenditure incurred on **raw construction materials** (cement, sand, aggregate, bricks, steel, timber, etc.) and **human effort** (skilled, semi-skilled, and unskilled labour) required to execute a specific item of work in a farm structure or building project. This forms the **base cost** upon which contractor's profit, overheads, and contingencies are added to arrive at the **all-inclusive unit rate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In farm structure estimation, materials typically account for **60–70%** and labour accounts for **25–30%** of the total direct cost, with the remaining **5–10%** reserved for overheads and contingencies.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are baking a cake for a school bake-sale. The **flour, sugar, eggs, and butter** are your *materials* — they have a price per kg. The **time you spend mixing, baking, and decorating** is your *labour* — measured in hours multiplied by an hourly rate. If you ask a friend to help (a labourer), you add their wages too. Now, to know the **true cost of one cake**, you sum up (Material Cost + Labour Cost) ÷ Number of Cakes produced. This is exactly how a Quantity Surveyor calculates the unit rate of, say, 1 m³ of brickwork or 1 m² of plastering on a farm building!

---

## 1.2 Detailed Estimate for Buildings — Overview

A **Detailed Estimate** (also called an *Item-wise Estimate* or *Bill of Quantities*) is a comprehensive document that breaks down the **entire project into individual work items** and provides:
- **Quantity** of each item (in standard units: m, m², m³, kg, No.)
- **Rate** per unit (₹/m, ₹/m², ₹/m³)
- **Amount** = Quantity × Rate

### Two Principal Methods of Quantity Calculation for Buildings

| S.No. | Method | Best Used For |
|:-----:|:-------|:--------------|
| 1 | **Centre Line Method** | Buildings with simple, symmetrical rectangular/square plans and walls of uniform cross-section |
| 2 | **Long Wall – Short Wall Method** | Buildings with complex plans, varying wall thicknesses, or unsymmetrical shapes (e.g., L-shaped, T-shaped farm godowns) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of the *Centre Line Method* as running a single continuous thread through the *center* of all walls (like the path of a marble rolling through a maze). The *Long Wall – Short Wall Method* is like measuring each wall segment *individually* along its longer and shorter dimensions.

---

## 1.3 Detailed Estimate for Sanitary and Water Supply Works

### Formal Definition
This is a specialized estimate covering all items associated with **plumbing, drainage, sewerage, and water distribution networks** in a farm structure — including sanitary fixtures (WCs, washbasins, sinks), pipe networks, septic tanks, soak pits, manholes, and water storage tanks.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of the **sanitary and water supply system** as the *circulatory system of a building*:
- **Water Supply Pipes** = Arteries (carry fresh water in)
- **Drainage/Sewer Pipes** = Veins (carry waste water out)
- **Sanitary Fixtures** = Organs (where water is used)
- **Septic Tank/Manhole** = Kidney (treats waste)

Just as a doctor estimates the cost of bypass surgery by listing every suture, drug, and tool, a Quantity Surveyor estimates sanitary works by listing every pipe, fitting, fixture, and the labour to install them.

### Standard Units Used
- **Earthwork for trenches** → m³
- **Pipes (PVC, GI, CI, AC)** → Running metre (m)
- **Sanitary fittings** → Number (No.)
- **Brickwork in manholes/septic tanks** → m³
- **Concrete in septic tanks** → m³
- **Water storage tanks** → Litres (L) or m³ capacity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used:** Assume **1 cumec = 1000 litres/second** for any flow-related calculation in water supply estimation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Total Cost

The total cost of any construction item is mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{Total Cost} = \text{Material Cost} + \text{Labour Cost} + \text{Overhead Charges} + \text{Contractor's Profit} + \text{Contingencies}$$

### 2.1.1 Material Cost ($C_m$)
The cost of all materials consumed (including wastage allowance of typically **5–10%**):

$$C_m = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( Q_i \times R_i \right) \times \left(1 + w\right)$$

Where:
- $Q_i$ = Quantity of material *i* required
- $R_i$ = Unit rate of material *i* (₹/unit)
- $w$ = Wastage factor (typically **0.05 to 0.10**)
- $n$ = Number of distinct materials

### 2.1.2 Labour Cost ($C_l$)
The cost of human effort categorized into:
- **Skilled Labour** (mason, carpenter, plumber) → Higher daily wage
- **Semi-skilled Labour** (helper, bhisti) → Medium daily wage
- **Unskilled Labour** (coolie, mazdoor) → Lower daily wage

$$C_l = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( N_j \times D_j \times W_j \right)$$

Where:
- $N_j$ = Number of labourers of category *j*
- $D_j$ = Number of working days
- $W_j$ = Daily wage of category *j* (₹/day)
- $m$ = Number of labour categories

### 2.1.3 Overheads, Profit & Contingencies

$$\text{Overhead Charges} = 1\% \text{ to } 2\% \text{ of } (C_m + C_l)$$

$$\text{Contractor's Profit} = 10\% \text{ to } 15\% \text{ of } (C_m + C_l + \text{Overheads})$$

$$\text{Contingencies} = 3\% \text{ to } 5\% \text{ of } \text{Subtotal}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For **farm structures** (godowns, poultry sheds, dairy buildings), overheads are usually taken as **1.5%**, profit as **10%**, and contingencies as **3%** of direct cost.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

### Master Formula Table — Estimation Methods

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Key Note |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:------:|:---------|
| 1 | Material Cost (with wastage) | $C_m = \Sigma(Q_i R_i)(1+w)$ | ₹ | $w$ = 5–10% |
| 2 | Labour Cost | $C_l = \Sigma(N_j D_j W_j)$ | ₹ | Use **mandays** |
| 3 | Unit Rate | $R = (C_m + C_l)(1+O+P)$ | ₹/unit | $O$=Overhead%, $P$=Profit% |
| 4 | **Centre Line Length** | Sum of **all internal & external wall centre-line lengths** | m | Add junctions once |
| 5 | **Long Wall Length (L)** | Centre-to-centre length of longest wall in a room | m | Exceeds out-to-out by **half-breadth** on each side |
| 6 | **Short Wall Length (S)** | Centre-to-centre length of shorter wall in a room | m | Less than out-to-out by **half-breadth** on each side |
| 7 | Long Wall Total | $\Sigma L_i$ for each successive course | m | Increases by **wall thickness** per course |
| 8 | Short Wall Total | $\Sigma S_i$ for each successive course | m | Decreases by **wall thickness** per course |
| 9 | Volume of Brickwork | $\text{Centre Length} \times \text{Cross-sectional Area}$ | m³ | **CL Method** |
| 10 | Volume of Excavation | $\text{Length} \times \text{Breadth} \times \text{Depth}$ | m³ | Add 0.30 m offset for working space |
| 11 | Sanitary Pipe Trench | $\text{Pipe Length} \times \text{Width} \times \text{Depth}$ | m³ | Depth = 0.60 m to 1.20 m typical |
| 12 | Septic Tank Capacity | $V = N \times q \times t \times 1.33$ | m³ | $N$=users, $q$=L/capita/day, $t$=detention days (1–2) |

---

## 2.3 Centre Line Method — Theory

### Operational Logic
1. Trace a **continuous centre line** running through the middle of *all* walls in the plan.
2. Calculate the **total length of this centre line** by summing up straight segments, with **no duplication at junctions**.
3. Multiply this **total centre length** by the **cross-sectional area** of the wall (to get volume of brickwork) or by the relevant dimension for plastering, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "no duplication at junctions"?** When two walls meet at a corner, the centre line of one wall *passes through* the centre line of the other. If you count both walls in full, you double-count the junction. The centre line method inherently avoids this by continuous tracing.

### Best Suited For
- **Rectangular** farm godowns
- **Square** poultry sheds with **uniform wall thickness** throughout
- Buildings where all walls have the **same cross-section**

### Limitation
**Cannot be used directly** when walls have *different thicknesses* (e.g., 230 mm outer walls and 115 mm partition walls).

---

## 2.4 Long Wall – Short Wall Method — Theory

### Operational Logic
1. For **each room** in the building plan, identify the **longer wall** and the **shorter wall**.
2. Calculate the **centre-to-centre** length of each:
   - **Long wall** = out-to-out length − half-breadth of wall − half-breadth of wall  
     i.e., $L = \text{Out-to-Out Length} - \text{wall thickness}$
   - **Short wall** = out-to-out length − half-breadth − half-breadth  
     i.e., $S = \text{Out-to-Out Breadth} - \text{wall thickness}$
3. For each **successive course** (e.g., footing, foundation, plinth, superstructure):
   - Long wall length **increases** by wall thickness (or decreases for excavation)
   - Short wall length **decreases** by wall thickness
4. Multiply by the **cross-sectional area** for that course.

### Long Wall / Short Wall Adjustment Rules

| Course | Long Wall Action | Short Wall Action |
|:-------|:-----------------|:------------------|
| **Excavation** | $L_{exc} = \text{c/c length} + \text{extra depth offset}$ | $S_{exc} = \text{c/c length} - \text{offset}$ |
| **Concrete Bed (PCC)** | $L_{pcc} = L_{exc} + 0.20\text{ m}$ (offset both sides) | $S_{pcc} = S_{exc} - 0.20\text{ m}$ |
| **Brickwork Footing** | $L_f = L_{pcc} + 2 \times \text{offset}$ | $S_f = S_{pcc} - 2 \times \text{offset}$ |
| **Superstructure** | $L_{ss} = \text{Out-to-Out length} - \text{wall thickness}$ | $S_{ss} = \text{Out-to-Out breadth} - \text{wall thickness}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Long Wall–Short Wall Method is the **most widely used** in real construction practice in Kerala because most farm structures (cattle sheds, dairy units, storage godowns) have **multiple rooms of different sizes** and walls of **varying thickness** in different courses.

---

## 2.5 Sanitary & Water Supply Estimation Theory

### Categories of Work
1. **Sanitary Fixtures** — WCs, EWC, washbasins, urinals, kitchen sinks, bath tubs, gully traps, floor traps
2. **Water Supply Pipes** — GI/CI/PVC mains and branches, fittings (T, elbow, union, valve)
3. **Drainage Pipes** — Stoneware/CI/PVC for soil and waste lines
4. **Storage & Treatment** — Overhead tanks, underground sumps, septic tanks, soak pits, manholes

### Estimation Approach
For each category, the surveyor prepares three sub-estimates:
- **Materials Estimate** (pipes by length, fittings by number, fixtures by number, cement/sand/aggregate by volume)
- **Labour Estimate** (plumber-mandays, helper-mandays, excavator-mandays)
- **Earthwork Estimate** (trench excavation: $L \times W \times D$ in m³)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Long Wall – Short Wall Method

### Problem Statement
> Estimate the quantities of the following items of work for a **rectangular farm godown** with internal dimensions 8 m × 6 m, wall thickness = 0.30 m (outer), and a 0.30 m offset on each side of the trench for excavation.
>
> 1. Earthwork in excavation in foundation trenches (depth = 1.00 m below GL)
> 2. PCC (1:4:8) in foundation bed (thickness = 0.20 m)
> 3. Brickwork in foundation and plinth (CM 1:6)

### Given Data
- **Internal dimensions** = 8.00 m × 6.00 m
- **Wall thickness** = 0.30 m
- **Excavation offset** = 0.30 m (each side)
- **PCC offset beyond trench** = 0.10 m (each side beyond excavation)
- **Excavation depth** = 1.00 m
- **PCC thickness** = 0.20 m

### Step 1: Calculate Out-to-Out Dimensions of the Building

$$\text{Out-to-Out Length} = \text{Internal Length} + 2 \times \text{Wall Thickness}$$

$$\text{Out-to-Out Length} = 8.00 + 2 \times 0.30 = 8.60 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Out-to-Out Breadth} = \text{Internal Breadth} + 2 \times \text{Wall Thickness}$$

$$\text{Out-to-Out Breadth} = 6.00 + 2 \times 0.30 = 6.60 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Long Wall and Short Wall (c/c) Lengths (at Superstructure Level)

$$\text{Long Wall (c/c)} = \text{Out-to-Out Length} - \text{One Wall Thickness}$$

$$L_{ss} = 8.60 - 0.30 = 8.30 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Short Wall (c/c)} = \text{Out-to-Out Breadth} - \text{One Wall Thickness}$$

$$S_{ss} = 6.60 - 0.30 = 6.30 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Dimensions for Earthwork Excavation

For excavation, we **add** the offset to the c/c length on the **outer side** (long wall) and **subtract** the offset from the c/c length on the **inner side** (short wall).

$$\text{Excavation Long Wall Length} = L_{ss} + 2 \times \text{Offset} = 8.30 + 2 \times 0.30 = 8.90 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Excavation Short Wall Length} = S_{ss} - 2 \times \text{Offset} = 6.30 - 2 \times 0.30 = 5.70 \text{ m}$$

> **Reasoning:** The long wall is *lengthened* by 0.30 m on each end because the trench extends 0.30 m beyond the wall edge for working space. The short wall is *shortened* correspondingly.

$$\text{Width of Trench} = \text{Wall Thickness} + 2 \times \text{Offset} = 0.30 + 2 \times 0.30 = 0.90 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4: Quantity of Earthwork in Excavation

$$\text{Volume} = (L_{exc} + S_{exc}) \times 2 \times W_{trench} \times D_{trench}$$

$$\text{Volume} = (8.90 + 5.70) \times 2 \times 0.90 \times 1.00$$

$$\text{Volume} = 14.60 \times 2 \times 0.90 \times 1.00$$

$$\text{Volume} = 26.28 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Reasoning:** The total long-wall length is summed once, short-wall length is summed once, then multiplied by 2 (since there are 2 long walls and 2 short walls in a rectangular plan).

---

### Step 5: Quantity of PCC (1:4:8) in Foundation Bed

The PCC bed extends **0.10 m** beyond the excavation trench on each side:

$$\text{PCC Long Wall Length} = 8.90 + 2 \times 0.10 = 9.10 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{PCC Short Wall Length} = 5.70 - 2 \times 0.10 = 5.50 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{PCC Width} = 0.90 + 2 \times 0.10 = 1.10 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{PCC Volume} = (9.10 + 5.50) \times 2 \times 1.10 \times 0.20$$

$$\text{PCC Volume} = 14.60 \times 2 \times 1.10 \times 0.20 = 6.424 \text{ m}^3$$

---

### Step 6: Quantity of Brickwork in Foundation and Plinth

Assume brickwork starts from the top of PCC bed (GL − 0.20 m) and goes up to plinth level (GL + 0.45 m, i.e., 0.45 m above ground level).

$$\text{Height of Foundation Brickwork} = 0.80 \text{ m (typical)}$$
$$\text{Height of Plinth Brickwork} = 0.45 \text{ m}$$

**For Foundation Course (c/c dimensions, wall thickness = 0.30 m):**

$$\text{Foundation Long Wall} = L_{ss} = 8.30 \text{ m (or as per plan)}$$

$$\text{Foundation Short Wall} = S_{ss} = 6.30 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Volume} = (8.30 + 6.30) \times 2 \times 0.30 \times 0.80$$

$$\text{Volume} = 14.60 \times 2 \times 0.30 \times 0.80 = 7.008 \text{ m}^3$$

**For Plinth Course (same c/c dimensions, wall thickness = 0.30 m):**

$$\text{Volume} = 14.60 \times 2 \times 0.30 \times 0.45 = 3.942 \text{ m}^3$$

**Total Brickwork Volume:**

$$V_{total} = 7.008 + 3.942 = 10.95 \text{ m}^3$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Centre Line Method

### Same Problem Re-solved

**Step 1: Calculate Total Centre Line Length**

For a rectangular plan, the centre line runs around the perimeter at the **center of the wall**:

$$\text{Centre Line Length} = 2 \times (\text{Internal Length} + \text{Internal Breadth}) + 4 \times \text{half-thickness}$$

$$= 2 \times (8.00 + 6.00) + 4 \times 0.15$$

$$= 2 \times 14.00 + 0.60 = 28.60 \text{ m}$$

> **Reasoning:** Two long walls of 8.00 m + two short walls of 6.00 m = 28.00 m, plus 4 quarter-thicknesses (one at each corner) = 0.60 m. **This is the total continuous centre line length** with no duplication.

**Step 2: Earthwork Excavation (with offset)**

When using the Centre Line Method, the offset is added on **all sides** uniformly, so we just extend the centre line by **half the offset on each end**:

$$\text{Effective Centre Length for Excavation} = 28.60 + 4 \times \text{half-offset}$$

$$= 28.60 + 4 \times 0.15 = 29.20 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Trench Width} = 0.30 + 2 \times 0.30 = 0.90 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Excavation Volume} = 29.20 \times 0.90 \times 1.00 = 26.28 \text{ m}^3$$

✅ **Matches the Long Wall–Short Wall result — confirming both methods give identical answers!**

---

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — Sanitary & Water Supply Estimate

### Problem
> Estimate the cost of providing and laying **100 m of 50 mm dia GI pipe** for the water supply system of a farm dairy unit. Also estimate the cost of a **septic tank** for 20 persons, with a detention period of 2 days.

### Given Rates (Hypothetical, KTU 2024 Standard)
- 50 mm GI pipe = ₹150 per running metre
- Plumber = ₹800/day
- Helper = ₹500/day
- Excavation = ₹200 per m³
- 1:2:4 Concrete = ₹5500 per m³
- 1:6 Brickwork = ₹4500 per m³

### Step 1: GI Pipe Material Cost

$$\text{Pipe Cost} = 100 \times 150 = ₹15{,}000$$

**Fittings (estimated 10% of pipe cost):** ₹1,500

$$\text{Total Material Cost (Pipes)} = ₹16{,}500$$

### Step 2: Trench Excavation

Assuming trench depth = 0.75 m and width = 0.50 m for 50 mm dia pipe:

$$\text{Excavation Volume} = 100 \times 0.50 \times 0.75 = 37.50 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\text{Excavation Cost} = 37.50 \times 200 = ₹7{,}500$$

### Step 3: Labour Cost (Laying Pipes)

- Plumber: 1 person × 3 days × ₹800 = ₹2,400
- Helper: 2 persons × 3 days × ₹500 = ₹3,000

$$\text{Labour Cost} = ₹5{,}400$$

### Step 4: Sub-total and Final Cost

$$\text{Direct Cost} = 16{,}500 + 7{,}500 + 5{,}400 = ₹29{,}400$$

$$\text{Overheads (1.5\%)} = 0.015 \times 29{,}400 = ₹441$$

$$\text{Contractor's Profit (10\%)} = 0.10 \times (29{,}400 + 441) = ₹2{,}984.10$$

$$\text{Contingencies (3\%)} = 0.03 \times (29{,}400 + 441 + 2{,}984.10) = ₹984.75$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total Cost} \approx ₹33{,}810}$$

---

### Step 5: Septic Tank Capacity Calculation

Using **NCPDP standard** (typical for Kerala farm design):

$$V = N \times q \times t \times 1.33$$

Where:
- $N$ = 20 persons
- $q$ = 150 L/capita/day (typical farm residential value)
- $t$ = 2 days (detention period)
- 1.33 = factor for sludge storage

$$V = 20 \times 150 \times 2 \times 1.33 = 7{,}980 \text{ L} = 7.98 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Sizing for Kerala Farm Septic Tank:** A 2-compartment tank of **2.0 m × 2.0 m × 2.0 m** (effective liquid depth 1.50 m) is commonly adopted for 20-person capacity, providing a useful volume of **6.0 m³** (conservative design).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Centre Line Method | Long Wall – Short Wall Method |
|:----------|:-------------------|:------------------------------|
| **Speed** | Faster (one calculation) | Slower (multiple rows) |
| **Accuracy for uniform walls** | Very High | Very High |
| **Accuracy for varying thickness** | **Poor (errs)** | **Excellent** |
| **Work item tracking** | All items at once | Item-by-item (course-wise) |
| **Best for** | Symmetrical buildings | Irregular or multi-course walls |
| **Junction handling** | Automatic (no duplication) | Manual adjustment needed |
| **KTU exam preference** | Occasional | **Predominant** |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Detailed Estimate" and list any four items that are included in it for a farm building project.**

**Model Answer:**
A *Detailed Estimate* is a comprehensive, item-wise costing document prepared before construction begins. It provides the **exact quantity, unit rate, and total amount** for every work item in a project. For a farm building, it includes:
1. Earthwork in excavation of foundation trenches
2. Concrete in foundation (PCC/RCC)
3. Brickwork/Masonry in foundation, plinth, and superstructure
4. Flooring, roofing, plastering, doors, windows
5. Sanitary and water supply installations
6. Electrical works
7. Contingencies and contractor's profit

*(Any 4 points for full 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Centre Line Method and the Long Wall–Short Wall Method of estimation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Centre Line Method | Long Wall – Short Wall Method |
|:------|:-------------------|:------------------------------|
| Principle | Single continuous centre line traced around walls | Each wall measured separately as long or short |
| Suitable for | Symmetrical, uniform thickness buildings | Complex plans with varying wall thickness |
| Speed | Faster | Slower |
| Accuracy with varying thickness | Low | High |
| Course-wise breakdown | Not itemized course-wise | Itemized course by course |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Prepare a detailed estimate for a rectangular farm godown with the following data:**

- Internal dimensions: 10.00 m × 7.00 m
- Foundation: 0.30 m thick brick wall
- Depth of excavation below GL: 1.00 m
- PCC (1:4:8) bed: 0.20 m thick with 0.10 m offset on each side beyond trench
- Brickwork in foundation: 0.80 m height
- Plinth height above GL: 0.45 m
- Plinth offset (offset of brickwork beyond trench): 0.075 m on each side
- Superstructure height: 3.50 m

**Use Long Wall – Short Wall Method. Estimate quantities for:**
- (a) Earthwork in excavation
- (b) PCC in foundation bed, and Brickwork in foundation + plinth

#### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Out-to-Out Dimensions**

$$\text{Out-to-Out Length} = 10.00 + 2(0.30) = 10.60 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Out-to-Out Breadth} = 7.00 + 2(0.30) = 7.60 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Long Wall & Short Wall c/c Lengths (Superstructure Level)**

$$L_{ss} = 10.60 - 0.30 = 10.30 \text{ m}$$

$$S_{ss} = 7.60 - 0.30 = 7.30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Earthwork Excavation Dimensions**

$$\text{Excavation Offset} = 0.30 \text{ m each side}$$

$$L_{exc} = 10.30 + 2(0.30) = 10.90 \text{ m}$$

$$S_{exc} = 7.30 - 2(0.30) = 6.70 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{trench} = 0.30 + 2(0.30) = 0.90 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Quantity of Earthwork**

$$V_{exc} = 2 \times (L_{exc} + S_{exc}) \times W_{trench} \times D_{trench}$$

$$V_{exc} = 2 \times (10.90 + 6.70) \times 0.90 \times 1.00$$

$$V_{exc} = 2 \times 17.60 \times 0.90 = 31.68 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_{exc} = 31.68 \text{ m}^3}$$

#### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: PCC Bed Dimensions**

PCC extends 0.10 m beyond trench on each side:

$$L_{pcc} = 10.90 + 2(0.10) = 11.10 \text{ m}$$

$$S_{pcc} = 6.70 - 2(0.10) = 6.50 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{pcc} = 0.90 + 2(0.10) = 1.10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: PCC Quantity**

$$V_{pcc} = 2 \times (11.10 + 6.50) \times 1.10 \times 0.20$$

$$V_{pcc} = 2 \times 17.60 \times 1.10 \times 0.20$$

$$\boxed{V_{pcc} = 7.744 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 3: Brickwork in Foundation (offset 0.075 m on each side beyond trench)**

$$L_f = 10.90 + 2(0.075) = 11.05 \text{ m}$$

$$S_f = 6.70 - 2(0.075) = 6.55 \text{ m}$$

$$V_{foundation} = 2 \times (11.05 + 6.55) \times 0.30 \times 0.80$$

$$V_{foundation} = 2 \times 17.60 \times 0.30 \times 0.80 = 8.448 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Brickwork in Plinth (c/c at superstructure level)**

$$L_{plinth} = 10.30 \text{ m}, \quad S_{plinth} = 7.30 \text{ m}$$

$$V_{plinth} = 2 \times (10.30 + 7.30) \times 0.30 \times 0.45$$

$$V_{plinth} = 2 \times 17.60 \times 0.30 \times 0.45 = 4.752 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Total Brickwork**

$$\boxed{V_{brickwork} = 8.448 + 4.752 = 13.20 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Prepare a detailed estimate of a septic tank suitable for **25 persons** of a farm residential quarter. Assume standard data. List all items. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the cost of providing and laying **150 m of 75 mm dia PVC pipe** for water supply in a farm dairy, including excavation, refilling, and testing. Use assumed rates. **(7 Marks)**

#### Sub-Part (a) — Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Septic Tank Capacity (using NCPDP standard)**

$$V = N \times q \times t \times 1.33$$

$$V = 25 \times 150 \times 2 \times 1.33 = 9{,}975 \text{ L} = 9.975 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Adopted Practical Dimensions**

A two-compartment tank of **2.40 m × 2.10 m × 2.00 m** (effective liquid depth 1.50 m) provides:

$$V_{eff} = 2.40 \times 2.10 \times 1.50 = 7.56 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (inlet compartment)}$$

**Step 3: Bill of Quantities**

| S.No. | Item | Quantity | Unit |
|:------|:-----|:--------:|:----:|
| 1 | Earthwork in excavation (2.40 + 0.60) × (2.10 + 0.60) × 2.50 = 16.50 m³ | 16.50 | m³ |
| 2 | PCC (1:4:8) bed 0.15 m thick (3.00 × 2.70 × 0.15) | 1.215 | m³ |
| 3 | RCC (1:2:4) base slab 0.20 m thick (3.00 × 2.70 × 0.20) | 1.620 | m³ |
| 4 | Brickwork in CM 1:6 for walls (0.30 m thick, 1.80 m high) | 5.832 | m³ |
| 5 | RCC (1:2:4) cover slab 0.12 m thick (3.00 × 2.70 × 0.12) | 0.972 | m³ |
| 6 | 100 mm dia CI inlet pipe (with T) | 1 | No. |
| 7 | 100 mm dia CI outlet pipe | 1 | No. |
| 8 | Manhole cover (heavy duty) | 2 | No. |

**Step 4: Cost Summary (using assumed rates)**

- Excavation: 16.50 × ₹200 = ₹3,300
- PCC: 1.215 × ₹4,500 = ₹5,468
- RCC: (1.620 + 0.972) × ₹6,000 = ₹15,552
- Brickwork: 5.832 × ₹4,500 = ₹26,244
- Pipes & covers: ₹5,000

$$\text{Subtotal} = ₹55{,}564$$

$$\text{Overhead (1.5\%)} + \text{Profit (10\%)} + \text{Contingency (3\%)} \approx ₹8{,}113$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total Septic Tank Cost} \approx ₹63{,}677}$$

---

#### Sub-Part (b) — Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Given:** 150 m of 75 mm dia PVC pipe.

**Step 1: Material Cost**

- 75 mm PVC pipe: 150 m × ₹120/m = ₹18,000
- Fittings (5% of pipe cost): ₹900
- Gate valve, sockets, etc.: ₹2,000

$$\text{Total Material} = ₹20{,}900$$

**Step 2: Trench Excavation**

Width = 0.50 m, Depth = 0.75 m:

$$V_{exc} = 150 \times 0.50 \times 0.75 = 56.25 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{refill} = V_{exc} - V_{pipe} \approx 56.25 - 0.66 = 55.59 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\text{Excavation Cost} = 56.25 \times ₹200 = ₹11{,}250$$

$$\text{Refilling Cost} = 55.59 \times ₹80 = ₹4{,}447$$

**Step 3: Labour Cost**

- Plumber: 1 × 5 days × ₹800 = ₹4,000
- Helpers: 2 × 5 days × ₹500 = ₹5,000
- Testing charges: ₹1,500

$$\text{Total Labour} = ₹10{,}500$$

**Step 4: Grand Total**

$$\text{Direct Cost} = 20{,}900 + 11{,}250 + 4{,}447 + 10{,}500 = ₹47{,}097$$

$$\text{Overheads + Profit + Contingency} \approx ₹6{,}870$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total Water Supply Cost} \approx ₹53{,}967}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget to add the **2× offset** when calculating long wall length for excavation, leading to under-estimation by ~6%.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the direction of length change — long wall **increases** and short wall **decreases** by the offset/breadth. Reversing this direction causes a typical loss of **2–3 marks** per sub-part.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to multiply by **2** (for two long walls and two short walls) — this alone can lose **3 full marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** In sanitary estimates, students often compute **only pipe cost** and forget trench excavation, refilling, fittings, and testing — losing up to **4 marks**.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Not adding **wastage factor (5–10%)** for materials like cement, sand, aggregate, and bricks — a standard 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

### 🔑 Core Concept Checklist
- ✅ **Detailed Estimate** = Quantity × Rate, for **every** work item in the project
