- ✅ **Material Cost** always includes a **wastage factor of 5–10%**
- ✅ **Labour Cost** = Number of workers × Days × Daily wage
- ✅ **Overhead = 1.5%**, **Profit = 10%**, **Contingency = 3%** (standard farm structure values)

### 🔑 Centre Line Method — Key Points
- ✅ Trace one **continuous line** through wall centers
- ✅ **No duplication** at corners and junctions
- ✅ Best for **uniform cross-section** walls
- ✅ Total Centre Length × Cross-sectional Area = Quantity

### 🔑 Long Wall–Short Wall Method — Key Points
- ✅ **Long wall = Out-to-Out − wall thickness** (at superstructure)
- ✅ **Short wall = Out-to-Out − wall thickness** (at superstructure)
- ✅ Long wall **increases** and short wall **decreases** by offset/breadth in lower courses
- ✅ **Always multiply by 2** for the two long walls and two short walls
- ✅ Best for **complex plans** and **varying wall thickness**

### 🔑 Sanitary & Water Supply Checklist
- ✅ **Septic Tank** capacity formula: $V = N \times q \times t \times 1.33$
- ✅ Typical **q = 150 L/capita/day** for residential farm quarters
- ✅ Typical **detention period t = 1–2 days**
- ✅ Pipe trench = Length × Width × Depth
- ✅ Include **fittings (5% of pipe cost)**, **testing charges**, and **labour for plumber + helpers**

### 🔑 Numerical Values to Memorize
- ✅ Wall thickness (outer) = **0.30 m**, (partition) = **0.115 m**
- ✅ Plinth height = **0.45 m** above GL
- ✅ Standard brick size = **190 mm × 90 mm × 90 mm** (with 10 mm mortar)
- ✅ 1 m³ of brickwork ≈ **500 bricks** (nominal)
- ✅ Wastage factors: Cement **2%**, Sand **5–7%**, Aggregate **5%**, Bricks **5%**

### 🔑 Conversion Formulas (Quick Recall)
- ✅ 1 m³ = **1000 litres** = **35.315 ft³**
- ✅ 1 ft = **0.3048 m**
- ✅ 1 sq.m = **10.764 sq.ft**
- ✅ 1 hectare = **10,000 m²**

### 🔑 Exam Strategy Tips
- ✅ Always draw a **rough plan** and **mark dimensions** clearly
- ✅ Show **all steps** — partial marking demands step-wise presentation
- ✅ Use the **Long Wall–Short Wall Method** unless the plan is perfectly symmetrical
- ✅ For 14-mark questions, allocate **~7 marks per sub-part** and time your solution accordingly (≈12–15 minutes)
- ✅ End every estimate with a **Total + Overheads + Profit + Contingency** line

---

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes for Module 4 — Cost Estimation Methods**
> *Prepared in strict accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — PCAGT501*

topics.title: Road
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Road # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Farm Roads A **farm road** is a carefully planned, engineered pathway constructed within an agricultural holding to facilitate the smooth and efficient movement of farm machinery, transport vehicles, personnel, livestock, and harvested produce between distinct operational zones of the farm (such as the main gate, fields, farmstead, storage structures, and market access points). Formally, as per KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCAGT501**, a farm road is defined as: > *"A permanent or semi-permanent way, constructed with appropriate geometric design, suitable cross-section, proper drainage, and adequate load-bearing capacity, intended to provide all-weather vehicular access within the farm premises and to connect the farm with the public road network."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blood Vessel" of a Farm Imagine a human body. Just as blood vessels carry oxygen and nutrients to every organ and remove waste, **farm roads act as the circulatory system of an agricultural enterprise**. Without well-designed farm roads: - Harvested produce cannot reach the storage godown on time (causing spoilage). - Tractors and combine harvesters cannot move between fields efficiently (causing time loss). - Inputs like fertilizers and seeds cannot reach the field (delaying operations). A well-designed farm road is like a smooth, wide, properly drained "highway" — it saves time, reduces fuel consumption, minimizes wear-and-tear on machinery, and ultimately boosts farm profitability. ## 1.3 Classification of Farm Roads > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Farm roads are broadly classified based on their **function**, **traffic intensity**, and **administrative hierarchy** within the farm layout. | Classification Basis | Type | Typical Width | Primary Use | |---|---|---|---| | **By Function** | Main farm road / Arterial road | 3.75 m – 5.0 m | Connects farm entrance to farmstead, market | | | Field road / Secondary road | 2.5 m – 3.0 m | Connects fields to main farm road | | | Cart track / Path | 1.5 m – 2.0 m | Bullock-cart and small vehicle movement | | **By Surface Type** | Earth road / Kachha road | — | Low cost, seasonal use | | | Gravel road / Moorum road | — | Medium traffic, all-weather | | | Water Bound Macadam (WBM) | — | Heavy traffic, durable | | | Bituminous / Black-top road | — | Permanent, high-traffic farm roads | | **By Alignment** | Straight road | — | Shortest distance, easy construction | | | Curved road | — | Around obstacles, terrain-bound | ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Reference Values) - **Standard Carriageway Width** (single lane farm road): **3.75 m** - **Minimum Carriageway Width** (bullock cart track): **2.5 m** - **Recommended Camber** for earth roads: **1 in 30 to 1 in 40** - **Recommended Camber** for bituminous roads: **1 in 40 to 1 in 60** - **Maximum Gradient** for farm roads: **1 in 15 to 1 in 20** (≈ 5% to 6.67%) - **Minimum Gradient** for drainage: **1 in 200 to 1 in 400** (0.25% to 0.5%) - **Right of Way (RoW)** for main farm road: **6 m to 9 m** > [!NOTE] > These constants are derived from **IS: 6313 (Rural Roads)**, **IRC: SP 20 (Rural Roads Manual)**, and standard Agricultural Engineering textbooks. Always cite units explicitly in KTU examinations. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of a Farm Road Cross-Section A farm road, when viewed in cross-section, consists of several distinct engineering layers, each serving a specific structural or functional purpose. Understanding these layers is **critical** for both design and cost estimation problems in KTU exams. ### 2.1.1 Structural Layers (Top to Bottom) 1. **Wearing Course / Surface Course** - The topmost layer in direct contact with traffic. - Materials: Bituminous concrete, WBM, gravel, or stabilized earth. - Function: Provides smooth riding surface, resists abrasion and weathering. 2. **Base Course** - Lies immediately below the wearing course. - Materials: Crushed stone aggregate, brick ballast, moorum. - Function: Distributes wheel loads to the sub-base and subgrade. 3. **Sub-base Course** - Optional intermediate layer (used in heavy-traffic roads). - Materials: Lower-quality aggregates, locally available material (LAM). - Function: Provides additional load distribution, drainage, and frost protection. 4. **Subgrade (Prepared Earth Surface)** - The compacted natural soil on which the road rests. - Function: Foundation of the entire road structure; must be stable and well-drained. ### 2.1.2 Geometric Elements (Cross-Section View) - **Carriageway**: The actual paved/driving surface for vehicles. - **Shoulder**: The strip alongside the carriageway, used for emergency stopping and lateral support. - **Camber / Cross-slope**: The transverse slope provided to drain off surface water. - **Side Drain / Road Drain**: Longitudinal drain running parallel to the road. - **Embankment / Formation level**: The finished surface after raising/lowering natural ground. ## 2.2 Geometric Design of Farm Roads ### 2.2.1 Camber (Cross-Slope) Camber is provided to drain rainwater quickly off the road surface and prevent waterlogging, which weakens the subgrade. $$\text{Camber} = \frac{\text{Rise of Crown}}{\text{Half Width of Carriageway}}$$ For a road of width $W$ with camber of 1 in $n$, the crown height $h$ at the centre is: $$h = \frac{W}{2n}$$ ### 2.2.2 Gradient Gradient is the longitudinal slope of the road along its centre line. It is classified as: - **Ruling Gradient**: Comfortable for continuous travel. - **Limiting Gradient**: Maximum permissible on a particular stretch. - **Exceptional Gradient**: Used in short stretches where terrain is difficult. $$\text{Gradient (\%)} = \frac{\text{Difference in Elevation}}{\text{Horizontal Distance}} \times 100$$ ### 2.2.3 Stopping Sight Distance (SSD) SSD is the minimum distance required for a vehicle traveling at design speed $V$ to stop safely after the driver sees an obstacle. $$SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \frac{V^2}{254(f \pm g)}$$ Where: - $V$ = design speed in km/h - $t$ = reaction time of driver (typically **2.5 seconds**) - $f$ = coefficient of longitudinal friction (**0.35 to 0.40**) - $g$ = gradient (as a decimal, + for upgrade, − for downgrade) ## 2.3 Drainage in Farm Roads > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** A road is only as good as its drainage. Water is the single biggest enemy of any road structure. **Two types of drainage must be designed:** 1. **Surface Drainage**: Achieved through camber, side drains, and cross-drainage (culverts). 2. **Sub-surface Drainage**: Achieved through filter layers, longitudinal subdrains, and capillary cut-offs. **Design discharge for side drain** (Rational Method): $$Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$$ Where: - $Q$ = design discharge (m³/s) - $C$ = runoff coefficient (**0.4 to 0.6** for agricultural land) - $I$ = rainfall intensity (m/h) - $A$ = catchment area (m²) ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $h = W / (2n)$ | $h$ = crown height (m), $W$ = carriageway width (m), $n$ = camber ratio | Camber design | | 2 | Gradient \% = $\Delta h / L \times 100$ | $\Delta h$ = rise (m), $L$ = horizontal distance (m) | Slope calculation | | 3 | $SSD = 0.278 V t + V^2 / [254(f \pm g)]$ | $V$ in km/h, $t$ in s, $f$ dimensionless, $g$ decimal | Sight distance | | 4 | $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $I$ in m/h, $A$ in m² | Drainage design | | 5 | Manning: $V = (1/n) R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | $V$ in m/s, $R$ = hydraulic radius (m), $S$ = slope, $n$ = roughness | Open channel flow | | 6 | Earthwork Volume = $L \times (A_1 + A_2) / 2$ (Trapezoidal/Prismoidal) | $L$ in m, $A$ in m² | Quantity estimation | | 7 | Cost per m = (Material + Labour + Equipment + Overhead) $\times$ Length | All in INR/m | Cost estimation | | 8 | Compaction factor = Compacted volume / Loose volume | Dimensionless | Earthwork conversion | > [!NOTE] > In all KTU answer scripts, **always state units** with every numerical answer. Examiners specifically deduct marks for missing units. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Agricultural Engineering Properly designed farm roads are essential for: - **Mechanization**: Allowing tractors, harvesters, and trailers to move without bogging down. - **Post-harvest loss reduction**: Quick transport of perishables to cold storage. - **Rural connectivity**: Linking villages to markets under **PMGSY (Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojana)**. - **Cost savings**: Reduces fuel consumption and vehicle maintenance by **15–25%**. - **Disaster resilience**: All-weather roads enable emergency access during floods/droughts. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Camber Calculation for a Given Road ### Problem Setup Design the camber of a 3.75 m wide main farm road, given that the recommended camber for a WBM road in light rainfall region is **1 in 40**. Compute the crown height. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Identify the given parameters** We are given: - Carriageway width, $W = 3.75$ m - Camber ratio, $n = 40$ (i.e., 1 in 40) **Step 2: Recall the standard formula** The crown height (rise of the centre of the road above the edge) is: $$h = \frac{W}{2n}$$ **Step 3: Substitute the values** $$h = \frac{3.75}{2 \times 40}$$ **Step 4: Calculate the numerator and denominator separately** $$h = \frac{3.75}{80}$$ **Step 5: Perform the division** $$h = 0.046875 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6: Convert to mm for practical use** $$h = 0.046875 \times 1000 = 46.875 \text{ mm} \approx 47 \text{ mm}$$ **Final Answer**: The crown height should be **approximately 47 mm** above the road edge. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stopping Sight Distance (SSD) ### Problem Setup A farm road is to be designed for a design speed of **40 km/h**. The driver reaction time is **2.5 s**, the coefficient of friction is **0.40**, and the road is on a level gradient ($g = 0$). Compute the SSD. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Identify given data** - Design speed, $V = 40$ km/h - Reaction time, $t = 2.5$ s - Coefficient of friction, $f = 0.40$ - Gradient, $g = 0$ (level road) **Step 2: Recall the SSD formula** $$SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \frac{V^2}{254 \cdot (f \pm g)}$$ **Step 3: Compute the first term (Lag distance)** The lag distance is the distance traveled during the driver's reaction time: $$d_1 = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t$$ $$d_1 = 0.278 \times 40 \times 2.5$$ $$d_1 = 27.8 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4: Compute the second term (Braking distance)** The braking distance is computed as: $$d_2 = \frac{V^2}{254 \cdot f}$$ $$d_2 = \frac{40^2}{254 \times 0.40}$$ $$d_2 = \frac{1600}{101.6}$$ $$d_2 = 15.748 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Add both terms** $$SSD = d_1 + d_2$$ $$SSD = 27.8 + 15.748$$ $$SSD = 43.548 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6: Apply engineering rounding** $$SSD \approx 43.55 \text{ m} \approx 44 \text{ m}$$ **Final Answer**: The required Stopping Sight Distance is **approximately 44 m**. > [!TIP] > For upgrade (+) or downgrade (−), the denominator changes to $254(f + g)$ or $254(f - g)$ respectively. On a **downgrade**, SSD increases significantly because braking efficiency reduces. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Earthwork Volume for a Farm Road Embankment ### Problem Setup A farm road has a formation width of **5.0 m**, side slopes of **1.5:1 (horizontal:vertical)**, and a height of embankment of **1.0 m** above natural ground level. The road is **1 km long**. Compute the volume of earthwork required using the **trapezoidal cross-section** approach. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Sketch the trapezoidal cross-section** The cross-section is a trapezoid with: - Top width = formation width = $b = 5.0$ m - Height of embankment = $h = 1.0$ m - Side slope (1.5 H : 1 V) means each side extends horizontally by $1.5 h$ **Step 2: Calculate the bottom width of the trapezoid** The bottom width is: $$b_{bottom} = b + 2 \cdot s \cdot h$$ Where $s$ = side slope (horizontal per unit vertical) = 1.5 $$b_{bottom} = 5.0 + 2 \times 1.5 \times 1.0$$ $$b_{bottom} = 5.0 + 3.0 = 8.0 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3: Compute the cross-sectional area** For a trapezoid with parallel sides $b$ (top) and $b_{bottom}$ (bottom) and height $h$: $$A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (b + b_{bottom}) \cdot h$$ $$A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (5.0 + 8.0) \cdot 1.0$$ $$A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 13.0 \cdot 1.0$$ $$A = 6.5 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4: Compute the total volume** Volume = Cross-sectional area $\times$ Length $$V = A \times L = 6.5 \times 1000 = 6500 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 5: Account for compaction shrinkage** Earth shrinks on compaction. A typical **compaction factor (shrinkage)** is 10–15%, meaning we need to excavate more loose soil. Loose volume required: $$V_{loose} = V_{compacted} \times (1 + \text{shrinkage factor})$$ Assuming shrinkage = 10%: $$V_{loose} = 6500 \times 1.10 = 7150 \text{ m}^3$$ **Final Answer**: The compacted earthwork required is **6500 m³**, and the corresponding loose excavation volume is **7150 m³**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Farm Road Types | Parameter | Earth Road (Kachha) | Moorum / Gravel Road | WBM Road | Bituminous Road | |---|---|---|---|---| | Initial cost per m | Lowest | Low | Medium | High | | Life (years) | 1–2 | 3–5 | 5–8 | 8–15 | | Maintenance | Very high | High | Medium | Low | | All-weather suitability | Poor | Moderate | Good | Excellent | | Camber | 1 in 30 | 1 in 33 | 1 in 40 | 1 in 45 | | CBR requirement of subgrade | < 2% | 2–5% | 5–10% | > 10% | | Typical use | Cart track, light traffic | Secondary farm road | Main farm road | Approach road | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CBR (California Bearing Ratio)** is the ratio of the force required to penetrate a soil sample to that required for a standard crushed stone, expressed as a percentage. It is the **key indicator of subgrade strength** for road design. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cross-Section of a Typical Farm Road (Block Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a standard WBM farm road cross-section, showing the interaction between each layer and the drainage system. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ROAD["FARM ROAD CROSS SECTION"] direction TB A["WEARING COURSE
Bituminous Surface or WBM
Thickness: 20 to 25 mm"] B["BASE COURSE
Crushed Stone Aggregate
Thickness: 75 to 100 mm"] C["SUB BASE COURSE
Locally Available Material
Thickness: 100 to 150 mm"] D["SUBGRADE
Compacted Natural Soil
CBR Greater Than 5 percent"] E["SHOULDER
Stabilized Earth Strip
Width: 1.0 m each side"] F["SIDE DRAIN
Lined Open Channel
Triangular or Trapezoidal"] G["CAMBER
Cross Slope 1 in 40
Drains Water to Side"] end A --> B B --> C C --> D A -.crown.-> G G -.water flow.-> F F -.discharge.-> H["CULVERT OR OUTLET"] E -.supports.-> A style A fill:#3d3d3d,color:#ffffff style B fill:#6b6b6b,color:#ffffff style C fill:#a0a0a0,color:#000000 style D fill:#c4a574,color:#000000 style E fill:#8b7355,color:#ffffff style F fill:#4a7ba8,color:#ffffff style G fill:#d4a017,color:#000000 style H fill:#2e5e3e,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Hierarchical Layout of Farm Road Network ```mermaid flowchart LR P["PUBLIC HIGHWAY
District Road"] M["MAIN FARM ROAD
Width: 4.0 to 5.0 m
Bituminous Surface"] S1["SECONDARY ROAD 1
Width: 3.0 m
WBM Surface"] S2["SECONDARY ROAD 2
Width: 3.0 m
WBM Surface"] F1["FIELD ROAD A
Width: 2.5 m
Moorum Surface"] F2["FIELD ROAD B
Width: 2.5 m
Moorum Surface"] C["CART TRACK
Width: 1.5 m
Earth Surface"] P --> M M --> S1 M --> S2 S1 --> F1 S2 --> F2 F1 --> C style P fill:#1a3a5c,color:#ffffff style M fill:#3d6b3d,color:#ffffff style S1 fill:#6b8e6b,color:#ffffff style S2 fill:#6b8e6b,color:#ffffff style F1 fill:#a0a57a,color:#000000 style F2 fill:#a0a57a,color:#000000 style C fill:#c4a574,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow for Farm Road Construction ```mermaid flowchart TB ST1["STEP 1: SITE SURVEY
Topographic and Soil Investigation"] ST2["STEP 2: ALIGNMENT DESIGN
Horizontal and Vertical Curves"] ST3["STEP 3: EARTHWORK
Excavation and Embankment Filling"] ST4["STEP 4: SUBGRADE PREPARATION
Compaction and Grading"] ST5["STEP 5: SUB BASE LAYERING
Spreading and Compacting LAM"] ST6["STEP 6: BASE COURSE LAYING
WBM Aggregate Spreading"] ST7["STEP 7: SURFACE COURSE
Bituminous Layer or Final WBM"] ST8["STEP 8: DRAINAGE CONSTRUCTION
Side Drains and Culverts"] ST9["STEP 9: SHOULDER AND FINISHING
Stabilization and Marking"] ST1 --> ST2 --> ST3 --> ST4 --> ST5 --> ST6 --> ST7 --> ST8 --> ST9 style ST1 fill:#4a90b8,color:#ffffff style ST2 fill:#4a90b8,color:#ffffff style ST3 fill:#6b8e23,color:#ffffff style ST4 fill:#6b8e23,color:#ffffff style ST5 fill:#8b7355,color:#ffffff style ST6 fill:#8b7355,color:#ffffff style ST7 fill:#3d3d3d,color:#ffffff style ST8 fill:#4a7ba8,color:#ffffff style ST9 fill:#2e5e3e,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Schematic of Camber and Water Drainage ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CAMBER_DIAGRAM["CAMBER PROFILE - END VIEW"] direction TB L1["LEFT EDGE
Road Level: 0 mm"] C1["CENTRE / CROWN
Road Level: 47 mm"] R1["RIGHT EDGE
Road Level: 0 mm"] SD1["LEFT SIDE DRAIN
Water Collection"] SD2["RIGHT SIDE DRAIN
Water Collection"] end L1 -.slope upward.-> C1 C1 -.slope downward.-> R1 L1 --> SD1 R1 --> SD2 style L1 fill:#a0a0a0,color:#000000 style C1 fill:#d4a017,color:#000000 style R1 fill:#a0a0a0,color:#000000 style SD1 fill:#4a7ba8,color:#ffffff style SD2 fill:#4a7ba8,color:#ffffff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define a farm road. List any four types of farm roads based on surface type.** **Model Answer:** A **farm road** is a permanent or semi-permanent engineered pathway within a farm, designed to provide all-weather vehicular access for machinery, transport, and personnel between fields, farmstead, storage, and market. **Four types of farm roads based on surface type:** 1. **Earth road** (Kachha road) — unpaved, made of local soil. 2. **Moorum / Gravel road** — surfaced with compacted gravel or moorum. 3. **Water Bound Macadam (WBM)** — compacted aggregate layer bonded with water. 4. **Bituminous road** — surface treated with bitumen for durability. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: What is camber in a farm road? State the recommended camber for (i) earth road, and (ii) WBM road.** **Model Answer:** **Camber** is the transverse slope provided across the road surface to drain rainwater quickly to the side drains, preventing waterlogging and damage to the road structure. Recommended camber values: - **Earth road**: 1 in 30 to 1 in 40 (≈ 3.33% to 2.5%) - **WBM road**: 1 in 40 to 1 in 50 (≈ 2.5% to 2.0%) --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the different components of a farm road cross-section with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** A main farm road is 4.0 m wide. Calculate the crown height if the camber is 1 in 40. If the same road is extended for 1 km on level ground, compute the total volume of earthwork required for an embankment of height 0.8 m with side slopes 1.5:1 (H:V). **(7 marks)** #### Solution to Q A(a): The cross-section of a farm road consists of the following components: | # | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Wearing Course / Surface** | Top layer, direct traffic contact, abrasion resistance | | 2 | **Base Course** | Load distribution from surface to sub-base | | 3 | **Sub-base Course** | Secondary load distribution, capillary break | | 4 | **Subgrade** | Foundation, must be stable and well-compacted | | 5 | **Shoulders** | Lateral support, emergency stopping | | 6 | **Camber** | Cross-slope for drainage | | 7 | **Side Drains** | Longitudinal drainage channels | | 8 | **Formation / Embankment** | Finished road profile above natural ground | **Diagram (description):** Top to bottom: Wearing Course (20–25 mm) → Base Course (75–100 mm) → Sub-base (100–150 mm) → Subgrade (compacted earth). On both sides: Shoulder (1.0 m each) → Side Drain (triangular, lined) → Embankment slope (1.5:1). #### Solution to Q A(b): **Part 1: Crown Height Calculation** Given: $W = 4.0$ m, $n = 40$ $$h = \frac{W}{2n} = \frac{4.0}{2 \times 40} = \frac{4.0}{80} = 0.05 \text{ m} = 50 \text{ mm}$$ **Part 2: Earthwork Volume** Given: - Formation width, $b = 4.0$ m - Embankment height, $h = 0.8$ m - Side slope, $s = 1.5$ (H:V) - Length, $L = 1000$ m Bottom width of trapezoidal section: $$b_{bottom} = b + 2sh = 4.0 + 2 \times 1.5 \times 0.8 = 4.0 + 2.4 = 6.4 \text{ m}$$ Cross-sectional area: $$A = \frac{1}{2}(b + b_{bottom}) \cdot h = \frac{1}{2}(4.0 + 6.4)(0.8) = \frac{1}{2}(10.4)(0.8) = 4.16 \text{ m}^2$$ Total volume: $$V = A \times L = 4.16 \times 1000 = 4160 \text{ m}^3$$ **Final Answer**: Crown height = **50 mm**; Total earthwork volume = **4160 m³**. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: (a)** Discuss the geometric design considerations of a farm road. Explain camber, gradient, and sight distance with suitable formulas. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** Design the side drain for a 1 km farm road given: rainfall intensity = 60 mm/h, runoff coefficient = 0.50, and catchment width = 5 m. The drain is triangular with side slopes 1:1. Calculate the depth of flow. **(7 marks)** #### Solution to Q B(a): **Geometric Design Considerations of Farm Roads:** 1. **Camber (Cross-slope)**: Provided to drain water off the road surface. $$h = \frac{W}{2n}$$ 2. **Gradient (Longitudinal slope)**: Expressed as a percentage. $$\text{Gradient \%} = \frac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}} \times 100$$ Maximum gradient recommended for farm roads is **1 in 15 to 1 in 20** (5% to 6.67%). Minimum gradient for drainage is **0.25% to 0.5%**. 3. **Sight Distance**: Minimum visibility required for safe stopping. $$SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \frac{V^2}{254(f \pm g)}$$ Where $V$ = speed (km/h), $t$ = reaction time (s), $f$ = friction coefficient, $g$ = gradient (decimal). 4. **Other considerations**: Road width, curve radius, super-elevation, formation level, and right-of-way. #### Solution to Q B(b): **Given:** - Length of road, $L = 1000$ m - Rainfall intensity, $I = 60$ mm/h = $0.060$ m/h - Runoff coefficient, $C = 0.50$ - Catchment width = road width + shoulders = 5 m - Drain shape: Triangular with side slopes 1:1 **Step 1: Compute the design discharge** $$Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$$ Catchment area = $L \times \text{width} = 1000 \times 5 = 5000$ m² Convert rainfall intensity: $I = 60$ mm/h = $0.060/3600$ m/s = $1.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s $$Q = 0.50 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-5} \times 5000$$ $$Q = 0.04167 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2: Apply Manning's equation for triangular drain** For a triangular section with side slopes 1:1, the depth of flow is $y$. - Top width, $T = 2y$ - Cross-sectional area, $A = y^2$ - Wetted perimeter, $P = 2y\sqrt{2}$ - Hydraulic radius, $R = A/P = y^2 / (2y\sqrt{2}) = y/(2\sqrt{2})$ Manning's equation: $$Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$ Assume Manning's $n = 0.025$ (concrete-lined drain) and slope $S = 0.5\% = 0.005$. $$Q = \frac{1}{0.025} \cdot y^2 \cdot \left(\frac{y}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2/3} \cdot (0.005)^{1/2}$$ $$0.04167 = 40 \cdot y^2 \cdot \left(\frac{y}{2.828}\right)^{0.667} \cdot 0.0707$$ $$0.04167 = 2.828 \cdot y^2 \cdot y^{0.667} / 2.828^{0.667}$$ $$0.04167 = 2.828 \cdot y^{2.667} / 2.122$$ $$0.04167 = 1.332 \cdot y^{2.667}$$ $$y^{2.667} = 0.04167 / 1.332 = 0.0313$$ $$y = 0.0313^{(1/2.667)} = 0.0313^{0.375}$$ Taking logarithms: $$\log y = 0.375 \times \log(0.0313) = 0.375 \times (-1.5043) = -0.5641$$ $$y = 10^{-0.5641} \approx 0.273 \text{ m} \approx 273 \text{ mm}$$ **Final Answer**: Depth of flow in the triangular side drain ≈ **275 mm**. The drain should be designed with a freeboard of at least 100 mm, giving total drain depth ≈ **375 mm**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > - Students frequently **forget to convert units** (e.g., mm/h to m/s). This leads to answer being off by a factor of 1000. > - The **friction coefficient sign convention** in SSD is often misapplied — downgrade uses $(f - g)$, not $(f + g)$. > - In earthwork volume, many students use the **wrong formula** (cylinder vs. prismoid). For uniform embankment along the length, the **average end area (trapezoidal) method** is acceptable. > - Forgetting **shrinkage factor** (10–15%) in earthwork leads to underestimation of required loose soil volume. > - Always include a **neat labelled diagram** in cross-section questions — examiners award 1–2 marks for the diagram alone. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for the topic "Road" — Module 4** - **Farm Road Definition**: Permanent pathway for vehicular movement within the farm, connecting fields to storage and markets. - **Road Classification**: Main, secondary, field road, cart track (by function); earth, moorum, WBM, bituminous (by surface). - **Standard Widths**: Main farm road 3.75–5.0 m; field road 2.5–3.0 m; cart track 1.5–2.0 m. - **Camber**: Earth road 1 in 30; WBM road 1 in 40; Bituminous 1 in 45 to 1 in 60. - **Gradient**: Maximum 1 in 15; Minimum (for drainage) 1 in 200. - **Cross-section Layers**: Wearing course → Base course → Sub-base → Subgrade (top to bottom). - **Key Formula 1**: $h = W / (2n)$ for crown height calculation. - **Key Formula 2**: $SSD = 0.278 V t + V^2 / [254(f \pm g)]$ for stopping sight distance. - **Key Formula 3**: $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ (Rational Method) for drainage design. - **Manning's Equation**: $V = (1/n) R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ for open channel flow in side drains. - **Earthwork Volume (Trapezoidal)**: $V = L \times (A_1 + A_2) / 2$. - **Compaction Shrinkage**: Add 10–15% to compacted volume to get loose excavation volume. - **Drainage Types**: Surface (camber + side drains + culverts) and sub-surface (filter + capillary cut-off). - **CBR (California Bearing Ratio)**: Subgrade strength indicator; minimum 5% for WBM, 10% for bituminous. - **Always state units** with every numerical answer. - **Always draw a labelled diagram** for cross-section questions (1–2 marks reserved for diagrams). - **Right of Way (RoW)**: 6–9 m for main farm road, includes road + drains + shoulders.

topics.title: Irrigation Works
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Irrigation Works

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Irrigation Works** refer to the engineered civil structures and hydraulic systems constructed to convey, store, regulate, distribute, and apply water from its source to agricultural fields for crop production. In the context of farm structures, these works include farm ponds, check dams, diversion weirs, canal networks, drop structures, cross drainage works, and well structures (open wells, tube wells, filter points) designed to ensure efficient water delivery with minimum conveyance and application losses.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Design of Farm Structures and Cost Estimation*, irrigation works also include temporary and permanent field channels, water-lifting devices, and the structural components necessary to ensure **command area development**, **water economy**, and **soil-moisture sustainability**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)
> [!NOTE]
> Think of irrigation works as the **"plumbing system of a farm"** — exactly like the pipes, taps, overhead tanks, and pumps in a house. Just as a house needs a **source** (overhead tank), **conduits** (pipes), **control valves** (taps), and **end-use fixtures** (showers, washing machines), a farm needs a **water source** (river/well/pond), **carriers** (canals/pipelines), **regulators** (gates/weirs), and **applicators** (field channels/furrows/sprinklers). The **design engineer** plays the role of the plumber who sizes every component so that water reaches the right field at the right time, in the right quantity, without leakage or wastage.

## 1.3 Classification of Irrigation Works
Irrigation works are broadly classified based on their function, location, and permanence:

### A. Based on Function
| Category | Examples | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage Works** | Farm ponds, small reservoirs, tanks | Store water for delayed use |
| **Diversion Works** | Weirs, barrages, check dams | Divert river/stream flow into canals |
| **Conveyance Works** | Canals, field channels, pipelines | Transport water from source to field |
| **Regulator Works** | Head regulators, cross regulators, gates | Control discharge and water levels |
| **Cross-Drainage Works** | Aqueducts, syphon aqueducts, super-passages | Carry canal over/under natural drainage |
| **Falls & Drops** | Drop structures, falls, chutes | Dissipate excess head in sloping channels |
| **Outlet & Escapes** | Modular outlets, surplus weirs, escapes | Deliver water to fields and drain excess |

### B. Based on Source of Supply
1. **Surface Water Irrigation Works** — River, stream, tank, reservoir based
2. **Groundwater Irrigation Works** — Open wells, tube wells, filter points, dug-cum-bore wells

### C. Based on Permanence
- **Permanent Works** — Masonry/concrete weirs, lined canals, pucca structures
- **Temporary/Semi-permanent Works** — Earthen check dams, unlined field channels

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard values used in KTU 2024 evaluations:
> - **Duty of water (D):** typically **435 to 864 ha/cumec** for canal irrigation
> - **Base period (B):** days during which water is supplied to a crop
> - **Delta (Δ):** total depth of water (cm) supplied during base period
> - **Crop Water Requirement (ETc):** mm/day, computed from **ET₀ × Kc**
> - **Freeboard (F):** additional height above **HFL (High Flood Level)** for embankments
> - **Saturated unit weight of soil (γ_sat):** **18–22 kN/m³**
> - **Specific yield of wells:** **0.05 to 0.25** (dimensionless)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Irrigation Works
The design of any irrigation work rests upon three pillars:
1. **Hydrology** — determining the available water (runoff, yield, dependable flow)
2. **Hydraulics** — designing the conveyance, head, velocity, and discharge
3. **Soil-Structure Mechanics** — ensuring the foundation, banks, and side slopes are stable

### 2.1.1 Water Requirement Equations
The relationship between **Duty (D)**, **Delta (Δ)**, and **Base Period (B)** is foundational to the design of any canal-based irrigation work.

**Delta (Δ):** The total depth of water (in cm) actually required by a crop to mature, supplied during its base period.

**Duty (D):** The area (in hectares) that can be irrigated by a **continuous supply of 1 cumec (1 m³/s)** of water for the entire base period.

**Base Period (B):** The total number of days between the first and last watering of a crop.

### 2.1.2 Canal Design Equations
For an unlined earthen canal in **Kennedy's Theory**:
$$ V_o = 0.55 \, m \, D^{0.64} \quad (\text{Kennedy's critical velocity}) $$

For **Lacey's Theory** (regime velocity):
$$ V = \left[ \frac{Q \, f^2}{140} \right]^{1/6} $$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta = \dfrac{8.64 \, B}{D}$ | Delta–Duty–Base Period relation (Δ in cm, B in days, D in ha/cumec) | cm |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{2.778 \, \Delta \, A}{B}$ | Discharge required to irrigate Area A with delta Δ in base period B | cumec |
| 3 | $V_o = 0.55 \, m \, D^{0.64}$ | Kennedy's critical (non-silting, non-scouring) velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $P = 4.75 \sqrt{Q}$ | Lacey's wetted perimeter (regime channel) | m |
| 5 | $R = 0.47 \left(\dfrac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3}$ | Lacey's hydraulic radius (f = silt factor) | m |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{f^{5/3}}{3340 \, Q^{1/6}}$ | Lacey's bed slope (regime condition) | — |
| 7 | $Q = A \, C \, \sqrt{R \, S}$ | Manning's / Chezy's uniform flow equation | m³/s |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{2.725 \, k \, H^{5/3}}{D}$ | Discharge over a rectangular weir (Rehbock simplified) | m³/s |
| 9 | $V = C \sqrt{R S}$ | Chezy's formula for open channel flow | m/s |
| 10 | $T_{ph} = 0.18 \, \dfrac{(Q)^{1/2}}{n}$ | Top width of a regime channel (Lacey empirical) | m |
| 11 | $T = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ | Top width of trapezoidal channel (Q = discharge, V = velocity) | m |
| 12 | $H = 0.9 \, q^{2/3}$ | Specific head for design of drop / fall structure | m |
| 13 | $V_{max} = 0.55 \, m \, D^{0.64}$ (Kennedy), $V = 0.50 \, \sqrt{f \, R}$ (Lacey) | Permissible velocity in canals | m/s |
| 14 | $\text{Storage Volume} = \text{Area} \times \text{Depth} \times \text{1/3}$ (trapezoidal) | Volume of farm pond | m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silt factor (f):** Lacey's silt factor, typical values:
> - Fine silt: $f = 0.8$ to $1.0$
> - Medium silt: $f = 1.0$ to $1.2$
> - Coarse silt: $f = 1.2$ to $1.5$

## 2.3 Real-World Utility
- **Delta–Duty relations** are used in *Command Area Development* planning
- **Kennedy's and Lacey's equations** are still used in *Alluvial Canal Design* in India, especially in **Northern India** (Indo-Gangetic plains)
- **Drop structures** are essential in **South Indian black cotton soil** topography
- **Check dams** are increasingly used in **watershed development programs** in Kerala (e.g., *MGNREGA / Jal Jeevan Mission*)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Delta – Duty – Base Period Relationship

This is the **most fundamental derivation** for KTU ESE 14-mark problems and is *guaranteed* in any module dealing with irrigation works.

### Statement
Show that $\Delta = \dfrac{8.64 \, B}{D}$ where $\Delta$ is delta in cm, $B$ is base period in days, and $D$ is duty in ha/cumec.

### Step 1: Define Duty
A discharge of **1 cumec** running continuously for **24 hours** for the entire base period $B$ irrigates $D$ hectares of land.

### Step 2: Volume Supplied
Volume of water supplied by 1 cumec in 1 day:

$$
V_{day} = 1 \text{ cumec} \times 86400 \text{ s} = 86400 \text{ m}^3
$$

Volume of water supplied in B days:
$$
V_B = 86400 \times B \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 3: Volume per Hectare
This volume is distributed over $D$ hectares. Therefore, depth of water (in metres) over each hectare:
$$
h = \frac{86400 \, B}{D \times 10^4} \text{ m}
$$

(The denominator $10^4$ m² comes from 1 hectare = 10,000 m².)

### Step 4: Convert to Centimeters
Since $1 \text{ m} = 100 \text{ cm}$:
$$
\Delta = \frac{86400 \, B}{D \times 10^4} \times 100 \text{ cm}
$$

### Step 5: Simplify
$$
\Delta = \frac{86400 \times 100}{10^4} \times \frac{B}{D} = 8.64 \, \frac{B}{D}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta = \frac{8.64 \, B}{D} \text{ cm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important equation** in irrigation engineering. Memorize it — KTU ESE 14-mark problems invariably start with this.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (Analytical Matrix)

### Example 1: Duty–Delta Computation
A canal has a **duty of 500 ha/cumec** for a base period of **120 days**. Compute the **delta**.

**Solution:**

$$
\Delta = \frac{8.64 \times B}{D} = \frac{8.64 \times 120}{500} = \frac{1036.8}{500} = 2.0736 \text{ cm}
$$

> **Δ ≈ 2.074 cm** ✓

### Example 2: Discharge Required
A crop requires a delta of **30 cm** with a base period of **90 days**. The area to be irrigated is **5000 ha**. Find the **discharge (Q)**.

**Solution:**

Using $Q = \dfrac{2.778 \, \Delta \, A}{B}$ (since 1 cumec-day = 2.778 hectare-cm):

$$
Q = \frac{2.778 \times 30 \times 5000}{90} = \frac{416700}{90}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 4630 \text{ cumec-cm/day} = \frac{4630}{8.64} = 535.88 \text{ cumec-days}}
$$

> Equivalently, **Q ≈ 4630 hectare-cm per day** ≈ **0.5359 cumec** of continuous supply.

### Example 3: Kennedy's Critical Velocity
A canal has discharge $Q = 50$ cumec. If the depth of flow $D = 2.0$ m, find the **critical velocity** using **Kennedy's equation** (assume $m = 1.0$).

**Solution:**

$$
V_o = 0.55 \, m \, D^{0.64} = 0.55 \times 1.0 \times (2.0)^{0.64}
$$

$$
(2.0)^{0.64} = 2^{0.64} \approx 1.5576
$$

$$
V_o = 0.55 \times 1.5576 = 0.857 \text{ m/s}
$$

> **V₀ ≈ 0.86 m/s**

### Example 4: Farm Pond Capacity
A trapezoidal farm pond has top dimensions **30 m × 20 m**, bottom dimensions **20 m × 10 m**, and depth **3 m**. Compute its **storage capacity**.

**Solution:**

Volume of frustum/trapezoidal prism:
$$
V = \frac{H}{3} \left[ A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2} \right]
$$

where $A_1 = 30 \times 20 = 600 \text{ m}^2$ and $A_2 = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ m}^2$.

$$
V = \frac{3}{3} \left[ 600 + 200 + \sqrt{600 \times 200} \right]
$$

$$
V = 1 \times \left[ 800 + \sqrt{120000} \right] = 800 + 346.41
$$

$$
\boxed{V = 1146.41 \text{ m}^3}
$$

### Example 5: Lacey Regime Channel
Design an irrigation channel in alluvial soil using **Lacey's theory** for $Q = 30$ cumec, $f = 1.0$.

**Solution:**

$$
R = 0.47 \left( \frac{Q}{f} \right)^{1/3} = 0.47 \left( \frac{30}{1.0} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
(30)^{1/3} = 3.107
$$

$$
R = 0.47 \times 3.107 = 1.460 \text{ m}
$$

Velocity:
$$
V = 0.50 \sqrt{f \, R} = 0.50 \sqrt{1.0 \times 1.460} = 0.50 \times 1.208 = 0.604 \text{ m/s}
$$

Wetted perimeter:
$$
P = 4.75 \sqrt{Q} = 4.75 \sqrt{30} = 4.75 \times 5.477 = 26.02 \text{ m}
$$

Discharge check:
$$
Q_{calc} = A \times V = (P \times R) \times V = 26.02 \times 1.460 \times 0.604
$$

$$
Q_{calc} = 22.95 \approx 23 \text{ cumec (close to 30, slight iteration needed)}
$$

> **R ≈ 1.46 m, V ≈ 0.60 m/s, P ≈ 26.0 m** ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of an Irrigation System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Source] --> B[Intake / Headworks]
    B --> C[Storage Reservoir / Farm Pond]
    C --> D[Conveyance Main Canal]
    D --> E[Branch Canal]
    E --> F[Distributary Canal]
    F --> G[Field Channel / Watercourse]
    G --> H[Application Method Furrow / Sprinkler / Drip]
    H --> I[Crop Root Zone]
    I --> J[Drainage Outlet]
    C --> K[Regulator Gates]
    D --> L[Cross Drainage Works]
    E --> M[Drop / Fall Structure]
    F --> N[Modular Outlet]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style J fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
```

## 4.2 Section of a Trapezoidal Earthen Canal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Freeboard F] --> B[Bank Top]
    B --> C[Inner Side Slope 1.5H:1V]
    C --> D[Water Surface]
    D --> E[Flow Depth D]
    E --> F[Bed Width B]
    F --> G[Outer Side Slope 2H:1V]
    style D fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d
    style F fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#34495e
```

> [!NOTE]
> Standard canal section conventions (as per **IS 7112** and KTU ESE expected answers):
> - Inner side slope (wet slope) = **1.5H : 1V** (typical for alluvium)
> - Outer side slope (dry slope) = **2H : 1V**
> - Freeboard = **0.50 m** for small channels, **1.0 m** for large canals

## 4.3 Components of a Diversion Weir (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Upstream River Bed] --> B[Weir Body Masonry]
    B --> C[Crest of Weir]
    C --> D[Downstream Apron]
    D --> E[End Sill]
    E --> F[Cutoff Wall]
    F --> G[Scour Depth Foundation]
    B --> H[Undersluice / Scouring Sluice]
    H --> I[Tail Channel / Downstream River]
    B --> J[Head Regulator Gate]
    J --> K[Canal Offtake]
    style B fill:#f5b041,stroke:#b9770e
    style C fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#922b21
    style K fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#1e8449
```

## 4.4 Drop Structure (Notch Fall) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Upstream Channel] --> B[Approach Section]
    B --> C[Crest Wall Notch]
    C --> D[Vertical Drop Wall]
    D --> E[Downstream Stilling Basin]
    E --> F[Dissipating Blocks]
    F --> G[Tail Water Level]
    G --> H[Downstream Channel]
    style C fill:#f39c12,stroke:#9c640c
    style E fill:#5dade2,stroke:#1a5276
    style F fill:#7d3c98,stroke:#4a235a
```

## 4.5 Component Interaction Matrix (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hydrology] --> B[Runoff Estimation]
    A --> C[Yield Analysis]
    B --> D[Design Discharge]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Canal Design Lacey Kennedy]
    D --> F[Structure Design Weir Drop CDW]
    E --> G[Cost Estimation Module 5]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Final BoQ]
    style A fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#1b4f72
    style H fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Duty of water". What are the factors affecting it?**

**Model Answer:**
> **Duty of water (D)** is defined as the **area of land (in hectares)** that can be irrigated by a **continuous supply of 1 cumec (1 m³/s) of water** for the entire base period of the crop.
>
> **Factors affecting duty:**
> 1. **Soil type** — Sandy soils have higher duty; clayey soils have lower duty.
> 2. **Climate** — Temperature, humidity, wind, rainfall.
> 3. **Crop type** — Different crops have different water needs.
> 4. **Method of irrigation** — Drip/sprinkler > furrow > flooding.
> 5. **Canal conditions** — Seepage, evaporation, water-holding capacity.
> 6. **Slope and topography** of the command area.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Kennedy's and Lacey's theories of canal design.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Kennedy's Theory | Lacey's Theory |
|---|---|---|
| Year | 1895 | 1930 |
| Equation | $V_o = 0.55 \, m \, D^{0.64}$ | $V = 0.50 \sqrt{fR}$ |
| Foundation | Empirical, based on observations on Upper Bari Doab Canal | Semi-theoretical, based on regime concept |
| Variables | $m$ (critical velocity ratio), $D$ (depth) | $f$ (silt factor), $R$ (hydraulic radius) |
| Limitation | $m$ must be determined by trial; no silt factor | Difficult to estimate $f$ accurately |
| Applicability | Restricted to alluvial channels in equilibrium | Wider, including regime conditions |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DROP STRUCTURE & CANAL DESIGN**
> - **Do not** confuse *Delta* with *Duty* — Delta has units of **cm (depth)**, Duty has units of **ha/cumec**.
> - **Do not** forget to convert $1 \text{ cumec-day} = 8.64$ ha·m. The factor **2.778** comes from 1/(0.36), not from a generic conversion.
> - **Do not** use a silt factor $f$ without units; $f$ is **dimensionless** (Lacey's).
> - In freeboard problems, the answer is reported in **m**, not cm.
> - **Critical Step:** Always check that the calculated velocity falls between **non-silting** and **non-scouring** limits.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**(a) (7 Marks)** A crop requires a **delta of 50 cm** and has a **base period of 120 days**. Determine:
1. The **duty of water** in ha/cumec.
2. The **discharge (in cumec)** required to irrigate an area of **4000 hectares**.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Design a regime channel by **Lacey's theory** for a discharge of **40 cumec** in alluvium with silt factor **f = 1.1**. Also compute the **bed slope**.

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute Duty (D)**

Using the relation:
$$
\Delta = \frac{8.64 \, B}{D} \implies D = \frac{8.64 \, B}{\Delta}
$$

$$
D = \frac{8.64 \times 120}{50} = \frac{1036.8}{50} = 20.736 \text{ ha/cumec}
$$

> **D ≈ 20.74 ha/cumec** ✓ (1 mark)

**Step 2: Compute Discharge (Q)**

Using:
$$
Q = \frac{2.778 \, \Delta \, A}{B}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{2.778 \times 50 \times 4000}{120} = \frac{555600}{120} = 4630 \text{ cumec-cm/day}
$$

Convert to cumec:
$$
Q = \frac{4630}{86400} = 0.0536 \text{ cumec}
$$

Equivalently, $Q = 0.0536$ m³/s of **continuous** supply.

Or by alternate route (using the 1 cumec = 8.64 ha·m per day):
$$
Q = \frac{A}{D} = \frac{4000}{20.736} = 192.9 \text{ cumec-day/ha-equiv}
$$

Re-deriving: $Q$ (continuous) $= 4000 / (D \times 8.64) = 4000/(20.736 \times 8.64) = 22.32$ **hectare-m/day**

But the cleaner form is:
$$
\boxed{Q_{continuous} = 0.0536 \text{ cumec}}
$$

> (3 marks for the formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for the unit, 1 mark for the final answer)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Hydraulic radius (R)**
$$
R = 0.47 \left( \frac{Q}{f} \right)^{1/3} = 0.47 \left( \frac{40}{1.1} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
\frac{40}{1.1} = 36.364, \quad (36.364)^{1/3} = 3.310
$$

$$
R = 0.47 \times 3.310 = 1.556 \text{ m}
$$

> **R ≈ 1.56 m** (1 mark)

**Step 2: Velocity (V)**
$$
V = 0.50 \sqrt{f R} = 0.50 \sqrt{1.1 \times 1.556} = 0.50 \sqrt{1.7116} = 0.50 \times 1.308
$$

$$
V = 0.654 \text{ m/s}
$$

> **V ≈ 0.65 m/s** (1 mark)

**Step 3: Wetted Perimeter (P)**
$$
P = 4.75 \sqrt{Q} = 4.75 \sqrt{40} = 4.75 \times 6.3246
$$

$$
P = 30.04 \text{ m}
$$

> **P ≈ 30.04 m** (1 mark)

**Step 4: Cross-section Area (A)**
$$
A = P \times R = 30.04 \times 1.556 = 46.74 \text{ m}^2
$$

> **A ≈ 46.74 m²** (1 mark)

**Step 5: Bed Slope (S)**
$$
S = \frac{f^{5/3}}{3340 \, Q^{1/6}} = \frac{(1.1)^{5/3}}{3340 \times (40)^{1/6}}
$$

$$
(1.1)^{5/3} = 1.1696, \quad (40)^{1/6} = 1.848
$$

$$
S = \frac{1.1696}{3340 \times 1.848} = \frac{1.1696}{6172.32} = 0.000189
$$

> **S ≈ 1.89 × 10⁻⁴** or **1 in 5290** (2 marks)

**Final Answer for Part (b):**
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Hydraulic radius R | 1.56 m |
| Velocity V | 0.65 m/s |
| Wetted perimeter P | 30.04 m |
| Cross-section A | 46.74 m² |
| Bed slope S | 1.89 × 10⁻⁴ (1 in 5290) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**(a) (7 Marks)** A **farm pond** of trapezoidal section has top dimensions **40 m × 25 m**, bottom **20 m × 10 m**, and depth **4 m**. Calculate the **storage capacity** of the pond. If the design rainfall runoff from a 5 ha catchment is to be stored, with runoff coefficient **C = 0.3** and rainfall = **80 mm**, verify if the pond is adequate.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Explain with a neat sketch the **components of a diversion weir**. List the design considerations for a **weir foundation**.

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Storage Capacity of Farm Pond**

Trapezoidal frustum formula:
$$
V = \frac{H}{3} \left[ A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2} \right]
$$

where $H = 4$ m, $A_1 = 40 \times 25 = 1000$ m², $A_2 = 20 \times 10 = 200$ m².

$$
\sqrt{A_1 A_2} = \sqrt{1000 \times 200} = \sqrt{200000} = 447.21 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V = \frac{4}{3} \left[ 1000 + 200 + 447.21 \right] = \frac{4}{3} \times 1647.21
$$

$$
V = 2196.28 \text{ m}^3
$$

> **Storage Capacity ≈ 2196.3 m³** (4 marks)

**Step 2: Runoff Volume from Catchment**

$$
V_{runoff} = C \times P \times A
$$

where $C = 0.3$, $P = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08$ m, $A = 5 \text{ ha} = 50,000$ m².

$$
V_{runoff} = 0.3 \times 0.08 \times 50000 = 1200 \text{ m}^3
$$

> **Runoff Volume = 1200 m³** (2 marks)

**Step 3: Adequacy Check**

Since storage capacity (2196.3 m³) > runoff volume (1200 m³), the pond is **adequate**, with a **free capacity of 996.3 m³** (≈ 45% spare).

> (1 mark for conclusion)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Components of a Diversion Weir:**

1. **Weir body / main wall** — Masonry or concrete structure across the river that raises water level.
2. **Crest of the weir** — The top of the weir body over which water spills.
3. **Upstream and downstream aprons** — Horizontal masonry floors that prevent scour.
4. **End piers** — Used to separate the weir proper from the undersluice bays.
5. **Undersluices (scouring sluices)** — Sluice gates at the bed level for silt removal.
6. **Head regulator** — Controls flow into the off-taking canal.
7. **Cut-off walls** — Vertical walls at upstream and downstream ends to prevent piping.
8. **Talus or launching apron** — Loose stone protection at the toe.
9. **Fish ladder** (if needed) — For fish migration.
10. **Bridge / roadway** on top for inspection and traffic.

**Design Considerations for Weir Foundation:**

1. **Scour depth** — Foundation must be below maximum scour, calculated using **Lacey's equation** $D_{scour} = 1.34 \left( \frac{q^2}{f} \right)^{1/3}$.
2. **Permissible stress** — Foundation soil must safely bear the superimposed load.
3. **Piping failure** — Sheet piles / cut-off walls to prevent subsoil flow.
4. **Seepage uplift** — Hydraulic gradient must be within safe limits.
5. **Afflux** — Raise in upstream water level should not cause flooding.
6. **Stability against sliding and overturning** — Factor of safety ≥ **1.5**.
7. **Material availability** — Use locally available stone/brick in rural farm weirs.
8. **Subsoil water conditions** — Avoid highly pervious foundations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Irrigation Works**

- [ ] **Duty (D)** = ha/cumec; **Delta (Δ)** = cm (depth); **Base Period (B)** = days.
- [ ] **Golden formula:** $\Delta = 8.64 B / D$ and $Q = 2.778 \Delta A / B$.
- [ ] **Kennedy's velocity** $V_o = 0.55 m D^{0.64}$ — empirical, depth-based.
- [ ] **Lacey's regime equations** depend on **silt factor (f)**, dimensionless.
- [ ] **Lacey's R, V, P, S** — all derived from **Q and f** alone.
- [ ] **Trapezoidal channel** — inner slope **1.5H:1V**, outer slope **2H:1V**.
- [ ] **Freeboard** — **0.5 m** for minor, **1.0 m** for major canals.
- [ ] **Weir foundation** must extend **below scour depth**.
- [ ] **Farm pond** uses **trapezoidal frustum** volume formula.
- [ ] **Runoff estimation** $V = C \times P \times A$ (Rational Method).
- [ ] **Cross-drainage works** — Aqueduct, Syphon, Super-passage, Level Crossing.
- [ ] **Drop structure** designed using $H = 0.9 q^{2/3}$.
- [ ] **Diversion weir** components: crest, apron, undersluice, regulator, cut-off, end-pier.
- [ ] **Earthen dam** side slopes: upstream **2.5:1 to 3:1**, downstream **2:1 to 2.5:1** (with freeboard).
- [ ] **Tube well** discharge $Q = 2 \pi T s K / \ln(R/r_w)$ (Darcy / Theis approximation).

> **Master these 15 points and you are exam-ready for Module 4 — Irrigation Works.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steel Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Steel Structures # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Steel Structures** in the context of agricultural and farm engineering refer to load-bearing structural systems fabricated from hot-rolled or built-up structural steel sections, designed to resist gravitational, environmental, and functional loads as per the **Indian Standard IS 800:2007** (General Construction in Steel — Code of Practice) and **IS 875** (Code of Practice for Design Loads). These structures form the skeleton of farm buildings such as **go-downs, dairy sheds, poultry houses, machinery garages, warehouses, and processing halls**, where prefabrication, large column-free spans, speed of erection, and durability against agricultural chemicals are paramount. A steel structure is an assembly of **tension members (ties), compression members (struts/columns), flexural members (beams/purlins), and roof trusses**, interconnected through **bolted, riveted, or welded connections** and transferring all superimposed dead, live, wind, and crop-storage loads safely to the foundation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus**: Under Module 4, the emphasis is on selecting appropriate steel sections, understanding load paths in a typical farm roof truss, computing member forces, checking section capacity, and arriving at a preliminary cost estimate using the **steel weight × unit rate** methodology. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **bicycle wheel**: the outer rim is held rigid by a hub at the centre through thin **spokes**. The spokes do not bend; they only **pull** (tension) or **push** (compression). A steel roof truss works on the same principle — a triangulated network of straight steel bars (called *members*) where each bar either pulls or pushes along its axis. The whole assembly behaves like a rigid skeleton because triangles cannot deform without changing the length of at least one side. **Real-world farm example**: A 15 m × 30 m paddy storage godown in Thrissur has a **double-pitched roof truss** at every 4 m spacing. The top chord is in compression (like the leaning rafters), the bottom chord is in tension (like the bottom tie of a wooden door frame), and the vertical/diagonal web members carry wind and asymmetric loads — exactly like the spokes of a wheel. ## 1.3 Material Properties of Structural Steel Used in Farm Structures | Property | Value / Range | Significance | |---|---|---| | Density of steel ($\rho_s$) | **7850 kg/m³** | Used for self-weight calculation | | Young's Modulus ($E$) | **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** | Deflection and buckling check | | Poisson's ratio ($\nu$) | **0.30** | Strain transformation | | Yield stress ($f_y$) for **Fe 410** | **250 N/mm²** | Most common farm-grade steel | | Yield stress ($f_y$) for **Fe 540** | **410 N/mm²** | High-strength option | | Ultimate tensile strength ($f_u$) | 410–540 N/mm² | For connection design | | Coefficient of thermal expansion ($\alpha$) | **12 × 10⁻⁶ / °C** | Expansion joint design | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Fact**: For most farm buildings, **Fe 410 (IS 2062)** grade steel is permitted by IS 800:2007, with a characteristic yield strength $f_y = 250$ MPa. All permissible stress derivations in this module are anchored to this value. ## 1.4 Types of Steel Sections Used in Farm Construction 1. **Hot-Rolled I-Sections (ISMB / ISJB / ISLB)** — Primary columns and beams. 2. **Channels (ISMC / ISLC)** — Purlins, girts, and compound column legs. 3. **Equal and Unequal Angles (ISA / ISUA)** — Truss members (chords and webs) and bracing. 4. **Hollow Steel Sections (HSS / SHS / RHS)** — Modern trusses, handrails, livestock pens. 5. **Cold-Formed Sections (CFS)** — Light-gauge purlins and wall cladding girts in poultry houses. 6. **Plates and Flats** — Built-up sections and gusset plates at connections. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Loads Acting on a Farm Steel Structure Loads are computed as per **IS 875 (Parts 1 to 5)**. The four principal load types relevant to Module 4 are: ### (a) Dead Load (IS 875 Part 1) Self-weight of the structure **plus** permanent fittings (roof sheeting, purlins, sag rods, permanent equipment, fixed grain bins). $$D.L. = \text{Volume of member} \times \rho_s \times g$$ ### (b) Live Load / Imposed Load (IS 875 Part 2) For farm roofs accessible only for maintenance: **0.75 kN/m²** (slope $\leq 10°$) reducing to **0.40 kN/m²** (slope $> 20°$). For floors of grain godowns or processing halls: **5.0–10.0 kN/m²** depending on stored material bulk density. ### (c) Wind Load (IS 875 Part 3) The design wind pressure is given by: $$p_z = 0.6 \times V_z^2 \quad \text{(N/m²)}$$ where $V_z = V_b \times k_1 \times k_2 \times k_3 \times k_4$ is the design wind speed at height $z$, and the **0.6** factor converts kinetic to pressure. ### (d) Crop / Storage Load (Special to Agriculture) For onion storage structures, paddy godowns, or hay barns, the crop weight is treated as a **live load with impact factor** depending on filling method. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every KTU-board-critical formula** you must memorise for the Module 4 ESE. Symbol $A_g$ denotes gross area; $A_v$ denotes shear area; $Z_p$ is the plastic section modulus. | Member Type | Governing Formula | Permissible Stress Basis | |---|---|---| | Axial Tension | $T_d = A_g \times f_y / \gamma_{mT}$ | $\gamma_{mT} = 1.10$ | | Tension rupture (net section) | $T_{dn} = \alpha \times A_n \times f_u / \gamma_{mT}$ | $\alpha = 0.8$ for connected leg of angle | | Block shear | $T_{db} = \dfrac{A_{vg} \times f_y}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{m0}} + \dfrac{0.9\,A_{tn} \times f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$ | $\gamma_{m0}=1.10,\ \gamma_{m1}=1.25$ | | Axial Compression (short column) | $P_d = A_e \times f_{cd}$ | $f_{cd} = f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ if $\lambda \le 0.2$ | | Compression (intermediate) | $f_{cd} = \dfrac{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda^2}}$ | $\phi = 0.5\,\left[1 + \alpha(\lambda - 0.2) + \lambda^2\right]$ | | Slenderness ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r}$ | $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ | | Maximum $\lambda$ (truss members) | $\lambda_{max} \le 350$ (tension), $\le 180$ (compression) | IS 800 cl. 7.1.2 | | Bending stress (compact) | $M_d = Z_p \times f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ | | Shear capacity (web) | $V_d = A_v \times f_{yw} / (\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{m0})$ | $f_{yw} = f_y$ for rolled sections | | Deflection limit (purlins/beams) | $\delta_{max} = L / 360$ (industrial), $L / 180$ (roof) | Serviceability | | Bolted connection (bearing) | $V_{dpb} = 2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u / \gamma_{mb}$ | $k_b$ = 0.5 / 0.6 / 0.45 | | Welded connection (fillet) | $R_d = 0.7\,t_t \times f_{uw} / \gamma_{mw}$ | $f_{uw} = f_u / \sqrt{3}$ | | Steel weight for costing | $W = \text{Total length} \times \text{Unit weight (kg/m)}$ | Unit rate in ₹/kg | > [!NOTE] > **Critical pipe-safe note**: Wherever the absolute-value symbol was needed (e.g., $\vert \lambda \le 0.2 \vert$), it has been re-expressed using contextual phrasing to keep the markdown table parser stable. Inside LaTeX blocks you may freely use $\vert$ or $\mid$. ## 2.3 Design Philosophy — Limit State Method (LSM) IS 800:2007 mandates the **Limit State Method**, which ensures: 1. **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** — Structure must not collapse under factored loads ($1.5\,DL + 1.5\,LL$ or $0.9\,DL \pm 1.5\,WL$). 2. **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)** — Structure must remain functional under **un-factored** loads (deflection, vibration, corrosion). Partial safety factors $\gamma_m$ account for material variability: - $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ (yielding) - $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ (ultimate) - $\gamma_{mT} = 1.10$ (tension member) - $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (weld) - $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ (bolt) ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Farm Engineering - **Poultry sheds (clear-span 12–18 m)**: Pre-engineered steel buildings (PEB) with cold-formed Z-purlins and prismatic truss. - **Grain silos and godowns**: Composite steel-concrete columns and lattice trusses for 25–30 m clear spans. - **Dairy parlour sheds**: Light-gauge steel framing with anti-corrosion epoxy coating for ammonia-rich environments. - **Greenhouse frames**: Galvanised tubular steel sections for high corrosion resistance and transparency. - **Equipment garages / tractor sheds**: Conventional angle-section roof trusses with AC sheet roofing. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Euler's Critical Buckling Load for a Farm Column Consider a slender pin-ended steel column of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, subjected to axial compressive load $P$. Under a small lateral perturbation $y$, the bending moment is $M = -P\,y$, leading to the differential equation: $$ \begin{aligned} EI\,\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} + P\,y &= 0 \\ \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} + k^2\,y &= 0, \quad \text{where } k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI} \end{aligned} $$ The general solution is $y = A\sin(kx) + B\cos(kx)$. Applying boundary conditions $y(0)=0$ and $y(L)=0$: $$ \begin{aligned} y(0) &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; B = 0 \\ y(L) &= A\sin(kL) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sin(kL) = 0 \\ kL &= n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \end{aligned} $$ For the lowest (governing) buckling mode, $n = 1$, hence: $$ \begin{aligned} k^2 &= \dfrac{\pi^2}{L^2} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{EI} \\ P_{cr} &= \dfrac{\pi^2\,EI}{L^2} \end{aligned} $$ Introducing effective length $L_e = K\,L$ to handle other end conditions (fixed, free, partially restrained): $$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2\,EI}{L_e^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2\,EA}{(L_e/r)^2}$$ where $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ is the radius of gyration and $\lambda = L_e/r$ is the **slenderness ratio**. This expression is the foundation of every column-design problem in IS 800. ## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Length Factor $K$ for Typical Farm Truss End Conditions | End Condition Sketch | $K$ Value | Use in Farm Structure | |---|---|---| | Both ends pinned | **1.00** | Truss chords at supports | | One fixed, one pinned | **0.80** | Columns rigidly bolted at base | | Both ends fixed | **0.65** | Built-up rack columns | | One fixed, one free | **2.00** | Cantilever posts for solar dryers | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Design of a Farm Roof Truss Tension Member **Problem statement:** A bottom-chord member of a 16 m span paddy godown truss carries a factored tensile force of **210 kN**. Select a suitable double-angle section using **20 mm dia. bolts of grade 4.6** through the connected leg. ### Step 1 — Required gross area $$ \begin{aligned} T_d &= \dfrac{A_g \times f_y}{\gamma_{mT}} \\ A_{g,\text{req}} &= \dfrac{T_d \times \gamma_{mT}}{f_y} = \dfrac{210 \times 10^3 \times 1.10}{250} \\ A_{g,\text{req}} &= 924 \text{ mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Tentative double-angle selection From the steel section tables (SP:6), two **ISA 65 × 65 × 6 mm** angles back-to-back provide: $$ \begin{aligned} A_{g,\text{one angle}} &= 757 \text{ mm}^2 \\ A_g &= 2 \times 757 = 1514 \text{ mm}^2 \ \text{(satisfies Step 1)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Strength check (gross-section yielding) $$ \begin{aligned} T_{dg} &= \dfrac{A_g \times f_y}{\gamma_{mT}} = \dfrac{1514 \times 250}{1.10} \\ T_{dg} &= 344.1 \times 10^3 \text{ N} = 344.1 \text{ kN} \ \text{> } 210 \text{ kN} \ \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Net-section rupture check Assuming **one bolt-hole of 22 mm dia.** per angle through the connected leg: $$ \begin{aligned} A_n &= (65 - 6/2 - 22) \times 6 = (65 - 25)\times 6 = 240 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ per angle} \\ A_{n,\text{total}} &= 2 \times 240 = 480 \text{ mm}^2 \\ T_{dn} &= 0.8 \times A_n \times f_u / \gamma_{mT} \\ f_u &= 410 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ for Fe 410} \\ T_{dn} &= \dfrac{0.8 \times 480 \times 410}{1.10} = 143.1 \times 10^3 \text{ N} = 143.1 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **143.1 kN < 210 kN** — the section is **NOT safe at the net section**. We must either **upgrade** to ISA 75 × 75 × 8 or add **bypass holes / packing plates**. This is exactly the trap most students fall into during the ESE. ### Step 5 — Revised selection: ISA 75 × 75 × 8 (back-to-back) $$ \begin{aligned} A_g &= 2 \times 1150 = 2300 \text{ mm}^2 \\ A_n &= 2 \times (75 - 4 - 22) \times 8 = 2 \times 392 = 784 \text{ mm}^2 \\ T_{dn} &= 0.8 \times 784 \times 410 / 1.10 = 233.9 \text{ kN} > 210 \text{ kN} \ \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Connection Types | Parameter | Hot-Riveted | Black Bolted (Bearing-type) | HSFG Bolted (Friction-grip) | Site-Welded | |---|---|---|---|---| | Failure mode | Shear of rivet | Bearing + shear of bolt | Slip at interface | Throat shear | | Slip permitted? | No | Yes (limited) | **No (friction grip)** | No | | Speed of erection | Slow | Fast | Medium | Medium | | Skill required | High | Low | Medium | **Very high** | | Cost (₹/kN) | High | Low | Medium | Medium | | Typical farm use | Old legacy godowns | **Most common in PEB** | Heavy machinery halls | Truss chord splices | | IS code reference | IS 800 (older) | IS 800 cl. 10.3 | IS 800 cl. 10.4 | IS 816 / IS 800 cl. 11 | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Typical Farm Roof Truss — Geometry & Load Path (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A1["LEFT SUPPORT"] --> B1["BOTTOM CHORD
TENSION MEMBER
ISA 2L 75x75x8"] B1 --> C1["INTERNAL NODE 1
WEB CONNECTION
GUSSET PLATE 12MM"] C1 --> D1["TOP CHORD LEFT
COMPRESSION MEMBER
ISA 2L 80x80x10"] D1 --> E1["RIDGE NODE
APEX CONNECTION
GUSSET PLATE 12MM"] E1 --> F1["TOP CHORD RIGHT
COMPRESSION MEMBER
ISA 2L 80x80x10"] F1 --> G1["INTERNAL NODE 2
WEB CONNECTION"] G1 --> H1["BOTTOM CHORD RIGHT
TENSION MEMBER
ISA 2L 75x75x8"] H1 --> A2["RIGHT SUPPORT"] B1 -.->|"VERTICAL WEB
TENSION"| C1 C1 -.->|"DIAGONAL WEB
ZERO FORCE IF
SYMMETRIC LOAD"| F1 F1 -.->|"VERTICAL WEB
TENSION"| G1 D1 -.->|"PURLIN AT 1.5M c/c
LOAD FROM ROOF SHEET"| E1 style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style A2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style E1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000 style B1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style H1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style D1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style F1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram**: Yellow nodes are the supports (where the truss rests on columns). Green bars are tension members (the bottom chord pulls the walls inward). Blue bars are compression members (the top chord pushes down). The red apex node is the ridge where both rafters meet. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Load Path in a Farm Steel Godown ```mermaid flowchart LR L1["DEAD LOAD
Roof sheets, purlins, truss self-weight"] --> N1["PURRLIN
ISMC 100 or Z-150 CFS"] L2["LIVE LOAD
Maintenance access 0.75 kN/m2"] --> N1 L3["WIND LOAD
IS 875 Part 3
Suction or pressure"] --> N1 N1 --> N2["ROOF TRUSS
Double-pitched Pratt
Span L = 16m"] N2 --> N3["TRUSS TO COLUMN
Bolted shoe plate
connection"] N3 --> N4["STEEL COLUMN
ISMB 300 or built-up
2-ISMC 250"] N4 --> N5["BASE PLATE
12mm thick, M20 anchor bolts"] N5 --> N6["FOUNDATION
Isolated RCC footing
1.5m x 1.5m x 0.6m"] N6 --> N7["SOIL
Safe SBC assumed
150 kN/m2"] style L1 fill:#FFE4B5 style L2 fill:#FFE4B5 style L3 fill:#FFE4B5 style N1 fill:#B0E0E6 style N2 fill:#87CEEB style N3 fill:#FF6347 style N4 fill:#90EE90 style N5 fill:#FFA500 style N6 fill:#D3D3D3 style N7 fill:#8B4513 ``` ## 4.3 Bolt Connection Schematic (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["MAIN PLATE
Thickness t = 10mm"] --> B1["BOLT M16 GR 4.6
Shear plane"] P2["CONNECTED ANGLE LEG
Thickness t = 8mm"] --> B1 B1 --> N1["BEARING STRESS
sigma_b = V / d x t"] B1 --> N2["SHEAR STRESS
tau_v = V / pi x d^2 / 4"] B1 --> N3["SPACING CHECK
p >= 2.5d
e >= 1.5d"] style P1 fill:#C0C0C0 style P2 fill:#C0C0C0 style B1 fill:#FF6347 ``` ## 4.4 Tension vs Compression Member Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart LR A["AXIAL MEMBER
under load P"] --> B["TENSILE FORCE T"] A --> C["COMPRESSIVE FORCE C"] B --> B1["Section elongates
sigma = T / Ag"] B --> B2["Failure: yielding
or rupture at net
section"] C --> C1["Section shortens
sigma = C / Ae"] C --> C2["Failure: buckling
governed by KL / r
i.e. slenderness"] style B fill:#90EE90 style C fill:#FFA07A ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question] **List any four properties of structural steel that make it suitable for farm buildings.** **Model Answer (approx. 80–100 words):** (i) **High strength-to-weight ratio** allowing long clear spans for farm godowns without intermediate columns. (ii) **Homogeneous and isotropic** behaviour — predictable strength in all directions. (iii) **Ductility** enabling large deformations before failure, providing warning. (iv) **Speed of prefabrication** — bolted connections reduce on-site time, critical in remote farms. (v) **Reusability** — steel members from dismantled poultry sheds can be relocated. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question] **Distinguish between a tension member and a compression member with one farm-structure example each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Tension Member | Compression Member | |---|---|---| | Internal force | Pulls along axis | Pushes along axis | | Failure mode | Yielding / rupture of net section | Buckling | | Design governed by | Cross-sectional area | Slenderness ratio $\lambda = KL/r$ | | Farm example | Bottom chord of a godown roof truss | Top chord (rafter) of a hay barn truss | | Section preference | Solid flats / angles | Hollow / I-sections for higher $r$ | ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > KTU follows an **internal choice pattern in ESE Part B** for Module questions. Solve **either** (a) OR (b). Each sub-part carries **7 marks**, requiring a complete design solution with steps, sketches and final member selection. --- ### ❖ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **A single-storey dairy cattle shed is to be constructed as a steel structure with a clear span of 12 m and a length of 30 m. The roof is a double-pitched Pratt truss with a rise of 3 m at centre. Trusses are placed at 4 m centres. Roofing is with AC sheets. Design the bottom chord member assuming it carries a tensile force of 180 kN (factored). Adopt 20 mm dia. bolts of grade 4.6, Fe 410 steel, and double-angle section. Check also the slenderness and block shear capacity.** #### (a) [7 Marks] — Section selection and gross/net strength check **Step 1 — Required gross area:** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{g,\text{req}} &= \dfrac{T_d \times \gamma_{mT}}{f_y} = \dfrac{180 \times 10^3 \times 1.10}{250} = 792 \text{ mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Try 2 ISA 65 × 65 × 6 mm:** $A_g = 2 \times 757 = 1514 \text{ mm}^2$ **Step 3 — Gross section yielding:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{dg} = \dfrac{1514 \times 250}{1.10} = 344.1 \text{ kN} \gg 180 \text{ kN} \ \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Net section (one 22 mm hole per connected leg):** $$ \begin{aligned} A_n &= 2 \times (65 - 3 - 22) \times 6 = 2 \times 240 = 480 \text{ mm}^2 \\ T_{dn} &= \dfrac{0.8 \times 480 \times 410}{1.10} = 143.1 \text{ kN} \\ 143.1 \text{ kN} &< 180 \text{ kN} \ \boldsymbol{\times} \ \text{UNSAFE} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Upgrade to 2 ISA 70 × 70 × 8 mm:** $A_g = 2 \times 1070 = 2140 \text{ mm}^2$; $A_n = 2 \times (70 - 4 - 22) \times 8 = 704 \text{ mm}^2$ $$ T_{dn} = \dfrac{0.8 \times 704 \times 410}{1.10} = 210.0 \text{ kN} > 180 \text{ kN} \ \checkmark $$ #### (b) [7 Marks] — Slenderness and block-shear check **Step 6 — Slenderness of bottom chord (assume length 4 m between nodes):** $$ \begin{aligned} r_{\text{min}} \text{ for ISA 70×70×8} &= 13.6 \text{ mm (about z-z axis for single)} \\ r_{\text{combined (y-y)}} &\approx 21.6 \text{ mm (back-to-back, 8 mm gusset)} \\ \lambda &= \dfrac{KL}{r} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 4000}{21.6} = 185 \end{aligned} $$ Since $\lambda = 185 < \lambda_{max} = 350$ for tension members (IS 800 cl. 7.1.2) → **slenderness SAFE**. **Step 7 — Block shear capacity (2 bolts in line, edge distance 35 mm):** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{vg} &= 2 \times (35 + 35 + 35) \times 8 = 1680 \text{ mm}^2 \\ A_{tn} &= (35 + 35) \times 8 - 0.5 \times 22 \times 8 = 560 - 88 = 472 \text{ mm}^2 \\ T_{db} &= \dfrac{A_{vg} \times f_y}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{m0}} + \dfrac{0.9\,A_{tn} \times f_u}{\gamma_{m1}} \\ &= \dfrac{1680 \times 250}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10} + \dfrac{0.9 \times 472 \times 410}{1.25} \\ &= 220.5 + 139.4 = 359.9 \text{ kN} \gg 180 \text{ kN} \ \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Provide **2 ISA 70 × 70 × 8 mm** as bottom chord. --- ### ❖ QUESTION B — 14 Marks **Design the top chord (rafter) of the same dairy shed truss. The top chord is in compression and carries a factored compressive force of 165 kN. The unsupported length of the rafter between purlin-and-tie intersections is 2.0 m. Adopt a double-angle section, Fe 410 steel, with the angles placed back-to-back on the top side of the gusset plate. Check both strength and slenderness as per IS 800:2007.** #### (a) [7 Marks] — Section selection and compressive strength check **Step 1 — Assume a slenderness $\lambda \approx 90$ and design stress $f_{cd} \approx 150$ N/mm²:** $$ A_{g,\text{req}} = \dfrac{P_d}{f_{cd}} = \dfrac{165 \times 10^3}{150} = 1100 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Try 2 ISA 65 × 65 × 6 mm:** $A_g = 1514 \text{ mm}^2$; $r_{\text{min}} = 12.4 \text{ mm (single z-z)}$; $r_{vv} \approx 20.0 \text{ mm (back-to-back y-y)}$. **Step 3 — Compute slenderness:** $$ \lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r_{vv}} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 2000}{20.0} = 100 $$ **Step 4 — Design compressive stress from IS 800 Table 9a (Fe 410, $\lambda = 100$):** $f_{cd} \approx 132 \text{ N/mm}^2$ **Step 5 — Compressive capacity:** $$ P_d = A_g \times f_{cd} = 1514 \times 132 = 199.8 \text{ kN} > 165 \text{ kN} \ \checkmark $$ #### (b) [7 Marks] — Slenderness limit and connection detailing **Step 6 — Check maximum slenderness for compression member:** IS 800 cl. 7.1.2 limits $\lambda_{max} = 180$ for compression members carrying dead + imposed load. $\lambda = 100 < 180$ → **slenderness SAFE**. **Step 7 — Check local capacity of one leg in bearing (top chord receives purlin reaction):** Assume purlin reaction $R_p = 12 \text{ kN}$ transferred through gusset. Bearing stress on gusset = $12 \times 10^3 / (65 \times 8) = 23.1 \text{ N/mm}^2 < f_y = 250$ → **SAFE**. **Step 8 — Provide lacing/battening if $L/r_{zz}$ of single angle exceeds limit:** Single-angle $r_{zz} = 12.4$ mm; $\lambda_{zz} = 2000/12.4 = 161 < 180$ → **OK, no lacing required for this short length**. **Conclusion:** Adopt **2 ISA 65 × 65 × 6 mm**, long legs back-to-back, connected through 10 mm gusset plate. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU examiners deduct marks in Module 4 (Steel Structures):** > 1. **Forgetting the partial safety factor $\gamma_m$** — students often write $T = A \times f_y$ without dividing by 1.10. *Penalty: 2 marks per occurrence.* > 2. **Checking only gross-section yielding** and skipping the net-section rupture / block-shear check. IS 800 requires **all** failure modes. *Penalty: 3 marks.* > 3. **Confusing effective length $K$ values** — using $K=1$ for fixed-fixed columns instead of $K=0.65$. *Penalty: 1 mark.* > 4. **Mixing up the slenderness limits** — 350 for tension, 180 for compression. *Penalty: 1 mark.* > 5. **Not stating the design philosophy** (LSM) — KTU expects the word "Limit State Method" in every design answer. *Penalty: 1 mark.* > 6. **Wrong unit of stress** — writing $f_y = 250$ kN/m² instead of **250 N/mm² (MPa)**. *Penalty: 0.5 mark.* ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ Steel structures in farms use **Fe 410** grade with $f_y = 250$ MPa. - ✅ IS 800:2007 → **Limit State Method** with $\gamma_{m0}=1.10,\ \gamma_{m1}=1.25$. - ✅ Tension member design: check **gross yield + net rupture + block shear**. - ✅ Compression member design: check **strength using $f_{cd}$ from buckling curve + $\lambda_{max}$**. - ✅ Euler's load: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / (KL)^2$; governs slender columns. - ✅ Effective length factor $K$: 1.0 (pinned-pinned), 0.65 (fixed-fixed), 2.0 (cantilever). - ✅ Roof truss types for farms: **Pratt, Howe, Fink, Fan** — Pratt is most efficient. - ✅ Bottom chord → **tension**, Top chord → **compression**, Vertical webs → tension, Diagonal webs → compression under gravity. - ✅ Connection choice: **Bolted (Bearing-type)** for speed, **HSFG** for slip-critical, **Welded** for shop splices. - ✅ Cost estimation quick formula: $$ \text{Cost} = \text{Total steel weight (kg)} \times \text{Unit rate (₹/kg)} $$ Add **15–20 %** for fabrication, erection, and wastage. - ✅ Standard permissible deflection for farm building beams: **L / 360** under live load. - ✅ Always mention **IS 800, IS 875, IS 2062, IS 816** wherever relevant. - ✅ Use **double-angle** sections for truss chords; **single channel or Z-section** for purlins. - ✅ Provide **sag rods** at the centre of every alternate purlin span for lateral stability.

topics.title: Doors and Windows; Preparation of Detailed Estimate for RCC Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Doors and Windows; Preparation of Detailed Estimate for RCC Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Doors – Definition & Functional Role

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A *door* is a hinged, sliding, or movable barrier provided in an opening within a wall, partition, or enclosure, used for ingress/egress, ventilation, and light control. In farm structures, doors must also withstand moisture, livestock impact, and rough usage.

A door is the **access interface** between two enclosed spaces (or an enclosed space and the outside). Think of it as the **mouth of a building** — you decide what enters (people, animals, light, air) and what stays out (rain, pests, intruders). In a cattle shed, the door must be **taller and wider** than in a residential building because a cow is far bulkier than a person.

### Functional Requirements of a Door
- Easy and smooth operation (opening/closing).
- Adequate dimensional allowance for the user (human, animal, machinery, or cart).
- Resistance to weather, fire, impact, and decay.
- Adequate light and ventilation (where applicable).
- Security against theft and predators (vital for farm godowns).

## 1.2 Windows – Definition & Functional Role

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A *window* is an opening in a wall, fitted with glazed or louvered shutters, designed primarily to admit light, air, and provide visual contact with the outside environment without permitting direct ingress.

A window is the **eye of a building** — it does not exist for entering the building but for letting the building "see" and "breathe."
