In a poultry house, windows are critical for **cross-ventilation** because birds generate enormous heat and ammonia moisture. Think of a window as a **controlled vent**, a calibrated hole in the wall that lets light in but keeps rain out.

### Functional Requirements of a Window
- Sufficient daylight factor (illumination level inside).
- Cross ventilation without creating drafts.
- Weather-tightness (waterproof joint, sill projection).
- Safety (grills in ground-floor farm buildings).
- Ease of cleaning and durability.

## 1.3 Detailed Estimate – Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A *detailed estimate* is a precise and accurate computation of the quantities of various items of work, and the cost thereof, prepared from the *detailed design*, *drawings*, and *specifications* of the structure, by working out quantities of each individual item of work.

The detailed estimate is the **shopping list + bill** of a construction project. Imagine building a model airplane: the detailed estimate tells you exactly how many wings, how much glue, how many hours of work, and the **total rupees you will spend**. Without it, you either over-order (waste) or under-order (project halts). In farm RCC structures like silage pits, overhead tanks, and dairy parlours, the detailed estimate dictates the **loan amount sanctioned by NABARD/cooperative banks**.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

| Building Element | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Function |
|------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Door | Mouth of a cave | Access & privacy control |
| Window | Eyes of a building | Light & ventilation |
| Lintel over door | Eyebrow above an eye | Load transfer over opening |
| Detailed Estimate | Restaurant bill estimator | Quantifies cost & resources |

---

## 1.5 Standard Terminology (KTU Codification)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU follows **IS 1003 (Timber Panelled Shutters)**, **IS 2191 (Flush Doors)**, and **IS 1038 (Steel Windows)**. All farm door/window specifications should adhere to these BIS standards.

**Key Terms:**
- **Reveal:** The side face of the wall opening in which the frame is fixed.
- **Jamb:** The vertical side of the opening.
- **Sill:** The bottom horizontal member of a window frame.
- **Transom:** A horizontal bar across the window for sub-division.
- **Mullion:** A vertical bar dividing window panes.
- **Shutter:** The movable part of a door/window.
- **Holdfast:** A flat MS iron strip fixing the frame to the wall.
- **Architrave:** A decorative moulding around the frame to cover the joint.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Doors (KTU Board Focus)

### A. Based on Construction Material
1. **Timber Doors** — Most common; IS 1003 (paneled) and IS 2191 (flush).
2. **Metal Doors** — Steel/aluminium; IS 1038.
3. **RCC Doors** — For farm utility buildings; high durability.
4. **PVC / FRP Doors** — Moisture-resistant, used in dairies.
5. **Bamboo / Country Doors** — Low-cost farm structures.

### B. Based on Operating Mechanism
| Type | Description | Application in Farm |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| Hinged / Swinging | Mounted on pivots/hinges | Main barn entrance |
| Sliding | Moves laterally on track | Wide machinery sheds |
| Rolling Shutter | Coils up on a drum | Godowns, garages |
| Revolving | Rotates around central axis | High-traffic dairies |
| Collapsible | Steel lattice folds | Temporary closures |
| Folding / Accordion | Hinged panels fold | Poultry houses |
| Double-Acting | Swings both ways | Veterinary hospitals |

### C. Based on Shutter Design
- **Paneled Door** – Decorative panels within styles and rails.
- **Flush Door** – Smooth plywood/veneer face over solid core.
- **Glazed Door** – Glass panes (sash, casement, louver).
- **Wire-Gauzed Door** – For mosquito-proof sheds.
- **Louvered Door** – Slanted slats for ventilation (e.g., poultry).
- **Battened & Ledged Door** – Vertical battens on horizontal ledgers.
- **Skeleton Door** – Only frame visible; partial partition.

---

## 2.2 Components of a Paneled Door (Top-to-Bottom Anatomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Students must draw and label the components. Examiners frequently ask for neat sketches.

```
TOP RAIL        (Horizontal top member)
STYLE           (Vertical outer members on both sides)
LOCK RAIL       (Mid-height, where lock is fixed)
MULLION         (Vertical intermediate member)
FRIEZER RAIL    (Below top rail)
BOTTOM RAIL     (Horizontal bottom member; thickest)
PANEL           (Filling inside the frame)
}
} → All enclosed within a frame
DOOR FRAME (Rebate + Architrave)
HOLDFAST (3 per jamb, embedded in wall)
```

**Standard Paneled Door Sizes (as per IS 1003):**
- Single shutter: 0.9 m, 1.0 m, 1.1 m wide
- Double shutter: 1.6 m, 1.8 m, 2.0 m, 2.4 m wide
- Height: 2.1 m standard
- Frame thickness: 75 mm × 100 mm (single shutter)

---

## 2.3 Classification of Windows

| Type | Operating | Use Case in Farm |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| Casement | Side-hung like a door | Residential, office |
| Sash / Gliding | Vertical sliding sashes | Barns, godowns |
| Sliding (Horizontal) | Slides sideways | Wide ventilation |
| Pivoted | Rotates on central pivot | Toilet, bathroom |
| Louvered / Venetian | Angled slats | Dairy, poultry, bath |
| Bay / Bow | Projects outward | Premium offices |
| Skylight / Roof | Horizontal or inclined | Lofts, godowns |
| Dormer | Vertical in sloping roof | Attics |
| Gable | Triangular in gable end | End ventilation |
| Corner | At junction of two walls | Watch cabins |

---

## 2.4 Components of a Window

1. **Frame** – Outer frame fixed in the wall (Sill, Jamb, Head).
2. **Shutters** – Sash bars, glazing bars, glass panes, louver slats.
3. **Sash** – The frame holding the glass (in gliding windows).
4. **Sill** – Projected bottom to drain rainwater (slope 1 in 12).
5. **Mullion** – Vertical divider of a multi-pane window.
6. **Transom** – Horizontal divider.
7. **Architrave / Lining** – Decorative/internal cover.
8. **Holdfasts** – 3 per jamb; minimum 150 × 25 × 6 mm MS flat.
9. **Grills** – Decorative/anti-theft metal grills.

---

## 2.5 Detailed Estimate for RCC Structures

> [!NOTE]
> The **detailed estimate** consists of two distinct documents:
> 1. **Quantity Survey (Detailed Measurement Sheet)** – Quantities.
> 2. **Abstract of Estimate (Cost Summary)** – Costs.

### Steps in Preparation of Detailed Estimate
1. **Study Drawings** – Plan, elevation, section, structural drawings.
2. **Take Dimensions** – From the drawing; do not estimate from imagination.
3. **Apply Standard Methods of Measurement** – IS 1200 governs.
4. **Compute Quantities** – Length, area, volume, weight, numbers.
5. **Apply Unit Rates (SSR – Schedule of State Rates)** – Kerala PWD SSR current.
6. **Tabulate the Abstract** – Item × Quantity × Rate = Amount.
7. **Add Contingencies & Work Charge Establishment** – Usually 3% + 1.5%.
8. **Add Departmental Charges** – 1.5% to 2% supervision charges.
9. **Compute Grand Total** – Round to nearest rupee.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet – High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **gold** for KTU University Exam. Memorize before the exam hall.

| Item of Work | Unit | Standard Formula | Notes |
|--------------|------|------------------|-------|
| Earthwork excavation | m³ | $L \times B \times D$ | Add 10% for foundation working space |
| PCC in foundation | m³ | $\vert L_1 \times B_1 \times t_1 \vert + \vert L_2 \times B_2 \times t_2 \vert$ | Layers of different thickness |
| Brickwork / masonry | m³ | $\text{Length} \times \text{Breadth} \times \text{Thickness}$ | Net volume after deductions |
| RCC slab/beam/column | m³ | $\text{Volume} = \text{Length} \times \text{Breadth} \times \text{Depth}$ | **No deduction** for steel up to 1% volume |
| Deduction in plaster | m² | $\geq 0.5\ \text{m}^2$ openings | Deduction in plaster for door/window |
| Steel reinforcement | kg | $\text{Length of bar} \times \text{Unit weight per metre}$ | Includes wastage @ 5–8% |
| Centering & Shuttering (Formwork) | m² | $\text{Contact area of concrete with formwork}$ | Critical for slabs, beams, columns |
| Door area | m² | $1.0\ \text{m} \times 2.1\ \text{m} = 2.10\ \text{m}^2$ | Standard single shutter |
| Window area | m² | $1.2\ \text{m} \times 1.2\ \text{m} = 1.44\ \text{m}^2$ | Standard glazed window |

### Unit Weight of Standard Steel Bars (Must Know)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi\, 6\ \text{mm bar} &\Rightarrow 0.222\ \text{kg/m} \\
\phi\, 8\ \text{mm bar} &\Rightarrow 0.395\ \text{kg/m} \\
\phi\, 10\ \text{mm bar} &\Rightarrow 0.617\ \text{kg/m} \\
\phi\, 12\ \text{mm bar} &\Rightarrow 0.888\ \text{kg/m} \\
\phi\, 16\ \text{mm bar} &\Rightarrow 1.578\ \text{kg/m} \\
\phi\, 20\ \text{mm bar} &\Rightarrow 2.466\ \text{kg/m} \\
\phi\, 25\ \text{mm bar} &\Rightarrow 3.853\ \text{kg/m} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> General formula: $W = d^2 / 162$ in kg/m, where $d$ is diameter of bar in **mm**.

---

## 2.7 Long-Wall and Short-Wall Method (Most Frequently Asked)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **long-wall–short-wall method** is KTU's favourite estimation question. The convention is:
> - **Long wall** = wall running continuously; measured **in-to-in** (centre-to-centre of cross walls).
> - **Short wall** = wall running between two long walls; measured **out-to-out** of long walls.
> - **Same level footing** → use straight line.
> - **Different level footing / step footing** → use **centre-line method**.

### 2.7.1 Formulas

$$
L_{\text{long}} = L_{\text{centre-to-centre}} = \text{Overall length} - \frac{1}{2}\,b_1 - \frac{1}{2}\,b_2
$$

$$
L_{\text{short}} = L_{\text{out-to-out}} = \text{Overall width} - b_1
$$

> where $b_1, b_2$ = cross-wall thicknesses (one or both ends of long wall).

**Volume of footing / masonry =**
$$
V = \sum_{i} L_{\text{long},\,i} \times B_i \times D_i + \sum_{j} L_{\text{short},\,j} \times B_j \times D_j
$$

### 2.7.2 Centre-Line Method (Cross-checking Formula)

$$
\text{Total length of centre line} = 2 \times (L + B) \quad \text{(for rectangular plan with no junctions)}
$$

$$
V_{\text{footing}} = \text{Centre-line length} \times B \times D
$$

> Use this **only** when the wall section remains uniform throughout. If footings are stepped (different sizes), revert to **long-wall–short-wall method**.

---

## 2.8 Items Required in a Detailed Estimate for an RCC Farm Structure

A typical detailed estimate for an **RCC dairy parlour / cattle shed / poultry house** must include:

1. **Site clearance & earthwork in excavation** for foundations.
2. **PCC (Plain Cement Concrete)** bed in foundation (M10 or 1:4:8).
3. **Brickwork / RR masonry** in foundation and plinth.
4. **DPC (Damp Proof Course)** at plinth level.
5. **Brickwork in superstructure.**
6. **RCC work** in columns, beams, slabs, lintels, chajja.
7. **Steel reinforcement** (HYSD/TMT bars) for RCC members.
8. **Centering & shuttering** (formwork) for RCC.
9. **Doors and windows** (with shutters, frames, fittings).
10. **Flooring** (cement concrete flooring, IPS, kota stone).
11. **Plastering** (internal and external).
12. **Painting / distemper / whitewash / white-cement putty.**
13. **Plumbing, sanitary, electrification** (provisional lumpsum).
14. **Water supply & sanitation.**
15. **Miscellaneous** (jungle clearance, watering, contractor's profit).
16. **Contingencies** (3%) + **Establishment** (1.5%).

---

## 2.9 Format of the Abstract of Estimate

| Sl. No. | Item of Work | Quantity | Unit | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|---------|--------------|----------|------|----------|------------|
| 1 | Earthwork excavation in foundation | 50.00 | m³ | 250 | 12,500 |
| 2 | PCC 1:4:8 in foundation | 8.00 | m³ | 6,500 | 52,000 |
| 3 | RR masonry in foundation | 18.00 | m³ | 4,800 | 86,400 |
| 4 | Brickwork in CM 1:6 in superstructure | 22.00 | m³ | 7,200 | 1,58,400 |
| 5 | RCC M20 in columns & beams | 6.50 | m³ | 9,500 | 61,750 |
| 6 | TMT steel @ 80 kg/m³ of RCC | 520 | kg | 78 | 40,560 |
| 7 | Centering & shuttering | 65.00 | m² | 650 | 42,250 |
| 8 | Doors (panelled) | 4.00 | Nos. | 5,800 | 23,200 |
| 9 | Windows (glazed) | 6.00 | Nos. | 3,200 | 19,200 |
| 10 | 12 mm plastering | 180 | m² | 320 | 57,600 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
|  | **Subtotal (A)** |  |  |  | **5,53,860** |
|  | Add: Water & Sanitation (L.S.) 5% |  |  |  | 27,693 |
|  | Add: Electrification (L.S.) 3% |  |  |  | 16,616 |
|  | **Subtotal (B)** |  |  |  | **5,98,169** |
|  | Add: Contingencies 3% |  |  |  | 17,945 |
|  | Add: Work-charge Establishment 1.5% |  |  |  | 8,972 |
|  | **GRAND TOTAL** |  |  |  | **₹ 6,25,086** |

> The **rate column** is filled from the current **Kerala PWD / LSGD Schedule of Rates (SSR)**.

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> A detailed estimate is the **legal and financial backbone** of every farm construction project. It is required for:
> - Bank loan appraisal (NABARD, KVK, cooperative banks).
> - Government grant/subsidy release (Dairy Entrepreneurship Development Scheme).
> - Insurance valuation.
> - Arbitration in contractor disputes.
> - Final bill checking and audit by A.G. (Accountant General).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Door Sizing (Derivation of Brickwork Deduction)

> **Problem Context:** A wall is 0.30 m thick, with a door opening 1.0 m × 2.1 m. Find the volume of brickwork deducted.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Volume of opening} &= \text{Width} \times \text{Height} \times \text{Wall thickness} \\
&= 1.0\ \text{m} \times 2.1\ \text{m} \times 0.30\ \text{m} \\
&= 0.63\ \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> Per IS 1200 (Method of Measurement), **no deduction** is made for openings of area $\le 0.10\ \text{m}^2$ (e.g., small holdfast holes). For larger openings, the **full deduction** of brickwork is made as above.

---

## 3.2 Standard Steel Quantity in RCC Members

> **Problem Context:** A simply supported RCC beam of span 4.0 m, section 0.23 m × 0.45 m is reinforced with 4 nos. 16 mm $\phi$ bars at bottom (with 0.5 m anchorage on each end) and 2 nos. 12 mm $\phi$ hanger bars. Find total steel quantity.

**Step 1: Length of 16 mm bars (bottom)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{cut length of one bar}} &= \text{Span} + 2 \times \text{Anchorage} \\
&= 4.0 + 2 \times 0.5 = 5.0\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total length of 16 mm bars (4 nos.)**

$$
L_{16} = 4 \times 5.0 = 20.0\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3: Total weight of 16 mm bars**

$$
W_{16} = L_{16} \times 1.578\ \text{kg/m} = 20.0 \times 1.578 = 31.56\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 4: Length of 12 mm hanger bars (2 nos.)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{12} &= 2 \times \text{Span} = 2 \times 4.0 = 8.0\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Weight of 12 mm bars**

$$
W_{12} = 8.0 \times 0.888 = 7.104\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 6: Total steel weight**

$$
W_{\text{total}} = 31.56 + 7.104 = 38.664\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 7: Adding bending & wastage allowance (@ 8%)**

$$
W_{\text{with wastage}} = 38.664 \times 1.08 = 41.76\ \text{kg}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Approximately **42 kg of steel** is required for the bottom reinforcement of this beam.

---

## 3.3 Long-Wall and Short-Wall Method – Full Worked Example

> **Problem:** A farm godown 8 m × 5 m (internal dimensions) has 0.30 m thick brick walls. Compute the quantity of brickwork in foundation and plinth. Foundation depth = 1.0 m, with two footings:
> - First footing: 0.90 m wide × 0.30 m thick (PCC 1:4:8)
> - Second footing: 0.60 m wide × 0.45 m thick (Brickwork in CM 1:6)
> - Superstructure: 0.30 m thick brickwork, 3.0 m height (above GL to lintel)

### Step 1: Determine Long Wall and Short Wall lengths

**Long wall (front and rear) — measured in-to-in:**

$$
L_{\text{long}} = 8.0 - \frac{0.30}{2} - \frac{0.30}{2} = 8.0 - 0.30 = 7.70\ \text{m}
$$

**Short wall (left and right) — measured out-to-out of long wall:**

$$
L_{\text{short}} = 5.0 + \frac{0.30}{2} + \frac{0.30}{2} = 5.0 + 0.30 = 5.30\ \text{m}
$$

### Step 2: Volume of PCC in first footing

**Long wall PCC:**

$$
V_{\text{PCC,long}} = 7.70 \times 0.90 \times 0.30 = 2.079\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Short wall PCC:**

$$
V_{\text{PCC,short}} = 5.30 \times 0.90 \times 0.30 = 1.431\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Total PCC volume:**

$$
V_{\text{PCC}} = 2.079 + 1.431 = 3.510\ \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 3: Volume of brickwork in second footing

**Long wall brick footing (width 0.60 m, depth 0.45 m):**

$$
V_{\text{BR,long}} = 7.70 \times 0.60 \times 0.45 = 2.079\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Short wall brick footing:**

$$
V_{\text{BR,short}} = 5.30 \times 0.60 \times 0.45 = 1.431\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Total brick footing volume:**

$$
V_{\text{BR footing}} = 2.079 + 1.431 = 3.510\ \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 4: Volume of brickwork in superstructure (3.0 m height)

**Long wall superstructure:**

$$
V_{\text{SS,long}} = 7.70 \times 0.30 \times 3.0 = 6.93\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Short wall superstructure:**

$$
V_{\text{SS,short}} = 5.30 \times 0.30 \times 3.0 = 4.77\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Total superstructure brickwork:**

$$
V_{\text{SS}} = 6.93 + 4.77 = 11.70\ \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 5: Total Brickwork in foundation + plinth

$$
V_{\text{foundation brick}} = V_{\text{BR footing}} = 3.510\ \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 6: Deductions for Openings

> Suppose 1 door (1.0 m × 2.1 m) and 2 windows (1.2 m × 1.0 m each) are present.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{deduct, door}} &= 1.0 \times 2.1 \times 0.30 = 0.63\ \text{m}^3 \\
V_{\text{deduct, window}} &= 2 \times (1.2 \times 1.0 \times 0.30) = 0.72\ \text{m}^3 \\
V_{\text{total deduction}} &= 0.63 + 0.72 = 1.35\ \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Net Superstructure Brickwork

$$
V_{\text{SS,net}} = 11.70 - 1.35 = 10.35\ \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 8: Sanity Check using Centre-Line Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{centre}} &= 2 \times (L_{\text{long, out-to-out}} + B_{\text{short,out-to-out}}) \\
&= 2 \times (8.30 + 5.30) = 27.20\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
V_{\text{SS,centre}} = 27.20 \times 0.30 \times 3.0 = 24.48\ \text{m}^3 \quad \text{(before deduction)}
$$

> Note: This gives the **gross centre-line** volume (4 walls summed). To compare with the long-wall–short-wall result of 11.70 m³, divide by 2 (because long wall has 2 identical walls and short wall also has 2). Equivalent centre-line value = $24.48 / 2 = 12.24\ \text{m}^3$ which is close to 11.70 m³. The slight difference is due to corner double-counting in the centre-line method (which overcounts by the corner area once).

---

## 3.4 Centering & Shuttering (Formwork) Quantity – Slab Example

> **Problem:** A simply supported RCC slab 4.0 m × 3.5 m × 0.12 m. Find the area of formwork required.

**Step 1: Bottom area of slab = 4.0 × 3.5**

$$
A_{\text{bottom}} = 4.0 \times 3.5 = 14.0\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Side area of slab (edges only) — perimeter × thickness**

$$
A_{\text{sides}} = 2 \times (4.0 + 3.5) \times 0.12 = 1.80\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Total formwork area**

$$
A_{\text{formwork}} = 14.0 + 1.80 = 15.80\ \text{m}^2
$$

> For beams: formwork = bottom + 2 sides; for columns: formwork = 4 sides × height.

---

## 3.5 Plaster Quantity – Wall with Door and Window

> **Problem:** A wall 4.0 m × 3.0 m with one door (1.0 m × 2.1 m) and one window (1.2 m × 1.0 m). Compute net plaster area (plaster on both sides).

**Step 1: Gross wall area (one side)**

$$
A_{\text{gross}} = 4.0 \times 3.0 = 12.0\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Deductions (as per IS 1200, openings $\ge 0.5\ \text{m}^2$ fully deducted):**

$$
A_{\text{deduct}} = (1.0 \times 2.1) + (1.2 \times 1.0) = 2.10 + 1.20 = 3.30\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Net plaster area (one side)**

$$
A_{\text{net,one}} = 12.0 - 3.30 = 8.70\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4: For both sides**

$$
A_{\text{net,both}} = 2 \times 8.70 = 17.40\ \text{m}^2
$$

---

## 3.6 Conversion Logic Summary Table

| Item | Multiplier to convert from one unit to another |
|------|-------------------------------------------------|
| 1 sq.ft to sq.m | $0.0929$ |
| 1 cu.ft to cu.m | $0.0283$ |
| 1 kg to quintal | $0.01$ |
| 1 quintal to tonne | $0.1$ |
| 1 bag cement | $50\ \text{kg} \Rightarrow 0.035\ \text{m}^3$ |
| 1 cum sand | 12 bags cement (for M20 nominal mix ratio 1:2:4 → 1 cum requires 6 bags for cement, 0.42 cum sand, 0.84 cum aggregate) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List the different types of windows used in farm buildings. Describe louvered windows with a sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Types of windows used in farm buildings: (i) Casement window, (ii) Gliding/Sash window, (iii) Louvered/Venetian window, (iv) Pivoted window, (v) Sliding window.

> A **louvered window** consists of a series of horizontal, parallel, sloped slats (louvers) arranged one above the other within a frame. The slats are inclined to allow free passage of air while preventing rain, dust, and direct sunlight from entering. Each louver is held in a sloped groove on the jambs and can be fixed or movable. Louvers are typically 40–50 mm wide timber or glass strips spaced 25 mm apart.

> **Application:** Louvered windows are most suited for **dairy barns, poultry houses, and godowns** where continuous ventilation is required without admitting direct draughts on animals.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a detailed estimate? What are the contents of a detailed estimate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** A detailed estimate is the comprehensive computation of quantities of all items of work and their associated costs, prepared from detailed design drawings and specifications of the proposed structure.

> **Contents of a detailed estimate:**
> 1. **Detailed measurement sheet (Quantity Survey):** Length, area, volume, weight, and number of each item.
> 2. **Abstract of estimate (Cost summary):** Quantity × Unit Rate = Amount for each item.
> 3. **Contingencies** (3% of subtotal).
> 4. **Work-charge establishment** (1.5% of subtotal).
> 5. **Departmental / supervision charges** (1.5% of subtotal).
> 6. **Grand total** rounded to the nearest rupee.
> 7. **Report and specification** of materials and workmanship.
> 8. **Drawings:** Plan, elevation, section, structural details.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – Comprehensive Estimation Problem

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

> An **RCC-framed dairy parlour** has the following specification:
> - Plan dimensions (external): 10.0 m × 6.0 m
> - Wall thickness: 0.30 m
> - Plinth height above GL: 0.60 m
> - Foundation depth below GL: 1.20 m
> - PCC 1:4:8 in foundation: 0.90 m wide × 0.30 m thick
> - Brickwork in foundation CM 1:6: 0.60 m wide × 0.90 m thick
> - RCC columns 0.30 m × 0.30 m @ 4.0 m c/c in superstructure
> - Plinth beam 0.30 m × 0.40 m at GL
> - Lintel beam 0.30 m × 0.30 m at 2.4 m height
> - RCC roof slab 0.12 m thick
> - Doors: 2 nos. (1.0 m × 2.1 m); Windows: 4 nos. (1.2 m × 1.0 m)
>
> **Prepare the detailed estimate** by long-wall–short-wall method for:
> **(a)** Earthwork excavation, PCC, and brickwork in foundation. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** RCC in plinth beam, lintel, and slab, with steel @ 80 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

##### Part (a): Earthwork, PCC, and Foundation Brickwork

**Given:**
- External plan: 10.0 m × 6.0 m
- Wall thickness: 0.30 m
- Internal plan: 10.0 − 0.30 = 9.70 m, and 6.0 − 0.30 = 5.70 m

**Long wall (in-to-in):**

$$
L_{\text{long}} = 10.0 - 0.15 - 0.15 = 9.70\ \text{m}
$$

**Short wall (out-to-out):**

$$
L_{\text{short}} = 6.0 + 0.15 + 0.15 = 6.30\ \text{m}
$$

> Note: Long walls here are 10.0 m walls (front and rear) — measured in-to-in, while short walls are 6.0 m walls (left and right) — measured out-to-out.

**Step 1: Earthwork excavation (0.90 m wide, 1.20 m deep)**

Long wall:
$$
V_{\text{ew,long}} = 9.70 \times 0.90 \times 1.20 = 10.476\ \text{m}^3
$$

Short wall:
$$
V_{\text{ew,short}} = 6.30 \times 0.90 \times 1.20 = 6.804\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Total earthwork:**
$$
V_{\text{ew}} = 10.476 + 6.804 = 17.28\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2: PCC 1:4:8 (0.90 m wide × 0.30 m thick)**

Long wall PCC:
$$
V_{\text{PCC,long}} = 9.70 \times 0.90 \times 0.30 = 2.619\ \text{m}^3
$$

Short wall PCC:
$$
V_{\text{PCC,short}} = 6.30 \times 0.90 \times 0.30 = 1.701\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Total PCC:**
$$
V_{\text{PCC}} = 2.619 + 1.701 = 4.320\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Brickwork in foundation (0.60 m wide × 0.90 m thick)**

Long wall brickwork:
$$
V_{\text{BR,long}} = 9.70 \times 0.60 \times 0.90 = 5.238\ \text{m}^3
$$

Short wall brickwork:
$$
V_{\text{BR,short}} = 6.30 \times 0.60 \times 0.90 = 3.402\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Total foundation brickwork:**
$$
V_{\text{BR}} = 5.238 + 3.402 = 8.640\ \text{m}^3
$$

> **[End of Part (a) – 7 marks]**

---

##### Part (b): RCC in Plinth Beam, Lintel, and Slab with Steel

**Step 4: Plinth beam 0.30 m × 0.40 m, length = centre-to-centre of columns**

Assume 3 columns along the long wall @ 4.0 m c/c (or as per drawing). For simplicity:
- Plinth beam runs around the entire plinth: 2 × (10.0 + 6.0) = 32.0 m
- Deduct column widths: 6 columns × 0.30 m = 1.80 m

$$
L_{\text{plinth beam}} = 32.0 - 1.80 = 30.2\ \text{m}
$$

$$
V_{\text{plinth beam}} = 30.2 \times 0.30 \times 0.40 = 3.624\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Lintel beam 0.30 m × 0.30 m**

Lintel is provided over each door and window.

Length of lintel over each opening (assume 0.30 m bearing on each side):
- Door lintel: 1.0 + 0.30 + 0.30 = 1.60 m
- Window lintel: 1.2 + 0.30 + 0.30 = 1.80 m

Total lintel length:
- 2 doors: 2 × 1.60 = 3.20 m
- 4 windows: 4 × 1.80 = 7.20 m
- Total: 3.20 + 7.20 = 10.40 m

$$
V_{\text{lintel}} = 10.40 \times 0.30 \times 0.30 = 0.936\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 6: Roof slab 0.12 m thick**

Slab area (assumed = internal plan area):
$$
A_{\text{slab}} = 9.70 \times 5.70 = 55.29\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
V_{\text{slab}} = 55.29 \times 0.12 = 6.635\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 7: Total RCC Volume**

$$
V_{\text{RCC,total}} = 3.624 + 0.936 + 6.635 = 11.195\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 8: Steel @ 80 kg/m³ of RCC**

$$
W_{\text{steel}} = 11.195 \times 80 = 895.6\ \text{kg} \approx 896\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 9: Centering & Shuttering**

- Plinth beam: perimeter area = 30.2 × (0.30 + 0.40) = 21.14 m² (approx)
- Lintel beam: 10.40 × (0.30 + 0.30) = 6.24 m²
- Slab: bottom area = 55.29 m² + sides 2 × (9.70 + 5.70) × 0.12 = 3.70 m² = 58.99 m²

$$
A_{\text{shuttering}} = 21.14 + 6.24 + 58.99 = 86.37\ \text{m}^2
$$

> **[End of Part (b) – 7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> Discuss the different types of doors used in farm structures. With a neat sketch, explain the components of a **paneled door** and mention the standard sizes of doors for a **cattle shed**, **poultry house**, and **farm godown**. **(14 marks)**

#### Solution Outline

**Introduction (2 marks):** Definition of door, role in farm buildings.

**Types of Doors (5 marks):**
1. Panelled door
2. Flush door
3. Glazed door
4. Wire-gauzed door
5. Louvered door
6. Rolling shutter
7. Sliding door
8. Battened and ledged door
9. Revolving door

**Components of a Paneled Door (5 marks):**
- Frame: two styles, top rail, frieze rail, lock rail, bottom rail, mullions.
- Panels: plywood or glass.
- Fittings: hinges, handle, lock, tower bolt, door closer.
- Holdfasts: 3 per jamb (150 × 25 × 6 mm MS).
- Architrave: 50 × 12 mm decorative strip.

**Sketch (2 marks):** Labelled diagram of paneled door with all components.

**Standard Sizes for Farm Structures:**

| Farm Building | Door Size (m) | Type |
|---------------|---------------|------|
| Cattle Shed | 1.5 m × 2.4 m (2 shutters) | Swing / sliding |
| Dairy Parlour | 1.2 m × 2.1 m | Flush (hygiene) |
| Poultry House | 0.9 m × 2.0 m | Light timber / wire-gauzed |
| Farm Godown | 2.5 m × 3.0 m | Rolling shutter |
| Farm Office | 1.0 m × 2.1 m | Paneled / flush |
| Mushroom House | 0.9 m × 2.1 m | Single paneled |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Importance of choosing door type based on usage, climate, animal type, and durability requirements.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Verified by KTU Valuation Reports):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Long Wall and Short Wall direction** — Long wall is the **larger dimension** wall, measured **in-to-in** (centre-to-centre of cross walls). Short wall is the **shorter dimension** wall, measured **out-to-out** of long walls. Reversing these will give **wrong quantities** and **full mark deduction** in part (a).
>
> 2. **Forgetting deductions for openings** — Door and window openings in brickwork and plaster must be deducted as per IS 1200. Skipping deductions leads to **2–3 marks** loss.
>
> 3. **Unit mismatch** — Mixing m and cm, m² and sq.ft, m³ and cu.ft. Use the **unit given in SSR**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting wastage factor for steel** — Add **5–8%** for cutting, bending, and binding wire wastage.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning IS code references** — Always cite **IS 1200** for measurement and **IS 875** for loads when relevant. Examiners reward this.
>
> 6. **Wrong shuttering area calculation** — For beam, it is **bottom + 2 sides** (not 3 sides). For column, it is **4 sides × height**.
>
> 7. **Skipping neat sketch** — In door/window questions, an unlabelled or absent sketch costs **2–3 marks**.
>
> 8. **Writing "contingencies 10%"** — The Kerala SSR standard is **3%**, not 10%.
>
> 9. **Forgetting to deduct column volumes from the plinth beam length** — Lintel beam length must be measured over openings only, not over the entire wall.
>
> 10. **Wrong unit weight of steel** — Memorize: $W = d^2/162$ kg/m. Wrong value = full mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Exam Cram)**

- [ ] **Door definition** — hinged/sliding barrier for ingress.
- [ ] **Window definition** — opening for light/ventilation.
- [ ] **Door types** — paneled, flush, glazed, rolling, sliding, louvered, collapsible, revolving, battened, skeleton.
- [ ] **Window types** — casement, sash (gliding), pivoted, louvered, sliding, bay, gable, dormer, skylight.
- [ ] **Standard door sizes** — 0.9 m, 1.0 m, 1.1 m (single); 1.6 m, 1.8 m, 2.0 m, 2.4 m (double).
- [ ] **Standard window sizes** — 0.6 × 0.6 m, 1.0 × 1.0 m, 1.2 × 1.0 m, 1.2 × 1.2 m.
- [ ] **Components of door** — styles, rails, panels, mullions, holdfasts, hinges, lock, tower bolt, closer, architrave.
- [ ] **Holdfast size** — 150 × 25 × 6 mm MS flat, 3 per jamb.
- [ ] **Hinges** — 100 mm long, 3 nos. per shutter.
- [ ] **Detailed estimate** = Quantity Survey + Abstract of Cost.
- [ ] **Long wall** = in-to-in, **Short wall** = out-to-out.
- [ ] **Centre-line method** = $2 \times (L + B)$ for rectangular plan.
- [ ] **Deductions for openings** ≥ 0.5 m² as per IS 1200.
- [ ] **Unit weight of steel** — $W = d^2/162$ kg/m.
- [ ] **Standard bar weights** — $\phi 6 = 0.222$, $\phi 8 = 0.395$, $\phi 10 = 0.617$, $\phi 12 = 0.888$, $\phi 16 = 1.578$, $\phi 20 = 2.466$ kg/m.
- [ ] **Steel in RCC** — usually 80–120 kg per m³ of concrete.
- [ ] **Formwork (shuttering) area** = contact area of concrete with formwork.
- [ ] **Contingencies** = 3%, **Establishment** = 1.5%, **Departmental** = 1.5%.
- [ ] **Cement bag** = 50 kg = 0.035 m³ volume.
- [ ] **Sand density** = 1.6 t/m³ (loose); **Aggregate** = 1.5 t/m³ (loose).
- [ ] **Plaster deduction** — openings $\geq 0.5$ m² deducted; < 0.5 m² not deducted.
- [ ] **M20 nominal mix** = 1 : 1.5 : 3 (cement : sand : aggregate) by volume.
- [ ] **PCC 1:4:8** = lean concrete for foundation bed; **1:3:6** for flooring; **1:2:4** for general RCC.
- [ ] **IS codes** — IS 1003 (paneled), IS 2191 (flush), IS 1038 (steel windows), IS 1200 (measurement).

---

**End of Module 4 Notes – PCAGT501 Design of Farm Structures and Cost Estimation, KTU 2024 Scheme.**

topics.title: Preparation of Bar Bending Schedule; Detailed Specifications for Common Building Materials and Items of Work as per IS Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Bar Bending Schedule; Detailed Specifications for Common Building Materials and Items of Work as per IS Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bar Bending Schedule (BBS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bar Bending Schedule (BBS)** is a comprehensive, itemized tabular document prepared by the structural designer / site engineer that lists every reinforcement bar in a structural member, specifying its **identification mark, shape code, diameter, number, cutting length, total length, and total weight**. It serves as the primary communication tool between the design office, the site execution team, and the steel supplier for cutting, bending, placing, and billing of reinforcement steel as per IS 2502:1963 and IS 456:2000.

**Standard IS Reference:** IS 2502:1963 — *Code of Practice for Bending and Fixing of Bars for Concrete Reinforcement*.

## 1.2 Detailed Specifications — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Detailed Specifications** are precise written descriptions of the quality, proportioning, workmanship, and testing requirements of **materials** (cement, sand, aggregate, steel, brick, water) and **items of work** (excavation, PCC, RCC, masonry, plastering, flooring) drawn from the relevant Indian Standard (IS) codes. They are the legally binding quality contract between the owner and the contractor, and form the basis for **rate analysis and cost estimation**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Site Story

Imagine you are ordering a **tailored suit for a building**:
- The **structural drawing** = the body measurement chart.
- The **Bar Bending Schedule** = the tailor's cutting list — *exactly* how many pieces of cloth (bars), of what width (diameter), in what shape (bent/cut), with what extra margin for hems (hooks/laps), and total cloth required.
- The **IS Specifications** = the **quality manual** of the cloth manufacturer — what thread count, what color fastness, what stitching thread strength is acceptable.

Without the cutting list, the tailor either wastes cloth or runs short. Without quality specs, the cloth may tear on day one. **BBS controls quantity; IS Specifications control quality.**

## 1.4 Why BBS and IS Specs Matter for Farm Structures

In **PCAGT501 (Design of Farm Structures and Cost Estimation)**, the Module 4 content is essential because:

- Farm structures (dairy sheds, poultry houses, godowns, greenhouses, silos, irrigation channels) require accurate **quantity takeoff** for tendering.
- BBS enables **exact steel quantity** estimation → direct cost input for the **abstract of cost / Bill of Quantities (BoQ)**.
- IS specifications ensure that the rural/marginal contractor (often an informal agency in Kerala's farm sector) cannot compromise on quality, because every item has a benchmark.
- Cost estimation without IS specs is **not admissible in KTU valuation** — examiners award marks only when IS references are cited.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants & Metrics to Memorize for KTU Exam:**
> - Unit weight of steel bar (any diameter) = **d² / 162** kg/m, where **d** is in **mm**.
> - Density of mild steel ≈ **7850 kg/m³**.
> - 1 quintal = **100 kg**; 1 tonne = **1000 kg**.
> - Standard hook lengths (IS 2502): 180° = **16d**, 90° = **12d**, 135° (stirrup) = **12d**.
> - Bend deductions: 45° = **1d**, 90° = **2d**, 135° = **3d**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of BBS Preparation

The preparation of a Bar Bending Schedule follows a **7-stage reasoning chain**:

1. **Read the structural drawing** → Identify the structural member (beam, column, slab, footing).
2. **Identify bar types** → Main bars, distribution bars, stirrups, anchor bars, crank bars, curtailment bars.
3. **Determine bar shape** → Straight, L-bent, U-bent, cranked, with/without hooks.
4. **Compute the theoretical length** of each bar using:
   - Clear span / clear dimension
   - Concrete cover (as per IS 456)
   - Development length (L_d)
   - Hook length
   - Bend deduction
   - Lap length (if required)
5. **Compute the cutting length** = Theoretical length + hooks − bend deductions.
6. **Multiply by the number of bars** of that type → total length.
7. **Convert to weight** using **d²/162** → total kg → quintals/tonnes.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why deduct bend?** A 90° bent bar measured along its centerline appears longer than the straight segments alone, so we **deduct 2d** (the extra curved material isn't actually there).
- **Why add hook?** A hook provides **anchorage** against pull-out; the extra length gives bond strength equivalent to development length.
- **Why crank bars in slabs?** At supports, the sagging moment is small but the **shear is large**. Cranking the bottom bar up at 1/5th span (≈ 0.2L) into the top provides extra shear resistance, similar to a bent-up bar.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Item** | **Formula / Value** | **Unit** | **IS Reference** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Weight of bar per metre | $W = \dfrac{d^{2}}{162}$ | kg/m | IS 1786 |
| 2 | Weight of bar (total) | $W_{total} = L \times \dfrac{d^{2}}{162}$ | kg | — |
| 3 | 180° hook length | $L_{h} = 16d$ | mm | IS 2502 |
| 4 | 90° hook length | $L_{h} = 12d$ | mm | IS 2502 |
| 5 | 135° (stirrup) hook length | $L_{h} = 12d$ | mm | IS 2502 |
| 6 | Bend deduction 45° | $1d$ | mm | IS 2502 |
| 7 | Bend deduction 90° | $2d$ | mm | IS 2502 |
| 8 | Bend deduction 135° | $3d$ | mm | IS 2502 |
| 9 | Development length (HYSD, $\tau_{bd} = 1.4$ MPa) | $L_{d} = \dfrac{\phi \, \sigma_{s}}{4 \tau_{bd}} = 47.86\phi \approx 48\phi$ | mm | IS 456 Cl.26.2 |
| 10 | Development length (Mild steel) | $L_{d} = 57\phi$ | mm | IS 456 |
| 11 | Lap length (tension) | $L_{lap} = L_d$ or $2 L_d$ (per IS 456 Cl.26.2.5) | mm | IS 456 |
| 12 | Lap length (compression) | $L_{lap} = L_d$ | mm | IS 456 |
| 13 | Crank length (45° crank, height h) | $L_{crank} = h(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 0.414\,h$ | mm | — |
| 14 | Crank length (30° crank, height h) | $L_{crank} = h(2 - \sqrt{3}) \approx 0.268\,h$ | mm | — |
| 15 | Min. clear cover (beam) | $25$ mm | mm | IS 456 |
| 16 | Min. clear cover (slab) | $20$ mm (mild) / $15$ mm (HYSD) | mm | IS 456 |
| 17 | Min. clear cover (column) | $40$ mm | mm | IS 456 |
| 18 | Min. clear cover (footing) | $50$ mm | mm | IS 456 |
| 19 | Number of stirrups | $n = \dfrac{L_{clear}}{s} + 1$ | nos. | — |
| 20 | Effective depth (beam) | $d = D - clear\,cover - \phi_{stirrup} - \dfrac{\phi_{main}}{2}$ | mm | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the formula table, use `\vert` for absolute value (e.g., $W = \vert d^{2} \vert / 162$) — but in this syllabus it never occurs, so plain parentheses suffice. Always write $\phi$ (phi, the bar diameter) in **mm** for the $d^{2}/162$ formula to give kg/m.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Farm Engineering

- **Dairy Shed Beam**: Computing the BBS for a 6 m tie-beam allows the contractor in a rural Kerala panchayat to procure exactly **1.32 quintals of 12 mm Fe-500 bar** — no over-ordering, no shortage.
- **Poultry House Slab**: BBS reveals the cost share of steel in the slab (often **35–40 %** of total structural cost) — informs cost-optimization decisions like reducing bar diameter or increasing grade.
- **Silo Ring Beam**: Circular geometry requires special "ring-shape" bar codes (Type 22 in IS 2502) — without IS reference, the bar-bender cannot fabricate correctly.

## 2.5 Detailed Specifications — IS Code Mapping for Building Materials

| **Material** | **Relevant IS Code** | **Key Specification** |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) 43 grade | **IS 269:2015** | Min. compressive strength 43 MPa at 28 days |
| OPC 53 grade | **IS 12269:2013** | Min. compressive strength 53 MPa at 28 days |
| Portland Pozzolana Cement (PPC) | **IS 1489 (Part 1):2015** | Fly-ash based, for mass concrete & farm structures |
| Fine Aggregate (Sand) | **IS 383:2016** | Grading Zones I, II, III, IV; FM 2.2–4.0 |
| Coarse Aggregate | **IS 383:2016** | Nominal sizes 10, 20, 40 mm; crushed/rounded |
| Bricks (Burnt Clay) | **IS 1077:1992** | Min. compressive strength 3.5–10 MPa; water absorption ≤ 20 % |
| Water for Concrete | **IS 456:2000 Cl.5.4** | Potable; pH ≥ 6; max chlorides 500 mg/L (RCC), 2000 mg/L (PCC) |
| HYSD Bars (Fe-500) | **IS 1786:2008** | Yield strength 500 MPa; elongation 12 % |
| Mild Steel Bars | **IS 432 (Part 1):1982** | Yield strength 250 MPa |
| Structural Steel | **IS 2062:2011** | E250, E300, E350 grades |

> [!NOTE]
> **Farm-structure tip:** For rural farm buildings, **PPC (IS 1489)** is preferred over OPC because it has lower heat of hydration — ideal for **mass concrete in silo foundations and irrigation tanks** in Kerala's hot climate.

## 2.6 Detailed Specifications — Items of Work (IS-Based)

| **Item of Work** | **IS Reference** | **Key Specification Highlights** |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Earthwork Excavation | IS 1200 (Part 1) | Depth classification; lead & lift; PCC in foundation |
| PCC (Plain Cement Concrete) | IS 456:2000 | Mix ratio (e.g., 1:4:8); W/C ratio ≤ 0.6; 40 mm aggregate |
| RCC (Reinforced Cement Concrete) | IS 456:2000 | Mix design; W/C ≤ 0.45; vibration mandatory; curing 14 days |
| Brick Masonry | IS 2212:1991 | 1:6 cement-sand mortar; soak bricks 24 h; frog upward |
| Plastering | IS 1661:1972 / IS 2402 | 1:4 or 1:6 mortar; 12–20 mm thickness; curing 7 days |
| Flooring (Cement Concrete) | IS 1443 | 1:2:4 metal-aggregate; ≥ 40 mm thick; chequered finish |
| Centering & Shuttering | IS 14687 | Removal time; prop spacing; surface finish |
| Steel Reinforcement Work | IS 2502:1963 | BBS; cover blocks; binding wire 18 SWG |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cutting Length of a Beam's Main (Bottom) Bar

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:** A simply supported rectangular beam has clear span $L_{clear} = 5000$ mm, overall depth $D = 500$ mm, width $b = 230$ mm, clear cover = 25 mm, stirrup diameter = 8 mm, main bar diameter = 16 mm. Both ends have **180° hooks** anchored into the column. Find the **cutting length** of one bottom bar.

### Step 1 — Compute the Effective Depth
The centerline of the bottom bar sits above the bottom by:

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= D - \text{clear cover} - \phi_{stirrup} - \frac{\phi_{main}}{2} \\[4pt]
d &= 500 - 25 - 8 - \frac{16}{2} \\[4pt]
d &= 500 - 25 - 8 - 8 \\[4pt]
d &= 459 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

*Reasoning:* The bar's center must be `cover + stirrup dia + half of its own dia` above the beam's soffit to maintain minimum clear cover to the bar surface.

### Step 2 — Compute Centerline Span of the Bar

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{centerline} &= L_{clear} + 2 \times \left( \frac{b_{column}}{2} \right) \\[4pt]
L_{centerline} &= 5000 + 2 \times 115 \\[4pt]
L_{centerline} &= 5230 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

*Reasoning:* The bar extends to the **centerline of the supporting column** (assumed 230 mm × 230 mm), giving half-column widths of 115 mm on each side.

### Step 3 — Add Hook Lengths (180° hook = 16d each)

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{hooks} &= 2 \times 16\phi = 2 \times 16 \times 16 = 512 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

*Reasoning:* As per IS 2502, a 180° hook provides an effective anchorage length of 16 bar diameters beyond the bend.

### Step 4 — Bend Deductions
The bottom bar here is **straight** with hooks (no 90° bend in the run). Hence **0 mm** bend deduction.

### Step 5 — Final Cutting Length

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{cut} &= L_{centerline} + L_{hooks} - \text{bend deductions} \\[4pt]
L_{cut} &= 5230 + 512 - 0 \\[4pt]
L_{cut} &= 5742 \text{ mm} \approx 5.742 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cutting Length of an L-Shaped Bent-Up Bar (Beam Top)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Same beam as above. Top bar is **L-bent** at 90° at the ends, 230 mm leg projecting down. Compute cutting length of one top bar with 90° bends.

### Step 1 — Centerline Span

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{centerline} &= 5230 \text{ mm (same as before)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Add 90° Bend Legs (no hooks; just the L-bend)

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{leg} &= 2 \times 230 = 460 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

*Reasoning:* The top bar bends 90° downward at the supports to provide anchorage into the column zone.

### Step 3 — Subtract Bend Deductions (2 bends × 90° = 2d each)

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{bend\,ded} &= 2 \times 2d = 2 \times 2 \times 16 = 64 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

*Reasoning:* Each 90° bend appears as 2d of "phantom" length on the centerline; deduct it.

### Step 4 — Final Cutting Length

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{cut} &= 5230 + 460 - 64 \\[4pt]
L_{cut} &= 5626 \text{ mm} \approx 5.626 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cutting Length of a Rectangular Stirrup

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:** Same beam. Stirrups are 2-legged, 8 mm diameter, with 135° hooks (12d each). Cover = 25 mm, d = 459 mm, b = 230 mm. Spacing s = 150 mm c/c. Number of stirrups = (L_clear / s) + 1 = (5000/150) + 1 ≈ 34 nos. Compute cutting length of one stirrup.

### Step 1 — Compute the Stirrup's Centerline Dimensions

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{stirrup} &= b - 2 \times \text{cover} - 2 \times \phi_{stirrup} - \text{dia of inner bar} \\[4pt]
B_{stirrup} &= 230 - 2(25) - 2(8) - 16 \\[4pt]
B_{stirrup} &= 230 - 50 - 16 - 16 = 148 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

*Reasoning:* Subtract cover from both faces (50), then half-stirrup diameter on both vertical legs (16), then **half of main bar diameter** for clearance (16 mm — since the stirrup wraps around the corner main bar).

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{stirrup} &= d - \text{cover} - \phi_{stirrup} - \frac{\phi_{top}}{2} \\[4pt]
D_{stirrup} &= 459 - 25 - 8 - 8 = 418 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Perimeter (closed rectangular loop)

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 2 \times (B_{stirrup} + D_{stirrup}) \\[4pt]
P &= 2 \times (148 + 418) \\[4pt]
P &= 2 \times 566 = 1132 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Add 135° Hook Lengths (2 hooks × 12d)

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{hooks} &= 2 \times 12d = 2 \times 12 \times 8 = 192 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Subtract Bend Deductions (3 bends × 135° = 3d each)

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{bend\,ded} &= 3 \times 3d = 3 \times 3 \times 8 = 72 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Final Cutting Length of One Stirrup

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{cut,\,stirrup} &= 1132 + 192 - 72 \\[4pt]
L_{cut,\,stirrup} &= 1252 \text{ mm} \approx 1.252 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Total Weight of All Stirrups

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 34 \times 1.252 = 42.568 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
W_{total} &= L_{total} \times \frac{d^{2}}{162} = 42.568 \times \frac{8^{2}}{162} \\[4pt]
W_{total} &= 42.568 \times \frac{64}{162} = 42.568 \times 0.3951 \\[4pt]
W_{total} &= 16.82 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cutting Length of a Slab Main Bar (with Crank)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** One-way slab, clear span 3000 mm, slab thickness 150 mm, cover 20 mm, main bar 12 mm @ 150 mm c/c, crank at 45° with crank height = effective depth − cover = 130 − 20 = 110 mm.

### Step 1 — Compute Effective Depth

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= 150 - 20 - \frac{12}{2} = 150 - 20 - 6 = 124 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Bar Length Along Centerline (clear span + bearing)

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{base} &= 3000 + 2 \times 100 = 3200 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

*Reasoning:* Extend 100 mm (minimum bearing) on each support.

### Step 3 — Add Crank Length (45° crank, height h = d − cover = 110 − 20 = 90 mm above support bar)

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{crank} &= 2 \times 0.414h = 2 \times 0.414 \times 90 = 74.52 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

*Reasoning:* Crank length = $h(\sqrt{2} - 1) = 0.414h$ per crank; multiplied by 2 since there are two cranks (one at each end).

### Step 4 — Final Cutting Length

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{cut} &= L_{base} + L_{crank} = 3200 + 74.52 = 3274.52 \text{ mm} \approx 3.275 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Cutting Length Formulas

| **Bar Type** | **Cutting Length Formula** | **Typical Use** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Straight bar with 180° hooks (both ends) | $L_{cut} = L_{centerline} + 2(16d) - 0$ | Beam bottom bar, column main bar |
| L-bent (90°) bar with 2 bends | $L_{cut} = L_{centerline} + 2L_{leg} - 2(2d)$ | Beam top bar, lintel bar |
| Cranked slab bar (45°) | $L_{cut} = L_{base} + 2 \times 0.414h$ | One-way slab main bar |
| Rectangular stirrup (135° hooks) | $L_{cut} = 2(B + D) + 2(12d) - 3(3d)$ | Beam/column shear reinforcement |
| Circular hoop (column) | $L_{cut} = \pi (D - 2c - \phi) + L_{lap} + 2(12d)$ | Circular column ties |
| Footing crank bar (45°) | $L_{cut} = L + 2(0.414h) - 2c$ | Isolated footing main bar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal KTU Rule:** Every bar length is measured along the **centerline** of the bar. Hooks are **added**; bend deductions are **subtracted**. The total weight is **always** $\dfrac{d^2}{162} \times L$ in kg.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — BBS Preparation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Structural Drawing] --> B[Identify Structural Member]
    B --> C{Beam / Column / Slab / Footing?}
    C -->|Beam| D[Extract Bar Types]
    C -->|Column| D
    C -->|Slab| D
    C -->|Footing| D
    D --> E[Determine Bar Shape: Straight / L-bent / Crank / U-bent / Stirrup]
    E --> F[Compute Centerline Length]
    F --> G[Add Hook Lengths 16d 12d 12d]
    G --> H[Subtract Bend Deductions 1d 2d 3d]
    H --> I[Cutting Length per Bar]
    I --> J[Multiply by Number of Bars]
    J --> K[Compute Total Length]
    K --> L[Apply d squared over 162 formula]
    L --> M[Total Weight in kg]
    M --> N[Convert to Quintals or Tonnes]
    N --> O[Tabulate in BBS Format]
    O --> P[End: Final BBS Ready for Site]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style P fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style O fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
```

    C --> D[Number of Bars n]
    D --> E[Cutting Length Lc in m]
    E --> F[Total Length Lc x n]
    F --> G[Unit Weight d squared over 162]
    G --> H[Total Weight in kg]
    H --> I[Quintals or Tonnes]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style F fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000
    style G fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style I fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Bar Shape Codes (IS 2502)

flowchart TB

        S00[Type 00: Straight Bar]

    end

    S11 --> SC

    S21 --> SC

    S44 --> SC
    S51 --> SC

    style SC fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000
