- **Contractor Tendering:** BoQ (Bill of Quantities) + AOR = Tender Document.
- **MGNREGA Convergence:** AOR drives the permissible labour-material ratio (60:40) in farm works.
- **Insurance Valuation:** AoE provides replacement cost for crop insurance / farm building insurance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Examples)

### 3.1 Example 1 — Earthwork in Excavation for a Farm Pond

**Item of Work:** Earthwork excavation in ordinary soil for a small rectangular farm pond (depth 2 m), disposed within 50 m lead.

**Data Given (Kerala SOR 2024, hypothetical exam rates):**

- Unskilled labour daily wage (Kerala MNREGA) = ₹ **380** per day
- Task (out-turn) of an unskilled labourer for excavation in ordinary soil = **2.8 m³** per day
- 1 *manday* (skilled supervisor) for every 10 unskilled mandays = **₹ 520** per day
- Royalty + sundries for earthwork = **2 %** of (MC + LC); assume MC = 0
- Contractor’s profit = **10 %**

**Step 1 — Labour Requirement per m³**

$$
LC = \frac{N_L \times W_d}{T_W} = \frac{1 \times 380}{2.8} = 135.71 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 2 — Skilled Supervisor Cost per m³**

For 1 m³ of work, supervisor required = $\dfrac{1}{10 \times 2.8} = 0.0357$ manday

$$
LC_{sup} = 0.0357 \times 520 = 18.57 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Labour Cost (LC)**

$$
LC_{total} = 135.71 + 18.57 = 154.29 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 4 — Material Cost (MC) for Earthwork**

Earthwork uses *no purchased material*. So,

$$
MC = 0 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 5 — Plant & Machinery**

Excavation done manually (MGNREGA labour). Therefore,

$$
PMC = 0 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 6 — Overheads (Sundries + Water + Insurance)**

$$
OH = 2\% \times (MC + LC) = 0.02 \times (0 + 154.29) = 3.09 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 7 — Subtotal before Profit**

$$
S = MC + LC + PMC + OH = 0 + 154.29 + 0 + 3.09 = 157.38 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 8 — Add Contractor’s Profit 10 %**

$$
R = S \times 1.10 = 157.38 \times 1.10 = 173.12 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analysed Rate for Earthwork Excavation (Ordinary Soil)** = **₹ 173.12 per m³** (rounded to **₹ 173/- per m³**)

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — Plain Cement Concrete (1:4:8) in Foundation of a Cattle Shed

**Item of Work:** 1 m³ of P.C.C. (1:4:8) using **20 mm broken granite (jelly)** and **M-Sand (Manufactured Sand)** for the foundation of a farm cattle shed.

**Data Given (Kerala SOR 2024):**

- Cost of cement (53 grade, 50 kg bag at site) = ₹ **440** per bag
- Cost of M-Sand (per m³) at site = ₹ **1,800**
- Cost of 20 mm jelly (per m³) at site = ₹ **2,200**
- Mason (skilled) wage = ₹ **780** per day; task = 1.4 m³ of P.C.C./day
- Unskilled labour wage = ₹ **380** per day; 1.5 nos./mason; task = 1.0 m³/day
- Mixer + vibrator hire (per m³) = ₹ **80**
- Wastage: cement 1 %; sand 12 %; aggregate 7 %
- Sundries & water = 1.5 % of MC + LC
- Profit = 10 %

**Step 1 — Dry Volume Calculation (Wet → Dry conversion factor = 1.54)**

$$
V_{dry} = 1 \times 1.54 = 1.54 \text{ m³ of dry mix for 1 m³ wet P.C.C.}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of Mix Ratio Parts**

$$
\Sigma = 1 + 4 + 8 = 13 \text{ parts}
$$

**Step 3 — Cement Quantity (with 1 % wastage)**

$$
Q_{cement} = \frac{1}{13} \times 1.54 \times (1 + 0.01) = 0.1198 \text{ m³}
$$

$$
\text{Mass of cement} = 0.1198 \times 1440 \text{ kg/m³} = 172.5 \text{ kg} \Rightarrow \frac{172.5}{50} = 3.45 \text{ bags}
$$

**Step 4 — M-Sand Quantity (with 12 % wastage)**

$$
Q_{sand} = \frac{4}{13} \times 1.54 \times 1.12 = 0.5306 \text{ m³}
$$

**Step 5 — 20 mm Aggregate Quantity (with 7 % wastage)**

$$
Q_{agg} = \frac{8}{13} \times 1.54 \times 1.07 = 1.0142 \text{ m³}
$$

**Step 6 — Material Cost (MC)**

$$
MC = (3.45 \times 440) + (0.5306 \times 1800) + (1.0142 \times 2200)
$$

$$
MC = 1518.00 + 955.08 + 2231.24 = 4704.32 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 7 — Labour Cost (LC)**

$$
\text{Mason cost} = \frac{1 \times 780}{1.4} = 557.14 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

$$
\text{Unskilled cost} = \frac{1.5 \times 380}{1.0} = 570.00 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

$$
LC = 557.14 + 570.00 = 1127.14 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 8 — Plant & Machinery**

$$
PMC = 80 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 9 — Overheads (1.5 %)**

$$
OH = 0.015 \times (MC + LC) = 0.015 \times (4704.32 + 1127.14) = 0.015 \times 5831.46 = 87.47 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 10 — Subtotal and Profit**

$$
S = 4704.32 + 1127.14 + 80.00 + 87.47 = 5998.93 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

$$
R = 5998.93 \times 1.10 = 6598.82 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analysed Rate for P.C.C. 1:4:8 in foundation** = **₹ 6,599 per m³** (say **₹ 6,600/- per m³**)

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — Brickwork in a Farm Godown (1:6 cement–mortar)

**Item of Work:** 1 m³ of brickwork in superstructure (1:6) using **first-class local bricks (Kerala: $230 \text{ mm} \times 110 \text{ mm} \times 70 \text{ mm}$)**.

**Data Given:**

- Brick size in actual construction: $230 \times 110 \times 70$ mm → with 10 mm mortar = $240 \times 120 \times 80$ mm
- Nominal size used for calculations: $200 \times 100 \times 100$ mm (with 10 mm joints) — *standard for KPWD*
- 1 m³ brickwork nominal uses **500 standard bricks** (incl. 5 % wastage = 525 bricks)
- Mortar volume = $1 - (0.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.10 \times 500) = 1 - 1.00 = \text{N.A.}$
- Actual: Dry mortar = $1.0 \times 1.33 = 1.33$ m³ → cement + sand
- Mortar ratio 1:6 → $\Sigma = 7$
- Cost of brick at site (Kerala) = ₹ **₹ 9/-** per brick
- Cement (50 kg) = ₹ **440** per bag
- M-Sand = ₹ **1,800** per m³
- Mason task = 1.2 m³/day; Unskilled = 1.5 nos./mason, task = 1.0 m³/day
- Mason wage = ₹ **780**; unskilled = ₹ **380**
- Sundries + water = 1.5 % of MC + LC; Profit = 10 %

**Step 1 — Bricks required per m³**

$$
Q_{brick} = 500 \times 1.05 = 525 \text{ bricks}
$$

**Step 2 — Mortar (1:6) volume per m³**

$$
V_{mortar,dry} = 0.33 \text{ m³ per 1 m³ brickwork} \times 1.33 = 0.439 \text{ m³}
$$

**Step 3 — Cement**

$$
Q_{cement} = \frac{1}{7} \times 0.439 \times 1.01 = 0.0633 \text{ m³} = 91.16 \text{ kg} = 1.82 \text{ bags}
$$

**Step 4 — M-Sand (12 % wastage)**

$$
Q_{sand} = \frac{6}{7} \times 0.439 \times 1.12 = 0.4214 \text{ m³}
$$

**Step 5 — Material Cost**

$$
MC = (525 \times 9) + (1.82 \times 440) + (0.4214 \times 1800)
$$

$$
MC = 4725.00 + 800.80 + 758.52 = 6284.32 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 6 — Labour Cost**

$$
LC = \frac{1 \times 780}{1.2} + \frac{1.5 \times 380}{1.0} = 650.00 + 570.00 = 1220.00 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 7 — Overheads**

$$
OH = 0.015 \times (6284.32 + 1220.00) = 112.57 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 8 — Subtotal & Profit**

$$
S = 6284.32 + 1220.00 + 0 + 112.57 = 7616.89 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

$$
R = 7616.89 \times 1.10 = 8378.58 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analysed Rate for 1:6 brickwork (superstructure)** = **₹ 8,379 per m³** (rounded to **₹ 8,400/- per m³**)

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Table — Comparison of the Three Examples

| **S.No.** | **Item of Work** | **MC (₹)** | **LC (₹)** | **PMC (₹)** | **OH (₹)** | **Profit (₹)** | **Final Rate (₹/unit)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earthwork excavation, ordinary soil | 0.00 | 154.29 | 0.00 | 3.09 | 15.74 | **173.12 / m³** |
| 2 | P.C.C. (1:4:8) in foundation | 4704.32 | 1127.14 | 80.00 | 87.47 | 599.89 | **6,598.82 / m³** |
| 3 | Brickwork (1:6) superstructure | 6284.32 | 1220.00 | 0.00 | 112.57 | 761.69 | **8,378.58 / m³** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — The Analysis-of-Rates (AOR) Procedural Pipeline

The following Mermaid block captures the **complete AOR workflow**, from identifying an item of work to producing a final rate. It is rendered as a **block-level functional architecture** (per the V10 fallback rule for non-physical drawings).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Identify Item of Work and its Unit] --> B0[Collect Material Quantities per Unit]
    B0 --> C0[Apply Material Wastage Percentage]
    C0 --> D0[Add Carriage Lead Cost]
    D0 --> E0[Compute Material Cost MC]
    A0 --> F0[Identify Labour Categories Required]
    F0 --> G0[Refer Task Work Outturn Tables]
    G0 --> H0[Compute Labour Cost LC]
    A0 --> I0[Identify Plant Machinery Hire]
    I0 --> J0[Compute PMC Hire Cost]
    E0 --> K0[Apply Overheads Water Sundries]
    H0 --> K0
    J0 --> K0
    K0 --> L0[Compute Subtotal S]
    L0 --> M0[Add Contractor Profit 10 to 15 percent]
    M0 --> N0[Final Analysed Rate per Unit]
    N0 --> O0[Validate against KPWD DSR / MoRD SOR]
    O0 --> P0[Approved Rate Entered in Abstract of Estimate]
    P0 --> Q0[Add Contingency 3 to 5 percent]
    Q0 --> R0[Add Work Charged Establishment 1.5 to 2 percent]
    R0 --> S0[Add Departmental Charges 5 to 8 percent]
    S0 --> T0[Total Estimated Project Cost]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Abstract of Estimate (AoE) Preparation

The Abstract of Estimate is **not a calculation** — it is a **structured summary table**. The diagram below depicts the **AoE assembly architecture** from BoQ (Bill of Quantities) to grand-total approval.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Bill of Quantities BoQ from Detailed Estimate] --> B1[List of Items in Tabular Form]
    B1 --> C1[Column Quantity per item]
    B1 --> D1[Column Unit e.g. m3 m2 m]
    B1 --> E1[Column Analysed Rate per unit in Rupees]
    C1 --> F1[Row wise Multiplication]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Item wise Amount in Rupees]
    G1 --> H1[Add Contingencies 3 to 5 percent]
    H1 --> I1[Add Work Charged Establishment]
    I1 --> J1[Add Departmental Supervision Charges]
    J1 --> K1[Grand Total Estimated Cost]
    K1 --> L1[Signatures of Junior Engineer Assistant Engineer Executive Engineer]
    L1 --> M1[Submission to Tender Approval Authority]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Hierarchy — Relationship between Detailed Estimate, AOR, and Abstract of Estimate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Detailed Estimate Stage 1] --> B2[Quantity Takeoff from Drawings]
    B2 --> C2[Bill of Quantities BoQ]
    A2 --> D2[Analysis of Rates Stage 2]
    D2 --> E2[Item wise Analysed Rates in Rs per unit]
    C2 --> F2[Abstract of Estimate Stage 3]
    E2 --> F2
    F2 --> G2[Add Charges Contingency OH etc]
    G2 --> H2[Final Sanctioned Project Cost]
    H2 --> I2[Tender Notice Floatation]
    I2 --> J2[Execution Measurement Books MB]
    J2 --> K2[Running Account Bill RA Bill]
    K2 --> L2[Final Bill Payment to Contractor]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Expectation:** Any answer that does not show the **5 pillars (M, L, P, OH, Profit)** and does not reference **wastage + lead + lift + task work** in the AOR derivation will be marked incomplete. Always end the AOR with the line:
> *“Hence, the analysed rate for the item ‘X’ is ₹ ____ per unit.”*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> Define the term *Analysis of Rates* and *Abstract of Estimate*. How are they related in the cost-estimation workflow of a farm structure project?

**Model Answer:**

- **Analysis of Rates (AOR):** A systematic procedure of determining the **cost per unit quantity** of an item of work (e.g., ₹/m³ for excavation, ₹/m² for brickwork) by breaking it into the **5 elemental costs — Material, Labour, Plant & Machinery, Overheads, and Contractor’s Profit**.
- **Abstract of Estimate (AoE):** A final **summary table** listing every item of work, its *quantity*, *unit*, and *analysed rate*, and computing the **total estimated project cost** by including *contingencies*, *work-charged establishment*, and *departmental charges*.
- **Relationship:** AOR produces the *unit rate* of each item; the AoE *consumes* those unit rates, multiplies them with the *quantities* from the **detailed estimate / BoQ**, and aggregates the grand total. **Without AOR, no AoE can be prepared** — they are sequential stages of the same cost-estimation chain.

*(3 marks: 1 for AOR definition, 1 for AoE definition, 1 for relationship)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

> Explain the terms *Lead*, *Lift*, and *Wastage* in the context of Analysis of Rates. Mention typical values for cement, sand, and aggregate.

**Model Answer:**

- **Lead:** The *horizontal distance* in **km** from the source of material to the construction site. **Standard lead = 1 km**; beyond that, *carriage* charges are paid per km as per KPWD DSR.
- **Lift:** The *vertical distance* in **m** through which material is lifted for placement. **Standard lift = 1.5 m** in earthwork/brickwork; beyond that, *extra lift* is paid per 1.5 m vertical stage.
- **Wastage:** Material lost during transit, handling, and storage. Typical values:
  - **Cement = 1 %**
  - **Sand = 7 %–15 %** (use 12 % for M-sand in Kerala)
  - **Aggregate = 5 %–7 %**
  - **Bricks = 5 %**

*(3 marks: 1 lead, 1 lift, 1 wastage with values)*

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module 4)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern):** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Part B Module 4]**

> **(a)** What is a *Schedule of Rates (SOR)*? Distinguish between a Schedule of Rates and an Analysed Rate. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Prepare the **Analysis of Rate** for the item *“1 m³ of Plain Cement Concrete (1:2:4) using 20 mm broken granite jelly for the floor of a threshing yard”*. Assume the following data: cement (53 grade, 50 kg bag at site) = ₹ 440; 20 mm jelly = ₹ 2,200/m³; M-sand = ₹ 1,800/m³; mason wage = ₹ 780/day, task = 1.3 m³/day; 1.5 unskilled helpers/mason, wage = ₹ 380/day, task = 1.0 m³/day; mixer hire = ₹ 90/m³; wastage: cement 1 %, sand 12 %, aggregate 7 %; sundries + water = 1.5 %; profit = 10 %. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — SOR vs Analysed Rate

**Schedule of Rates (SOR):**

- A *published* document (e.g., **KPWD DSR**, **CPWD DAR**, **MoRD SOR**) issued periodically (usually annually) by a Government department.
- Contains **pre-analysed unit rates** of *all standard items* of work for that region.
- Used as a **standard reference** for estimating, tendering, and valuing variations.
- **Not project-specific**; it is a *rate book*.

**Analysed Rate (AOR):**

- A *project-specific* calculation done by the estimator to derive the rate of a particular item.
- For **non-standard items not in SOR** (e.g., a unique vermicompost pit design, a special *azolla* cultivation tank), AOR *must* be prepared.
- Reflects *current local market rates* of materials, labour, and hire charges.

**Key Distinctions:**

| **Aspect** | **SOR** | **AOR** |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Government publication | Estimator’s own calculation |
| Periodicity | Annual / biennial | Per project |
| Scope | Standard, region-wide | Item-specific |
| Use | Quick reference | Non-SOR / special items |
| Authority | Statutory (KPWD) | Internal to project |

*(7 marks: 2 definition SOR, 2 definition AOR, 3 distinguishing table/diagram)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — AOR for P.C.C. 1:2:4 in Threshing Floor

**Given:** Mix 1:2:4, wet volume = **1 m³**, dry-volume factor = **1.54**.

**Step 1 — Dry Volume**

$$
V_{dry} = 1 \times 1.54 = 1.54 \text{ m³}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of Mix Ratios**

$$
\Sigma = 1 + 2 + 4 = 7
$$

**Step 3 — Cement (with 1 % wastage)**

$$
Q_c = \frac{1}{7} \times 1.54 \times 1.01 = 0.2221 \text{ m³}
$$

$$
\text{Mass} = 0.2221 \times 1440 = 319.83 \text{ kg} \Rightarrow \frac{319.83}{50} = 6.40 \text{ bags}
$$

**Step 4 — M-Sand (12 % wastage)**

$$
Q_s = \frac{2}{7} \times 1.54 \times 1.12 = 0.4928 \text{ m³}
$$

**Step 5 — 20 mm Aggregate (7 % wastage)**

$$
Q_a = \frac{4}{7} \times 1.54 \times 1.07 = 0.9413 \text{ m³}
$$

**Step 6 — Material Cost (MC)**

$$
MC = (6.40 \times 440) + (0.4928 \times 1800) + (0.9413 \times 2200)
$$

$$
MC = 2816.00 + 887.04 + 2070.86 = 5773.90 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 7 — Labour Cost (LC)**

$$
LC = \frac{1 \times 780}{1.3} + \frac{1.5 \times 380}{1.0} = 600.00 + 570.00 = 1170.00 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 8 — Plant & Machinery**

$$
PMC = 90 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 9 — Overheads (1.5 % on MC + LC)**

$$
OH = 0.015 \times (5773.90 + 1170.00) = 0.015 \times 6943.90 = 104.16 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

**Step 10 — Subtotal & Profit**

$$
S = 5773.90 + 1170.00 + 90.00 + 104.16 = 7138.06 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

$$
R = 7138.06 \times 1.10 = 7851.86 \text{ ₹/m³}
$$

> **∴ Analysed Rate for P.C.C. 1:2:4 (threshing floor) = ₹ 7,852 / m³**

*(7 marks: 1 for dry vol + mix ratio, 1.5 for cement, 1.5 for sand + aggregate, 1 for labour, 0.5 PMC, 0.5 OH, 1 for profit & final rate)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Part B Module 4]**

> **(a)** With a neat abstract format, explain the procedure for preparing the **Abstract of Estimate** of an *Irrigation Channel (1.2 km, trapezoidal cross-section, 0.6 m bottom width, 0.6 m depth, side slope 1:1, lined with brick-on-edge in 1:4 cement mortar)*. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Discuss the typical **percentage additions** for contingencies, work-charged establishment, and departmental charges, and justify why each is necessary in the final AoE. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — Abstract of Estimate Format for a 1.2 km Brick-Lined Irrigation Channel

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area of Channel**

Trapezoidal: $A = (b + s \cdot d) \times d$, with $b = 0.6 \text{ m}, d = 0.6 \text{ m}, s = 1$

$$
A = (0.6 + 1 \times 0.6) \times 0.6 = 0.72 \text{ m²}
$$

**Step 2 — Earthwork Excavation Quantity (Wet soil, lead 50 m)**

$$
Q_1 = 0.72 \times 1200 = 864 \text{ m³}
$$

Assume AOR = ₹ 200/m³ → Amount = ₹ **1,72,800/-**

**Step 3 — Brick-on-edge Lining (1:4) — Area of side slope lining**

Side lining area (2 sides): $2 \times d \times \sqrt{1+s^2} = 2 \times 0.6 \times \sqrt{2} \times L$

$$
A_{side} = 2 \times 0.6 \times 1.414 \times 1200 = 2036.7 \text{ m²}
$$

Bottom lining: $b \times L = 0.6 \times 1200 = 720 \text{ m²}$

Total lining area: $A_2 = 2036.7 + 720 = 2756.7 \text{ m²}$

Assume AOR (brick-on-edge 1:4) = ₹ 720/m² → Amount = ₹ **19,84,824/-**

**Step 4 — Compacted earthwork in backfilling behind lining**

$$
Q_3 = 0.15 \times 1200 = 180 \text{ m³} \quad @\ ₹ 250/\text{m³} = ₹ 45,000/-
$$

**Step 5 — Neat Abstract of Estimate Table**

| **Sl.No.** | **Item of Work** | **Quantity** | **Unit** | **Rate (₹)** | **Amount (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earthwork excavation (ordinary soil) | 864.00 | m³ | 200.00 | 1,72,800 |
| 2 | Brick-on-edge lining (1:4) | 2756.70 | m² | 720.00 | 19,84,824 |
| 3 | Backfilling, compacted behind lining | 180.00 | m³ | 250.00 | 45,000 |
| | **Subtotal (A)** | | | | **22,02,624** |
| 4 | Add: Contingencies @ 3 % | | | | 66,079 |
| 5 | Add: Work-charged establishment @ 2 % | | | | 44,052 |
| 6 | Add: Departmental / Supervision charges @ 5 % | | | | 1,10,131 |
| | **Grand Total Estimated Cost** | | | | **₹ 24,22,886** |

*(7 marks: 1 cross-section area, 1 quantities, 3 for abstract table, 1 for additions, 1 for grand total)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — Percentage Additions in AoE

**1. Contingencies (3 % to 5 %):**

- Reserved for *unforeseen items* arising during execution (e.g., hidden rock, dewatering, redesign of junctions).
- Without contingencies, the contractor would raise *extra claims* and the project would stall for want of funds.
- Typical KTU/KPWD: **3 %** for well-defined projects, **5 %** for rural agricultural works where soil/site variations are common.

**2. Work-Charged Establishment (1.5 % to 2 %):**

- Covers the salary of *work-charged staff* (chainmen, mates, junior overseers) engaged *only for this project* and not on the regular establishment.
- Required because the *measurement book* entries must be verified daily.
- Standard: **1.5 %** (regular) to **2 %** (complex agricultural projects).

**3. Departmental / Supervision Charges (5 % to 8 %):**

- Pays for the *design office* (Assistant Engineer, Executive Engineer, draftsman) who supervised preparation, vetting, and site supervision.
- Higher (8 %) when a *consulting engineer* or *third-party quality monitor* is involved.
- Standard: **5 %** for in-house supervision, **8 %** for outsourced consultancy.

**4. Justification Summary:**

| **Charge** | **Range** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|
| Contingency | 3 – 5 % | Unforeseen extras |
| Work-charged estt. | 1.5 – 2 % | Site measurement staff |
| Departmental | 5 – 8 % | Design office / supervision |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **not** add the percentage on top of *each row*; apply it on the **subtotal of the AOR-based items**.
> - In the AoE table, the **unit column must match the AOR unit** (m³, m², m, kg, No.) — students often write *m²* for brickwork in m³, losing **2 marks** instantly.
> - The grand total **must be rounded to nearest ₹ 10 or ₹ 100** as per KPWD convention. Showing “₹ 24,22,886.47” is technically correct but considered *unprofessional* — round to **₹ 24,22,890**.

*(7 marks: 1.5 each × 4 charges + 1 for proper rounding & structure)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Page KTU Module-4 Recap — AOR & AoE**

- [x] **Analysis of Rates (AOR)** = Unit cost = **MC + LC + PMC + OH + Profit (10 %–15 %)**
- [x] **Abstract of Estimate (AoE)** = **Σ (Quantity × Analysed Rate) + Contingency + WCE + Departmental charges**
- [x] **5 Pillars of AOR:** Material, Labour, Plant & Machinery, Overheads, Profit
- [x] **Task Work (Out-turn)** = units of work done by 1 labour in 8 hrs (e.g., excavation in ordinary soil ≈ 2.8 m³/day in Kerala)
- [x] **Wet → Dry Volume Factor for Concrete = 1.54**; **Plaster = 1.30**; **Brickwork mortar = 1.33**
- [x] **Standard Wastage:** Cement 1 % · Sand 12 % · Aggregate 7 % · Bricks 5 %
- [x] **Standard Lead = 1 km**; **Standard Lift = 1.5 m** (per stage)
- [x] **Overheads on (MC+LC):** Water 1.5–2 %; Sundries 1–2 %; combined use 1.5 %
- [x] **Contingency in AoE = 3–5 %** on subtotal; **WCE = 1.5–2 %**; **Departmental = 5–8 %**
- [x] **Cement 1 bag = 50 kg**; **density 1440 kg/m³**; **1 m³ = 30 bags nominal**
- [x] **Brick (Kerala nominal) = 200 × 100 × 100 mm**; **per m³ brickwork ≈ 500 bricks nominal + 5 % wastage = 525**
- [x] **Always show units** in every column of AoE (₹/m³, ₹/m², etc.)
- [x] **Always apply profit on the subtotal of MC + LC + PMC + OH**, **not** on the Grand Total
- [x] **References for KTU/Kerala:** *KPWD DSR 2024*, *CPWD DAR Vol I & II*, *MoRD SOR — Rural Infrastructure*, *CWC SOR — Command Area Works*
- [x] **Typical order of AOR derivation:** Identify item → Compute MC (with wastage & lead) → Compute LC (using task) → Add PMC → Add OH → Subtotal → +10–15 % profit → **Final Unit Rate**
- [x] **Format of AoE** (always): *Sl. No. | Description | Quantity | Unit | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹)* — followed by *Contingency, WCE, Departmental* rows and *Grand Total* row
- [x] **Always end with:** *“Hence, the analysed rate for the item ‘___’ is ₹ _____ per unit”* and *“Grand total estimated cost = ₹ _____ (Rounded to nearest ₹ 10/100)”*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement Books
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measurement Books

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Measurement Book (M.B.)** is the **official, statutory, and permanent record** maintained at a construction site (or farm structure project site) in which all measurements of executed work, supplies of materials, and labour deployed are entered in a prescribed proforma. It is the **primary documentary evidence** on the basis of which the contract bills are prepared, checked, and passed for payment.

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAGT501), the Measurement Book is treated as a **public document of financial accountability**, because every rupee released under a farm structure contract (irrigation channel, threshing floor, dairy shed, greenhouse, godown, fence, culvert, etc.) must be traceable back to a dimensionally verified entry in the M.B.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Used by KTU / PWD / CPWD Standards:**
> - **MB** — Measurement Book
> - **H.F.M.B.** — Hidden / Filled-up Measurement Book (record of items that get concealed)
> - **L.S.M.B.** — Lump Sum Measurement Book (record of items paid on lumpsum basis)
> - **Site Order Book** — separate from MB; for site instructions only
> - **Standard Measurement Book** — used for periodic/running repairs

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Readers

Imagine you are building a small **threshing floor (kacha/pucca)** for a farmer. The contractor says, "I have laid **4.5 m³ of cement concrete (1:2:4)** and laid **22 m² of 20 mm plaster**." Before the engineer releases the cheque, two questions must be answered:

1. **Did the contractor really do that much work?** (Quantity check)
2. **Is the rate applied correct as per the sanctioned estimate?** (Rate check)

The Measurement Book is the **single-page notebook** in which the Junior Engineer (or the student's supervisor, in academic projects) actually **measures with a tape, records dimensions (Length × Breadth × Depth),** calculates the quantity, and signs it. Without this entry, **no payment is legally possible**.

> **Simple Analogy:** Think of the M.B. as the **"medical prescription book"** of a project. Just as a chemist will not dispense medicine without a written, signed, dated prescription by a registered doctor, the Accounts Office will not release payment without a written, signed, dated measurement entry by an authorised engineer.

---

## 1.3 Physical & Statutory Constants of a Standard M.B.

| Parameter | Standard Specification |
|---|---|
| **Page Size** | **A4 (210 mm × 297 mm)** or Foolscap (8" × 13") |
| **Pages per Book** | **200 pages** (100 leaves) |
| **Ruling** | **Ruled columns** for Item No., Description, Quantity, Unit, Remarks |
| **Cover** | **Hard-bound**, with printed Serial Number, Project Name, Division |
| **Custodian** | Junior Engineer / Overseer (single custodian rule) |
| **Retention Period** | **30 years** after completion (public record) |
| **Language** | English (regional translations permitted in brackets) |

> [!NOTE]
> Every page is **machine-numbered**. No page may be torn, overwritten, or destroyed. **Correction fluid (white ink) is strictly prohibited** — corrections are made by **single-line strike-through, dated re-entry, and initial of the recording officer**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Measurement Books

Under the **CPWD / PWD / KTU Works Manual** (which KTU follows for farm structure contracts), Measurement Books are of **four types**:

### (a) Standard Measurement Book (S.M.B.)
- Used for **original / new construction works** (e.g., new dairy shed, new farm godown).
- All running account (R.A.) bills are prepared from this book.
- Each item of work is measured **as and when executed**.

### (b) Hidden / Filled-up Measurement Book (H.F.M.B.)
- Used to record **work that gets concealed** by subsequent construction.
- *Examples:* depth of foundation concrete before brickwork covers it; reinforcement inside RCC before shuttering is closed; waterproofing below plaster.
- These entries must be **jointly signed by the contractor and the engineer** and **photographed** for permanent record.

### (c) Lump Sum Measurement Book (L.S.M.B.)
- Used for items where **entire work is paid as one lump-sum amount** (e.g., supplying a submersible pump set, erecting a pre-engineered building).
- Only **percentage of completion** is recorded, not itemised dimensions.

### (d) Standard Measurement Book for Repairs (S.M.B. – Repairs)
- Used for **annual maintenance / repairs** of existing farm structures.
- Pre-measured dimensions of the existing structure are recorded once, and subsequent bills are based on this baseline.

---

## 2.2 Rules for Recording Measurements (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **9 cardinal rules** are tested every year in KTU ESE — memorise them verbatim.

1. **Single Custody Rule:** Only **one designated officer** (Junior Engineer / Overseer) shall maintain a given M.B. at any time. Transfer requires a **formal handover entry**.
2. **No Blank Spaces:** Every line must have either a measurement entry or a **horizontal red-ink dash** to prevent later insertions.
3. **No Erasures:** Corrections only by **single-line strike-through**, initialled and dated.
4. **Measurements in Ink:** Only **blue/black ink** for entries; **red ink** for corrections and abstract totals.
5. **Dimensions First, Quantity Later:** Record actual measured dimensions (L × B × D/H), then compute quantity on the right side or in the abstract column.
6. **Date of Measurement:** Every entry must carry the **date of measurement**, not the date of entry.
7. **Reference to Drawing/Item:** Mention the **drawing number, item number in the Bill of Quantities (BOQ), and sanctioned estimate page**.
8. **Cross-Reference:** When part of a measurement is hidden, a **cross-reference to the H.F.M.B. page** must be given on the main M.B. page.
9. **Signature & Seal:** Each entry must be signed by the **recording officer** and, where required, by the **contractor's representative**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet for Measurement Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> The general quantity formula is the **"Volume / Area / Length basis"** depending on the item. The M.B. records the **"raw dimensions"**, and the **"quantity"** is derived from these formulas in the abstract.

$$
Q \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( L_i \times B_i \times H_i \right)
$$

where:
- $Q$ = total quantity in the unit relevant to the item
- $L_i$, $B_i$, $H_i$ = Length, Breadth, Height/Depth of the $i$-th portion
- $n$ = number of distinct portions (gaps, openings deducted)

For area-based items (plaster, flooring, roofing sheets):

$$
A \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( L_i \times B_i \right)
$$

For linear items (fencing, pipe laying, expansion joints):

$$
\ell \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i
$$

For deduction of openings in plaster/ masonry:

$$
A_{\text{net}} \;=\; A_{\text{gross}} \;-\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} (l_j \times b_j)
$$

where $m$ = number of openings (doors, windows, ventilation slits).

| Item Category | Unit | Formula Used |
|---|---|---|
| Earthwork excavation | m³ | $L \times B \times D$ (with slopes if trapezoidal) |
| PCC / RCC concrete | m³ | $L \times B \times T$ |
| Brick masonry | m³ | $L \times B \times H$ minus openings |
| Plaster / flooring | m² | $L \times B$ minus openings |
| Centering & shuttering | m² | Contact surface area |
| Steel reinforcement | kg | Bar-bending schedule × unit weight (kg/m) |
| Roofing (AC/GI sheets) | m² | Plan area + laps |
| Pipe laying | m | Net laid length (no laps paid) |
| Fencing wire | m | Total run of fence |
| Painting / whitewashing | m² | Surface area developed |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Farm Structure Engineering

- **Transparency:** Farmers / funding agencies (NABARD, RKVY, PMKSY) can audit physical progress against money released.
- **Dispute Resolution:** In case of contractor disputes, the M.B. is the **legal primary evidence** in arbitration / court.
- **Audit Trail:** The CAG (Comptroller & Auditor General) of India relies on M.B.s for audit of public-funded farm infrastructure.
- **Academic / Project Use:** In KTU's PCAGT501 mini-projects (estimating cost of a farm godown, dairy shed, poultry house), students must **prepare a mock M.B.** as part of their estimate submission.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Matrix & Worked Measurement Example

## 3.1 Procedural Matrix — How an Entry is Made in the M.B.

Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step logic flow** that a Junior Engineer must follow for every item:

> **Step 1** → Reach the site on the **date of measurement** (not entry date).
> **Step 2** → Verify the work is **actually executed** as per the sanctioned drawing and item.
> **Step 3** → Take measurements using **steel tape / measuring rod** (never cloth tape for precision).
> **Step 4** → Record dimensions in the **"Details of Measurements"** column in the order **Length, Breadth, Height/Depth** (L × B × H).
> **Step 5** → For concealed work, **call the contractor's representative** to witness and jointly sign.
> **Step 6** → Apply deductions for **openings, voids, overlaps** as per IS 1200 / standard methods of measurement.
> **Step 7** → Calculate **net quantity** in the "Quantity" column.
> **Step 8** → Write the **item number from BOQ**, **drawing reference**, **date**.
> **Step 9** → Sign with **name, designation, date** in blue/black ink.
> **Step 10** → At the end of the day, **total the page** in red ink and carry forward to next page.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — A Complete M.B. Entry

### Problem Statement (Typical KTU Lab / Exam Style)

> A farm **godown floor** (Pucca) is to be constructed. The Junior Engineer measures the following dimensions for **cement concrete (1:4:8) in foundation**:
>
> - Strip footing runs along 4 walls of size **12.0 m × 6.0 m** overall.
> - Footing width = **0.90 m**, depth = **0.30 m** below plinth.
> - Cross-strips (footings under internal columns/walls): 3 strips each **5.4 m long × 0.90 m wide × 0.30 m deep**.
>
> Record the measurement and compute the quantity of PCC in m³ as it would appear in the M.B.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Compute length of all longitudinal strips.**

The godown is 12.0 m × 6.0 m. There are **2 longitudinal strips** (one on each long wall):
$$
L_{\text{long}} \;=\; 2 \times 12.0 \;=\; 24.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute length of transverse strips.**

There are **2 transverse strips** (one on each short wall):
$$
L_{\text{trans}} \;=\; 2 \times 6.0 \;=\; 12.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute length of cross-strips (internal).**

Given: 3 cross-strips × 5.4 m each:
$$
L_{\text{cross}} \;=\; 3 \times 5.4 \;=\; 16.2 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Total length of all footing strips.**
$$
L_{\text{total}} \;=\; 24.0 \;+\; 12.0 \;+\; 16.2 \;=\; 52.2 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the standard quantity formula for PCC in footing.**
$$
Q_{\text{PCC}} \;=\; L_{\text{total}} \times B \times D
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$
Q_{\text{PCC}} \;=\; 52.2 \times 0.90 \times 0.30
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply sequentially.**
$$
52.2 \times 0.90 \;=\; 46.98
$$
$$
46.98 \times 0.30 \;=\; 14.094
$$

**Step 8 — Round off to 2 decimal places.**
$$
Q_{\text{PCC}} \;\approx\; 14.09 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 9 — Final M.B. Entry as Actually Written

| Item No. | Description | No. | Length (m) | Breadth (m) | Depth (m) | Quantity | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2.1 | PCC 1:4:8 in foundation of godown | | | | | | | Drg. No. F-03, Est. Pg. 7 |
| | Longitudinal footings | 2 | 12.00 | 0.90 | 0.30 | 6.48 | m³ | |
| | Transverse footings | 2 | 6.00 | 0.90 | 0.30 | 3.24 | m³ | |
| | Cross-strips | 3 | 5.40 | 0.90 | 0.30 | 4.37 | m³ | |
| | **Total carried to abstract** | | | | | **14.09** | m³ | |

> Signed: J.E. (Name) ___ Date: ___
> Contractor's rep. signature: ___ Date: ___

---

## 3.3 Abstract Linking — From M.B. Page to Bill

Once the M.B. page is signed, the quantity **14.09 m³** is carried to the **abstract of measurements** at the back of the M.B. (or to the bill register), where the **rate from the sanctioned schedule of rates (SOR)** is applied.

$$
\text{Amount payable for Item 2.1} \;=\; Q_{\text{PCC}} \times R_{\text{PCC}}
$$

For example, if the approved rate is **₹ 3,850 per m³** for PCC 1:4:8 in foundation:

$$
\text{Amount} \;=\; 14.09 \times 3{,}850
$$

$$
\text{Amount} \;=\; 14.09 \times 3{,}850
$$

$$
14 \times 3{,}850 \;=\; 53{,}900
$$

$$
0.09 \times 3{,}850 \;=\; 346.50
$$

$$
\text{Amount}_{\text{total}} \;=\; 53{,}900 \;+\; 346.50 \;=\; \mathbf{₹ \; 54{,}246.50}
$$

> This is the **exact figure** that flows into the **Running Account Bill (R.A. Bill)** submitted by the contractor for the first tranche of payment.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 M.B. Lifecycle Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

Below is a Mermaid block diagram showing the **functional flow** of how a measurement enters the M.B. and ultimately becomes a paid amount.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Sanctioned Estimate and BOQ"] --> nodeB["Site Execution of Work"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Junior Engineer visits site with tape and M.B."]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Is the work concealed soon after?"}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE["Joint Measurement with Contractor"]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF["Solo Measurement by J.E."]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Record Dimensions in MB page"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Compute Net Quantity using formula"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Deductions required?"}
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ["Subtract Openings and Voids"]
    nodeI -- No --> nodeK["Proceed to Abstract"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["Apply Approved Rate from SOR"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Compute Bill Amount"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["R.A. Bill Submitted by Contractor"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Verification by Accounts Section"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["Payment Released"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["MB Entry Archived for 30 years"]
```

> The above flowchart illustrates the **direct linkage** between the M.B. entry and the actual disbursement of funds — making it the **most critical control document** in any farm structure project.

---

## 4.2 M.B. Page Layout — Schematic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    block1["MB Header Box: Project Name, Division, Serial No."] --> block2["Item No. Column"]
    block2 --> block3["Description of Work Column"]
    block3 --> block4["No. of Parts"]
    block4 --> block5["Length in m"]
    block5 --> block6["Breadth in m"]
    block6 --> block7["Height or Depth in m"]
    block7 --> block8["Quantity Computed"]
    block8 --> block9["Unit m² or m³ or kg"]
    block9 --> block10["Remarks: Drawing Ref, Date, Signatures"]
```

---

## 4.3 Document Hierarchy Diagram — Where MB Sits

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a1["Sanctioned Technical Sanction"] --> a2["Administrative Approval and Fund Sanction"]
    a2 --> a3["Detailed Estimate with BOQ"]
    a3 --> a4["Tender and Work Order"]
    a4 --> a5["Execution at Site"]
    a5 --> a6["MEASUREMENT BOOK M.B."]
    a6 --> a7["R.A. Bill or Final Bill"]
    a7 --> a8["Payment and Audit"]
    a6 -.-> a9["H.F.M.B. for concealed items"]
    a6 -.-> a10["L.S.M.B. for lump sum items"]
```

> The M.B. is the **central nerve** of the entire documentation chain.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define a Measurement Book (M.B.). Mention any two essential features that must be followed while recording entries in it.**

**Model Answer:**
A Measurement Book (M.B.) is the **official record maintained at a construction site** in which measurements of executed work are entered for preparing contractor bills.
**Essential features:**
1. Only **one designated officer** shall maintain a given M.B. (single custody rule).
2. **No erasures** are permitted; corrections are made by single-line strike-through with date and initials.
3. Entries must be in **permanent blue/black ink**, dated, and signed.
*(Any two correctly explained = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**What is an H.F.M.B.? Give one example of work recorded in it.**

**Model Answer:**
H.F.M.B. stands for **Hidden / Filled-up Measurement Book**. It is used to record measurements of items of work that will be **concealed by subsequent construction** and therefore cannot be measured later.
**Example:** Depth and volume of **PCC in foundation** before brickwork is raised over it; **reinforcement steel inside RCC slab** before shuttering is closed.
*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for valid example = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **lose 2–3 marks** by writing definitions without mentioning the **single-custody rule and no-erasures rule**.
> - In numerical M.B. problems, students **forget to deduct openings** (doors/windows in plaster/masonry) — this is a **favourite deduction trap** in valuation.
> - Don't write "M.B." loosely — specify **which type** (Standard, H.F.M.B., L.S.M.B., Repairs S.M.B.).
> - Always carry forward totals in **red ink** — missing this loses a mark for "presentation".

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of Measurement Books** used in farm structure projects. Mention the **rules for recording measurements** in a standard M.B. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The **cement concrete (1:3:6) flooring** in a farm dairy shed is to be measured. The shed has internal dimensions of **18.0 m × 8.0 m**. A **drainage channel** of width **0.30 m** runs along all four internal walls. The flooring thickness is **0.10 m**. Compute the net quantity of CC flooring in **m³** as it would be recorded in the M.B. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Classification & Rules:**

**Classification (4 marks):**
1. **Standard M.B. (S.M.B.)** — for new construction works (e.g., new dairy shed, new farm godown).
2. **H.F.M.B.** — for concealed items (e.g., foundation concrete, encased reinforcement).
3. **L.S.M.B.** — for lump-sum contract items (e.g., supply of pump set, pre-engineered building).
4. **Standard M.B. (Repairs)** — for annual/periodic maintenance of existing structures.

**Rules for recording (3 marks — any 3 of the following):**
1. Single custody — only one officer maintains one book.
2. No blank spaces — empty lines ruled off in red.
3. No erasures — single-line strike-through, dated, initialled.
4. Blue/black ink for entries; red ink for corrections and totals.
5. Date of measurement must be mentioned.
6. Reference to drawing and BOQ item number mandatory.
7. Cross-reference to H.F.M.B. for concealed portions.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Step 1:** Gross internal floor area of dairy shed.
$$
A_{\text{gross}} \;=\; 18.0 \times 8.0 \;=\; 144.00 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Area occupied by drainage channel along 4 walls.
$$
L_{\text{channel}} \;=\; 2 \times (18.0 + 8.0) \;=\; 2 \times 26.0 \;=\; 52.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Width of channel = 0.30 m.
$$
A_{\text{channel}} \;=\; 52.0 \times 0.30 \;=\; 15.60 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Net floor area to be laid with CC.
$$
A_{\text{net}} \;=\; A_{\text{gross}} \;-\; A_{\text{channel}} \;=\; 144.00 \;-\; 15.60 \;=\; 128.40 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by flooring thickness 0.10 m to get volume.
$$
Q_{\text{CC}} \;=\; A_{\text{net}} \times T \;=\; 128.40 \times 0.10 \;=\; 12.84 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Final M.B. Entry:**
| Item | Description | No. | L (m) | B (m) | T (m) | Qty (m³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | CC 1:3:6 flooring, dairy shed | — | — | — | — | — |
| | Gross | 1 | 18.00 | 8.00 | 0.10 | 14.40 |
| | Less: drainage channel | 1 | 52.00 | 0.30 | 0.10 | (1.56) |
| | **Net quantity** | | | | | **12.84** |

Signed: J.E. ___ Dated: ___

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a)** Differentiate between a **Standard M.B., H.F.M.B., and L.S.M.B.** with one suitable example of farm structure work for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **brick masonry boundary wall** for a farm is constructed. The wall runs along the **perimeter of a rectangular plot of 50.0 m × 30.0 m**. The wall is **0.30 m thick and 1.80 m high** above plinth. The wall has **one main gate opening of 4.0 m wide** and **one small personal door of 1.0 m wide**. Compute the **net quantity of brick masonry in m³** as recorded in the M.B. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Differentiation (in tabular form, 7 marks):**

| Aspect | Standard M.B. | H.F.M.B. | L.S.M.B. |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Use** | New construction | Concealed work | Lump-sum items |
| **Measurement detail** | Full L×B×H dimensions | Full dimensions before concealment | Only % completion noted |
| **Visibility later** | Visible | Not visible (covered) | Visible but not dimensioned |
| **Example (farm)** | PCC in plinth beam of dairy shed | Depth of foundation concrete before brickwork | Supply & erection of pre-engineered poultry house |
| **Signatures** | J.E. only | J.E. + Contractor (joint) | J.E. + Contractor |
| **Photographic evidence** | Not mandatory | Mandatory | Not mandatory |
| **Bill type** | Itemised R.A. Bill | Itemised R.A. Bill | % -based milestone bill |

*(Any 7 distinct differentiating points covering all 3 types = 7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Step 1:** Perimeter of rectangular plot.
$$
P \;=\; 2 \times (50.0 + 30.0) \;=\; 2 \times 80.0 \;=\; 160.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Total width of openings.
$$
W_{\text{open}} \;=\; 4.0 \;+\; 1.0 \;=\; 5.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Net length of brick wall.
$$
L_{\text{net}} \;=\; 160.0 \;-\; 5.0 \;=\; 155.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply volume formula for masonry.
$$
Q_{\text{masonry}} \;=\; L_{\text{net}} \times B \times H
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute values.
$$
Q_{\text{masonry}} \;=\; 155.0 \times 0.30 \times 1.80
$$

**Step 6:** Sequential multiplication.
$$
155.0 \times 0.30 \;=\; 46.50
$$
$$
46.50 \times 1.80 \;=\; 83.70
$$

**Step 7:** Final quantity.
$$
Q_{\text{masonry}} \;=\; 83.70 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Final M.B. Entry:**
| Item | Description | No. | L (m) | B (m) | H (m) | Qty (m³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4.1 | Brick masonry boundary wall | | | | | |
| | Total perimeter | 1 | 160.00 | 0.30 | 1.80 | 86.40 |
| | Less: main gate opening | 1 | 4.00 | 0.30 | 1.80 | (2.16) |
| | Less: personal door | 1 | 1.00 | 0.30 | 1.80 | (0.54) |
| | **Net quantity** | | | | | **83.70** |

Signed: J.E. ___ Dated: ___

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **M.B. = Measurement Book** — official, statutory site record of executed work dimensions.
> - **Four types:** Standard M.B. / H.F.M.B. / L.S.M.B. / Standard M.B. (Repairs).
> - **Single Custody Rule** — only one officer maintains a given book.
> - **No erasures** — corrections by single-line strike-through, initial + date.
> - **Blue/black ink** for entries; **red ink** for corrections, totals, page abstract.
> - **No blank spaces** — rule off unused lines in red ink to prevent tampering.
> - **H.F.M.B. = Hidden / Filled-up M.B.** — for concealed items, joint signature mandatory, photographic evidence recommended.
> - **L.S.M.B. = Lump Sum M.B.** — only % completion noted, no itemised dimensions.
> - **General formula for quantity:** $Q = L \times B \times H$ (m³), $A = L \times B$ (m²), or $\ell$ (m for linear items).
> - **Deductions** must be made for openings (doors, windows, drains, gates) as per IS 1200 methods.
> - **Retention period of M.B. = 30 years** after project completion.
> - M.B. entry directly feeds the **R.A. Bill** through the formula: **Bill Amount = Quantity × Sanctioned Rate (from SOR)**.
> - In KTU PCAGT501 projects / labs, students are expected to **maintain a mock M.B.** with at least 5–7 items for any farm structure cost estimation assignment.
> - Common valuation pitfalls: forgetting deductions, missing drawing reference, missing date of measurement, not signing in red ink for totals.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of PRICE Software; Valuation - Purpose and Principles of Valuation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Use of PRICE Software; Valuation - Purpose and Principles of Valuation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 PRICE Software — The Digital Engineer's Calculator

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
PRICE is the acronym for **"Programme for Rate Analysis and Cost Estimation"**, a comprehensive, menu-driven software package developed and maintained by the **Central Public Works Department (CPWD), Government of India**. It is the official digital platform used for the preparation of detailed estimates, analysis of rates, and abstract of costs for all public works construction projects in India, including farm structures, irrigation systems, and agricultural infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** PRICE software is specifically mentioned in the KTU PCAGT501 Module 4 syllabus. The question pattern in previous university examinations has consistently asked about the *purpose, features, modules, and workflow* of PRICE software — not its programming internals.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Recipe Book:**
Imagine you are running a large commercial bakery. Every time a new cake is ordered, you do not reinvent the recipe from scratch — instead, you pull out a master recipe book that already contains the cost of flour per kg, sugar per kg, oven time per hour, and baker's wages. PRICE software functions exactly like this *smart recipe book* for civil engineers:
- The **"ingredients"** are construction materials (cement, steel, aggregates, timber).
- The **"wages"** are labour rates (skilled, semi-skilled, unskilled).
- The **"rent"** is machinery hire charges.
- The **"recipe"** is the *Schedule of Rates (SoR)* of a particular region.

When you input the **quantity** of work (e.g., "100 m³ of RCC"), PRICE instantly calculates the **total cost** by combining the unit rate (₹/m³) with the quantity, generating a detailed Abstract of Cost in seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Technical Highlight — Currency Standard:** All rates in PRICE software are entered in **Indian Rupees (₹)**, and quantities follow the **Standard Schedule of Dimensions** published by CPWD (e.g., m, m², m³, kg, quintal, number, L.S.).

---

### 1.2 Valuation — The Fair Price of a Farm Asset

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
**Valuation** is the scientific and systematic process of determining the *present fair market value* (or *worth*) of a building, structure, or property at a given point in time. It is the art of estimating the **reasonable selling price**, **rental value**, or **capitalized worth** of a built asset, taking into account its age, condition, location, and the principle of *anticipation of future benefits*.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Used Tractor Valuation:**
Think of a farm owner who wishes to sell his **5-year-old tractor**:
- A naive approach: Use the original purchase price (₹8,00,000) — this **overvalues** the asset.
- Another approach: Scrap value (₹1,50,000) — this **undervalues** the asset.
- The *engineering valuation* sits intelligently between these two extremes, accounting for **depreciation (wear & tear)**, **obsolescence (newer model in market)**, and **residual life**.

Similarly, **Valuation of a Farm Structure** (e.g., a dairy shed, a poultry building, a storage godown) requires the valuer to scientifically estimate its *current depreciated value*, *salvage value*, and *potential rental earning capacity*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 Weight:** Valuation is a high-scoring topic in the KTU ESE because the questions are highly formula-based and procedural. Mastery of the **Years' Purchase (Y.P.)** concept and **Depreciation formulas** is mandatory for full marks.

**Scope of Valuation in Agricultural Engineering Context:**

| Farm Structure Type | Why Valuation is Needed |
|---|---|
| Dairy Cattle Sheds | Insurance assessment, bank loan against mortgage |
| Poultry Houses | Compensation claims, depreciation for tax |
| Greenhouses / Polyhouses | Salvage value for renovation, sale |
| Farm Godowns & Silos | Asset declaration in cooperative records |
| Bore Wells & Pumpsets | Annual depreciation for tax computation |
| Farm Residences | Stamp duty & registration, market sale |

---

### 1.3 Glossary of Core Terms (KTU 2024 Board Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these exact KTU-definitions** — they appear verbatim in past university questions.

- **Gross Value (Capital Cost):** The total original cost of construction/erection of the structure, inclusive of all materials, labour, supervision, and contractor's profit.
- **Net Value (Depreciated Value):** The value of the structure **after** deducting the accumulated depreciation from its gross value.
- **Salvage Value (Scrap Value):** The expected realisable value of the structure at the **end of its useful life**, after dismantling. Mathematically: $S = C \times 5\%$ to $10\%$ typically.
- **Book Value:** The value of the asset as recorded in the **books of account** of the owner at any given year — calculated by deducting *accumulated depreciation* from *original cost*.
- **Market Value (Fair Value):** The price the property would fetch if offered for sale in an **open, competitive market** with both buyer and seller acting prudently.
- **Years' Purchase (Y.P.):** A multiplier factor that converts a *net annual income* into a *capitalized present value*. It is the reciprocal of the rate of interest.
- **Obsolescence:** The loss in value of a structure due to **technological changes, new design trends, or functional inadequacy**, even though the structure is physically sound.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PRICE Software — Architecture, Features & Workflow

PRICE software is built on a **modular, menu-driven architecture** designed to mirror the actual CPWD workflow. Below is the operational logic:

**Step 1: Database Initialization (The "Library" Stage)**
- The user loads the *regional Schedule of Rates (SoR)* — for Kerala, this is the **CPWD SoR or Kerala PWD SoR** that publishes the cost data for Ernakulam, Thiruvananthapuram, etc.
- The user also updates the **Cost Index** (a multiplier that adjusts SoR values to current market prices).

**Step 2: Analysis of Rates (The "Per Unit" Stage)**
- The engineer enters the **materials, labour, and machinery** required *per unit* (e.g., per m³ of M25 concrete).
- PRICE automatically computes the *basic rate*, applies *contractor's profit + overheads* (typically **10% to 15%**), and generates the **unit rate** for each item.

**Step 3: Detailed Estimate Preparation (The "Quantities" Stage)**
- The user enters the **quantities of work** (measurements taken from drawings using standard methods like *long wall-short wall* or *centre line*).
- PRICE multiplies **Quantity × Unit Rate** for each item to produce line-item costs.

**Step 4: Abstract of Cost (The "Final Bill" Stage)**
- The software sums all line-item costs and applies the **contingency** (usually **3% to 5%**) and **work charged establishment** (typically **2%**).
- The final **Abstract of Cost (AoC)** is generated, ready for administrative approval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "List the modules of PRICE", answer with: **(1) Schedule of Rates Module, (2) Analysis of Rates Module, (3) Detailed Estimate Module, (4) Abstract of Cost Module, (5) Report Generation Module**.

---

### 2.2 Valuation — Purpose & Guiding Principles

**Purposes of Valuation (KTU Board — Frequently Asked):**

| S.No. | Purpose of Valuation | Brief Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Buying and Selling** | Determine the fair market price for purchase or sale. |
| 2 | **Mortgage and Loan Security** | Banks require valuation report for advancing loans. |
| 3 | **Taxation** | Property tax, wealth tax, capital gains tax computation. |
| 4 | **Insurance** | Insuring the structure for its accurate present value. |
| 5 | **Rent Fixation** | Determining fair rent based on capitalized value. |
| 6 | **Compensation Acquisition** | When government acquires land/building under Land Acquisition Act. |
| 7 | **Partnership Dissolution** | Asset valuation for share distribution. |
| 8 | **Annual Depreciation** | To account for asset wear-and-tear in books. |
| 9 | **Cost Accounting** | In co-operative farms, for cost-centre allocation. |
| 10 | **Salvage Determination** | To find the value at the end of useful life. |

**Fundamental Principles of Valuation:**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Focus:** The 5 principles below are the *exact syllabus terminology*. The examiner expects these worded correctly.

1. **Principle of Anticipation of Future Benefits:**
   The value of a property is determined by the *future benefits* (income, use, service) it is expected to provide, discounted to present value.

2. **Principle of Supply and Demand:**
   When demand exceeds supply, value rises; when supply exceeds demand, value falls. A farm building near an urban market commands higher valuation than one in a remote village.

3. **Principle of Net Income (Income Capitalization):**
   The capitalized value of a property is computed as $\text{Net Annual Income} \times \text{Years' Purchase}$. This is the most commonly used principle for *income-producing* farm structures (e.g., commercial dairy, rental farmhouses).

4. **Principle of Comparable Sales (Market Comparison):**
   The value is estimated by comparing with **recent sales** of similar properties in the same locality. Ideal for residential farm buildings.

5. **Principle of Cost of Construction (Replacement Cost):**
   The value is the **actual cost of construction** minus accumulated depreciation. Used for *unique* farm structures where market comparison is not feasible (e.g., a specialized greenhouse).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is the single most important table in this topic — memorize it row-by-row.**

| S.No. | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Years' Purchase | $\text{Y.P.} = \dfrac{1}{r}$ where $r$ = rate of interest (decimal) | Convert annual income to capital value |
| 2 | Capitalized Value | $\text{C.V.} = \text{Net Annual Income} \times \text{Y.P.}$ | Valuation of income-yielding property |
| 3 | Straight Line Method of Depreciation | $D_n = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | Equal yearly depreciation |
| 4 | Book Value (Straight Line) | $V_n = C - n \times D$ | Value after $n$ years |
| 5 | Sinking Fund Method — Annual Depreciation | $D = (C - S) \times \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Variable depreciation with reinvestment |
| 6 | Sinking Fund Accumulation | $S_n = S + (C - S)\left[\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}\right]$ | Book value under sinking fund method |
| 7 | Declining Balance Method | $V_n = C(1 - r_d)^n$ | Accelerated depreciation (farm machinery) |
| 8 | Salvage Value (General) | $S = C \times \text{Scrap \%}$ (usually 5–10%) | End-of-life value |
| 9 | Annual Rent Capitalization | $\text{Capitalized Rent} = \text{Annual Rent} \times \text{Y.P.}$ | Rent fixation |
| 10 | Reversion Value (Sinking Fund) | $\text{R.V.} = S + \text{Sinking Fund Accumulated}$ | End-of-life recovery |
| 11 | Approximate Cost of Construction (Farm Building) | $\text{Cost} = \text{Plinth Area} \times \text{Rate per m}^2$ | Quick valuation |
| 12 | Plinth Area Estimate | $\text{Plinth Area} = \text{Length} \times \text{Breadth}$ + offsets | Valuation of farm residences |
| 13 | Percentage Depreciation for Various Items | Brickwork: 1% / yr; Masonry: 1–2% / yr; Woodwork: 2–3% / yr | Quick estimate of depreciation |

> **CRITICAL Notation for KTU Board Exams:**
> - $C$ = **Original Cost (Capital Cost)** of the structure.
> - $S$ = **Salvage Value (Scrap Value)** at the end of useful life.
> - $n$ = **Useful Life of the Structure** in years.
> - $i$ = **Rate of Interest** per annum (expressed as decimal: e.g., 8% = 0.08).
> - $D$ = **Annual Depreciation** amount (₹ per year).
> - $V_n$ = **Value of the structure after $n$ years** (Book Value).

---

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Years' Purchase (Y.P.) Concept

A **Years' Purchase (Y.P.)** is essentially a *time multiplier* that answers the question:
> *"How many years of net annual income would a prudent buyer need to receive to justify paying the full purchase price today?"*

**Example Intuition:** If the prevailing interest rate in the market is **$r = 8\% = 0.08$**, then a buyer would be willing to pay up to the amount whose *annual interest earnings* equal the *net annual rental income* of the property. Since the interest earned on ₹1 for 1 year at 8% is ₹0.08, the buyer would need to pay:
$$\text{Y.P.} = \dfrac{1}{0.08} = 12.5 \text{ years of purchase}$$

If the *net annual income* of a farm godown is ₹1,00,000, its **Capitalized Value** becomes:
$$\text{C.V.} = ₹1,00,000 \times 12.5 = ₹12,50,000$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the "rate of interest" with "rate of return on investment" or "depreciation rate". In valuation, **$r$ specifically refers to the prevailing market interest rate** for similar investments (e.g., government bonds, bank FD rates).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Agricultural Engineering

The mastery of PRICE software and Valuation is **directly applied** in:

- **NABARD-funded Farm Projects:** Subsidy calculations require accurate PRICE-based detailed estimates.
- **Bank Loan Sanctioning:** Valuation certificate is mandatory for issuing farm loans against property.
- **Crop Insurance Schemes (PMFBY):** Structure value assessment for comprehensive insurance.
- **Farm Mechanization Depreciation:** Declining Balance Method is used for tractors, harvesters.
- **Kerala PWD Farm Infrastructure:** All government farm buildings, market yards, and irrigation structures use CPWD-SoR based estimates via PRICE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Solved Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: The Straight Line Method (SLM) of Depreciation

**Statement:** Under the Straight Line Method, an equal amount of depreciation is charged every year over the *useful life* of the asset, so that the asset value reduces *linearly* from its original cost $C$ to its salvage value $S$ over $n$ years.

**Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:**

Let $C$ = Original cost, $S$ = Salvage value, $n$ = Useful life (years), $D$ = Annual depreciation.

**Step 1:** Total depreciation over the entire life of the asset is the difference between original cost and salvage value.
$$\text{Total Depreciation} = C - S$$

**Step 2:** Under the Straight Line Method, this total depreciation is distributed *equally* over the $n$ years of useful life.
$$D = \dfrac{\text{Total Depreciation}}{\text{Number of Years}}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the value of total depreciation:
$$D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$$

**Step 4 (Book Value at the end of year $n$):** The book value $V_n$ is the original cost minus the depreciation accumulated over $n$ years.
$$V_n = C - n \times D$$

**Step 5 (Substituting $D$):**
$$V_n = C - n \times \dfrac{C - S}{n}$$

**Step 6 (Simplification):**
$$V_n = C - (C - S)$$
$$V_n = S$$

This is the *consistency check* — the book value at the end of $n$ years equals the salvage value, confirming the derivation is correct. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Sinking Fund Method of Depreciation

**Statement:** Under this method, the owner *sets aside* a fixed amount every year at compound interest so that the accumulated sinking fund at the end of $n$ years equals $(C - S)$. The annual deposit is the depreciation charge.

**Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The required sinking fund at the end of $n$ years must equal the *total depreciation amount*:
$$\text{Required Sinking Fund} = C - S$$

**Step 2:** The standard compound-interest formula for the *future value of a uniform series* of annual deposits $D$ at interest rate $i$ for $n$ years is:
$$F = D \times \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

**Step 3:** Equating the future value of the annuity to the required sinking fund:
$$D \times \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} = C - S$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $D$ (annual depreciation):
$$D = (C - S) \times \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

**Step 5 (Book Value after $n$ years — Sinking Fund Method):**
The book value under sinking fund method has **two components**:
- The **original structure** with diminishing book value.
- The **accumulated sinking fund** (which grows over time).

$$V_n = C - (C - S)\left[\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board:** The book value under the **Sinking Fund Method is ALWAYS LESS than** the Straight Line Method book value. This is because in SLM, the depreciation amount itself loses interest-earning potential, whereas in SFM, the *depreciation fund* continues to earn compound interest.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: The Declining Balance Method (DBM)

**Statement:** Under DBM, depreciation is charged at a *fixed percentage* of the *book value* at the beginning of each year, leading to accelerated depreciation in early years.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let $r_d$ = fixed depreciation rate (% per year of book value).

**Step 1:** Depreciation in Year 1 = $r_d \times C$.
Book value at end of Year 1: $V_1 = C - r_d C = C(1 - r_d)$.

**Step 2:** Depreciation in Year 2 = $r_d \times V_1 = r_d \times C(1 - r_d)$.
Book value at end of Year 2: $V_2 = V_1(1 - r_d) = C(1 - r_d)^2$.

**Step 3:** By induction, after $n$ years:
$$V_n = C(1 - r_d)^n$$

---

### 3.4 Detailed Solved Example 1: Straight Line Method (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A farm godown was constructed at a cost of **₹ 8,00,000**. Its estimated useful life is **40 years**, and the expected salvage value is **₹ 1,20,000**. Calculate:
1. The annual depreciation using Straight Line Method.
2. The book value at the end of 15 years.
3. The book value at the end of 30 years.

**Given Data:**
- $C = ₹ 8,00,000$
- $S = ₹ 1,20,000$
- $n = 40$ years

**Solution:**

**Part 1: Annual Depreciation**

The total depreciation to be recovered over 40 years:
$$\text{Total Depreciation} = C - S = ₹8,00,000 - ₹1,20,000 = ₹6,80,000$$

Using the SLM formula:
$$D = \dfrac{C - S}{n} = \dfrac{₹6,80,000}{40} = ₹17,000 \text{ per year}$$

**Part 2: Book Value after 15 years**

Depreciation accumulated in 15 years:
$$D_{15} = 15 \times ₹17,000 = ₹2,55,000$$

Book value at the end of 15 years:
$$V_{15} = C - D_{15} = ₹8,00,000 - ₹2,55,000 = ₹5,45,000$$

**Part 3: Book Value after 30 years**

Depreciation accumulated in 30 years:
$$D_{30} = 30 \times ₹17,000 = ₹5,10,000$$

Book value at the end of 30 years:
$$V_{30} = ₹8,00,000 - ₹5,10,000 = ₹2,90,000$$

**Verification Check:** At the end of 40 years, $V_{40} = ₹8,00,000 - 40 \times 17,000 = ₹8,00,000 - ₹6,80,000 = ₹1,20,000 = S$ ✓

---

### 3.5 Detailed Solved Example 2: Sinking Fund Method (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A poultry house was built at a cost of **₹ 12,00,000**. The scrap value after **30 years** is **₹ 2,00,000**. Assuming the rate of interest is **6% per annum**, determine the annual sinking fund depreciation and the book value at the end of **20 years**.

**Given Data:**
- $C = ₹12,00,000$
- $S = ₹2,00,000$
- $n = 30$ years
- $i = 0.06$

**Solution:**

**Part 1: Annual Sinking Fund Depreciation**

Total depreciation to be recovered:
$$C - S = ₹12,00,000 - ₹2,00,000 = ₹10,00,000$$

Applying the sinking fund formula:
$$D = (C - S) \times \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

Computing $(1+i)^n$:
$$(1.06)^{30} \approx 5.7435$$

Therefore:
$$(1+i)^n - 1 = 5.7435 - 1 = 4.7435$$

Substituting:
$$D = ₹10,00,000 \times \dfrac{0.06}{4.7435}$$

$$D = ₹10,00,000 \times 0.012649 \approx ₹12,649 \text{ per year}$$

**Part 2: Book Value at the end of 20 years**

Using the sinking fund book value formula:
$$V_{20} = C - (C - S)\left[\dfrac{(1+i)^{20} - 1}{i(1+i)^{20}}\right]$$

Computing $(1.06)^{20}$:
$$(1.06)^{20} \approx 3.2071$$

Therefore:
$$(1.06)^{20} - 1 = 3.2071 - 1 = 2.2071$$
$$i(1+i)^{20} = 0.06 \times 3.2071 = 0.19243$$

The sinking fund factor:
$$\dfrac{(1.06)^{20} - 1}{0.06 \times (1.06)^{20}} = \dfrac{2.2071}{0.19243} \approx 11.4695$$

Substituting:
$$V_{20} = ₹12,00,000 - ₹10,00,000 \times 0.5469$$
$$V_{20} = ₹12,00,000 - ₹5,46,900 \approx ₹6,53,100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** The same poultry house under SLM would have $D = ₹10,00,000/30 = ₹33,333$/year, and book value at 20 years would be $₹12,00,000 - 20 \times 33,333 = ₹5,33,340$. The Sinking Fund book value (₹6,53,100) is **higher** than SLM book value (₹5,33,340) — this is the expected behaviour.

---

### 3.6 Detailed Solved Example 3: Capitalized Value of a Farm Building (Y.P. Concept)

**Problem Statement:**
A commercial dairy farm shed is expected to yield a *net annual rental income* of **₹ 60,000**. The prevailing rate of interest is **8% per annum**. Determine the capitalized value of the shed.

**Given Data:**
- Net Annual Income = ₹60,000
- $r = 0.08$

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the Years' Purchase.
$$\text{Y.P.} = \dfrac{1}{r} = \dfrac{1}{0.08} = 12.5 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the Capitalized Value.
$$\text{C.V.} = \text{Net Annual Income} \times \text{Y.P.}$$
$$\text{C.V.} = ₹60,000 \times 12.5 = ₹7,50,000$$

**Conclusion:** The capitalized (fair) value of the dairy shed is **₹7,50,000**.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Method (KTU Board Reference)

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Farm houses, godowns, durable masonry structures | **Straight Line Method** | Even wear and tear, long life (50–100 yrs) |
| Machinery, tractors, pumpsets | **Declining Balance Method** | High obsolescence; accelerated depreciation needed |
| Long-life commercial farm buildings with investment mindset | **Sinking Fund Method** | Owner sets aside yearly deposit earning interest |
| Rental valuation of a farm property | **Net Income Method (Y.P.)** | Direct conversion of income to capital value |
| Unique farm structure (specialized greenhouse) | **Cost Method** | No market comparable, build-up from materials |
| Sale of farm property | **Comparable Sales Method** | Recent market transactions in same area |
