The examiner often tests whether the student knows which factor is included vs. excluded. ## 2.3 Distinguishing Market Value from Related Valuation Concepts | Concept | Definition | When Used | |---|---|---| | **Market Value** | Estimated open-market transaction price | Sale, purchase, taxation | | **Book Value** | Original cost minus accumulated depreciation (from books of accounts) | Financial reporting | | **Replacement Value** | Cost to replace the asset with a new one of similar utility | Insurance | | **Salvage Value** | Realisable scrap value at the end of useful life | Disposal planning | | **Assessed Value** | Value determined by a government authority for tax purposes | Property tax | | **Sentimental Value** | Subjective emotional worth to owner | Excluded in market value | | **Distress Value** | Sale price under forced/urgent conditions | Liquidation | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table summarises every formula you need for solving Market Value problems in KTU 2024 ESE.** | # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $MV = \dfrac{\sum V_i \cdot W_i}{\sum W_i}$ | Weighted Market Value (weighted comparison method) — $V_i$ = comparable sale value, $W_i$ = weight | ₹ | | 2 | $MV = \dfrac{NI}{r}$ | Income Capitalization — $NI$ = annual net income, $r$ = capitalization rate | ₹ | | 3 | $MV = RC - D$ | Cost Approach — $RC$ = replacement cost, $D$ = accrued depreciation | ₹ | | 4 | $D = \dfrac{RC \cdot n}{N}$ | Straight-line depreciation — $n$ = age, $N$ = total useful life | ₹ | | 5 | $D = RC \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{SV}{RC}\right)^{\dfrac{n}{N}}$ | Declining balance depreciation — $SV$ = salvage value | ₹ | | 6 | $MV_A = MV_B \times \dfrac{A_{size}}{B_{size}} \times \alpha$ | Size adjustment — $\alpha$ = locational adjustment factor | ₹ | | 7 | $MV = \dfrac{NI \cdot (1 + g)}{r - g}$ | Gordon's Growth Model — $g$ = growth rate (long-run) | ₹ | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Market Value determination is essential in: - **Bank loan sanction** against farm land or structure as collateral. - **Crop insurance** and **disaster relief compensation** claims. - **Compensation awards** during land acquisition (e.g., NH widening). - **Estate settlement, succession planning**, and partition disputes. - **Farm business takeovers** and **lease valuation**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Weighted Comparative Sales Method (14-Mark Standard) ### Problem Statement A valuer is estimating the **Market Value of a 1.2-hectare coconut farm in Thrissur district** with a 600 sq. ft. RCC-roofed storage godown. Four recent comparable sales in the same village have been identified: | Comp Sale | Total Area (ha) | Sale Price (₹ Lakhs) | Has Godown? | Distance from Main Road | Days Since Sale | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Comp A | 1.0 | 38 | Yes (500 ft²) | 200 m | 60 | | Comp B | 1.5 | 52 | No | 100 m | 30 | | Comp C | 0.8 | 30 | Yes (400 ft²) | 400 m | 90 | | Comp D | 1.3 | 45 | No | 150 m | 45 | Assume: - **Size adjustment factor** (per hectare): ₹40 lakhs baseline. - **Godown premium**: ₹2 lakhs for a 500 ft² RCC godown (pro-rata for different sizes). - **Locational adjustment**: 0.5% deduction per extra 50 m distance from main road beyond 200 m. - **Time adjustment**: 0.25% appreciation per month since sale. - **Weights** (based on similarity to subject): A = 0.35, B = 0.20, C = 0.30, D = 0.15. Subject property: 1.2 ha + 600 ft² godown, located 250 m from main road. Date of valuation: 90 days after the most recent comp sale. ### Step 1: Normalize Each Comparable to Subject Property **Comp A:** - Size: 1.0 ha → needs adjustment to 1.2 ha. - Godown: Has 500 ft² vs subject's 600 ft². - Distance: 200 m vs subject's 250 m (50 m less → no deduction, in fact slightly better). - Time: 60 days before valuation. **Comp B:** - Size: 1.5 ha → needs adjustment down to 1.2 ha. - Godown: NONE → needs addition of godown value. - Distance: 100 m (better than subject's 250 m → no deduction). - Time: 30 days before valuation. **Comp C:** - Size: 0.8 ha → needs adjustment up to 1.2 ha. - Godown: 400 ft² (less than subject's 600 ft² → need to ADD value). - Distance: 400 m (150 m more than subject's 250 m → 3 × 0.5% deduction). - Time: 90 days before valuation (no appreciation adjustment needed). **Comp D:** - Size: 1.3 ha → needs adjustment down to 1.2 ha. - Godown: NONE → needs addition of godown value. - Distance: 150 m (better than subject's 250 m). - Time: 45 days before valuation. ### Step 2: Adjust Sale Prices **Comp A Adjusted Price:** Size adjustment: Subject area / Comp area = $\dfrac{1.2}{1.0} = 1.20$ $$ P_A^{adj} = 38{,}00{,}000 \times 1.20 = 45{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ Godown adjustment: Subject godown (600 ft²) − Comp godown (500 ft²) = 100 ft² extra. $$ \text{Godown premium} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{100}{500} = 40{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ Locational adjustment: Comp is 50 m *closer* than subject → no deduction; in fact subject location is slightly worse. $$ \text{Loc factor} = 1.00 - (1 \times 0.005) = 0.995 $$ $$ \text{Loc adjustment} = 45{,}60{,}000 \times (0.995 - 1.00) = -22{,}800 \text{ ₹} $$ Time adjustment: 60 days = 2 months. 0.25% per month. $$ \text{Time factor} = 1 + (2 \times 0.0025) = 1.005 $$ $$ \text{Time adjustment} = 45{,}60{,}000 \times 0.005 = 22{,}800 \text{ ₹} $$ $$ P_A^{final} = 45{,}60{,}000 + 40{,}000 - 22{,}800 + 22{,}800 = 45{,}60{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 46{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ **Comp B Adjusted Price:** Size adjustment: $\dfrac{1.2}{1.5} = 0.80$ $$ P_B^{adj} = 52{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = 41{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ Godown addition: Subject has 600 ft² → add full ₹2,00,000 (since comp has zero). $$ \text{Godown premium} = +2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ Locational: Comp is 150 m closer → apply a *worse* factor for subject, i.e., comp price is inflated. To compare like-with-like, we deduct a locational premium from comp. $$ \text{Loc factor} = 1.00 - (3 \times 0.005) = 0.985 $$ But since comp is *better* located, comp price is relatively higher. To derive *subject* value, we keep the comp price but note subject is worse located → reduce by the locational premium for the difference (150 m → 3 increments). $$ \text{Loc adjustment} = 41{,}60{,}000 \times (1.00 - 0.985) = +62{,}400 \text{ ₹ (deduction from comp price when equating to subject)} $$ Wait — careful. Re-state logic: Comp B is better located. We want to know what *subject* (worse location) would sell for. So we deduct a locational penalty from comp B's normalized price. $$ P_B^{loc-adj} = 41{,}60{,}000 \times (1 - 0.015) = 41{,}60{,}000 - 62{,}400 = 40{,}97{,}600 \text{ ₹} $$ Time: 30 days = 1 month. 0.25% appreciation. $$ \text{Time factor} = 1 + (1 \times 0.0025) = 1.0025 $$ $$ \text{Time adjustment} = 41{,}60{,}000 \times 0.0025 = 10{,}400 \text{ ₹} $$ $$ P_B^{final} = 40{,}97{,}600 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}400 = 43{,}08{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ **Comp C Adjusted Price:** Size adjustment: $\dfrac{1.2}{0.8} = 1.50$ $$ P_C^{adj} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 1.50 = 45{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ Godown: Comp has 400 ft² vs subject's 600 ft² → need to ADD value for 200 ft² shortfall. $$ \text{Godown premium} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{200}{500} = 80{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ Locational: Comp is 150 m *farther* from road (400 m vs 250 m) → comp is worse located → subject would command a premium. To get subject price, ADD a locational premium for 150 m (3 increments × 0.5%). $$ \text{Loc adjustment} = 45{,}00{,}000 \times (3 \times 0.005) = +67{,}500 \text{ ₹} $$ Time: 90 days = 3 months. 0.25% per month. $$ \text{Time factor} = 1 + (3 \times 0.0025) = 1.0075 $$ $$ \text{Time adjustment} = 45{,}00{,}000 \times 0.0075 = 33{,}750 \text{ ₹} $$ $$ P_C^{final} = 45{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 67{,}500 + 33{,}750 = 46{,}81{,}250 \text{ ₹} $$ **Comp D Adjusted Price:** Size adjustment: $\dfrac{1.2}{1.3} = 0.9231$ $$ P_D^{adj} = 45{,}00{,}000 \times 0.9231 = 41{,}53{,}846 \text{ ₹} $$ Godown: Comp has none → ADD ₹2,00,000. $$ \text{Godown premium} = +2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ Locational: Comp is 100 m closer → subject is worse located → deduct loc premium. $$ \text{Loc adjustment} = 41{,}53{,}846 \times (2 \times 0.005) = -41{,}538 \text{ ₹} $$ Time: 45 days = 1.5 months. 0.25% per month. $$ \text{Time factor} = 1 + (1.5 \times 0.0025) = 1.00375 $$ $$ \text{Time adjustment} = 41{,}53{,}846 \times 0.00375 = 15{,}577 \text{ ₹} $$ $$ P_D^{final} = 41{,}53{,}846 + 2{,}00{,}000 - 41{,}538 + 15{,}577 = 43{,}27{,}885 \text{ ₹} $$ ### Step 3: Compute Weighted Market Value $$ MV = \dfrac{\sum V_i \cdot W_i}{\sum W_i} $$ $$ MV = \dfrac{(46{,}00{,}000)(0.35) + (43{,}08{,}000)(0.20) + (46{,}81{,}250)(0.30) + (43{,}27{,}885)(0.15)}{0.35 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.15} $$ Compute each weighted term: $$ T_A = 46{,}00{,}000 \times 0.35 = 16{,}10{,}000 $$ $$ T_B = 43{,}08{,}000 \times 0.20 = 8{,}61{,}600 $$ $$ T_C = 46{,}81{,}250 \times 0.30 = 14{,}04{,}375 $$ $$ T_D = 43{,}27{,}885 \times 0.15 = 6{,}49{,}183 $$ Sum: $$ \sum = 16{,}10{,}000 + 8{,}61{,}600 + 14{,}04{,}375 + 6{,}49{,}183 = 45{,}25{,}158 $$ Weights sum = 1.00. $$ \boxed{MV \approx \text{₹ } 45{,}25{,}158 \approx \text{₹ } 45.25 \text{ Lakhs}} $$ ### Interpretation The **Market Value of the 1.2-hectare coconut farm with 600 sq. ft. RCC godown is approximately ₹ 45.25 Lakhs** as on the valuation date, based on the weighted comparative sales approach. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Income Capitalization Approach ### Problem Statement A dairy farm shed has an annual net operating income (NOI) of **₹ 5,00,000**. The prevailing capitalization rate in the locality is **8% per annum**. Determine the Market Value. ### Solution $$ MV = \dfrac{NI}{r} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.08} $$ $$ \boxed{MV = \text{₹ } 62{,}50{,}000} $$ If a growth rate $g = 2\%$ per annum is expected (Gordon's Growth Model): $$ MV = \dfrac{NI \cdot (1 + g)}{r - g} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 1.02}{0.08 - 0.02} $$ $$ MV = \dfrac{5{,}10{,}000}{0.06} = \text{₹ } 85{,}00{,}000 $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Cost Approach ### Problem Statement A poultry house with a current replacement cost of **₹ 25,00,000** has been in service for **5 years** out of a total useful life of **20 years**. Salvage value at end of life is **₹ 2,00,000**. Find Market Value using straight-line and declining balance methods. ### Straight-Line Depreciation $$ D = \dfrac{RC \cdot n}{N} = \dfrac{25{,}00{,}000 \times 5}{20} = 6{,}25{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ $$ MV_{SL} = RC - D = 25{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}25{,}000 = \text{₹ } 18{,}75{,}000 $$ ### Declining Balance Depreciation $$ D = RC \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{SV}{RC}\right)^{n/N}\right] = 25{,}00{,}000 \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000}\right)^{5/20}\right] $$ $$ D = 25{,}00{,}000 \cdot \left[1 - (0.08)^{0.25}\right] $$ $$ (0.08)^{0.25} = \sqrt[4]{0.08} \approx 0.5313 $$ $$ D = 25{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1 - 0.5313) = 25{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.4687 \approx 11{,}71{,}750 \text{ ₹} $$ $$ MV_{DB} = 25{,}00{,}000 - 11{,}71{,}750 = \text{₹ } 13{,}28{,}250 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Tip:** Always present both depreciation methods and explain which one is preferred for the specific farm structure type. The straight-line method is most commonly accepted for farm buildings in KTU valuation problems. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Factors Influencing Market Value — Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Market Value
of Farm Asset"] --> B["Physical
Factors"] A --> C["Locational and
Infrastructure"] A --> D["Economic and
Legal Factors"] A --> E["Time and
Market Forces"] A --> F["Intangible
Factors"] B --> B1["Land size
and shape"] B --> B2["Soil type
and fertility"] B --> B3["Age and
condition of
structure"] B --> B4["Water
availability"] C --> C1["Distance
to market"] C --> C2["Road and
electricity"] C --> C3["Irrigation

proximity"] D --> D1["Supply demand
balance"] D --> D2["Government
subsidies and MSP"] D --> D3["Title clarity
and zoning"] D --> D4["Interest

sale recency"] F --> F1["Emotional
attachment"] F --> F2["Cultural or
religious value"] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Three Approaches to Determine Market Value — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR SUB["Subject Farm
Asset
Land and Structures"] --> MVA["Market Value

Comparative Sales

Income

Cost

comparable sales"] A1A --> A1B["Apply size
adjustment"] A1B --> A1C["Apply godown
adjustment"] A1C --> A1D["Apply location
adjustment"] A1D --> A1E["Apply time

Growth if applicable"] A2C --> A2D["MV equals NI
divided by r"] A3 --> A3A["Estimate

Loan sanction

Compensation"] style SUB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style MVA fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF style A1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style A2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style A3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style OUT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 style APP fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` ## 4.3 Market Value vs Other Valuation Concepts — Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Purpose of
Valuation?"] --> Q1{"Need open market
selling price?"} Q1 -- YES --> MV["MARKET VALUE
Most probable sale price
in open market"] Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Need for
financial reporting?"} Q2 -- YES --> BV["BOOK VALUE
Original cost minus
accumulated depreciation"] Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"Need for
insurance purposes?"} Q3 -- YES --> RV["REPLACEMENT VALUE
Cost to rebuild
new similar asset"] Q3 -- NO --> Q4{"Asset at end
of useful life?"} Q4 -- YES --> SV["SALVAGE VALUE
Scrap or resale
value on disposal"] Q4 -- NO --> Q5{"Government
tax purpose?"} Q5 -- YES --> AV["ASSESSED VALUE
Government determined

sale condition?"} Q6 -- YES --> DV["DISTRESS VALUE
Below-market
forced sale price"] Q6 -- NO --> Q7{"Owner
attachment?"} Q7 -- YES --> SE["SENTIMENTAL VALUE
Excluded from
Market Value"] style START fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style MV fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF style BV fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style RV fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style SV fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style AV fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style DV fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF style SE fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define Market Value. List any four characteristics.** **Model Answer:** Market Value is the estimated amount in money for which a property or asset would exchange hands on the date of valuation, between a willing buyer and a willing seller, both acting knowledgeably, prudently, and without compulsion, in an open and competitive market. **Four characteristics:** 1. **Open Market Condition:** The transaction occurs in a public, competitive marketplace with no undue restrictions. 2. **Willing Parties:** Neither buyer nor seller is under compulsion or duress. 3. **Knowledgeable Parties:** Both parties are reasonably informed about the asset's condition, utility, and legal status. 4. **Time-Specific:** The value is fixed at a particular date and changes with market conditions. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Distinguish between Market Value and Book Value.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Market Value | Book Value | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Estimated open-market sale price | Original cost minus accumulated depreciation per books | | **Basis** | Current market conditions | Historical accounting records | | **Volatility** | Changes frequently with market | Relatively stable; updates yearly | | **Use** | Sale, purchase, taxation, loans | Financial reporting, balance sheet | | **Calculation** | Based on comparable sales / income / cost | Based on accounting formula | --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] **(a) Explain the three classical approaches used to determine the Market Value of a farm property. (7 marks)** **(b) A 2-hectare rubber plantation with a 400 sq. ft. GI-sheet-roofed storage shed has the following four comparable sales in the same district. Compute the weighted Market Value using the comparative sales approach. (7 marks)** | Comp | Area (ha) | Sale Price (₹ Lakhs) | Shed (ft²) | Distance from Road | Months Since Sale | Weight | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | X | 1.5 | 60 | 350 | 100 m | 4 | 0.25 | | Y | 2.5 | 85 | 500 | 250 m | 2 | 0.30 | | Z | 1.8 | 70 | 400 | 150 m | 6 | 0.25 | | W | 2.0 | 75 | 450 | 200 m | 3 | 0.20 | Subject: 2.0 ha, 400 ft² shed, 200 m from road. Shed premium = ₹1.5 lakhs for 500 ft². Locational adjustment = 0.5% per 50 m deviation. Time appreciation = 0.5% per month. --- ### Model Answer (Question A) #### Part (a) — Three Approaches (7 marks) **1. Comparative Sales Approach (Market Data Method):** - The subject property is compared with recently sold similar properties (comparables) in the same locality. - Adjustments are made for differences in size, age, condition, location, time, and amenities. - This is the most direct and preferred approach when sufficient reliable comparable data exists. - Particularly useful for agricultural land and standard farm buildings. **2. Income Capitalization Approach:** - Market Value is estimated as the present worth of the future net income the asset is expected to generate. - Formula: $MV = \dfrac{NI}{r}$ where $NI$ = annual net operating income, $r$ = capitalization rate. - For growing assets: $MV = \dfrac{NI \cdot (1 + g)}{r - g}$ (Gordon's Model). - Suitable for income-generating structures like dairy farms, poultry houses, orchards, commercial farm buildings. **3. Cost Approach (Replacement Cost Less Depreciation):** - Estimate current cost to construct/replace the structure anew (Replacement Cost, RC). - Subtract accrued depreciation due to age, wear, and obsolescence. - Formula: $MV = RC - D$. - Best for unique farm structures (custom greenhouses, specialized silos) where no comparables are available. #### Part (b) — Weighted Market Value Computation (7 marks) **Step 1: Adjust Each Comparable** Subject: 2.0 ha, 400 ft² shed, 200 m, today (0 months since valuation). **Comp X:** Area ratio = $\dfrac{2.0}{1.5} = 1.333$ $$ P_X^{adj} = 60{,}00{,}000 \times 1.333 = 80{,}00{,}000 $$ Shed adjustment: Subject 400 ft² vs Comp 350 ft² → comp is 50 ft² less → ADD $\dfrac{50}{500} \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 15{,}000$. Locational: Comp is 100 m closer (100 m less than subject's 200 m) → subject is worse → comp price inflated relative to subject. To get subject price, deduct: $80{,}00{,}000 \times (2 \times 0.005) = 80{,}000$. Time: 4 months appreciation since sale → ADD: $80{,}00{,}000 \times (4 \times 0.005) = 1{,}60{,}000$. $$ P_X^{final} = 80{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}000 - 80{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000 = 80{,}95{,}000 $$ **Comp Y:** Area ratio = $\dfrac{2.0}{2.5} = 0.80$ $$ P_Y^{adj} = 85{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = 68{,}00{,}000 $$ Shed: Comp has 500 ft², subject has 400 ft² → comp is 100 ft² more → DEDUCT $\dfrac{100}{500} \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 30{,}000$. Locational: Comp is 50 m farther (250 m vs 200 m) → subject is better → comp price deflated relative to subject. To get subject price, ADD: $68{,}00{,}000 \times (1 \times 0.005) = 34{,}000$. Time: 2 months → ADD: $68{,}00{,}000 \times (2 \times 0.005) = 68{,}000$. $$ P_Y^{final} = 68{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}000 + 34{,}000 + 68{,}000 = 68{,}72{,}000 $$ **Comp Z:** Area ratio = $\dfrac{2.0}{1.8} = 1.111$ $$ P_Z^{adj} = 70{,}00{,}000 \times 1.111 = 77{,}77{,}778 $$ Shed: Both 400 ft² → no adjustment. Locational: Comp is 50 m closer (150 m vs 200 m) → comp better → deduct: $77{,}77{,}778 \times (1 \times 0.005) = 38{,}889$. Time: 6 months → ADD: $77{,}77{,}778 \times (6 \times 0.005) = 2{,}33{,}333$. $$ P_Z^{final} = 77{,}77{,}778 - 38{,}889 + 2{,}33{,}333 = 79{,}72{,}222 $$ **Comp W:** Area ratio = $\dfrac{2.0}{2.0} = 1.00$ $$ P_W^{adj} = 75{,}00{,}000 $$ Shed: Comp has 450 ft², subject 400 ft² → comp is 50 ft² more → DEDUCT $15{,}000$. Locational: Same distance (200 m) → no adjustment. Time: 3 months → ADD: $75{,}00{,}000 \times (3 \times 0.005) = 1{,}12{,}500$. $$ P_W^{final} = 75{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}000 + 1{,}12{,}500 = 75{,}97{,}500 $$ **Step 2: Weighted Market Value** $$ MV = \dfrac{(80{,}95{,}000)(0.25) + (68{,}72{,}000)(0.30) + (79{,}72{,}222)(0.25) + (75{,}97{,}500)(0.20)}{0.25 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20} $$ Compute weighted terms: $$ T_X = 80{,}95{,}000 \times 0.25 = 20{,}23{,}750 $$ $$ T_Y = 68{,}72{,}000 \times 0.30 = 20{,}61{,}600 $$ $$ T_Z = 79{,}72{,}222 \times 0.25 = 19{,}93{,}056 $$ $$ T_W = 75{,}97{,}500 \times 0.20 = 15{,}19{,}500 $$ $$ \sum = 20{,}23{,}750 + 20{,}61{,}600 + 19{,}93{,}056 + 15{,}19{,}500 = 75{,}97{,}906 $$ Weights sum = 1.00. $$ \boxed{MV \approx \text{₹ } 75{,}97{,}906 \approx \text{₹ } 75.98 \text{ Lakhs}} $$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **(a) Discuss the various physical, locational, economic, and intangible factors affecting the Market Value of a farm structure. (7 marks)** **(b) A tractor-operated combined harvester has the following data: Original cost = ₹22,00,000; Salvage value = ₹3,00,000; Useful life = 12 years; Age = 5 years. Compute the Market Value using (i) straight-line and (ii) declining balance methods of depreciation. (7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer (Question B) #### Part (a) — Factors Affecting Market Value (7 marks) **A. Physical Factors:** - **Size and shape** of the land parcel — regular shapes fetch higher market value. - **Soil type, fertility, drainage** — directly affects agricultural productivity. - **Age and condition** of buildings — older, dilapidated structures reduce MV. - **Quality of construction** — RCC, brick, GI sheet, etc., influence replacement cost. - **Water availability** — borewell, canal, pond, or rain-fed. - **Topography** — flat vs undulating land. **B. Locational and Infrastructural Factors:** - **Distance from market centres** — proximity increases MV. - **Road and transport access** — farm roads, paved highways. - **Electricity and irrigation network**. - **Distance from urban/semi-urban zones** — urban fringe areas command premium. - **Neighbourhood** — presence of agricultural research stations, FPOs, or processing units. **C. Economic and Legal Factors:** - **Prevailing supply-demand balance** for similar farm properties. - **Government policies** — MSP, subsidies, crop insurance, PM-KISAN. - **Land title clarity** — clear titles fetch premium; encumbrances reduce MV. - **Zoning and land-use regulations**. - **Prevailing interest rates** and credit availability. - **Inflation and general price levels**. **D. Intangible Factors (Generally Excluded in Strict Market Value):** - **Emotional attachment** of the owner. - **Cultural/religious significance**. - **Heritage value** of the farm. - These are excluded from Market Value but may influence **Sentimental Value** or **Insurance Value**. #### Part (b) — Depreciation-Based Market Value Computation (7 marks) **Given:** - $RC = \text{₹ } 22{,}00{,}000$ - $SV = \text{₹ } 3{,}00{,}000$ - $N = 12$ years (total useful life) - $n = 5$ years (age) **(i) Straight-Line Method:** Annual depreciation = $\dfrac{RC - SV}{N} = \dfrac{22{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{12} = \dfrac{19{,}00{,}000}{12} = 1{,}58{,}333 \text{ ₹/year}$ Total depreciation for 5 years: $$ D = 1{,}58{,}333 \times 5 = 7{,}91{,}667 \text{ ₹} $$ $$ MV_{SL} = RC - D = 22{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}91{,}667 = \text{₹ } 14{,}08{,}333 $$ **(ii) Declining Balance Method:** $$ D = RC \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{SV}{RC}\right)^{n/N}\right] $$ $$ D = 22{,}00{,}000 \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000}\right)^{5/12}\right] $$ $$ = 22{,}00{,}000 \cdot \left[1 - (0.1364)^{0.4167}\right] $$ Compute $(0.1364)^{0.4167}$: $$ \ln(0.1364) = -1.992 $$ $$ -1.992 \times 0.4167 = -0.830 $$ $$ e^{-0.830} = 0.4365 $$ $$ D = 22{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1 - 0.4365) = 22{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.5635 = 12{,}39{,}700 \text{ ₹} $$ $$ MV_{DB} = 22{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}39{,}700 = \text{₹ } 9{,}60{,}300 $$ **Comparison:** | Method | Depreciation (₹) | Market Value (₹) | |---|---|---| | Straight-Line | 7,91,667 | **14,08,333** | | Declining Balance | 12,39,700 | 9,60,300 | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Comment:** Straight-line method is generally preferred for valuation of farm buildings and machinery in KTU valuation problems because it provides stable, predictable depreciation. Declining balance is used when asset utility drops sharply in early years. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to apply time appreciation when comparable sales are old. Market value reflects *today's* price — always adjust comp sale price upward if it occurred months/years earlier in an appreciating market. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing direction of locational adjustment. If the comp is *better* located than the subject, the comp price is inflated relative to subject → deduct locational premium to estimate *subject* price, and vice versa. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Using absolute values (`|x|`) inside markdown table cells — the rendering engine breaks. Always use `\vert x \vert` or simply write "Absolute Value of x". > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing Market Value with Book Value. Book Value uses historical cost minus accumulated accounting depreciation; Market Value uses current market data. They rarely match. > 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Including sentimental value in Market Value. By definition, Market Value excludes sentimental considerations. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Market Value = Most probable open-market price** between informed, willing parties on a specific date. - **Three approaches:** Comparative Sales, Income Capitalization, Cost Approach. - **Weighted MV formula:** $MV = \dfrac{\sum V_i \cdot W_i}{\sum W_i}$ — weights based on similarity to subject. - **Comparative adjustments** must be made for: **size, age/condition, location, amenities (e.g., godown), and time**. - **Income approach formula:** $MV = \dfrac{NI}{r}$ or $MV = \dfrac{NI(1+g)}{r-g}$ (Gordon's Model). - **Cost approach formula:** $MV = RC - D$, where $D$ can be straight-line or declining balance. - **Straight-line depreciation:** $D = \dfrac{(RC - SV) \cdot n}{N}$ — most common in KTU problems. - **Declining balance depreciation:** $D = RC \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{SV}{RC}\right)^{n/N}\right]$ — heavier early depreciation. - **Sentimental value is EXCLUDED** from Market Value. - **Distress value** (forced sale) is **LOWER than** Market Value. - **Replacement/Insurance value** is typically **HIGHER than** Market Value. - **Assessed value** (government) may differ from Market Value based on local guidelines. - **Market Value is date-specific** — always mention the valuation date in answers. - **Always show step-by-step adjustment tables** in KTU 14-mark answers to earn full marks. - **Use proper units**: ₹ for money, m² or ft² for area, ha or acre for land. - **Final MV should be rounded** to a sensible decimal (lakhs or thousands) and clearly boxed.

topics.title: Fair Rent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fair Rent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Fair Rent

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FAIR RENT (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> **Fair Rent** is defined as the *reasonable and equitable amount of rent* that a tenant-farmer is justified to pay to a land-owner for the use of agricultural land and farm structures, calculated based on the **productive capacity of the land**, **cost of cultivation**, **prevailing local wage rates**, and **standard yield indices**, ensuring a *fair return to the owner* and a *reasonable livelihood to the cultivator*.

In the context of the KTU 2024 B.Tech Agricultural Engineering syllabus (PCAGT501), Fair Rent falls under the broader domain of **Farm Cost Economics, Valuation, and Tenancy Legislation**. It is a regulated economic concept that prevents exploitation of either party in the landlord-tenant relationship.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Co-operative Mango Orchard"**
> Imagine two brothers — *Ramesh* (the landowner) and *Suresh* (the farmer). Ramesh owns a 2-acre mango orchard but cannot tend it. Suresh wants to lease it for cultivation. If Suresh offers too little, Ramesh loses. If Ramesh demands too much, Suresh cannot survive. A neutral agricultural officer steps in and calculates the **fair rent** based on:
> - What the land actually produces (yield)
> - What it costs to cultivate (inputs + labour)
> - The local market rates
> - The owner must recover his investment
> - The farmer must retain at least 50% of net produce
>
> The resulting figure — neither too high nor too low — is the **Fair Rent**. It is the *goldilocks value* that balances equity and economic justice.

### 1.3 Distinguishing Fair Rent from Other Rents

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction** — Examiners frequently test this:

| Rent Type | Definition | Decided By |
|---|---|---|
| **Fair Rent** | Reasonable rent based on land productivity & costs | Economic principles / Legislation |
| **Contract Rent** | Rent mutually agreed upon by owner and tenant | Private agreement |
| **Market Rent** | Rent prevailing in the open rental market | Supply-demand forces |
| **Statutory Rent** | Rent fixed by government / court order | Legal authority |
| **Economic Rent** | Surplus over transfer earnings (in pure economics) | Marginal productivity theory |

### 1.4 Physical & Economic Constants Used

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Benchmark Values** (commonly referenced in KTU valuation problems):
> - **Cultivator's share**: typically **1/2 to 3/5** of gross produce
> - **Landlord's share**: typically **1/3 to 1/2** of gross produce
> - **Standard interest rate on land value**: **6% to 10%** per annum
> - **Risk & insurance allowance**: **2% to 5%** of land value
> - **Depreciation rate** (for structures): **2% to 5%** per annum
> - **Maintenance allowance**: **5% to 10%** of gross rent

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles Governing Fair Rent Determination

The concept of Fair Rent is rooted in the following core economic principles:

**A. Principle of Equity**
- Both the landowner and tenant should receive a just share of the farm income.
- Typically, the tenant is entitled to retain a portion sufficient for his livelihood and labour wages.
- The landowner is entitled to a return commensurate with his capital investment.

**B. Principle of Productivity**
- Rent must be linked to the *gross produce* of the land rather than to a fixed monetary amount.
- This protects tenants during years of crop failure (drought, flood).

**C. Principle of Cost Recovery**
- The owner must recover: (i) interest on land value, (ii) depreciation on structures, (iii) maintenance cost, (iv) insurance premium, and (v) a reasonable margin for risk.

**D. Principle of Local Comparability**
- Fair rent must align with the rents of comparable farms in the same agro-climatic zone, soil type, and irrigation status.

**E. Principle of Sustainability**
- Fair rent must not push tenants into poverty — it must allow for sustainable farming practices, soil conservation, and reinvestment.

### 2.2 Methods of Determining Fair Rent (KTU Exam Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 — 5 Standard Methods** — At least one numerical is guaranteed in the ESE.

**Method 1 — Produce Share Method (Most Common)**
- Fair Rent is fixed as a fraction of the **gross produce**.
- Common ratios: **1/4, 1/3, 1/2, 2/5** depending on region and inputs supplied.

**Method 2 — Yield-Based Monetary Method**
- Total expected yield (quintals/ha) × Market Price (₹/quintal) = Gross Income
- Fair Rent = Fixed share × Gross Income

**Method 3 — Land Value Capitalisation Method**
- Fair Rent = (Capitalised Value of Land × Standard Interest Rate) / 100

**Method 4 — Cost-of-Production Method**
- Fair Rent = Gross Income − Cost of Cultivation − Cultivator's Wages − Profit Margin

**Method 5 — Comparative Rent Method**
- Average of rents of similar neighbouring farms with similar soil, irrigation, and crop pattern.

### 2.3 Components Considered While Calculating Fair Rent

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always list these 5 components in any 7-mark answer for full credit.

1. **Interest on the capital value of land** (cost of owned capital)
2. **Depreciation** of farm buildings, wells, fences, and other permanent structures
3. **Maintenance and repair allowance** for owned assets
4. **Insurance premium** for structures and standing crops
5. **Risk and management allowance** for the landlord

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Fair Rent Master Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas verified against standard Agricultural Economics textbooks prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

| Sl. | Method | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Produce Share Method** | $FR = R_{ratio} \times Y \times P$ | $R_{ratio}$ = landlord's share ratio (dimensionless); $Y$ = yield (quintals); $P$ = price (₹/quintal) |
| 2 | **Capitalisation Method** | $FR = \dfrac{C \times i}{100}$ | $C$ = capitalised value of land (₹); $i$ = interest rate (% p.a.) |
| 3 | **Cost-of-Production Method** | $FR = GI - (CC + W + PM)$ | $GI$ = gross income (₹); $CC$ = cultivation cost (₹); $W$ = wages (₹); $PM$ = profit margin (₹) |
| 4 | **Comprehensive Method** | $FR = \dfrac{(C \times i) + D + M + I + R}{1}$ | $C$ = capital (₹); $i$ = rate (%); $D$ = depreciation (₹); $M$ = maintenance (₹); $I$ = insurance (₹); $R$ = risk allowance (₹) |
| 5 | **Comparative Method** | $FR = \dfrac{\sum R_{i}}{n}$ | $R_{i}$ = rent of i-th comparable farm (₹); $n$ = number of farms |
| 6 | **Net Income Method** | $FR = NI - W_{cult} - Pr$ | $NI$ = net income (₹); $W_{cult}$ = cultivator's wages (₹); $Pr$ = cultivator's profit (₹) |
| 7 | **Gross Produce Ratio** | $\dfrac{FR}{GP} = \dfrac{L_share}{T_share}$ | $L_share$ = landlord share; $T_share$ = total share; $GP$ = gross produce |

> [!NOTE]
> **NOTATION SAFEGUARD:** All absolute-value expressions use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` symbol to avoid Markdown table breakage.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the discipline of **Farm Structures and Cost Estimation**, Fair Rent calculations are essential for:

- **Project appraisal** of agri-business ventures
- **Bank loan valuation** — Banks sanction crop loans based on expected net rent
- **Crop insurance premium** calculations
- **Government land acquisition** compensation
- **Court adjudication** in tenancy disputes (especially under Kerala Land Reforms Ordinance / Kerala Tenancy Act)
- **Designing rental models** for farm machinery and equipment

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Comprehensive Fair Rent Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** The fair rent of an agricultural holding must equal the sum of (a) interest on capital invested in land, (b) depreciation of permanent structures, (c) maintenance allowance, (d) insurance, and (e) risk & management margin — divided appropriately to allow the cultivator his livelihood.

**Step 1 — Identify the Landlord's Investment Components**

Let the landlord's total capital investment in the holding be split into two parts:
- Land (with soil, water-table access, irrigation potential)
- Permanent structures (farmhouse, storage godown, well, fencing, threshing floor)

**Step 2 — Express Each Component Mathematically**

Let:
- $V_L$ = market value of bare land (₹)
- $V_S$ = depreciated value of permanent structures (₹)
- $C$ = total capital = $V_L + V_S$
- $i$ = prevailing rate of interest on long-term capital (% per annum)
- $r$ = depreciation rate for structures (% per annum)
- $m$ = annual maintenance rate (% of $V_S$)
- $p$ = insurance premium rate (% of $V_S$)
- $k$ = risk & management allowance rate (% of $C$)

**Step 3 — Build the Landlord's Annual Cost Block**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Interest on Capital} &= \dfrac{C \times i}{100} \\[6pt]
\text{Annual Depreciation on Structures} &= \dfrac{V_S \times r}{100} \\[6pt]
\text{Annual Maintenance} &= \dfrac{V_S \times m}{100} \\[6pt]
\text{Annual Insurance} &= \dfrac{V_S \times p}{100} \\[6pt]
\text{Annual Risk \& Management} &= \dfrac{C \times k}{100}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum All Components to Get Total Fair Rent**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FR_{total} &= \dfrac{C \cdot i}{100} + \dfrac{V_S \cdot r}{100} + \dfrac{V_S \cdot m}{100} + \dfrac{V_S \cdot p}{100} + \dfrac{C \cdot k}{100} \\[8pt]
FR_{total} &= \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ C(i + k) + V_S(r + m + p) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Per-Acre or Per-Hectare Normalisation**

If the holding area is $A$ hectares, then:

$$
FR_{per\ hectare} = \dfrac{FR_{total}}{A}
$$

**Step 6 — Per Quintal or Per Tonne Conversion**

If the expected gross yield is $Y$ quintals:

$$
FR_{per\ quintal} = \dfrac{FR_{total}}{Y}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]:** A 4-hectare coconut farm has the following particulars. Calculate the fair rent per hectare per annum using the **Comprehensive Method**.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Market value of land | ₹ 8,00,000 per hectare |
| Depreciated value of structures (well, godown, fencing) | ₹ 2,00,000 per hectare |
| Interest rate on capital | **8% p.a.** |
| Depreciation rate | **3% p.a.** |
| Maintenance rate | **5% p.a.** |
| Insurance rate | **2% p.a.** |
| Risk & management allowance | **4% p.a.** |
| Total holding area | 4 hectares |

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Capital Values per Hectare**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_L &= \text{₹ } 8{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
V_S &= \text{₹ } 2{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
C &= V_L + V_S = 8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 10{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Comprehensive Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FR_{per\ hectare} &= \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ C(i + k) + V_S(r + m + p) \right] \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ 10{,}00{,}000 \times (8 + 4) + 2{,}00{,}000 \times (3 + 5 + 2) \right] \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ 10{,}00{,}000 \times 12 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 10 \right] \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 \right] \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{1{,}40{,}00{,}000}{100} \\[8pt]
&= \text{₹ } 1{,}40{,}000 \text{ per hectare per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Fair Rent for 4 Hectares**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FR_{total} &= 1{,}40{,}000 \times 4 = \text{₹ } 5{,}60{,}000 \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Monthly Equivalent**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FR_{monthly} &= \dfrac{5{,}60{,}000}{12} \approx \text{₹ } 46{,}667 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Fair Rent = **₹ 1,40,000 per hectare per annum** or **₹ 5,60,000 per annum for the 4-hectare holding**.

### 3.3 Alternative Worked Example — Produce Share Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 2-hectare paddy field yields **30 quintals per hectare** per crop. Two crops are grown annually. Market price = **₹ 2,200 per quintal**. Landlord's share (per Kerala Tenancy Act norms) = **1/3 of gross produce**. Calculate fair rent.

**Step 1 — Gross Produce Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GP_{per\ hectare\ per\ year} &= 30 \times 2 = 60 \text{ quintals} \\[4pt]
GP_{total} &= 60 \times 2 = 120 \text{ quintals}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Gross Income**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GI &= 120 \times 2{,}200 = \text{₹ } 2{,}64{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fair Rent (1/3 Share)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FR &= \dfrac{1}{3} \times 2{,}64{,}000 = \text{₹ } 88{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Per Hectare Fair Rent**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FR_{per\ hectare} &= \dfrac{88{,}000}{2} = \text{₹ } 44{,}000 \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Fair Rent = **₹ 44,000 per hectare per annum** under the Produce Share Method.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Comprehensive Method | Produce Share Method | Capitalisation Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Required** | Detailed cost data | Yield + price + share ratio | Land value + interest rate |
| **Accuracy** | High | Moderate-High | Low-Moderate |
| **Use Case** | Court disputes, project reports | Traditional Kerala tenancy | Quick estimates |
| **Sensitivity to Yield** | Low | High | None |
| **Sensitivity to Inflation** | Moderate | Low | High |
