## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define the term "Fair Rent" as applied in agricultural land valuation. How is it different from "Contract Rent"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Fair Rent** is the reasonable and equitable rental value of an agricultural holding, determined objectively on the basis of the productive capacity of the land, the cost of cultivation, prevailing wage rates, and standard yield indices. It ensures a fair return to the landlord and a sustainable livelihood to the tenant.

> **Contract Rent**, on the other hand, is the rent *mutually agreed* upon by the landlord and tenant through a private agreement or lease deed. It may or may not reflect the true economic value of the land — it is often influenced by bargaining power, regional customs, or personal relationships.

**Key Difference:** Fair Rent is *scientifically determined* by objective economic principles; Contract Rent is *privately negotiated* and can be higher, lower, or equal to Fair Rent.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List any four factors that influence the determination of Fair Rent of an agricultural holding.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four most important factors influencing Fair Rent are:

1. **Productivity of the soil** — soil type, fertility, irrigation status, drainage
2. **Cropping pattern and yield index** — Kharif/Rabi crops, multiple cropping intensity
3. **Cost of cultivation** — input costs (seeds, fertilisers, labour, irrigation)
4. **Prevailing market prices** of the produce and local wage rates
5. **Capital invested by landlord** in land and permanent structures
6. **Risk and uncertainty** factors — drought, flood, pest attacks
7. **Local tenancy laws and government regulations**

*(Any four well-explained factors carry 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Method**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **principles** governing the determination of fair rent in agricultural holdings. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A farmer owns a 6-hectare rubber plantation. The market value of land is **₹ 12,00,000 per hectare**. The depreciated value of farm structures (tapping shed, fencing, drainage channels) is **₹ 3,00,000 per hectare**. The applicable rates are: Interest = **9%**, Depreciation = **4%**, Maintenance = **6%**, Insurance = **2.5%**, Risk & Management = **5%**. Calculate the **annual fair rent per hectare** using the comprehensive method and the **total fair rent per month** for the holding. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A**

**(a) Principles Governing Fair Rent (7 Marks):**

1. **Principle of Equity (2 Marks):** Fair rent must ensure that the landlord receives a reasonable return on his investment while the tenant retains sufficient income for his labour and livelihood. Neither party should be exploited. The traditional 1/3–2/3 or 1/2–1/2 produce split embodies this principle.

2. **Principle of Productivity-Based Rent (2 Marks):** Rent must be linked to the actual productive output of the land, not a fixed monetary value. This protects the tenant against crop failure due to drought, pests, or market crashes.

3. **Principle of Cost Recovery (1.5 Marks):** The landlord's annual rent must recover (i) interest on capital, (ii) depreciation of structures, (iii) maintenance costs, (iv) insurance, and (v) risk allowance.

4. **Principle of Local Comparability (1 Mark):** Fair rent must align with rents of comparable neighbouring farms in the same agro-climatic zone, soil type, and irrigation status.

5. **Principle of Sustainability (0.5 Marks):** Fair rent should not force the tenant into distress farming, debt, or soil-degrading practices.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Area = 6 hectares
- $V_L$ = ₹ 12,00,000 per hectare
- $V_S$ = ₹ 3,00,000 per hectare
- $i$ = 9%, $r$ = 4%, $m$ = 6%, $p$ = 2.5%, $k$ = 5%

**Step 1 — Total Capital per Hectare**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= V_L + V_S \\[4pt]
&= 12{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ } 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ per hectare}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Comprehensive Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FR_{ha} &= \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ C(i + k) + V_S(r + m + p) \right] \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ 15{,}00{,}000 \times (9 + 5) + 3{,}00{,}000 \times (4 + 6 + 2.5) \right] \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ 15{,}00{,}000 \times 14 + 3{,}00{,}000 \times 12.5 \right] \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ 2{,}10{,}00{,}000 + 37{,}50{,}000 \right] \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{2{,}47{,}50{,}000}{100} \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ } 2{,}47{,}500 \text{ per hectare per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Fair Rent for 6 Hectares**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FR_{total} &= 2{,}47{,}500 \times 6 = \text{₹ } 14{,}85{,}000 \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Monthly Fair Rent**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FR_{monthly} &= \dfrac{14{,}85{,}000}{12} = \text{₹ } 1{,}23{,}750 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Fair Rent = **₹ 2,47,500 per hectare per annum** | Total = **₹ 14,85,000 per annum** | Monthly = **₹ 1,23,750**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Produce Share Method + Cost of Production**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Produce Share Method** of determining fair rent. State its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-hectare banana farm has an expected annual yield of **400 quintals per hectare**. The market price is **₹ 1,800 per quintal**. The landlord supplies irrigation and the tenant supplies labour. As per local tenancy norms, the landlord's share is **40%** of gross produce. Calculate the fair rent per annum and per hectare. If the cost of cultivation (excluding family labour) is **₹ 50,000 per hectare**, verify whether the tenant's net income is reasonable (assume family labour value = **₹ 60,000 per hectare**). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B**

**(a) Produce Share Method — Explanation, Advantages, Limitations (7 Marks):**

**Explanation (3 Marks):** Under the Produce Share Method (also called *Crop Share Method* or *Batai Method*), fair rent is determined as a fixed proportion of the gross produce of the land rather than as a fixed monetary value. The landlord and tenant physically divide the harvested crop at the threshing floor or collection point in a pre-agreed ratio (commonly 1/3, 2/5, 1/2, or 2/3). The landlord's share in monetary terms is calculated by multiplying the share ratio with the gross produce and the prevailing market price.

**Advantages (2 Marks):**
- Automatically adjusts to crop failure and bumper harvests
- Simple and transparent — no complex calculation required
- Widely accepted in Indian agriculture and codified in state tenancy laws
- Protects tenant against price crashes (rent falls in cash value)
- Protects landlord against price rises (rent rises in cash value)

**Limitations (2 Marks):**
- Tenant has less incentive to invest in productivity (moral hazard)
- Disputes arise over actual yield and quality at time of division
- In-kind payment is inconvenient for landlord needing cash
- Difficult to apply to perennial crops (coconut, rubber, mango)
- Crop wastage and transportation losses are ambiguous

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Area = 3 hectares
- Yield = 400 quintals/hectare/year
- Price = ₹ 1,800/quintal
- Landlord share = 40%
- Cost of cultivation = ₹ 50,000/hectare
- Family labour value = ₹ 60,000/hectare

**Step 1 — Gross Produce Total**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GP &= 400 \times 3 = 1200 \text{ quintals per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Gross Income**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GI &= 1200 \times 1{,}800 = \text{₹ } 21{,}60{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fair Rent (40% Landlord Share)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FR_{total} &= 0.40 \times 21{,}60{,}000 = \text{₹ } 8{,}64{,}000 \text{ per annum} \\[4pt]
FR_{per\ hectare} &= \dfrac{8{,}64{,}000}{3} = \text{₹ } 2{,}88{,}000 \text{ per hectare per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Tenant's Net Income Verification**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tenant's Gross Share} &= 0.60 \times 21{,}60{,}000 = \text{₹ } 12{,}96{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Less: Cultivation Cost} &= 50{,}000 \times 3 = \text{₹ } 1{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Less: Family Labour Value} &= 60{,}000 \times 3 = \text{₹ } 1{,}80{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Tenant's Net Income} &= 12{,}96{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ } 9{,}66{,}000 \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:**
> Fair Rent = **₹ 8,64,000 per annum** | **₹ 2,88,000 per hectare per annum**
> Tenant's Net Income = **₹ 9,66,000 per annum** — which is reasonable and sustainable.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Students 2–4 Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Fair Rent with Contract Rent** — Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Always define both.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+ k" Risk Allowance term** — Many students omit the risk and management component. This is a 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** — quintals vs kg, hectares vs acres, per annum vs per month. Always state units explicitly.
> 4. **Not normalising to per-hectare** — KTU expects fair rent expressed per hectare for comparison.
> 5. **Mixing up landlord share vs tenant share** — In Kerala, the **tenant typically gets the larger share** (1/2 to 3/5). Getting this reversed = 1-mark penalty.
> 6. **Skipping the "principles" sub-question** — Q.(a) on principles carries 7 marks; many students write only 3–4 points.
> 7. **No final answer statement** — Always box or bold your final numerical answer.

---

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Bullet-Point Density:**

- **Fair Rent** = *scientific, objective* rental value; **Contract Rent** = *negotiated, subjective* rental value
- The **five principles** of fair rent: Equity, Productivity, Cost Recovery, Local Comparability, Sustainability
- The **five methods**: Comprehensive, Produce Share, Capitalisation, Cost of Production, Comparative
- The **master formula**: $FR = \frac{1}{100} [C(i+k) + V_S(r+m+p)]$
- **Landlord share** typically = **1/3 to 1/2** of gross produce (varies by state law)
- **Tenant share** typically = **1/2 to 2/3** of gross produce
- **Standard interest rate** = **6% to 10%** p.a.
- **Depreciation** = **2% to 5%** p.a.
- **Risk allowance** = **2% to 5%** of capital
- Always **normalise per hectare** for comparison
- **Per quintal rent** = total fair rent ÷ total yield
- Fair rent **auto-adjusts** to yield under the Produce Share Method
- The **Comprehensive Method** is **preferred in court cases** for its transparency
- For Kerala Land Reforms context: remember the **Kerala Tenancy Act provisions**
- **Memorise the variable meanings** — C, i, k, V_S, r, m, p — these appear every exam
- Always **verify tenant's net income** in numerical problems to confirm reasonableness
- For perennial crops (rubber, coconut, mango) — use **Comprehensive Method** not Produce Share
- For annual crops (paddy, wheat, vegetables) — use **Produce Share Method**
- For quick estimates — use **Capitalisation Method**
- For bank/insurance valuations — use **Comprehensive Method**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Year's Purchase, etc.; Methods of Valuation - Replacement Cost Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Year's Purchase, etc.; Methods of Valuation - Replacement Cost Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: VALUATION OF FARM STRUCTURES

## 1.1 Year's Purchase (YP) — The Core Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Official):**
> *Year's Purchase is defined as the number of years' purchase of the net annual income which the property is expected to yield, the rate of interest being the controlling factor. Mathematically, it is the capital sum required to produce a given net annual income at a given rate of interest.*

$$
YP = \frac{100}{r} = \frac{1}{i}
$$

Where:
- $YP$ = Year's Purchase (a dimensionless number, often expressed in "years")
- $r$ = Rate of interest in **percent per annum** (e.g., 5 %)
- $i$ = Rate of interest as a decimal (e.g., 0.05)
- $N$ = Number of years for limited-period income

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Years Purchase is NOT a period of time.** It is a *multiplier* used to convert a stream of future annual income into a single present-day lump sum (Present Value / Capitalized Value).

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a neighbour offers to pay you **₹1,00,000 every year forever** as rent for your farm shed. You want to know "what is this rental stream *worth* today as a single cheque?"

To compute that, you ask your bank: *"If I deposit a lump sum in a savings account paying 5 % interest per year, how much money do I need to deposit so that the bank pays me ₹1,00,000 of interest **every year, forever**, without ever touching my principal?"*

The answer is: *Capital × 5 % = 1,00,000 → Capital = ₹20,00,000.*

So, the perpetual rent of ₹1 lakh/year is **equivalent to ₹20 lakhs today** at 5 % interest. We say the **Year's Purchase = 100/5 = 20**.

> **Real-world meaning:** A YP of 20 means *20 years' worth of net annual income* is the fair capitalized value of the property. The higher the YP, the more valuable the property (typically because the area is in high demand, lowering the prevailing interest/return rate).

### Modified YP Formulas (Limited Life Income)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the income is for a **limited number of years** (e.g., a lease of 30 years), the standard YP formula is modified using the **Inwood's Table** or the **Present Worth Factor (PWF)**.

For a *perpetual* income: $YP = \frac{1}{i}$

For a *limited-period* income of $N$ years: $YP = \frac{(1+i)^{N} - 1}{i(1+i)^{N}}$ — this is the **Present Worth Factor of an Annuity**.

### Categories of YP Used in Practice

| S.No | Scenario | Formula | Use |
|------|----------|---------|-----|
| 1 | Perpetual income (freehold) | $YP = 1/i$ | Fee-simple / freehold properties |
| 2 | Limited period $N$ years (leasehold) | $YP = \frac{(1+i)^{N}-1}{i(1+i)^{N}}$ | Short-term leases |
| 3 | Deferred annuity (after $m$ years, for $N$ years) | $YP = \frac{(1+i)^{N}-1}{i(1+i)^{m+N}}$ | Future cash flows |
| 4 | Sinking Fund YP (with redemption) | $YP = \frac{1}{i + \frac{i}{(1+i)^{N}-1}}$ | When the capital is to be recovered at the end |

---

## 1.2 Methods of Valuation — An Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU):** *Valuation is the art of estimating the present fair market value of a property, considering its age, location, condition, future utility, and the legal rights attached.*

There are **five principal methods** of valuation used for farm structures and agricultural buildings:

1. **Rental Method of Valuation** (based on net annual income × YP)
2. **Direct Comparison Method** (market-data / sales comparison)
3. **Replacement Cost Method** ← *focus of this topic*
4. **Profit-Based Method** (for commercial farm buildings)
5. **Development Method** (for undeveloped land)

We will now examine the **Replacement Cost Method** in exhaustive detail.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. REPLACEMENT COST METHOD — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replacement Cost Method (RCM):**
> *The value of a property is estimated as the **current cost of replacing the existing structure** with a **new one of similar utility, design, and specifications**, using **modern materials and current labour rates**, and then deducting an amount for **accumulated depreciation** plus the **market value of the land**.*

$$
V = (RC - D) + LV
$$

Where:
- $V$ = Present market value of the property (₹)
- $RC$ = Current Replacement Cost of the structure (₹)
- $D$ = Accumulated depreciation (₹)
- $LV$ = Market value of land only (₹)

## 2.2 Why is the Replacement Cost Method Used for Farm Structures?

Farm structures — like **cattle sheds, poultry houses, greenhouses, dairy parlours, godowns, cold storage, threshing floors, and irrigation structures** — are **purpose-built** and rarely transact frequently in the open market. Therefore, the *Direct Comparison Method* is often impractical.

The **Rental Method** is also unsuitable for many farm structures because the owner uses the structure (not leased out). Hence, the **Replacement Cost Method** becomes the most scientifically rigorous and legally accepted method for valuing farm infrastructure.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Step 1 — Quantity Survey:** Measure the structure (plinth area, cubical content, plinth perimeter) and prepare a detailed Bill of Quantities (BOQ) using the *Schedule of Rates (SOR)* of the current year.
2. **Step 2 — Cost Indexation:** If the original construction cost is known, inflate it to present-day prices using a **construction cost index** (e.g., Reserve Bank of India BCI, CPWD Index, or local PWD index).
3. **Step 3 — Depreciation Deduction:** Compute the *total depreciation* accrued during the *effective age* of the structure using one of the standard methods.
4. **Step 4 — Land Valuation:** Add the current market value of the **bare land** (obtained independently via market comparison).
5. **Step 5 — Add Ancillary Values:** Add value of water supply, electrification, fencing, gates, etc.
6. **Step 6 — Final Value:** Sum the depreciated building value and land/ancillary value to get the *total present worth*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** When asked to *estimate the cost of a building*, the examiner usually expects the student to compute the **plinth area rate** or **cubical content rate** method, NOT a full BOQ.

## 2.4 Methods of Estimating Replacement Cost

| S.No | Sub-Method | Formula | When to Use |
|------|-----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Plinth Area Method** | $RC = P_A \times P_R \times S_F$ | Quick approximate estimate |
| 2 | **Cubical Content Method** | $RC = V \times C_R$ | More accurate for warehouse/farm sheds |
| 3 | **Detailed BOQ / Item-Rate Method** | $RC = \sum (Q_i \times R_i)$ | Most accurate, used for final accounts |
| 4 | **Index Method** | $RC = OC \times \dfrac{CI_{new}}{CI_{old}}$ | When original cost data is available |

Where:
- $P_A$ = Plinth Area (m² or sq.ft)
- $P_R$ = Plinth Area Rate (₹/m²)
- $S_F$ = Storey Factor (1.0 for single-storey, 1.5 for two-storey, etc.)
- $V$ = Volume of building (m³ or cft)
- $C_R$ = Cubical Rate (₹/m³)
- $OC$ = Original Cost, $CI$ = Cost Index

## 2.5 The Four Standard Methods of Calculating Depreciation

Depreciation is the *loss in value* of a structure due to **age, wear and tear, obsolescence, and structural decay**.

| S.No | Depreciation Method | Formula | Annual Depreciation |
|------|---------------------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Straight Line Method (SLM)** | $D_n = \dfrac{C - S}{N}$ | Constant every year |
| 2 | **Sinking Fund Method (SFM)** | $D_n = (C - S) \times \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$ | Constant every year, includes interest on S.F. |
| 3 | **Constant Percentage / Declining Balance Method** | $BV_n = C(1-r)^{n}$ and $D_n = BV_{n-1} \times r$ | Decreases every year |
| 4 | **Quantity Survey Method** | $D = \text{Cost of actually wasted materials and labour}$ | Empirical, used for old buildings |

Where:
- $C$ = Original Cost, $S$ = Scrap/Salvage Value, $N$ = Life in years
- $i$ = Sinking fund rate of interest, $r$ = Constant percentage rate
- $BV_n$ = Book Value at end of $n$ years

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banks & NABARD** use RCM to value farm assets offered as collateral for crop loans and KCC.
- **Insurance companies** use RCM to assess *Sum Insured* for fire, storm, and flood policies on farm buildings.
- **Government** uses RCM for **compensation assessment** under the *Land Acquisition Act, 2013* (for farm buildings, not land).
- **Court of Law** accepts RCM as the standard method when a property is rarely sold (e.g., temples, agricultural godowns, dairy plants).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL EXAMPLES

## 3.1 Derivation of Year's Purchase for a Perpetuity

> **Background:** A perpetuity is a stream of equal cash flows that continues *forever*.

Let $A$ be the annual net income, $i$ be the rate of interest, and $P$ be the present capitalized value (the principal).

By the definition of simple interest:

$$
A = P \times i \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = \frac{A}{i}
$$

If we define the *multiplier* that converts annual income to present value, we get the Year's Purchase:

$$
YP = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{i} = \frac{100}{r} \text{ (when } r \text{ is in \%)}
$$

This is the foundation of the **Rental Method of Valuation**:

$$
\text{Capitalized Value} = \text{Net Annual Income} \times YP
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Sinking Fund Depreciation

> **Background:** The owner sets aside a fixed sum every year (a "sinking fund") such that at the end of the asset's life, the accumulated amount equals the *depreciable amount* $(C - S)$. The Sinking Fund earns compound interest at rate $i$.

Let $A_{sf}$ be the annual sinking fund deposit. After $N$ years, the future value of the annuity must equal $(C - S)$:

$$
A_{sf} \times \frac{(1+i)^{N} - 1}{i} = (C - S)
$$

Solving for $A_{sf}$:

$$
A_{sf} = (C - S) \times \frac{i}{(1+i)^{N} - 1}
$$

The **total annual depreciation charge** is the Sinking Fund deposit *plus* the interest *lost* on the depreciated capital $(C - S)$:

$$
D_{n} = (C - S) \times \left[ i + \frac{i}{(1+i)^{N} - 1} \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The first term, $i(C-S)$, is the *interest on the original capital*. The second term is the *Sinking Fund instalment*. The owner "recovers" the depreciated amount by collecting rent equal to $D_n$ every year.

## 3.3 Derivation of Constant Percentage (Declining Balance) Method

> **Background:** In this method, a *fixed percentage* $r$ of the *book value at the start of the year* is written off as depreciation.

Let $BV_0 = C$ (initial book value). After Year 1:

$$
BV_1 = C - C \cdot r = C(1 - r)
$$

After Year 2:

$$
BV_2 = BV_1 - BV_1 \cdot r = BV_1(1 - r) = C(1 - r)^{2}
$$

Generalizing for $n$ years:

$$
BV_n = C(1 - r)^{n}
$$

The depreciation *in year $n$* (i.e., between years $n-1$ and $n$):

$$
D_n = BV_{n-1} \cdot r = C(1 - r)^{n-1} \cdot r
$$

## 3.4 Worked-Out KTU Numerical Example (Replacement Cost Method)

> **Question (KTU Pattern):** A farm godown was constructed 15 years ago at a cost of ₹8,00,000. Its expected useful life is 50 years with a scrap value of ₹40,000. The present construction cost index is 280 against the base index of 140 at the time of construction. The land on which the godown stands is currently valued at ₹6,00,000 in the open market. Find the **present value of the property** by the *Replacement Cost Method* using the **Straight Line Method of Depreciation**.

### Step 1 — Replacement Cost (using Index Method)

$$
RC = OC \times \frac{CI_{new}}{CI_{old}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{280}{140}
$$

$$
RC = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = \text{₹}16{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 2 — Depreciation per Year (Straight Line)

$$
D_{annual} = \frac{C - S}{N} = \frac{16{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}000}{50}
$$

$$
D_{annual} = \frac{15{,}60{,}000}{50} = \text{₹}31{,}200 \text{ per year}
$$

### Step 3 — Accumulated Depreciation for 15 Years

$$
D_{15} = 31{,}200 \times 15 = \text{₹}4{,}68{,}000
$$

### Step 4 — Depreciated Value of the Building

$$
V_{building} = RC - D_{15} = 16{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}68{,}000 = \text{₹}11{,}32{,}000
$$

### Step 5 — Add the Land Value

$$
V_{total} = V_{building} + LV = 11{,}32{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}17{,}32{,}000
$$

### Final Answer

> **The present value of the farm godown property is ₹17,32,000 (Seventeen Lakhs Thirty-Two Thousand Only).**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Table of Depreciation Methods

| Parameter | Straight Line | Sinking Fund | Declining Balance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|-------------------|
| Annual depreciation amount | **Constant** | **Constant** | **Decreases** |
| Book value decreases | Linearly | Compound curve | Exponentially |
| Accounts for time value of money? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Recovery of capital at end of life | No | Yes (via S.F.) | No (asymptotic) |
| Preferred for | Simple accounts | Long-life assets | Tax/IT depreciation |
| Suitability for farm structures | ✅ High | ✅ High | ⚠️ Moderate |

## 3.6 Cross-Validation Using the Rental Method

For the *same* farm godown, suppose the **net annual rent** if it were let out is ₹1,10,000 and the local interest rate is **6 % per annum**.

$$
YP = \frac{100}{r} = \frac{100}{6} = 16.67 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\text{Capitalized Value} = 1{,}10{,}000 \times 16.67 = \text{₹}18{,}33{,}333
$$

> The two methods give comparable figures (₹17.32 lakh vs ₹18.33 lakh) — a difference of ~6 %, which is acceptable in valuation practice. This cross-check is highly valued in KTU answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Flowchart — Year Purchase and Valuation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Property for Valuation] --> B{Type of Property?}
    B -- Income Producing --> C[Rental Method]
    B -- Owner Occupied --> D[Replacement Cost Method]
    B -- Frequently Sold --> E[Direct Comparison Method]
    C --> F[Net Annual Income x YP = Capitalized Value]
    D --> G[Current Cost - Depreciation + Land Value]
    E --> H[Compare with Recent Sales of Similar Properties]
    F --> I[FINAL VALUATION REPORT]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Replacement Cost Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Step 1: Quantity Survey of Structure] --> A2[Step 2: Calculate Plinth Area / Cubical Content]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Apply Current Schedule of Rates]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Compute Replacement Cost RC]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Estimate Effective Age of Structure]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Calculate Annual Depreciation]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Multiply x Effective Age = Total D]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: RC minus D = Depreciated Building Value]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9: Add Market Value of Land LV]
    A9 --> A10[Step 10: Add Ancillary Values]
    A10 --> A11[FINAL PRESENT VALUE OF PROPERTY]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Breakdown — Methods of Depreciation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[DEPRECIATION CALCULATION] --> D1[Straight Line Method]
    D0 --> D2[Sinking Fund Method]
    D0 --> D3[Declining Balance Method]
    D0 --> D4[Quantity Survey Method]
    D1 --> D1a["Formula: Cn minus Sn = C minus S over N"]
    D2 --> D2a["Formula: Cn minus Sn = A_sf over SF factor"]
    D3 --> D3a["Formula: BVn = C times 1 minus r to the n"]
    D4 --> D4a["Empirical: Physical inspection based"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Interaction of Key Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS
        I1[Original Cost OC]
        I2[Cost Index CI new vs old]
        I3[Effective Age n years]
        I4[Useful Life N years]
        I5[Scrap Value S]
        I6[Land Value LV]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING
        P1[Compute RC by Index Method]
        P2[Compute Annual Depreciation]
        P3[Compute Total Depreciation D]
        P4[Compute Depreciated Value]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[Total Present Value of Property]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    I3 --> P2
    I4 --> P2
    I5 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P1 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    I6 --> O1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Q1. Define Year's Purchase. What is its significance in the valuation of farm structures?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Year's Purchase (YP) is the number of years' net annual income that must be capitalized to determine the present market value of a property. Mathematically, $YP = 100/r$, where $r$ is the rate of interest per annum.

**Significance (1.5 marks):** YP is the multiplier that converts the **net annual income** of a property into its **capitalized (present) value**. It is the backbone of the **Rental Method of Valuation**, which is widely used for income-producing farm structures like dairy farms, poultry houses, and leased-out godowns. YP essentially captures the *time value of money* — a rupee earned 20 years from now is worth less than a rupee earned today, and YP quantifies this conversion.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Q2. List any four methods of calculating depreciation of a building. Which method is most suitable for a long-life farm structure like a cold storage unit?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Four Methods (2 marks):**
1. Straight Line Method
2. Sinking Fund Method
3. Declining Balance / Constant Percentage Method
4. Quantity Survey Method

**Most Suitable Method (1 mark):** For a **long-life farm structure** such as a **cold storage unit** (life 40–60 years), the **Sinking Fund Method** is most suitable because it accounts for the *time value of money* by accumulating a compounded fund that fully recovers the depreciated capital at the end of the useful life.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]**

A poultry house for a layer farm was constructed 12 years ago at a cost of ₹5,00,000. The useful life of the structure is 40 years, and the scrap value at the end of its life is estimated at ₹50,000. The construction cost index has risen from 150 (at the time of construction) to 315 (current). The land on which the structure stands has a present market value of ₹3,50,000.

**(a)** Estimate the **present value of the property** using the *Replacement Cost Method* with the *Straight Line Method* of depreciation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same structure were to be let out at a **net annual rent of ₹65,000** and the local rate of interest is **6 % per annum**, estimate its value using the *Rental Method*. Compare and comment on the two values. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Replacement Cost Method (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute Replacement Cost (RC) using Index Method (2 marks):**

$$
RC = OC \times \frac{CI_{current}}{CI_{original}} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{315}{150}
$$

$$
RC = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 2.10 = \text{₹}10{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Depreciation using SLM (2 marks):**

$$
D_{annual} = \frac{RC - S}{N} = \frac{10{,}50{,}000 - 50{,}000}{40}
$$

$$
D_{annual} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{40} = \text{₹}25{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Depreciation for 12 years (1 mark):**

$$
D_{12} = 25{,}000 \times 12 = \text{₹}3{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Depreciated Value of Building (1 mark):**

$$
V_{building} = RC - D_{12} = 10{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}7{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Add Land Value (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{V_{total} = 7{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹}11{,}00{,}000}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Rental Method of Valuation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute Year's Purchase (1 mark):**

$$
YP = \frac{100}{r} = \frac{100}{6} = 16.67
$$

**Step 2 — Capitalize the Net Annual Rent (2 marks):**

$$
CV = \text{Net Rent} \times YP = 65{,}000 \times 16.67
$$

$$
CV = \text{₹}10{,}83{,}550 \text{ (Capitalized Value of Structure only)}
$$

**Step 3 — Add Land Value (1 mark):**

$$
V_{total}^{rental} = 10{,}83{,}550 + 3{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹}14{,}33{,}550
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and Comment (3 marks):**

| Method | Present Value of Property |
|--------|---------------------------|
| Replacement Cost Method | ₹11,00,000 |
| Rental Method | ₹14,33,550 |
| **Difference** | ₹3,33,550 (~30 % higher in Rental) |

**Comment:** The Rental Method yields a *higher* value because the rental income assumes *optimum utilization* of the structure. The Replacement Cost Method, on the other hand, is more *conservative* as it accounts for *physical depreciation*. In practice, the **final reported valuation** is often the **average of the two methods** (or weighted average: 70 % RCM + 30 % Rental), giving a fair estimate of ₹12,66,775. The Rental Method is preferred when *comparable rental data* is robust; otherwise, RCM is more reliable for farm structures.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Sinking Fund Method of Depreciation** with a neat derivation. Why is it considered the most scientifically rigorous method for valuing long-life farm buildings? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dairy farm building was constructed at a cost of ₹12,00,000 with an estimated life of 50 years and a scrap value of ₹1,00,000. The owner sets up a **sinking fund** that earns interest at **5 % per annum compound interest**. Calculate:
   (i) The **annual sinking fund instalment**.
   (ii) The **total annual depreciation charge** to be levied.
   (iii) The **book value of the building** at the end of 20 years. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Sinking Fund Method Theory (7 marks)**

**Concept (2 marks):** Under this method, the owner assumes that the **depreciated amount** $(C - S)$ of the building will be **recovered** at the end of the useful life by means of a **sinking fund**. Every year, a fixed sum is deposited into the sinking fund (which earns compound interest at the prevailing rate), such that at the end of $N$ years, the accumulated amount equals $(C - S)$.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Let $A_{sf}$ = annual sinking fund instalment, $i$ = rate of interest.

The future value of an annuity of $A_{sf}$ deposited at the end of each year for $N$ years at rate $i$ is:

$$
FV = A_{sf} \times \frac{(1+i)^{N} - 1}{i}
$$

For the sinking fund to fully recover the depreciated amount, $FV = (C - S)$:

$$
A_{sf} \times \frac{(1+i)^{N} - 1}{i} = (C - S)
$$

$$
A_{sf} = (C - S) \times \frac{i}{(1+i)^{N} - 1}
$$

The total annual depreciation = Annual Sinking Fund deposit + Interest on $(C - S)$:

$$
D_{annual} = (C - S) \left[ i + \frac{i}{(1+i)^{N} - 1} \right]
$$

**Why most rigorous (2 marks):** It is the *only* method that (a) **accounts for the time value of money**, and (b) **guarantees full recovery of the original capital** at the end of the asset's life. Hence it is preferred for long-life farm structures like cold storages (life 50+ years) and dairy processing plants.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

Given: $C = ₹12{,}00{,}000$, $S = ₹1{,}00{,}000$, $N = 50$ years, $i = 5\% = 0.05$.

**(i) Annual Sinking Fund Instalment (3 marks):**

$$
A_{sf} = (C - S) \times \frac{i}{(1+i)^{N} - 1}
$$

$$
A_{sf} = 11{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.05}{(1.05)^{50} - 1}
$$

$$
(1.05)^{50} = 11.4674
$$

$$
A_{sf} = 11{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.05}{10.4674} = 11{,}00{,}000 \times 0.004777
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{sf} = \text{₹}5{,}254.72 \text{ per year}}
$$

**(ii) Total Annual Depreciation Charge (2 marks):**

$$
D_{annual} = 11{,}00{,}000 \times \left[ 0.05 + 0.004777 \right]
$$

$$
D_{annual} = 11{,}00{,}000 \times 0.054777 = \text{₹}60{,}254.72 \text{ per year}
$$

**(iii) Book Value at End of 20 years (2 marks):**

After 20 years, the *depreciation accumulated* = $D_{annual} \times 20$ (only the Sinking Fund component accumulates with interest, but at the KTU level, this is simplified):

$$
\text{Accumulated Depreciation} = 60{,}254.72 \times 20 = \text{₹}12{,}05{,}094.40
$$

But total depreciable amount is only $(C - S) = ₹11,00,000$. The excess represents the **interest earned** on the sinking fund, which lies within the fund itself. Hence the **Book Value of the building** is:

$$
\boxed{BV_{20} = C - 11{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}00{,}000 \text{ (equals scrap value)}}
$$

This confirms the elegance of the Sinking Fund Method — the book value approaches the scrap value *exactly* at the end of the useful life.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Valuation Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add the Land Value** — students often subtract depreciation and stop. Always remember the formula: $V = (RC - D) + LV$. **Loss: 1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Using Original Cost instead of Replacement Cost** — when cost indices are given, you **MUST** index the original cost to present-day value before computing depreciation. **Loss: 2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong Year Purchase Formula** — confusing the perpetuity formula $YP = 1/i$ with the annuity formula $YP = \frac{(1+i)^{N}-1}{i(1+i)^{N}}$. For limited-period income (lease), use the latter. **Loss: 1 mark**.
> 4. **Mixing up scrap value** — the scrap value is the **terminal value** at the end of useful life, NOT today's salvage. In SLM: $D = (C - S)/N$. **Loss: 1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping units and currency symbols** — examiners reward well-formatted answers. Always write **₹** symbols and explicit units.
> 6. **Forgetting to add ancillary values** — in the Replacement Cost Method, add value of *water supply, electrical fittings, fencing, compound wall, etc.* if mentioned in the problem.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ⭐ **Year's Purchase (YP) = 100/r** for perpetuity; **YP = PWF = annuity factor** for limited-period income.
- ⭐ **Capitalized Value = Net Annual Income × YP** — foundation of the Rental Method.
- ⭐ **Five Methods of Valuation:** Rental, Direct Comparison, Replacement Cost, Profit-Based, Development.
- ⭐ **Replacement Cost Formula:** $V = (RC - D) + LV + \text{Ancillary Value}$.
- ⭐ **RC Computation Methods:** Plinth Area, Cubical Content, Detailed BOQ, **Index Method** (most common in KTU).
- ⭐ **Index Method Formula:** $RC = OC \times (CI_{new} / CI_{old})$.
- ⭐ **Four Depreciation Methods:** SLM (constant), SFM (constant with interest), Declining Balance (decreasing), Quantity Survey (empirical).
- ⭐ **SLM Depreciation:** $D_n = (C - S)/N$ — constant every year, ignores time value of money.
- ⭐ **Sinking Fund Depreciation:** $D_n = (C-S) \times \frac{i}{(1+i)^{N} - 1}$ — only method that *fully recovers* capital at end of life.
- ⭐ **Declining Balance:** $BV_n = C(1-r)^{n}$ — book value decreases exponentially, never reaches zero.
- ⭐ **Replacement Cost Method is preferred for farm structures** because they are owner-occupied and rarely transacted in the market.
- ⭐ **Cross-check valuations** using a second method (Rental vs RCM) to arrive at a *fair final value*.
- ⭐ **Always include Land Value (LV) separately** — land does NOT depreciate.
- ⭐ **Salvage/Scrap Value** is the value at the **end of useful life**, used in SLM and SFM formulas.
- ⭐ **KTU 2024 Scheme weightage:** Year Purchase + Replacement Cost together typically carries **~12–14 marks** (one full Part B question) in the End Semester Exam.
- ⭐ **Examiner loves tabular answers and formula boxes** — present depreciation computations in a clean table format.
- ⭐ **Memorize the 4-step structure:** (1) Index the cost → (2) Compute depreciation → (3) Depreciated value of building → (4) Add land value.
- ⭐ **Currency & Units:** Always write ₹ and m² / m³ explicitly in the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rental Return Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT501 - DESIGN OF FARM STRUCTURES AND COST ESTIMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rental Return Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rental Return Method (RRM)** is an income-based technique of property valuation in which the **Capitalized Value (CV)** of a farm structure is determined by capitalizing its **Net Annual Rent (NAR)** at an appropriate rate of interest, expressed through a multiplier known as the **Years Purchase (YP)**.

Mathematically, the fundamental valuation identity is:

$$
\text{Capitalized Value} \;=\; \text{Net Annual Rent} \times \text{Years Purchase}
$$

In the context of **farm structures** (cattle sheds, poultry houses, dairy barns, godowns, greenhouses, farm offices), this method is preferred when the structure yields a measurable, recurring income — e.g., a leased dairy unit, a hired-out storage godown, or a commercial greenhouse rented to an SHG/FPO.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Mango Tree"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a fully grown mango tree in your farm. Every year, *after* you pay for water, pesticides, pruning labour, and fertilizer, the tree puts **₹10,000 of net profit** into your pocket — and it will keep doing so for the **next 25 years** (its productive life). If a buyer asks, *"What is a fair price for this tree today?"* — you would say: *"Pay me the lump sum that, if invested at 8% interest, would generate ₹10,000 every year for 25 years."* That lump sum is exactly the **Capitalized Value** under the Rental Return Method. The tree's rent (₹10,000) is the **Net Annual Rent**, the multiplier is the **Years Purchase**, and the lump sum is the **Valuation**.

The same logic applies to a poultry shed leased for ₹2,00,000/year for 15 years. The structure is the "tree," the rent is the "mango profit," and the buyer wants the present-day lump-sum equivalent.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU Answers)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Gross Rent (GR)** | Total contractual annual rent receivable from the tenant (₹/year). |
| **Outgoings (O)** | Annual expenses the owner must still bear (repairs, taxes, insurance, sinking fund, management, vacancy loss). |
| **Net Annual Rent (NAR)** | $NAR = GR - O$ — the actual income the structure yields. |
| **Years Purchase (YP)** | A dimensionless multiplier that converts a recurring annual income into a one-time capitalized sum. |
| **Rate of Interest (i)** | Prevailing market return on safe investments, expressed as a decimal (e.g., **8% = 0.08**). |
| **Sinking Fund (SF)** | A periodic deposit made so that, with accumulated interest, it exactly replaces the structure at the end of its life. |
| **Capitalized Value (CV)** | The present lump-sum worth of the structure, i.e., the answer the method produces. |
| **Perpetuity** | An income stream assumed to continue **forever** (e.g., freehold land rent). |
| **Limited Period** | An income stream lasting a finite number of years **n** (the typical case for farm structures). |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners award **1 mark** in 14-mark problems for clearly defining *Net Annual Rent* and *Years Purchase* in your own words. Always begin long answers with a crisp two-line definition.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Six Sequential Steps

The valuation engineer follows a deterministic, six-step pipeline. Memorize this order; it is the **canonical solution outline** for KTU 14-mark questions.

1. **Step 1 — Establish Gross Rent (GR).** Obtain the *fair, sustainable* annual rent from comparable lettings (lease deeds, market enquiry, government circle rates for agricultural buildings).
2. **Step 2 — Itemize Outgoings (O).** Quantify every annual recurring expenditure the owner cannot pass to the tenant. The five classical heads are:
   - **Repairs & Maintenance (R&M):** typically **1% to 2%** of the *original capital cost* of the structure.
   - **Insurance premium:** typically **0.5% to 1%** of the capital cost (fire, flood, theft cover).
   - **Property / Municipal Tax:** levied by the local Panchayat or Municipality on built-up area.
   - **Management & Supervision charges:** **5% to 10%** of *gross rent* (only if owner is actively managing).
   - **Sinking Fund (SF):** periodic annual deposit that accumulates to the *replacement cost* at the end of the structure's useful life.
3. **Step 3 — Compute Net Annual Rent.**
   $$NAR \;=\; GR - O$$
4. **Step 4 — Select the appropriate Years Purchase (YP).** This depends on two questions:
   - (a) Is the income stream *perpetual* (freehold) or *limited* (lease of n years)?
   - (b) Is the structure's life *infinite* (land) or *finite* (n years of useful life)?
5. **Step 5 — Apply the Capitalized Value formula.**
   $$CV \;=\; NAR \times YP$$
6. **Step 6 — Sanity check & reporting.** Compare with other methods (e.g., cost-based, depreciation-based) and discuss any divergence.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Component

- **Why subtract outgoings?** Because the buyer's *true* annual cash-flow is rent minus expenses — only that stream represents the structure's real earning power.
- **Why a Sinking Fund?** A farm structure has a *finite life* (15–30 years). The buyer must rebuild it eventually; a SF provision ensures the *replacement capital* is available when needed, mirroring the mathematical idea of an annuity.
- **Why use YP and not just divide by interest?** Because pure division ($NAR/i$) assumes the rent never stops — true only for freehold land. For a building, the rent stops when the building collapses, so the multiplier must be *finite* and *time-decaying*.
- **Why does management charge appear as a percentage of GR, not NAR?** By convention it scales with revenue (more rent → more managerial effort), not with the residual income.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table — it covers **>90%** of all numerical problems KTU sets on the Rental Return Method.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Net Annual Rent | $NAR = GR - O$ | ₹/year | Always, as the first computation. |
| 2 | Outgoings (sum) | $O = R\&M + Ins. + Tax + Mgmt. + SF$ | ₹/year | Step 2. |
| 3 | Sinking Fund Instalment (SFI) | $SFI = \dfrac{S \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | ₹/year | When the structure has a finite life $n$ and replacement cost $S$. |
| 4 | YP — Perpetuity (freehold) | $YP_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{i}$ | dimensionless | Only for land or structures of infinite life. |
| 5 | YP — Limited Period (n years) | $YP_{n} = \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ | dimensionless | Most common — a lease of $n$ years. |
| 6 | YP — Deferred Perpetuity (k years) | $YP_{deferred} = \dfrac{1}{i(1+i)^{k}}$ | dimensionless | Income starts after $k$ years. |
| 7 | Capitalized Value | $CV = NAR \times YP$ | ₹ | The final valuation figure. |
| 8 | Capitalized Value with SF provision | $CV = (NAR - SFI)\cdot YP_{n} + \dfrac{S}{(1+i)^{n}}$ | ₹ | The *purest* form, used in advanced KTU problems. |

> **Convention used in the table:** $i$ = annual rate (decimal), $n$ = remaining useful life in years, $S$ = future replacement cost (₹), $k$ = deferment period in years.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In agricultural and farm-engineering practice, the Rental Return Method is the **gold standard for valuing**:
- **Dairy farm buildings** leased to a co-operative society.
- **Poultry sheds** given on long-term rent to integrators.
- **Cold storage godowns** and **warehouses** in market yards.
- **Greenhouse clusters** rented to FPOs (Farmer Producer Organizations).
- **Aquaculture ponds with pucca bund structures** on lease.

It is *less* appropriate for purely owner-occupied farmhouses (no rent stream) and *inappropriate* for newly-built, un-let structures (no market data on rent yet — use the cost method instead).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Years Purchase for a Perpetuity ($YP_{\infty}$)

> **Premise:** A buyer pays a lump sum $P$ today. Invested at rate $i$, it generates a *perpetual* annual interest income of $R = P \cdot i$. The buyer would be indifferent between holding the asset and investing the money if the income streams are equal. Therefore, by equilibrium:
> $$P \cdot i \;=\; R \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P \;=\; \dfrac{R}{i}$$
> The multiplier $\dfrac{1}{i}$ is the **Years Purchase for perpetuity**.

$$
\boxed{\,YP_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{i}\,}
$$

**Sanity check (numerical):** At $i = 8\% = 0.08$,
$$YP_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{0.08} = 12.5$$
meaning ₹1 of perpetual annual rent is worth ₹12.5 today. This matches the well-known "12.5% cap-rate inversion."

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Years Purchase for a Limited Period ($YP_{n}$)

> **Premise:** The income stream of ₹$R$ runs for exactly $n$ years and then stops. We need the *present value* of an ordinary annuity of $R$ for $n$ periods at discount rate $i$.

Let $P$ be the present value. The cash flows at the end of years 1, 2, …, $n$ are each $R$. Discounting each:

$$
P = \dfrac{R}{(1+i)} + \dfrac{R}{(1+i)^{2}} + \dfrac{R}{(1+i)^{3}} + \cdots + \dfrac{R}{(1+i)^{n}}
$$

This is a **finite geometric series** with first term $a = \dfrac{R}{1+i}$, common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$, and $n$ terms. Using the geometric-series sum formula $S_{n} = a \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^{n}}{1 - r}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{R}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}} \\
  &= \frac{R}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}} \\
  &= R \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the multiplier:

$$
\boxed{\,YP_{n} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}\,}
$$

**Equivalent closed form (often used by KTU):**

$$
YP_{n} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Sanity check:** As $n \to \infty$, $(1+i)^{-n} \to 0$, and $YP_{n} \to 1/i = YP_{\infty}$. ✔ (Consistency with Perpetuity case.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Sinking Fund Instalment (SFI)

> **Premise:** The owner must save a *constant* annual deposit $A$ at the end of each year so that, with interest compounded at rate $i$, the accumulated value at the end of year $n$ equals the replacement cost $S$.

The future value of an ordinary annuity of $A$ for $n$ years is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i) + A \\
  &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $A$ (the annual SFI):

$$
\boxed{\,SFI = A = S \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}\,}
$$

**Numerical sanity check:** Let $S = ₹10,00,000$, $i = 0.08$, $n = 20$ years.

$$
SFI = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.08}{(1.08)^{20} - 1} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.08}{4.6610 - 1} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.08}{3.6610} \approx ₹21{,}852 \text{ / year}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — The "Full-Bodied" Capitalized Value (CV with SF Provision)

> **Premise:** This is the *most rigorous* form. The owner receives $NAR$ per year but must annually set aside $SFI$ to rebuild. The *net spendable income* is therefore $(NAR - SFI)$ for $n$ years. At year $n$, the owner recovers the *replacement lump sum* $S$ (the new structure). The total capitalized value is:

$$
CV = \underbrace{(NAR - SFI) \times YP_{n}}_{\text{PV of net annual income}} + \underbrace{\frac{S}{(1+i)^{n}}}_{\text{PV of replacement value at year } n}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,CV = (NAR - SFI)\cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} + S \cdot (1+i)^{-n}\,}
$$

This formula appears in **Part B (14-mark)** questions with the explicit phrasing *"allowing for a sinking fund"*.

---

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Matrix (Five Canonical Problem Types)

Below is a *taxonomy* of the numerical archetypes KTU sets. Every problem reduces to one of these.

| Type | Data Given | Unknown | Key Formula Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | $GR$, all outgoings, $i$ | $CV$ (perpetual) | $NAR = GR - O$, then $CV = NAR / i$ |
| B | $GR$, all outgoings, $i$, $n$ | $CV$ (limited period) | $CV = NAR \cdot YP_{n}$ |
| C | $GR$, $S$, $i$, $n$, $R\&M\%$ etc. | $CV$ *with SF* | $CV = (NAR - SFI) \cdot YP_{n} + S(1+i)^{-n}$ |
| D | $GR$ deferred by $k$ years, $i$ | $CV$ (deferred) | $CV = NAR \times YP_{deferred}$ |
| E | *Reverse:* Given $CV$ and other data | $GR$ or fair rent | Algebraic inversion of any row above |

---

## 3.6 Fully Solved Numerical Illustration (Type C — Most Common in KTU)

> **Problem:** A poultry shed in a KTU-model farm is let out at a **gross annual rent of ₹4,00,000**. The structure has a **remaining useful life of 15 years** and an estimated **replacement cost of ₹25,00,000** at the end of its life. Annual outgoings are estimated as: repairs **1.5%** of replacement cost, insurance **0.5%** of replacement cost, and management **8%** of gross rent. The prevailing market rate of interest is **9%**. Compute the capitalized value of the shed, properly accounting for a sinking fund.

### Step 1 — Itemize the Outgoings

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Repairs} &= 1.5\% \times 25{,}00{,}000 = ₹37{,}500 \text{ / year} \\
\text{Insurance} &= 0.5\% \times 25{,}00{,}000 = ₹12{,}500 \text{ / year} \\
\text{Management} &= 8\% \times 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹32{,}000 \text{ / year} \\
\text{Total Outgoings } (O) &= 37{,}500 + 12{,}500 + 32{,}000 = ₹82{,}000 \text{ / year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Net Annual Rent

$$
NAR = GR - O = 4{,}00{,}000 - 82{,}000 = \text{₹}3{,}18{,}000 \text{ / year}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Sinking Fund Instalment

$$
SFI = S \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = 25{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.09}{(1.09)^{15} - 1}
$$

Compute $(1.09)^{15}$:
$$
(1.09)^{15} \approx 3.6425 \quad\text{(using standard compound-interest tables)}
$$

$$
SFI = 25{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.09}{3.6425 - 1} = 25{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.09}{2.6425} = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.03405 \approx \text{₹}85{,}125 \text{ / year}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Years Purchase for 15 years at 9%

$$
YP_{15} = \frac{1 - (1.09)^{-15}}{0.09} = \frac{1 - \dfrac{1}{3.6425}}{0.09} = \frac{1 - 0.2745}{0.09} = \frac{0.7255}{0.09} \approx 8.061
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Capitalized Value

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= (NAR - SFI) \times YP_{15} + S \times (1.09)^{-15} \\
   &= (3{,}18{,}000 - 85{,}125) \times 8.061 + 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2745 \\
   &= 2{,}32{,}875 \times 8.061 + 6{,}86{,}250 \\
   &= 18{,}77{,}205 + 6{,}86{,}250 \\
   &= \text{₹}25{,}63{,}455 \;\approx\; \text{₹}25.63 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A buyer should pay approximately **₹25.63 lakhs** today to acquire this poultry shed, given the 9% market rate, the 15-year remaining life, and the obligation to rebuild it at ₹25 lakhs after 15 years.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow — The Six-Step Valuation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete procedural logic of the Rental Return Method as it must be executed for any KTU 14-mark numerical problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Rental Return Method"] --> B["Step 1: Identify Gross Rent GR"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Itemize Outgoings O"]
    C --> D1["Repairs and Maintenance"]
    C --> D2["Insurance Premium"]
    C --> D3["Property Tax"]
    C --> D4["Management Charges"]
    C --> D5["Sinking Fund Instalment SFI"]
    D1 --> E["Sum all outgoings to get O"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F["Step 3: Compute NAR equals GR minus O"]
    F --> G["Step 4: Decide income duration"]
    G --> G1{"Perpetual or Limited"}
    G1 -- Perpetual --> H1["Use YP equals 1 divided by i"]
    G1 -- Limited --> H2["Use YP equals 1 minus 1 plus i power minus n divided by i"]
    G1 -- Deferred --> H3["Use YP equals 1 divided by i times 1 plus i power k"]
    H1 --> I["Step 5: Compute Capitalized Value"]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J["Step 6: Report and Cross Check"]
    J --> K["END: Final CV in Rupees"]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style G1 fill:#ffcc00,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#d62728,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Component-Interaction Block Diagram — Inputs, Engine, Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT DATA BLOCK"]
        P1["Gross Rent GR rupees per year"]
        P2["Useful Life n in years"]
        P3["Replacement Cost S in rupees"]
        P4["Interest Rate i percent"]
        P5["Outgoing Percentages for R M Tax Mgmt"]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE["COMPUTATION ENGINE"]
        Q1["Compute Outgoings O"]
        Q2["Compute NAR"]
        Q3["Compute SFI from S i n"]
        Q4["Compute YP from i and n"]
        Q5["Apply CV Formula"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["FINAL OUTPUT"]
        R1["Capitalized Value CV in rupees"]
        R2["Years Purchase YP dimensionless"]
        R3["Annual SFI in rupees per year"]
    end

    P1 --> Q2
    P2 --> Q3
    P2 --> Q4
    P3 --> Q3
    P4 --> Q3
    P4 --> Q4
    P5 --> Q1
    P1 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q5
    Q3 --> Q5
    Q4 --> Q5

    Q5 --> R1
    Q4 --> R2
    Q3 --> R3

    style INPUTS fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style ENGINE fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style OUTPUTS fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in a KTU answer sheet:** The first diagram is the *procedural* map (what to do, in what order). The second is the *structural* map (what data flows into what sub-formula). Reproducing the procedural map (or even a textual list mirroring it) at the start of a 14-mark answer earns a **1-mark "approach" credit** from KTU examiners.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the Rental Return Method of valuation. State the formula for Capitalized Value.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Rental Return Method** is an income-based technique of property valuation in which the capitalized value of a structure is found by multiplying its **Net Annual Rent (NAR)** by an appropriate multiplier called the **Years Purchase (YP)**.

$$
\text{Capitalized Value (CV)} = \text{Net Annual Rent (NAR)} \times \text{Years Purchase (YP)}
$$

The NAR is obtained by deducting all annual outgoings (repairs, insurance, taxes, management, sinking fund) from the **Gross Annual Rent**, and the YP is selected based on the period of the income stream (perpetual: $1/i$; limited: $\frac{1-(1+i)^{-n}}{i}$). **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for the explanation of NAR and YP.)**

---

### **Q2. Distinguish between the 'Perpetual' and 'Limited Period' forms of Years Purchase.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Perpetual YP | Limited-Period YP |
|---|---|---|
| Income duration | **Infinite** (forever) | **Finite**, lasts $n$ years |
| Formula | $YP = \dfrac{1}{i}$ | $YP = \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ |
| Applicable to | Freehold land, perpetual leases | Buildings, sheds, finite-life structures |
| Numerical example @ 8% | $1/0.08 = 12.5$ | At $n=20$, $YP = 9.818$ |
| Property type | Land parcel | Poultry shed, godown, dairy barn |

**(1 mark each for: distinction, formula pair, and correct example.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below are two **fully independent** alternatives — answer either **Q.A** or **Q.B**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** A farm godown in Thrissur is let out at a **gross rent of ₹3,60,000 per annum**. The owner estimates annual outgoings as: repairs **2% of capital cost**, insurance **1% of capital cost**, property tax **₹18,000**, and management charges **6% of gross rent**. The capital cost of the structure is **₹20,00,000**, the prevailing rate of interest is **8%**, and the remaining useful life is **25 years**. Determine the capitalized value of the godown using the Rental Return Method. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of a **Sinking Fund** in valuation. Why is it necessary to deduct the Sinking Fund Instalment from the Net Annual Rent before applying Years Purchase? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Itemize outgoings (per year):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Repairs} &= 2\% \times 20{,}00{,}000 = ₹40{,}000 \\
\text{Insurance} &= 1\% \times 20{,}00{,}000 = ₹20{,}000 \\
\text{Property Tax} &= ₹18{,}000 \\
\text{Management} &= 6\% \times 3{,}60{,}000 = ₹21{,}600 \\
\hline
\text{Total Outgoings } (O) &= 40{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 21{,}600 = ₹99{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Annual Rent:**

$$
NAR = GR - O = 3{,}60{,}000 - 99{,}600 = \text{₹}2{,}60{,}400 \text{ / year}
$$

**Step 3 — Years Purchase for 25 years at 8%:**

$$
YP_{25} = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-25}}{0.08}
$$

Compute $(1.08)^{25} \approx 6.8485$, so $(1.08)^{-25} \approx 0.1460$:

$$
YP_{25} = \frac{1 - 0.1460}{0.08} = \frac{0.8540}{0.08} = 10.6748
$$

**Step 4 — Capitalized Value:**

$$
CV = NAR \times YP_{25} = 2{,}60{,}400 \times 10.6748 \approx \text{₹}27{,}79{,}716 \approx \text{₹}27.80 \text{ lakhs}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):** $\text{Capitalized Value} \approx \text{₹}27{,}79{,}716$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

A **Sinking Fund (SF)** is a compulsory periodic annual deposit, made by the owner into a *separate interest-bearing account*, so that at the end of the structure's useful life the accumulated amount — original deposits plus compound interest — exactly equals the **replacement cost** of the structure. The annual deposit is the **Sinking Fund Instalment (SFI)**:

$$
SFI = S \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

The SFI is deducted from the NAR **before** applying Years Purchase for three rigorous reasons:

1. **Replacement obligation is real, not nominal.** A farm structure has a finite life. If the buyer does not set aside the replacement cost, the investment is *depleting* — the buyer will face a future liability. The SFI provision explicitly converts the depleting asset into a *self-renewing* one, treating the structure as if its income stream were perpetual.

2. **Correctness of the perpetuity assumption.** When we *remove* the SFI from the annual income and *add back* the discounted replacement value at the end, the resulting cash-flow is mathematically equivalent to a perpetuity. This is the bridge that lets the *limited-period* structure be valued as though it were *infinite-life* land.

3. **Fairness to both parties.** Without SFI deduction, the buyer overpays (because the seller is pocketing money that should have been reserved for rebuilding). With SFI deduction, the valuation is *internally consistent*: the buyer funds the replacement and keeps the net income stream.

**(Marks distribution: 3 marks SFI definition + formula; 2 marks for "why deduct"; 2 marks for the three reasons.)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** A dairy barn owned by a college is leased to a private dairy firm. The lease provides a **gross annual rent of ₹5,00,000** for the next **20 years**. The annual outgoings are: repairs **₹45,000**, insurance **₹15,000**, management **₹40,000**, and property tax **₹20,000**. The rate of interest is **7.5%**. The structure has a replacement cost of **₹30,00,000** and a remaining useful life equal to the lease period. Compute the *full-bodied* capitalized value, properly accounting for the sinking fund. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A freehold agricultural land parcel near the campus yields a **perpetual net annual rent of ₹1,20,000**. Compute its capitalized value at **(i) 6%**, **(ii) 8%**, and **(iii) 10%** rate of interest. Comment on the inverse relationship between the rate of interest and the capitalized value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Net Annual Rent (excluding SFI):**

$$
O = 45{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 20{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
NAR = GR - O = 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹}3{,}80{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Sinking Fund Instalment:**

$$
SFI = 30{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.075}{(1.075)^{20} - 1}
$$

$(1.075)^{20} \approx 4.2479$:

$$
SFI = 30{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.075}{4.2479 - 1} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.075}{3.2479} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.02309 \approx \text{₹}69{,}270
$$

**Step 3 — Years Purchase (20 years, 7.5%):**

$$
YP_{20} = \frac{1 - (1.075)^{-20}}{0.075} = \frac{1 - 0.2354}{0.075} = \frac{0.7646}{0.075} \approx 10.194
$$

**Step 4 — Discounted Replacement Value at Year 20:**

$$
\frac{S}{(1.075)^{20}} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2354 \approx \text{₹}7{,}06{,}200
$$

**Step 5 — Full-Bodied Capitalized Value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= (NAR - SFI) \times YP_{20} + \frac{S}{(1.075)^{20}} \\
  &= (3{,}80{,}000 - 69{,}270) \times 10.194 + 7{,}06{,}200 \\
  &= 3{,}10{,}730 \times 10.194 + 7{,}06{,}200 \\
  &= 31{,}67{,}182 + 7{,}06{,}200 \\
  &= \text{₹}38{,}73{,}382
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{Capitalized Value} \approx \text{₹}38.73 \text{ lakhs}$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

For a perpetuity, $CV = NAR / i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } i = 6\%: \quad CV &= \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{0.06} = \text{₹}20{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{At } i = 8\%: \quad CV &= \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{0.08} = \text{₹}15{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{At } i = 10\%: \quad CV &= \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{0.10} = \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on the inverse relationship:**

As the rate of interest $i$ increases, the buyer requires a *higher return per rupee invested*, so she is willing to pay *less* today for a fixed annual income stream. The Capitalized Value is **inversely proportional** to the rate of interest when NAR is constant — a 1% rise in $i$ (e.g., from 8% to 9%) causes CV to fall by approximately the same percentage (e.g., from ₹15 lakh to ~₹13.33 lakh). This is the *capitalization rate* concept, fundamental in real-estate and farm-land valuation.

**(Marks distribution: 1.5 marks each for the three CVs, 2.5 marks for the explanation.)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Rental Return Method Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract SFI from NAR** *before* multiplying by YP. Many students correctly compute SFI but then incorrectly use the full NAR in the formula. **Solution:** Always write $CV = (NAR - SFI) \cdot YP_{n} + S(1+i)^{-n}$ explicitly in the answer sheet.
>
> 2. **Mixing up discount rate and capitalization rate.** The YP formula uses the *interest* (discount) rate. Some students use the *inflation* rate by mistake.
>
> 3. **Wrong units of $i$.** KTU expects $i$ as a decimal (8% = 0.08). Using 8 directly inflates the answer by 100×.
>
> 4. **Omitting the replacement-value term** in the full-bodied formula. The second term, $S(1+i)^{-n}$, is mandatory when SFI is being deducted.
>
> 5. **Skipping the verbal definition** of NAR and YP at the start of a 14-mark answer. **1 mark** is reserved for this; missing it is a guaranteed loss.
>
> 6. **Not showing the substitution step** for compound interest. Examiners want to see $(1.08)^{15} = \ldots$ explicitly written, not just the final YP value.
>
> 7. **Arithmetic slip in geometric series:** Forgetting the parentheses in $\frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ is the #1 silent killer of 14-mark answers. Double-check the bracketing before writing the final number.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this section to your wall the night before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ The Rental Return Method values a property by capitalizing its **Net Annual Rent (NAR)** via a multiplier called the **Years Purchase (YP)**.
- ✅ $CV = NAR \times YP$ is the **golden identity** — write it first on the answer sheet.
- ✅ $NAR = \text{Gross Rent} - \text{All Outgoings (R\&M, Insurance, Tax, Management, SFI)}$.
- ✅ **Perpetual YP** $= 1/i$ — only for freehold land / infinite-life assets.
- ✅ **Limited-period YP** $= \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ — the *workhorse* formula for farm structures.
- ✅ **Sinking Fund Instalment** $= S \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ — the *replacement-provision* formula.
- ✅ **Full-bodied CV** $= (NAR - SFI)\cdot YP_{n} + S(1+i)^{-n}$ — the *most rigorous* form; use it whenever SFI is mentioned.
- ✅ The Capitalized Value is **inversely proportional** to the rate of interest — a higher $i$ means lower $CV$.
- ✅ Always **quote $i$ as a decimal** (8% = 0.08, *not* 8).
- ✅ Always **state the verbal definition** of NAR and YP at the start of a 14-mark answer.
- ✅ **Six-step pipeline**: (1) GR → (2) Outgoings → (3) NAR → (4) YP choice → (5) CV → (6) Sanity check.
- ✅ For a **deferred income stream** (rent starts after $k$ years), use $YP_{deferred} = \dfrac{1}{i(1+i)^{k}}$.
- ✅ For a **freehold land parcel** with a perpetual net rent, $CV$ reduces to the simplest form $NAR/i$.
- ✅ **Sinking Fund provision** converts a finite-life structure's income into a *perpetual-equivalent* stream, which is the conceptual key to the full-bodied formula.
- ✅ In KTU Part B, always reproduce the **6-step pipeline** in your answer — examiners reward structured, methodical working.
- ✅ Cross-check: the *limited-period* YP **must** be less than the *perpetual* YP for the same $i$. If your answer violates this, you have made an arithmetic error.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Rental Return Method**
